topics.title: Introduction and Significance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET303 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction and Significance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Thermal Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Engineering** is the specialized branch of mechanical engineering that deals with the **study, analysis, design, and application of systems involving the conversion, transfer, and utilization of thermal (heat) energy into useful mechanical work or vice versa.**

It fundamentally bridges the gap between **energy in motion (thermodynamics)** and **energy in transit (heat transfer)**, forming the bedrock for the design of prime movers, refrigeration systems, and power generation plants.

### The Three Pillars of Thermal Engineering

The discipline is traditionally subdivided into three interconnected pillars:

1. **Engineering Thermodynamics** — Governs the *why* (energy conservation, state changes, cycle analysis).
2. **Heat Transfer** — Governs the *how fast* (conduction, convection, radiation rates).
3. **Thermal Systems & Power Plant Engineering** — Governs the *practical application* (IC engines, boilers, turbines, condensers, HVAC).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course PCIET303 explicitly focuses on the practical application of thermodynamic principles to reciprocating and rotary machines, treating heat transfer as a supporting analytical tool.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Energy Currency Exchange"

Think of **Thermal Engineering** as a **Currency Exchange Booth at an International Airport**.

| Thermal Engineering Concept | Everyday Analogy |
|-----------------------------|------------------|
| **Heat Energy ($Q$)** | Foreign currency (raw thermal resource). |
| **Mechanical Work ($W$)** | Local currency (what your machine actually needs). |
| **Working Fluid (Steam/Air)** | The customer carrying the money. |
| **Prime Mover (Engine/Turbine)** | The teller at the booth executing the exchange. |
| **Heat Loss / Rejection** | Exchange fee / taxes (inevitable loss). |
| **Cycle Efficiency ($\eta$)** | The best possible exchange rate you can get. |

> Just as a teller converts dollars to rupees using a regulated rate (with some loss as commission), a heat engine converts high-temperature heat energy into mechanical work, but **never at 100% efficiency** — some energy is always rejected to a low-temperature sink. The *engineer's job* is to maximize this exchange rate using clever cycle design.

---

## 1.3 Why is Thermal Engineering Significant?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy is the lifeblood of modern civilization, and ~80% of the world's primary energy is still processed through thermal systems.**

### Key Reasons for Significance:

- **Power Generation Backbone:** Approximately **62% of global electricity** is generated via steam Rankine cycles and gas turbine Brayton cycles.
- **Transportation Dominance:** All internal combustion (IC) engines — petrol, diesel, jet — are thermal machines.
- **Industrial Process Heating:** Refineries, cement plants, paper mills, and steel plants depend entirely on thermal fluid systems.
- **Comfort & Preservation:** Refrigeration, air-conditioning, and cryogenics (LNG, medical cold chain) are inverted thermal cycles.
- **Renewable Integration:** Solar thermal, geothermal, biomass, and Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC) all rely on thermal engineering fundamentals.
- **Environmental Imperative:** Modern engineers design for **Net-Zero emissions**, making understanding of thermal efficiency and waste heat recovery essential.

### Standard Physical Constants Referenced Throughout

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|-------|------|
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314** | $\text{kJ/kmol}\cdot\text{K}$ |
| Characteristic Gas Constant (Air) | $R_{air}$ | **0.287** | $\text{kJ/kg}\cdot\text{K}$ |
| Adiabatic Index of Air ($\gamma$) | $\gamma$ | **1.4** | — |
| Adiabatic Index of Combustion Gases | $\gamma$ | **1.33** | — |
| Standard Atmospheric Pressure | $P_{atm}$ | **101.325** | kPa |
| Stefan-Boltzmann Constant | $\sigma$ | **5.67 × 10⁻⁸** | $\text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$ |

---

## 1.4 Historical Evolution (Brief)

The timeline below shows how thermal engineering evolved through landmark discoveries:

- **1824** — Sadi Carnot published *Réflexions sur la puissance motrice du feu*, founding the **2nd Law of Thermodynamics**.
- **1876** — Nikolaus Otto built the **4-stroke Otto cycle engine**.
- **1885** — Gottlieb Daimler & Wilhelm Maybach built the first **high-speed gasoline engine**.
- **1892** — Rudolf Diesel patented the **Diesel cycle engine**.
- **1897** — Charles Parsons invented the **steam turbine** for power generation.
- **1930s** — Frank Whittle & Hans von Ohain developed the **jet engine (Brayton cycle)**.
- **20th–21st C** — Combined cycle, cogeneration, and supercritical power plants push efficiencies toward **60–65%**.


<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Core Operational Concept: Energy Conversion via Working Fluid

Every thermal device operates on a single fundamental philosophy:

> **A working fluid is taken through a closed sequence of processes (a cycle) such that net heat is absorbed at high temperature, partially converted to work, and the remainder rejected at low temperature.**

This is captured mathematically by the **First Law applied to a cycle**:

$$\sum Q = \sum W$$

And constrained by the **Second Law (Kelvin-Planck statement)**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kelvin-Planck Statement:** *It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no other effect than the transfer of heat from a single thermal reservoir and the production of an equivalent amount of work.*

This leads directly to the concept of **Thermal Efficiency**, the most important metric in thermal engineering.

---

## 2.2 Defining the Performance Metrics

### A. Thermal Efficiency ($\eta_{th}$)

Applicable to **power cycles** (engines, turbines — converting heat *into* work).

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{supplied}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{rejected}}{Q_{supplied}}$$

### B. Coefficient of Performance (COP)

Applicable to **refrigeration & heat pump cycles** (consuming work *to* move heat).

$$COP_{Ref} = \frac{Desired\ Output}{Required\ Input} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}}$$

$$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** An efficiency $\eta$ is always between 0 and 1, but a **COP can be greater than 1** (e.g., a domestic AC with COP = 3.5 moves 3.5 kW of heat per 1 kW of electricity).

### C. Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC) — Practical Metric

Used for IC engines where fuel is the input.

$$SFC = \frac{m_{fuel}}{W_{net}} \quad \left[\frac{\text{kg/kWh}}{\text{or}}\right]$$

$$BSFC = \frac{m_{fuel}}{W_{brake}} \quad \text{(Brake Specific Fuel Consumption)}$$

### D. Mean Effective Pressure (MEP)

A standard measure of an engine's "ability to do work per unit volume."

$$MEP = \frac{W_{net}}{V_{swept}} \quad \left[\text{kPa}\right]$$

---

## 2.3 Classification of Thermal Systems

| Classification | Energy Direction | Examples | Governing Cycles |
|----------------|------------------|----------|------------------|
| **Power Plants** | Heat → Work | Steam Plant, Gas Turbine, Nuclear | Rankine, Brayton |
| **IC Engines** | Chemical Energy → Work | Petrol, Diesel, Gas Engines | Otto, Diesel, Dual |
| **Jet Propulsion** | Heat → Kinetic Energy | Aircraft engines, Rockets | Brayton (modified) |
| **Refrigeration** | Work → Heat (extraction) | Domestic fridge, Cold storage | Vapour Compression, Vapour Absorption |
| **Heat Pumps** | Work → Heat (delivery) | Building heating, Geyser | Reverse Rankine |
| **Compressors** | Work → Pressure Energy | Air compressor, Gas compressor | Isentropic compression |

---

## 2.4 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA CHEAT SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **master reference** for Module 1 problem-solving. Memorize it for the University Exam.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $W_{net} = \oint P\,dV$ | Work in a closed cycle | P-V diagram analysis | kJ |
| 2 | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_R}{Q_S}$ | Thermal Efficiency | All power cycles | dimensionless |
| 3 | $COP_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}}$ | Refrigeration COP | AC, Fridge, Cold storage | dimensionless |
| 4 | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net}}$ | Heat Pump COP | Building heating | dimensionless |
| 5 | $COP_{Carnot,Ref} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Carnot COP (Ref.) | Upper bound benchmark | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Carnot Efficiency | Upper bound benchmark | dimensionless |
| 7 | $SFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{\dot{W}_{net}} \cdot 3600$ | Specific Fuel Consumption | Engine economy | kg/kWh |
| 8 | $MEP = \dfrac{W_{net}}{V_s}$ | Mean Effective Pressure | Engine performance | kPa |
| 9 | $PV = mRT$ | Ideal Gas Law | All thermodynamic calcs. | — |
| 10 | $Q = m c_p \Delta T$ | Sensible Heat | Heat transfer | kJ |
| 11 | $Q = m h_{fg}$ | Latent Heat | Phase change | kJ |
| 12 | $\dot{Q} = U A \Delta T_{lm}$ | Overall heat transfer | Heat exchangers | kW |
| 13 | $Nu = \dfrac{hL}{k}$ | Nusselt Number | Convection analysis | dimensionless |
| 14 | $\dot{q}_{rad} = \varepsilon \sigma (T_s^4 - T_{\infty}^4)$ | Radiation heat flux | High-temp surfaces | W/m² |

> **Note on notation:** $\dot{m}$ denotes mass flow rate (kg/s), $\dot{W}$ denotes power (kW), and $V_s$ is swept volume (m³).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles learned in this module directly enable engineers to:

- **Audit Energy Efficiency** of industrial boilers (saving lakhs of rupees annually).
- **Size Compressors & Pumps** for HVAC systems in malls, hospitals, data centers.
- **Design Refrigeration Chains** for pharmaceutical and food industries.
- **Select Prime Movers** for ships, locomotives, and aircraft.
- **Optimize Power Plants** to push thermal efficiency from 33% (subcritical) to **45%+ (supercritical)** and **60%+ (combined cycle)**.
- **Reduce Carbon Footprint** through waste heat recovery (WHR), cogeneration, and combined cycle plants.


<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: General Thermal Efficiency from First & Second Law

### Step 1: Apply the First Law to a Cyclic Process

For any working fluid undergoing a closed cycle, the change in internal energy over a complete cycle is zero ($\Delta U_{cycle} = 0$).

By the First Law:

$$\Delta U = Q_{net} - W_{net} = 0$$

Therefore:

$$Q_{net} = W_{net}$$

### Step 2: Split Heat Transfer into Two Parts

The cycle exchanges heat with two reservoirs: a high-temperature source ($T_H$) and a low-temperature sink ($T_L$).

$$Q_{net} = Q_S - Q_R$$

where $Q_S$ = heat supplied by source, $Q_R$ = heat rejected to sink.

### Step 3: Substitute into Work Expression

The net work output is the difference:

$$W_{net} = Q_S - Q_R$$

### Step 4: Define Thermal Efficiency

By definition, thermal efficiency is work output divided by heat input:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_S} = \frac{Q_S - Q_R}{Q_S}$$

### Step 5: Final Simplified Form

$$\boxed{\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{Q_R}{Q_S}}$$

> **Interpretation:** To maximize $\eta_{th}$, an engineer must either **minimize $Q_R$ (waste heat)** or **maximize $Q_S$ utilization**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Carnot Efficiency (Theoretical Upper Limit)

### Step 1: Apply Second Law — Reversible Process

For a *reversible* (frictionless, quasi-static) cycle, there is **no entropy generation**:

$$\Delta S_{universe} = 0$$

### Step 2: Entropy Change for Each Reservoir

When a reservoir loses heat $Q_S$ at constant $T_H$, its entropy decreases by $Q_S / T_H$.
When a reservoir gains heat $Q_R$ at constant $T_L$, its entropy increases by $Q_R / T_L$.

Setting the total entropy change to zero:

$$\frac{Q_R}{T_L} - \frac{Q_S}{T_H} = 0$$

### Step 3: Rearrange for the Heat Ratio

$$\frac{Q_R}{Q_S} = \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

### Step 4: Substitute into Efficiency Expression

Using the result of Section 3.1:

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{Q_R}{Q_S} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Temperatures MUST be in Kelvin (K)**. The Carnot efficiency is the **absolute ceiling** — no real engine operating between $T_H$ and $T_L$ can exceed it.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Coefficient of Performance of a Carnot Refrigerator

### Step 1: Use Carnot Heat Ratio Relationship

From Section 3.2, for a reversible cycle:

$$\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

### Step 2: Apply First Law

Energy balance on the refrigerator (work $W$ is input):

$$W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$$

### Step 3: Write COP Definition

$$COP_R = \frac{Desired\ Effect}{Work\ Input} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \frac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}$$

### Step 4: Substitute the Heat Ratio

Divide numerator and denominator by $Q_L$:

$$COP_R = \frac{1}{\dfrac{Q_H}{Q_L} - 1}$$

Using $Q_H / Q_L = T_H / T_L$:

$$COP_R = \frac{1}{\dfrac{T_H}{T_L} - 1} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{T_H - T_L}{T_L}}$$

$$\boxed{COP_{R,Carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}}$$

> **Insight:** A smaller temperature difference $(T_H - T_L)$ yields a *higher* COP. This is why refrigeration systems are easier to build for mild cooling (e.g., 25°C → 5°C) than for extreme cryogenics (e.g., 300 K → 4 K for liquid helium).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Performance Metrics

| Parameter | Power Cycle (Engine) | Refrigeration Cycle | Heat Pump |
|-----------|---------------------|---------------------|-----------|
| **Purpose** | Produce Work ($W$) | Remove Heat ($Q_L$) | Supply Heat ($Q_H$) |
| **Desired Output** | $W_{net}$ | $Q_L$ | $Q_H$ |
| **Required Input** | $Q_S$ (from hot source) | $W_{net}$ | $W_{net}$ |
| **Performance Metric** | $\eta_{th}$ | $COP_R$ | $COP_{HP}$ |
| **Ideal (Carnot) Value** | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - T_L/T_H$ | $T_L/(T_H - T_L)$ | $T_H/(T_H - T_L)$ |
| **Typical Range** | 25% – 45% | 2.0 – 4.5 | 2.5 – 5.0 |
| **Temperature Direction** | $T_H \to T_L$ (heat flows down) | $T_L \to T_H$ (heat pumped up) | $T_L \to T_H$ (heat pumped up) |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Determining COP of an Ideal Refrigerator

**Problem:** A domestic refrigerator maintains its freezer compartment at $-10°\text{C}$ while the room temperature is $27°\text{C}$. Calculate the Carnot COP.

### Step 1: Convert Temperatures to Kelvin

$$T_L = -10 + 273 = 263\ \text{K}$$

$$T_H = 27 + 273 = 300\ \text{K}$$

### Step 2: Apply Carnot COP Formula

$$COP_R = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{263}{300 - 263}$$

$$COP_R = \frac{263}{37}$$

$$\boxed{COP_R = 7.11}$$

### Step 3: Interpretation

> For every 1 kW of electrical power consumed, the ideal refrigerator could remove **7.11 kW** of heat from the cold space. Real refrigerators achieve only 1.5 – 3.5 due to irreversibilities (friction, finite temperature differences, pressure drops).

---

## 3.6 Worked Example: Carnot Efficiency of a Steam Power Plant

**Problem:** A thermal power plant operates between a boiler temperature of $500°\text{C}$ and a condenser temperature of $30°\text{C}$. Find the maximum possible efficiency.

### Step 1: Convert to Kelvin

$$T_H = 500 + 273 = 773\ \text{K}$$

$$T_L = 30 + 273 = 303\ \text{K}$$

### Step 2: Substitute into Carnot Formula

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{303}{773}$$

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - 0.3920$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{Carnot} = 0.6080 = 60.80\%}$$

> **Note:** Actual plant efficiency is **~38–42%** because real Rankine cycles are irreversible and have temperature glide across boiler tubes.

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Three Pillars of Thermal Engineering

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Thermal Engineering] --> B[Engineering Thermodynamics]
    A --> C[Heat Transfer]
    A --> D[Thermal Systems Applications]
    B --> B1[First Law Energy Balance]
    B --> B2[Second Law Entropy Analysis]
    B --> B3[Property Relations P V T]
    C --> C1[Conduction Fourier Law]
    C --> C2[Convection Newtons Law]
    C --> C3[Radiation Stefan Boltzmann]
    D --> D1[Power Generation Rankine Brayton]
    D --> D2[IC Engines Otto Diesel]
    D --> D3[Refrigeration Vapour Compression]
    D --> D4[Gas Turbines Jet Propulsion]
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** The figure maps the hierarchical decomposition of Thermal Engineering into its three foundational pillars, each branching into its constituent sub-topics as per the KTU 2024 syllabus structure.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Thermal System Classification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TF[Thermal Devices] --> P[Power Producing]
    TF --> R[Refrigeration]
    TF --> HP[Heat Pumps]
    P --> P1[IC Engines]
    P --> P2[Steam Turbines]
    P --> P3[Gas Turbines]
    P1 --> P1a[Otto Cycle Petrol]
    P1 --> P1b[Diesel Cycle]
    P1 --> P1c[Dual Cycle]
    P2 --> P2a[Rankine Cycle Subcritical]
    P2 --> P2b[Supercritical Rankine]
    P3 --> P3a[Open Brayton]
    P3 --> P3b[Closed Brayton]
    P3 --> P3c[Jet Engine]
    R --> R1[Vapour Compression]
    R --> R2[Absorption Refrigeration]
    HP --> HP1[Air Source Heat Pump]
    HP --> HP2[Ground Source Heat Pump]
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** This taxonomy separates thermal devices based on their primary energy conversion direction — either producing mechanical work from heat, or consuming work to manipulate heat.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Real vs. Ideal Thermal Cycle Performance

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IDEAL [Ideal Reversible Cycle]
        I1[Source TH] --> I2[Isothermal Heat Addition]
        I2 --> I3[Isentropic Expansion]
        I3 --> I4[Isothermal Heat Rejection]
        I4 --> I5[Isentropic Compression]
        I5 --> I1
    end
    subgraph REAL [Real Irreversible Cycle]
        R1[Source TH] --> R2[Heat Addition with losses]
        R2 --> R3[Expansion with friction]
        R3 --> R4[Heat Rejection with finite dT]
        R4 --> R5[Compression with friction]
        R5 --> R1
    end
    IDEAL -.ETA Equals 1 minus TL over TH.-> IDEFF[ETA Carnot Maximum]
    REAL -.ETA actual less than ETA Carnot.-> REALEFF[ETA Real Lower]
    REALEFF --> LOSS[Losses due to friction finite dT and pressure drops]
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** Compares the ideal reversible (Carnot) and real irreversible (actual) operating cycles, highlighting that real cycles always underperform due to irreversibilities — a cornerstone concept in 2nd-Law analysis.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Energy Flow in a Typical Thermal Power Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CHEM[Chemical Energy in Fuel] -->|Combustion| HEAT[High Temp Heat Qs]
    HEAT -->|Boiler| STEAM[Steam Generator]
    STEAM -->|High P T| TURB[Turbine]
    TURB -->|Work Wnet| ELEC[Electrical Generator]
    ELEC --> GRID[Power Grid]
    TURB -->|Exhaust Steam| COND[Condenser]
    COND -->|Heat Rejected QR| SINK[Cooling Tower or Environment]
    COND -->|Condensate| PUMP[Pump]
    PUMP -->|Pressurized Liquid| STEAM
    LOSS1[Combustion Losses] --- HEAT
    LOSS2[Condenser Losses] --- COND
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** This block-level architecture traces the energy conversion path from chemical energy in fuel to electrical energy at the grid, while explicitly identifying the points of energy loss — critical for thermal efficiency calculations in Rankine cycle analysis.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define thermal efficiency and coefficient of performance. How are they different from each other?**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> 
> **Thermal Efficiency ($\eta_{th}$):** It is the ratio of net work output to the heat supplied in a power cycle. It is dimensionless and always less than 1.
> 
> $$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_S} = 1 - \frac{Q_R}{Q_S}$$
> 
> **Coefficient of Performance (COP):** It is the ratio of the desired effect (heat removed in refrigeration $Q_L$ or heat delivered in heat pump $Q_H$) to the work input $W_{net}$. Unlike efficiency, COP can be greater than 1.
> 
> $$COP_R = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}}, \quad COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}}$$
> 
> **Key Difference:** $\eta$ applies to *power* cycles (heat → work) and is bounded by 1. COP applies to *refrigeration/heat pump* cycles (work → heat) and has no such upper bound. **(3 Marks)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State and explain the Kelvin-Planck statement of the second law of thermodynamics.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> 
> **Statement:** *"It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no other effect than the extraction of heat from a single thermal reservoir and the performance of an equivalent amount of work."*
> 
> **Explanation:** This statement implies that a heat engine *must* reject some heat to a low-temperature sink — it cannot convert 100% of the heat input from a single source into work. The corollary is that the thermal efficiency of any cyclic heat engine operating between two reservoirs is always less than 1, and bounded above by the Carnot efficiency: $\eta \le 1 - T_L / T_H$. **(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Choice-Based)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Exam (ESE) pattern**, Part B carries 14 marks per question. Students answer ONE full question (with sub-parts) out of a choice of TWO. Each sub-part is typically 7 marks.

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**A. (a)** Define a thermodynamic cycle. Explain with a neat sketch the working of a Carnot heat engine. Derive an expression for its thermal efficiency.

**(b)** A heat engine operates between a source at $800°\text{C}$ and a sink at $30°\text{C}$. If the engine receives $1200\ \text{kJ}$ of heat from the source, calculate: (i) Carnot efficiency, (ii) Work output, (iii) Heat rejected to the sink.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks**
> 
> **Definition (1 Mark):** A thermodynamic cycle is a sequence of processes that returns the working fluid to its initial state. The net change in internal energy is zero, hence $Q_{net} = W_{net}$.
> 
> **Carnot Cycle (3 Marks):** The Carnot cycle consists of four reversible processes:
> 1. **1 → 2:** Isothermal heat addition at $T_H$.
> 2. **2 → 3:** Isentropic (reversible adiabatic) expansion.
> 3. **3 → 4:** Isothermal heat rejection at $T_L$.
> 4. **4 → 1:** Isentropic compression.
> 
> **Derivation (3 Marks):**
> 
> Apply Second Law to a reversible cycle: $\oint \dfrac{dQ}{T} = 0$
> 
> For the two isothermal processes:
> 
> $$\frac{Q_S}{T_H} = \frac{Q_R}{T_L}$$
> 
> Therefore:
> 
> $$\frac{Q_R}{Q_S} = \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$
> 
> Substituting into efficiency definition:
> 
> $$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks**
> 
> **Given:** $T_H = 800 + 273 = 1073\ \text{K}$; $T_L = 30 + 273 = 303\ \text{K}$; $Q_S = 1200\ \text{kJ}$
> 
> **(i) Carnot Efficiency (2 Marks):**
> 
> $$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{303}{1073} = 1 - 0.2824 = 0.7176 = 71.76\%$$
> 
> **(ii) Work Output (2.5 Marks):**
> 
> $$W_{net} = \eta_{Carnot} \times Q_S = 0.7176 \times 1200 = 861.12\ \text{kJ}$$
> 
> **(iii) Heat Rejected (2.5 Marks):**
> 
> $$Q_R = Q_S - W_{net} = 1200 - 861.12 = 338.88\ \text{kJ}$$
> 
> **Verification:** $Q_R / T_L = 338.88 / 303 = 1.118\ \text{kJ/K} = Q_S / T_H = 1200 / 1073$ ✓

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**B. (a)** What is the significance of thermal engineering in modern industry? Discuss its major applications in power generation, transportation, and refrigeration.

**(b)** A domestic refrigerator operates between $-5°\text{C}$ (interior) and $35°\text{C}$ (ambient). If the refrigerator requires $2\ \text{kW}$ of cooling, calculate: (i) Carnot COP, (ii) Minimum power required, (iii) Heat rejected to the room.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks**
> 
> **Significance (2 Marks):** Thermal engineering forms the foundation of energy conversion systems. With growing global energy demand and the push for Net-Zero emissions, optimizing thermal processes is critical for economic and environmental sustainability.
> 
> **Applications (5 Marks):**
> 
> | Sector | Application | Underlying Principle |
> |--------|-------------|---------------------|
> | **Power Generation** | Thermal (coal/nuclear/solar) plants, Combined cycle | Rankine & Brayton cycles |
> | **Power Generation** | Cogeneration plants | Combined heat & power |
> | **Transportation** | Petrol cars, Trucks, Buses | Otto & Diesel cycles |
> | **Transportation** | Aircraft, Rockets | Modified Brayton cycle |
> | **Transportation** | Ships, Locomotives | Diesel & Steam cycles |
> | **Refrigeration** | Domestic fridges, ACs | Vapour compression |
> | **Refrigeration** | Cold storage, LNG | Cascade refrigeration |
> | **HVAC** | Building climate control | Heat pumps, Chillers |

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks**
> 
> **Given:** $T_L = -5 + 273 = 268\ \text{K}$; $T_H = 35 + 273 = 308\ \text{K}$; $Q_L = 2\ \text{kW}$
> 
> **(i) Carnot COP (2 Marks):**
> 
> $$COP_R = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{268}{308 - 268} = \frac{268}{40} = 6.70$$
> 
> **(ii) Minimum Power (2.5 Marks):**
> 
> $$W_{min} = \frac{Q_L}{COP_R} = \frac{2}{6.70} = 0.2985\ \text{kW} \approx 298.5\ \text{W}$$
> 
> **(iii) Heat Rejected to Room (2.5 Marks):**
> 
> $$Q_H = Q_L + W = 2 + 0.2985 = 2.2985\ \text{kW}$$
> 
> **Note:** The room gains ~2.3 kW of heat — this is why leaving a fridge door open *heats* the room rather than cooling it.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Temperature Conversion Errors:** The single most common mistake is using Celsius in the Carnot formula. *Always convert to Kelvin first*. Failure to do so = 1 full mark deduction.
> 2. **Confusing $\eta$ with COP:** Students often write $\eta = T_L / (T_H - T_L)$, which is actually the *COP formula*, not the efficiency. The correct efficiency is $\eta = 1 - T_L / T_H$.
> 3. **Unit Inconsistencies in Energy vs Power:** $Q_S$ in kJ gives $W$ in kJ (energy). $Q_S$ in kW gives $W$ in kW (power). Be consistent — mixing them costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 4. **Sign Convention:** In KTU, $Q$ supplied is *positive*, $Q$ rejected is *positive magnitude* when plugging into $Q_R = Q_S - W$. Don't use negative signs in cycle analysis.
> 5. **COP Confusion with Heat Pump vs. Refrigerator:** $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ (since $Q_H = Q_L + W$). Forgetting this relationship is a frequent loss point.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these high-yield points for the KTU University Exam — they appear in nearly every question paper.**

- ✅ **Thermal Engineering** = study of energy conversion (heat ↔ work) using a working fluid.
- ✅ **Three Pillars:** Thermodynamics, Heat Transfer, Thermal Systems.
- ✅ **Thermal Efficiency:** $\eta_{th} = 1 - Q_R / Q_S$ — always between 0 and 1.
- ✅ **COP (Refrigeration):** $COP_R = Q_L / W$ — can exceed 1.
- ✅ **COP (Heat Pump):** $COP_{HP} = Q_H / W = COP_R + 1$.
- ✅ **Carnot Efficiency:** $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - T_L / T_H$ — **temperatures in Kelvin**!
- ✅ **Carnot COP (Refrigerator):** $COP_{R,Carnot} = T_L / (T_H - T_L)$.
- ✅ **Kelvin-Planck:** No engine can convert heat from a *single* reservoir fully into work.
- ✅ **Clausius:** Heat cannot spontaneously flow from cold to hot body without external work.
- ✅ **First Law (Cycle):** $\sum Q = \sum W$ (no net $\Delta U$).
- ✅ **Second Law (Cycle, Reversible):** $\oint \dfrac{dQ}{T} = 0$.
- ✅ **Power Cycles:** Otto, Diesel, Dual, Rankine, Brayton, Stirling, Ericsson.
- ✅ **Refrigeration Cycles:** Vapour Compression, Vapour Absorption, Air Refrigeration.
- ✅ **Key Constants:** $R = 8.314\ \text{kJ/kmol·K}$, $R_{air} = 0.287\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$, $\gamma_{air} = 1.4$.
- ✅ **Real-world dominance:** ~80% of global primary energy flows through thermal systems.
- ✅ **Sign Convention:** All KTU numericals follow the *thermodynamic* sign convention (heat/work *into* system = positive in some texts, but for cycle analysis, use magnitudes and apply $\eta = 1 - Q_R/Q_S$).


<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Concepts of Thermodynamics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET303 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Concepts of Thermodynamics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Thermodynamics?

**Thermodynamics** is the branch of engineering science that deals with the study of **energy**, its various forms, the transformations of energy from one form to another, and the ability of energy to do **work** or transfer **heat**. It is fundamentally concerned with the macroscopic behavior of matter and the laws governing energy interactions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** "Thermodynamics is the science that deals with the relationships between heat, work, temperature, and the properties of matter, including energy transformations governed by the laws of thermodynamics."

In the context of **PCIET303 – Thermal Engineering**, this foundational module builds the conceptual framework required to analyze engines, turbines, compressors, refrigerators, and all heat-based energy systems.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Bank Account of Energy"

Imagine a student's **bank account**:
- The **money in the account** is like **energy** in a system.
- **Deposits** are like **energy added** (heat input `Q_in`).
- **Withdrawals** are like **energy leaving** (work output `W_out`).
- The **bank rules** that govern how money flows are analogous to the **Laws of Thermodynamics**.
- The **balance** at any instant is the **state** of the system.

Just as a bank tracks money carefully (conservation), thermodynamics tracks energy carefully. You cannot create or destroy energy — you can only convert it. **Thermodynamics is essentially the "accounting science of energy."**

---

## 1.3 Thermodynamic System & Surroundings

The most fundamental concept in thermodynamics is identifying **what we are studying**.

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **System** | A definite quantity of matter or a region in space chosen for study. |
| **Surroundings** | Everything external to the system that can interact with it. |
| **Boundary** | The real or imaginary surface that separates the system from its surroundings. |
| **Universe** | The system plus its surroundings. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Point:** The boundary may be **fixed** (rigid tank) or **moving** (piston-cylinder). It may be **real** (physical wall) or **imaginary** (a defined control volume around a jet engine).

---

## 1.4 Types of Thermodynamic Systems

### (a) Closed System (Control Mass)
- A **fixed amount of mass** is enclosed within the boundary.
- **Energy** (heat and work) can cross the boundary, but **mass cannot**.
- **Example:** A piston-cylinder device, a sealed pressure cooker.

### (b) Open System (Control Volume)
- Both **mass and energy** can cross the boundary.
- A **control volume** is a region in space with boundaries through which mass and energy may flow.
- **Example:** Compressors, turbines, nozzles, heat exchangers, jet engines.

### (c) Isolated System
- Neither **mass nor energy** can cross the boundary.
- It is **completely sealed** from all external influences.
- **Example:** A perfectly insulated rigid container (thermos flask, ideally).

---

## 1.5 Macroscopic vs. Microscopic Approach

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Topic:** Examiners frequently ask the difference between these two approaches.

| Approach | Description | Discipline |
|---|---|---|
| **Macroscopic (Classical) Thermodynamics** | Considers the system as a whole; no consideration of molecular behavior. Uses bulk measurable properties like `P`, `V`, `T`. | Classical Engineering |
| **Microscopic (Statistical) Thermodynamics** | Treats matter as a collection of molecules; uses molecular behavior and statistical averaging. | Statistical Mechanics / Kinetic Theory |

---

## 1.6 Properties of a System

Any characteristic that can be measured or calculated is a **property**. Properties describe the **state** of a system.

### Two Major Categories:
1. **Intensive Properties** — Independent of mass of the system.
   - Examples: **Pressure (`P`)**, **Temperature (`T`)**, **Density (`ρ`)**, **Specific Volume (`v`)**, **Velocity**.
2. **Extensive Properties** — Dependent on the size (mass) of the system.
   - Examples: **Mass (`m`)**, **Volume (`V`)**, **Total Energy (`E`)**, **Entropy (`S`)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Specific Properties** are obtained by dividing extensive properties by mass. They become intensive.
> Examples: **Specific Volume `v = V/m`**, **Specific Energy `e = E/m`**, **Specific Enthalpy `h = H/m`**.

---

## 1.7 State, Process, and Cycle

- **State:** The condition of a system described by its properties. A system is in a definite state when each of its properties has a definite value.
- **Process:** A transformation of a system from one state to another. The **path** is the series of states through which the system passes.
- **Cycle:** A process (or series of processes) in which the system returns to its **initial state**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Concept — State Postulate:** The state of a simple compressible system is completely specified by **two independent intensive properties**.

---

## 1.8 Forms of Energy

Energy exists in numerous forms that may be classified as:

1. **Stored (Static) Energy** — Possessed by a system by virtue of its position, configuration, or composition.
   - Potential Energy (`PE = mgz`)
   - Kinetic Energy (`KE = mv²/2`)
   - Internal Energy (`U`) — energy at molecular level
   - Chemical Energy, Nuclear Energy
2. **Transient (In-Transit) Energy** — Crosses the system boundary.
   - **Heat (`Q`)** — energy transfer due to temperature difference.
   - **Work (`W`)** — energy transfer due to force acting through a displacement.

> [!NOTE]
> Both Heat and Work are **boundary phenomena** — they are recognized only when they cross the boundary, not when they reside within the system.

---

## 1.9 Temperature and the Zeroth Law

**Temperature** is the property that determines the direction of heat flow. Two systems in thermal contact will reach thermal equilibrium when their temperatures are equal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics:** If two systems are each in thermal equilibrium with a third system, they are in thermal equilibrium with each other.
> This law is the basis for **temperature measurement** using thermometers.

---

## 1.10 Standard Physical Constants & Units (KTU 2024)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **SI Unit** of all energy forms is the **Joule (J)**. Heat and work are measured in J, not in K or °C.

| Quantity | SI Unit | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Force | Newton | `N` |
| Pressure | Pascal | `Pa = N/m²` |
| Energy / Work / Heat | Joule | `J = N·m` |
| Power | Watt | `W = J/s` |
| Temperature | Kelvin | `K` |
| Mass | Kilogram | `kg` |
| Universal Gas Constant | 8.314 J/(mol·K) | `R_u` |
| Acceleration due to gravity | 9.81 m/s² | `g` |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 Detailed Analysis of Thermodynamic Concepts

### 2.1.1 Thermodynamic Equilibrium

A system is in **thermodynamic equilibrium** when it satisfies **all** of the following simultaneously:

1. **Mechanical Equilibrium** — No unbalanced forces or pressure gradients.
2. **Thermal Equilibrium** — No temperature differences (no tendency for heat transfer).
3. **Chemical Equilibrium** — No chemical reactions or phase changes occurring.
4. **Phase Equilibrium** — No tendency for mass to transform between phases.

> [!NOTE]
> A truly **equilibrium state** is independent of the **path** taken to reach it — this is the property of being a **point function**.

### 2.1.2 Path vs. Point Functions

- **Point Function (State Function):** Depends only on the initial and final states. Examples: `T`, `P`, `v`, `U`, `H`, `S`.
- **Path Function:** Depends on the path followed between states. Examples: `W` (work), `Q` (heat).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Calculus Form:** For a point function `Z`, the differential `dZ` is **exact**; the cyclic integral `∮ dZ = 0`. For a path function like `δW`, the cyclic integral is **not zero** in general.

### 2.1.3 Quasi-Equilibrium (Quasi-Static) Process

A process is **quasi-static** when the system remains **infinitesimally close to equilibrium** at every instant. The system passes through a continuous sequence of equilibrium states.

- Such processes can be **reversed** by an infinitesimal change — these are **reversible processes**.
- **All reversible processes are quasi-static, but not all quasi-static processes are reversible** (friction, for example, prevents reversibility).

### 2.1.4 Pressure and Pressure Measurement

Absolute pressure is the true thermodynamic pressure.

$$P_{absolute} = P_{gauge} + P_{atmospheric}$$

$$P_{vacuum} = P_{atmospheric} - P_{absolute}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trap:** Always use **absolute pressure (in kPa or Pa)** in thermodynamic equations such as the ideal gas law. Using gauge pressure is a common error that leads to mark loss.

### 2.1.5 Specific Volume and Density

$$v = \frac{V}{m} \quad \left[ \text{m}^3/\text{kg} \right]$$

$$\rho = \frac{m}{V} = \frac{1}{v} \quad \left[ \text{kg}/\text{m}^3 \right]$$

### 2.1.6 The Ideal Gas Equation

For an **ideal gas**, the relationship between pressure, volume, and temperature is given by:

$$P V = m R T \quad \text{or} \quad P v = R T$$

where:
- `P` = absolute pressure in kPa
- `V` = volume in m³
- `m` = mass in kg
- `R` = specific gas constant in kJ/(kg·K)
- `T` = absolute temperature in K

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Universal Gas Constant** `R_u = 8.314 kJ/(kmol·K)` and the **specific gas constant** `R` are related by `R = R_u / M`, where `M` is the molar mass in kg/kmol.

### 2.1.7 Work — Boundary Work for a Closed System

For a **quasi-equilibrium** process in a piston-cylinder assembly:

$$\delta W = P \, dV$$

The total boundary work is:

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$$

For an **isobaric (constant pressure)** process:

$$W = P (V_2 - V_1) = m R (T_2 - T_1)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention (KTU Standard):** **Work done BY the system on the surroundings is positive.** Heat added TO the system is positive. This is the **Engineering Sign Convention**.

### 2.1.8 Heat Transfer

Heat `Q` is the form of energy transferred due to a **temperature difference**. The modes of heat transfer are:
- **Conduction** — Fourier's Law: `q = -k (dT/dx)`
- **Convection** — Newton's Law of Cooling: `q = h (T_s - T_∞)`
- **Radiation** — Stefan-Boltzmann Law: `q = ε σ T⁴`

### 2.1.9 Energy of a System

The **total energy** of a closed system is the sum of:

$$E = U + KE + PE$$

$$E = U + \frac{1}{2} m v^2 + m g z$$

The **change in energy** is:

$$\Delta E = \Delta U + \Delta KE + \Delta PE$$

### 2.1.10 First Law of Thermodynamics (Closed System)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **First Law Statement:** Energy can neither be created nor destroyed; it can only be transformed from one form to another.

For a closed system undergoing a cycle: `∮ δQ = ∮ δW`

For a change of state:

$$Q - W = \Delta U + \Delta KE + \Delta PE$$

For a stationary closed system (no `KE`, `PE` change):

$$Q - W = \Delta U \quad \text{or} \quad Q = \Delta U + W$$

### 2.1.11 Enthalpy (H)

Enthalpy is a composite property defined as:

$$H = U + P V \quad [\text{kJ}]$$

$$\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta (P V)$$

Specific enthalpy:

$$h = u + P v \quad [\text{kJ/kg}]$$

> [!NOTE]
> Enthalpy is particularly useful in **open system analysis** because it combines `U` and **flow work** `PV` into a single property.

### 2.1.12 Specific Heats

- **Specific Heat at Constant Volume** `c_v` (kJ/kg·K): Energy required to raise unit mass by 1 K at constant `V`.
- **Specific Heat at Constant Pressure** `c_p` (kJ/kg·K): Energy required to raise unit mass by 1 K at constant `P`.

For an ideal gas:

$$c_p - c_v = R$$

$$k = \frac{c_p}{c_v} \quad \text{(Specific Heat Ratio)}$$

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **master reference** for Module 1 problems. Memorize all units carefully.

| # | Formula | Description | SI Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `E = U + KE + PE = U + mv²/2 + mgz` | Total energy of a system | J or kJ |
| 2 | `ΔE = Q - W` | First Law (closed system, no KE/PE) | J or kJ |
| 3 | `Q - W = ΔU + ΔKE + ΔPE` | First Law (general closed system) | J or kJ |
| 4 | `W = ∫ P dV` | Boundary work (quasi-equilibrium) | J or kJ |
| 5 | `W = P(V₂ - V₁)` | Boundary work (isobaric process) | J or kJ |
| 6 | `P_abs = P_gauge + P_atm` | Absolute pressure relation | kPa |
| 7 | `v = V/m`, `ρ = 1/v` | Specific volume and density | m³/kg, kg/m³ |
| 8 | `PV = mRT` | Ideal gas law | — |
| 9 | `R = R_u / M` | Specific gas constant | kJ/(kg·K) |
| 10 | `H = U + PV` | Enthalpy definition | kJ |
| 11 | `h = u + Pv` | Specific enthalpy | kJ/kg |
| 12 | `c_p - c_v = R` | Mayer's relation | kJ/(kg·K) |
| 13 | `k = c_p / c_v` | Specific heat ratio | dimensionless |
| 14 | `Q = m c ΔT` | Sensible heat transfer | kJ |
| 15 | `q = h (T_s - T_∞)` | Convective heat flux | W/m² |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Closed System Analysis:** Applied in **piston-cylinder engines, pressure vessels, balloons** — where a fixed mass of gas is studied.
- **Open System Analysis:** Applied in **turbines, compressors, nozzles, pumps, rocket engines, jet propulsion** — mass continuously flows in and out.
- **Enthalpy (H):** A direct property used in **steam tables, refrigeration tables, and compressor/turbine calculations** because flow work is inherently included.
- **Zeroth Law:** Foundation of **all temperature-measuring devices** (thermocouples, RTDs, thermistors).
- **First Law:** Forms the energy balance for every thermal device — **every engineering problem in PCIET303** will begin with a First Law statement.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Boundary Work for a Closed System (Piston-Cylinder)

**Given:** A gas enclosed in a piston-cylinder device. The piston moves quasi-statically under a constant external pressure equivalent to the internal gas pressure `P`.

**To Derive:** Expression for boundary work `W = ∫ P dV`.

**Logic:** The work done by the gas on the piston when the piston moves an infinitesimal distance `dx` is the product of force `F` and displacement `dx`. By substituting pressure and area relations, we arrive at the classical work integral.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Force exerted by gas on the piston:**

The pressure `P` acting on piston area `A` produces a force:

$$F = P \cdot A$$

**Step 2 — Infinitesimal work done when piston moves by `dx`:**

The work done by the gas when the piston moves outward by `dx` is:

$$\delta W = F \cdot dx$$

**Step 3 — Substitute force expression:**

Substituting `F = P·A` into the work expression:

$$\delta W = (P \cdot A) \cdot dx = P \cdot (A \cdot dx)$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the volume change:**

The infinitesimal volume swept by the piston is `dV = A · dx`. Therefore:

$$\delta W = P \, dV$$

**Step 5 — Integrate over the entire process from `V_1` to `V_2`:**

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$$

> [!NOTE]
> This integral equals the **area under the P-V curve** between states 1 and 2. This is a foundational result for Module 2 (Work and Heat) calculations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Work in an Isobaric (Constant Pressure) Process

**Given:** Ideal gas undergoing an isobaric process in a piston-cylinder device.

**To Derive:** Expression for work in terms of temperature change.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Start with the boundary work equation:**

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$$

**Step 2 — Since `P` is constant, take it outside the integral:**

$$W = P \int_{V_1}^{V_2} dV = P (V_2 - V_1)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the ideal gas law to express volumes in terms of temperature:**

From `PV = mRT`, we have `V_1 = mRT_1 / P` and `V_2 = mRT_2 / P`. Substituting:

$$W = P \left( \frac{m R T_2}{P} - \frac{m R T_1}{P} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Simplify by cancelling `P`:**

$$W = m R (T_2 - T_1)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This result is **highly useful** for KTU problems involving isobaric heating/cooling of ideal gases, especially in Otto and Diesel cycle preliminary calculations.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: First Law of Thermodynamics for a Closed System

**Given:** A closed system undergoing a thermodynamic cycle (returns to initial state).

**To Derive:** The cyclic integral relation `∮ δQ = ∮ δW`.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Apply conservation of energy to the system:**

Energy can neither be created nor destroyed. For a closed system:

$$\Delta E_{system} = Q_{in} - W_{out}$$

**Step 2 — Consider a cyclic process:**

In a cycle, the system returns to its initial state, hence `ΔE_system = 0`. Therefore:

$$Q_{net,in} - W_{net,out} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Express the net quantities as cyclic integrals:**

$$Q_{net,in} = \oint \delta Q \quad \text{and} \quad W_{net,out} = \oint \delta W$$

**Step 4 — Final cyclic form:**

$$\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is **Kelvin-Planck's mathematical statement** of the First Law. It states that the net heat transfer in a cycle equals the net work output.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Relation `c_p - c_v = R` (Mayer's Relation)

**Given:** An ideal gas undergoing a reversible process.

**To Derive:** The relationship between specific heats and the gas constant.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define specific enthalpy:**

Enthalpy per unit mass is:

$$h = u + P v$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate both sides with respect to temperature at constant pressure:**

For an ideal gas, both `u` and `h` are functions of temperature alone. Differentiating at constant `P`:

$$\left( \frac{\partial h}{\partial T} \right)_P = \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial T} \right)_V + \left( \frac{\partial (P v)}{\partial T} \right)_P$$

**Step 3 — Identify each term:**

- By definition, `c_p = (\partial h / \partial T)_P`.
- By definition, `c_v = (\partial u / \partial T)_V`.
- For an ideal gas, `Pv = RT`, so the last term equals `R`.

**Step 4 — Final result:**

$$c_p = c_v + R$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is **Mayer's Relation** — a staple result in KTU 2024 module exams. It links the two specific heats to the gas constant and is critical for cycle analysis.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Analysis of Key Concepts

| Concept | System Type | Mass Transfer? | Energy Transfer? | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Closed System** | Control Mass | No | Yes (Q, W) | Piston-cylinder engine, pressure cooker |
| **Open System** | Control Volume | Yes | Yes (Q, W) | Turbine, compressor, nozzle |
| **Isolated System** | None | No | No | Thermos flask (ideal), universe |
| **Adiabatic System** | Any | May or may not | No heat (`Q = 0`) | Insulated turbine, nozzle |
| **Steady-State** | Open | Continuous | In = Out (steady) | Operating turbine |
| **Transient** | Open | Time-dependent | In ≠ Out (storage) | Filling a balloon |

---

## 3.6 Illustrative Example: KTU-Style Numerical

**Problem:** A piston-cylinder device contains 0.5 m³ of air at 100 kPa and 27 °C. The air is compressed isothermally to 0.1 m³. Calculate:
(a) The mass of the air
(b) The boundary work done
(c) The heat transfer

**Given:**
- `V_1 = 0.5 m³`, `V_2 = 0.1 m³`
- `P_1 = 100 kPa`
- `T_1 = 27 + 273 = 300 K`
- `R_{air} = 0.287 kJ/(kg·K)`

### Solution

**(a) Mass of the air:**

From `P V = m R T`:

$$m = \frac{P_1 V_1}{R T_1} = \frac{(100)(0.5)}{(0.287)(300)}$$

$$m = \frac{50}{86.1} = 0.5806 \, \text{kg}$$

**(b) Boundary work (isothermal process):**

For an isothermal process of an ideal gas:

$$W = P_1 V_1 \ln\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

$$W = (100)(0.5) \ln\left(\frac{0.1}{0.5}\right)$$

$$W = 50 \cdot \ln(0.2) = 50 \cdot (-1.6094)$$

$$W = -80.47 \, \text{kJ}$$

**Interpretation:** Negative sign means **work is done ON the gas** (compression).

**(c) Heat transfer:**

For an isothermal process of an ideal gas, `ΔU = 0`. From the First Law:

$$Q = W = -80.47 \, \text{kJ}$$

**Interpretation:** Negative sign means **heat is rejected FROM the gas** to the surroundings to maintain constant temperature.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common mistake is using `°C` directly in the gas law. Always convert to **Kelvin** for thermodynamic calculations. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark consistently for this error.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Classification of Thermodynamic Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Thermodynamic System] --> B[Closed System - Control Mass]
    A --> C[Open System - Control Volume]
    A --> D[Isolated System]

    B --> B1[Energy crosses boundary]
    B --> B2[Mass is fixed]
    B1 --> B1a[Heat Q in or out]
    B1 --> B1b[Work W in or out]
    B2 --> B2a[Example: Piston Cylinder]

    C --> C1[Mass crosses boundary]
    C --> C2[Energy crosses boundary]
    C1 --> C1a[Mass flow in - m dot sub i]
    C1 --> C1b[Mass flow out - m dot sub e]
    C2 --> C2a[Example: Turbine]
    C2 --> C2b[Example: Compressor]
    C2 --> C2c[Example: Nozzle]

    D --> D1[No mass crosses]
    D --> D2[No energy crosses]
    D2 --> D2a[Example: Thermos Flask - Ideal]

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#10b981,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#f59e0b,color:#ffffff
    style B1 fill:#e0f2fe,color:#000000
    style B2 fill:#e0f2fe,color:#000000
    style C1 fill:#d1fae5,color:#000000
    style C2 fill:#d1fae5,color:#000000
    style D1 fill:#fef3c7,color:#000000
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between intensive and extensive properties of a thermodynamic system. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

**Intensive Properties:**
- Properties that are **independent of the mass** (size) of the system.
- They are the **same at every point** within a homogeneous system and can be measured locally.
- **Examples:** Pressure `P`, Temperature `T`, Specific volume `v`, Density `ρ`, Specific enthalpy `h`.

**Extensive Properties:**
- Properties that **depend on the mass (size)** of the system.
- They vary with the amount of substance present.
- **Examples:** Mass `m`, Total Volume `V`, Total Internal Energy `U`, Total Enthalpy `H`, Total Entropy `S`.

> [!NOTE]
> Extensive properties become **intensive** when expressed per unit mass — these are called **specific properties**.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State and explain the Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics. What is its significance?**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** "If two systems are each in thermal equilibrium with a third system, they are in thermal equilibrium with each other."

**Explanation:** Consider three systems A, B, and C. If system A is in thermal equilibrium with system C (same temperature), and system B is in thermal equilibrium with system C, then systems A and B are also in thermal equilibrium with each other. This means `T_A = T_C` and `T_B = T_C`, therefore `T_A = T_B`.

**Significance:**
1. It establishes the **concept of temperature** as a measurable, comparable property.
2. It is the **basis for temperature measurement** using thermometers.
3. It allows the formulation of **temperature scales** (Celsius, Kelvin, Fahrenheit).
4. It is the logical foundation upon which the **First, Second, and Third Laws** are built (hence named "Zeroth" — placed before the First).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

---

### Question A. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a) (7 marks)** Define a thermodynamic system. With neat sketches, explain the three types of thermodynamic systems and give one practical example of each.

**(b) (7 marks)** A rigid tank contains 0.8 kg of nitrogen (R = 0.2968 kJ/kg·K) at 200 kPa and 150 °C. Determine:
1. The volume of the tank.
2. The final pressure if the gas is cooled to 30 °C.
3. The amount of heat transfer during this cooling process.

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam — Alternative Choice]

**(a) (7 marks)** State the First Law of Thermodynamics for a closed system. Derive the boundary work expression `W = ∫ P dV` for a quasi-equilibrium process in a piston-cylinder device.

**(b) (7 marks)** A piston-cylinder device contains 0.005 m³ of water at 400 kPa and 200 °C. Heat is added at constant pressure until the temperature reaches 500 °C. Determine:
1. The mass of water.
2. The boundary work done.
3. The total heat transferred (use water tables / approximate data).

---

## Model Solutions for Question A

### Solution to Q.A (a):

**Definition:** A thermodynamic system is a **defined quantity of matter or a region in space** chosen for study, separated from its surroundings by a real or imaginary boundary.

**Three types:**

**1. Closed System (Control Mass):**
- Mass is **fixed** within the boundary.
- Energy (heat and work) can cross.
- **Sketch:** Piston-cylinder with gas inside, arrows showing `Q_in` and `W_out`.
- **Example:** Pressure cooker, sealed water bottle, piston-cylinder engine.

**2. Open System (Control Volume):**
- Mass and energy **both** cross the boundary.
- A control volume is selected around the region of interest.
- **Sketch:** A turbine with inlet and outlet pipes, arrows showing mass and energy flow.
- **Example:** Turbine, compressor, nozzle, jet engine, heat exchanger.

**3. Isolated System:**
- Neither mass nor energy crosses the boundary.
- **Sketch:** A perfectly insulated rigid container.
- **Example:** An ideal thermos flask, the universe as a whole.

> [!NOTE]
> Properly labeled **neat sketches** with arrows for energy/mass transfer earn **full 7 marks** in KTU valuation.

---

### Solution to Q.A (b):

**Given:**
- Mass of nitrogen, `m = 0.8 kg`
- Initial pressure, `P_1 = 200 kPa`
- Initial temperature, `T_1 = 150 + 273 = 423 K`
- Specific gas constant for N₂, `R = 0.2968 kJ/(kg·K)`
- Final temperature, `T_2 = 30 + 273 = 303 K`

**Part (1): Volume of the tank**

Using ideal gas law `P V = m R T`:

$$V = \frac{m R T_1}{P_1} = \frac{(0.8)(0.2968)(423)}{200}$$

$$V = \frac{100.46}{200} = 0.5023 \, \text{m}^3$$

**Part (2): Final pressure after cooling**

Since the tank is **rigid**, volume remains constant. Using the combined gas law:

$$\frac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2 V_2}{T_2} \Rightarrow P_2 = P_1 \cdot \frac{T_2}{T_1}$$

$$P_2 = 200 \times \frac{303}{423}$$

$$P_2 = 143.26 \, \text{kPa}$$

**Part (3): Heat transfer during cooling**

For a **rigid tank** (constant volume process), the boundary work `W = 0` because `dV = 0`.

The change in internal energy for an ideal gas is:

$$\Delta U = m \, c_v \, (T_2 - T_1)$$

For nitrogen, `c_v ≈ 0.743 kJ/(kg·K)`.

$$\Delta U = (0.8)(0.743)(303 - 423)$$

$$\Delta U = (0.8)(0.743)(-120)$$

$$\Delta U = -71.33 \, \text{kJ}$$

Applying the First Law (`Q = ΔU + W`):

$$Q = -71.33 + 0 = -71.33 \, \text{kJ}$$

**Interpretation:** Heat is **rejected by the gas** (negative sign) to the surroundings as it cools.

---

## Model Solutions for Question B

### Solution to Q.B (a):

**Statement of First Law:** Energy can neither be created nor destroyed; it can only be transformed from one form to another. The net energy transfer to a closed system equals the change in its total energy.

**Mathematical Form:** `Q - W = ΔU + ΔKE + ΔPE`

**Derivation of Boundary Work:**

Consider a gas in a piston-cylinder device. The piston of area `A` is pushed out by an infinitesimal distance `dx` under pressure `P`.

**Step 1:** Force on piston `F = P · A`

**Step 2:** Infinitesimal work `δW = F · dx = P · A · dx`

**Step 3:** Volume swept by piston `dV = A · dx`

**Step 4:** Therefore `δW = P · dV`

**Step 5:** Total work done by gas: `W = ∫_{V_1}^{V_2} P dV`

This integral equals the **area under the P-V diagram** of the process.

---

### Solution to Q.B (b):

**Given:**
- `V = 0.005 m³`, `P = 400 kPa`, `T_1 = 200 °C = 473 K`, `T_2 = 500 °C = 773 K`
- For water (saturated/approximate), `R_{water} = 0.4615 kJ/(kg·K)`

**Part (1): Mass of water**

$$m = \frac{P V}{R T_1} = \frac{(400)(0.005)}{(0.4615)(473)} = \frac{2}{218.29} = 0.00916 \, \text{kg}$$

**Part (2): Boundary work (constant pressure process)**

$$W = P (V_2 - V_1) = m R (T_2 - T_1)$$

$$W = (0.00916)(0.4615)(773 - 473)$$

$$W = (0.00916)(0.4615)(300) = 1.268 \, \text{kJ}$$

**Part (3): Heat transfer**

For a constant pressure process, `Q = m c_p ΔT`. For water vapor, `c_p ≈ 1.872 kJ/(kg·K)`.

$$Q = (0.00916)(1.872)(300) = 5.146 \, \text{kJ}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Temperature conversion error:** Using `°C` instead of `K` in the gas law. Always convert to Kelvin. (**−2 marks**)
> 2. **Pressure conversion error:** Using `bar` or `psi` directly without converting to `kPa` or `Pa`. (**−1 mark**)
> 3. **Sign convention confusion:** Students mix engineering and physics sign conventions. Stick to **engineering convention** (W by system is positive, Q to system is positive) for KTU 2024. (**−1 mark**)
> 4. **Missing units in final answer:** Always write `J`, `kJ`, `m³`, `kg`, `K` explicitly. (**−1 mark**)
> 5. **No diagram:** A neat labeled sketch of a piston-cylinder or control volume **earns** extra credit in KTU valuation.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Basic Concepts of Thermodynamics (Module 1)**

- **System, Surroundings, Boundary:** System is what we study; surroundings are everything else; boundary separates them. Boundary may be fixed or moving, real or imaginary.

- **Three System Types:**
  - **Closed (Control Mass):** Fixed mass, energy can cross. Example: piston-cylinder.
  - **Open (Control Volume):** Mass and energy both cross. Example: turbine.
  - **Isolated:** Neither mass nor energy crosses. Example: ideal thermos.

- **Properties:**
  - **Intensive:** Independent of mass (`P`, `T`, `v`, `ρ`, `h`).
  - **Extensive:** Dependent on mass (`V`, `U`, `H`, `S`, `m`).
  - **Specific:** Extensive per unit mass — becomes intensive.

- **State Postulate:** A simple compressible system is fully specified by **two independent intensive properties**.

- **State vs. Path Functions:**
  - State (Point): `T`, `P`, `v`, `U`, `H`, `S` — `dZ` is exact, `∮ dZ = 0`.
  - Path: `W`, `Q` — depends on process route.

- **Equilibrium Types:** Mechanical, Thermal, Chemical, Phase — all must hold for true equilibrium.

- **Quasi-Equilibrium Process:** Infinitesimally close to equilibrium at every instant; reversible if no friction/unrestored effects.

- **Zeroth Law:** Foundation of temperature measurement. If A = C and B = C, then A = B (in temperature).

- **First Law (Closed System):** `Q − W = ΔU + ΔKE + ΔPE`. For a cycle, `∮ δQ = ∮ δW`.

- **Enthalpy:** `H = U + PV` (kJ); `h = u + Pv` (kJ/kg). Includes **flow work** — vital for open systems.

- **Boundary Work:** `δW = P dV`; total `W = ∫ P dV`. For isobaric: `W = P(V₂ − V₁) = mR(T₂ − T₁)`.

- **Mayer's Relation:** `c_p − c_v = R` (ideal gas); specific heat ratio `k = c_p / c_v`.

- **Ideal Gas Law:** `PV = mRT`; `R = R_u / M`; `R_u = 8.314 kJ/(kmol·K)`.

- **Pressure:** **Always use absolute** in thermodynamic equations. `P_abs = P_gauge + P_atm`.

- **Sign Convention:** Engineering — `W_by` system is **positive**; `Q_to` system is **positive**.

- **Common Unit Conversions to Memorize:**
  - `1 atm = 101.325 kPa`
  - `1 bar = 100 kPa`
  - `1 kJ = 1000 J`
  - `T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15`
  - `1 kgf/cm² = 98.066 kPa`

- **Engineering Applications:** Closed systems (engines, tanks), open systems (turbines, compressors), enthalpy (steam tables, gas flow), Zeroth law (temperature sensors), First law (energy balance in every thermal device).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laws of Thermodynamics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET303 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Laws of Thermodynamics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Introduction to the Laws of Thermodynamics

Thermodynamics is the branch of engineering science that deals with **energy**, its **transformations**, and its capacity to bring about **change** in a system. The entire subject is founded upon four fundamental postulates — known as the **Zeroth, First, Second, and Third Laws** — which together form the bedrock upon which all thermal engineering (engines, refrigerators, turbines, compressors, and even rocket propulsion) is built.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 of PCIET303 specifically expects students to articulate all four laws of thermodynamics with mathematical rigor, apply them to closed/open systems, and use them as the analytical basis for deriving relations in subsequent modules (Power Cycles, IC Engines, Refrigeration).

---

### 1.2 Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics (Thermal Equilibrium)

**Formal Definition (KTU Terminology):**
> *"When two systems are each in thermal equilibrium with a third system, they are in thermal equilibrium with each other."*

This seemingly trivial statement is what allows us to define **temperature** as a measurable, transitive physical property. Without the Zeroth Law, no thermometer could ever give a consistent reading.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
> Imagine three friends — *A*, *B*, and *C* — sharing a quiet room. If *A* feels "neither hot nor cold" and *B* also feels "neither hot nor cold" (relative to the same room), then *A* and *B* will feel the same when they meet each other. The room acts as the **third system (the thermometer)**. This *transitivity* of "hotness" is the entire essence of the Zeroth Law.

**Mathematical Statement:**
$$T_A = T_B \quad \text{and} \quad T_B = T_C \;\Rightarrow\; T_A = T_C$$

---

### 1.3 First Law of Thermodynamics (Conservation of Energy)

**Formal Definition:**
> *"Energy can neither be created nor destroyed; it can only be transformed from one form to another. The total energy of an isolated system is constant."*

For a **closed system (control mass)**, the First Law is written as:

$$\delta Q = dU + \delta W$$

where $\delta Q$ is the infinitesimal heat added, $dU$ is the change in internal energy, and $\delta W$ is the infinitesimal work done **by** the system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> Think of your **bank account**. Money coming in (salary) is like *heat added*; money going out (rent, food) is like *work done*. Your *balance* (internal energy) is the leftover. The First Law simply says: **what goes in must come out or stay as balance** — the money is conserved, only its form changes.

**Sign Convention (KTU Standard):**
- $Q$ **positive** → Heat supplied **to** the system.
- $W$ **positive** → Work done **by** the system (expansion work).
- $U$ increases → Temperature or phase rises.

---

### 1.4 Second Law of Thermodynamics (Direction of Processes)

**Formal Definition:**
> *"It is impossible to construct a device operating in a cycle that produces no other effect than the transfer of heat from a single body at a higher temperature to a body at a lower temperature (Clausius Statement), OR to convert heat completely into work in a cycle (Kelvin–Planck Statement)."*

Mathematically, the Second Law introduces the property **Entropy ($S$)** and states that for any real (irreversible) process, the entropy of the universe increases:

$$dS_{universe} \ge 0$$

For a **reversible** process in a closed system:

$$dS = \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> Drop an egg onto the floor — it splatters. The reverse (a splattered egg re-assembling and jumping back into your hand) **never** happens spontaneously, even though it does not violate energy conservation. The First Law says "energy is balanced." The Second Law says "**spilled milk doesn't un-spill**." It defines the **arrow of time** for all thermal processes.

---

### 1.5 Third Law of Thermodynamics (Absolute Zero)

**Formal Definition:**
> *"The entropy of a perfect crystalline substance approaches zero as the temperature approaches absolute zero (0 K)."*

$$\lim_{T \to 0\,K} S = 0$$

This law has two key corollaries:
1. **Absolute zero is unattainable** in a finite number of steps (Nernst's Heat Theorem).
2. Specific heats $C_p, C_v \to 0$ as $T \to 0$ K.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Third Law matters in KTU:** It allows us to compute **absolute entropy** (not just changes) — essential for chemical equilibria, refrigeration at cryogenic temperatures, and accurate gas tables.

---

### 1.6 Quick Comparison Table

| Law | Core Concept | Key Property Defined | Physical Statement |
|---|---|---|---|
| Zeroth | Thermal Equilibrium | **Temperature ($T$)** | Transitivity of equilibrium |
| First | Energy Conservation | **Internal Energy ($U$)** | $\delta Q = dU + \delta W$ |
| Second | Process Direction | **Entropy ($S$)** | $dS_{universe} \ge 0$ |
| Third | Absolute Zero Limit | **Absolute Entropy** | $S \to 0$ as $T \to 0$ K |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Zeroth Law — Theoretical Depth

The Zeroth Law gives **thermodynamic temperature** its rigorous basis. The formal statement is logically equivalent to the **Ergodic Hypothesis** for systems in equilibrium.

**Three Logical Steps:**
1. Systems $A$ and $B$ are brought into thermal contact via a **diathermic wall** (allows heat flow, no work).
2. After sufficient time, no net heat flow occurs → they are in **thermal equilibrium**.
3. A third system $C$ (the thermometer) is used as a **reference**; equality of empirical readings on $C$ implies $T_A = T_B = T_C$.

**Engineering Utility:** Calibration of all temperature-measuring devices (RTDs, thermocouples, gas thermometers) relies on this law.

---

### 2.2 First Law — Theoretical Depth (Closed & Open Systems)

#### (a) Closed System (Control Mass)

For a finite process from state 1 to state 2:

$$Q_{1\to 2} = (U_2 - U_1) + W_{1\to 2}$$

For a **cyclic process** ($\oint dU = 0$):

$$\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W = W_{net,cycle}$$

#### (b) Open System (Control Volume) — Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)

The most applied form in thermal engineering (nozzles, turbines, compressors, pumps, boilers):

$$\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dot{m}\left[(h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)\right]$$

**Neglecting kinetic energy (KE) and potential energy (PE):**

$$\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1)$$

Where:
- $h = u + Pv$ → **specific enthalpy** (kJ/kg)
- $\dot{W}$ → shaft work (turbine/compressor work)
- $\dot{Q}$ → heat transfer rate

#### (c) Special Cases (KTU Favourites)

| Device | Assumption | Simplified SFEE |
|---|---|---|
| **Nozzle** | $\dot{Q}=0,\ \dot{W}=0$ | $h_1 + \tfrac{V_1^2}{2} = h_2 + \tfrac{V_2^2}{2}$ |
| **Turbine** | $\dot{Q}=0$ | $\dot{W}_T = \dot{m}(h_1 - h_2)$ |
| **Compressor** | $\dot{Q}=0$ | $\dot{W}_C = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1)$ |
| **Boiler/Throttle** | $\dot{W}=0$ | $h_1 = h_2$ (iso-enthalpic) |
| **Condenser/Pump** | as appropriate | derived per problem |

---

### 2.3 Second Law — Theoretical Depth

#### Kelvin-Planck Statement (Heat Engine Side)
> "It is impossible to construct a heat engine that, operating in a cycle, produces no effect other than the extraction of heat from a single reservoir and the performance of an equivalent amount of work."

**Mathematical:** No engine can have $\eta = 100\%$.

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H} \le 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

#### Clausius Statement (Refrigerator Side)
> "It is impossible to construct a refrigerator that, operating in a cycle, transfers heat from a low-temperature body to a high-temperature body without external work input."

**Mathematical:** $\text{COP}_R \le \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$

#### Carnot Theorems (KTU High-Yield)

1. **Carnot Theorem 1:** No heat engine operating between two given reservoirs can be more efficient than a **reversible** (Carnot) engine.
2. **Carnot Theorem 2:** All reversible engines operating between the same two reservoirs have the **same efficiency**, regardless of working fluid.

#### Entropy — The Core Second-Law Quantity

For a **reversible** process: $dS = \dfrac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$

For an **irreversible** process (Clausius Inequality): $\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T} \le 0$

For an **isolated system**: $\Delta S \ge 0$ (always increases or stays constant).

---

### 2.4 Third Law — Theoretical Depth

- At $T = 0$ K, all molecular motion (translation, rotation, vibration) ceases in a perfect crystal.
- Specific heats obey the **Debye $T^3$ law** near absolute zero: $C_v \propto T^3 \to 0$.
- It is impossible to reach **0 K in a finite number of thermodynamic operations** — this is the **Unattainability Principle**.

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Condition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\delta Q = dU + \delta W$ | First Law (Closed System, differential) | kJ |
| 2 | $Q = \Delta U + W$ | First Law (Closed System, finite) | kJ |
| 3 | $\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W$ | First Law (Cyclic Process) | kJ |
| 4 | $\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1)$ | SFEE (neglect KE, PE) | kW |
| 5 | $\dot{W}_T = \dot{m}(h_1 - h_2)$ | Turbine work | kW |
| 6 | $\dot{W}_C = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1)$ | Compressor work | kW |
| 7 | $h_1 + \tfrac{V_1^2}{2} = h_2 + \tfrac{V_2^2}{2}$ | Nozzle energy equation | kJ/kg |
| 8 | $h_1 = h_2$ | Throttling process (isoenthalpic) | kJ/kg |
| 9 | $W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P\, dV$ | Boundary (expansion) work | kJ |
| 10 | $\Delta U = m C_v (T_2 - T_1)$ | Internal energy change, ideal gas | kJ |
| 11 | $\Delta H = m C_p (T_2 - T_1)$ | Enthalpy change, ideal gas | kJ |
| 12 | $\eta_{th,Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Carnot efficiency (reversible) | dimensionless |
| 13 | $\text{COP}_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Refrigerator COP (reversible) | dimensionless |
| 14 | $\text{COP}_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ | Heat Pump COP (reversible) | dimensionless |
| 15 | $dS = \dfrac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$ | Entropy definition (reversible) | kJ/K |
| 16 | $\Delta S = m C_p \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - m R \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | Entropy change, ideal gas | kJ/K |
| 17 | $\Delta S = m C_v \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right) + m R \ln\!\left(\dfrac{v_2}{v_1}\right)$ | Entropy change, ideal gas (alt.) | kJ/K |
| 18 | $\Delta S_{univ} = \Delta S_{sys} + \Delta S_{surr} \ge 0$ | Second Law (isolated) | kJ/K |
| 19 | $\lim_{T \to 0} S = 0$ | Third Law | kJ/K |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility in Real Systems:**
> - **First Law (SFEE)** → design of nozzles, turbines in power plants, rocket engines.
> - **Carnot efficiency** → theoretical upper bound for any heat engine (steam, gas, nuclear).
> - **COP formulae** → sizing of refrigerators and heat pumps.
> - **Third Law** → cryogenic storage (LNG, superconductors, space applications).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Boundary Work for a Polytropic Process

A **polytropic process** follows $PV^n = \text{constant}$, where $n$ is the polytropic index. This single relation generalizes isobaric ($n=0$), isothermal ($n=1$), isentropic ($n=k$), and isochoric ($n=\infty$) processes.

**Starting Point — Definition of boundary work:**

$$W_{1\to 2} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P\, dV$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the polytropic relation:**
From $PV^n = C$, we get $P = C \cdot V^{-n}$. Differentiating both sides:

$$V^n\, dP + P \cdot nV^{n-1}\, dV = 0$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $dV$:**
Rearranging:

$$nPV^{n-1}\, dV = -V^n\, dP$$

$$dV = -\frac{V}{nP}\, dP$$

**Step 3 — Use $PV = mRT$ (ideal gas) to eliminate $V/P$:**

$$\frac{V}{P} = \frac{mRT}{P^2} \;\text{or equivalently}\; \frac{V}{P} = \frac{RT}{P \cdot v} \text{ per kg}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into the integral:**

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P\, dV = \int_{P_1}^{P_2} P \left(-\frac{V}{nP}\right) dP = -\frac{1}{n}\int_{P_1}^{P_2} V\, dP$$

**Step 5 — Express $V$ in terms of $P$ using the polytropic relation:**

$$V = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{1/n} = C^{1/n} P^{-1/n}$$

**Step 6 — Final integration (closed-form result):**

$$\boxed{W_{1\to 2} = \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1 - n} = \frac{mR(T_2 - T_1)}{1 - n}}$$

**Special-Case Verification:**

| Value of $n$ | Process | Formula reduces to |
|---|---|---|
| $n=0$ (Isobaric) | $W = P(V_2 - V_1)$ | $\dfrac{mR(T_2 - T_1)}{1-0} = P\Delta V$ ✓ |
| $n=1$ (Isothermal) | $W = P_1 V_1 \ln(V_2/V_1)$ | (limit using L'Hôpital) ✓ |
| $n=k$ (Isentropic) | $W = \dfrac{mR(T_2 - T_1)}{1 - k}$ | Standard formula ✓ |
| $n=\infty$ (Isochoric) | $W = 0$ | (denominator dominates) ✓ |

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Carnot Efficiency from First and Second Laws

**Step 1 — Consider a reversible (Carnot) cycle operating between a hot reservoir at $T_H$ and a cold reservoir at $T_L$.**

The cycle consists of four reversible processes:
- **1 → 2:** Isothermal expansion at $T_H$ → absorbs $Q_H$ from hot source.
- **2 → 3:** Adiabatic (isentropic) expansion → $T$ drops from $T_H$ to $T_L$.
- **3 → 4:** Isothermal compression at $T_L$ → rejects $Q_L$ to cold sink.
- **4 → 1:** Adiabatic (isentropic) compression → $T$ rises from $T_L$ to $T_H$.

**Step 2 — Apply the First Law to the cycle:**

$$W_{net} = Q_{H,in} - Q_{L,out}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Second Law (Clausius equality) for a reversible cycle:**

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = 0$$

Expanding for the four processes:

$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} + 0 - \frac{Q_L}{T_L} + 0 = 0$$

where the two adiabatic processes contribute zero (since $\delta Q = 0$).

**Step 4 — Solve for the ratio $Q_L / Q_H$:**

$$\frac{Q_L}{T_L} = \frac{Q_H}{T_H}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \frac{T_L}{T_H}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the thermal efficiency definition:**

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = \frac{Q_H - Q_L}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{th,Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}}$$

**Key Observations for KTU Exam:**
- For any **irreversible** engine, $\eta < 1 - T_L/T_H$ (the inequality is strict).
- $T_H, T_L$ are **absolute temperatures** (Kelvin scale).
- $\eta \to 1$ only if $T_L \to 0$ K — unattainable per Third Law.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: SFEE from General First Law (Reynolds Transport Theorem)

**Starting Point:** First Law for a control volume over time $\Delta t$:

$$Q - W = m_2\!\left(u_2 + \tfrac{V_2^2}{2} + gz_2\right) - m_1\!\left(u_1 + \tfrac{V_1^2}{2} + gz_1\right)$$

**Step 1 — For steady flow:** $m_1 = m_2 = m$ (mass in = mass out). Let $\dot{m} = m/\Delta t$.

Divide through by $\Delta t$:

$$\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dot{m}\!\left[(u_2 - u_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)\right]$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $h = u + Pv$:**

$$u_2 - u_1 = (h_2 - P_2 v_2) - (h_1 - P_1 v_1)$$

$$u_2 - u_1 = (h_2 - h_1) - (P_2 v_2 - P_1 v_1)$$

**Step 3 — Use $\dot{m} v = \dot{V}$ (volumetric flow rate) and steady-flow continuity ($\dot{m}_{in} = \dot{m}_{out}$):**

The term $\dot{m}(P_2 v_2 - P_1 v_1)$ represents the **flow work** needed to push fluid in and out. By including it in $h$, it gets absorbed.

**Step 4 — Final SFEE:**

$$\boxed{\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dot{m}\!\left[(h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)\right]}$$

**Step 5 — Special case (no heat, no work, no PE change):** Nozzle equation
$$h_1 + \tfrac{V_1^2}{2} = h_2 + \tfrac{V_2^2}{2}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Entropy Change of an Ideal Gas

**Starting from the First Law (reversible process):**

$$\delta Q_{rev} = dU + P\, dV$$

Divide both sides by $T$:

$$dS = \frac{dU}{T} + \frac{P\, dV}{T}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute $dU = mC_v\, dT$ and $PV = mRT$ → $P/T = mR/V$:**

$$dS = \frac{mC_v\, dT}{T} + \frac{mR\, dV}{V}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from state 1 to state 2:**

$$\Delta S = mC_v \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) + mR \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\Delta S = mC_v \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) + mR \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)}$$

**Step 3 — Alternative form using pressure ratio (using $PV = mRT \Rightarrow V \propto T/P$):**

$$\Delta S = mC_p \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - mR \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The entropy change of an ideal gas is a **state function** — it depends only on initial and final states, NOT on the path. This is a consequence of the First Law combined with reversibility.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Clausius Inequality (Foundation of the Second Law)

**Step 1 — Take a small system in contact with a large reservoir at temperature $T_{res}$.**
An amount of heat $\delta Q$ crosses the boundary. The reservoir's entropy change:

$$dS_{res} = -\frac{\delta Q}{T_{res}}$$

**Step 2 — The system + reservoir is isolated → by the Second Law:**

$$dS_{sys} + dS_{res} \ge 0$$

$$dS_{sys} - \frac{\delta Q}{T_{res}} \ge 0$$

**Step 3 — For a cycle, integrate over the complete cycle:**

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \le 0 \quad \text{(equality only for reversible cycle)}$$

**Step 4 — For a reversible cycle specifically:**

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad dS = \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$$

This single inequality is the **mathematical heart of the Second Law**.

Boiler: $\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}(h_3 - h_2)$. Turbine: $\dot{W}_T = \dot{m}(h_3 - h_4)$. Condenser: $\dot{Q}_{out} = \dot{m}(h_4 - h_1)$. Pump: $\dot{W}_P = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1)$. Overall cycle efficiency: $\eta = \dot{W}_{net}/\dot{Q}_{in}$.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023 (Model Paper 1)]**
**State the Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics. What physical property does it define?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Statement:** *"When two systems are each in thermal equilibrium with a third system, they are in thermal equilibrium with each other."*
>
> **Property defined:** **Temperature (T)**
>
> **Significance:** The Zeroth Law establishes temperature as a fundamental thermodynamic property that is **transitive** — i.e., measurable with a consistent reference. It is the **logical foundation** that allows the construction of thermometers and the assignment of a single temperature value to a system in equilibrium. (1 mark for statement, 1 mark for property, 1 mark for significance.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 (Supplementary)]**
**State the Kelvin-Planck and Clausius statements of the Second Law of Thermodynamics. Are they equivalent?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Kelvin-Planck:** *"It is impossible to construct a device operating in a cycle that produces no effect other than absorbing heat from a single reservoir and converting it completely into work."*
>
> **Clausius:** *"It is impossible to construct a device operating in a cycle whose sole effect is the transfer of heat from a cooler body to a hotter body without external work input."*
>
> **Equivalence:** Yes, the two statements are **logically equivalent** — a violation of one implies a violation of the other.
