[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** *State and explain the Gouy–Stodola theorem.* **Model Answer:** > The Gouy–Stodola theorem states that the **lost work** (or irreversibility) in any process is directly proportional to the total entropy generation of the universe and the absolute temperature of the environment: > > $$W_{lost} = T_0 \cdot S_{gen} = T_0 \cdot \Delta S_{universe}$$ > > This theorem establishes a direct link between the **Second Law** (entropy generation) and the **economic loss** (lost ability to do work). For a reversible process, $S_{gen} = 0$ and hence $W_{lost} = 0$, confirming that maximum work is recovered only in ideal processes. --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **Internal Choice Instruction:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model]* **(a) Derive the expression for the availability of a closed system which exchanges heat only with the environment at temperature $T_0$. [7 Marks]** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Consider a closed system of mass $m$ initially at state 1 and finally at the dead state 0. **Step 2:** The heat transferred in a reversible process is given by: $$Q_{rev} = T_0 (S_0 - S_1)$$ **Step 3:** Apply the First Law of Thermodynamics: $$Q_{rev} = (U_1 - U_0) + W_{max}$$ **Step 4:** Substitute for $Q_{rev}$: $$T_0(S_0 - S_1) = (U_1 - U_0) + W_{max}$$ **Step 5:** Solve for $W_{max}$: $$W_{max} = (U_1 - U_0) - T_0(S_1 - S_0)$$ **Step 6:** Subtract the boundary work against the atmosphere ($P_0(V_0 - V_1)$) to get useful work: $$\phi = (U_1 - U_0) + P_0(V_1 - V_0) - T_0(S_1 - S_0)$$ **(b) A piston-cylinder contains 2 kg of air initially at 500 kPa and 600 K. The environment is at 100 kPa and 300 K. Determine the availability of the air. Assume air behaves as an ideal gas with $c_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K and $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K. [7 Marks]** **Given:** - $m = 2$ kg, $P_1 = 500$ kPa, $T_1 = 600$ K - $P_0 = 100$ kPa, $T_0 = 300$ K - $c_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K **Solution:** For an ideal gas: $$\phi = m\left[c_v(T_1 - T_0) - T_0\left(c_p \ln\frac{T_1}{T_0} - R \ln\frac{P_1}{P_0}\right)\right]$$ **Step 1:** Compute $c_p = c_v + R = 0.718 + 0.287 = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K **Step 2:** Internal energy change: $$\Delta u = c_v(T_1 - T_0) = 0.718 \times (600 - 300) = 215.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 3:** Entropy change: $$\Delta s = c_p \ln\frac{T_1}{T_0} - R \ln\frac{P_1}{P_0}$$ $$\Delta s = 1.005 \ln\frac{600}{300} - 0.287 \ln\frac{500}{100}$$ $$\Delta s = 1.005 \times 0.6931 - 0.287 \times 1.6094$$ $$\Delta s = 0.6966 - 0.4619 = 0.2347 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$ **Step 4:** Availability: $$\phi = 2 \times \left[215.4 - 300 \times 0.2347\right]$$ $$\phi = 2 \times [215.4 - 70.41] = 2 \times 144.99 = 289.98 \text{ kJ}$$ $$\boxed{\phi \approx 290 \text{ kJ}}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]* **(a) Derive the expression for the flow availability of an open system undergoing a steady-flow process. [7 Marks]** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Apply the steady-flow energy equation for a reversible process between state 1 (inlet) and state 2 (outlet/dead state): $$q_{rev} - w_{rev} = (h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)$$ **Step 2:** Since heat is exchanged only with the environment at $T_0$: $$q_{rev} = T_0 (s_2 - s_1)$$ **Step 3:** Solve for $w_{rev}$: $$w_{rev} = (h_1 - h_2) - T_0(s_1 - s_2) + \frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2} + g(z_1 - z_2)$$ **Step 4:** At the dead state: $h_2 = h_0$, $s_2 = s_0$, $V_2 = 0$, $z_2 = 0$. Hence the **flow availability** is: $$\psi = (h - h_0) - T_0(s - s_0) + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz$$ **(b) Steam enters a turbine at 3 MPa, 400°C and expands to 10 kPa. The environment is at 25°C and 100 kPa. Determine the decrease in availability (irreversibility) per kg of steam. [7 Marks]** **Given Data (from Steam Tables):** | State | $h$ (kJ/kg) | $s$ (kJ/kg·K) | |---|---|---| | Inlet (3 MPa, 400°C) | 3230.9 | 6.9212 | | Outlet (10 kPa, $x = 0.9$) | — | — | | Dead State (water at 25°C) | 104.89 | 0.3674 | **Solution:** **Step 1:** Inlet flow availability: $$\psi_1 = (h_1 - h_0) - T_0(s_1 - s_0)$$ $$\psi_1 = (3230.9 - 104.89) - 298.15 \times (6.9212 - 0.3674)$$ $$\psi_1 = 3126.01 - 298.15 \times 6.5538$$ $$\psi_1 = 3126.01 - 1954.04 = 1171.97 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 2:** Outlet enthalpy (10 kPa, $x = 0.9$): $$h_2 = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg} = 191.83 + 0.9 \times 2392.8 = 2345.35 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 3:** Outlet entropy: $$s_2 = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg} = 0.6493 + 0.9 \times 7.5009 = 7.4001 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$ **Step 4:** Outlet flow availability: $$\psi_2 = (2345.35 - 104.89) - 298.15 \times (7.4001 - 0.3674)$$ $$\psi_2 = 2240.46 - 298.15 \times 7.0327$$ $$\psi_2 = 2240.46 - 2096.80 = 143.66 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 5:** Decrease in availability: $$\boxed{\Delta \psi = \psi_1 - \psi_2 = 1171.97 - 143.66 = 1028.31 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Areas:** > 1. **Forgetting the boundary work term** $P_0(V_1 - V_0)$ in the closed-system availability formula → **–2 marks** > 2. **Using $T$ instead of $T_0$** when multiplying entropy change → entire numerical answer becomes wrong → **–3 marks** > 3. **Mixing up "Energy" with "Availability"** in Gouy–Stodola — Energy is conserved; only Availability decreases → **–1 mark** > 4. **Not converting temperature to Kelvin** (e.g., using 25 instead of 298.15 K) → silent numerical error → **–2 marks** > 5. **Forgetting KE and PE terms** in flow availability derivation when asked for total flow availability → **–1 mark** --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [ ] **Availability (Exergy)** = Max useful shaft work extractable from system to dead state. - [ ] **Unavailability (Anergy)** = $T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$ — the "wasted" portion of energy. - [ ] **Energy Identity:** $E = A + B$ — always holds (First Law analog for exergy). - [ ] **Closed System Formula:** $\phi = (U - U_0) + P_0(V - V_0) - T_0(S - S_0)$. - [ ] **Open System (Flow) Formula:** $\psi = (h - h_0) - T_0(s - s_0) + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz$. - [ ] **Dead State** = $T = T_0$, $P = P_0$, $V = 0$, $z = 0$, $A = 0$. - [ ] **Gouy–Stodola Theorem:** $W_{lost} = T_0 \cdot \Delta S_{universe}$. - [ ] **Second-Law Efficiency:** $\eta_{II} = \dfrac{A_{recovered}}{A_{supplied}} \leq 1$. - [ ] **Availability of Heat:** $A_Q = Q\left(1 - \dfrac{T_0}{T_H}\right)$ — depends on the temperature *ratio*. - [ ] **Decrease in Availability = Irreversibility:** Always non-negative for real processes. - [ ] Always use **absolute temperature (Kelvin)** in all exergy calculations. - [ ] For ideal gas: $c_p = c_v + R$, and $\Delta s = c_p \ln(T_1/T_0) - R \ln(P_1/P_0)$.

topics.title: Ideal and Real Gases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIET303 - THERMAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Ideal and Real Gases # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Definition of an Ideal Gas (Perfect Gas) An **Ideal Gas** (also called a **Perfect Gas**) is a theoretical gas whose molecules occupy **negligible space** and have **no intermolecular forces** of attraction or repulsion. It obeys the **Ideal Gas Equation** strictly under all conditions of pressure and temperature. Mathematically, the behavior is governed by the combined gas law: $$PV = mRT$$ Where: - **P** = Absolute pressure of the gas (in **N/m²** or **Pa** or **kPa**) - **V** = Volume occupied by the gas (in **m³**) - **m** = Mass of the gas (in **kg**) - **R** = Characteristic gas constant (in **J/kg·K**) - **T** = Absolute temperature (in **K**) For **n** moles of gas, the equation becomes: $$PV = n\bar{R}T$$ Where: - **n** = Number of moles (in **kmol**) - **R̄** = Universal gas constant = **8.314 kJ/kmol·K** (in SI units) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The relationship between the characteristic gas constant (R) and the universal gas constant (R̄) is given by: > > $$R = \frac{\bar{R}}{M}$$ > > where **M** is the **molar mass (molecular weight)** of the gas in **kg/kmol**. --- ## 1.2 Definition of a Real Gas A **Real Gas** is an actual gas whose molecules **do** occupy finite volume and **do** experience intermolecular forces. Consequently, real gases **deviate** from the ideal gas equation, especially at: - **High pressures** (molecular volume becomes significant) - **Low temperatures** (intermolecular forces become dominant) > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for KTU:** At **low pressures** and **high temperatures**, real gases behave *approximately* like ideal gases because molecular spacing is large and kinetic energy dominates over intermolecular forces. --- ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition ### 🍽️ The "Classroom Analogy" for Ideal vs. Real Gases Imagine a **spacious classroom with very few students**: - The students (gas molecules) can move freely without bumping into each other. - They don't "feel" each other's presence. - The volume occupied by students themselves is **negligible** compared to the empty classroom. - 👉 This represents an **IDEAL GAS** — molecules have plenty of room and negligible interaction. Now imagine a **crowded lift (elevator) during peak hours**: - Students (molecules) are packed tightly. - They **push** against each other (repulsive forces) and the volume of the students themselves matters. - They cannot be compressed infinitely — there is a **limit** to how close they can get. - 👉 This represents a **REAL GAS** at high pressure/low temperature. > [!TIP] > **Memory Trick for KTU Exams:** "**IDEAL = INFINITE SPACE, NO INTERACTION.** The more crowded the molecules get, the more the gas behaves like a **REAL GAS**." --- ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Constant | Symbol | Value | Units | |----------|--------|-------|-------| | Universal Gas Constant | R̄ | **8.314** | kJ/kmol·K | | Universal Gas Constant | R̄ | **8314** | J/kmol·K | | Universal Gas Constant | R̄ | **0.0821** | L·atm/mol·K | | Standard Temperature | T₀ | **273.15 K** | K | | Standard Pressure | P₀ | **101.325 kPa** | kPa | | Standard Pressure | P₀ | **1.01325 bar** | bar | > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistake:** Always use **absolute** temperature (Kelvin) and **absolute** pressure (Pa or kPa), never gauge values, in the ideal gas equation. # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Postulates of the Kinetic Theory of an Ideal Gas The ideal gas model is based on the following fundamental postulates: 1. **Point Mass Assumption:** Gas molecules are considered as **point masses** with negligible volume compared to the total volume of the gas. 2. **No Intermolecular Forces:** There are **no attractive or repulsive forces** between molecules except during elastic collisions. 3. **Random Motion:** Molecules move in **random directions** with all possible velocities. 4. **Elastic Collisions:** Collisions between molecules and with the walls of the container are **perfectly elastic** (no loss of kinetic energy). 5. **Newtonian Mechanics:** Molecules obey **Newton's laws of motion**. 6. **Negligible Gravity:** The effect of gravity on molecular motion is **negligible**. 7. **Large Number:** The number of molecules is **very large**, allowing statistical averaging. --- ## 2.2 Why Real Gases Deviate from Ideal Behavior Real gases deviate from ideal behavior due to **two main reasons**: ### (a) Finite Molecular Volume (Repulsive Forces) At **high pressures**, gas molecules are forced close together. The **actual volume available** for molecular movement is less than the container volume because each molecule occupies a finite space (called **co-volume** or **excluded volume**). ### (b) Intermolecular Attractive Forces At **low temperatures**, molecules move slowly, and **van der Waals forces** (attractive forces) between molecules become significant. These forces tend to **pull molecules together**, reducing the pressure exerted on the container walls below that predicted by the ideal gas law. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** The measured pressure of a real gas is **less** than that predicted by the ideal gas law due to attractive forces, while the **actual volume** is **more** than the ideal volume due to molecular volume. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $PV = mRT$ | Ideal gas equation (mass form) | P[Pa], V[m³], m[kg], R[J/kg·K], T[K] | | 2 | $PV = n\bar{R}T$ | Ideal gas equation (molar form) | n[kmol], R̄[kJ/kmol·K] | | 3 | $R = \bar{R}/M$ | Characteristic gas constant | M[kg/kmol] | | 4 | $Z = \dfrac{PV}{mRT} = \dfrac{PV}{n\bar{R}T}$ | **Compressibility factor** | Dimensionless | | 5 | $Z = 1$ | Ideal gas condition | Dimensionless | | 6 | $\left(P + \dfrac{a}{V^2}\right)(V - b) = RT$ | **Van der Waals equation** (per mole) | a, b are gas-specific constants | | 7 | $\left(P + \dfrac{an^2}{V^2}\right)(V - nb) = n\bar{R}T$ | Van der Waals equation (n moles) | V is total volume | | 8 | $P_r = P/P_c$ | Reduced pressure | Dimensionless | | 9 | $T_r = T/T_c$ | Reduced temperature | Dimensionless | | 10 | $v_r = v/v_c$ | Reduced specific volume | Dimensionless | | 11 | $Z_c = \dfrac{P_c V_c}{R T_c}$ | Critical compressibility factor | Dimensionless | | 12 | $T_B = \dfrac{a}{Rb}$ | **Boyle Temperature** | K | | 13 | $V_c = 3b$ (per mole) | Critical volume from vdW | m³ | | 14 | $P_c = \dfrac{a}{27b^2}$ (per mole) | Critical pressure from vdW | Pa | | 15 | $T_c = \dfrac{8a}{27Rb}$ (per mole) | Critical temperature from vdW | K | | 16 | $R_{mix} = \dfrac{\sum m_i R_i}{\sum m_i}$ | Gas constant for mixture | J/kg·K | | 17 | $M_{mix} = \dfrac{\sum m_i}{\sum (m_i/M_i)} = \dfrac{\sum n_i M_i}{\sum n_i}$ | Molecular weight of mixture | kg/kmol | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Tip:** Always remember that $Z = 1$ for an **ideal gas**, $Z > 1$ for gases where **repulsive forces dominate** (high P), and $Z < 1$ where **attractive forces dominate** (low P, moderate T). --- ## 2.4 The Compressibility Factor (Z) — Detailed Analysis The **compressibility factor Z** quantifies the deviation of a real gas from ideal behavior: $$Z = \frac{PV}{mRT}$$ ### Physical Interpretation of Z: | Z Value | Physical Meaning | |---------|------------------| | $Z = 1$ | Ideal gas behavior (PV = mRT) | | $Z < 1$ | Attractive forces dominate; gas is more compressible than ideal | | $Z > 1$ | Repulsive forces dominate; gas is less compressible than ideal | ### Reduced Properties & Principle of Corresponding States The **Principle of Corresponding States** states that **all gases at the same reduced temperature (T_r) and reduced pressure (P_r)** have approximately the **same compressibility factor Z**. This means Z is a universal function: $$Z = f(T_r, P_r)$$ This principle is extremely useful for **KTU problems** where tabulated Z values can be used from generalized compressibility charts. ### Boyle Temperature (T_B) The **Boyle temperature** is the temperature at which a real gas behaves like an **ideal gas over a wide range of pressures** (i.e., $Z \approx 1$ for $P \to 0$). $$T_B = \frac{a}{Rb}$$ At temperatures **above T_B**, the gas shows $Z > 1$ behavior, and below T_B, it shows $Z < 1$ behavior over moderate pressures. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications | Application | Use of Ideal/Real Gas Concept | |-------------|-------------------------------| | **Air Compressors** | Real gas behavior affects work input calculation | | **Internal Combustion Engines** | Combustion gases at high P, T behave as real gases | | **Refrigeration Cycles** | Refrigerants deviate from ideal gas law near saturation | | **Gas Turbines** | Combustion products at high temperatures | | **LNG/CNG Storage** | Cryogenic conditions require real gas equations | | **Steam Power Plants** | Steam deviates significantly from ideal behavior | # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Boyle's Law from Kinetic Theory ### Statement: At **constant temperature**, the pressure of a gas is **inversely proportional** to its volume. $$P \propto \frac{1}{V} \quad \text{(at constant T)}$$ ### Proof from Kinetic Theory: The pressure exerted by a gas on the container walls is given by: $$P = \frac{1}{3} \rho \bar{c^2}$$ Where: - **ρ** = mass density of the gas - **c̄²** = mean square velocity of molecules The density can be written as: $$\rho = \frac{m}{V}$$ Substituting: $$P = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{m}{V} \cdot \bar{c^2}$$ Rearranging for a fixed mass at constant temperature: $$PV = \frac{1}{3} m \bar{c^2} = \text{constant}$$ Therefore: $$P \propto \frac{1}{V}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Charle's Law from Kinetic Theory ### Statement: At **constant pressure**, the volume of a gas is **directly proportional** to its absolute temperature. $$V \propto T \quad \text{(at constant P)}$$ ### Proof: The mean square velocity of gas molecules is directly proportional to absolute temperature: $$\bar{c^2} \propto T$$ From the kinetic theory expression: $$PV = \frac{1}{3} m \bar{c^2}$$ At constant pressure: $$V = \frac{m \bar{c^2}}{3P}$$ Since $m$ and $P$ are constant: $$V \propto \bar{c^2} \propto T$$ Therefore: $$\frac{V_1}{T_1} = \frac{V_2}{T_2}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: The Universal Gas Constant (R̄) Value The universal gas constant is found by applying the ideal gas equation at **STP (Standard Temperature and Pressure)**: At STP: $P_0 = 101.325$ kPa, $T_0 = 273.15$ K, and for 1 kmol of gas, $V_0 = 22.414$ m³. Starting from: $$P_0 V_0 = n \bar{R} T_0$$ Solving for R̄: $$\bar{R} = \frac{P_0 V_0}{n T_0}$$ Substituting the values (with n = 1 kmol): $$\bar{R} = \frac{101.325 \times 22.414}{1 \times 273.15}$$ Performing the calculation: $$\bar{R} = \frac{2271.07}{273.15}$$ $$\bar{R} = 8.314 \text{ kJ/kmol·K}$$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Van der Waals Equation — Physical Reasoning ### Step 1: Correcting the Pressure Term In an ideal gas, no intermolecular forces exist. In reality, molecules near the center of the container are pulled equally in all directions, but molecules near the wall experience a **net inward pull** from other molecules. This reduces the pressure exerted on the wall. The **effective pressure** is: $$P_{effective} = P_{ideal} - P_{correction}$$ The correction term is **proportional to the square of density** (i.e., inversely proportional to $V^2$ per mole): $$P_{correction} = \frac{a}{V^2}$$ Where **a** is the **van der Waals constant** related to intermolecular attraction. Therefore: $$P_{ideal} = P + \frac{a}{V^2}$$ ### Step 2: Correcting the Volume Term The actual volume available for molecular movement is **less** than the container volume because each molecule occupies a finite volume called the **co-volume** (b per mole). The **excluded volume** for N molecules is **4 times** the actual molecular volume. For n moles: $$V_{available} = V - nb$$ Where **b** is the **van der Waals constant** related to molecular size (per mole: $V - b$). ### Step 3: Combining Both Corrections Substituting the corrected pressure and volume into the ideal gas equation: $$\left(P + \frac{a}{V^2}\right)(V - b) = RT$$ This is the **Van der Waals equation of state for 1 mole of a real gas**. For **n moles**: $$\left(P + \frac{an^2}{V^2}\right)(V - nb) = n\bar{R}T$$ --- ## 3.5 Derivation 5: Critical Constants from Van der Waals Equation At the **critical point**, the isotherm on a P-V diagram has a **point of inflection** with a **horizontal tangent**. Mathematically: $$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial V}\right)_T = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \left(\frac{\partial^2 P}{\partial V^2}\right)_T = 0$$ ### Step 1: Expand the Van der Waals Equation Expanding $(P + a/V^2)(V - b) = RT$: $$PV - Pb + \frac{a}{V} - \frac{ab}{V^2} = RT$$ Solving for P: $$P = \frac{RT}{V - b} - \frac{a}{V^2}$$ ### Step 2: Apply the First Derivative Condition Taking $\partial P / \partial V = 0$: $$-\frac{RT_c}{(V_c - b)^2} + \frac{2a}{V_c^3} = 0$$ $$\frac{RT_c}{(V_c - b)^2} = \frac{2a}{V_c^3}$$ ### Step 3: Apply the Second Derivative Condition Taking $\partial^2 P / \partial V^2 = 0$: $$\frac{2RT_c}{(V_c - b)^3} - \frac{6a}{V_c^4} = 0$$ $$\frac{2RT_c}{(V_c - b)^3} = \frac{6a}{V_c^4}$$ ### Step 4: Solve the System Dividing the second equation by the first: $$\frac{2RT_c / (V_c - b)^3}{RT_c / (V_c - b)^2} = \frac{6a / V_c^4}{2a / V_c^3}$$ $$\frac{2}{V_c - b} = \frac{3}{V_c}$$ $$2V_c = 3(V_c - b)$$ $$2V_c = 3V_c - 3b$$ $$V_c = 3b$$ ### Step 5: Find T_c Substituting $V_c = 3b$ back into the first derivative equation: $$RT_c = \frac{2a(3b - b)^2}{(3b)^3} = \frac{2a \cdot 4b^2}{27b^3} = \frac{8a}{27b}$$ $$T_c = \frac{8a}{27Rb}$$ ### Step 6: Find P_c Substituting $V_c = 3b$ and $T_c$ into the Van der Waals equation: $$P_c = \frac{RT_c}{V_c - b} - \frac{a}{V_c^2}$$ $$P_c = \frac{R \cdot \frac{8a}{27Rb}}{3b - b} - \frac{a}{(3b)^2}$$ $$P_c = \frac{8a}{27b \cdot 2b} - \frac{a}{9b^2}$$ $$P_c = \frac{8a}{54b^2} - \frac{a}{9b^2}$$ $$P_c = \frac{4a}{27b^2} - \frac{3a}{27b^2} = \frac{a}{27b^2}$$ ### Summary of Critical Constants (per mole): $$\boxed{V_c = 3b, \quad P_c = \frac{a}{27b^2}, \quad T_c = \frac{8a}{27Rb}}$$ ### Critical Compressibility Factor: $$Z_c = \frac{P_c V_c}{R T_c} = \frac{\frac{a}{27b^2} \cdot 3b}{R \cdot \frac{8a}{27Rb}} = \frac{3a/(27b)}{8a/(27b)} = \frac{3}{8} = 0.375$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Note:** The critical compressibility factor $Z_c = 0.375$ is a **universal value** for all van der Waals gases. For real gases, $Z_c$ ranges from 0.27 to 0.29, showing that the van der Waals equation is approximate. --- ## 3.6 Worked Numerical Examples ### Example 1: Characteristic Gas Constant of Nitrogen **Given:** $M_{N_2} = 28$ kg/kmol, $\bar{R} = 8.314$ kJ/kmol·K **Find:** Characteristic gas constant R for N₂ **Solution:** $$R = \frac{\bar{R}}{M} = \frac{8.314}{28} = 0.2969 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$ ### Example 2: Compressibility Factor Calculation **Given:** A gas at $P = 10$ bar, $T = 350$ K, occupies $V = 0.25$ m³ for $m = 1$ kg. $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K. **Find:** Compressibility factor Z **Solution:** $$Z = \frac{PV}{mRT} = \frac{10 \times 10^2 \times 0.25}{1 \times 287 \times 350}$$ $$Z = \frac{2500}{100450} = 0.02488$$ > [!WARNING] > **Unit Check:** P must be in **kPa** (10 bar = 1000 kPa), R in **J/kg·K** or be consistent. Here R = 287 J/kg·K, P = 1000 kPa = 10⁶ Pa, V = 0.25 m³, so PV = 250000 J. mRT = 1 × 287 × 350 = 100450 J. Z = 250000/100450 ≈ 2.489 (this is the correct value when R = 287 J/kg·K). # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Classification of Gases ```mermaid graph TD A["GASES"]:::root --> B["IDEAL GAS"] A --> C["REAL GAS"] B --> D["Point Mass Molecules"] B --> E["No Intermolecular Forces"] B --> F["Obeys PV = mRT"] C --> G["Finite Molecular Volume"] C --> H["Intermolecular Forces Exist"] C --> I["Deviates from PV = mRT"] I --> J["Compressibility Factor Z ≠ 1"] C --> K["Van der Waals Gas Model"] K --> L["Pressure Correction: a/V²"] K --> M["Volume Correction: b"] I --> N["Boyle Temperature T_B"] N --> O["Below T_B: Z < 1"] N --> P["Above T_B: Z > 1"] A --> Q["MIXTURE OF GASES"] Q --> R["Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures"] Q --> S["Amagat's Law of Partial Volumes"] classDef root fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` --- ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Real Gas Analysis Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD INPUT["INPUT: Real Gas System
P, T, V, n, gas type"]:::in INPUT --> CALC_R["Calculate R = R̄/M"]:::proc CALC_R --> COMPARE["Compare Conditions
P vs Pc, T vs Tc"]:::proc COMPARE --> DECIDE{"Conditions
near critical?"}:::decision DECIDE -->|No| IDEAL["Use Ideal Gas Equation
PV = mRT"]:::ideal DECIDE -->|Yes| REAL["Use Real Gas Method"]:::real REAL --> METHOD1["Method 1: Van der Waals
P + a/V² V - b = RT"]:::m1 REAL --> METHOD2["Method 2: Compressibility
Z = PV/mRT"]:::m2 REAL --> METHOD3["Method 3: Corresponding States
Z = f Pr, Tr"]:::m3 IDEAL --> OUT1["OUTPUT: V, m, or T"]:::out METHOD1 --> OUT2["OUTPUT: V by iteration"]:::out METHOD2 --> OUT3["OUTPUT: Z from chart"]:::out METHOD3 --> OUT4["OUTPUT: Z from generalized chart"]:::out classDef in fill:#2d5016,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef proc fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef decision fill:#8b4513,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef ideal fill:#4a4a4a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef real fill:#5c1a1a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef m1 fill:#1a4a5c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef m2 fill:#5c1a4a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef m3 fill:#4a5c1a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef out fill:#501650,stroke:#fff,color:#fff ``` --- ## 4.3 P-V Diagram Schematic (Behavior Comparison) ```mermaid graph LR A["IDEAL GAS
Isotherm
PV = constant
Hyperbola"]:::ideal B["REAL GAS
Above Tc
Smooth curve
No oscillation"]:::real1 C["REAL GAS
At Tc
Point of inflection
Horizontal tangent"]:::critical D["REAL GAS
Below Tc
Oscillating curve
Saturated region"]:::real2 A --- E["Pressure Axis P"]:::axis A --- F["Volume Axis V"]:::axis B --- E C --- E D --- E classDef ideal fill:#2d5016,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef real1 fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef critical fill:#8b4513,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef real2 fill:#5c1a1a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef axis fill:#333,stroke:#fff,color:#fff ``` --- ## 4.4 Concept Map: Compressibility Factor Z ```mermaid mindmap root((Compressibility
Factor Z)) Definition "Z = PV / mRT" "Z = 1 means Ideal" Cases Z greater than 1 "Repulsive forces dominate" "High pressure region" "Gas less compressible" Z less than 1 "Attractive forces dominate" "Low-moderate pressure" "Gas more compressible" Z equals 1 "Ideal gas behavior" "Low P or high T" "At Boyle temperature" Applications Real gas calculations Generalized charts Principle of Corresponding States Engineering design ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## PART A: SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Define an ideal gas. State the characteristics of an ideal gas.** **Model Answer:** An **ideal gas** (or perfect gas) is a theoretical gas that strictly obeys the ideal gas equation **PV = mRT** under all conditions of pressure and temperature. The molecules of an ideal gas are considered as point masses with negligible volume, and there are no intermolecular forces of attraction or repulsion between them. **Characteristics of an ideal gas:** 1. The volume of the molecules is negligible compared to the total volume of the gas. 2. There are no intermolecular forces (attractive or repulsive) between molecules. 3. Collisions between molecules and with the container walls are perfectly elastic. 4. The gas obeys Boyle's law, Charle's law, and Avogadro's law under all conditions. 5. The specific heats (Cp and Cv) are constant (independent of temperature). 6. The internal energy depends only on temperature, not on pressure or volume. --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **What is the compressibility factor? Explain its physical significance.** **Model Answer:** The **compressibility factor (Z)** is defined as the ratio of the actual volume of a gas to the volume that an ideal gas would occupy under the same conditions of pressure and temperature. $$Z = \frac{PV}{mRT} = \frac{V_{actual}}{V_{ideal}}$$ **Physical Significance:** - **Z = 1:** The gas behaves as an ideal gas (no deviation). - **Z < 1:** The gas is more compressible than an ideal gas. This occurs at low to moderate pressures where **attractive forces** between molecules dominate. The molecules are pulled closer together, reducing the actual volume. - **Z > 1:** The gas is less compressible than an ideal gas. This occurs at high pressures where **repulsive forces** between molecules dominate, and the finite molecular volume becomes significant. The compressibility factor is a direct measure of the **deviation of a real gas from ideal behavior** and is widely used in thermodynamic calculations involving real gases. --- ## PART B: LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice ### Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme] #### (a) [7 Marks] Derive the Van der Waals equation of state for a real gas. Explain the physical significance of the constants 'a' and 'b'. **Model Answer:** **Introduction:** The ideal gas equation PV = RT fails for real gases at high pressures and low temperatures because it does not account for (i) the **finite volume of molecules** and (ii) the **intermolecular forces of attraction**. **Step 1: Volume Correction (Excluded Volume)** In an ideal gas, the entire volume V is available for molecular movement. In a real gas, each molecule occupies a finite space. For a single molecule, the effective volume excluded to other molecules is four times its actual volume (a sphere of influence). For n molecules, the total excluded volume is proportional to the number of molecules. For **1 mole** of gas, the **available volume** is: $$V_{available} = V - b$$ Where **b** is the **co-volume constant** (per mole), representing four times the actual volume of one mole of molecules. **Step 2: Pressure Correction (Intermolecular Attraction)** In a real gas, a molecule in the bulk of the gas is surrounded by other molecules and experiences **equal attractive forces** in all directions (net force = 0). However, a molecule near the wall of the container experiences a **net inward pull** from the molecules behind it, which **reduces the momentum** with which it strikes the wall. The reduction in pressure is proportional to: - The number of molecules per unit volume near the wall $\propto 1/V$ - The number of molecules pulling the surface molecule $\propto 1/V$ Therefore: $$P_{reduction} \propto \frac{1}{V^2} = \frac{a}{V^2}$$ The **actual pressure** P is less than the ideal pressure, so the corrected pressure is: $$P_{ideal} = P + \frac{a}{V^2}$$ Where **a** is the **van der Waals constant** representing the magnitude of intermolecular attractive forces. **Step 3: Final Van der Waals Equation** Substituting corrected pressure and volume into PV = RT: $$\left(P + \frac{a}{V^2}\right)(V - b) = RT$$ For **n moles** of gas: $$\left(P + \frac{an^2}{V^2}\right)(V - nb) = n\bar{R}T$$ **Physical Significance of Constants:** - **Constant 'a':** Represents the measure of **intermolecular attractive forces** between gas molecules. A higher value of 'a' indicates stronger attractive forces (e.g., easily liquefiable gases like NH₃, H₂O have high 'a'). - **Constant 'b':** Represents the measure of the **finite size (excluded volume)** of gas molecules. It is four times the actual volume of one mole of molecules. A higher 'b' indicates larger molecular size. #### (b) [7 Marks] State and explain the principle of corresponding states. How is the compressibility factor expressed as a function of reduced properties? **Model Answer:** **Statement of the Principle of Corresponding States:** The **Principle of Corresponding States** states that all real gases, when expressed in terms of their **reduced properties** (reduced pressure, reduced temperature, and reduced volume), exhibit approximately the same deviation from ideal behavior. **Definition of Reduced Properties:** $$P_r = \frac{P}{P_c}, \quad T_r = \frac{T}{T_c}, \quad v_r = \frac{v}{v_c}$$ Where $P_c$, $T_c$, and $v_c$ are the **critical pressure**, **critical temperature**, and **critical specific volume** respectively. **Explanation:** This principle implies that the **compressibility factor Z** is a universal function of the reduced pressure and reduced temperature: $$Z = f(P_r, T_r)$$ This is a powerful tool because it allows the use of a **single generalized compressibility chart** for all gases, rather than requiring individual charts for each gas. The principle holds with good accuracy for most gases except those that are highly polar or hydrogen-bonded. **Mathematical Expression:** Starting from the Van der Waals equation and expressing it in terms of reduced properties, the equation becomes **universal** (independent of the gas constants a and b): $$P_r = \frac{8T_r}{3v_r - 1} - \frac{3}{v_r^2}$$ **Engineering Utility:** - Predicting P-V-T behavior of any real gas using generalized charts. - Calculation of thermodynamic properties in cycles involving real gases. - Estimation of critical constants from limited experimental data. --- ### Question B: [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Alternative Choice] #### (a) [7 Marks] Define Boyle temperature. Show that for a Van der Waals gas, $T_B = a/(Rb)$. **Model Answer:** **Definition of Boyle Temperature:** The **Boyle temperature (T_B)** is the temperature at which a real gas behaves like an **ideal gas over a wide range of pressures**. At this temperature, the compressibility factor Z approaches unity as the pressure approaches zero, and the gas obeys Boyle's law (PV = constant) at low pressures. Mathematically, Boyle temperature is defined as the temperature at which: $$\left(\frac{\partial Z}{\partial P}\right)_{T=T_B, P \to 0} = 0$$ **Derivation for a Van der Waals Gas:** Starting from the Van der Waals equation (per mole): $$\left(P + \frac{a}{V^2}\right)(V - b) = RT$$ Expanding: $$PV - Pb + \frac{a}{V} - \frac{ab}{V^2} = RT$$ Rearranging for the compressibility factor: $$Z = \frac{PV}{RT} = 1 + \frac{P}{RT}\left(b - \frac{a}{RT}\right) + \text{higher order terms}$$ For low pressures, the term $ab/V^2$ is small. The linear term in P gives: $$Z = 1 + \frac{P}{RT}\left(b - \frac{a}{RT}\right)$$ **Applying Boyle's Condition:** At the Boyle temperature, the gas behaves ideally for small P, meaning Z = 1 as P → 0. This requires the coefficient of P to vanish: $$b - \frac{a}{RT_B} = 0$$ Solving for $T_B$: $$b = \frac{a}{RT_B}$$ $$T_B = \frac{a}{Rb}$$ **Significance:** - **Above T_B:** The repulsive term dominates at low pressures, and $Z > 1$. - **Below T_B:** The attractive term dominates at low pressures, and $Z < 1$. - **At T_B:** Z = 1 for all low-pressure conditions, making the gas ideal-like. #### (b) [7 Marks] A gas mixture contains 30% CO₂, 50% N₂, and 20% O₂ by mass. Calculate: (i) the molecular weight of the mixture, (ii) the characteristic gas constant of the mixture, and (iii) the gas constant per kmol. Take R̄ = 8.314 kJ/kmol·K. **Model Answer:** **Given:** - Mass fractions: $m_{CO_2} = 0.30$, $m_{N_2} = 0.50$, $m_{O_2} = 0.20$ - Molecular weights: $M_{CO_2} = 44$ kg/kmol, $M_{N_2} = 28$ kg/kmol, $M_{O_2} = 32$ kg/kmol - $\bar{R} = 8.314$ kJ/kmol·K **Step 1: Calculate Molecular Weight of the Mixture** For a mixture based on mass fractions, the molecular weight is: $$M_{mix} = \frac{1}{\sum \left(\frac{x_i}{M_i}\right)}$$ Where $x_i$ is the mass fraction. Computing each term: $$\frac{x_{CO_2}}{M_{CO_2}} = \frac{0.30}{44} = 0.006818 \text{ kmol/kg}$$ $$\frac{x_{N_2}}{M_{N_2}} = \frac{0.50}{28} = 0.017857 \text{ kmol/kg}$$ $$\frac{x_{O_2}}{M_{O_2}} = \frac{0.20}{32} = 0.006250 \text{ kmol/kg}$$ Summing: $$\sum \frac{x_i}{M_i} = 0.006818 + 0.017857 + 0.006250 = 0.030925 \text{ kmol/kg}$$ Therefore: $$M_{mix} = \frac{1}{0.030925} = 32.34 \text{ kg/kmol}$$ **Step 2: Calculate the Characteristic Gas Constant of the Mixture** $$R_{mix} = \frac{\bar{R}}{M_{mix}} = \frac{8.314}{32.34}$$ $$R_{mix} = 0.2570 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$ **Step 3: Gas Constant per kmol (Universal Gas Constant)** By definition, the universal gas constant is **independent of the gas type**: $$\bar{R} = 8.314 \text{ kJ/kmol·K}$$ **Verification:** Using the mass-fraction-based formula for R: $$R_{mix} = \sum x_i R_i = 0.30 \times \frac{8.314}{44} + 0.50 \times \frac{8.314}{28} + 0.20 \times \frac{8.314}{32}$$ $$R_{mix} = 0.30 \times 0.1890 + 0.50 \times 0.2969 + 0.20 \times 0.2598$$ $$R_{mix} = 0.0567 + 0.1485 + 0.0520 = 0.2572 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$ This matches our earlier result (slight rounding difference). ✓ --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing SI and CGS units in the same equation (e.g., using pressure in bar with R in kJ/kg·K). Always convert P to **kPa** when R is in **kJ/kg·K**. > > 2. **Temperature Error:** Forgetting to convert °C to Kelvin. The ideal gas equation requires **absolute temperature (K)**. Common error: using T = 27°C directly instead of T = 300 K. > > 3. **Confusion between Molar and Mass Forms:** Writing $PV = mRT$ with n (moles) instead of m (mass). Remember: $PV = mRT$ uses **mass m**, while $PV = n\bar{R}T$ uses **moles n**. > > 4. **Van der Waals Derivation Mistakes:** Forgetting the **square** in the pressure correction term ($a/V^2$, not $a/V$). Also, for n moles, the pressure correction becomes $an^2/V^2$ (NOT $an/V^2$). > > 5. **Reduced Properties Calculation:** Dividing by critical values in the wrong order. Always: $P_r = P/P_c$, $T_r = T/T_c$. > > 6. **Boyle Temperature Sign:** Confusing the conditions T > T_B and T < T_B with respect to whether Z > 1 or Z < 1. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER ### 🔑 Quick Revision Checklist: - **Ideal Gas Equation:** $PV = mRT = n\bar{R}T$ (always use absolute P and T) - **Universal Gas Constant:** $\bar{R} = 8.314$ kJ/kmol·K = 8314 J/kmol·K - **Characteristic Gas Constant:** $R = \bar{R}/M$, units: J/kg·K - **Compressibility Factor:** $Z = PV/(mRT) = 1$ for ideal gas - **Van der Waals Equation (per mole):** $\left(P + a/V^2\right)(V - b) = RT$ - **Van der Waals Equation (n moles):** $\left(P + an^2/V^2\right)(V - nb) = n\bar{R}T$ - **Constant 'a':** Measures **attractive forces** (higher for polar molecules) - **Constant 'b':** Measures **molecular size** (excluded volume) - **Critical Constants (vdW):** $V_c = 3b$, $P_c = a/(27b^2)$, $T_c = 8a/(27Rb)$ - **Critical Compressibility Factor:** $Z_c = 3/8 = 0.375$ (universal for vdW) - **Reduced Properties:** $P_r = P/P_c$, $T_r = T/T_c$, $v_r = v/v_c$ - **Principle of Corresponding States:** $Z = f(P_r, T_r)$ — universal for all gases - **Boyle Temperature:** $T_B = a/(Rb)$ — gas behaves ideally at low P - **Mixture Molecular Weight:** $M_{mix} = 1/\sum(x_i/M_i)$ for mass fractions ### 📚 Key Relationships to Memorize: 1. $Z = 1$: Ideal gas 2. $Z < 1$: Attractive forces dominant (low-moderate P, low T) 3. $Z > 1$: Repulsive forces dominant (high P, very low T) 4. At critical point: $\partial P/\partial V = 0$ and $\partial^2P/\partial V^2 = 0$ 5. For mixtures: $R_{mix} = \sum x_i R_i$ (mass-weighted average) ### 🎯 KTU Exam Strategy Tips: - Always write the **assumptions** clearly when using the ideal gas equation. - For numerical problems, **show unit conversions explicitly** to score full marks. - When asked to "**compare**" ideal and real gases, use a **tabular form** to gain easy marks. - For derivations, **label every step** and state the **physical meaning** of corrections. - In Part B (14-mark) questions, **include diagrams** (P-V or P-T) wherever possible.

topics.title: Thermodynamic Relations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET303 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thermodynamic Relations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Thermodynamic Relations — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermodynamic Relations** are a set of exact mathematical identities derived from the combined application of the **First Law**, **Second Law**, and the **Exact Differential** property of state functions (such as internal energy, enthalpy, entropy, and Gibbs free energy). These relations allow engineers to express difficult-to-measure thermodynamic properties (e.g., entropy change, internal energy) in terms of easily measurable ones (e.g., pressure, volume, temperature, specific heats).

In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, thermodynamic relations form the **analytical bridge** between the abstract postulates of classical thermodynamics and the practical data required for solving engineering problems involving real fluids, gases, vapours, and refrigeration cycles.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Hidden Map Analogy"**

Imagine you are in a dense forest (an engineering system). You want to reach a destination (a desired thermodynamic state). Direct paths (measuring entropy or internal energy directly) are blocked by cliffs and rivers — they are **difficult to measure experimentally**.

A wise guide (Thermodynamic Relations) gives you a **secret map** that converts your blocked path into an alternative route along smooth roads like **P, V, and T**, which are easy to measure with barometers, pressure gauges, and thermocouples.

In simple words:

$$\text{Secrets of the system} \longrightarrow \text{Mapped via Maxwell's Relations} \longrightarrow \text{Measurable Quantities (P, V, T, C_P, C_V)}$$

The four Maxwell's Relations are like **four secret tunnels** through the forest. Each tunnel connects a hidden property (entropy, internal energy) to a measurable one (P, V, T).

---

## 1.3 Fundamental Building Blocks (Prerequisites)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Mathematical Pillars of Thermodynamic Relations**

1. **State Postulate**: A simple compressible system is completely defined by **two independent properties**.
2. **Exact Differentials**: For any property $Z = f(x, y)$, the differential $dZ = M\,dx + N\,dy$ is exact if and only if:

$$\left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right)_x = \left(\frac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right)_y$$

3. **Cyclic Property of Partial Derivatives**:

$$\left(\frac{\partial x}{\partial y}\right)_z \cdot \left(\frac{\partial y}{\partial z}\right)_x \cdot \left(\frac{\partial z}{\partial x}\right)_y = -1$$

---

## 1.4 Why These Relations Matter in Engineering

- They eliminate the need for **elaborate calorimetric experiments** to find entropy.
- They help in **tabulating thermodynamic properties** of steam, refrigerants, and gases.
- They are the foundation of **generalized charts** (e.g., Generalized Enthalpy Departure Charts).
- Essential for **compressible flow analysis**, **nozzle design**, and **refrigeration cycle calculations**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Maxwell's Relations — The Four Pillars

The combined First and Second Law gives us four fundamental relations between thermodynamic properties. Each can be transformed into a **Maxwell Relation** by applying the **Exact Differential Criterion**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Origin: The Combined First & Second Law Equation (Gibbs Equation)**
>
> $$dU = T\,ds - P\,dv$$
>
> This is the most fundamental equation in classical thermodynamics. From this single line, all Maxwell's Relations are derived.

### 2.1.1 Derivation Logic of Maxwell's Relations

**Step 1 — Write Gibbs Equations for all four thermodynamic potentials:**

$$dU = T\,ds - P\,dv$$
$$dH = T\,ds + v\,dP$$
$$dF = -s\,dT - P\,dv$$
$$dG = -s\,dT + v\,dP$$

**Step 2 — Compare each with the standard exact differential form $dZ = M\,dx + N\,dy$.**

**Step 3 — Apply the exactness condition $\left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right)_x = \left(\frac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right)_y$.**

This yields the four Maxwell's Relations.

---

## 2.2 The Four Maxwell's Relations (Detailed Form)

### **Relation 1** (From $dU$)

$$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial v}\right)_s = -\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial s}\right)_v$$

> **Physical Meaning:** Links the isentropic temperature variation with volume to the change of pressure with entropy at constant volume.

### **Relation 2** (From $dH$)

$$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_s = \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial s}\right)_P$$

> **Physical Meaning:** Connects the isentropic temperature change with pressure to the volume-entropy slope at constant pressure.

### **Relation 3** (From $dF$)

$$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v = \left(\frac{\partial s}{\partial v}\right)_T$$

> **Physical Meaning:** One of the most used relations — connects entropy-volume changes to measurable pressure-temperature derivatives.

### **Relation 4** (From $dG$)

$$\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P = -\left(\frac{\partial s}{\partial P}\right)_T$$

> **Physical Meaning:** Links entropy-pressure dependence to the easily measurable thermal expansion behavior.

---

## 2.3 The Two Tds Equations (Gibbs Equations)

Starting from $dU = T\,ds - P\,dv$ and using $U = U(T, v)$:

$$dU = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_v dT + \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial v}\right)_T dv$$

By definition: $\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_v = C_v$

After algebraic manipulation and substitution of Maxwell's Relation 3, we obtain:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tds Equation — Form 1 (in terms of $T$ and $v$):**
>
> $$T\,ds = C_v\,dT + T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v dv$$

Similarly, treating enthalpy $H = H(T, P)$:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tds Equation — Form 2 (in terms of $T$ and $P$):**
>
> $$T\,ds = C_p\,dT - T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P dP$$

---

## 2.4 Internal Energy & Enthalpy as Functions of T and v / T and P

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General Internal Energy Equation:**
>
> $$dU = C_v\,dT + \left[T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v - P\right] dv$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General Enthalpy Equation:**
>
> $$dH = C_p\,dT + \left[v - T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P\right] dP$$

The bracketed terms are called **"Internal Energy Departure"** and **"Enthalpy Departure"** respectively. For an ideal gas, these departures become zero.

---

## 2.5 Specific Heat Difference Relation

By equating the two Tds equations and applying the **Cyclic Rule**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General $C_p - C_v$ Relation:**
>
> $$C_p - C_v = T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v$$

For an ideal gas, $Pv = RT$:

$$C_p - C_v = -T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P^{-1} = R$$

> **This is Mayer's Relation** — a cornerstone for ideal gas analysis.

---

## 2.6 Clausius Inequality & Entropy Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clausius Inequality (Second Law Statement):**
>
> $$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$$

- Equality holds for **reversible cycles**.
- Inequality holds for **irreversible cycles**.
- It is the foundation of the **entropy principle**: $dS \geq \frac{\delta Q}{T}$.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\mid` for absolute value indicators. Do **NOT** use the bare vertical pipe inside the table.

| # | Relation / Equation | Mathematical Form | Primary Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Exact Differential Condition | $\left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right)_x = \left(\frac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right)_y$ | Tests if a differential is exact |
| 2 | Cyclic Rule | $\left(\frac{\partial x}{\partial y}\right)_z \left(\frac{\partial y}{\partial z}\right)_x \left(\frac{\partial z}{\partial x}\right)_y = -1$ | Interconverting derivatives |
| 3 | Maxwell Relation 1 | $\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial v}\right)_s = -\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial s}\right)_v$ | From $dU$ |
| 4 | Maxwell Relation 2 | $\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_s = \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial s}\right)_P$ | From $dH$ |
| 5 | Maxwell Relation 3 | $\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v = \left(\frac{\partial s}{\partial v}\right)_T$ | From $dF$ |
| 6 | Maxwell Relation 4 | $\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P = -\left(\frac{\partial s}{\partial P}\right)_T$ | From $dG$ |
| 7 | Tds Equation (T,v) | $T\,ds = C_v\,dT + T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v dv$ | Entropy change |
| 8 | Tds Equation (T,P) | $T\,ds = C_p\,dT - T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P dP$ | Entropy change |
| 9 | Internal Energy Equation | $dU = C_v\,dT + \left[T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v - P\right] dv$ | Property evaluation |
| 10 | Enthalpy Equation | $dH = C_p\,dT + \left[v - T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P\right] dP$ | Property evaluation |
| 11 | $C_p - C_v$ Relation | $C_p - C_v = T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v$ | General fluids |
| 12 | Mayer's Relation (Ideal Gas) | $C_p - C_v = R$ | Ideal gas specific heats |
| 13 | Clausius Inequality | $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$ | Second law statement |
| 14 | Volume Expansivity | $\beta = \frac{1}{v}\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P$ | Real fluid property |
| 15 | Isothermal Compressibility | $\kappa_T = -\frac{1}{v}\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_T$ | Real fluid property |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Plant Design**: Mayer's relation is used to find specific heat at constant volume when only $C_p$ is known experimentally.
- **Refrigeration**: Tds equations are used to evaluate entropy changes across compressors and throttling devices.
- **Gas Turbine Analysis**: $C_p - C_v$ relation determines the ratio $\gamma$, which governs isentropic efficiency.
- **Chemical Process Industries**: Maxwell Relation 4 is used to calculate entropy changes of liquids from PVT data alone.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maxwell's Relation 3 (From Helmholtz Free Energy)

> **Starting Equation:**
>
> $$dF = -s\,dT - P\,dv$$
>
> Comparing with $dF = M\,dT + N\,dv$:
>
> $$\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial T}\right)_v = -s \quad \text{and} \quad \left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial v}\right)_T = -P$$

**Step 1 — Apply exact differential criterion:**

$$\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial v}\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial T}\right)_v\right]_T = \left[\frac{\partial}{\partial T}\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial v}\right)_T\right]_v$$

**Step 2 — Substitute partial derivatives:**

$$\left[\frac{\partial(-s)}{\partial v}\right]_T = \left[\frac{\partial(-P)}{\partial T}\right]_v$$

**Step 3 — Simplify signs:**

$$-\left(\frac{\partial s}{\partial v}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v$$

**Step 4 — Final Maxwell Relation 3:**

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v = \left(\frac{\partial s}{\partial v}\right)_T}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Tds Equation (Form 1) — In Terms of T and v

> **Starting Equation (Gibbs Equation):**
>
> $$dU = T\,ds - P\,dv \quad \cdots (1)$$

**Step 1 — Express internal energy as $U = U(T, v)$:**

$$dU = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_v dT + \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial v}\right)_T dv \quad \cdots (2)$$

**Step 2 — Equate (1) and (2):**

$$T\,ds - P\,dv = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_v dT + \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial v}\right)_T dv$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $T\,ds$:**

$$T\,ds = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_v dT + \left[\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial v}\right)_T + P\right] dv$$

**Step 4 — Recognize $\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_v = C_v$:**

$$T\,ds = C_v\,dT + \left[\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial v}\right)_T + P\right] dv \quad \cdots (3)$$

**Step 5 — We need to eliminate $\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial v}\right)_T$.** From equation (1):

$$\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial v}\right)_T = T\left(\frac{\partial s}{\partial v}\right)_T - P$$

**Step 6 — Apply Maxwell Relation 3: $\left(\frac{\partial s}{\partial v}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v$:**

$$\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial v}\right)_T = T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v - P$$

**Step 7 — Substitute back into equation (3):**

$$T\,ds = C_v\,dT + \left[T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v - P + P\right] dv$$

$$T\,ds = C_v\,dT + T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v dv$$

**Step 8 — Final Result (Tds Equation 1):**

$$\boxed{T\,ds = C_v\,dT + T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v dv}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Tds Equation (Form 2) — In Terms of T and P

> **Starting Point:** Treat enthalpy $H = H(T, P)$.
>
> $$dH = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_P dT + \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_T dP$$

**Step 1 — Identify $\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_P = C_p$:**

$$dH = C_p\,dT + \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_T dP \quad \cdots (4)$$

**Step 2 — From $dH = T\,ds + v\,dP$, we get $T\,ds = dH - v\,dP$:**

$$T\,ds = C_p\,dT + \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_T dP - v\,dP$$

$$T\,ds = C_p\,dT + \left[\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_T - v\right] dP \quad \cdots (5)$$

**Step 3 — Derive $\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_T$:** Differentiating $H = U + Pv$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial P}\right)_T + v + P\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_T$$

**Step 4 — Express $\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial P}\right)_T$ via $U = U(T, v)$ and cyclic rule:**

$$\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial P}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial v}\right)_T \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_T = \left[T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v - P\right] \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_T$$

**Step 5 — Apply cyclic rule $\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_T \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial v}\right)_P = -1$:**

This eventually reduces to (after extensive algebra):

$$\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_T = v - T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

**Step 6 — Substitute into (5):**

$$T\,ds = C_p\,dT + \left[v - T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P - v\right] dP$$

**Step 7 — Final Result (Tds Equation 2):**

$$\boxed{T\,ds = C_p\,dT - T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P dP}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: The General $C_p - C_v$ Relation

> **Step 1 — Start from Tds Equation 2:**
>
> $$T\,ds = C_p\,dT - T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P dP$$

For a **constant volume process**, $dv = 0$, so $dP$ can be related to $dT$ via:

$$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v = \frac{1}{\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_v}$$

**Step 2 — At constant volume, Tds Equation 1 gives:**

$$T\,ds = C_v\,dT + T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v (0) = C_v\,dT$$

**Step 3 — But also from Tds Equation 2 at constant $v$:**

Since $v = v(T, P)$, at constant $v$: $dv = \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P dT + \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_T dP = 0$

Solving for $dP$:

$$dP = -\frac{\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P}{\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_T} dT$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into Tds Equation 2:**

$$T\,ds = C_p\,dT - T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P \left[-\frac{\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P}{\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_T}\right] dT$$

$$T\,ds = C_p\,dT + T \frac{\left[\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P\right]^2}{\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_T} dT$$

**Step 5 — Equate with $T\,ds = C_v\,dT$:**

$$C_v = C_p + T \frac{\left[\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P\right]^2}{\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_T}$$

**Step 6 — Use the cyclic identity:**

$$\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\frac{1}{\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial v}\right)_T} \cdot \frac{1}{\text{(via cyclic)}}$$

Actually, using the identity:

$$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial v}\right)_P \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_T = -1$$

We get:

$$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v = -\frac{\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P}{\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_T}$$

**Step 7 — Final General Relation:**

$$\boxed{C_p - C_v = -T \frac{\left[\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P\right]^2}{\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_T} = T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Mayer's Relation for Ideal Gas

> **Step 1 — For ideal gas, $Pv = RT$, so:**
>
> $$\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{R}{P}$$
>
> $$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v = \frac{R}{v}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into $C_p - C_v$ relation:**

$$C_p - C_v = T \cdot \frac{R}{P} \cdot \frac{R}{v} = \frac{T R^2}{Pv}$$

**Step 3 — Use ideal gas law $Pv = RT$:**

$$C_p - C_v = \frac{T R^2}{RT} = R$$

**Step 4 — Final Mayer's Relation:**

$$\boxed{C_p - C_v = R}$$

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Internal Energy Equation for Ideal Gas (Joule's Law)

> **Step 1 — Start from the general internal energy relation:**
>
> $$dU = C_v\,dT + \left[T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v - P\right] dv$$

**Step 2 — For ideal gas:** $\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v = \frac{R}{v}$

So: $T \cdot \frac{R}{v} - P = \frac{RT}{v} - P = \frac{Pv}{v} - P = P - P = 0$

**Step 3 — Therefore:**

$$\boxed{dU = C_v\,dT \quad \Rightarrow \quad U = U(T) \text{ only}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** For an ideal gas, internal energy is a function of **temperature alone** (Joule's Law). The "departure" from this in real gases is called **Joule-Thomson Effect**.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **State and prove the Clausius Inequality. Why is the equality valid only for reversible cycles?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Statement:** For any thermodynamic cycle operating between reservoirs, the cyclic integral of $\delta Q/T$ is less than or equal to zero.

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$$

**Proof Sketch:**

Consider an irreversible cycle operating between a hot reservoir at $T_H$ and cold reservoir at $T_C$. To make the combined system reversible, we add a reversible engine operating between the same reservoirs. The total entropy change of the universe (reservoirs) for the reversible compensating engine must equal the negative of the entropy transferred by the irreversible cycle. Hence $\oint \delta Q/T \leq 0$.

**Equality Condition:** A reversible cycle produces no entropy in the universe, so $\oint \delta Q/T = 0$ exactly.

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **Derive the Maxwell Relation $\left(\frac{\partial s}{\partial v}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v$ from the fundamental property relations.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Starting from Helmholtz free energy: $F = U - TS \Rightarrow dF = -s\,dT - P\,dv$.

Since $dF$ is exact, by Euler's reciprocity:

$$\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial v}(-s)\right]_T = \left[\frac{\partial}{\partial T}(-P)\right]_v$$

$$-\left(\frac{\partial s}{\partial v}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v$$

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{\partial s}{\partial v}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### 🔵 Question A — 14 Marks
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

> **(a)** Starting from the combined First and Second Law, derive the **two Tds equations** in terms of measurable properties. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** For a gas following the equation of state $P(v - b) = RT$ (where $b$ is a constant), determine $C_p - C_v$ using the general specific heat difference relation. (7 Marks)

---

#### Solution to Q.A (a) — Derivation of Two Tds Equations (7 Marks)

**Starting Point (1 Mark):**

$$dU = T\,ds - P\,dv \quad \cdots \text{(Combined 1st and 2nd Law)}$$

**Case 1: $s = s(T, v)$** (1 Mark)

$$ds = \left(\frac{\partial s}{\partial T}\right)_v dT + \left(\frac{\partial s}{\partial v}\right)_T dv$$

Multiply by $T$:

$$T\,ds = T\left(\frac{\partial s}{\partial T}\right)_v dT + T\left(\frac{\partial s}{\partial v}\right)_T dv \quad \cdots (1)$$

Recognize that at constant $v$:

$$T\left(\frac{\partial s}{\partial T}\right)_v = C_v$$

And by Maxwell Relation 3: $\left(\frac{\partial s}{\partial v}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v$.

**Final Tds Equation 1 (1 Mark):**

$$T\,ds = C_v\,dT + T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v dv$$

**Case 2: $s = s(T, P)$** (1 Mark)

$$ds = \left(\frac{\partial s}{\partial T}\right)_P dT + \left(\frac{\partial s}{\partial P}\right)_T dP$$

Multiply by $T$:

$$T\,ds = T\left(\frac{\partial s}{\partial T}\right)_P dT + T\left(\frac{\partial s}{\partial P}\right)_T dP \quad \cdots (2)$$

Recognize: $T\left(\frac{\partial s}{\partial T}\right)_P = C_p$ and by Maxwell Relation 4: $\left(\frac{\partial s}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P$.

**Final Tds Equation 2 (1 Mark):**

$$T\,ds = C_p\,dT - T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P dP$$

> ✅ **Validity Check (2 Marks):** These are the two master Tds equations that allow entropy calculation from PVT data and specific heats alone. For an ideal gas ($Pv = RT$), both equations reduce to $Tds = C_v dT + R(P/v)dv$ and $Tds = C_p dT - R(dP/P)$ respectively, which are consistent with standard textbooks.

---

#### Solution to Q.A (b) — $C_p - C_v$ for $P(v-b) = RT$ (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Identify the equation of state (1 Mark):**

$$P(v - b) = RT$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P$ (2 Marks):**

Differentiating implicitly: $P\,dv = R\,dT$ at constant $P$.

$$\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{R}{P}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v$ (2 Marks):**

Differentiating: $\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v (v - b) = R$

$$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v = \frac{R}{v - b}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into $C_p - C_v$ relation (1 Mark):**

$$C_p - C_v = T \cdot \frac{R}{P} \cdot \frac{R}{v - b} = \frac{T R^2}{P(v-b)}$$

**Step 5 — Apply $P(v - b) = RT$ (1 Mark):**

$$C_p - C_v = \frac{T R^2}{RT} = R$$

> ✅ **Final Answer:** $\boxed{C_p - C_v = R}$ — Same as ideal gas! This shows that the constant $b$ in van der Waals-type equation represents only **volume exclusion** and does not affect the specific heat difference (only $a$ — the attraction term — affects it).

---

### 🟢 Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **(a)** Derive the **four Maxwell's Relations** from the four fundamental thermodynamic property equations. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** Steam initially at **2 MPa and 350°C** expands isothermally and reversibly to **500 kPa**. Using the Maxwell Relation $\left(\frac{\partial s}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P$ and steam tables, determine the change in specific entropy. (7 Marks)

---

#### Solution to Q.B (a) — Four Maxwell's Relations (7 Marks)

**Setup (1 Mark):** The four potentials are $U, H, F, G$ with differential forms:

$$dU = T\,ds - P\,dv$$
$$dH = T\,ds + v\,dP$$
$$dF = -s\,dT - P\,dv$$
$$dG = -s\,dT + v\,dP$$

**Relation 1 — From $dU$** (1.5 Marks): Treating $U = U(s, v)$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial v}\right)_s = -\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial s}\right)_v$$

**Relation 2 — From $dH$** (1.5 Marks): Treating $H = H(s, P)$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_s = \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial s}\right)_P$$

**Relation 3 — From $dF$** (1.5 Marks): Treating $F = F(T, v)$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v = \left(\frac{\partial s}{\partial v}\right)_T$$

**Relation 4 — From $dG$** (1.5 Marks): Treating $G = G(T, P)$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P = -\left(\frac{\partial s}{\partial P}\right)_T$$

> ✅ **Examiner's Note (Implicit):** Each relation is derived by matching the differential coefficients of $U, H, F, G$ with the cross-partial reciprocity theorem.

---

#### Solution to Q.B (b) — Steam Isothermal Expansion (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Recognize that isothermal implies $dT = 0$** (1 Mark):

The Maxwell Relation gives:

$$T\,ds = C_p\,dT - T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P dP \xrightarrow{dT = 0} ds = -\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P dP$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from State 1 to State 2** (2 Marks):

$$\Delta s = s_2 - s_1 = -\int_{P_1}^{P_2} \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P dP$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate using steam tables** (2 Marks):

At **State 1: $P_1 = 2$ MPa, $T_1 = 350°C$ (superheated):**
- $v_1 \approx 0.13857 \, m^3/kg$
- $s_1 \approx 6.5940 \, kJ/kg\cdot K$

At **State 2: $P_2 = 500$ kPa, $T_2 = 350°C$ (superheated, since $T_{sat} = 151.86°C$):**
- $v_2 \approx 0.57011 \, m^3/kg$
- $s_2 \approx 7.9447 \, kJ/kg\cdot K$

**Step 4 — Compute change in entropy** (2 Marks):

$$\Delta s = s_2 - s_1 = 7.9447 - 6.5940 = 1.3507 \, kJ/kg\cdot K$$

> ✅ **Alternative Verification (Implicit):** The same result can be obtained by direct steam table lookup because the relation $\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P$ essentially captures the slope of isobars in PVT space.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Students 2-4 Marks Each**
>
> 1. **Sign Errors in Maxwell's Relations:** Relation 1 and Relation 4 carry a **negative sign**. Forgetting this is a guaranteed half-mark loss. Remember: $dU$ and $dG$ equations carry **negative** coefficients for $P$ and $s$ respectively.
>
> 2. **Confusing $C_p$ and $C_v$ in Tds Equations:** Tds Equation 1 has $C_v$ with $dv$, Tds Equation 2 has $C_p$ with $dP$. Mixing them is the #1 mistake.
>
> 3. **Wrong Sign in $C_p - C_v$ Derivation:** Some students write $C_p - C_v = -T(...) $. The correct form has **positive** $T$ multiplied by **positive** derivative product.
>
> 4. **Assuming Mayer's Relation Holds for Real Gases:** Mayer's Relation ($C_p - C_v = R$) is **ONLY for ideal gases**. Real gases have a temperature- and pressure-dependent difference.
>
> 5. **Confusing Cyclic Rule Sign:** The cyclic rule has a **negative sign** = $-1$, not $+1$. Forgetting the minus sign propagates through every transformation.
>
> 6. **Forgetting Constant Property Indicator:** Every partial derivative MUST have the held-constant variable as subscript. Missing subscripts = zero credit in ESE.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> **🎯 Key Takeaways for Last-Minute Revision**

- ✅ **Three Pillars**: Exact Differential Condition + Cyclic Rule + Combined 1st/2nd Law.
- ✅ **Four Gibbs Equations**: $dU$, $dH$, $dF$, $dG$ — memorize each.
- ✅ **Four Maxwell's Relations**: MR-1, MR-2, MR-3, MR-4 — note the negative signs in MR-1 and MR-4.
- ✅ **Two Tds Equations**: One with $C_v$ and $dv$; the other with $C_p$ and $dP$.
- ✅ **Mayer's Relation**: $C_p - C_v = R$ is **ideal gas only**.
- ✅ **Internal Energy of Ideal Gas**: $dU = C_v dT$ — depends on temperature alone (Joule's Law).
- ✅ **General $C_p - C_v$ Relation**: $C_p - C_v = T(\partial v/\partial T)_P (\partial P/\partial T)_v$.
- ✅ **Clausius Inequality**: $\oint \delta Q/T \leq 0$ — equality only for reversible cycles.
- ✅ **Engineering Uses**: Gas property tables, refrigeration cycles, nozzle/throttle calculations, gas turbine design.
- ✅ **Real Gases**: "Departure functions" arise from the bracketed correction terms in $dU$ and $dH$ equations.
- ✅ **Volume Expansivity** $\beta$ and **Isothermal Compressibility** $\kappa_T$ are the two fundamental PVT response coefficients.
- ✅ **Cyclic Rule**: Always $-1$ — never $+1$.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module 1 Notes — Thermodynamic Relations**
>
> These notes fully cover the **2024 Scheme PCIET303** Module 1 syllabus on Thermodynamic Relations, including Maxwell's Relations, Tds Equations, General Internal Energy & Enthalpy Equations, $C_p - C_v$ Relations, Mayer's Relation, and Clausius Inequality — all aligned with KTU Board Examination patterns and Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels (Apply, Analyze, Evaluate).

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIET303 - THERMAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Fundamentals of Steam (Properties of Steam) # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Definition of Steam > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** > **Steam** is the gaseous phase of water (H₂O) obtained when water is heated beyond its saturation temperature at a given pressure. In thermodynamics, steam is classified as a **working substance** that undergoes a **phase change** during the addition or rejection of heat, making it one of the most widely used fluids in thermal power generation, process industries, and heating applications. **Classification of Steam (KTU 2024 Scheme Classification):** | Type of Steam | Definition | Key Property | |---|---|---| | **Wet Steam** | A two-phase mixture of saturated water and dry steam | Contains moisture (1 - x) | | **Dry Saturated Steam** | Steam at saturation temperature containing no moisture | Dryness fraction x = 1.0 | | **Superheated Steam** | Steam heated above its saturation temperature | Superheat degree > 0 | | **Super-saturated Steam** | Steam in metastable state (rare) | Not used in KTU problems | ## 1.2 Formation of Steam — The Three Stages When water is heated in a boiler at constant pressure, it passes through **three distinct thermodynamic stages** before becoming usable superheated steam: ### **Stage 1: Sensible Heating (Liquid Water → Saturated Water)** - Water temperature rises from initial temperature to **saturation temperature (T_sat)** at the given pressure. - Heat added = **Sensible Heat** (sensible = "can be sensed by thermometer"). - The water at the end of this stage is called **Saturated Liquid** or **Saturated Water**. ### **Stage 2: Latent Heat Addition (Saturated Water → Saturated Steam)** - At constant temperature (T_sat) and pressure, water changes phase into steam. - Heat added = **Latent Heat of Vaporization (h_fg)**. - The mixture is called **Wet Steam**, characterized by **Dryness Fraction (x)**. ### **Stage 3: Superheating (Saturated Steam → Superheated Steam)** - Steam temperature rises above saturation temperature. - Heat added = **Superheat (C_p × ΔT)**. - The resulting steam is called **Superheated Steam**. > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy (Real-World):** > Think of boiling water in a pressure cooker. As you heat it: > 1. First, the water gets hot — this is **sensible heating** (you can feel the temperature rise). > 2. Then it starts boiling vigorously, but the temperature **stops rising** — this is **latent heating** (the heat is "hidden" inside, converting water to steam). > 3. If you keep heating the steam, it gets even hotter (superheated) — this is the **superheating** stage. > > Steam engines and power plants exploit this huge latent heat because a tiny bit of water can carry enormous amounts of energy as steam! ## 1.3 Key Properties of Steam (KTU 2024 Scheme — High Priority) The following intensive properties of steam must be memorized for KTU examinations: - **Pressure (P)** — measured in **bar** or **MPa** (1 bar = 10⁵ Pa = 0.1 MPa). - **Temperature (T)** — measured in **°C** or **K**. - **Specific Volume (v)** — volume per unit mass, in **m³/kg**. - **Specific Enthalpy (h)** — total heat content, in **kJ/kg**. - **Specific Entropy (s)** — measure of disorder, in **kJ/kg·K**. - **Specific Internal Energy (u)** — in **kJ/kg**. - **Dryness Fraction (x)** — mass of actual dry steam per kg of wet steam (dimensionless, 0 ≤ x ≤ 1). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The properties of steam are obtained from **Steam Tables** (provided in the KTU examination booklet). Students are NOT expected to memorize numerical values, only the **symbols, units, and calculation methodology**. --- ## 1.4 The Phase-Change Energy Chain (Energy Required to Produce Steam) The total heat required to convert 1 kg of water at 0 °C into superheated steam is: $$ Q_{total} = h_{fw} + h_{fg} + C_p \cdot \Delta T_{sup} $$ Where: - $h_{fw}$ = Enthalpy of feed water (sensible heat from 0 °C to saturation) - $h_{fg}$ = Latent heat of vaporization - $C_p \cdot \Delta T_{sup}$ = Superheat enthalpy This energy chain is the **backbone of all KTU numerical problems** on steam properties. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Theoretical Analysis of Steam Properties ### 2.1.1 Dryness Fraction (Quality) — x The **dryness fraction** is the most important KTU parameter because wet steam always contains some moisture in real boilers. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition:** Dryness fraction (x) is the ratio of the mass of actual dry steam to the total mass of the wet steam mixture. > > **KTU Formula:** > $$x = \frac{m_{steam}}{m_{steam} + m_{water}}$$ **Properties of wet steam (using x):** | Property | Formula | Description | |---|---|---| | **Specific Volume** | $v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg}$ | Volume per kg of mixture | | **Specific Enthalpy** | $h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$ | Total heat per kg | | **Specific Internal Energy** | $u = u_f + x \cdot u_{fg}$ | Internal energy per kg | | **Specific Entropy** | $s = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg}$ | Entropy per kg | | **Density** | $\rho = \dfrac{1}{v}$ | Mass per unit volume | > [!NOTE] > **Exam Trick:** $v_f$ (specific volume of water) is so small compared to $v_g$ that KTU problems often allow the approximation $v \approx x \cdot v_g$. However, for precise answers, use the full formula. ### 2.1.2 Superheated Steam When wet steam is fully vaporized (x = 1) and further heated, the temperature rises above T_sat. The **degree of superheat** is: $$ \Delta T_{sup} = T_{superheated} - T_{sat} $$ For superheated steam, properties are read directly from the **Superheated Steam Table** at the given pressure and temperature (NOT calculated using x). ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **This table is the SINGLE MOST IMPORTANT REFERENCE for solving KTU numerical problems on Fundamentals of Steam.** | # | Property (Wet Steam) | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Specific Enthalpy** | $h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$ | kJ/kg | $h_f$ from sat. water table | | 2 | **Specific Volume** | $v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg}$ | m³/kg | $v_f$ usually negligible | | 3 | **Specific Internal Energy** | $u = h - P \cdot v$ | kJ/kg | $P$ in kPa, $v$ in m³/kg | | 4 | **Specific Entropy** | $s = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg}$ | kJ/kg·K | Direct from tables | | 5 | **Latent Heat** | $h_{fg} = h_g - h_f$ | kJ/kg | Always positive | | 6 | **Total Heat of Steam** | $H = m \cdot h$ | kJ | For m kg of steam | | 7 | **Heat to Form Steam** | $Q = h_g - h_f = h_{fg}$ | kJ/kg | Phase change only | | 8 | **Internal Latent Heat** | $u_{fg} = h_{fg} - P \cdot v_g$ | kJ/kg | Important for work calcs | | 9 | **Specific Heat (Superheated)** | $C_p \approx 2.1$ kJ/kg·K | kJ/kg·K | Approx. for steam | | 10 | **Steam Quality Limit** | $0 \le x \le 1$ | — | Boundary condition | ### 2.2.1 Critical Point of Steam (KTU Important Constant) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Point Constants of Water/Steam:** > - **Critical Pressure:** $P_c = 22.064$ MPa ≈ **221.2 bar** > - **Critical Temperature:** $T_c = 374.15$ °C ≈ **647.3 K** > - **Critical Specific Volume:** $v_c = 0.003155$ m³/kg > - **Critical Enthalpy:** $h_c = 2087.5$ kJ/kg **Why is the critical point important?** - Above the critical point, there is **no distinction** between liquid and gaseous phases. - The concept of **latent heat ceases to exist** above $P_c$ and $T_c$. - Modern supercritical boilers (used in ultra-supercritical power plants) operate above this point. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Thermal Power Plants (Rankine Cycle):** Steam is the working fluid; ~80% of the world's electricity comes from steam-based plants. - **Nuclear Power Plants:** Pressurized Water Reactors (PWR) use steam at high pressure to drive turbines. - **Process Industries:** Sugar mills, paper mills, refineries use steam for heating. - **Marine Propulsion:** Steam turbines drive nuclear submarines and aircraft carriers. - **HVAC Systems:** District heating and sterilization plants rely on steam properties. --- ## 2.4 Steam Table Reading Methodology (KTU Exam Strategy) > [!NOTE] > **Step-by-Step Procedure for KTU Numerical Problems:** 1. **Identify the State of Steam:** Wet, dry saturated, or superheated. 2. **If Two Independent Properties are given:** - Pressure (P) and Temperature (T) → If $T = T_{sat}$, it's saturated; if $T > T_{sat}$, it's superheated. - Pressure (P) and Dryness Fraction (x) → Always wet steam. - Enthalpy (h) and Entropy (s) → Use Mollier chart or iterative table look-up. 3. **Look up the base values** ($h_f, h_g, s_f, s_g, v_f, v_g$) from the appropriate table. 4. **Apply the formula** to compute the unknown property. 5. **Cross-check units** (P in bar → convert to kPa when multiplying with v in m³/kg). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Wet Steam Properties ### 3.1.1 Derivation of Specific Enthalpy of Wet Steam **Given:** 1 kg of wet steam containing x kg of dry saturated steam and (1 − x) kg of saturated water. **Step 1 — Total Enthalpy = Sum of Component Enthalpies:** $$ H_{total} = (1 - x) \cdot h_f + x \cdot h_g $$ **Step 2 — Expand and Group:** $$ H_{total} = h_f - x \cdot h_f + x \cdot h_g $$ **Step 3 — Factor Out x:** $$ H_{total} = h_f + x \cdot (h_g - h_f) $$ **Step 4 — Recognize that $h_g - h_f = h_{fg}$:** $$ \boxed{H = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}} $$ > **Why this works:** The mixture enthalpy is the **mass-weighted average** of the enthalpies of the two phases, weighted by their respective mass fractions. --- ### 3.1.2 Derivation of Specific Volume of Wet Steam **Step 1 — Total Volume:** $$ V_{total} = (1 - x) \cdot v_f + x \cdot v_g $$ **Step 2 — Divide by total mass (= 1 kg):** $$ v = (1 - x) \cdot v_f + x \cdot v_g $$ **Step 3 — Expand:** $$ v = v_f - x \cdot v_f + x \cdot v_g $$ **Step 4 — Group terms with x:** $$ \boxed{v = v_f + x \cdot (v_g - v_f) = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg}} $$ --- ### 3.1.3 Derivation of Specific Internal Energy of Wet Steam From the fundamental thermodynamic relation: $$ h = u + P \cdot v \quad \Rightarrow \quad u = h - P \cdot v $$ **Step 1 — Substituting wet steam h and v:** $$ u = [h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}] - P \cdot [v_f + x \cdot v_{fg}] $$ **Step 2 — Grouping saturated water and steam contributions:** $$ u = (h_f - P \cdot v_f) + x \cdot (h_{fg} - P \cdot v_{fg}) $$ **Step 3 — Recognizing that $u_f = h_f - P \cdot v_f$ and $u_{fg} = h_{fg} - P \cdot v_{fg}$:** $$ \boxed{u = u_f + x \cdot u_{fg}} $$ --- ## 3.2 Worked-Out KTU Numerical Problems (Analytical Matrix) ### **Problem 1 (Classic KTU Pattern — 7 Marks)** **Question:** Determine the dryness fraction, specific volume, specific enthalpy, specific entropy, and specific internal energy of steam at **10 bar** pressure with a **temperature of 200 °C**. **Given:** - $P = 10$ bar - $T = 200$ °C **Step 1 — Check Saturation Condition at 10 bar (from steam table):** At P = 10 bar: $T_{sat} = 179.9$ °C ≈ **180 °C** Since $T_{given} = 200$ °C > $T_{sat} = 180$ °C, the steam is **SUPERHEATED**. **Step 2 — Look up properties from the Superheated Steam Table at 10 bar, 200 °C:** | Property | Value (from KTU steam table) | |---|---| | $v$ (specific volume) | **0.2060 m³/kg** | | $h$ (specific enthalpy) | **2827.9 kJ/kg** | | $s$ (specific entropy) | **6.6940 kJ/kg·K** | | $u$ (specific internal energy) | **2621.9 kJ/kg** | **Step 3 — Final Answer (Superheated Steam):** $$ \boxed{x \rightarrow \text{not defined for superheated steam}} $$ $$ \boxed{v = 0.2060 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}, \quad h = 2827.9 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad s = 6.6940 \text{ kJ/kg·K}, \quad u = 2621.9 \text{ kJ/kg}} $$ --- ### **Problem 2 (Wet Steam — KTU Standard 7-Mark Pattern)** **Question:** A boiler produces wet steam at **15 bar** with a dryness fraction of **0.85**.
