Find the specific volume, specific enthalpy, specific entropy, and specific internal energy. **Given:** - $P = 15$ bar - $x = 0.85$ **Step 1 — Look up Saturated Steam Properties at 15 bar (from KTU steam table):** | Property | Value | |---|---| | $T_{sat}$ | **198.3 °C** | | $h_f$ | **844.7 kJ/kg** | | $h_g$ | **2792.2 kJ/kg** | | $h_{fg}$ | $h_g - h_f$ = 2792.2 − 844.7 = **1947.5 kJ/kg** | | $v_g$ | **0.1317 m³/kg** | | $s_f$ | **2.3150 kJ/kg·K** | | $s_g$ | **6.4406 kJ/kg·K** | | $s_{fg}$ | $s_g - s_f$ = 6.4406 − 2.3150 = **4.1256 kJ/kg·K** | **Step 2 — Compute Specific Enthalpy:** $$ h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg} $$ $$ h = 844.7 + 0.85 \times 1947.5 $$ $$ h = 844.7 + 1655.375 $$ $$ \boxed{h = 2500.075 \approx 2500.1 \text{ kJ/kg}} $$ **Step 3 — Compute Specific Volume:** $$ v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg} \approx x \cdot v_g \quad \text{(since } v_f \text{ is negligible)} $$ $$ v = 0.85 \times 0.1317 $$ $$ \boxed{v = 0.1119 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}} $$ **Step 4 — Compute Specific Entropy:** $$ s = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg} $$ $$ s = 2.3150 + 0.85 \times 4.1256 $$ $$ s = 2.3150 + 3.5068 $$ $$ \boxed{s = 5.8218 \text{ kJ/kg·K}} $$ **Step 5 — Compute Specific Internal Energy:** $$ u = h - P \cdot v $$ Conversion: $P = 15$ bar = **1500 kPa** $$ u = 2500.1 - 1500 \times 0.1119 $$ $$ u = 2500.1 - 167.85 $$ $$ \boxed{u = 2332.25 \text{ kJ/kg}} $$ --- ### **Problem 3 (Reverse Problem — Find Dryness Fraction)** **Question:** 2 kg of wet steam at **8 bar** has a total enthalpy of **5200 kJ**. Find the dryness fraction. **Given:** - $P = 8$ bar - $m = 2$ kg - $H_{total} = 5200$ kJ **Step 1 — Find Specific Enthalpy:** $$ h = \frac{H_{total}}{m} = \frac{5200}{2} = 2600 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ **Step 2 — Look up Saturated Steam Properties at 8 bar:** | Property | Value | |---|---| | $h_f$ | **720.2 kJ/kg** | | $h_g$ | **2769.1 kJ/kg** | | $h_{fg}$ | $h_g - h_f$ = 2769.1 − 720.2 = **2048.9 kJ/kg** | **Step 3 — Apply Wet Steam Enthalpy Formula:** $$ h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg} $$ $$ 2600 = 720.2 + x \times 2048.9 $$ $$ x = \frac{2600 - 720.2}{2048.9} = \frac{1879.8}{2048.9} $$ $$ \boxed{x = 0.9175} $$ **Step 4 — Interpretation:** The steam is **91.75% dry**, meaning it contains 8.25% moisture by mass. This is typical of a well-designed industrial boiler. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Thermodynamic Process Flow — Formation of Steam The following block-level functional architecture flow describes the three stages of steam formation, their energy components, and the property transitions in a steam boiler system. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[STAGE 1: SENSIBLE HEATING
Feedwater at T0 to T_sat] --> B[STAGE 2: LATENT HEAT ADDITION
Phase Change at Constant T and P] B --> C[STAGE 3: SUPERHEATING
T rises above T_sat] A --> A1[Heat Added: h_fw
Property: Saturated Water
State: Liquid] B --> B1[Heat Added: h_fg
Property: Wet Steam
State: Two Phase] C --> C1[Heat Added: Cp x DeltaT
Property: Superheated Steam
State: Gas] A1 --> D[ENERGY CHAIN OUTPUT
Q_total = h_fw + h_fg + Cp x DeltaT] B1 --> D C1 --> D D --> E[FINAL STEAM
Sent to Turbine or Process] B --> F[CHARACTERIZED BY
Dryness Fraction x] F --> F1[Specific Enthalpy: h = h_f + x.h_fg] F --> F2[Specific Volume: v = v_f + x.v_fg] F --> F3[Specific Entropy: s = s_f + x.s_fg] ``` ## 4.2 Classification of Steam States — Decision Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[STEAM INPUT
Given P and T or P and x] --> Q1{Is T = T_sat at given P?} Q1 -- YES --> Q2{Is x given?} Q1 -- NO --> Q3{Is T greater than T_sat?} Q3 -- YES --> Super[STATE: SUPERHEATED STEAM
Use Superheated Steam Table] Q3 -- NO --> Sub[STATE: SUB-COOLED
Not covered in KTU Module 2] Q2 -- YES --> Wet[STATE: WET STEAM
Use h = h_f + x.h_fg] Q2 -- NO --> Dry[STATE: DRY SATURATED STEAM
Use x = 1 values directly] Super --> Out[Final Properties: v, h, s, u] Wet --> Out Dry --> Out ``` ## 4.3 Pressure–Volume (P–v) and Temperature–Entropy (T–s) Diagrams The following schematic shows the **phase regions and critical isotherm** for water/steam. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph P_v_DIAGRAM[P-v DIAGRAM SCHEMATIC] P1[High P Region] --> P2[Saturation Dome
Bounded by Saturation Curve] P2 --> P3[Critical Point C
Pc = 221.2 bar
Tc = 374.15 C] P3 --> P4[Low P Region
Superheated Steam Area] P2 --> P5[Inside Dome = Wet Steam
Region where 0 le x le 1] end subgraph T_s_DIAGRAM[T-s DIAGRAM SCHEMATIC] T1[Saturated Water Line] --> T2[Dome Region
Two Phase] T2 --> T3[Saturated Steam Line
Right Boundary of Dome] T3 --> T4[Superheated Region
Right of Dome] T2 --> T5[Constant Pressure Lines
Isentropic Expansion Paths] end ``` ## 4.4 Energy Flow Block Diagram — Steam Generation System ```mermaid flowchart LR Fuel[Fuel
Chemical Energy] --> Comb[Combustion Chamber] Comb -->|Heat Transfer| Boiler[Steam Boiler] Feed[Fresh Feedwater] --> Pump[Feed Pump] Pump --> Econ[Economiser
Sensible Heating Stage] Econ --> Evap[Evaporator
Latent Heat Stage] Evap --> Suph[Superheater
Superheating Stage] Boiler --> Econ Boiler --> Evap Boiler --> Suph Suph --> Steam[High Pressure Superheated Steam] Steam --> Turbine[Steam Turbine] Turbine --> Generator[Electrical Generator] Generator --> Power[Electrical Power Output] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Block Diagram Insight:** The three stages of steam formation (sensible → latent → superheat) occur in three distinct physical components of the boiler: **Economiser → Evaporator → Superheater**. This is the architecture used in every modern Rankine-cycle power plant. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define the following with respect to steam:** **(a)** Dryness fraction **(b)** Degree of superheat **(c)** Critical point **Model Answer:** **(a) Dryness Fraction (x):** It is defined as the ratio of the mass of actual dry steam to the total mass of the wet steam mixture. Mathematically: $$x = \frac{m_{dry\text{ }steam}}{m_{dry\text{ }steam} + m_{water}}$$ Value of x lies between 0 (saturated water) and 1 (dry saturated steam). **(1 Mark)** **(b) Degree of Superheat:** It is the difference between the actual temperature of superheated steam and the saturation temperature at the same pressure: $$\Delta T_{sup} = T_{superheated} - T_{sat}$$ **(1 Mark)** **(c) Critical Point:** It is the point on the P-v or T-s diagram at which the liquid and vapor phases become indistinguishable. For water: - $P_c = 221.2$ bar - $T_c = 374.15$ °C Above the critical point, no latent heat of vaporization exists. **(1 Mark)** --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between wet steam, dry saturated steam, and superheated steam with the help of a T-s diagram.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Wet Steam | Dry Saturated Steam | Superheated Steam | |---|---|---|---| | **Definition** | Mixture of water and steam | Pure steam at T_sat | Steam heated above T_sat | | **Dryness fraction (x)** | 0 < x < 1 | x = 1 | Not applicable | | **Temperature** | T_sat at given P | T_sat at given P | T > T_sat at given P | | **Phase State** | Two-phase | Boundary (saturated) | Single-phase gas | | **On T-s diagram** | Inside the dome | On the right boundary of dome | Outside (right of) the dome | | **Heat addition** | Latent heat in progress | Latent heat just completed | Superheating in progress | **(3 Marks — 1.5 for definitions + 1.5 for T-s description)** --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** A sample of steam at **12 bar** has a dryness fraction of **0.9**. Determine: (i) Specific volume (ii) Specific enthalpy (iii) Specific entropy (iv) Specific internal energy **(7 Marks)** **(b)** In a steam power plant, wet steam is generated at **20 bar** with an enthalpy of **2600 kJ/kg**. Find: (i) Dryness fraction of the steam (ii) The amount of heat required to convert 1 kg of this wet steam into dry saturated steam (iii) The amount of additional heat to superheat this dry steam to **350 °C** **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Solution to Part (a) — Wet Steam Property Calculation** **Given:** P = 12 bar, x = 0.9 **Step 1 — Steam Table Values at 12 bar (from KTU-supplied steam table):** | Property | Value | |---|---| | $T_{sat}$ | 188.0 °C | | $h_f$ | 798.6 kJ/kg | | $h_g$ | 2784.8 kJ/kg | | $h_{fg}$ | 1986.2 kJ/kg | | $v_g$ | 0.1633 m³/kg | | $s_f$ | 2.2159 kJ/kg·K | | $s_g$ | 6.2966 kJ/kg·K | | $s_{fg}$ | 4.0807 kJ/kg·K | **Step 2 — Specific Enthalpy:** $$h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg} = 798.6 + 0.9 \times 1986.2 = 798.6 + 1787.58 = 2586.18 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 3 — Specific Volume:** $$v = x \cdot v_g = 0.9 \times 0.1633 = 0.14697 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$ **Step 4 — Specific Entropy:** $$s = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg} = 2.2159 + 0.9 \times 4.0807 = 2.2159 + 3.6726 = 5.8885 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$ **Step 5 — Specific Internal Energy:** Converting P = 12 bar to **1200 kPa**: $$u = h - P \cdot v = 2586.18 - 1200 \times 0.14697 = 2586.18 - 176.36 = 2409.82 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Final Results:** $$\boxed{h = 2586.18 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad v = 0.1470 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}, \quad s = 5.8885 \text{ kJ/kg·K}, \quad u = 2409.82 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$ --- #### **Solution to Part (b) — Dryness Fraction & Heat Calculations** **Given:** P = 20 bar, h = 2600 kJ/kg, T_superheat = 350 °C **Step 1 — Steam Table Values at 20 bar:** | Property | Value | |---|---| | $h_f$ | 908.6 kJ/kg | | $h_g$ | 2799.5 kJ/kg | | $h_{fg}$ | 1890.9 kJ/kg | | $T_{sat}$ | 212.4 °C | **Step 2 — Dryness Fraction:** From $h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$: $$x = \frac{h - h_f}{h_{fg}} = \frac{2600 - 908.6}{1890.9} = \frac{1691.4}{1890.9}$$ $$\boxed{x = 0.8945}$$ **Step 3 — Heat to Convert Wet Steam to Dry Saturated Steam:** This is the **remaining latent heat**: $$Q_1 = h_g - h = h_g - (h_f + x \cdot h_{fg})$$ $$Q_1 = h_g - h_f - x \cdot h_{fg} = (1 - x) \cdot h_{fg}$$ $$Q_1 = (1 - 0.8945) \times 1890.9 = 0.1055 \times 1890.9$$ $$\boxed{Q_1 = 199.49 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$ **Step 4 — Heat to Superheat to 350 °C:** From superheated steam table at **20 bar, 350 °C**: - $h_{superheated} = 3137.0$ kJ/kg (approximate value from standard table) $$\Delta T_{sup} = 350 - 212.4 = 137.6 \text{ °C}$$ $$Q_2 = h_{superheated} - h_g = 3137.0 - 2799.5$$ $$\boxed{Q_2 = 337.5 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$ **Verification using average $C_p$ ≈ 2.1 kJ/kg·K:** $$Q_2 \approx 2.1 \times 137.6 = 288.96 \text{ kJ/kg (approximate)}$$ The exact value from the superheated table (337.5 kJ/kg) is preferred for KTU exams. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** With the help of a **Pressure-Enthalpy (P-h) diagram**, explain the constant pressure heat addition process in a steam power plant. Mark the following points on the diagram: (i) Saturated water line (ii) Saturated steam line (iii) Critical point (iv) Wet steam region (v) Superheated region **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A steam generator operates at **30 bar** and produces steam with a dryness fraction of **0.95**. If the steam flow rate is **5 kg/s**, calculate the total rate of heat transfer in the boiler per second. Take feedwater inlet temperature as **120 °C**. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Solution to Part (a) — P-h Diagram Explanation** **Definition of P-h Chart:** The Pressure-Enthalpy (P-h) diagram plots pressure (logarithmic) on the y-axis against specific enthalpy on the x-axis. It is the **Mollier diagram** variant. **Key Points on P-h Diagram:** 1. **Saturated Water Line (Left boundary):** Line connecting all states of saturated water at different pressures. **(1 Mark)** 2. **Saturated Steam Line (Right boundary):** Line connecting all states of dry saturated steam. The critical point (P_c = 221.2 bar, h_c = 2087.5 kJ/kg) lies at the top of this dome. **(1 Mark)** 3. **Critical Point:** The apex of the saturated liquid-vapor dome where the two phases become identical. **(1 Mark)** 4. **Wet Steam Region:** The area enclosed inside the dome where 0 < x < 1. **(1 Mark)** 5. **Superheated Region:** The area to the right of the saturated steam line, where T > T_sat. **(1 Mark)** **Constant Pressure Heat Addition Process (1→2→3→4):** - **1→2:** Sensible heating of feedwater (sub-cooled → saturated water). Heat = h_2 − h_1. - **2→3:** Latent heat addition (saturated water → wet steam → dry saturated steam). Heat = h_3 − h_2 = h_fg. - **3→4:** Superheating (dry saturated → superheated). Heat = h_4 − h_3. - **Total Heat:** $q_{in} = h_4 - h_1$. **(2 Marks)** --- #### **Solution to Part (b) — Heat Transfer Rate Calculation** **Given:** - $P = 30$ bar - $x = 0.95$ - $\dot{m} = 5$ kg/s - $T_{feedwater} = 120$ °C **Step 1 — Saturated Steam Table at 30 bar:** | Property | Value | |---|---| | $T_{sat}$ | 233.9 °C | | $h_f$ | 1008.4 kJ/kg | | $h_{fg}$ | 1795.7 kJ/kg | | $h_g$ | 2804.1 kJ/kg | **Step 2 — Specific Enthalpy of Generated Steam:** $$h_{steam} = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg} = 1008.4 + 0.95 \times 1795.7$$ $$h_{steam} = 1008.4 + 1705.92 = 2714.32 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 3 — Specific Enthalpy of Feedwater at 120 °C:** From feedwater (sub-cooled liquid) tables at T = 120 °C: $$h_{fw} \approx 503.7 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 4 — Heat Required per kg of Steam:** $$q = h_{steam} - h_{fw} = 2714.32 - 503.7 = 2210.62 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 5 — Total Rate of Heat Transfer:** $$\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \times q = 5 \times 2210.62$$ $$\boxed{\dot{Q} = 11053.1 \text{ kW} \approx 11.05 \text{ MW}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit Mismatch:** When calculating $u = h - P \cdot v$, students often forget to convert bar → kPa. Always remember: **1 bar = 100 kPa**. > 2. **Confusing h_f and h_g:** $h_f$ is the enthalpy of **saturated liquid** (water), and $h_g$ is the enthalpy of **saturated vapor** (steam). Swapping these yields wrong answers. > 3. **Using v_f in v calculation:** $v_f$ is typically $0.001$ m³/kg — negligible. Using the exact formula $v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg}$ is correct, but $v \approx x \cdot v_g$ is accepted. > 4. **Superheated vs Wet confusion:** If $T > T_{sat}$, do NOT use the wet steam formula. Read directly from the **superheated table**. > 5. **Forgetting to multiply by mass:** When asked for total heat, multiply the specific enthalpy by the total mass of steam. > 6. **Sign convention:** $h_{fg}$ is always **positive** (heat absorbed during vaporization). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fundamentals of Steam** - 🔹 **Steam** = gaseous water; the working fluid in Rankine cycles. - 🔹 **Three Stages of Steam Formation:** Sensible Heating → Latent Heat Addition → Superheating. - 🔹 **Dryness fraction (x):** Mass fraction of dry steam in wet steam; $0 \le x \le 1$. - 🔹 **Wet Steam Formulas:** $h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$, $v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg}$, $s = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg}$. - 🔹 **Superheated Steam:** Read directly from the superheated steam table at given (P, T). - 🔹 **Critical Point of Water:** $P_c = 221.2$ bar, $T_c = 374.15$ °C — above this, no latent heat. - 🔹 **Energy Chain:** $Q_{total} = h_{fw} + x \cdot h_{fg} + C_p \cdot \Delta T_{sup}$. - 🔹 **Steam Tables:** Always provided in KTU exam; memorize symbols, not numerical values. - 🔹 **Unit Conversion:** 1 bar = 100 kPa = 0.1 MPa. Always convert P to kPa when computing $u = h - P \cdot v$. - 🔹 **Saturation Condition:** If $T = T_{sat}$ at given P, steam is saturated; if $T > T_{sat}$, superheated. - 🔹 **Approximation:** $v \approx x \cdot v_g$ is acceptable because $v_f \ll v_g$. - 🔹 **Quality Limit:** x = 0 → saturated water; x = 1 → dry saturated steam. - 🔹 **Latent Heat Behavior:** $h_{fg}$ decreases with increase in pressure and becomes zero at critical point. - 🔹 **Mollier Diagram (h-s chart):** Used for advanced problems involving isentropic expansion in turbines. - 🔹 **Sensible Heat:** Heat that changes temperature (sensed by thermometer). - 🔹 **Latent Heat:** Heat that changes phase (temperature remains constant). - 🔹 **Turbine Inlet:** Steam is always superheated for high thermal efficiency and to avoid turbine blade erosion. - 🔹 **Condenser Outlet:** Wet steam with low dryness fraction (typically x < 0.5) — wet steam at low pressure. - 🔹 **Feedwater Heating:** Done in the economiser using waste flue gas heat (regenerative cycle).

topics.title: T-s Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET303 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** T-s Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **T-s Diagram (Temperature-Entropy Diagram):** A thermodynamic state-space chart in which the **absolute temperature (T)** is plotted on the **ordinate (y-axis)** in Kelvin, and the **specific entropy (s)** is plotted on the **abscissa (x-axis)** in kJ/kg·K. It is the fundamental graphical tool used to visualize the thermodynamic state of a pure substance (most notably water/steam) and to evaluate the **net heat transfer** of any closed or open system undergoing a reversible process, because the area enclosed under or between curves equals the heat exchanged.

For a pure substance like water, the T-s diagram is constructed using **saturation properties** from steam tables at varying pressures. The plot reveals three distinct regions separated by the **saturation dome (vapor dome)**:

1. **Subcooled (Compressed) Liquid Region** — left of the saturated liquid line.
2. **Saturated Liquid–Vapor Mixture (Wet) Region** — inside the dome, bounded by the saturated liquid line (left) and saturated vapor line (right).
3. **Superheated Vapor Region** — right of the saturated vapor line.

The **critical point (CP)** sits at the apex of the dome, marking the condition beyond which no distinct liquid–vapor phase boundary exists.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are tracking a long-distance cyclist's journey. The horizontal axis (entropy) is like the **distance traveled**, and the vertical axis (temperature) is the **altitude**. As the cyclist climbs a hill, the area beneath their altitude–distance path is the **total work done against gravity**.

Now, replace "altitude" with **temperature** and "distance" with **entropy** — the area under the T-s curve becomes the **heat added or rejected** by the working fluid. So a T-s diagram is essentially a "heat-accounting ledger" drawn visually. Tall-and-narrow regions = large heat transfer; flat regions = little heat transfer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** Always remember — the area under a reversible process curve on a T-s diagram is the heat transfer for that process. This is the single most important takeaway from this topic.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Universal Gas Constant:** R = **8.314 kJ/kmol·K**
- **Specific Gas Constant for Air:** R = **0.287 kJ/kg·K**
- **Specific Heat of Water (Liquid):** c_p ≈ **4.18 kJ/kg·K**
- **Reference Entropy:** Arbitrary (s = 0 at 0 K by the Third Law for pure crystalline substances)
- **Critical Point of Water:** T_cr = **647.1 K (373.95 °C)**, P_cr = **22.06 MPa**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why T-s and not P-v?

The **Second Law of Thermodynamics** (Clausius Inequality) states that for any process:

$$
dS \geq \frac{\delta Q}{T}
$$

For a **reversible process**, equality holds:

$$
dS = \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta Q_{rev} = T\,dS
$$

Integrating between states 1 and 2:

$$
Q_{rev} = \int_{1}^{2} T\,dS
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Because temperature (T) is the **integrand** and entropy (s) is the **variable of integration**, plotting T on the y-axis and s on the x-axis makes the heat transfer literally the area under the curve. No other commonly used diagram (P-v, h-s/Mollier) has this clean, direct visual interpretation for heat.

### 2.2 The Significance of Constant-Property Lines on the T-s Diagram

| Line Type | Shape on T-s Diagram | Mathematical Basis |
|---|---|---|
| **Isothermal (T = const)** | Horizontal straight line | $dS = \delta Q_{rev}/T \Rightarrow \delta Q_{rev} = T\,dS$ — heat is proportional to entropy change |
| **Isobaric (P = const)** | Curve rising to the right (slope = $(\partial T/\partial s)_P$) | $T\,ds = dh - v\,dP \Rightarrow T\,ds = dh$ at const P |
| **Isochoric (v = const)** | Curve with **steeper slope** than isobaric in superheated region | $T\,ds = du + P\,dv \Rightarrow T\,ds = du$ at const v |
| **Isentropic (s = const)** | **Vertical straight line** | $ds = 0$ — no heat transfer in reversible adiabatic (isentropic) process |
| **Saturation Dome** | Inverted-U curve, apex at critical point | Defined by saturated liquid ($x = 0$) and saturated vapor ($x = 1$) states |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Condition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\delta q_{rev} = T\,ds$ | Differential heat transfer (reversible) | kJ/kg |
| 2 | $q_{rev} = \int_{1}^{2} T\,ds$ | Finite reversible heat = area under T-s curve | kJ/kg |
| 3 | $ds = c_p \dfrac{dT}{T} - R\dfrac{dp}{p}$ | Ideal-gas entropy change (form 1) | kJ/kg·K |
| 4 | $ds = c_v \dfrac{dT}{T} + R\dfrac{dv}{v}$ | Ideal-gas entropy change (form 2) | kJ/kg·K |
| 5 | $s_2 - s_1 = c_p \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R \ln\!\left(\dfrac{p_2}{p_1}\right)$ | For ideal gas with const c_p | kJ/kg·K |
| 6 | $s_{fg} = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{T_{sat}}$ | Latent heat of vaporization / saturation temp | kJ/kg·K |
| 7 | $q = T \cdot \Delta s$ | Heat in isothermal reversible process | kJ/kg |
| 8 | $\Delta s_{universe} \geq 0$ | Second Law (irreversibility check) | kJ/kg·K |
| 9 | $q_{net} = \oint T\,ds$ | Net heat in a cycle = area of closed loop on T-s | kJ/kg |
| 10 | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{q_{out}}{q_{in}}$ | Thermal efficiency (any cycle) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Avoid Markdown Breaking:** When writing absolute values or divisions in tables above, `R \ln(p_2/p_1)` is used instead of `R ln \vert p_2/p_1 \vert` to preserve table integrity.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Steam Power Plants (Rankine Cycle):** T-s diagrams make the heat added in the boiler and the heat rejected in the condenser instantly visible as areas, enabling quick efficiency estimation.
- **Refrigeration & HVAC (Vapor Compression Cycle):** Identifies the actual COP and pinpoints irreversibilities.
- **Gas Turbine Analysis (Brayton Cycle):** T-s plot of the closed loop is more physically meaningful than P-v for evaluating compressor/ turbine work and heat exchanger performance.
- **Cryogenics & LNG Liquefaction:** T-s diagrams of multi-component refrigerants (e.g., mixed-refrigerant cycles) guide minimum work-of-liquefaction design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Area Under T-s Curve Equals Heat Transfer

**Starting Point (Clausius Equality for a Reversible Process):**

The Second Law of Thermodynamics for a closed system undergoing an internally reversible process gives the differential form:

$$
dS = \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}
$$

**Step 1 — Multiply both sides by T:**

Multiply both sides of the above equation by the absolute temperature T to isolate the heat term. This is the mathematical pivot that links T-s area to heat:

$$
\delta Q_{rev} = T \, dS
$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from state 1 to state 2:**

For a finite reversible process, integrate both sides between the initial state (s_1) and the final state (s_2):

$$
\int_{1}^{2} \delta Q_{rev} = \int_{s_1}^{s_2} T \, ds
$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the right-hand side as a geometric area:**

The right-hand integral $\int_{s_1}^{s_2} T\, ds$ is, by the definition of a definite integral, exactly the **area between the process curve, the s-axis, and the vertical lines s = s_1 and s = s_2** on the T-s diagram.

$$
Q_{rev} = \text{Area under the process curve on the T-s diagram}
$$

**Step 4 — Net heat for a closed cycle:**

For a complete cycle returning to its initial state ($\oint ds = 0$ for a property), the **net heat transfer** is the **enclosed area of the loop**:

$$
Q_{net,cycle} = \oint T \, ds
$$

If the cycle is traversed **clockwise** on a T-s diagram, $Q_{net} > 0$ (heat engine); if **counter-clockwise**, $Q_{net} < 0$ (refrigerator/heat pump).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is the single highest-weight KTU question on this topic. Practice writing it three times from memory.

### 3.2 Derivation: Entropy Change of an Ideal Gas

Starting from the **First Law** for a closed system (reversible, closed, neglecting KE/PE):

$$
\delta q = du + \delta w_{rev} = du + P\,dv
$$

**Step 1 — Express du in terms of T:**

For an ideal gas, internal energy depends only on temperature:

$$
du = c_v \, dT
$$

**Step 2 — Use the ideal-gas equation of state:**

Substitute $P = RT/v$ into the work term:

$$
P\,dv = \frac{RT}{v}dv
$$

**Step 3 — Combine and divide by T:**

$$
\delta q = c_v\, dT + \frac{RT}{v}dv
$$

Divide through by T to get the differential entropy change:

$$
\frac{\delta q}{T} = ds = c_v \frac{dT}{T} + R\frac{dv}{v}
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate (assuming constant c_v):**

$$
s_2 - s_1 = c_v \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) + R \ln\!\left(\frac{v_2}{v_1}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to a pressure form (using pv = RT):**

Dividing $v_2/v_1 = (T_2 p_1)/(T_1 p_2)$ and substituting yields the standard pressure form:

$$
s_2 - s_1 = c_p \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R \ln\!\left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU Board loves to combine both forms in a single sub-question worth 7 marks, so memorize both forms and the linkage via $c_p - c_v = R$.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Reading Common Processes on a T-s Diagram

| Process | Path on T-s | Heat Transfer Sign | Area Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Isothermal Expansion | Horizontal line, s increasing | Heat **IN** ($Q > 0$) | Rectangle: $q = T \cdot \Delta s$ |
| Isothermal Compression | Horizontal line, s decreasing | Heat **OUT** ($Q < 0$) | Rectangle: $q = T \cdot \Delta s$ |
| Isentropic Compression/Expansion | **Vertical** line | **Adiabatic reversible** ($Q = 0$) | No area enclosed with the s-axis |
| Isobaric Heating | Curve rising rightward (in superheat) | Heat IN | Area under curve |
| Polytropic ($pv^n$ = const) | General curved path | Heat = area under curve (sign depends on slope) | Evaluate by integration |
| Carnot Cycle | Rectangle: two isothermals + two isentropics | $Q_{in}$ = upper rectangle area, $Q_{out}$ = lower rectangle area | $\eta = 1 - T_L/T_H$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Anatomy of a T-s Diagram for Water

The physical shape of a T-s diagram is impossible to render with pure ASCII or basic Mermaid without specialized charting libraries. Instead, the following **Mermaid flowchart** maps the *functional architecture* — the regions, boundaries, and how to read the diagram — exactly as a KTU examiner expects a student to label it.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["ABSOLUTE TEMPERATURE T (K)"] --> node2["VERTICAL AXIS OF T-s DIAGRAM"]
    node3["SPECIFIC ENTROPY s (kJ per kg K)"] --> node4["HORIZONTAL AXIS OF T-s DIAGRAM"]
    node2 --> node5["REGION A: SUBCOOLED LIQUID s less than sf"]
    node2 --> node6["REGION B: WET SATURATED MIXTURE sf less than s less than sg"]
    node2 --> node7["REGION C: SUPERHEATED VAPOR s greater than sg"]
    node5 --> node8["LEFT BOUNDARY sf CURVE: SATURATED LIQUID LINE x equals 0"]
    node6 --> node9["DOME INTERIOR: TWO PHASE WITH QUALITY x"]
    node7 --> node10["RIGHT BOUNDARY sg CURVE: SATURATED VAPOR LINE x equals 1"]
    node8 --> node11["APEX: CRITICAL POINT Tcr 647.1 K Pcr 22.06 MPa"]
    node10 --> node11
    node9 --> node12["ISOTHERMAL LINE: HORIZONTAL CONST T PATH"]
    node9 --> node13["ISOBARIC LINE: SLOPED UPWARD CONST P PATH"]
    node9 --> node14["ISENTROPIC LINE: VERTICAL CONST s PATH"]
    node9 --> node15["AREA UNDER T-s CURVE: REVERSIBLE HEAT TRANSFER Q rev"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Reading Process Areas (Heat = Area)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP1["STATE 1: s1 T1"] --> nodeP2["PROCESS 1 to 2: REVERSIBLE"]
    nodeP2 --> nodeP3["STATE 2: s2 T2"]
    nodeP2 --> nodeP4["AREA UNDER CURVE: EQUALS Q rev FROM 1 TO 2"]
    nodeP4 --> nodeP5["SIGN RULE: CLOCKWISE LOOP = HEAT ENGINE, Qnet positive"]
    nodeP4 --> nodeP6["SIGN RULE: COUNTERCLOCKWISE LOOP = REFRIGERATOR, Qnet negative"]
    nodeP5 --> nodeP7["NET WORK OR HEAT equals AREA ENCLOSED BY LOOP"]
    nodeP6 --> nodeP7
```

### 4.3 Schematic Sketch (Hand-Drawn Reproduction Reference)

Although Mermaid cannot plot a smooth saturation dome, a student is expected to draw the following sketch by hand in the exam. The verbal description below is what should appear on paper:

> **Y-axis:** T (K), increasing upward, with two horizontal dashed lines marking the **critical temperature (647.1 K)** at the dome apex and a lower reference temperature.
>
> **X-axis:** s (kJ/kg·K), increasing rightward.
>
> **Saturation Dome:** An inverted-U starting at the saturated liquid line (steeply rising from the origin) curving over and meeting the saturated vapor line (gently rising) at the **critical point**. Label the left side as **Saturated Liquid** (x = 0) and the right side as **Saturated Vapor** (x = 1).
>
> **Interior of Dome:** Draw **horizontal constant-pressure** lines (isobars) and a **constant-temperature** line cutting across the dome as a horizontal chord — its length visually represents $s_{fg} = s_g - s_f$.
>
> **Outside the Dome — Superheat Region:** Draw two isobars (low pressure and high pressure) as curves rising rightward, with the high-pressure isobar steeper.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
**Define a T-s diagram. What is its principal thermodynamic significance?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A T-s diagram is a thermodynamic chart in which absolute temperature (T) is plotted on the vertical axis and specific entropy (s) on the horizontal axis. Its **principal significance** is that the **area under a reversible process curve** on this diagram **directly represents the heat transfer** for that process: $q_{rev} = \int T\,ds$. This makes it the most convenient diagram for the visual analysis of heat engines, refrigeration cycles, and any process where heat accounting is the primary concern. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
**State the Clausius equality. How does it justify the use of the T-s diagram as a heat-transfer chart?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Clausius equality states that for a reversible process, $dS = \delta Q_{rev}/T$, which rearranges to $\delta Q_{rev} = T\,dS$. Integrating, $Q_{rev} = \int T\,ds$, which is geometrically the area under the T-s curve. Hence, the T-s plot directly visualizes heat transfer as area, making it the standard "heat diagram" of thermodynamics. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

**(a) Derive the expression $Q_{rev} = \int_{1}^{2} T\,ds$ and explain why the area under the T-s curve represents heat transfer for a reversible process. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Start with the Clausius equality** (1 mark):

For any internally reversible process, the Second Law of Thermodynamics gives:

$$
dS = \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by T** (1 mark):

$$
\delta Q_{rev} = T \, dS
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate between states 1 and 2** (1 mark):

$$
Q_{rev} = \int_{1}^{2} T \, dS
$$

**Step 4 — Geometric interpretation** (2 marks):

The integral $\int_{s_1}^{s_2} T\,ds$ is, by the geometric definition of a definite integral, the **area bounded by the process curve, the entropy axis, and the two vertical ordinates at $s_1$ and $s_2$** on the T-s diagram. Hence, this area is the heat transferred in the reversible process from state 1 to state 2.

**Step 5 — Sign convention and physical meaning** (2 marks):

- If the process proceeds in the direction of **increasing entropy** (rightward), heat is **added** to the system ($Q > 0$).
- If entropy **decreases** (leftward), heat is **rejected** ($Q < 0$).
- For a **closed cycle**, the net heat is the area enclosed by the loop: clockwise → heat engine; counter-clockwise → refrigerator.

> **Award full 7 marks** for stating Clausius equality, the algebraic manipulation, the integral, the geometric interpretation, and the sign convention.

---

**(b) Sketch the T-s diagram of a Carnot vapor cycle and identify the heat addition, heat rejection, and isentropic processes. Why is its efficiency the maximum possible? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Sketch description (4 marks):**
The Carnot cycle on a T-s diagram appears as a **rectangle** operating between two temperatures $T_H$ and $T_L$, with its top horizontal side inside the **superheated** or **wet** region as the case demands:

- **Process 1 → 2 (Isothermal Heat Addition):** Horizontal line at $T = T_H$, s increasing from $s_1$ to $s_2$. Area under this line = $Q_{in} = T_H (s_2 - s_1)$.
- **Process 2 → 3 (Isentropic Expansion):** Vertical line downward at $s = s_2$, from $T_H$ to $T_L$.
- **Process 3 → 4 (Isothermal Heat Rejection):** Horizontal line at $T = T_L$, s decreasing from $s_2$ to $s_1$. Area = $Q_{out} = T_L (s_2 - s_1)$.
- **Process 4 → 1 (Isentropic Compression):** Vertical line upward at $s = s_1$, from $T_L$ back to $T_H$.

**Efficiency derivation (2 marks):**

$$
\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{T_L (s_2 - s_1)}{T_H (s_2 - s_1)} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}
$$

**Why maximum? (1 mark):**
The Carnot cycle is the only cycle operating between two thermal reservoirs that is **fully reversible** (no internal irreversibilities). By the Second Law, no heat engine operating between the same two reservoirs can exceed the efficiency of a reversible cycle, so $\eta_{Carnot}$ is the theoretical upper bound.

---

#### **Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**(a) With the help of a neat T-s diagram, show the constant pressure, constant volume, and constant temperature lines for water/steam and explain the shape of each. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Draw the saturation dome (1 mark):**
Draw the standard inverted-U saturation dome with the critical point at the apex. Identify subcooled, wet, and superheated regions.

**Step 2 — Constant temperature (isothermal) lines (2 marks):**
Inside the dome, isothermals are **horizontal straight lines**. The horizontal length of such a line equals $s_g - s_f$ at that temperature. Outside the dome (in superheated region), the isotherms **diverge slightly upward** from the isobars.

**Step 3 — Constant pressure (isobaric) lines (2 marks):**
Isobars are **curved lines rising to the right** in the superheated region. As pressure increases, the isobar becomes **steeper** and shifts left. Inside the dome, all isobars coincide with the constant-temperature line at the saturation temperature corresponding to that pressure.

**Step 4 — Constant volume (isochoric) lines (2 marks):**
Isometric (constant specific volume) lines are also curved and have a **steeper slope** than the corresponding isobars in the superheated region, because $T\,ds = c_v\,dT$ versus $T\,ds = c_p\,dT$ for an isobar at the same location. They are spaced more closely in the wet region because a small change in quality produces a large change in specific volume.

---

**(b) Two kilograms of air in a closed system undergo a reversible isothermal expansion from 200 kPa, 400 K to 100 kPa. Calculate the heat transferred, work done, and change in entropy. Take R = 0.287 kJ/kg·K. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given (1 mark):**
- m = 2 kg
- P_1 = 200 kPa, T_1 = T_2 = 400 K (isothermal)
- P_2 = 100 kPa
- R = 0.287 kJ/kg·K

**Step 1 — Entropy change (2 marks):**

$$
\Delta s = -R \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -0.287 \ln\!\left(\frac{100}{200}\right) = -0.287 \times (-0.6931) = 0.1989 \text{ kJ/kg·K}
$$

Total entropy change:

$$
\Delta S = m \cdot \Delta s = 2 \times 0.1989 = 0.3978 \text{ kJ/K}
$$

**Step 2 — Heat transferred (2 marks):**
For a reversible isothermal process of an ideal gas, $\Delta U = 0$, so by the First Law, $Q = W$. Also $q = T \cdot \Delta s$:

$$
Q = m \cdot T \cdot \Delta s = 2 \times 400 \times 0.1989 = 159.1 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Step 3 — Work done (2 marks):**
By First Law (isothermal ideal gas → $\Delta U = 0$):

$$
W = Q = 159.1 \text{ kJ}
$$

(Or, equivalently, $W = m R T \ln(P_1/P_2) = 2 \times 0.287 \times 400 \times \ln(2) = 159.1$ kJ — same answer.) **(7 marks total)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign of $Q$ in isothermal compression** — if entropy decreases, $Q$ is rejected, not added. Marks lost on sign.
> 2. **Using the wrong constant-specific-heat form** — for variable $c_p$, the integral $\int c_p/T\,dT$ must be used, not a flat $\ln(T_2/T_1)$ value.
> 3. **Confusing entropy change with heat transfer** — $\Delta S$ has units of kJ/K; $Q$ has units of kJ. They are NOT the same number.
> 4. **Sketching the saturation dome upside down** — the critical point is at the top apex, not the bottom. Easy 1-mark cut.
> 5. **Forgetting to multiply by mass** in entropy/heat problems — $\Delta S = m \cdot \Delta s$, not $\Delta s$ alone.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **T-s diagram** has T on the y-axis, s on the x-axis, with the **saturation dome** for pure substances.
- ✅ The **area under** any reversible process curve on a T-s diagram = **reversible heat transfer** $q_{rev} = \int T\,ds$.
- ✅ **Isothermal** processes are **horizontal** lines; **isentropic** processes are **vertical** lines.
- ✅ The **Carnot cycle** appears as a **rectangle** on the T-s diagram, with $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - T_L/T_H$.
- ✅ The **net heat of a closed cycle** equals the **enclosed loop area**: clockwise = heat engine, counter-clockwise = refrigerator.
- ✅ For an **ideal gas**: $ds = c_p dT/T - R dp/p$ (pressure form) **OR** $ds = c_v dT/T + R dv/v$ (volume form).
- ✅ For an **isothermal ideal-gas process**: $\Delta s = -R \ln(p_2/p_1) = R \ln(v_2/v_1)$.
- ✅ The **critical point** of water (T = 647.1 K, P = 22.06 MPa) is the apex of the saturation dome — beyond it, no phase distinction.
- ✅ The **slope of an isobar** on a T-s diagram: $(\partial T/\partial s)_P = T/c_p$.
- ✅ The **slope of an isochore** is steeper: $(\partial T/\partial s)_v = T/c_v$.
- ✅ Memorize the relation $c_p - c_v = R$ for ideal-gas quick conversions.
- ✅ **Drawing a neat, labelled T-s diagram in the exam** (with axes, dome, critical point, and process arrows) carries **2–3 easy marks** — do not skip the sketch.
- ✅ **Sign convention is sacred:** entropy increase ⇒ heat added; entropy decrease ⇒ heat rejected; vertical line ⇒ adiabatic reversible.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mollier Chart
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET303 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mollier Chart

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Mollier Chart** (also known as the **Enthalpy–Entropy diagram** or **h–s diagram**) is a thermodynamic chart in which **enthalpy (h)** is plotted as the ordinate (vertical axis) and **entropy (s)** as the abscissa (horizontal axis). It was developed by the German physicist **Richard Mollier (1904)** and is an indispensable graphical tool for the analysis of steam power plants, refrigeration cycles, and gas turbine processes.

The Mollier chart is preferred over the T–s diagram in steam engineering because the heat transferred during a steady-flow process at constant pressure is given directly by the change in enthalpy, i.e.,

$$q = \Delta h \quad \text{(at constant pressure)}$$

This allows the engineer to read **work output, work input, and heat transfer** as direct vertical distances on the chart.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Mollier Chart is the primary analytical tool used to evaluate the **Rankine Cycle** and its modifications (reheat, regenerative). Every KTU exam question on Rankine cycle analysis requires a sound command over Mollier chart operations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Thermodynamic Map"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: A Topographic Map of Steam States**
>
> Imagine the Mollier Chart as a **topographic map of a mountain landscape**, where:
> - The **saturation dome** resembles a mountain peak whose left slope is the **saturated liquid line** and right slope is the **saturated vapor line**.
> - The **critical point** is the **summit of the mountain** — the unique state where saturated liquid and saturated vapor become indistinguishable.
> - **Constant pressure (isobar) lines** are like **horizontal trails** running across the mountain.
> - **Constant temperature (isotherm) lines** are **steep switchback paths** climbing the mountain.
> - **Inside the dome (wet steam region)** is the **misty valley** where water and vapor coexist.
> - **Above the dome (superheated region)** is the **clear, dry sky**.
> - A **turbine expansion** is a **rock rolling down the mountain** (vertically downward, almost horizontal) — it loses "altitude" (enthalpy) while gaining "chaos" (entropy), thereby producing work.
> - A **pump compression** is a **rock being pushed slowly up the slope** (almost vertical, slight rightward drift) — gaining a small amount of altitude with very little entropy change.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Values (Memorize for KTU Exams):**
> - **Critical Pressure of Water:** $P_c = 22.064$ **MPa**
> - **Critical Temperature of Water:** $T_c = 374.15$ **°C (647.3 K)**
> - **Specific Gas Constant for Steam:** $R = 0.4615$ **kJ/(kg·K)**
> - **Specific Heat of Superheated Steam (approx.):** $C_p \approx 2.1$ **kJ/(kg·K)**

### 1.3 Why Enthalpy–Entropy and Not Temperature–Entropy?

The T–s diagram is a thermodynamic general-purpose diagram, but the Mollier chart is *engineer-centric*. The reasons are:

| Feature | T–s Diagram | Mollier Chart (h–s) |
|---|---|---|
| Heat at constant pressure | Area under curve (must integrate) | **Direct vertical distance** ($\Delta h$) |
| Turbine/Pump work | Indirect (requires $h$ from tables) | **Direct vertical distance** ($h_1 - h_2$) |
| Saturation dome visibility | Visible | Visible |
| Convenience for cycles | Moderate | **Excellent** |

This direct readability of work and heat makes the Mollier chart the **gold standard** for Rankine cycle, reheat, regeneration, and refrigeration cycle analysis in KTU examinations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Geometric Construction of the Mollier Chart

The Mollier chart for water/steam is constructed in the **h–s plane** with the following features:

**A. Axes**
- Vertical axis: Enthalpy $h$ in **kJ/kg**
- Horizontal axis: Entropy $s$ in **kJ/(kg·K)**
- The y-axis is **tilted** (rotated) in some Mollier charts used for psychrometrics, but in steam tables, it is conventionally a straight vertical axis.

**B. The Saturation Dome (Bell Curve)**
- Formed by joining all **saturated liquid states** ($h_f$, $s_f$) on the left and all **saturated vapor states** ($h_g$, $s_g$) on the right.
- The dome encloses the **wet steam region** where liquid and vapor phases coexist.
- The **peak of the dome** is the **critical point** $(h_c, s_c)$.
- **Left branch** (saturated liquid line): from triple point to critical point.
- **Right branch** (saturated vapor line): from triple point to critical point.

**C. Constant Pressure Lines (Isobars)**
- Inside the wet region, isobars are **horizontal straight lines** (because in wet region, $T$ and $P$ are linked, and $dh = T\,ds$ at constant $T$).
- In the superheated region, isobars **diverge** — they rise gently with a slope approximately equal to the saturation temperature at that pressure.
- Higher pressure isobars lie **above** lower pressure isobars.

**D. Constant Temperature Lines (Isotherms)**
- Inside the wet region, isotherms **coincide with isobars** (horizontal).
- In the superheated region, isotherms **diverge upward steeply**, with lower temperature isotherms lying below higher temperature isotherms.
- Isotherms in the superheated region have a slope greater than the isobars.

**E. Constant Dryness Fraction Lines (Constant Quality, $x$)**
- These are **straight lines** radiating from the critical point connecting points of equal quality inside the dome.
- Used to read dryness fraction graphically.

**F. Constant Volume Lines (Isochors)**
- Curve more steeply than isobars in the superheated region.
- Used for analyzing reciprocating compressor processes.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Why This Chart Works

**Step 1 — Starting Point (First Law for Open System):**
For a steady-flow process,
$$q - w_s = h_2 - h_1$$
where $w_s$ is the shaft work.

**Step 2 — Reversible Process (Second Law):**
For a reversible process, $T\,ds = dh - v\,dP$, so:
$$q_{rev} = \int T\,ds$$

**Step 3 — Constant Pressure Process:**
At $dP = 0$, $T\,ds = dh$, so $q = h_2 - h_1$. Heat is read directly as a **vertical distance** on the h–s diagram. This is the magical property that makes the Mollier chart superior for engineering analysis.

**Step 4 — Adiabatic Reversible (Isentropic) Process:**
$ds = 0$, so the process is **vertical** (straight up or down). Turbine and pump processes are typically modeled as isentropic in the ideal Rankine cycle.

**Step 5 — Reading Work:**
For a turbine/pump (adiabatic, steady-flow, $\Delta ke = \Delta pe = 0$):
$$W_T = h_1 - h_2 \quad \text{(turbine output)}$$
$$W_P = h_2 - h_1 \quad \text{(pump input)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** On the Mollier chart, **all work and heat quantities appear as vertical distances** between state points. This single fact is the most common answer-verification trick in KTU board papers.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | Mollier Chart Application |
|---|---|
| **Thermal Power Plants** | Rankine cycle, reheat cycle, regenerative feedwater heating analysis |
| **Nuclear Power Plants** | Moisture carryover analysis in low-pressure turbines |
| **Refrigeration & HVAC** | Two-stage compression, flash chamber, cascade cycle analysis |
| **Process Industries** | Steam distribution networks, de-superheater design |
| **Compressor Design** | Multi-stage compression with intercooling |
| **Marine Engineering** | Waste heat recovery cycles (Organic Rankine Cycle) |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $h = u + Pv$ | Fundamental enthalpy definition | kJ/kg |
| 2 | $T\,ds = dh - v\,dP$ | Combined 1st & 2nd law (Tds equation) | kJ/kg |
| 3 | $h = h_f + x\,h_{fg}$ | Enthalpy of wet steam | kJ/kg |
| 4 | $s = s_f + x\,s_{fg}$ | Entropy of wet steam | kJ/(kg·K) |
| 5 | $s_{fg} = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{T_{sat}}$ | Latent entropy relation (Clausius equality) | kJ/(kg·K) |
| 6 | $h = h_g + C_p (T - T_{sat})$ | Enthalpy of superheated steam (approx.) | kJ/kg |
| 7 | $s = s_g + C_p \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T}{T_{sat}}\right)$ | Entropy of superheated steam (approx., $P$ = const) | kJ/(kg·K) |
| 8 | $W_T = h_1 - h_2$ | Ideal turbine work output | kJ/kg |
| 9 | $W_P = v_f (P_2 - P_1)$ | Ideal pump work (incompressible liquid) | kJ/kg |
| 10 | $W_P = h_2 - h_1$ | Pump work via enthalpy (general) | kJ/kg |
| 11 | $q_{in} = h_1 - h_4$ | Heat added in boiler | kJ/kg |
| 12 | $q_{out} = h_3 - h_4'$ | Heat rejected in condenser (4' = saturated liquid at $P_3$) | kJ/kg |
| 13 | $x_2 = \dfrac{s_2 - s_f}{s_{fg}}$ | Dryness fraction at turbine exit | dimensionless |
| 14 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{q_{in}} = \dfrac{(h_1 - h_2) - (h_4 - h_3)}{h_1 - h_4}$ | Thermal efficiency of Rankine cycle | dimensionless |
| 15 | $\eta_{Rankine} = 1 - \dfrac{q_{out}}{q_{in}}$ | Alternate efficiency form | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention Note:** State numbers correspond to: 1 → Turbine inlet, 2 → Turbine exit, 3 → Pump inlet, 4 → Pump exit. The Mollier chart is used to read $h_1, h_2, h_3, h_4$ directly for cycle evaluation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why Heat at Constant Pressure Equals Change in Enthalpy

This is the foundational derivation that justifies the Mollier chart's superiority.

**Starting Point — First Law for Open System (Steady Flow):**

$$\dot{Q} - \dot{W}_s = \dot{m}\!\left[(h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)\right]$$

**Step 1 — Negligible kinetic and potential energy changes** (boilers, condensers, turbines satisfy this assumption in most KTU problems):

$$\dot{Q} - \dot{W}_s = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the constraint of a constant-pressure process.** In a boiler, the fluid flows through at essentially constant pressure. The only shaft work is shaft work, but for a boiler (no shaft), $\dot{W}_s = 0$:

$$\dot{Q} = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1)$$

**Step 3 — Divide by mass flow rate** to get specific quantities:

$$q = h_2 - h_1$$

**Step 4 — Conclude:** For a constant-pressure, steady-flow process with no work and negligible KE/PE changes, **heat transfer per unit mass equals the change in specific enthalpy.** Therefore, on the h–s chart, this is a **vertical distance**. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: The Slope of Isobars in the Superheated Region

**Step 1 — Start from the combined Tds equation:**

$$T\,ds = dh - v\,dP$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $dh$:**

$$dh = T\,ds + v\,dP$$

**Step 3 — At constant pressure** ($dP = 0$):

$$\left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial s}\right)_P = T$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:** The slope of an isobar on the h–s diagram at any point equals the **absolute temperature** at that point. This is why higher-temperature isobars (which lie above) are steeper than lower-temperature isobars in the superheated region.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Connection:** This derivation is frequently asked as a 3-mark question: *"Show that the slope of a constant pressure line on the Mollier chart equals the absolute temperature."*

---

### 3.3 Derivation: The Lever Rule for Dryness Fraction on the Mollier Chart

**Step 1 — Express the horizontal distance from the saturated liquid line to a wet steam state:**

At a given pressure (inside the dome), the entropy of wet steam is:
$$s = s_f + x\,s_{fg}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange:**

$$s - s_f = x\,s_{fg}$$

**Step 3 — Express in terms of enthalpies using the identity $s_{fg} = h_{fg}/T_{sat}$:**

$$s - s_f = \frac{x\,h_{fg}}{T_{sat}}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply both sides by $T_{sat}$:**

$$T_{sat}(s - s_f) = x\,h_{fg}$$

**Step 5 — Now examine the Mollier chart geometrically.** The horizontal distance from saturated liquid line to the wet steam state is $(s - s_f)$. The "altitude" of the dome at that pressure is $h_{fg}$. Inside the dome, the isobar is horizontal at height $h_f$, and the isotherm coincides.

The **distance (along a line of slope $T_{sat}$)** from the saturated liquid point to the state point equals $h - h_f$. Since $h = h_f + x\,h_{fg}$:

$$h - h_f = x\,h_{fg}$$

**Step 6 — Conclude (Lever Rule):** The dryness fraction equals the ratio of the segment from saturated liquid line to the state point, divided by the total height of the dome at that pressure:

$$\boxed{x = \frac{h - h_f}{h_{fg}} = \frac{\overline{\text{State to Sat. Liquid}}}{\overline{\text{Sat. Liquid to Sat. Vapor}}}}$$

This is the **lever rule** — it allows direct reading of dryness fraction from the Mollier chart. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.4 Worked Analytical Example: Rankine Cycle on Mollier Chart

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Steam enters a turbine at 3 MPa and 350 °C, expands isentropically to 10 kPa. Determine the dryness fraction at turbine exit, turbine work, pump work, heat added, and thermal efficiency using Mollier chart methodology.

**Given:**
- State 1 (turbine inlet): $P_1 = 3$ MPa, $T_1 = 350$ °C → Superheated
- $P_2 = P_3 = P_4 = 10$ kPa (condenser pressure)
- $P_4' = 10$ kPa, saturated liquid
- $P_3 = 10$ kPa, $P_4 = 3$ MPa
- Process 1-2: Isentropic expansion ($s_2 = s_1$)

**Step 1 — State 1 (Turbine Inlet):**
At 3 MPa, 350 °C (superheated steam from Mollier chart / steam tables):
- $h_1 \approx 2988.1$ kJ/kg
- $s_1 \approx 6.6940$ kJ/(kg·K)

**Step 2 — State 2 (Turbine Exit, isentropic):**
At $P_2 = 10$ kPa, with $s_2 = s_1 = 6.6940$ kJ/(kg·K).
At 10 kPa, $s_f = 0.6493$ kJ/(kg·K), $s_{fg} = 7.5010$ kJ/(kg·K), $s_g = 8.1503$ kJ/(kg·K).
Since $s_f < s_2 < s_g$, the state lies inside the dome (wet steam).

**Step 3 — Calculate Dryness Fraction at State 2:**

$$x_2 = \frac{s_2 - s_f}{s_{fg}} = \frac{6.6940 - 0.6493}{7.5010}$$

$$x_2 = \frac{6.0447}{7.5010} \approx 0.8059$$

**Step 4 — Calculate $h_2$:**
At 10 kPa: $h_f = 191.81$ kJ/kg, $h_{fg} = 2392.1$ kJ/kg.

$$h_2 = h_f + x_2 \cdot h_{fg} = 191.81 + 0.8059 \times 2392.1$$

$$h_2 = 191.81 + 1927.9 \approx 2119.7 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 5 — State 3 (Pump Inlet):**
Saturated liquid at 10 kPa:
$$h_3 = h_f = 191.81 \text{ kJ/kg}$$
$$v_3 = v_f = 0.001010 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

**Step 6 — State 4 (Pump Exit):**
Pump work (isentropic compression of liquid):

$$W_P = v_3 (P_4 - P_3) = 0.001010 \times (3000 - 10)$$

$$W_P = 0.001010 \times 2990 = 3.020 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$h_4 = h_3 + W_P = 191.81 + 3.020 = 194.83 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 7 — Cycle Performance:**

Turbine Work:
$$W_T = h_1 - h_2 = 2988.1 - 2119.7 = 868.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Pump Work:
$$W_P = h_4 - h_3 = 3.020 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Net Work:
$$W_{net} = W_T - W_P = 868.4 - 3.020 = 865.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Heat Added in Boiler:
$$q_{in} = h_1 - h_4 = 2988.1 - 194.83 = 2793.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Thermal Efficiency:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{q_{in}} = \frac{865.4}{2793.3} \approx 0.3098 \approx 30.98\%$$

**Step 8 — Verification with Mollier Chart:**
- $h_1$ read directly from superheated region at $(3 \text{ MPa}, 350°\text{C})$.
- Draw vertical line down (isentropic) to intersect 10 kPa isobar inside the dome.
- Read $h_2$ and $x_2$ at intersection.
- All heat and work quantities are vertical distances on the chart.

$\blacksquare$

---

### 3.5 Sensitivity Analysis Matrix

| Parameter Change | Effect on $h_1$ | Effect on $W_T$ | Effect on $\eta_{th}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Boiler Pressure ($P_1$) | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ (up to optimal $P_1$) |
| ↑ Turbine Inlet Temp ($T_1$) | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ |
| ↓ Condenser Pressure ($P_2$) | — | ↑ | ↑ |
| ↑ Moisture at exit (↓ $x_2$) | — | ↓ $W_T$ | ↓ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Trend:** The board is increasingly emphasizing **regenerative Rankine cycles** and **reheat cycles**, both of which require the Mollier chart to draw extraction lines, bleed points, and mixing chambers.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Mollier Chart — Schematic Block Map of Regions

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Type:** Mermaid block-level functional architecture mapping all regions, isobars, isotherms, and key features of the Mollier chart.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Title["MOLLIER CHART h-s DIAGRAM FOR STEAM"]:::title

    Title --> Dome["SATURATION DOME BELL CURVE"]:::dome

    Dome -->|Left Branch| SatLiq["Saturated Liquid Line hf sf curve"]:::left
    Dome -->|Right Branch| SatVap["Saturated Vapor Line hg sg curve"]:::right
    Dome -->|Peak| CritPt["CRITICAL POINT Pc=22.064 MPa Tc=374.15 C"]:::critical

    Dome --> WetReg["WET STEAM REGION INSIDE DOME"]:::wet
    Dome -->|Above| SuperReg["SUPERHEATED STEAM REGION"]:::super
    Dome -->|Below| CompReg["COMPRESSED LIQUID SUB COOLED REGION"]:::subcooled

    WetReg --> IsobarWet["Isobars are HORIZONTAL coinciding with isotherms"]:::feature
    WetReg --> ConstX["Constant Dryness Fraction Lines x=const radiate from Critical Point"]:::feature
    WetReg --> LeverRule["Lever Rule for x: x = h_minus_hf over h_fg"]:::feature

    SuperReg --> IsobarSuper["Isobars DIVERGE upward gently slope equals T"]:::feature
    SuperReg --> IsothermSuper["Isotherms DIVERGE upward STEEPLY"]:::feature
    SuperReg --> ConstV["Constant Volume Isochors curve steeper than isobars"]:::feature

    Subcooled --> SubNote["Almost vertical lines water slightly compressed"]:::feature

    SatLiq --> TriplePt["TRIPLE POINT 0.611 kPa 0.01 C"]:::triple

    CritPt --> RankineApp["APPLICATION Rankine Cycle Analysis"]:::app
    IsobarSuper --> RankineApp
    WetReg --> RankineApp

    RankineApp --> CycleLoop["State 1 Turbine Inlet superheated n State 2 Turbine Exit wet isentropic drop n State 3 Pump Inlet saturated liquid n State 4 Pump Exit compressed liquid n Back to State 1 in boiler"]:::app

    classDef title fill:#1a1a2e,color:#ffffff,stroke:#e94560,stroke-width:3px
    classDef dome fill:#ff6b6b,color:#ffffff,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:2px
    classDef left fill:#3498db,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:1px
    classDef right fill:#e74c3c,color:#ffffff,stroke:#922b21,stroke-width:1px
    classDef critical fill:#f39c12,color:#000000,stroke:#d35400,stroke-width:2px
    classDef wet fill:#85c1e9,color:#000000,stroke:#2874a6,stroke-width:1px
    classDef super fill:#fdebd0,color:#000000,stroke:#b9770e,stroke-width:1px
    classDef subcooled fill:#a9dfbf,color:#000000,stroke:#196f3d,stroke-width:1px
    classDef triple fill:#bb8fce,color:#ffffff,stroke:#6c3483,stroke-width:1px
    classDef feature fill:#fcf3cf,color:#000000,stroke:#7d6608,stroke-width:1px
    classDef app fill:#d5dbdb,color:#000000,stroke:#1b2631,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Rankine Cycle on the Mollier Chart — State Point Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Boil["BOILER P = P1 constant"]
        S4["State 4 h4 compressed liquid"]
        S1["State 1 h1 superheated steam"]
    end

    subgraph Turb["TURBINE s = s1 isentropic"]
        S1
        S2["State 2 h2 wet steam x2 less than 1"]
    end

    subgraph Cond["CONDENSER P = P2 constant"]
        S2
        S3["State 3 h3 saturated liquid"]
    end

    subgraph Pump["PUMP s nearly constant"]
        S3
        S4
    end

    S1 -->|h1 minus h2 WT turbine work| S2
    S2 -->|h2 minus h3 qout rejected| S3
    S3 -->|h4 minus h3 WP pump work| S4
    S4 -->|h1 minus h4 qin heat added| S1

    classDef state fill:#fdebd0,color:#000000,stroke:#7d6608,stroke-width:2px
    classDef proc fill:#d6eaf8,color:#000000,stroke:#1f618d,stroke-width:1px
    class S1,S2,S3,S4 state
    class Boil,Turb,Cond,Pump proc
```

### 4.3 Reading the Chart — Decision Tree for Dry Steam vs Wet Steam

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Given State: P and T or h"]:::start
    Start --> LookSat["Find Tsat at given P from saturation tables"]:::step
    LookSat --> CompareT{"Compare T with Tsat"}:::decision

    CompareT -->|"T greater than Tsat"| Super["SUPERHEATED STEAM Use superheated tables h1 s1 directly"]:::super
    CompareT -->|"T equals Tsat"| SatVap["SATURATED VAPOR DRY SATURATED h = hg s = sg"]:::satvap
    CompareT -->|"T less than Tsat"| Sub["SUBCOOLED COMPRESSED LIQUID Approximate as saturated liquid at T"]:::sub

    SatVap --> End["Apply Lever Rule x = 1.0 if wet region needed"]:::end
    Super --> End
    Sub --> End

    classDef start fill:#f9e79f,color:#000000,stroke:#7d6608,stroke-width:2px
    classDef step fill:#aed6f1,color:#000000,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:1px
    classDef decision fill:#f5b7b1,color:#000000,stroke:#78281f,stroke-width:2px
    classDef super fill:#fdebd0,color:#000000,stroke:#b9770e,stroke-width:1px
    classDef satvap fill:#e74c3c,color:#ffffff,stroke:#922b21,stroke-width:2px
    classDef sub fill:#a9dfbf,color:#000000,stroke:#196f3d,stroke-width:1px
    classDef end fill:#d5dbdb,color:#000000,stroke:#1b2631,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to Use These Diagrams in KTU Exams:**
> 1. The **Mollier chart architecture diagram** is for **conceptual understanding** — useful for 3-mark "Explain the Mollier chart" questions.
> 2. The **Rankine cycle state point flow** diagram explains the **closed loop** of states and identifies which processes are isobaric, isentropic, and isothermal.
> 3. The **decision tree** is the **algorithm you must follow** when given a state to classify (saturated/wet/superheated) — a common first step in numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
**"What is a Mollier chart? Why is it preferred over the T-s diagram for steam power cycle analysis?" (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Mollier chart is a thermodynamic chart in which **enthalpy (h)** is plotted on the vertical axis against **entropy (s)** on the horizontal axis. It is used to represent the states of a working substance (commonly water/steam) and analyze thermodynamic processes.

**Reasons for Preference over T-s diagram:**

1. **Direct Reading of Work and Heat:** In the h-s diagram, the heat transferred at constant pressure equals the **change in enthalpy**, which appears as a **direct vertical distance** ($q = h_2 - h_1$). In the T-s diagram, heat is read as the **area under the curve**, requiring integration.

2. **Direct Reading of Turbine/Pump Work:** Shaft work in adiabatic devices appears as a **vertical distance** between inlet and outlet states ($W_T = h_1 - h_2$). This eliminates intermediate calculations involving internal energy.

3. **Engineering Convenience:** In a Rankine cycle, the work output, work input, and heat input can all be read off the chart as **vertical segments**, making cycle evaluation **faster and more visual** during design.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often write "Mollier chart has more accuracy" — this is **incorrect**. Both charts are based on the same steam tables. The advantage is **convenience and direct readability**, not accuracy.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
**"Explain the significance of the saturation curve (dome) on the Mollier chart. Name the regions formed by it." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **saturation curve (dome)** is a bell-shaped curve formed by joining the saturated liquid states (left branch) and saturated vapor states (right branch) on the Mollier chart. Its **peak** is the **critical point** of water.

**Significance:**
- It separates the **single-phase regions** from the **two-phase (wet) region**.
- It defines the **boundary** beyond which the substance cannot exist as a mixture of liquid and vapor at equilibrium.

**Three Regions Formed:**

1. **Wet Steam Region (Inside the Dome):** Two-phase mixture of liquid and vapor. Dryness fraction $0 \le x \le 1$. Isobars and isotherms coincide (horizontal lines).

2. **Superheated Steam Region (Above the Dome):** Steam at temperature higher than saturation temperature. Isobars and isotherms diverge; isotherms are steeper.

3. **Compressed Liquid / Sub-cooled Liquid Region (Below the Dome):** Water at pressure higher than saturation pressure corresponding to its temperature. Approximated as saturated liquid at the given temperature for engineering accuracy.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse "sub-cooled liquid" with "saturated liquid." Saturated liquid lies **on the left branch of the dome**; sub-cooled liquid lies **to the left of the dome** at lower enthalpy/entropy.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A** — [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model Paper]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and main features of the Mollier chart for steam. Mention the significance of constant pressure, constant temperature, constant volume, and constant dryness fraction lines. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In a Rankine cycle, steam enters the turbine at 30 bar and 350 °C, and the condenser pressure is 0.1 bar. Assuming isentropic expansion in the turbine and isentropic compression in the pump, determine using Mollier chart:
   - (i) Quality of steam at turbine exit
   - (ii) Work output of the turbine per kg of steam
   - (iii) Work input to the pump per kg of steam
   - (iv) Heat supplied in the boiler per kg of steam
   - (v) Thermal efficiency of the cycle **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Features of the Mollier Chart (7 Marks)**

The Mollier chart is constructed in the **enthalpy–entropy (h-s) plane** with:
- **Vertical axis:** Enthalpy $h$ in kJ/kg
- **Horizontal axis:** Entropy $s$ in kJ/(kg·K)

**Main Features:**

1. **Saturation Dome (Bell Curve):**
   - Left branch: Saturated liquid line ($h_f, s_f$)
   - Right branch: Saturated vapor line ($h_g, s_g$)
   - Peak: Critical point at $P_c = 22.064$ MPa, $T_c = 374.15$ °C

2. **Constant Pressure Lines (Isobars):**
   - In wet region: Horizontal (coincide with isotherms)
   - In superheated region: Gently rising curves; slope equals absolute temperature

3. **Constant Temperature Lines (Isotherms):**
   - In wet region: Coincide with isobars
   - In superheated region: Rise steeply; higher temperature isotherms are above lower temperature ones

4. **Constant Volume Lines (Isochors):**
   - Curve more steeply than isobars in superheated region
   - Important for compressor analysis

5. **Constant Dryness Fraction Lines (Constant $x$):**
   - Straight lines radiating from the critical point
   - Used to read dryness fraction via lever rule

**Significance:**
- **Isobars** (constant P) — Represent processes in boilers and condensers.
- **Isotherms** (constant T) — Useful for analyzing heat exchangers.
- **Isochors** (constant v) — Used for reciprocating compressor processes.
- **Constant $x$ lines** — For determining moisture content in low-pressure turbine stages.

---

**(b) Rankine Cycle Numerical (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $P_1 = 30$ bar = 3 MPa, $T_1 = 350$ °C
- $P_2 = P_3 = 0.1$ bar = 10 kPa
- Processes 1-2 and 3-4 are isentropic

**Step 1 — State 1 (Turbine Inlet):**
At 3 MPa, 350 °C (superheated steam from Mollier chart / steam tables):
- $h_1 = 2988.1$ kJ/kg
- $s_1 = 6.6940$ kJ/(kg·K)

**Step 2 — State 2 (Turbine Exit, isentropic, $s_2 = s_1$):**
At 10 kPa (from steam tables):
- $s_f = 0.6493$ kJ/(kg·K)
- $s_{fg} = 7.5010$ kJ/(kg·K)
- $h_f = 191.81$ kJ/kg
- $h_{fg} = 2392.1$ kJ/kg

**(i) Quality of steam at turbine exit:**

$$x_2 = \frac{s_2 - s_f}{s_{fg}} = \frac{6.6940 - 0.6493}{7.5010} = \frac{6.0447}{7.5010} = 0.8059$$

**Step 3 — State 3 (Pump Inlet, saturated liquid at 10 kPa):**
$$h_3 = h_f = 191.81 \text{ kJ/kg}$$
$$v_3 = v_f = 0.001010 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

**Step 4 — State 4 (Pump Exit):**
$$W_P = v_3 (P_4 - P_3) = 0.001010 \times (3000 - 10) = 3.02 \text{ kJ/kg}$$
$$h_4 = h_3 + W_P = 191.81 + 3.02 = 194.83 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**(ii) Work output of turbine:**

First compute $h_2$:
$$h_2 = h_f + x_2 \cdot h_{fg} = 191.81 + 0.8059 \times 2392.1 = 191.81 + 1927.88 = 2119.7 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$W_T = h_1 - h_2 = 2988.1 - 2119.7 = 868.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**(iii) Work input to pump:**

$$W_P = 3.02 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**(iv) Heat supplied in boiler:**

$$q_{in} = h_1 - h_4 = 2988.1 - 194.83 = 2793.27 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**(v) Thermal efficiency:**

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_T - W_P}{q_{in}} = \frac{868.4 - 3.02}{2793.27} = \frac{865.38}{2793.27} = 0.3098 = 30.98\%$$

**Summary Table:**

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Quality at turbine exit | $x_2$ | 0.8059 |
| Turbine work | $W_T$ | 868.4 kJ/kg |
| Pump work | $W_P$ | 3.02 kJ/kg |
| Heat added | $q_{in}$ | 2793.27 kJ/kg |
| Thermal efficiency | $\eta_{th}$ | 30.98% |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to compute $h_2$** — many students directly write $W_T = h_1 - h_2$ without first finding $h_2$ from $x_2$. This loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Using $h_g$ instead of $h_{fg}$** in $h_2 = h_f + x_2 h_{fg}$ — this is a fatal error. Always use $h_{fg} = h_g - h_f$, NOT $h_g$.
> 3. **Sign convention for pump work** — write $W_P = h_4 - h_3$ (positive value) and subtract in net work formula, OR write $W_P = h_3 - h_4$ (negative value) and add. Be consistent.
> 4. **Mixing units** — pressures must be in **kPa or bar consistently**; enthalpies in **kJ/kg**; entropy in **kJ/(kg·K)**.

---

#### **QUESTION B** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model Paper] (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Derive the expression for the slope of a constant pressure line on the Mollier chart. State its physical significance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Steam at a pressure of 15 bar and 0.95 dryness fraction is throttled to a pressure of 1.5 bar in a throttling calorimeter. Find the quality of steam at the exit of the calorimeter. If this steam is further expanded isentropically in a nozzle to a pressure of 0.2 bar, find the final dryness fraction. Use Mollier chart / steam tables. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Slope of Isobar on Mollier Chart (7 Marks)**

**Starting Point — Combined First and Second Law (Tds Equation):**

For a pure substance undergoing a reversible process:

$$T\,ds = dh - v\,dP$$

**Step 1 — Solve for $dh$:**

$$dh = T\,ds + v\,dP$$

**Step 2 — At constant pressure** ($dP = 0$):

$$dh = T\,ds$$

**Step 3 — Therefore, the slope of the constant pressure line on the h-s diagram is:**

$$\left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial s}\right)_P = T$$

**Physical Significance:**
- The slope of any isobar at a given state on the Mollier chart **equals the absolute temperature** of the substance at that state.
- This is why isobars in the **superheated region diverge** (slope $\approx$ high T), while isobars in the **wet region are flat** (slope = saturation temperature, but they are horizontal because the isotherm coincides).
- In the superheated region, higher-pressure isobars have **gentler slopes** (lower $T_{sat}$), while lower-pressure isobars have **steeper effective curvature** when traced from wet to superheated.

---

**(b) Throttling and Isentropic Expansion Problem (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- State 1 (before throttling): $P_1 = 15$ bar, $x_1 = 0.95$
- State 2 (after throttling): $P_2 = 1.5$ bar
- State 3 (after nozzle): $P_3 = 0.2$ bar
- Throttling is isenthalpic: $h_2 = h_1$
- Nozzle expansion is isentropic: $s_3 = s_2$

**Step 1 — State 1:**
At 15 bar: $h_f = 844.84$ kJ/kg, $h_{fg} = 1947.3$ kJ/kg
$$h_1 = h_f + x_1 h_{fg} = 844.84 + 0.95 \times 1947.3 = 844.84 + 1849.94 = 2694.78 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Also:
$$s_f = 2.3147 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}, \quad s_{fg} = 4.1280 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$$
$$s_1 = s_f + x_1 s_{fg} = 2.3147 + 0.95 \times 4.1280 = 2.3147 + 3.9216 = 6.2363 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$$

**Step 2 — State 2 (After Throttling):**
For throttling, $h_2 = h_1 = 2694.78$ kJ/kg.
At 1.5 bar: $h_f = 467.11$ kJ/kg, $h_{fg} = 2226.5$ kJ/kg, $h_g = 2693.6$ kJ/kg, $s_f = 1.4336$, $s_{fg} = 5.7897$ kJ/(kg·K).

Since $h_2 \approx h_g$ at 1.5 bar (within 0.04%), the steam is **nearly dry saturated**. To be precise:

$$x_2 = \frac{h_2 - h_f}{h_{fg}} = \frac{2694.78 - 467.11}{2226.5} = \frac{2227.67}{2226.5} = 1.0005$$

**Interpretation:** Steam is **superheated** at 1.5 bar (slightly, because $x_2 > 1$).

We need $s_2$. Since it's slightly superheated, we approximate using the superheated steam tables at 1.5 bar, finding the state with $h = 2694.78$ kJ/kg. From superheated tables (interpolating between $T_{sat} = 111.4$ °C and higher temperatures), the entropy is approximately $s_2 \approx 7.297$ kJ/(kg·K).

**Step 3 — State 3 (After Isentropic Expansion in Nozzle):**
At $P_3 = 0.2$ bar: $s_f = 0.8320$ kJ/(kg·K), $s_{fg} = 7.0766$ kJ/(kg·K), $s_g = 7.9086$ kJ/(kg·K), $h_f = 251.40$ kJ/kg, $h_{fg} = 2357.5$ kJ/kg.

Since $s_3 = s_2 = 7.297$ kJ/(kg·K) and $s_f < s_3 < s_g$, the state is **wet steam**.

**Quality at state 3:**

$$x_3 = \frac{s_3 - s_f}{s_{fg}} = \frac{7.297 - 0.8320}{7.0766} = \frac{6.465}{7.0766} = 0.9136$$

**Final Answer:**
- **Quality after throttling (state 2):** Steam is **superheated** at 1.5 bar with $h_2 = 2694.78$ kJ/kg (slightly above $h_g = 2693.6$ kJ/kg at 1.5 bar).
- **Quality after isentropic expansion (state 3):** $x_3 = 0.9136$ (91.36% dry).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Throttling ≠ Isothermal:** Many students assume temperature remains constant during throttling. This is **wrong**. Pressure drops, enthalpy is constant, temperature may increase or decrease depending on the initial state. In wet steam throttling, temperature usually **increases** because moisture evaporates.
> 2. **Sign of entropy change in throttling:** Throttling is **irreversible**, so entropy **increases**: $s_2 > s_1$. Do not write $s_2 = s_1$ in a throttling process — that's a common confusion with isentropic nozzle flow.
> 3. **Nozzle process IS isentropic (ideal).** Do not confuse the two processes.
> 4. **Numerical values vary** by ±5% depending on the steam tables edition used. KTU accepts any standard steam table.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mollier Chart — Key Facts to Memorize Before KTU Exam**

- **Definition:** Chart of enthalpy (y-axis) vs entropy (x-axis), developed by Richard Mollier in 1904 for steam analysis.

- **Three Regions:**
  1. **Wet steam** — inside the dome ($0 \le x \le 1$), isobars and isotherms coincide (horizontal).
  2. **Superheated steam** — above the dome, isobars and isotherms diverge.
  3. **Sub-cooled liquid** — to the left of the dome, approximated as saturated liquid.

- **Critical Point:** $P_c = 22.064$ MPa, $T_c = 374.15$ °C, $h_c \approx 2087.6$ kJ/kg, $s_c \approx 4.4120$ kJ/(kg·K).

- **Slope of Isobar:** $\left(\dfrac{\partial h}{\partial s}\right)_P = T$ (absolute temperature).

- **Constant $x$ lines:** Radiate from the **critical point** — this is the **geometric key** for the lever rule.

- **Lever Rule:** $x = \dfrac{h - h_f}{h_{fg}} = \dfrac{s - s_f}{s_{fg}}$.

- **Work Formulas (Direct from Mollier Chart):**
  - Turbine work: $W_T = h_1 - h_2$ (vertical drop on chart).
  - Pump work: $W_P = h_4 - h_3 = v_f(P_4 - P_3)$ (small vertical rise).
  - Heat added: $q_{in} = h_1 - h_4$ (vertical rise in boiler).
  - Heat rejected: $q_{out} = h_2 - h_3$ (vertical drop in condenser).

- **Mollier chart is read right-to-left for the Rankine cycle:** State 1 (top right, superheated) → State 2 (inside dome, wet) → State 3 (left branch, saturated liquid) → State 4 (slightly right of 3, compressed liquid) → back to State 1.

- **Common KTU Questions:**
  - Why slope of isobar = T? (Derive from $T\,ds = dh - v\,dP$)
  - Find $x$ at turbine exit (use lever rule).
  - Rankine cycle efficiency (read all 4 enthalpies from chart).
  - Throttling + nozzle combined problem.
  - Compare efficiencies with and without superheat.

- **Watch for Unit Consistency:** Pressures in **kPa or bar**; enthalpies in **kJ/kg**; entropy in **kJ/(kg·K)**. Convert before substituting into formulas.

- **KTU 2024 Trends:** Expect a **reheat cycle** or **regenerative Rankine cycle** question with Mollier chart, or a **throttling calorimeter** problem to determine dryness fraction of wet steam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steam Power Cycles (Rankine Cycle)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET303 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Steam Power Cycles (Rankine Cycle)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Rankine Cycle** is the idealized thermodynamic cycle that describes the operation of a **vapour-compression steam power plant**. It serves as the fundamental analytical model for converting heat energy from a high-temperature source into mechanical work using water/steam as the working fluid.

In the KTU 2024 framework, the cycle is defined as a **reversible thermodynamic cycle** comprising four sequential processes executed on a closed-loop working fluid (water). The reference working fluid is a **pure substance (H₂O)** that undergoes phase changes between liquid and vapour phases during the cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition:**
> *"The Rankine cycle is the ideal cycle for vapour power plants, consisting of a reversible isentropic compression in a pump, a constant-pressure heat addition in a boiler, a reversible isentropic expansion in a turbine, and a constant-pressure heat rejection in a condenser."*

### Standard State Points of the Rankine Cycle

| State Point | Location | Physical Description | Typical Phase |
|:-----------:|:---------|:---------------------|:-------------|
| **1** | Pump Inlet / Condenser Outlet | Saturated liquid at low pressure | Liquid |
| **2** | Pump Outlet / Boiler Inlet | Compressed liquid at high pressure | Sub-cooled liquid |
| **3** | Turbine Inlet / Boiler Outlet | High-energy vapour (saturated or superheated) | Vapour / Superheated steam |
| **4** | Turbine Outlet / Condenser Inlet | Low-pressure wet vapour (mixture) | Wet vapour |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Elevator"

Imagine a **hydraulic elevator system** in a tall building that uses water instead of cables:

1. **Pump (Liquid Lifter):** Just as a small electric pump pushes water from a ground-level reservoir to a rooftop tank, the Rankine cycle's **feedwater pump** uses relatively little energy to compress cold water to high pressure.
2. **Boiler (Heating Tower):** At the rooftop, a heater boils the water into high-energy steam, similar to how solar collectors on a roof heat water. The fluid absorbs **heat input ($Q_{in}$)** at constant pressure.
3. **Turbine (Energy Discharge):** The high-pressure steam rushes down through a turbine, spinning blades and generating electricity — like water falling through a hydroelectric generator produces power.
4. **Condenser (Cooling Basin):** The low-pressure exhaust steam enters a cooling chamber where it condenses back to liquid, releasing **waste heat ($Q_{out}$)** to the environment — analogous to the rooftop water cooling back into a holding tank.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** The Rankine cycle is preferred over the Carnot cycle for steam plants because the liquid pump handles an *incompressible* fluid (very low work), whereas the Carnot cycle would require compressing a two-phase mixture — an engineering nightmare!

---

## 1.3 The Four Processes at a Glance

The Rankine cycle operates through the following reversible processes:

- **Process 1 → 2:** Isentropic compression in the **feedwater pump** (constant entropy).
- **Process 2 → 3:** Isobaric (constant pressure) heat addition in the **steam generator/boiler**.
- **Process 3 → 4:** Isentropic expansion in the **steam turbine** (constant entropy).
- **Process 4 → 1:** Isobaric (constant pressure) heat rejection in the **condenser**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Constants Used in Rankine Cycle Analysis:**
> - Specific enthalpy of saturated liquid: $h_f$ in **kJ/kg**
> - Latent heat of vaporization: $h_{fg}$ in **kJ/kg**
> - Specific volume of saturated liquid: $v_f$ in **m³/kg**
> - Steam quality at turbine exit: $x_4$ (dimensionless, $0 < x_4 < 1$)
> - Standard atmospheric pressure: **101.325 kPa**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Processes — Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

### Process 1 → 2: Isentropic Compression (Feedwater Pump)

**What happens physically:**
