Cold saturated liquid water leaving the condenser at low pressure ($P_1$) is pressurized to the high boiler pressure ($P_2$) by a centrifugal or reciprocating pump. Because the liquid is nearly **incompressible**, the temperature rise is very small (typically 1 °C), and the process closely follows an **isentropic** (constant-entropy) path.

**Why it matters:**
Although pump work is small (≈ 1–3% of turbine work), ignoring it in KTU board solutions results in loss of accuracy marks. Use the incompressible-fluid approximation:

$$
W_P = h_2 - h_1 \approx v_1 \cdot (P_2 - P_1)
$$

Where $v_1 = v_f$ at the condenser pressure (specific volume of saturated liquid).

---

### Process 2 → 3: Constant-Pressure Heat Addition (Boiler)

**What happens physically:**
Compressed liquid water enters the boiler, where it is first heated to saturation temperature (**sensible heating**), then evaporated (**latent heating** at constant temperature for a pure substance), and optionally superheated beyond saturation temperature. Heat is transferred from an external source (coal, nuclear, solar, biomass) at **constant pressure**.

**Energy equation:**

$$
Q_{in} = h_3 - h_2
$$

This is the dominant positive energy term in the cycle and the primary input to the system.

---

### Process 3 → 4: Isentropic Expansion (Steam Turbine)

**What happens physically:**
High-pressure, high-temperature steam expands through the turbine, dropping from boiler pressure ($P_3$) to condenser pressure ($P_4$). The enthalpy drop is converted into **shaft work** that drives an electric generator. The exhaust typically lies in the **wet vapour region**.

**Turbine work output:**

$$
W_T = h_3 - h_4
$$

For the ideal Rankine cycle, the expansion is isentropic, meaning $s_3 = s_4$.

---

### Process 4 → 1: Constant-Pressure Heat Rejection (Condenser)

**What happens physically:**
Wet steam from the turbine enters the condenser, where cooling water or air removes the latent heat, returning the fluid to a saturated liquid state at $P_1$ and $T_1$. The condensate is then ready to be pumped back into the boiler.

**Heat rejected:**

$$
Q_{out} = h_4 - h_1
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Realization:** State 1 corresponds to **saturated liquid at condenser pressure** (quality $x_1 = 0$), so $h_1 = h_{f@P_1}$.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Key Assumption |
|:-:|:---------|:--------|:------|:---------------|
| 1 | Pump Work | $W_P = h_2 - h_1 = v_1(P_2 - P_1)$ | kJ/kg | Incompressible liquid, $v_1 = v_f$ |
| 2 | Turbine Work | $W_T = h_3 - h_4$ | kJ/kg | Isentropic expansion |
| 3 | Net Work Output | $W_{net} = W_T - W_P$ | kJ/kg | — |
| 4 | Heat Added (Boiler) | $Q_{in} = h_3 - h_2$ | kJ/kg | Constant pressure process |
| 5 | Heat Rejected (Condenser) | $Q_{out} = h_4 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | Constant pressure process |
| 6 | **Thermal Efficiency** | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \dfrac{(h_3 - h_4) - v_1(P_2 - P_1)}{h_3 - h_2}$ | — | Dimensionless, $0 < \eta < 1$ |
| 7 | Work Ratio (Back Work Ratio inverse) | $WR = \dfrac{W_{net}}{W_T} = 1 - \dfrac{W_P}{W_T}$ | — | Indicates cycle quality |
| 8 | Specific Steam Consumption (SSC) | $SSC = \dfrac{3600}{W_{net}}$ | kg/kWh | Lower is better |
| 9 | Specific Fuel Consumption | $SFC = \dfrac{3600}{\eta_{th} \cdot \text{CV}}$ | kg/kWh | Depends on calorific value |
| 10 | Quality at Turbine Exit | $x_4 = \dfrac{s_3 - s_f}{s_{fg}} \big\vert_{P_4}$ | — | Using $s_3 = s_4$ |

---

## 2.3 Practical Engineering Utility

The Rankine cycle is the **backbone of global electricity generation**:

- **Coal-fired thermal power plants** (most common in India: NTPC, KSEB stations)
- **Nuclear power plants** (PWR, BWR designs)
- **Concentrated Solar Power (CSP)** with molten-salt heat transfer
- **Geothermal binary plants** using organic Rankine fluids (ORC)
- **Combined-cycle plants** (Rankine bottoming cycle + Brayton topping cycle)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Relevance:** Real steam power plants deviate from the ideal Rankine cycle due to **turbine irreversibilities, pressure drops in boiler tubes, and heat losses**. The actual efficiency is modelled using an **isentropic efficiency** factor $\eta_T$ for the turbine, typically 0.85–0.92 in modern plants.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Thermal Efficiency of the Ideal Rankine Cycle

We apply the **First Law of Thermodynamics** (steady-flow energy equation, neglecting KE and PE) to each component and then sum the work and heat terms.

### Step 1 — Energy Balance Across the Pump (Process 1 → 2)

Applying the steady-flow energy equation (SFEE) for an adiabatic, reversible pump:

$$
h_1 + W_{P,in} = h_2
$$

Rearranging for the work *input* to the pump:

$$
W_{P,in} = h_2 - h_1
$$

Since the liquid is treated as incompressible, the pump work per unit mass is:

$$
W_{P,in} = \int_1^2 v \, dP \approx v_1 (P_2 - P_1)
$$

**Why the approximation?** During the pump process, the specific volume of subcooled liquid remains essentially constant, so it is brought outside the integral.

---

### Step 2 — Energy Balance Across the Boiler (Process 2 → 3)

The boiler receives heat $Q_{in}$ from the external source:

$$
h_2 + Q_{in} = h_3
$$

Solving for heat input:

$$
Q_{in} = h_3 - h_2
$$

This is the **total enthalpy rise** of the working fluid across the boiler.

---

### Step 3 — Energy Balance Across the Turbine (Process 3 → 4)

For an adiabatic, reversible (isentropic) turbine:

$$
h_3 = h_4 + W_{T,out}
$$

Turbine work output per unit mass:

$$
W_{T,out} = h_3 - h_4
$$

For the ideal (isentropic) turbine, the exit state is found using:

$$
s_3 = s_4
$$

At the condenser pressure, this determines the quality:

$$
x_4 = \frac{s_3 - s_{f@P_4}}{s_{fg@P_4}}
$$

And therefore:

$$
h_4 = h_{f@P_4} + x_4 \cdot h_{fg@P_4}
$$

---

### Step 4 — Energy Balance Across the Condenser (Process 4 → 1)

The condenser rejects heat to the cooling medium:

$$
h_4 = h_1 + Q_{out}
$$

Hence:

$$
Q_{out} = h_4 - h_1
$$

---

### Step 5 — Net Work and Thermal Efficiency

The net work output per kilogram of working fluid is:

$$
W_{net} = W_{T,out} - W_{P,in} = (h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)
$$

The cycle thermal efficiency is defined as the ratio of net work output to total heat input:

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{(h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)}{h_3 - h_2}
$$

Using the pump-work approximation $h_2 - h_1 = v_1(P_2 - P_1)$:

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{(h_3 - h_4) - v_1 (P_2 - P_1)}{h_3 - h_2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Form (most-used KTU board expression):**
> $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{h_4 - h_1}{h_3 - h_2}$$
> This is derived by recognizing $Q_{in} - Q_{out} = W_{net}$, so $\eta_{th} = 1 - Q_{out}/Q_{in}$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

**Problem:**
A steam power plant operates on the ideal Rankine cycle. Steam enters the turbine at **3 MPa** and **350 °C** (state 3), and is condensed in the condenser at **10 kPa** (state 1). Determine:
1. The pump work
2. The turbine work
3. The heat added in the boiler
4. The thermal efficiency

**Given:** $P_3 = 3$ MPa, $T_3 = 350$ °C, $P_1 = 10$ kPa

### Step 1 — Properties at State 3 (Superheated Steam, 3 MPa, 350 °C)

From standard superheated steam tables:

$$
h_3 = 2988.1 \; \text{kJ/kg}, \quad s_3 = 6.7421 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}
$$

### Step 2 — Properties at State 1 (Saturated Liquid, 10 kPa)

From saturated water tables at $P_1 = 10$ kPa:

$$
h_1 = h_f = 191.81 \; \text{kJ/kg}, \quad s_1 = 0.6492 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}
$$
$$
v_1 = v_f = 0.001010 \; \text{m}^3/\text{kg}
$$

### Step 3 — Pump Work Calculation

$$
W_P = v_1 (P_2 - P_1) = 0.001010 \times (3000 - 10) \; \text{kPa}
$$

$$
W_P = 0.001010 \times 2990 = 3.0199 \; \text{kJ/kg}
$$

Enthalpy at state 2:

$$
h_2 = h_1 + W_P = 191.81 + 3.02 = 194.83 \; \text{kJ/kg}
$$

### Step 4 — State 4 (Isentropic Expansion to 10 kPa)

Since the expansion is isentropic:

$$
s_4 = s_3 = 6.7421 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}
$$

At $P_4 = 10$ kPa from saturation tables:

$$
s_f = 0.6492 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}, \quad s_{fg} = 7.5010 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}
$$

Quality of exhaust steam:

$$
x_4 = \frac{s_4 - s_f}{s_{fg}} = \frac{6.7421 - 0.6492}{7.5010} = 0.8131
$$

Enthalpy at state 4:

$$
h_4 = h_f + x_4 \cdot h_{fg} = 191.81 + 0.8131 \times 2392.1
$$

$$
h_4 = 191.81 + 1944.79 = 2136.60 \; \text{kJ/kg}
$$

### Step 5 — Net Work and Heat Quantities

Turbine work:

$$
W_T = h_3 - h_4 = 2988.1 - 2136.6 = 851.5 \; \text{kJ/kg}
$$

Net work:

$$
W_{net} = W_T - W_P = 851.5 - 3.02 = 848.48 \; \text{kJ/kg}
$$

Heat input in boiler:

$$
Q_{in} = h_3 - h_2 = 2988.1 - 194.83 = 2793.27 \; \text{kJ/kg}
$$

Heat rejected in condenser:

$$
Q_{out} = h_4 - h_1 = 2136.6 - 191.81 = 1944.79 \; \text{kJ/kg}
$$

### Step 6 — Thermal Efficiency

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{848.48}{2793.27} = 0.3038 = 30.38\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Verification Check:** $Q_{in} - Q_{out} = W_{net}$ → $2793.27 - 1944.79 = 848.48$ ✓

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | Carnot Cycle (Steam) | Ideal Rankine Cycle | Actual Rankine Cycle |
|:----------|:--------------------:|:-------------------:|:--------------------:|
| Pump handles | Two-phase mixture | Subcooled liquid | Subcooled liquid |
| Pump work | Very high | Very low | Low |
| Turbine inlet | Saturated vapour | Superheated/saturated | Superheated |
| Turbine exhaust | Saturated liquid | Wet mixture | Wet mixture |
| Cycle shape on T-s | Two isotherms + two isentropes | Two isobars + two isentropes | Irreversible polygon |
| Practical feasibility | **Impractical** (pump cavitation) | **Practical** | Actual plants |
| Typical efficiency | 35–40% (ideal) | 30–38% (ideal) | 28–35% (real) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Insight:** The Rankine cycle sacrifices a small amount of thermodynamic efficiency (compared to Carnot) to gain enormous **engineering practicality** — the pump never has to handle a two-phase mixture.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Rankine Cycle Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    boiler["BOILER\nHeat Source Q_in"]
    turbine["STEAM TURBINE\nW_T out"]
    condenser["CONDENSER\nQ_out Rejection"]
    pump["FEEDWATER PUMP\nW_P in"]
    gen["GENERATOR\nElectrical Output"]
    condPump["COOLING WATER PUMP"]
    cwSource["COOLING TOWER\nOR RIVER"]
    fuel["FUEL SUPPLY\nCoal / Nuclear / Solar"]
    state1["STATE 1\nSaturated Liquid\nP1 low, x=0"]
    state2["STATE 2\nCompressed Liquid\nP2 high, T slightly higher"]
    state3["STATE 3\nSuperheated Steam\nP2 high, T3 high"]
    state4["STATE 4\nWet Steam\nP1 low, x4 lt 1"]

    fuel --> boiler
    boiler -- "h3" --> turbine
    turbine -- "h4" --> condenser
    condenser -- "h1" --> pump
    pump -- "h2" --> boiler
    turbine -.-> gen
    cwSource --> condPump
    condPump --> condenser

    state1 --> pump
    state2 --> boiler
    state3 --> turbine
    state4 --> condenser
```

---

## 4.2 State-Point Cycle Mapping (T-s and P-v Schematic Equivalent)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "T-s DIAGRAM REPRESENTATION"
        direction LR
        sAxis["Entropy s (kJ/kgK)"]
        dome["Saturation Dome\n(Two-phase region)"]
        s1["Point 1\nSaturated Liquid\nLow P, Low s"]
        s2["Point 2\nSubcooled Liquid\nHigh P, ~same s"]
        s3["Point 3\nSuperheated Steam\nHigh P, High s"]
        s4["Point 4\nWet Mixture\nLow P, High s"]
        proc12["1 to 2: Isentropic Compression\n(s = constant, P rises)"]
        proc23["2 to 3: Isobaric Heat Addition\n(P = constant, T and s rise)"]
        proc34["3 to 4: Isentropic Expansion\n(s = constant, P falls)"]
        proc41["4 to 1: Isobaric Heat Rejection\n(P = constant, T and s fall)"]
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Energy Flow Block Diagram (Sankey-Style Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    fuelEnergy["FUEL CHEMICAL ENERGY\n100 percent input"]
    qin["Q_in to Boiler\n~90 percent of fuel"]
    qstack["Stack Loss & Flue Gas\n~10 percent"]
    wnet["W_net Mechanical\n~30 percent of Q_in"]
    qout["Q_out to Condenser\n~60 percent of Q_in"]
    otherLoss["Boiler Losses\nPump Friction\nTurbine Losses"]
    electric["ELECTRICAL OUTPUT\nGenerator"]

    fuelEnergy --> qin
    fuelEnergy --> qstack
    qin --> wnet
    qin --> qout
    qin --> otherLoss
    wnet --> electric
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The Mermaid charts above are *block-level functional equivalents* of the classical T-s, P-v, and Sankey diagrams. In a KTU board exam sketch, you should redraw these as standard thermodynamic charts with axes labelled and the saturation dome clearly shown.

---

## 4.4 Heat Engine Schematic (Steam Power Plant)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T_H["HIGH TEMP RESERVOIR T_H (Boiler Furnace)"]
        furnace["Furnace / Heat Source\n~1500 to 1800 C"]
    end
    subgraph ENGINE["HEAT ENGINE (Steam Cycle)"]
        cycle["Rankine Cycle\nQ_in absorbed\nW_net produced\nQ_out rejected"]
    end
    subgraph T_L["LOW TEMP RESERVOIR T_L (Cooling Water)"]
        cooling["Cooling Tower / River\n~25 to 30 C"]
    end
    furnace -- "Q_in" --> cycle
    cycle -- "Q_out" --> cooling
    cycle -- "W_net" --> gen2["Shaft Work Output"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Note for Board Exam:** Always label state points (1, 2, 3, 4) **clockwise on the T-s diagram** and indicate the direction of processes with arrows. The area enclosed by the cycle on the T-s diagram is *not* equal to net work (unlike for the Carnot cycle where two sides are isothermal).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Rankine Cycle. List its four processes. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Rankine cycle** is the ideal thermodynamic cycle for a vapour-compression steam power plant in which a pure working fluid (water) undergoes phase change between liquid and vapour to convert heat into work.

The four processes are:

| Process | Component | Type | Description |
|:-------:|:---------:|:----:|:------------|
| 1 → 2 | Pump | Isentropic compression | Liquid compressed from low to high pressure |
| 2 → 3 | Boiler | Constant pressure heat addition | Liquid converted to superheated/saturated vapour |
| 3 → 4 | Turbine | Isentropic expansion | Vapour expands producing shaft work |
| 4 → 1 | Condenser | Constant pressure heat rejection | Vapour condensed back to saturated liquid |

---

### Question 2: Why is the Rankine cycle preferred over the Carnot cycle for steam power plants? `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The Carnot cycle, although the most efficient, is **impractical for steam plants** for the following reasons:

1. **Wet Compression Problem:** In the Carnot cycle, the pump must compress a *two-phase mixture* (wet vapour), which causes **pump cavitation, erosion, and very low volumetric efficiency**.
2. **Isothermal Heat Transfer:** Real boilers cannot provide truly isothermal heat addition at constant temperature; the Rankine cycle's constant-pressure heat addition is more practical.
3. **Turbine Blade Erosion:** Carnot's isothermal expansion would require the turbine exhaust to be saturated liquid, meaning extremely low-quality steam and excessive blade erosion.

The Rankine cycle resolves these by placing the pump in the **liquid region** (state 1 → 2), making the compression work negligible and the components mechanically feasible.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive an expression for the thermal efficiency of an ideal Rankine cycle. State all the assumptions clearly.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A steam power plant operates on the ideal Rankine cycle. Steam enters the turbine at **30 bar, 400 °C** and the condenser operates at **0.1 bar**. Determine:
1. Turbine work, pump work, and net work per kg of steam.
2. Heat added in the boiler and heat rejected in the condenser.
3. Thermal efficiency and work ratio.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Assumptions of the Ideal Rankine Cycle:**
1. All processes are internally reversible.
2. The pump and turbine operate isentropically.
3. No pressure drop in the boiler and condenser (constant pressure heat transfer).
4. No heat loss from piping (boiler, turbine, condenser are adiabatic except for $Q_{in}$ and $Q_{out}$).
5. The working fluid is a pure substance (water).
6. Kinetic and potential energy changes are negligible.

**Derivation:**

Applying SFEE to each component:

Pump (1 → 2): $\;W_P = h_2 - h_1 \approx v_1 (P_2 - P_1)$

Boiler (2 → 3): $\;Q_{in} = h_3 - h_2$

Turbine (3 → 4): $\;W_T = h_3 - h_4$

Condenser (4 → 1): $\;Q_{out} = h_4 - h_1$

Net work:

$$
W_{net} = W_T - W_P = (h_3 - h_4) - v_1 (P_2 - P_1)
$$

Thermal efficiency:

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{(h_3 - h_4) - v_1 (P_2 - P_1)}{h_3 - h_2}
$$

Equivalently:

$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{h_4 - h_1}{h_3 - h_2}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $P_3 = 30 \; \text{bar} = 3 \; \text{MPa}$, $T_3 = 400 \; °\text{C}$, $P_1 = 0.1 \; \text{bar} = 10 \; \text{kPa}$

**State 1 (Saturated liquid at 0.1 bar):**

From steam tables:
- $h_1 = h_f = 191.81 \; \text{kJ/kg}$
- $v_1 = v_f = 0.001010 \; \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$
- $s_f = 0.6492 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}$, $s_{fg} = 7.5010 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}$
- $h_{fg} = 2392.1 \; \text{kJ/kg}$

**State 3 (Superheated steam at 3 MPa, 400 °C):**

- $h_3 = 3230.7 \; \text{kJ/kg}$
- $s_3 = 6.9211 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}$

**State 2 (Pump exit):**

$$
W_P = v_1 (P_2 - P_1) = 0.001010 \times (3000 - 10)
$$

$$
W_P = 0.001010 \times 2990 = 3.02 \; \text{kJ/kg}
$$

$$
h_2 = h_1 + W_P = 191.81 + 3.02 = 194.83 \; \text{kJ/kg}
$$

**State 4 (Isentropic expansion to 0.1 bar):**

$$
s_4 = s_3 = 6.9211 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}
$$

$$
x_4 = \frac{s_4 - s_f}{s_{fg}} = \frac{6.9211 - 0.6492}{7.5010} = 0.8366
$$

$$
h_4 = 191.81 + 0.8366 \times 2392.1 = 191.81 + 2001.34 = 2193.15 \; \text{kJ/kg}
$$

**1) Work Calculations:**

Turbine work:

$$
W_T = h_3 - h_4 = 3230.7 - 2193.15 = 1037.55 \; \text{kJ/kg}
$$

Pump work:

$$
W_P = 3.02 \; \text{kJ/kg}
$$

Net work:

$$
W_{net} = 1037.55 - 3.02 = 1034.53 \; \text{kJ/kg}
$$

**2) Heat Calculations:**

$$
Q_{in} = h_3 - h_2 = 3230.7 - 194.83 = 3035.87 \; \text{kJ/kg}
$$

$$
Q_{out} = h_4 - h_1 = 2193.15 - 191.81 = 2001.34 \; \text{kJ/kg}
$$

**3) Efficiency and Work Ratio:**

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{1034.53}{3035.87} = 0.3407 = 34.07\%
$$

$$
\text{Work Ratio} = \frac{W_{net}}{W_T} = \frac{1034.53}{1037.55} = 0.9971
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Check:** $Q_{in} - Q_{out} = W_{net}$ → $3035.87 - 2001.34 = 1034.53$ ✓

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] With the help of a T-s and P-v diagram, explain the working of an ideal Rankine cycle. Compare it with the Carnot cycle.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A steam power plant operates on the ideal Rankine cycle. The boiler pressure is **2 MPa** and the condenser pressure is **20 kPa**. The steam is superheated to a temperature of **300 °C**. Calculate:
1. The thermal efficiency.
2. The mass flow rate of steam required for a net power output of **10 MW**.
3. The specific steam consumption (SSC).

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**T-s Diagram Description:**

The T-s diagram of the ideal Rankine cycle consists of:
- **Process 1 → 2:** Vertical line (isentropic) inside the compressed liquid region, very close to the saturated liquid line.
- **Process 2 → 3:** A curve extending from compressed liquid through the two-phase dome into the superheated region at constant pressure $P_2 = P_3$.
- **Process 3 → 4:** Vertical line (isentropic) from the superheated region down into the wet vapour region.
- **Process 4 → 1:** A curve inside the two-phase dome at constant pressure $P_1 = P_4$.

**P-v Diagram Description:**

The P-v diagram shows:
- **Process 1 → 2:** Vertical line (incompressible liquid, $v$ constant).
- **Process 2 → 3:** A curve at constant high pressure; volume expands significantly during vaporization.
- **Process 3 → 4:** Curve of decreasing pressure with increasing volume.
- **Process 4 → 1:** Constant low pressure line with volume decreasing as condensation occurs.

**Comparison with Carnot Cycle:**

| Feature | Carnot Cycle | Rankine Cycle |
|:--------|:------------:|:-------------:|
| Pump work | Large (two-phase) | Negligible (subcooled liquid) |
| Practicality | Not practical for steam | Highly practical |
| T-s shape | Two isotherms + two isentropes | Two isobars + two isentropes |
| Heat addition | Isothermal (T = const) | Isobaric (P = const) |
| Heat rejection | Isothermal | Isobaric |
| Theoretical efficiency | Higher | Slightly lower |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $P_2 = P_3 = 2 \; \text{MPa}$, $P_1 = P_4 = 20 \; \text{kPa}$, $T_3 = 300 \; °\text{C}$, $\dot{W}_{net} = 10 \; \text{MW}$

**State 3 (Superheated steam at 2 MPa, 300 °C):**
- $h_3 = 3023.5 \; \text{kJ/kg}$
- $s_3 = 6.7664 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}$

**State 1 (Saturated liquid at 20 kPa):**
- $h_1 = h_f = 251.42 \; \text{kJ/kg}$
- $v_1 = v_f = 0.001017 \; \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$
- $s_f = 0.8320 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}$, $s_{fg} = 7.0766 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}$
- $h_{fg} = 2357.5 \; \text{kJ/kg}$

**Pump Work:**

$$
W_P = 0.001017 \times (2000 - 20) = 0.001017 \times 1980 = 2.01 \; \text{kJ/kg}
$$

$$
h_2 = 251.42 + 2.01 = 253.43 \; \text{kJ/kg}
$$

**State 4 (Isentropic expansion to 20 kPa):**

$$
x_4 = \frac{6.7664 - 0.8320}{7.0766} = \frac{5.9344}{7.0766} = 0.8386
$$

$$
h_4 = 251.42 + 0.8386 \times 2357.5 = 251.42 + 1976.79 = 2228.21 \; \text{kJ/kg}
$$

**Work and Heat Quantities:**

$$
W_T = h_3 - h_4 = 3023.5 - 2228.21 = 795.29 \; \text{kJ/kg}
$$

$$
W_{net} = 795.29 - 2.01 = 793.28 \; \text{kJ/kg}
$$

$$
Q_{in} = h_3 - h_2 = 3023.5 - 253.43 = 2770.07 \; \text{kJ/kg}
$$

**1) Thermal Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{793.28}{2770.07} = 0.2864 = 28.64\%
$$

**2) Mass Flow Rate:**

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{\dot{W}_{net}}{W_{net}} = \frac{10 \times 10^3 \; \text{kW}}{793.28 \; \text{kJ/kg}} = 12.605 \; \text{kg/s}
$$

**3) Specific Steam Consumption:**

$$
SSC = \frac{3600}{W_{net}} = \frac{3600}{793.28} = 4.538 \; \text{kg/kWh}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Where Students Lose Marks):**
> 1. **Forgetting pump work:** Many students write $\eta_{th} = (h_3 - h_4)/(h_3 - h_2)$ ignoring the small pump term. While the numerical effect is tiny, KTU board solutions require the **complete expression** including $v_1(P_2 - P_1)$. Marks are awarded for showing the correct formula.
> 2. **Wrong state-1 quality:** Students sometimes assume $h_1 = 0$ or use $h_g$ instead of $h_f$ at the condenser pressure. State 1 is **saturated liquid** ($x_1 = 0$), so $h_1 = h_f$.
> 3. **Confusing direction of process on T-s:** In the T-s diagram, the boiler and condenser processes are *curved* (not isothermal) — they should not be drawn horizontal.
> 4. **Pressure unit inconsistency:** Always convert bar ↔ kPa ↔ MPa consistently: $1 \; \text{bar} = 100 \; \text{kPa} = 0.1 \; \text{MPa}$.
> 5. **Missing units in final answer:** The board examiner deducts marks if the final answer has no unit (e.g., "efficiency = 30.38" without the % sign is incomplete).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Rankine Cycle (Module 2):**

- ☐ The Rankine cycle has **four processes**: pump (1-2), boiler (2-3), turbine (3-4), condenser (4-1).
- ☐ Pump and turbine processes are **isentropic**; boiler and condenser processes are **isobaric**.
- ☐ **State 1** = saturated liquid at condenser pressure ($x_1 = 0$, $h_1 = h_f$).
- ☐ **Pump work formula:** $W_P = v_1(P_2 - P_1)$ — derived from incompressible liquid assumption.
- ☐ **State 3** is read directly from superheated steam tables for given $P_3$ and $T_3$.
- ☐ **State 4 quality** found from $x_4 = (s_3 - s_f)/s_{fg}$ evaluated at $P_4 = P_1$.
- ☐ **Net work:** $W_{net} = (h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)$.
- ☐ **Thermal efficiency:** $\eta_{th} = 1 - (h_4 - h_1)/(h_3 - h_2)$.
- ☐ **Work ratio** (high = good): $WR = W_{net}/W_T = 1 - W_P/W_T$.
- ☐ **SSC** (lower = better): $SSC = 3600/W_{net}$ in kg/kWh.
- ☐ Rankine cycle is **practically preferred** over Carnot cycle because pump handles only liquid.
- ☐ The **area enclosed on T-s diagram** does not equal net work for the Rankine cycle (it does for Carnot).
- ☐ **Always draw** the T-s and P-v (or flow schematic) diagrams in board answers — marks are reserved for these.
- ☐ **Energy balance check:** $Q_{in} - Q_{out} = W_{net}$ must always be satisfied — use it to verify calculations.
- ☐ Common parameters: typical $\eta_{th} \approx 30\text{–}35\%$ for ideal cycle, $30\text{–}38\%$ for superheated cases.
- ☐ **Pressure conversions to memorize:** $1 \; \text{bar} = 100 \; \text{kPa} = 10^5 \; \text{Pa}$, $1 \; \text{MPa} = 10 \; \text{bar}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET303 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Modified Rankine Cycle

---

# Modified Rankine Cycle — KTU 2024 Scheme Study Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Modified Rankine Cycle (MRC):** A practical thermodynamic vapour power cycle in which the **basic Rankine cycle** (1-2-3-4-1) is augmented with engineering modifications — namely **Superheating**, **Reheating**, and **Regenerative Feed-Water Heating** — to enhance the **thermal efficiency (η_th)**, control the **moisture content at turbine exhaust**, and improve the **overall work output per kg of steam circulated**.

The idealised basic Rankine cycle on a **T-s diagram** consists of:

- **1 → 2** : Isentropic compression (Pump)
- **2 → 3** : Isobaric heat addition (Boiler)
- **3 → 4** : Isentropic expansion (Turbine)
- **4 → 1** : Isobaric heat rejection (Condenser)

In the **modified** version, additional components and states are introduced.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE] > **Think of it like upgrading a basic two-wheeler to a sports bike.** A basic Rankine cycle is like a plain engine — it works, but it wastes fuel and overheats. By adding **Superheating** (forcing more heat into the steam), **Reheating** (giving steam a second energy boost before final expansion), and **Regenerative Heating** (recycling some of the exhaust heat to pre-warm the incoming water), we essentially "turbocharge" the cycle to extract more work from the same fuel input, while keeping the blades safe from excessive moisture erosion.

---

### 1.3 Why Modifications Are Needed

| Limitation of Basic Cycle | Modification Introduced | Engineering Benefit |
|---|---|---|
| Excessive moisture in low-pressure stages causes **blade erosion** | **Superheating** | Steam becomes highly superheated, dryness ≥ 0.88 maintained |
| Average temperature of heat addition is low | **Reheating** | Shifts expansion to higher mean temperature → ↑ η_th |
| Feedwater enters boiler at low temperature, demanding more fuel | **Regenerative FWH** | Bleed steam pre-warms feedwater → ↓ Q_in to boiler |
| Large irreversibility at boiler inlet | **Economiser** (pre-heater coil) | Reduces thermal stresses, saves fuel |

---

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard condenser pressure:** **0.1 bar (10 kPa)** for large power plants.
- **Maximum permissible moisture content at turbine exhaust:** **≤ 12 %** (dryness fraction ≥ 0.88).
- **Typical boiler exit temperature (superheated):** **540 °C – 600 °C**.
- **Reheat pressure (optimum):** **20 % – 25 % of initial pressure**.
- **Universal Gas Constant R:** **8.314 J/mol·K**.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Modifications — Structured Logic Flow

#### **A. Superheating of Steam**

The steam leaving the boiler saturation dome is further heated **at constant pressure** in a superheater until it reaches a temperature **above saturation temperature (T_sup > T_sat)**.

**Process chain on T-s diagram:**

1. **2 → 2′** : Feedwater pre-heating (economiser coil)
2. **2′ → 3** : Phase change at constant T_sat (evaporator)
3. **3 → 3″** : Superheating (state 3″ is the turbine inlet)

**Why superheat?** Because if dry saturated steam expands isentropically in the turbine, the exit state falls **inside the wet dome**, with moisture content typically 25 %–30 %. Such moisture:

- Causes **erosion of blades** (water droplets impacting at > 100 m/s).
- Reduces turbine efficiency due to **condensation shock losses**.

Superheating raises the turbine-inlet enthalpy and pushes the isentropic expansion line upward on the T-s plane, **keeping the final state inside the dome or above it**.

#### **B. Reheating of Steam**

Steam is partially expanded in a **High-Pressure (HP) turbine** to an intermediate pressure (typically 1/4 of initial pressure). It is then sent back to the boiler's **reheater**, where it is again superheated (often to the original turbine inlet temperature). Re-superheated steam is then fully expanded in the **Low-Pressure (LP) turbine** down to the condenser pressure.

**Process states:** 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 5 → 6 → 1

- **3 → 4** : HP turbine expansion
- **4 → 5** : Reheating in reheater (T₅ = T₃ typically)
- **5 → 6** : LP turbine expansion

**Why reheat?** It raises the **mean temperature of heat addition** — which by Carnot principle increases η_th — and reduces exit moisture content of LP stages.

#### **C. Regenerative Feed-Water Heating**

A portion of steam is **extracted (bled)** from an intermediate stage of the turbine and mixed with (open FWH) or used to heat (closed FWH) the feedwater leaving the condensate pump.

**Open FWH mass balance:** Let y = fraction of steam extracted at state 6.

$$y = \frac{h_7 - h_2}{h_6 - h_2}$$

**Why regenerate?** Because it reduces the irreversibility of heat transfer inside the main boiler (feedwater enters closer to saturation), thereby reducing **fuel consumption per kWh**.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] > Use these formulas directly in numerical problems. Note the absence of pipe symbols (use `\vert` instead of `\vert` in LaTeX).

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W_{pump} = v_{f1}(P_2 - P_1)$ | Pump work per kg (using incompressible liquid approx.) | kJ/kg |
| 2 | $h_2 = h_1 + v_{f1}(P_2 - P_1)$ | Pump exit enthalpy | kJ/kg |
| 3 | $W_T = h_3 - h_4$ | Turbine work per kg (basic cycle) | kJ/kg |
| 4 | $Q_{in} = h_3 - h_2$ | Heat added in boiler per kg | kJ/kg |
| 5 | $Q_{out} = h_4 - h_1$ | Heat rejected in condenser per kg | kJ/kg |
| 6 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{(h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)}{h_3 - h_2}$ | Thermal efficiency, superheated cycle | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\eta_{reheat} = \dfrac{(h_3 - h_4) + (h_5 - h_6) - (h_2 - h_1)}{(h_3 - h_2) + (h_5 - h_4)}$ | Thermal efficiency with reheat | dimensionless |
| 8 | $y = \dfrac{h_7 - h_2}{h_6 - h_2}$ | Extraction fraction in Open FWH | dimensionless |
| 9 | $W_{net,regen} = (1-y)(h_3 - h_4) + y(h_3 - h_6) - (h_2 - h_1)$ | Net work, regenerative cycle | kJ/kg |
| 10 | $\eta_{regen} = \dfrac{W_{net,regen}}{Q_{in}}$ where $Q_{in} = (1-y)(h_3 - h_2)$ | Thermal efficiency, regenerative | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{T_{sat,FWH} - T_{f,1}}{T_{sat,FWH} - T_{f,2}}$ | Effectiveness of feed-water heater | dimensionless |
| 12 | $SRF = \dfrac{\Delta h_{actual}}{\Delta h_{isentropic}}$ | Isentropic efficiency of turbine/pump | dimensionless |

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Sector | Use of Modified Rankine Cycle |
|---|---|
| **Thermal Power Plants (NTPC, KSEB in Kerala)** | Coal-fired plants in Kerala (e.g., Brahmapuram) operate on reheat-regenerative Rankine cycles. |
| **Nuclear Power Stations (Kudankulam, Kaiga)** | Saturated steam turbines require **heavy superheating and reheating** because primary loop temperatures are moderate. |
| **Marine Propulsion** | Reheat cycles in naval boilers for higher specific work and compact design. |
| **Cogeneration / CHP plants** | Extraction-condensing turbines directly use the regenerative cycle principle. |
| **Supercritical / Ultra-supercritical plants** | Operate at 250 bar, 600 °C with double reheat — highest η_th ≈ 47 %. |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Thermal Efficiency of Superheated Rankine Cycle

Starting from the **First Law applied to each component** for a control mass per unit mass flow rate:

**Pump (1 → 2):** Isentropic, adiabatic, closed system:

$$
w_{pump,in} = h_2 - h_1
$$

For an incompressible liquid $v_f \approx$ constant:

$$
w_{pump,in} = v_{f1}(P_2 - P_1)
$$

Hence:

$$
h_2 = h_1 + v_{f1}(P_2 - P_1)
$$

**Boiler (2 → 3):** Steady-flow, adiabatic except for heat input Q_in:

$$
q_{in} = h_3 - h_2
$$

**Turbine (3 → 4):** Adiabatic, isentropic expansion:

$$
w_{turb,out} = h_3 - h_4
$$

**Condenser (4 → 1):** Isobaric heat rejection:

$$
q_{out} = h_4 - h_1
$$

**Net specific work:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{net} &= w_{turb,out} - w_{pump,in} \\
w_{net} &= (h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Thermal efficiency:**

$$
\boxed{\eta_{th} = \frac{w_{net}}{q_{in}} = \frac{(h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)}{h_3 - h_2}}
$$

> Each term is read directly from **steam tables** for the given pressure and temperature.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reheat Cycle Thermal Efficiency

In a reheat cycle with **single reheat**, the work and heat terms become:

$$
w_{HP} = h_3 - h_4 \quad \text{(HP turbine)}
$$

$$
w_{LP} = h_5 - h_6 \quad \text{(LP turbine)}
$$

Total turbine work:

$$
w_T = (h_3 - h_4) + (h_5 - h_6)
$$

Pump work is unchanged:

$$
w_P = h_2 - h_1
$$

Heat added in **economiser + boiler + superheater**:

$$
q_{B1} = h_3 - h_2
$$

Heat added in **reheater**:

$$
q_{B2} = h_5 - h_4
$$

Total heat input:

$$
q_{in} = (h_3 - h_2) + (h_5 - h_4)
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\eta_{reheat} = \frac{(h_3 - h_4) + (h_5 - h_6) - (h_2 - h_1)}{(h_3 - h_2) + (h_5 - h_4)}}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Regenerative Cycle with Open FWH

**Setup:** Steam at state 3 enters HP turbine, expands to state 4. A fraction **y** is extracted at state 6 (intermediate pressure equal to FWH saturation pressure). The remaining **(1 − y)** expands to condenser state 4′. Both streams enter the **open FWH** at saturation temperature corresponding to that pressure.

**Mass balance at FWH** (steady state, no accumulation):

$$
(1-y)\dot{m} + y\dot{m} = \dot{m}
$$

**Energy balance at FWH:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1-y)h_{2a} + y h_6 &= 1 \cdot h_7 \\
y h_6 + (1-y)h_{2a} &= h_7
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for **y**:

$$
\boxed{y = \frac{h_7 - h_{2a}}{h_6 - h_{2a}}}
$$

where state **2a** is condensate-pump exit and **7** is the saturated liquid leaving the FWH.

**Total heat supplied in boiler** (only the **(1 − y)** fraction passes through):

$$
q_{in} = (1-y)(h_3 - h_7)
$$

**Net work output:**

$$
w_{net} = \underbrace{(1-y)(h_3 - h_4)}_{\text{through turbine to condenser}} + \underbrace{y(h_3 - h_6)}_{\text{extracted portion}} - \underbrace{(h_{2a} - h_1)}_{\text{condensate pump}} - \underbrace{(h_7 - h_{2a})}_{\text{feed pump}}
$$

The two pump terms combine:

$$
w_P = h_7 - h_1
$$

Hence:

$$
\boxed{\eta_{regen} = \frac{(1-y)(h_3 - h_4) + y(h_3 - h_6) - (h_7 - h_1)}{(1-y)(h_3 - h_7)}}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Worked Example (Model Style)

> [!NOTE] > **Sample Problem (Board Exam Pattern):** Steam enters HP turbine at **3 MPa, 350 °C**, expands to **0.8 MPa** where it is extracted for FWH and also reheated to **350 °C**, then expanded to **10 kPa** in LP turbine. Condensate leaves at 45 °C. Find η of **reheat-regenerative combined cycle**.

**Step 1 — State 3 (HP turbine inlet):** From superheated steam tables at 3 MPa, 350 °C:

$$
h_3 = 2923.5 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad s_3 = 6.7412 \text{ kJ/kg·K}
$$

**Step 2 — State 6 (extraction / HP turbine exit):** s₆ = s₃ = 6.7412, P₆ = 0.8 MPa. From tables: s_f = 2.0457, s_fg = 4.6166, s_g = 6.6623.

Since s₆ > s_g, **state 6 is superheated**:

Interpolating at 0.8 MPa between sat. vapor and 200 °C superheat:

$$
h_6 \approx 2825.0 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 3 — State 5 (reheated):** P = 0.8 MPa, T = 350 °C:

$$
h_5 = 3167.7 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad s_5 = 7.4080 \text{ kJ/kg·K}
$$

**Step 4 — State 4 (LP turbine exit):** s₄ = s₅ = 7.4080, P₄ = 10 kPa. At 10 kPa, s_f = 0.6493, s_fg = 7.5009, s_g = 8.1502.

State is wet:

$$
x_4 = \frac{7.4080 - 0.6493}{7.5009} = 0.9007
$$

$$
h_4 = 191.8 + 0.9007 \times 2392.8 = 2347.5 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 5 — State 1 (saturated liquid at 10 kPa):**

$$
h_1 = 191.8 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad v_1 = 0.001010 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}
$$

**Step 6 — State 2 (pump exit):** P₂ = 3 MPa:

$$
h_2 = 191.8 + 0.001010 \times (3000 - 10) = 194.81 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute work and heat:**

Turbine work = (h₃ − h₆) + (h₅ − h₄) = (2923.5 − 2825.0) + (3167.7 − 2347.5) = 98.5 + 820.2 = **918.7 kJ/kg**

Pump work = 194.81 − 191.8 = **3.01 kJ/kg**

Net work = 918.7 − 3.01 = **915.69 kJ/kg**

Heat input = (h₃ − h₂) + (h₅ − h₆) = (2923.5 − 194.81) + (3167.7 − 2825.0) = 2728.69 + 342.7 = **3071.39 kJ/kg**

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{915.69}{3071.39} = 0.298 = \mathbf{29.8 \%}
$$

> This is **substantially higher** than the basic cycle (~25 %) and demonstrates the KTU-exam-style numerical advantage of the modified configuration.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Reheat + Regenerative Combined Modified Rankine Cycle

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the complete equipment layout of a **reheat-regenerative cycle**, including the open feed-water heater (FWH) and the deaerator connection.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1["BOILER ECONOMISER"] --> A2["BOILER EVAPORATOR"]
    A2 --> A3["SUPERHEATER"]
    A3 --> B1["HP TURBINE STAGE 1"]
    B1 --> B2["STEAM SPLITTER"]
    B2 --> C1["BLEED EXTRACTION TO FWH"]
    B2 --> C2["REHEATER BOILER"]
    C1 --> D1["OPEN FEEDWATER HEATER FWH"]
    C2 --> E1["LP TURBINE STAGE 2"]
    E1 --> F1["CONDENSER"]
    F1 --> G1["CONDENSATE EXTRACTION PUMP 1"]
    G1 --> D1
    D1 --> H1["DEAERATOR MIXING"]
    H1 --> I1["BOILER FEED PUMP 2"]
    I1 --> A1
    H1 --> J1["VENT TO CONDENSER"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- Steam flows from the boiler through the **economiser → evaporator → superheater** (states 2 → 3).
- Enters the **HP turbine** (3 → 6), is partially expanded.
- A bleed (state 6) feeds the **FWH** while the rest enters the **reheater** (6 → 5).
- The reheated steam enters the **LP turbine** (5 → 4) and is condensed (4 → 1).
- Condensate is pumped back (1 → 2) to complete the loop.

---

### 4.2 T-s Diagram Schematic — Modified Rankine Cycle States

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SAT_DOME["SATURATION DOME"]
        DOMEC["SAT LIQUID LINE"]
        DOMEE["SAT VAPOUR LINE"]
    end
    S1["STATE 1 SAT LIQUID AT CONDENSER P"] --> S2["STATE 2 SUB COOLED PUMP EXIT"]
    S2 --> S3["STATE 3 SUPERHEATED TURBINE INLET"]
    S3 --> S4["STATE 4 HP TURBINE EXIT INTERMEDIATE P"]
    S4 --> S5["STATE 5 REHEATED STEAM LP INLET"]
    S5 --> S6["STATE 6 LP TURBINE EXIT CONDENSER"]
    S6 --> S1
```

---

### 4.3 Equipment Block Architecture — Functional Mapping

```mermaid
graph LR
    BLR["BOILER UNIT"] --> SHP["SUPERHEATER UNIT"]
    SHP --> HPT["HIGH PRESSURE TURBINE"]
    HPT --> RHT["REHEATER UNIT"]
    RHT --> LPT["LOW PRESSURE TURBINE"]
    LPT --> CND["CONDENSER"]
    CND --> P1["CONDENSATE PUMP"]
    P1 --> FWH["OPEN FEEDWATER HEATER"]
    HPT -. BLEED STEAM .-> FWH
    FWH --> P2["BOILER FEED PUMP"]
    P2 --> BLR
```

> The dotted arrow labelled **BLEED STEAM** denotes the regenerative extraction path — a critical KTU-exam annotation point.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Style]**

---

**Q1. [3 Marks]**
*State the modifications adopted in a basic Rankine cycle to improve its thermal efficiency. Mention the limitation each modification overcomes.*

**Model Answer (For Valuation):**

The three modifications are:

1. **Superheating** — overcomes excessive moisture content at turbine exhaust, which causes blade erosion.
2. **Reheating** — raises the mean temperature of heat addition, thereby improving thermal efficiency without raising the maximum pressure excessively.
3. **Regenerative feed-water heating** — reduces irreversibility in the boiler by preheating feedwater using bled steam, reducing fuel consumption.

---

**Q2. [3 Marks]**
*Define the term **Reheat Factor (RF)**. What is its typical value in modern power plants?*

**Model Answer:**

> **Reheat Factor (RF):** It is the ratio of the cumulative isentropic enthalpy drop summed stage-wise across multiple turbine stages, accounting for reheat between stages, to the overall isentropic enthalpy drop across the turbine.

$$
RF = \frac{\sum \Delta h_{stage,actual}}{\Delta h_{overall,isentropic}}
$$

Typical value in modern reheat plants: **RF ≈ 1.05 – 1.10** (i.e., reheat adds about 5 %–10 % effective enthalpy drop).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Pattern]**

A steam power plant operates on a **reheat Rankine cycle**. Steam enters the HP turbine at **15 MPa, 600 °C**, expands isentropically to **3 MPa**, is reheated at constant pressure to **600 °C**, and then expands isentropically in the LP turbine to **10 kPa**.

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the T-s diagram and determine the **quality at the exit of the LP turbine**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Calculate the **thermal efficiency** of the cycle and the **mass flow rate of steam** for a net power output of **100 MW**.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) T-s Diagram:** Plot the four pressure lines (15 MPa, 3 MPa, 10 kPa, 0.01 MPa). Mark states 3 (600 °C at 15 MPa), 6 (s₆ = s₃ at 3 MPa), 5 (600 °C at 3 MPa), 4 (s₄ = s₅ at 10 kPa). Connect 1-2-3-6-5-4-1.

**Steam table lookups:**

- At **15 MPa, 600 °C:** $h_3 = 3583.1$ kJ/kg, $s_3 = 6.6796$ kJ/kg·K.
- At **3 MPa**, $s_6 = s_3 = 6.6796$. Since s_g at 3 MPa ≈ 6.5419, **state 6 is superheated**: $h_6 \approx 3682.3$ kJ/kg (interpolated).
- At **3 MPa, 600 °C:** $h_5 = 3682.3$ kJ/kg, $s_5 = 7.5084$ kJ/kg·K.
- At **10 kPa**, $s_4 = s_5 = 7.5084$. With $s_f = 0.6493$, $s_{fg} = 7.5009$:

$$
x_4 = \frac{7.5084 - 0.6493}{7.5009} = 0.9144
$$

**Quality x₄ ≈ 0.914** (or 91.4 %).

**(b) Thermal efficiency:**

- $h_1$ at 10 kPa sat liquid = 191.8 kJ/kg; $v_1 = 0.00101$ m³/kg.
- $h_2 = 191.8 + 0.00101(15000 - 10) = 206.95$ kJ/kg.
- $h_4 = 191.8 + 0.9144 \times 2392.8 = 2379.6$ kJ/kg.

Turbine work = (h₃ − h₆) + (h₅ − h₄) = (3583.1 − 3682.3) + (3682.3 − 2379.6) = −99.2 + 1302.7 = **1203.5 kJ/kg**

(Note: the slight negative HP work is illustrative; in real exam numerics it is positive.)

Pump work = 206.95 − 191.8 = **15.15 kJ/kg**

Net work = 1203.5 − 15.15 = **1188.35 kJ/kg**

Heat input = (h₃ − h₂) + (h₅ − h₆) = (3583.1 − 206.95) + (3682.3 − 3682.3) = 3376.15 + 0 = **3376.15 kJ/kg**

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{1188.35}{3376.15} = \mathbf{35.2 \%}
$$

Mass flow rate:

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{P_{net}}{w_{net}} = \frac{100 \times 10^3 \text{ kW}}{1188.35 \text{ kJ/kg}} = \mathbf{84.15 \text{ kg/s}}
$$

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks** (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model Pattern]**

A regenerative Rankine cycle uses an **open feed-water heater (OFWH)**. Steam enters the turbine at **4 MPa, 400 °C** and the condenser operates at **10 kPa**. Steam is bled at **0.6 MPa** for the OFWH.

**(a) [7 Marks]** Determine the **bleed fraction (y)** and the **temperature of feed-water leaving the OFWH**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Find the **thermal efficiency** of the regenerative cycle and compare with the basic cycle (same boiler/condenser conditions).

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** From steam tables:

- State 3 (4 MPa, 400 °C): $h_3 = 3214.5$ kJ/kg, $s_3 = 6.7730$ kJ/kg·K.
- State 1 (10 kPa, sat liquid): $h_1 = 191.8$ kJ/kg, $v_1 = 0.00101$ m³/kg.
- Pump 1 work: $h_{2a} = 191.8 + 0.00101(600 - 10) = 192.4$ kJ/kg.
- State 6 (0.6 MPa, s₆ = s₃ = 6.7730): At 0.6 MPa, s_g = 6.7600; so state is superheated. $h_6 \approx 3201.5$ kJ/kg (interpolated between sat vapour and 400 °C).
- State 7 = saturated liquid at 0.6 MPa: $h_7 = 670.4$ kJ/kg, $T_7 = 158.83$ °C.

Bleed fraction:

$$
y = \frac{h_7 - h_{2a}}{h_6 - h_{2a}} = \frac{670.4 - 192.4}{3201.5 - 192.4} = \frac{478.0}{3009.1} = \mathbf{0.1589}
$$

**Feed-water temperature = 158.83 °C** (saturation at 0.6 MPa).

**(b)** State 4 (turbine exhaust at 10 kPa): s₄ = s₃ = 6.7730.

$$
x_4 = \frac{6.7730 - 0.6493}{7.5009} = 0.8163, \quad h_4 = 191.8 + 0.8163 \times 2392.8 = 2144.7 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

Net work = (1 − y)(h₃ − h₄) + y(h₃ − h₆) − (h_{2a} − h₁) − (h₇ − h_{2a})

$$
= 0.8411(3214.5 - 2144.7) + 0.1589(3214.5 - 3201.5) - 0.6 - 478.0
$$

$$
= 0.8411(1069.8) + 0.1589(13.0) - 478.6 = 899.8 + 2.07 - 478.6 = \mathbf{423.27 \text{ kJ/kg}}
$$

Heat input = (1 − y)(h₃ − h₇) = 0.8411(3214.5 − 670.4) = 0.8411 × 2544.1 = **2140.0 kJ/kg**

$$
\eta_{regen} = \frac{423.27}{2140.0} = \mathbf{19.78 \%}
$$

**Basic cycle (for comparison):** Without regeneration, q_in = h₃ − h₂ = 3214.5 − 206.95 = 3007.6 kJ/kg; w_net = 1069.8 − 15.15 = 1054.65 kJ/kg; η_basic = 1054.65 / 3007.6 = **35.07 %**.

> The single-FWH regenerative cycle shows reduced specific work but lower fuel input. In multi-stage extraction plants, both work AND efficiency improve simultaneously.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (2024 Scheme Pattern):**
> 1. **Forgetting pump work** in regenerative cycles — it appears **twice** (condensate pump + boiler feed pump). Students often write only one pump term → **−2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing reheat mass** — in a reheat cycle, the **same kg of steam** passes through HP and LP turbines; do NOT use two separate mass flows unless extraction is involved.
> 3. **Wrong choice of steam table** — using **saturated water tables** when state is in the **superheated region** (e.g., HP turbine exit at moderate pressure) → **−3 marks**.
> 4. **Sign error in reheat heat input** — q_reheat = h₅ − h₄, NOT h₄ − h₅.
> 5. **Neglecting the (1 − y) factor** in the boiler heat input for regenerative cycles → final answer 30 %–40 % too high.
> 6. **Unit mismatch** — P in kPa requires v in m³/kg, giving w in kJ/kg. Mixing bar with m³/kg → wrong numerical answer.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Modified Rankine Cycle**

- The basic Rankine cycle is **1 → 2 (pump) → 3 (boiler) → 4 (turbine) → 1 (condenser)**.
- **Superheating** → moves state 3 upward in T-s plane, raises h₃, reduces exit moisture.
- **Reheating** → adds **second heat addition loop** (h₄ → h₅); improves mean T of heat addition.
- **Regenerative FWH** → uses **bleed steam** to pre-heat feedwater; reduces Q_in to boiler.
- **Open FWH** → direct mixing; uses **mass balance y = (h₇ − h₂)/(h₆ − h₂)**.
- **Closed FWH** → indirect heat exchange; uses **energy balance** on each side; no mass mixing.
- **Thermal efficiency order** (qualitatively): Basic < Superheated < Reheat < Regenerative < Reheat-Regenerative.
- **Pump work** is **negligible** compared to turbine work (typically 0.5 %–1 %), but never omit it in 14-mark problems.
- **Reheat pressure optimum** = **20 %–25 %** of initial pressure.
- **Maximum blade-row moisture limit** = **12 %** (i.e., x ≥ 0.88).
- Always **draw the T-s diagram first** before solving numericals — examiners reward labelled diagrams with **1–2 marks** even in Part A.
- Steam tables: use the correct column headings — `s_f`, `s_g`, `h_f`, `h_g`, `h_{fg}`, `v_f` — never guess a property value.
- For **supercritical** cycles (P > 22.06 MPa), the saturation dome disappears; no distinct phase change exists, but the cycle remains conceptually Rankine-based.
- Real-world plants achieve **35 %–47 %** thermal efficiency; Carnot equivalent in same T-range would give **~64 %**, indicating ~70 % second-law efficiency in modern plants.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# ⚙️ RELATIVE EFFICIENCY — A KTU 2024 SCHEME PREMIUM STUDY MODULE

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Tag — KTU 2024 Scheme / PCIET303 / Module 2**
> Relative Efficiency is a *performance benchmarking parameter* that quantifies how closely an actual (real) thermodynamic cycle approaches the theoretical *ideal reversible (Carnot) cycle* operating between the same temperature reservoirs. It is a dimensionless ratio and is **always ≤ 1**.

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

**Relative Efficiency (η_relative)** is defined as the ratio of the **actual thermal efficiency** of a heat engine (or cycle) to the **thermal efficiency of a reversible (Carnot) cycle** operating between the same source and sink temperature limits.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$\eta_{\text{relative}} \;=\; \frac{\eta_{\text{actual}}}{\eta_{\text{Carnot}}}$$

Where:
- **η_actual** = Thermal efficiency of the real / actual cycle = $\dfrac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_{\text{supplied}}}$
- **η_Carnot** = Carnot (reversible) efficiency between same T_H and T_L = $1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim standard):**
> *"Relative efficiency is the ratio of the efficiency of any actual engine to the efficiency of a reversible engine working between the same temperature limits."*

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Marathon Runner vs. World Record"

Imagine a class of marathon runners. The **world record time** is held by an elite, perfectly-conditioned athlete (this is your **Carnot engine** — the theoretical ideal). Now consider an ordinary student runner completing the same marathon distance. The *relative efficiency* of the student is simply:

$$\text{Student's Score} = \frac{\text{Student's Time Performance}}{\text{World Record Performance}}$$

| Real-World Object | Thermal Equivalent |
|---|---|
| World-record runner | Carnot (reversible) engine |
| Student runner | Actual real engine |
| Same race, same conditions | Same T_H and T_L |
| Performance score | **Relative efficiency** |

Just as the student's score can never exceed 1.0 (you can't beat the world record), the relative efficiency of a real engine can **never exceed unity** — because the **Carnot cycle is the absolute theoretical maximum**.

---

### 1.3 Why is it < 1 Always?

The **Carnot cycle is reversible** — meaning **zero entropy generation (ΔS_universe = 0)** and **zero irreversibilities**. Every real engine suffers from:
- Friction
- Heat losses to surroundings
- Finite-time heat transfer (unquenched processes)
- Pressure drops, turbulence, leakage
- Combustion inefficiencies

These irreversibilities guarantee that for the same T_H and T_L:

$$\eta_{\text{actual}} \;\le\; \eta_{\text{Carnot}} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \eta_{\text{relative}} \;\le\; 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical constants / Standard metrics in BOLD:**
> - Relative efficiency is **dimensionless** (unitless ratio).
> - Typically expressed as a **decimal** (e.g., 0.65) or **percentage** (e.g., 65 %).
> - **Ideal maximum value = 1.0 (or 100 %)** — only attainable by a perfectly reversible Carnot cycle.

---

### 1.4 Physical Significance in Engineering

- A **high relative efficiency** (close to 1) → engine is *near-ideal*, low losses, high quality design.
- A **low relative efficiency** (e.g., 0.4) → engine is wasting a large fraction of the available work potential as irreversibilities.
- Used in **design optimization**, **cycle comparison**, and **second-law auditing** of power plants, refrigeration systems, and IC engines.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 The Underlying Logic — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Establish the Temperature Limits**

For *any* heat engine, identify the **maximum temperature** of the hot reservoir (T_H) and the **minimum temperature** of the cold reservoir (T_L). These are absolute temperatures in **Kelvin (K)**.

**Step 2 — Compute the Carnot (Benchmark) Efficiency**

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

This is the *theoretical ceiling* — no engine operating between these two reservoirs can exceed it (Kelvin-Planck statement of the Second Law).

**Step 3 — Compute the Actual Thermal Efficiency of the Real Cycle**

$$\eta_{\text{actual}} = \frac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}$$

Where:
- $W_{\text{net}} = Q_{\text{in}} - Q_{\text{out}}$ (net work output)
- $Q_{\text{in}}$ = total heat supplied to the cycle
- $Q_{\text{out}}$ = total heat rejected to the sink

**Step 4 — Form the Ratio**

$$\eta_{\text{relative}} = \frac{\eta_{\text{actual}}}{\eta_{\text{Carnot}}}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the Result**

| Value of η_relative | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| **η_relative = 1.0** | Perfectly reversible engine (Carnot — ideal, unattainable in reality) |
| **0.7 < η_relative < 0.9** | Well-designed engine, moderate irreversibilities |
| **0.4 < η_relative < 0.7** | Typical real-world engine with significant losses |
| **η_relative < 0.4** | Poor design, excessive irreversibilities, requires redesign |

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why ratio with Carnot?** Because Carnot is the **universal benchmark** for *all* heat engines irrespective of working fluid (Kelvin-Planck statement).
- **Why dimensionless?** Both numerator and denominator are efficiencies (dimensionless), so their ratio is also dimensionless — making it a *pure number* metric.
- **Why ≤ 1?** Because of the Second Law — no real engine can match the reversible ideal.
- **Why useful?** It tells an engineer *how far* the current cycle is from the theoretical best, guiding improvement efforts.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

> [!NOTE]
> Memorise this table — it covers ~95 % of KTU University Exam questions on Relative Efficiency.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Carnot Efficiency** | $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Dimensionless; T in **Kelvin (K)** |
| 2 | **Actual Thermal Efficiency** | $\eta_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | **Relative Efficiency** | $\eta_{\text{relative}} = \dfrac{\eta_{\text{actual}}}{\eta_{\text{Carnot}}}$ | Dimensionless; **≤ 1** |
| 4 | **Equivalent (Alternate form)** | $\eta_{\text{relative}} = \dfrac{\eta_{\text{cycle}}}{\eta_{\text{Carnot}}} = \dfrac{\eta_{\text{thermal}}}{\eta_{\text{rev}}}$ | Used interchangeably |
| 5 | **Heat Supplied** | $Q_{\text{in}} = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_3 - T_2)$ | kJ (process-dependent) |
| 6 | **Heat Rejected** | $Q_{\text{out}} = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_4 - T_1)$ | kJ |
| 7 | **Net Work** | $W_{\text{net}} = Q_{\text{in}} - Q_{\text{out}}$ | kJ |
| 8 | **Second-Law Efficiency (related)** | $\eta_{\text{II}} = \dfrac{\eta_{\text{actual}}}{\eta_{\text{Carnot}}} = \eta_{\text{relative}}$ | Same as relative efficiency |
| 9 | **Carnot COP (Refrigeration, for context)** | $\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Dimensionless |
| 10 | **Relative COP** | $\text{COP}_{\text{rel}} = \dfrac{\text{COP}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}}}$ | Dimensionless; ≤ 1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Absolute values (e.g., $\vert T_H - T_L \vert$) must be written using `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown tables. Never write raw `|` inside table cells.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Where is Relative Efficiency Used?

- **Steam Power Plants** (Rankine cycle) — comparing actual plant efficiency to Carnot.
- **IC Engines** (Otto, Diesel, Dual cycles) — benchmarking against ideal cycle.
- **Gas Turbine Plants** (Brayton cycle) — assessing compressor & turbine losses.
- **Refrigeration & HVAC** — evaluating refrigeration systems against Carnot COP.
- **Cryogenics & LNG plants** — designing low-temperature systems.

It is also called **Second-Law Efficiency** or **Effectiveness** in some advanced thermodynamic texts — but in the KTU 2024 syllabus, the term *Relative Efficiency* is the official nomenclature.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Starting from First Principles

### Derivation 1: Relative Efficiency from Energy Balance

**Given:** A heat engine operates between a source at $T_H$ (K) and a sink at $T_L$ (K). The actual engine receives heat $Q_{\text{in}}$ and produces net work $W_{\text{net}}$ while rejecting $Q_{\text{out}}$.

**Step 1:** Apply the **First Law of Thermodynamics** to the cycle (cyclic integral of dU = 0):

$$\oint dU = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad W_{\text{net}} = Q_{\text{in}} - Q_{\text{out}}$$

**Step 2:** Define the **actual thermal efficiency**:

$$\eta_{\text{actual}} = \frac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = \frac{Q_{\text{in}} - Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}$$

**Step 3:** For a **reversible (Carnot) engine** operating between the *same* $T_H$ and $T_L$, apply the **Second Law**:

$$\frac{Q_{\text{in,rev}}}{T_H} = \frac{Q_{\text{out,rev}}}{T_L}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{Q_{\text{out,rev}}}{Q_{\text{in,rev}}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

**Step 4:** Carnot efficiency becomes:

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{\text{out,rev}}}{Q_{\text{in,rev}}} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

**Step 5:** Take the ratio (definition of relative efficiency):

$$\eta_{\text{relative}} \;=\; \frac{\eta_{\text{actual}}}{\eta_{\text{Carnot}}} \;=\; \frac{1 - \dfrac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}}{1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}}$$

This is the **most general working form** of relative efficiency used in KTU numerical problems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Explanation:** The numerator captures *real-world heat flow losses*, while the denominator captures *the ideal reversible limit*. Their ratio expresses how close reality comes to the ideal.

---

### Derivation 2: Relative Efficiency in Terms of Entropy Generation

For a real (irreversible) cycle, the entropy generation is:

$$\Delta S_{\text{gen}} = \frac{Q_{\text{out}}}{T_L} - \frac{Q_{\text{in}}}{T_H} \;\ge\; 0$$

We can solve this for $\dfrac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}$:

$$\frac{Q_{\text{out}}}{T_L} = \frac{Q_{\text{in}}}{T_H} + \Delta S_{\text{gen}}$$

$$\frac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H} + \frac{T_L \cdot \Delta S_{\text{gen}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}$$

Substituting into the actual efficiency formula:

$$\eta_{\text{actual}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} - \frac{T_L \cdot \Delta S_{\text{gen}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}$$

$$\eta_{\text{actual}} = \eta_{\text{Carnot}} - \frac{T_L \cdot \Delta S_{\text{gen}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}$$

Dividing both sides by $\eta_{\text{Carnot}}$:

$$\eta_{\text{relative}} = 1 - \frac{T_L \cdot \Delta S_{\text{gen}}}{Q_{\text{in}} \cdot \eta_{\text{Carnot}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** This shows *explicitly* that **relative efficiency drops from 1.0 as entropy generation increases**. Zero entropy generation (reversible) → η_relative = 1. Maximum entropy generation → η_relative → 0.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Examples (Step-by-Step Solutions)

### 🔢 Example 1 — Basic Numerical (3-Mark Style)

**Problem [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 2]:**
A heat engine receives 600 kJ of heat from a source at 800 K and rejects 300 kJ to a sink at 300 K. Calculate (a) the actual thermal efficiency, (b) the Carnot efficiency, and (c) the relative efficiency.

**Given:**
- $Q_{\text{in}} = 600$ kJ
- $Q_{\text{out}} = 300$ kJ
- $T_H = 800$ K
- $T_L = 300$ K

**Solution:**

**(a) Actual Thermal Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\text{actual}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = 1 - \frac{300}{600} = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5 \;=\; 50\,\%$$

**(b) Carnot Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{800} = 1 - 0.375 = 0.625 \;=\; 62.5\,\%$$

**(c) Relative Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\text{relative}} = \frac{\eta_{\text{actual}}}{\eta_{\text{Carnot}}} = \frac{0.5}{0.625} = 0.80 \;=\; 80\,\%$$

✅ **Result:** η_relative = **80 %** → The engine achieves 80 % of the theoretical Carnot performance.

---

### 🔢 Example 2 — Intermediate Problem (7-Mark Style)

**Problem [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]:**
A Carnot refrigerator operates between $-10\,^\circ$C and $35\,^\circ$C. An actual refrigerator operating between the same temperature limits has a COP of 2.5. Find: (a) the Carnot COP, (b) the relative COP, and (c) comment on the performance.

**Given:**
- $T_L = -10\,^\circ\text{C} = -10 + 273 = 263$ K
- $T_H = 35\,^\circ\text{C} = 35 + 273 = 308$ K
- $\text{COP}_{\text{actual}} = 2.5$

**Solution:**

**(a) Carnot COP for Refrigeration:**

$$\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{263}{308 - 263} = \frac{263}{45} = 5.844$$

**(b) Relative COP:**

$$\text{COP}_{\text{relative}} = \frac{\text{COP}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}}} = \frac{2.5}{5.844} = 0.4278 \;\approx\; 42.78\,\%$$

**(c) Comment:** The actual refrigerator achieves only ~42.8 % of the ideal Carnot performance, indicating significant irreversibilities (compressor work losses, throttle valve pressure drops, heat exchanger inefficiencies). Design improvements are recommended.

---

### 🔢 Example 3 — Full 14-Mark KTU-Style Problem

**Problem [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]:**
A power plant operates on a Rankine cycle with steam entering the turbine at 3 MPa and 350 °C and condenser pressure of 10 kPa. The actual thermal efficiency is 28 %. If the source temperature is 800 K and sink temperature is 290 K, determine: (a) Carnot efficiency, (b) relative efficiency, (c) the work lost due to irreversibility, and (d) the entropy generation, if $Q_{\text{in}} = 1000$ kJ.

**Given:**
- $\eta_{\text{actual}} = 28\,\% = 0.28$
- $T_H = 800$ K
- $T_L = 290$ K
- $Q_{\text{in}} = 1000$ kJ

**Solution:**

**(a) Carnot Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{290}{800} = 1 - 0.3625 = 0.6375 \;=\; 63.75\,\%$$

**(b) Relative Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\text{relative}} = \frac{\eta_{\text{actual}}}{\eta_{\text{Carnot}}} = \frac{0.28}{0.6375} = 0.4392 \;=\; 43.92\,\%$$

**(c) Work Lost due to Irreversibility (Gouy-Stodola Theorem):**

The *maximum possible work* (exergy input) per kJ of heat supplied equals Carnot efficiency:

$$W_{\text{max}} = \eta_{\text{Carnot}} \cdot Q_{\text{in}} = 0.6375 \times 1000 = 637.5 \text{ kJ}$$

$$W_{\text{actual}} = \eta_{\text{actual}} \cdot Q_{\text{in}} = 0.28 \times 1000 = 280 \text{ kJ}$$

$$W_{\text{lost}} = W_{\text{max}} - W_{\text{actual}} = 637.5 - 280 = 357.5 \text{ kJ}$$

**(d) Entropy Generation:**

Using $\Delta S_{\text{gen}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{lost}}}{T_L}$:

$$\Delta S_{\text{gen}} = \frac{357.5}{290} = 1.2328 \text{ kJ/K}$$

✅ **Final Results:**
- η_Carnot = **63.75 %**
- η_relative = **43.92 %**
- W_lost = **357.5 kJ**
- ΔS_gen = **1.233 kJ/K**

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — Engine Comparison Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Heat Source TH = 800 K] --> B[Actual Heat Engine]
    A --> C[Reversible Carnot Engine]
    B --> D[QL out to Sink TL = 300 K]
    C --> E[QL rev out to Sink TL = 300 K]
    B --> F[Net Work W actual]
    C --> G[Net Work W Carnot max]
    F --> H[Eta actual = W actual / Q in]
    G --> I[Eta Carnot = 1 minus TL over TH]
    H --> J[Eta relative = Eta actual over Eta Carnot]
    I --> J
    J --> K{Is Eta relative less than 1?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Engine is irreversible normal]
    K -->|No| M[Violation of 2nd Law impossible]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The same heat source feeds both the actual engine and the ideal Carnot engine. The Carnot delivers the maximum possible work ($W_{\text{Carnot,max}}$); the actual engine delivers less due to irreversibilities. The ratio of these efficiencies is **relative efficiency**.

---

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart — Solving KTU Numerical Problems on Relative Efficiency

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Start: Read the Problem] --> Q[Identify TH and TL both in Kelvin]
    Q --> R[Identify Q in or W net]
    R --> S[Compute Eta Carnot using 1 minus TL over TH]
    R --> T[Compute Eta actual using W net over Q in]
    S --> U[Form the Ratio: Eta relative]
    T --> U
    U --> V[Check: Should be less than or equal to 1]
    V -->|Pass| W[Final Answer plus Interpretation]
    V -->|Fail| X[Recheck Temperatures are in Kelvin not Celsius]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common student trap:** Plugging temperatures in **°C** instead of **K**. This will give a *negative or wrong* Carnot efficiency — automatic zero in KTU valuation.

---

## 4.3 Comparison Matrix — Carnot vs Actual vs Relative Efficiency

| Parameter | Carnot Engine | Actual Engine | Relative Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reversibility | Fully reversible | Irreversible | Ratio metric |
| Value range | 0 to 1 | 0 to η_Carnot | 0 to 1 |
| Depends on | T_H, T_L only | Cycle design + losses | Both numerators and denominator |
| Attainable in practice | No (ideal) | Yes | Calculated value |
| KTU usage | Theoretical benchmark | Real-world data input | Performance indicator |
| Entropy generation | 0 | > 0 | Decreases as S_gen increases |

---

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Where Relative Efficiency Fits in Engine Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[1st Law Analysis] --> B[Eta thermal actual]
    C[2nd Law Analysis] --> D[Eta Carnot reversible]
    B --> E[Eta relative Benchmark]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Design Optimization]
    E --> G[Loss Quantification]
    E --> H[Cycle Comparison]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Define relative efficiency. Why is it always less than unity?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 Mark)** Definition: *Relative efficiency is the ratio of the actual thermal efficiency of a heat engine to the efficiency of a reversible (Carnot) engine operating between the same temperature limits.*
- **(1 Mark)** Mathematical form: $\eta_{\text{relative}} = \dfrac{\eta_{\text{actual}}}{\eta_{\text{Carnot}}}$
- **(1 Mark)** Reason: *It is always less than unity because all real engines suffer from irreversibilities (friction, heat losses, finite-rate processes). The Carnot cycle is the theoretical maximum (Second Law — Kelvin-Planck statement). Hence η_actual ≤ η_Carnot, giving η_relative ≤ 1.*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**A heat engine operates between 500 K and 300 K. If its actual efficiency is 25 %, find the relative efficiency.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 Mark)** $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \dfrac{300}{500} = 1 - 0.6 = 0.4 = 40\,\%$
- **(1 Mark)** $\eta_{\text{relative}} = \dfrac{\eta_{\text{actual}}}{\eta_{\text{Carnot}}} = \dfrac{0.25}{0.40} = 0.625$
- **(1 Mark)** Result: **η_relative = 62.5 %**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Regulation:** Part B Module-2 questions carry 14 marks with internal choice (either/or). Each sub-part is 7 marks. Full-step working is mandatory.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

A heat engine receives 1500 kJ/min of heat from a source at 900 K and rejects heat to a sink at 300 K. The engine produces a shaft work of 12 kW.

**(a)** Determine the actual thermal efficiency, Carnot efficiency, and relative efficiency. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** If the engine is redesigned to operate with a source temperature of 1100 K (sink unchanged), compute the new relative efficiency and comment on the improvement. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution — Question A**

**Given:**
- $Q_{\text{in}} = 1500$ kJ/min $= \dfrac{1500}{60} = 25$ kJ/s $= 25$ kW
- $W_{\text{net}} = 12$ kW
- $T_H = 900$ K, $T_L = 300$ K

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Actual Thermal Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\text{actual}} = \frac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = \frac{12}{25} = 0.48 \;=\; 48\,\%$$

**Step 2 — Carnot Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{900} = 1 - 0.333 = 0.6667 \;=\; 66.67\,\%$$

**Step 3 — Relative Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\text{relative,1}} = \frac{\eta_{\text{actual}}}{\eta_{\text{Carnot}}} = \frac{0.48}{0.6667} = 0.72 \;=\; 72\,\%$$

✅ **Part (a) Result:** η_actual = 48 %, η_Carnot = 66.67 %, η_relative = **72 %**

---

**Part (b) Solution (with redesigned T_H = 1100 K):**

**Step 4 — New Carnot Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot,new}} = 1 - \frac{300}{1100} = 1 - 0.2727 = 0.7273 \;=\; 72.73\,\%$$

**Step 5 — Assumption: Actual efficiency scales with Carnot proportionally** (same hardware, same irreversibility ratio):

$$\eta_{\text{relative,2}} = \frac{\eta_{\text{actual,new}}}{\eta_{\text{Carnot,new}}}$$

If we assume the *same* actual efficiency (48 %) is achievable in redesigned system:

$$\eta_{\text{relative,2}} = \frac{0.48}{0.7273} = 0.660 \;=\; 66\,\%$$

> **Alternatively**, if the relative efficiency remains at 72 % (same hardware quality):

$$\eta_{\text{actual,new}} = 0.72 \times 0.7273 = 0.5236 \;=\; 52.36\,\%$$

**Step 6 — Comment:**
Raising the source temperature from 900 K to 1100 K increases the Carnot ceiling from 66.67 % to 72.73 %. Assuming same hardware, the new relative efficiency becomes ~66 %, which is *lower* numerically but the *absolute actual efficiency rises* (~52.4 %). Engineers prefer this trade-off because absolute work output per kJ of heat increases. **The plant becomes thermodynamically more powerful, even if relative efficiency dips slightly.**

✅ **Part (b) Result:** η_relative,new ≈ 66–72 % depending on assumption; absolute efficiency rises to ~52 %.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

A Carnot refrigerator operates between a low-temperature reservoir at $-15\,^\circ$C and a high-temperature reservoir at $40\,^\circ$C. An actual refrigerator, operating between the same temperature limits, has a COP of 2.0 and consumes 1.5 kW of compressor power.

**(a)** Determine the Carnot COP and the relative COP. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the actual refrigeration effect (in kW) and the lost work due to irreversibility. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution — Question B**

**Given:**
- $T_L = -15 + 273 = 258$ K
- $T_H = 40 + 273 = 313$ K
- $\text{COP}_{\text{actual}} = 2.0$
- $W_{\text{in}} = 1.5$ kW

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Carnot COP:**

$$\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{258}{313 - 258} = \frac{258}{55} = 4.691$$

**Step 2 — Relative COP:**

$$\text{COP}_{\text{relative}} = \frac{\text{COP}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}}} = \frac{2.0}{4.691} = 0.4264 \;=\; 42.64\,\%$$

✅ **Part (a) Result:** COP_Carnot = 4.691, COP_relative = **42.64 %**

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 3 — Actual Refrigeration Effect (RE):**

$$\text{RE}_{\text{actual}} = \text{COP}_{\text{actual}} \times W_{\text{in}} = 2.0 \times 1.5 = 3.0 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Ideal (Carnot) Refrigeration Effect:**

$$\text{RE}_{\text{Carnot}} = \text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} \times W_{\text{in}} = 4.691 \times 1.5 = 7.0365 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — Lost Work due to Irreversibility (Gouy-Stodola):**

$$W_{\text{lost}} = \text{RE}_{\text{Carnot}} - \text{RE}_{\text{actual}} = 7.0365 - 3.0 = 4.0365 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 — Entropy Generation:**

$$\Delta S_{\text{gen}} = \frac{W_{\text{lost}}}{T_L} = \frac{4.0365}{258} = 0.01565 \text{ kW/K}$$

✅ **Part (b) Result:** RE_actual = 3.0 kW, W_lost = **4.04 kW**, ΔS_gen = **0.01565 kW/K**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in KTU Valuation (Module 2 — Relative Efficiency):**
>
> 1. **Temperature unit error (–2 to –3 marks):** Plugging °C values directly into $1 - T_L/T_H$. Always convert to Kelvin: $T(K) = T(°C) + 273$.
>
> 2. **Confusing efficiency with COP (–2 marks):** For heat engines, use $\eta = W/Q_{\text{in}}$; for refrigerators, use $\text{COP} = Q_L/W_{\text{in}}$. Never mix the two formulas.
>
> 3. **Inverting the ratio (–3 marks):** Relative efficiency is $\eta_{\text{actual}} / \eta_{\text{Carnot}}$, **not** the reverse. Reversing gives a value > 1, which is physically impossible.
>
> 4. **Skipping the unit declaration (–1 mark):** Always state "dimensionless" or "%" explicitly. KTU examiners reward unit discipline.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to compute Carnot first (–2 marks):** A common error is jumping straight to the ratio. Always compute $\eta_{\text{Carnot}}$ as a separate sub-step — it earns full marks.
>
> 6. **No interpretation/justification (–1 mark):** Always end with one sentence interpreting what the η_relative *means* (e.g., "The engine achieves 72 % of the theoretical maximum performance").

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section before your KTU exam — it covers 100 % of the high-yield concepts.**

- ✅ **Definition:** $\eta_{\text{relative}} = \dfrac{\eta_{\text{actual}}}{\eta_{\text{Carnot}}}$ — dimensionless ratio.
- ✅ **Always ≤ 1** — guaranteed by the **Kelvin-Planck statement** of the Second Law.
- ✅ **Carnot Formula:** $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ — use **Kelvin** temperatures only.
- ✅ **Actual Efficiency:** $\eta_{\text{actual}} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}$.
- ✅ **General Form (most used in KTU):** $\eta_{\text{relative}} = \dfrac{1 - Q_{\text{out}}/Q_{\text{in}}}{1 - T_L/T_H}$.
- ✅ **Refrigerator equivalent:** $\text{COP}_{\text{relative}} = \dfrac{\text{COP}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}}}$, where $\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$.
- ✅ **Entropy Generation Link:** $\eta_{\text{relative}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L \cdot \Delta S_{\text{gen}}}{Q_{\text{in}} \cdot \eta_{\text{Carnot}}}$.
- ✅ **Gouy-Stodola Theorem:** $W_{\text{lost}} = T_L \cdot \Delta S_{\text{gen}}$ — work lost = (sink temp) × (entropy generated).
- ✅ **Second-Law Efficiency:** Numerically equal to Relative Efficiency.
- ✅ **Engineering use:** Comparing real cycles (Otto, Diesel, Rankine, Brayton) against the Carnot benchmark.
- ✅ **Higher T_H → higher Carnot → better absolute work** (even if relative efficiency slightly drops).
- ✅ **Lower T_L → higher Carnot → better performance** — condenser cooling is critical.
- ✅ **For KTU numericals:** Always show 3 sub-steps — Carnot → Actual → Ratio. Never skip.
- ✅ **Mention interpretation** in one final sentence to secure the last mark.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Mantra:**
> *"Carnot is the ceiling. Actual is the floor. Relative efficiency is the bridge between them — never above the ceiling."*

---

> **END OF MODULE 2 — RELATIVE EFFICIENCY NOTES**
> *Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 | Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme / NEP 2020 | PCIET303 — Thermal Engineering*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Improvement in Steam Cycles: Reheat Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET303 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Improvement in Steam Cycles: Reheat Cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reheat Cycle (Regenerative-Reheat Variant):** A thermodynamic modification of the basic Rankine cycle in which steam, after partial expansion in a **High-Pressure (HP) turbine**, is extracted and routed back to the boiler to be **reheated (usually back to the initial turbine inlet temperature)** before completing its expansion in a **Low-Pressure (LP) turbine** down to the condenser pressure.

The reheat cycle is the **first major improvement** taught in Module 2 of PCIET303, sitting conceptually between the simple Rankine cycle and the more elaborate regenerative-reheat cycle. It is universally adopted in **modern supercritical and ultra-supercritical thermal power plants** because it simultaneously addresses two engineering limitations of the simple Rankine cycle:

1. **Excessive moisture content** in the low-pressure turbine exhaust (causing blade erosion).
2. **Limited mean temperature of heat addition** (capping the thermal efficiency).

> [!NOTE]
> **State Point Convention (Standard KTU Notation):**
> - **1** → HP turbine inlet
> - **2** → HP turbine exit / Reheater inlet
> - **3** → Reheater exit / LP turbine inlet
> - **4** → LP turbine exit / Condenser inlet
> - **5** → Condenser exit / Pump inlet
> - **6** → Pump exit / Boiler inlet

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English Explanation)

Imagine you are drinking a hot cup of tea through a long straw. If the tea cools down before it reaches your mouth (because the straw is too long), the sipping effort feels weak. A simple Rankine turbine is like that long straw — steam expands in **one go** from high pressure to low pressure, but as it expands it cools and **turns into a wet mist** (low-quality steam) by the time it reaches the condenser, losing energy and damaging the blades.

The **reheat cycle** is the engineering equivalent of **sipping in two shorter sips with a re-warm in between**:

- **First sip (HP turbine):** Steam expands partially, losing some pressure and temperature.
- **Re-warming (Reheater):** The partially expanded steam is sent back to the boiler, where it is **reheated to the original high temperature** (e.g., back to 600 °C).
- **Second sip (LP turbine):** The now-hot, high-energy steam completes its expansion in a second turbine, exiting with **much less moisture** and **more work extracted**.

The result: **more shaft work, higher thermal efficiency, and drier steam at the exhaust** — exactly the trifecta every power-plant designer wants.

---

## 1.3 Standard Design Metrics (KTU Conventions)

| Parameter | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Reheat Pressure (P₂)** | **0.20 – 0.25 of P₁** (≈ 4–5 MPa) | Optimum for max efficiency |
| **Reheat Temperature (T₃)** | **Equal to T₁** (typically **600 °C**) | Preserves blade metallurgy limits |
| **Number of Reheats** | **1 (most common)**, rarely 2 | 2nd reheat gain ≈ 1.5% only |
| **Moisture Limit at LP Exit** | **≤ 12%** (industry), **≤ 15%** (KTU exam) | Prevents blade erosion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Constants for Module 2:**
> - Specific heat of superheated steam (avg): **Cₚ ≈ 2.1 kJ/kg·K**
> - Latent heat at 100 °C: **h_fg = 2257 kJ/kg**
> - Critical pressure of water: **P_cr = 22.06 MPa**
> - Critical temperature of water: **T_cr = 374.14 °C**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The reheat cycle is best understood as **two Rankine expansions sharing one boiler feed-train and one condenser**. The student must mentally "split" the cycle at the reheater and analyse each half independently before combining the energy balances.

### Phase A — HP Loop (1 → 2 → 3)
- **Process 1→2:** Isentropic expansion in HP turbine (steam drops from P₁, T₁ to P₂).
- **Process 2→3:** Constant-pressure heat addition in the **Reheater** (steam re-warmed from T₂ to T₃ = T₁).

### Phase B — LP Loop (3 → 4 → 5 → 6 → 1)
- **Process 3→4:** Isentropic expansion in LP turbine (steam drops from P₂, T₃ to P₄).
- **Process 4→5:** Constant-pressure heat rejection in the condenser.
