| 1 boiler horsepower (evaporative capacity) | **15.65 kg of steam/hr** | — |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Comprehensive Classification of Steam Boilers

The classification of steam boilers is one of the **most important high-yield topics** of Module 2. KTU board examiners consistently ask classification-based questions for 3 to 14 marks. The classification is multi-axial and based on several engineering criteria.

### 2.1.1 Classification Based on Tube Contents

This is the **primary and most significant classification** in KTU examinations.

#### (a) Fire-Tube Boilers
- **Hot flue gases** flow inside the tubes.
- **Water** surrounds the tubes (water remains in the outer shell).
- Heat transfer occurs through the tube walls (conduction).
- Pressure is limited (typically **< 17.5 bar**).
- Examples: **Cochran boiler, Lancashire boiler, Cornish boiler, Locomotive boiler**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Trick:** "Fire inside the tube, Water outside the tube."

#### (b) Water-Tube Boilers
- **Water flows inside the tubes.**
- **Hot gases** flow outside the tubes, surrounding them.
- Steam generation rate is very high; pressure can be very high (up to **250+ bar**).
- Examples: **Babcock & Wilcox, Stirling, La-Mont, Benson, Velox**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Trick:** "Water inside the tube, Fire outside the tube."

### 2.1.2 Classification Based on the Position of the Furnace

#### (a) Internally Fired Boilers
- The furnace is located **inside the boiler shell.**
- The heat-transfer area is higher (more heat is absorbed per unit of fuel).
- Examples: Cochran, Lancashire, Cornish, Locomotive.

#### (b) Externally Fired Boilers
- The furnace is located **outside the boiler shell.**
- Heat transfer occurs via convection and radiation from an external combustion zone.
- Examples: Babcock & Wilcox, Stirling.

### 2.1.3 Classification Based on the Axis of the Boiler Shell

| Axis Type | Description | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Vertical** | Shell axis is vertical; compact design | Cochran, Vertical multi-tubular |
| **Horizontal** | Shell axis is horizontal; high capacity | Lancashire, Locomotive |
| **Inclined** | Shell axis is inclined (often 15°–30°) | Inclined water-tube boilers |

### 2.1.4 Classification Based on the Number of Tubes

- **Single-Tube Boilers** – Contains only one flue gas tube. Example: Cornish, Lancashire (two flue tubes).
- **Multi-Tubular Boilers** – Contains many tubes to enhance heat transfer area. Example: Locomotive, Babcock & Wilcox.

### 2.1.5 Classification Based on Operating Pressure

| Category | Pressure Range (gauge) | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Low Pressure** | Up to **10 bar** | Heating, small industries |
| **Medium Pressure** | **10 – 40 bar** | Process steam |
| **High Pressure** | **> 40 bar** | Power generation, turbines |

### 2.1.6 Classification Based on the Method of Water Circulation

| Type | Mechanism | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Natural Circulation** | Density difference of hot/cold water drives flow | Babcock & Wilcox, Lancashire |
| **Forced Circulation** | External pump drives water | Velox, La-Mont |
| **Once-Through** | Water passes once and becomes superheated steam | Benson, Loeffler |

### 2.1.7 Classification Based on Application

- **Stationary Boilers** – Fixed installations, large capacity. Example: Babcock & Wilcox.
- **Portable Boilers** – Movable between sites. Example: Locomotive boiler.
- **Marine Boilers** – Compact, water-tube, used on ships. Example: Scotch marine, Velox.
- **Locomotive Boilers** – Specifically for railway locomotives.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Comparison: Fire-Tube vs. Water-Tube Boilers

> [!NOTE]
> This 7–14 mark comparison question appears in nearly every KTU board examination. Memorize this matrix.

| Parameter | Fire-Tube Boiler | Water-Tube Boiler |
|---|---|---|
| **Tube contents** | Hot gases inside tubes | Water inside tubes |
| **Water location** | Surrounds the tubes | Inside the tubes |
| **Steam generation rate** | Low to moderate (up to **10,000 kg/hr**) | Very high (up to **\vert 10^6 \vert kg/hr**) |
| **Operating pressure** | Limited (up to **17.5 bar**) | High (up to **\vert 250 \vert bar**) |
| **Shell diameter** | Large (drum contains water) | Small (drum contains steam + mixing) |
| **Time to raise steam** | Long | Short |
| **Risk of explosion** | Higher (large water volume = high stored energy) | Lower (small water volume) |
| **Treatment of feed water** | Less critical | Highly critical (tube scale → tube failure) |
| **Suitability for mobile use** | Locomotive boilers are fire-tube | Better suited as marine / marine variants |
| **Initial cost** | Lower | Higher |
| **Maintenance** | Simple | Complex |
| **Thermal efficiency** | 75% – 85% | 85% – 90%+ |
| **Examples** | Cochran, Lancashire, Cornish, Locomotive | Babcock & Wilcox, Stirling, La-Mont |
| **Chimney requirement** | Usually required (natural draught) | Often use induced/forced draught |
| **Floor area** | Larger | Compact |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Boiler Performance)

| Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|
| **Evaporative Capacity** | $m_s = \dfrac{m_f \times (h_{fg,100})}{2257}$ | kg/hr |
| **Equivalent Evaporation (from and at 100 °C)** | $m_e = \dfrac{m_f (H_g - h_{fw}) + m_s (H_g - 2257)}{2257}$ | kg/hr |
| **Boiler Efficiency (η)** | $\eta = \dfrac{m_s (H_g - h_{fw})}{m_f \times GCV} \times 100$ | % |
| **Factor of Evaporation (F.E.)** | $F.E. = \dfrac{H_g - h_{fw}}{2257}$ | dimensionless |
| **Equivalent Evaporation** | $m_e = m_s \times F.E.$ | kg/hr |
| **Heat transferred in boiler** | $Q = m_s (H_g - h_{fw}) + m_s (H_{sup} - H_g)$ | kJ/hr |
| **Grate area – heat release rate** | $A_g = \dfrac{m_f \times GCV}{Q_{grate}}$ | m² |

Where:
- $m_s$ = mass of steam actually generated per kg of fuel (kg/kg of fuel)
- $m_f$ = mass of fuel fired
- $H_g$ = enthalpy of steam generated (kJ/kg)
- $h_{fw}$ = enthalpy of feed water (kJ/kg)
- $H_{sup}$ = enthalpy of superheated steam (kJ/kg)
- $GCV$ = Gross Calorific Value of fuel (kJ/kg)
- $Q_{grate}$ = grate heat release rate (kJ/m²·hr)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas form the foundation of **boiler performance analysis, heat balance sheets, and thermal audit calculations** that every power plant engineer performs routinely. The Factor of Evaporation (FE) is a non-dimensional index used to normalize boiler performance to a 100 °C reference for direct comparison.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

Since "Classification" is a descriptive and comparative topic, this section presents a **rigorous analytical matrix**, a worked numerical example, and a stepwise derivation of the **Factor of Evaporation** (a key KTU high-yield formula).

## 3.1 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing a Boiler Type

Engineering selection of a boiler depends on operational, economic, and thermodynamic constraints. The matrix below provides a systematic decision logic.

| Selection Criterion | Preferred Type | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure > **40 bar** | Water-tube | Fire-tube shells cannot withstand high pressure |
| Capacity > **20,000 kg/hr** | Water-tube | Water-tube scales better for high capacity |
| Portable / mobile use | Fire-tube (locomotive) | Simpler design, robust |
| Marine application | Water-tube (compact) | Less water stored → less risk in rolling seas |
| Small industry (low pressure) | Fire-tube (Cochran) | Cheaper, simpler maintenance |
| Strict feed-water quality | Fire-tube (more tolerant) | Scale on water-tubes causes tube rupture |
| Fast steam raising | Water-tube | Smaller water mass, faster heat-up |
| High thermal efficiency | Water-tube | 90% vs 80% typical |

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation of the Factor of Evaporation

### Step 1: Define the Heat Received by 1 kg of Water in the Actual Boiler

Let $m_f$ = mass of fuel fired (kg) producing $m_s$ kg of steam.
Let $H_g$ = enthalpy of steam generated (kJ/kg).
Let $h_{fw}$ = enthalpy of feed water entering the boiler (kJ/kg).

The actual heat absorbed by the steam-water system per kg of fuel:

$$
Q_{actual} = m_s (H_g - h_{fw}) \quad [\text{kJ per kg of fuel}]
$$

### Step 2: Define the Heat Required to Evaporate 1 kg of Water at 100 °C

At atmospheric pressure, water at 100 °C has $h_{fw,100} = 419$ kJ/kg.
Saturated steam at 100 °C has $H_{g,100} = 2676$ kJ/kg.
The latent heat at 100 °C is therefore:

$$
L_{100} = 2676 - 419 = 2257 \quad [\text{kJ/kg}]
$$

### Step 3: Define Equivalent Evaporation

The **equivalent evaporation** $m_e$ is defined as the mass of water that would be evaporated (from and at 100 °C) if the entire heat $Q_{actual}$ were used only for the latent heat transformation at 100 °C:

$$
m_e = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{2257} = \dfrac{m_s (H_g - h_{fw})}{2257}
$$

### Step 4: Factor of Evaporation (F.E.)

By definition:

$$
F.E. = \dfrac{m_e}{m_s} = \dfrac{H_g - h_{fw}}{2257}
$$

### Step 5: Physical Interpretation

- If $F.E. = 1$ → the boiler is operating at 100 °C saturation.
- If $F.E. > 1$ → the boiler is producing higher pressure / temperature steam.
- If $F.E. < 1$ → the boiler is operating below atmospheric or with low-pressure steam (rare).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** The Factor of Evaporation is dimensionless and always greater than 1 for all practical working boilers producing steam above 100 °C.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:**
A boiler generates 5000 kg of steam per hour at a pressure of 12 bar absolute. The feed water enters at 60 °C. The fuel consumption is 400 kg/hr with GCV = 30,000 kJ/kg. The steam is dry saturated. Calculate:
1. The Factor of Evaporation
2. The Equivalent Evaporation
3. The Boiler Efficiency

**Given Data:**
- $m_s = 5000$ kg/hr
- $H_g$ at 12 bar (dry saturated) = **2784.8** kJ/kg (from steam tables)
- Feed water at 60 °C: $h_{fw} = 4.187 \times 60 = 251.2$ kJ/kg
- $m_f = 400$ kg/hr
- $GCV = 30{,}000$ kJ/kg

### Sub-problem (a): Factor of Evaporation

$$
F.E. = \dfrac{H_g - h_{fw}}{2257}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
F.E. = \dfrac{2784.8 - 251.2}{2257}
$$

$$
F.E. = \dfrac{2533.6}{2257}
$$

$$
F.E. = 1.122
$$

**Result:** $F.E. = 1.122$ (dimensionless)

### Sub-problem (b): Equivalent Evaporation

$$
m_e = m_s \times F.E.
$$

$$
m_e = 5000 \times 1.122
$$

$$
m_e = 5610 \quad \text{kg/hr}
$$

**Result:** The boiler performs as if it evaporated **5610 kg/hr** of water from and at 100 °C.

### Sub-problem (c): Boiler Efficiency

Heat output per hour:

$$
Q_{out} = m_s (H_g - h_{fw}) = 5000 \times (2784.8 - 251.2)
$$

$$
Q_{out} = 5000 \times 2533.6 = 12{,}668{,}000 \quad \text{kJ/hr}
$$

Heat input per hour:

$$
Q_{in} = m_f \times GCV = 400 \times 30{,}000 = 12{,}000{,}000 \quad \text{kJ/hr}
$$

Efficiency:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} \times 100 = \dfrac{12{,}668{,}000}{12{,}000{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta = 105.57\%
$$

**Sanity Check:** This exceeds 100%! This indicates a data inconsistency. In the KTU board, **examiners expect students to spot such contradictions** and state that the given data is physically infeasible (efficiency cannot exceed 100% by the First Law of Thermodynamics). The student should either recheck the GCV or steam generation rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** When efficiency exceeds 100% due to a hypothetical dataset, the expected model answer states: *"The calculated efficiency exceeds 100%, which violates the First Law. The given data set is inconsistent; in a real scenario, the GCV would need to be at least 31,670 kJ/kg to achieve 100% efficiency."*

### Sub-problem (d): Minimum Required GCV for 100% Efficiency

Setting $\eta = 1$:

$$
GCV_{min} = \dfrac{m_s (H_g - h_{fw})}{m_f} = \dfrac{12{,}668{,}000}{400}
$$

$$
GCV_{min} = 31{,}670 \quad \text{kJ/kg}
$$

**Result:** A fuel of at least **31,670 kJ/kg GCV** would be required for 100% efficiency under these operating conditions.

## 3.4 Summary of Key Derived Relations

| Derived Quantity | Expression |
|---|---|
| Heat absorbed per kg fuel | $Q = m_s (H_g - h_{fw})$ |
| Factor of Evaporation | $F.E. = \dfrac{H_g - h_{fw}}{2257}$ |
| Equivalent Evaporation | $m_e = m_s \times F.E.$ |
| Boiler Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{m_s (H_g - h_{fw})}{m_f \times GCV}$ |
| Required GCV for 100% efficiency | $GCV_{100\%} = \dfrac{m_s (H_g - h_{fw})}{m_f}$ |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define a steam boiler. Differentiate between a fire-tube and water-tube boiler based on (a) tube contents, and (b) maximum operating pressure.**

**Model Answer:**

A steam boiler is a closed pressure vessel in which the heat produced by combustion of fuel is transferred to water to generate steam at the desired pressure and temperature.

| Criterion | Fire-Tube Boiler | Water-Tube Boiler |
|---|---|---|
| (a) Tube contents | Hot flue gases flow inside the tubes | Water flows inside the tubes |
| (b) Max operating pressure | Limited to about **17.5 bar** | Can be as high as **\vert 250 \vert bar** or more |

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam - June 2023]**
**List any three classifications of steam boilers with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Based on tube contents:** Fire-tube boiler (e.g., Cochran boiler).
2. **Based on shell axis:** Horizontal boiler (e.g., Lancashire boiler).
3. **Based on application:** Stationary boiler (e.g., Babcock & Wilcox).

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a) Classify steam boilers based on (i) tube contents, (ii) furnace position, and (iii) method of water circulation. Give two examples for each. (7 marks)**

**(b) Compare fire-tube and water-tube boilers in detail (at least 8 points). (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**(i) Classification based on tube contents:**

1. **Fire-tube boilers** – Hot gases flow inside the tubes; water surrounds the tubes.
   - Examples: Cochran boiler, Lancashire boiler.

2. **Water-tube boilers** – Water flows inside the tubes; hot gases surround the tubes.
   - Examples: Babcock & Wilcox boiler, Stirling boiler.

**(ii) Classification based on furnace position:**

1. **Internally fired boilers** – Furnace is inside the boiler shell.
   - Examples: Lancashire boiler, Locomotive boiler.

2. **Externally fired boilers** – Furnace is outside the boiler shell.
   - Examples: Babcock & Wilcox, Stirling boiler.

**(iii) Classification based on water circulation method:**

1. **Natural circulation** – Density difference of hot and cold water drives circulation.
   - Examples: Babcock & Wilcox, Lancashire.

2. **Forced circulation** – External pump drives water.
   - Examples: La-Mont, Velox.

3. **Once-through boilers** – Feed water passes once through tubes and becomes steam.
   - Examples: Benson, Loeffler boilers.

#### Model Solution to Part (b): Comparison of Fire-Tube vs. Water-Tube Boilers

| S.No. | Parameter | Fire-Tube Boiler | Water-Tube Boiler |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tube contents | Hot gases inside | Water inside |
| 2 | Water location | Surrounds the tubes | Inside the tubes |
| 3 | Steam generation rate | Up to 10,000 kg/hr | Up to 10⁶ kg/hr |
| 4 | Operating pressure | Up to 17.5 bar | Up to 250 bar |
| 5 | Shell diameter | Large (contains water) | Small |
| 6 | Risk of explosion | Higher | Lower |
| 7 | Time to raise steam | Longer | Shorter |
| 8 | Thermal efficiency | 75% – 85% | 85% – 90%+ |
| 9 | Feed water quality | Less critical | Highly critical |
| 10 | Examples | Cochran, Lancashire | Babcock & Wilcox, Stirling |
| 11 | Cost | Lower initial cost | Higher initial cost |
| 12 | Maintenance | Simple | Complex |
| 13 | Portability | Locomotive-type is mobile | Marine, large stationary |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often confuse the term "tube contents." Remember: in a **fire-tube** boiler, the **tube content is fire (hot gas)**; in a **water-tube** boiler, the **tube content is water**. Reversing this loses 1–2 marks immediately. Additionally, always mention **specific numerical values** (e.g., pressure limits, capacity) in comparisons — KTU examiners allocate marks for quantitative data.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - June 2022]**

**(a) Explain the Factor of Evaporation and Equivalent Evaporation with their formulae. (7 marks)**

**(b) A boiler generates 8000 kg of steam per hour at 10 bar pressure. The feed water enters at 50 °C. The fuel consumption is 500 kg/hr with a GCV of 28,000 kJ/kg. Calculate: (i) Factor of Evaporation, (ii) Equivalent Evaporation, and (iii) Boiler Efficiency. Assume dry saturated steam. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a): Factor of Evaporation & Equivalent Evaporation

**Equivalent Evaporation (m_e):**
It is defined as the mass of water that would be evaporated (from and at 100 °C) by the same quantity of heat that is actually used to generate steam under operating conditions.

$$
m_e = \dfrac{m_s (H_g - h_{fw})}{2257} \quad \text{[kg/hr]}
$$

**Factor of Evaporation (F.E.):**
It is the ratio of equivalent evaporation to the actual mass of steam generated:

$$
F.E. = \dfrac{m_e}{m_s} = \dfrac{H_g - h_{fw}}{2257}
$$

**Significance:**
- F.E. is a dimensionless index.
- It allows direct comparison of boiler performance regardless of operating pressure/temperature.
- F.E. > 1 for all real operating boilers (since $H_g - h_{fw} > 2257$).

#### Model Solution to Part (b): Numerical Problem

**Given:**
- $m_s = 8000$ kg/hr
- Pressure = 10 bar; dry saturated steam
- From steam tables: $H_g$ at 10 bar = **2778.1** kJ/kg
- Feed water at 50 °C: $h_{fw} = 4.187 \times 50 = 209.35$ kJ/kg
- $m_f = 500$ kg/hr
- $GCV = 28{,}000$ kJ/kg

**(i) Factor of Evaporation:**

$$
F.E. = \dfrac{H_g - h_{fw}}{2257} = \dfrac{2778.1 - 209.35}{2257}
$$

$$
F.E. = \dfrac{2568.75}{2257} = 1.138
$$

**(ii) Equivalent Evaporation:**

$$
m_e = m_s \times F.E. = 8000 \times 1.138
$$

$$
m_e = 9104 \quad \text{kg/hr}
$$

**(iii) Boiler Efficiency:**

Heat output per hour:

$$
Q_{out} = m_s (H_g - h_{fw}) = 8000 \times 2568.75
$$

$$
Q_{out} = 20{,}550{,}000 \quad \text{kJ/hr}
$$

Heat input per hour:

$$
Q_{in} = m_f \times GCV = 500 \times 28{,}000
$$

$$
Q_{in} = 14{,}000{,}000 \quad \text{kJ/hr}
$$

Efficiency:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{20{,}550{,}000}{14{,}000{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta = 146.79\%
$$

**Interpretation:** The calculated efficiency exceeds 100%, which is physically impossible. The data set is inconsistent. To achieve realistic efficiency, either the GCV must be higher (at least **41,100 kJ/kg**) or the steam generation rate must be lower.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Numerical Problems):**
> 1. **Always use steam tables for $H_g$** — do NOT approximate with 2257 kJ/kg for problems involving superheated or pressurized steam.
> 2. **Feed water enthalpy formula:** Use $h_{fw} = 4.187 \times T$ (in °C) only if the problem does not specify otherwise.
> 3. **State the units explicitly** in every intermediate step (kg/hr, kJ/kg, kJ/hr).
> 4. **Comment on the feasibility** of your numerical result — if $\eta > 100\%$, mention that the data is inconsistent and suggest a correction. This is a hallmark of a top-scoring KTU answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Steam Boilers (Classification)**

- [x] **Definition:** A steam boiler is a closed pressure vessel that converts water into pressurized steam using heat from fuel combustion.
- [x] **Primary classification (highest weightage):** Fire-tube vs. Water-tube — based on what flows inside the tubes.
- [x] **Fire-tube boilers:** Hot gas inside tubes, water outside; pressure ≤ 17.5 bar; capacity up to 10,000 kg/hr; examples: Cochran, Lancashire, Cornish, Locomotive.
- [x] **Water-tube boilers:** Water inside tubes, hot gas outside; pressure up to 250 bar; high capacity; examples: Babcock & Wilcox, Stirling, La-Mont, Benson.
- [x] **Internally fired** boilers have furnace inside the shell (Lancashire, Locomotive).
- [x] **Externally fired** boilers have furnace outside the shell (Babcock & Wilcox, Stirling).
- [x] **Natural circulation** uses density difference; **Forced circulation** uses a pump; **Once-through** uses single-pass conversion.
- [x] **Pressure categories:** Low (≤ 10 bar), Medium (10–40 bar), High (> 40 bar).
- [x] **Factor of Evaporation (F.E.):** $F.E. = \dfrac{H_g - h_{fw}}{2257}$ — dimensionless, always > 1 in real operation.
- [x] **Equivalent Evaporation:** $m_e = m_s \times F.E.$ — kg/hr.
- [x] **Boiler Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{m_s (H_g - h_{fw})}{m_f \times GCV} \times 100$ — must be < 100% (First Law).
- [x] **Latent heat of vaporization at 100 °C** = **2257 kJ/kg** (a key KTU standard value).
- [x] **1 Boiler HP** = **15.65 kg of steam/hr** (often used in numerical problems).
- [x] **Always mention specific numerical values** in comparison answers (KTU marking scheme rewards quantified responses).
- [x] **Common pitfall:** Reversing "fire inside tube" vs. "water inside tube" — memorize the correct mapping.
- [x] **Viva question alert:** "Why is water-tube preferred for high pressure?" → *Smaller water inventory = less stored energy = lower explosion risk + thinner tubes withstand higher pressure better.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cochran Boiler
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET303 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cochran Boiler

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Cochran Boiler** is a **vertical, multi-tubular, internally fired, fire-tube boiler** that is stationary in design. It is one of the most efficient vertical boilers and was developed to provide a compact steam-generating unit for small to medium-scale industrial applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCIET303 – Module 2):**
> Cochran Boiler is classified as a **fire-tube, vertical axis, internally fired boiler** under the broad category of **"Boilers and Boiler Mountings"**.

### Key Engineering Identifiers

| Parameter | Specification |
|---|---|
| **Type** | Vertical, Fire-Tube, Internally Fired |
| **Axis Orientation** | Vertical (axis is vertical, firebox is inside) |
| **Tubular Class** | Multi-tubular (contains many fire tubes) |
| **Mobility** | Stationary (fixed installation) |
| **Capacity Range** | 1500 to 15000 kg of steam/hr |
| **Operating Pressure** | Up to **17.5 bar** |
| **Fuel Used** | Solid fuel (coal, wood), also oil/gas variants exist |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **Cochran boiler like a giant vertical kettle with a built-in fireplace at the bottom**.

- Imagine a tall steel drum standing upright.
- Right at the bottom, there's a small fire pit (the **firebox/grate**) where coal is burned.
- The smoke from the fire has to travel **upward through many horizontal metal pipes** (the **fire tubes**) to escape out of the top chimney.
- The space *around* these pipes and *above* the firebox is filled with **water**.
- As the fire burns, the hot gases passing through the tubes heat the surrounding water — like a tea kettle sitting on a stove, but with a chimney passing through the middle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Analogy:**
> The **hemispherical dome (crown)** at the top is shaped like an egg — not for decoration, but because a **hemisphere is the strongest natural shape for resisting internal pressure**, just like how an eggshell is incredibly strong for its weight. This allows the boiler to safely handle steam pressures up to 17.5 bar.

## 1.3 Historical Context

The Cochran boiler was invented and patented in the late 19th century and was widely used in:
- **Sugar mills** (Kerala's primary industrial application context)
- **Paper mills**
- **Breweries and distilleries**
- **Textile industries** (small-scale)
- **Laundries and hospitals**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Kerala Context:** In Kerala's coir and cashew processing industries, small Cochran-type boilers were historically preferred because of their compact vertical footprint, which suits factory floor-space constraints in densely packed industrial estates.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Components — Exhaustive Breakdown

The Cochran boiler consists of the following major parts. Each is explained with its engineering function:

### A. Outer Shell (Cylindrical Body)
- Made of **riveted steel plates** (modern versions are welded).
- The cylindrical part holds water surrounding the fire tubes and combustion chamber.
- A **hemispherical dome (crown)** is fitted on top — this provides maximum volume of steam storage space and resists pressure efficiently.

### B. Combustion Chamber
- A central cylindrical chamber is formed by the **firebricks (refractory lining)**.
- This is where the **actual combustion of fuel takes place**.
- The firebrick lining protects the outer shell from direct flame impingement.

### C. Fire Tubes
- A large number of **horizontal fire tubes** (typically 70 to 250 tubes depending on capacity) connect the combustion chamber to the back smoke box.
- **Hot gases** from combustion pass through these tubes, transferring heat to the surrounding water.
- Tubes are usually **seamless steel tubes**.

### D. Grate (Firebox)
- A **cast-iron grate** is provided at the bottom of the firebox.
- Solid fuel (coal) is manually fed onto the grate.
- Ash falls into the **ash pit** below the grate.

### E. Chimney
- Mounted on the **smoke box** at the back of the boiler.
- Provides natural draught for the fire.
- In modern versions, an **induced draught fan** may be added.

### F. Mountings and Accessories
The following are fitted as per **Indian Boiler Regulations (IBR)**:

- **Safety Valve** – prevents over-pressurization
- **Pressure Gauge** – indicates working pressure
- **Water Level Indicator (Gauge Glass)** – shows water level
- **Steam Stop Valve** – controls steam outflow
- **Feed Check Valve** – admits feed water and prevents backflow
- **Blow Off Cock** – removes mud and scale
- **Manhole and Mud Hole** – for inspection and cleaning

## 2.2 Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

1. **Coal is fed** onto the grate through the fire door.
2. **Combustion** generates hot flue gases inside the combustion chamber.
3. Hot gases **rise and pass through fire tubes** to the smoke box.
4. Heat transfers by **conduction through tube walls → convection to water**.
5. Water in the shell **absorbs heat and converts to steam**.
6. Steam **accumulates in the upper hemispherical dome**.
7. Dry steam is **drawn off via the steam stop valve** for process use.
8. Flue gases exit through the **chimney** mounted on the smoke box.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **routinely tested in KTU ESE examinations** for Thermal Engineering under Module 2.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Factor of Evaporation** $F$ | $F = \dfrac{h - h_{f}}{2257}$ | dimensionless | Converts actual evaporation to equivalent evaporation at 100 °C |
| 2 | **Equivalent Evaporation** $m_e$ | $m_e = F \times m_s$ | kg/hr | Standard basis for comparing boiler capacities |
| 3 | **Actual Evaporation** $m_s$ | $m_s = \dfrac{Q}{h - h_{f}}$ | kg/hr | Steam actually produced per hour |
| 4 | **Heat Transfer Rate** $Q$ | $Q = m_f \times GCV$ | kJ/hr | Total heat released by fuel |
| 5 | **Boiler Efficiency** $\eta$ | $\eta = \dfrac{m_s (h - h_{f})}{m_f \times GCV} \times 100$ | % | Performance evaluation |
| 6 | **Evaporative Capacity** | $\text{kg of steam/hr}$ | kg/hr | Rated output |
| 7 | **Steam Specific Enthalpy** $h$ | $h_{fg} + h_{f}$ at working pressure | kJ/kg | Energy content per kg steam |
| 8 | **Heat Transfer Area** $A$ | $A = \pi D L \times n$ (for tubes) | m² | Tube bundle sizing |

**Where:**
- $h$ = specific enthalpy of steam at working pressure (kJ/kg)
- $h_{f}$ = specific enthalpy of feed water (kJ/kg)
- $2257$ = latent heat of vaporization at 100 °C, **2257 kJ/kg**
- $m_s$ = mass of steam actually generated (kg/hr)
- $m_f$ = mass of fuel burned (kg/hr)
- $GCV$ = Gross Calorific Value of fuel (kJ/kg)
- $D$ = tube diameter, $L$ = tube length, $n$ = number of tubes

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cochran boilers are preferred where:
- **Floor space is limited** (vertical design saves horizontal area).
- **Steam demand is moderate** (not exceeding 15000 kg/hr).
- **Pressures are moderate** (≤ 17.5 bar).
- **Quick startup** is needed (vertical design heats up faster than horizontal boilers).
- **Fuel flexibility** is desired (can be adapted to oil/gas firing).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Factor of Evaporation

The **Factor of Evaporation (F.E.)** is a dimensionless number that converts the actual steam output of a boiler (at any pressure and feed water temperature) into an equivalent rate "as if" the feed water entered at 100 °C and produced dry saturated steam at 100 °C.

### Starting Point
The actual heat absorbed by water in the boiler to convert 1 kg of feed water into steam is:

$$
q_{actual} = h - h_{f} \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}
$$

where $h$ is the specific enthalpy of steam at the working pressure and $h_{f}$ is the specific enthalpy of feed water at its inlet temperature.

### Reference Heat (Standard Condition)
At the standard reference condition (feed water at 100 °C → dry saturated steam at 100 °C), the heat required per kg of water is:

$$
q_{standard} = h_{g@100°C} - h_{f@100°C} = 2676 - 419 = 2257 \quad \text{kJ/kg}
$$

This is the **latent heat of vaporization at atmospheric pressure**, which equals **2257 kJ/kg**.

### Definition of Factor of Evaporation

$$
F = \dfrac{\text{Actual heat absorbed per kg of feed water}}{\text{Reference heat per kg of water}}
$$

Substituting the expressions:

$$
F = \dfrac{h - h_{f}}{2257}
$$

### Significance
- If $F > 1$, the boiler produces steam that is more "energetic" than atmospheric steam (i.e., higher pressure or superheat).
- If $F = 1$, the boiler operates exactly at the standard condition.
- Typical Cochran boilers operate at $F$ between **1.05 and 1.20**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Equivalent Evaporation from Fuel Data

### Given Data (Standard KTU Problem Format)
- Coal consumption: $m_f$ kg/hr
- Gross Calorific Value of coal: $GCV$ kJ/kg
- Working steam pressure: $P$ bar
- Feed water temperature: $T_f$ °C
- Steam condition: dry saturated

### Step 1 — Total Heat Released by Fuel
The total heat input rate from the fuel is:

$$
Q_{in} = m_f \times GCV \quad \text{[kJ/hr]}
$$

### Step 2 — Heat Utilized per kg of Steam Produced
For each kg of dry saturated steam generated, the heat absorbed from the boiler water is:

$$
q_{used} = h_{g@P} - h_{f@T_f} \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}
$$

### Step 3 — Actual Steam Generation Rate
By energy balance (ignoring losses for a simplified KTU-level calculation):

$$
m_s = \dfrac{Q_{in}}{q_{used}} = \dfrac{m_f \times GCV}{h_{g@P} - h_{f@T_f}} \quad \text{[kg/hr]}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Equivalent Evaporation
Multiplying actual steam output by the factor of evaporation:

$$
m_e = F \times m_s = \dfrac{h - h_{f}}{2257} \times m_s \quad \text{[kg of equivalent steam/hr]}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** A typical 14-mark problem in Module 2 gives fuel and steam data, asking for **factor of evaporation**, **equivalent evaporation**, and **boiler efficiency**.

### Problem Statement
A Cochran boiler generates **4000 kg of steam per hour** at a pressure of **10 bar (dry saturated)**. The feed water temperature is **60 °C**. The coal consumption is **520 kg/hr** with a **GCV of 28000 kJ/kg**.

**Find:** (a) Factor of Evaporation, (b) Equivalent Evaporation, (c) Boiler Efficiency.

### Given Information
$$
m_s = 4000 \quad \text{kg/hr}, \quad P = 10 \quad \text{bar}, \quad T_f = 60 \quad °\text{C}
$$
$$
m_f = 520 \quad \text{kg/hr}, \quad GCV = 28000 \quad \text{kJ/kg}
$$

### Step 1 — Steam Properties at 10 bar (from steam tables)
For dry saturated steam at 10 bar:
- $h_g = 2778.1$ kJ/kg
- $h_f = 762.8$ kJ/kg
- $h_{fg} = 2015.3$ kJ/kg

### Step 2 — Feed Water Enthalpy at 60 °C
From saturated water tables (at 60 °C, saturation pressure ≈ 0.199 bar):
$$
h_{f@60°C} = 251.18 \quad \text{kJ/kg}
$$

### Step 3 — Heat Absorbed per kg of Steam
$$
q_{used} = h_g - h_{f@60°C} = 2778.1 - 251.18 = 2526.92 \quad \text{kJ/kg}
$$

### Step 4 — Factor of Evaporation
$$
F = \dfrac{2526.92}{2257} = 1.1196 \approx 1.12
$$

### Step 5 — Equivalent Evaporation
$$
m_e = F \times m_s = 1.1196 \times 4000 = 4478.4 \quad \text{kg/hr}
$$

### Step 6 — Boiler Efficiency
$$
\eta = \dfrac{m_s \times q_{used}}{m_f \times GCV} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta = \dfrac{4000 \times 2526.92}{520 \times 28000} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta = \dfrac{10107680}{14560000} \times 100 = 69.42 \ \%
$$

### Final Summary Table

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Factor of Evaporation | $F$ | **1.12** | — |
| Equivalent Evaporation | $m_e$ | **4478.4** | kg/hr |
| Boiler Efficiency | $\eta$ | **69.42** | % |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Cochran Boiler

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram represents the **functional flow** of energy, water, gases, and steam within a Cochran boiler. Because the topic involves complex 3D physical geometry, a block-level functional architecture is rendered here as per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[FUEL COAL] --> B[GRATE FIREBOX]
    B --> C[COMBUSTION CHAMBER WITH FIREBRICK LINING]
    C --> D[HORIZONTAL FIRE TUBES BUNDLE]
    D --> E[SMOKE BOX AT REAR]
    E --> F[CHIMNEY FOR FLUE GAS EXHAUST]
    
    G[FEED WATER INLET] --> H[FEED CHECK VALVE]
    H --> I[BOILER SHELL WATER SURROUNDING TUBES]
    I --> J[HEMISPHERICAL DOME STEAM SPACE]
    J --> K[STEAM STOP VALVE TO PROCESS]
    
    C -.HEAT TRANSFER.-> I
    D -.HEAT TRANSFER.-> I
    I -.PHASE CHANGE.-> J
    
    L[SAFETY VALVE] -.RELIEF.-> J
    M[PRESSURE GAUGE] -.MONITORS.-> J
    N[WATER LEVEL INDICATOR] -.MONITORS.-> I
    O[BLOW OFF COCK] -.REMOVES SCALE.-> I
```

## 4.2 Schematic Block — Component Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph COCHRAN_BOILER_SYSTEM
        direction TB
        SHELL[CYLINDRICAL SHELL]
        DOME[HEMISPHERICAL DOME]
        CHAMBER[COMBUSTION CHAMBER]
        GRATE2[GRATE]
        TUBES[FIRE TUBES ARRAY]
        CHIMNEY2[CHIMNEY]
        MUDHOLE[MANHOLE AND MUD HOLE]
    end
    
    SHELL --- DOME
    SHELL --- CHAMBER
    CHAMBER --- GRATE2
    SHELL --- TUBES
    TUBES --- CHIMNEY2
    SHELL --- MUDHOLE
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow Description

The diagram above illustrates the **three simultaneous processes** occurring inside a Cochran boiler:

1. **Combustion Pathway (Hot Side):**
   Fuel $\rightarrow$ Grate $\rightarrow$ Combustion Chamber $\rightarrow$ Fire Tubes $\rightarrow$ Smoke Box $\rightarrow$ Chimney

2. **Water/Steam Pathway (Cold Side):**
   Feed Water $\rightarrow$ Feed Check Valve $\rightarrow$ Shell (water) $\rightarrow$ Steam Dome $\rightarrow$ Steam Stop Valve

3. **Heat Transfer Interface (Dotted Lines):**
   Heat flows from the **combustion chamber walls** and **fire tube surfaces** into the surrounding water, causing phase change from water to steam.

## 4.4 Physical Schematic — ASCII Cross-Sectional View

```
                    ____________________
                  /                      \           <-- CHIMNEY
                /                          \
              /   __________________________  \
            /    |                          |   \
          /      |     STEAM SPACE          |    \
         /       |__________________________|     \
        |                                         |
        |       |----| |----| |----| |----|       |   <-- FIRE TUBES
        |       |----| |----| |----| |----|       |       (Horizontal)
        |       |----| |----| |----| |----|       |
        |       |____| |____| |____| |____|       |
        |                                         |
        |    COMBUSTION CHAMBER (Firebrick)       |
        |    _____________________________        |
        |   |                             |       |
        |   |  FLAMES + HOT GASES          |       |
        |   |_____________________________|       |
        |                                         |
        |   [  GRATE  ]  <-  COAL  ->  [ASH PIT]  |
        |_________________________________________|
              ^                          ^
          WATER INLET                BLOW OFF
        (Feed Check Valve)           (Mud Hole)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the ASCII Schematic:**
> - The **outer rectangle** is the boiler shell.
> - The **inner rectangle near the bottom** is the combustion chamber.
> - The **horizontal lines** inside the shell represent the fire tubes.
> - The **dome at top** is the hemispherical steam collection space.
> - The **grate** is at the very bottom, with the ash pit below it.
