# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"List any six important parts of a Cochran boiler and state the function of each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.5 marks per part):**

| Part | Function |
|---|---|
| 1. Cylindrical Shell | Holds water and provides structural support |
| 2. Hemispherical Dome | Collects steam and resists pressure efficiently |
| 3. Combustion Chamber | Houses the burning fuel; lined with firebricks |
| 4. Fire Tubes | Carry hot gases; transfer heat to surrounding water |
| 5. Grate | Supports solid fuel during combustion |
| 6. Chimney | Discharges flue gases; creates natural draught |

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"State any three advantages and three disadvantages of a Cochran boiler."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.5 marks per point):**

**Advantages:**
1. **Compact vertical design** requires less floor space.
2. **Quick steam generation** due to large heating surface and vertical water circulation.
3. **Easy accessibility** of all internal parts through the manhole.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Limited capacity** — not suitable for large steam demands.
2. **Cannot withstand high pressures** beyond 17.5 bar.
3. **Inspection of fire tubes is difficult** because tubes are horizontal and densely packed.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

#### (a) With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a Cochran boiler. List its applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (4 marks):**
A Cochran boiler is a **vertical, multi-tubular, internally fired, fire-tube boiler**. It consists of:

- A **cylindrical shell** made of steel plates, with a **hemispherical dome (crown)** riveted/welded at the top.
- A **combustion chamber** centrally located in the lower part, lined with **refractory firebricks**.
- A large number of **horizontal fire tubes** (typically 70 to 250) connecting the combustion chamber to the smoke box at the rear.
- A **cast-iron grate** at the bottom for solid fuel firing, with an **ash pit** below.
- A **chimney** mounted on the smoke box to discharge flue gases.
- Standard mountings: **safety valve, pressure gauge, water level indicator, steam stop valve, feed check valve, blow off cock, manhole, and mud hole.**

**Working (2 marks):**
Coal is fed onto the grate and ignited. Combustion produces hot gases that rise through the combustion chamber and pass through the **fire tubes** to the smoke box and out via the chimney. Heat is transferred through the tube walls to the surrounding water, generating steam that accumulates in the upper hemispherical dome. Dry steam is drawn off through the **steam stop valve** for use.

**Applications (1 mark):**
- Sugar mills, paper mills, breweries, textile processing units, small-scale industries, laundries, and hospitals where moderate steam demand exists.

#### (b) Compare Cochran boiler with a Lancashire boiler. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Cochran Boiler | Lancashire Boiler |
|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Vertical, fire-tube, internally fired | Horizontal, fire-tube, internally fired |
| **Number of Tubes** | Many (70–250) horizontal fire tubes | Two large inclined fire tubes |
| **Capacity** | 1500–15000 kg/hr | Up to 50000 kg/hr or more |
| **Pressure** | Up to 17.5 bar | Up to 17.5 bar |
| **Floor Space** | Less (vertical) | More (horizontal) |
| **Inspection** | Difficult (many small tubes) | Easy (only two large tubes) |
| **Steam Generation Speed** | Faster (compact heating area) | Slower |
| **Application Scale** | Small to medium industry | Medium to large industry |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

#### (a) Explain the working principle of a Cochran boiler. State any four of its salient features. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle (4 marks):**
The Cochran boiler operates on the principle of **fire-tube heat exchange**:
- Solid fuel is burned on the **grate** inside a centrally placed **combustion chamber**.
- The **hot combustion gases** are forced to pass through a bundle of **horizontal fire tubes** that traverse the water-filled shell.
- As the gases give up heat to the surrounding water through the tube walls, the water converts to steam, which rises and accumulates in the **hemispherical dome** at the top.
- The dry steam is delivered to the process through the **steam stop valve**, while flue gases exit via the **chimney**.

**Salient Features (1 mark each, any four):**
1. **Vertical axis design** — saves floor space.
2. **Internally fired** — heat loss to surroundings is minimized.
3. **Multi-tubular construction** — large heating surface per unit volume.
4. **Hemispherical dome** — high pressure-withstanding capability.
5. **Quick steam raising** — high rate of heat transfer.

#### (b) A Cochran boiler produces **3000 kg of dry saturated steam per hour** at a pressure of **8 bar**. The feed water enters at **50 °C**. Coal is burned at the rate of **450 kg/hr** with a **GCV of 25000 kJ/kg**. Calculate: (i) Factor of Evaporation, (ii) Equivalent Evaporation, (iii) Thermal Efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
$m_s = 3000$ kg/hr, $P = 8$ bar, $T_f = 50$ °C, $m_f = 450$ kg/hr, $GCV = 25000$ kJ/kg

**From Steam Tables:**
- $h_g$ at 8 bar $= 2769.1$ kJ/kg
- $h_f$ at 50 °C $= 209.34$ kJ/kg

**Step (i) — Factor of Evaporation (2 marks):**
$$
F = \dfrac{h_g - h_{f@50°C}}{2257} = \dfrac{2769.1 - 209.34}{2257} = \dfrac{2559.76}{2257} = 1.134
$$

**Step (ii) — Equivalent Evaporation (2 marks):**
$$
m_e = F \times m_s = 1.134 \times 3000 = 3402 \quad \text{kg/hr}
$$

**Step (iii) — Thermal Efficiency (3 marks):**
$$
\eta = \dfrac{m_s \times (h_g - h_{f@50°C})}{m_f \times GCV} \times 100
$$
$$
\eta = \dfrac{3000 \times 2559.76}{450 \times 25000} \times 100
$$
$$
\eta = \dfrac{7679280}{11250000} \times 100 = 68.26 \ \%
$$

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Factor of Evaporation | **1.134** |
| Equivalent Evaporation | **3402 kg/hr** |
| Thermal Efficiency | **68.26 %** |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Steam Table Values:** Marks are deducted if students use approximate values without specifying that they are "from steam tables at 8 bar." Always write the units (kJ/kg) explicitly.
> 2. **Feed Water Enthalpy:** A common error is using $h_f = 4.18 \times T_f$ instead of the steam table value. While the linear approximation is sometimes acceptable in Class 12, **KTU ESE expects the steam table value** for any pressure > 1 bar.
> 3. **Factor of Evaporation:** The denominator must always be **2257 kJ/kg** — this is the latent heat at 100 °C. Using $h_{fg}$ at the working pressure is a **frequent mistake** that costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Units Consistency:** If the answer is in kg/hr, write it explicitly. Sloppy unit handling costs **0.5 to 1 mark** per occurrence.
> 5. **Efficiency Definition:** The formula $\eta = \dfrac{m_s(h - h_f)}{m_f \times GCV}$ assumes **GCV** (not NCV). If the problem gives NCV, mention that you are assuming GCV.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Cochran Boiler:**

- **Definition:** Vertical, multi-tubular, internally fired, fire-tube, **stationary** boiler.
- **Capacity:** 1500 – 15000 kg/hr.
- **Pressure:** Up to **17.5 bar**.
- **Inventor Era:** Late 19th century; popular in **Kerala's sugar/coir industries**.
- **Key Parts (must know all):** Shell, hemispherical dome, combustion chamber (firebrick-lined), fire tubes, grate, ash pit, chimney, smoke box.
- **Standard Mountings:** Safety valve, pressure gauge, water level indicator, steam stop valve, feed check valve, blow off cock, manhole, mud hole.
- **Working Sequence:** Coal on grate $\rightarrow$ Combustion $\rightarrow$ Hot gases through fire tubes $\rightarrow$ Heat to water $\rightarrow$ Steam in dome $\rightarrow$ Steam drawn off.
- **Advantages (3):** Compact, quick steam, easy maintenance access.
- **Disadvantages (3):** Limited capacity, low max pressure, difficult tube inspection.
- **Applications:** Sugar mills, paper mills, breweries, textiles, hospitals, small industries.
- **Factor of Evaporation:** $F = \dfrac{h - h_f}{2257}$
- **Equivalent Evaporation:** $m_e = F \times m_s$
- **Boiler Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{m_s(h - h_f)}{m_f \times GCV} \times 100$
- **Numericals to Master:** Problems combining factor of evaporation + equivalent evaporation + efficiency.
- **Comparison to Remember:** Cochran vs. Lancashire vs. Babcock-Wilcox (vertical vs. horizontal vs. water-tube).
- **Steam Table Data:** For 8 bar → $h_g \approx 2769$ kJ/kg; for 10 bar → $h_g \approx 2778$ kJ/kg; for atmospheric → $h_g = 2676$ kJ/kg.
- **Key Constant:** Latent heat at 100 °C = **2257 kJ/kg**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip:**
> When asked to "list parts and state functions," structure your answer in a **two-column table** (Part $\vert$ Function). Examiners award **partial marks** generously for tabular answers, even if one function is partially incorrect. Always prefer tables over long paragraphs for part-listing questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Babcock and Wilcox Boiler
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET303 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Babcock and Wilcox Boiler

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> The **Babcock and Wilcox (B&W) Boiler** is a **horizontal, stationary, externally fired, natural circulation, water-tube boiler** employing **bent water tubes** that connect an upper **steam drum** to a lower **water/mud drum**. It is a high-capacity, high-pressure boiler designed for power generation and large industrial steam demands, capable of producing steam up to **500,000 kg/hr** at pressures of **100 bar** and above.

The boiler was developed by **Stephen Wilcox and George Herman Babcock** in 1867, primarily to overcome the inherent safety limitations of the fire-tube (shell) boilers, namely **low steam pressure, low evaporation rate, and risk of catastrophic shell rupture**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Emphasis (2024 Scheme):**
> The B&W boiler is a **"high-yield topic"** under Module 2 (Steam Boilers). Examiners frequently test: (a) constructional details, (b) working principle with circulation, (c) the role of cross tubes and headers, and (d) comparison with Cochran/Lancashire boilers.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Multi-Story Water Elevator"

Imagine a **multi-story building** with a large water tank on the **roof** (steam drum) and a smaller sump tank in the **basement** (mud drum). Now connect the two with **bent transparent pipes** that pass through a hot furnace zone in the middle floors.

- Cold water from the basement is pushed up the pipes (by **convection + density difference**) into the roof tank as it gets heated.
- Steam forms at the top, water slides back down — creating a **continuous natural loop**.
- The bent shape allows for **thermal expansion** without cracking the tubes.

This is *exactly* how the B&W boiler works — the **bent tubes act as a natural elevator** for water, and the heat of the furnace does all the pumping work for free (no external pump needed for circulation).

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU Reference Values)

| Parameter | Standard Range |
|---|---|
| **Steam Capacity** | **50,000 – 500,000 kg/hr** |
| **Operating Pressure** | **17.5 – 100 bar** |
| **Efficiency (with accessories)** | **80 – 90 %** |
| **Type of Circulation** | **Natural (thermo-siphon)** |
| **Fuel Used** | Coal / Oil / Gas |
| **Grate Type** | Step grate / Travelling grate / Pulverized fuel |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used in B&W Boiler Analysis:**
> - Latent heat of vaporization of water, $h_{fg} = 2257 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (at $100\,^\circ\text{C}$)
> - Specific heat of superheated steam, $c_{ps} = 2.1 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$
> - Specific heat of water, $c_{pw} = 4.18 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Main Constructional Components (KTU Board Favourite — Asked Almost Every Year)

The B&W boiler consists of the following **major assemblies**:

1. **Steam Drum (Upper Drum)**
   - A large horizontal cylindrical drum made of riveted/ welded steel plates.
   - Contains **steam space** at the top and **water space** at the bottom.
   - Internally fitted with **steam separators / dryers** (baffles) to remove entrained moisture from steam.
   - Fitted with **manholes**, **safety valves**, **pressure gauges**, **water level glass**, and **feed check valve**.

2. **Water Drum / Mud Drum (Lower Drum)**
   - Smaller drum placed below the furnace.
   - Collects **sediment, sludge, and mud** (hence the name) — periodically blown down.
   - Houses the **cross tubes** and **headers**.

3. **Bent Water Tubes**
   - The defining feature of the B&W boiler.
   - Number of tubes ranges from **100 to 500** depending on capacity.
   - Diameter typically **75–100 mm**, expanded into the tube sheets.
   - Bent at an angle to allow **thermal expansion** without stressing the tube joints.
   - The lower ends are connected to the mud drum, the upper ends to the steam drum.

4. **Cross Tubes and Headers**
   - **Cross tubes** (short horizontal tubes) connect the front and rear water headers inside the mud drum.
   - They allow water to flow freely between headers and improve circulation.
   - **Headers** are the boxes at both front and rear ends into which the bent tubes are expanded.

5. **Furnace and Combustion Chamber**
   - Located just below the water tubes.
   - Furnace is refractory-lined, designed for complete combustion.
   - In modern versions, **water walls** (tubes lined with refractory) line the furnace walls.

6. **Superheater**
   - Placed in the path of hot flue gases (above the main bank of tubes).
   - Raises steam temperature from saturation to **$300\,^\circ\text{C}$ – $500\,^\circ\text{C}$** depending on pressure.
   - Improves thermal efficiency and prevents condensation in steam engines/turbines.

7. **Feed Water Supply and Check Valve**
   - Feed water enters the steam drum through a **feed check valve**.
   - Internally, it is distributed through **perforated distributing pipes** to avoid thermal shock.

8. **Baffles (Fire Bricks / Tube Baffles)**
   - Refractory baffles force the hot gases to take a **zig-zag / 3-pass path** through the tube bank.
   - Increases heat transfer and ensures all tubes receive hot gases.

9. **Grate (Step Grate / Travelling Grate)**
   - Supports the coal bed; in modern versions, replaced by **pulverized fuel burners**.

10. **Chimney / Flue Gas Exit**
    - Exhausts the combustion gases after they have surrendered heat to water, superheater, economizer, and air preheater.

### 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The B&W boiler operates on the principle of "Natural (Thermo-syphon) Circulation":**

1. **Feed water** enters the steam drum via the feed pump and check valve.
2. Water flows down through the downcomer pipes / large diameter tubes into the **mud drum** (lower header).
3. From the mud drum, water rises through the **bent water tubes** that are exposed to hot furnace gases on the outside.
4. The water absorbs heat, partially converts to **steam-water mixture** (this mixture is lighter than cold water).
5. The lighter mixture **rises** rapidly into the steam drum (the density difference between cold water in the downcomer and the hot mixture in the riser tubes creates a **natural convective flow**).
6. In the steam drum, steam separates from water (using **internally fitted baffles/separators**).
7. The separated steam passes over the **superheater** (a bank of small-bore tubes in the gas path) and gets superheated.
8. Superheated steam leaves the boiler for the turbine/engine.
9. The separated water returns to the mud drum to repeat the cycle.
10. Flue gases, after giving up heat, exit through the **chimney**.

**Why the tubes are bent?**
- Bent tubes can expand freely when heated, **preventing thermal stresses** and tube failures.
- Straight tubes would crack at the joints due to differential expansion.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are examinable under KTU 2024 Scheme. Memorize the units!**

| S.No. | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Equivalent Evaporation | $E_e = \dfrac{m_s \times (h_{sup} - h_{fw})}{2257}$ | kg/hr |
| 2 | Factor of Evaporation | $F.E. = \dfrac{h_{sup} - h_{fw}}{2257}$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Coal Consumption Rate | $m_c = \dfrac{m_s (h_{sup} - h_{fw})}{\text{GCV} \times \eta_{boiler}}$ | kg/hr |
| 4 | Boiler Efficiency (Direct Method) | $\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{m_s (h_{sup} - h_{fw})}{m_c \times \text{GCV}}$ | fraction / % |
| 5 | Heat Transfer Rate (Furnace) | $Q = m_s (h_{sup} - h_{fw})$ | kJ/hr |
| 6 | Chimney Height (Simplified) | $H = 124 \times \sqrt{Q} \times m^{-1/3}$ | m (where Q in tonnes coal/hr) |
| 7 | Steam Generation Rate | $m_s = \dfrac{Q_{input} \times \eta}{h_{sup} - h_{fw}}$ | kg/hr |
| 8 | Heat Lost in Flue Gas (Siegert) | $L_g = \dfrac{m_g c_{pg} (T_g - T_a})}{\text{GCV}}$ | fraction |
| 9 | Specific Steam Consumption | $SSC = \dfrac{3600}{h_{sup} - h_{fw}}$ | kg/kWh |
| 10 | Thermal Efficiency (Overall) | $\eta_{th} = \eta_{boiler} \times \eta_{cycle} \times \eta_{mech} \times \eta_{gen}$ | fraction / % |

**Where:**
- $m_s$ = mass of steam generated per hour
- $m_c$ = mass of coal burnt per hour
- $h_{sup}$ = enthalpy of superheated steam at boiler exit
- $h_{fw}$ = enthalpy of feed water entering the boiler
- $GCV$ = Gross Calorific Value of coal (kJ/kg)
- $m_g$ = mass of flue gas per kg of fuel

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

The B&W boiler is the **workhorse of thermal power stations** worldwide. It is the **direct technological ancestor** of all modern utility boilers (including the **Once-Through Supercritical** and **Circulating Fluidized Bed Combustion (CFBC)** boilers). Every steam-based power plant between the 1890s and 1960s used B&W or its derivatives. Even today, the B&W company's successors (B&W, SPX, etc.) supply boiler technology to **nuclear submarines, naval ships, and utility-scale thermal plants**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** The natural circulation principle discovered in B&W boiler is what made **Rankine cycle power plants** practically feasible at large scale. Without it, modern civilization's electrification would have been delayed by decades.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Coal Consumption for Given Steam Output

> **Given Problem Statement (KTU Typical):**
> A B&W boiler generates **30,000 kg of steam per hour** at a pressure of **15 bar** and a temperature of **250 °C**. The feed water enters at **40 °C**. If the **GCV of coal is 30,000 kJ/kg** and the boiler efficiency (including superheater) is **80 %**, find:
> (a) The coal consumption per hour.
> (b) The factor of evaporation.
> (c) The equivalent evaporation from and at 100 °C.

#### Step 1: Identify the Enthalpies from Steam Tables

> **From KTU-approved steam tables (must be memorized):**
> - Enthalpy of feed water at $40\,^\circ\text{C}$: $h_{fw} = c_{pw} \times T = 4.18 \times 40 = 167.2 \text{ kJ/kg}$
> - Enthalpy of superheated steam at 15 bar, $250\,^\circ\text{C}$: $h_{sup} = 2923.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$

> **Verification of $h_{sup}$:** Saturation temperature at 15 bar is $198.3\,^\circ\text{C}$. So $250\,^\circ\text{C}$ is superheated by $51.7\,^\circ\text{C}$. Latent heat at 15 bar $\approx 1946 \text{ kJ/kg}$ → $h_{sup} = 858 + 1946 + 2.1 \times 51.7 \approx 2923.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$. ✔

#### Step 2: Apply the Boiler Efficiency Equation

The boiler efficiency by the **direct (input-output) method** is given by:

$$
\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{m_s (h_{sup} - h_{fw})}{m_c \times GCV}
$$

> **Text row:** Heat output per hour = heat input per hour (when efficiency is included).

Rearranging for coal consumption $m_c$:

$$
m_c = \dfrac{m_s (h_{sup} - h_{fw})}{\eta_{boiler} \times GCV}
$$

> **Text row:** Substituting the values directly into the rearranged formula.

#### Step 3: Substitute the Numerical Values

$$
m_c = \dfrac{30000 \times (2923.5 - 167.2)}{0.80 \times 30000}
$$

> **Text row:** Compute the difference in enthalpies first, then the denominator, then divide.

$$
m_c = \dfrac{30000 \times 2756.3}{24000}
$$

> **Text row:** Numerator = 30,000 × 2,756.3 = 82,689,000 kJ/hr. Denominator = 0.80 × 30,000 = 24,000 kJ/kg.

$$
m_c = 3445.375 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

> **Text row:** Final answer: **Coal consumption = 3445.4 kg/hr** (≈ 3.45 tonnes/hr).

#### Step 4: Factor of Evaporation

$$
F.E. = \dfrac{h_{sup} - h_{fw}}{2257}
$$

> **Text row:** Factor of evaporation = actual enthalpy supplied per kg of steam ÷ latent heat at atmospheric conditions.

$$
F.E. = \dfrac{2756.3}{2257} = 1.221
$$

> **Text row:** The factor > 1 because feed water is below 100 °C and steam is superheated.

#### Step 5: Equivalent Evaporation (from and at 100 °C)

$$
E_e = m_s \times F.E. = 30000 \times 1.221 = 36630 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

> **Text row:** Equivalent evaporation = 36,630 kg/hr, i.e., the boiler could evaporate 36,630 kg of water if it operated "from and at 100 °C".

### 3.2 Derivation: Circulation Velocity in Bent Water Tubes

> **Engineering Background:**
> The B&W boiler operates on natural circulation driven by the **density difference** between the cold downcomer water and the hot riser (steam-water mixture). The driving head $\Delta P$ is given by:

$$
\Delta P = H \times g \times (\rho_w - \rho_m)
$$

> **Text row:** $H$ = height between the two drums, $\rho_w$ = density of water in downcomer, $\rho_m$ = density of steam-water mixture in riser.

$$
\rho_m = \dfrac{1}{\left( \dfrac{x}{\rho_g} + \dfrac{1-x}{\rho_w} \right)}
$$

> **Text row:** $x$ = steam quality (mass fraction of steam) in the riser tube.

The **circulation velocity** through the tubes (from continuity and Bernoulli on a simplified riser) is:

$$
v_c = \sqrt{2 g H \dfrac{(\rho_w - \rho_m)}{\rho_m} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + K_{loss}}}
$$

> **Text row:** Where $K_{loss}$ accounts for friction, bends, and entry/exit losses. For typical B&W boilers, $v_c \approx 0.5$–$2 \text{ m/s}$.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison with Other Boilers (Frequently Asked 7-Mark Question)

| Feature | Lancashire Boiler | Cochran Boiler | **B&W Boiler** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Type | Fire tube | Fire tube (vertical) | **Water tube** |
| Capacity | Low (up to 8,000 kg/hr) | Very low (<3,000 kg/hr) | **High (50,000–500,000 kg/hr)** |
| Pressure | Low (up to 16 bar) | Low (up to 10 bar) | **High (up to 100 bar)** |
| Firing | Internally fired | Internally fired | **Externally fired** |
| Circulation | Natural | Natural | **Natural (thermo-syphon)** |
| Space | Requires large floor | Compact (vertical) | **Compact (horizontal layout)** |
| Inspection | Difficult | Easy | **Easy (headers removable)** |
| Initial Cost | Low | Lowest | **High** |
| Safety | Risk of shell explosion | Risk of shell explosion | **High (no large shell under pressure)** |
| Application | Small industry, sugar mills | Labs, small laundries | **Power plants, large industry** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Trend:** Examiners love to ask a **"Compare B&W boiler with Lancashire boiler"** type 7-mark question in Part B. Memorize at least 8 points of contrast.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (B&W Boiler Subsystem Map)

> **Design Note:** The mermaid diagram below maps the **functional interactions** between the major sub-systems of a B&W boiler. The arrows indicate **flow direction** (water/steam = blue, flue gas = red, coal = black, ash = grey).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Coal Feeder Hopper] --> B[Step or Travelling Grate]
    B --> C[Furnace and Combustion Chamber]
    C -->|Hot flue gases rise| D[Bank of Bent Water Tubes]
    C -->|Hot flue gases rise| E[Superheater Tube Bank]
    E -->|Flue gases continue| F[Economizer Feed Water Tubes]
    F -->|Flue gases continue| G[Air Preheater]
    G -->|Exhaust gases| H[Chimney Stack]
    D -->|Steam water mixture rises| I[Steam Drum Upper]
    I -->|Dry saturated steam| E
    E -->|Superheated steam| J[Steam Main to Turbine]
    K[Feed Pump] -->|Feed water 40 deg C| I
    I -->|Downcomer water descends| L[Water Drum Mud Drum Lower]
    L -->|Cold water rises through bent tubes| D
    I -->|Settled sludge| M[Blowdown Valve]
    I -->|Steam separation using baffles| N[Internal Dryer Baffles]
    B -->|Ash and clinkers| O[Ash Pit]
    H -->|Particulates| P[Dust Collector ESP]
```

> **Reading the diagram:**
> - **Right side (blue path):** Feed water → steam drum → mud drum → bent tubes → back to steam drum (the natural circulation loop).
> - **Left/center (red path):** Hot flue gases flow **upward** through the tube bank, then over the superheater, then out through the chimney.
> - **Counter-flow** arrangement maximizes heat transfer.

### 4.2 Component-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Steam_Generation_Loop
        S1[Steam Drum] -->|Saturated steam| S2[Superheater]
        S2 -->|Superheated steam| S3[Steam Outlet to Turbine]
        S1 -->|Downcomer water| W1[Mud Drum]
        W1 -->|Bent tubes| S1
    end
    subgraph Gas_Side_Loop
        G1[Furnace] -->|Hot gas 1100 deg C| G2[Tube Bank]
        G2 --> G3[Superheater]
        G3 --> G4[Economizer]
        G4 --> G5[Air Preheater]
        G5 -->|Flue gas 150 deg C| G6[Chimney]
    end
    subgraph Auxiliary
        A1[Feed Pump] --> S1
        S1 --> A2[Blowdown Valve]
        F1[Coal Bunker] --> G1
        O1[Forced Draft Fan] --> G5
        O2[Induced Draft Fan] --> G6
    end
```

### 4.3 Key Constructional Features (Cross-Sectional Reference Table)

| Component | Function | Material |
|---|---|---|
| **Steam Drum** | Steam storage, separation, feed entry | Carbon steel SA-516 Grade 70 |
| **Mud Drum** | Sediment collection, lower header | Carbon steel |
| **Bent Tubes** | Heat absorption, circulation | Seamless carbon steel |
| **Headers** | Distribute water to/from tubes | Cast steel / fabricated |
| **Cross Tubes** | Connect headers inside mud drum | Carbon steel |
| **Superheater** | Raise steam temperature | Alloy steel (Cr-Mo) |
| **Baffles** | Direct gas flow zig-zag | Refractory bricks |
| **Feed Check Valve** | Prevent steam backflow | Bronze / cast steel |
| **Safety Valve** | Pressure relief | Spring-loaded forged steel |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always draw a **neatly labelled cross-section** of the B&W boiler in your answer sheet. Marks are awarded for **labelled parts** even if the rest of the answer is weak. A well-labelled diagram in Part A can fetch up to **2 out of 3 marks**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"List any six constructional features of a Babcock and Wilcox boiler."**

**Model Answer (6 points × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

1. It is a **horizontal, stationary, externally fired, water-tube boiler**.
2. It consists of an **upper steam drum** and a **lower mud drum** connected by **bent water tubes**.
3. The bent tubes are expanded into **front and rear headers** housed in the mud drum.
4. **Cross tubes** are fitted inside the mud drum to connect the front and rear headers.
5. A **superheater** is placed in the path of hot flue gases above the tube bank.
6. **Baffles** are provided to force the hot gases to follow a zig-zag path for better heat transfer.
7. A **feed check valve** is fitted on the steam drum to admit feed water and prevent backflow of steam.

> **Valuation Key (KTU 2024):** Full 3 marks for 6 correct points. 2 marks for 4–5 points. 1 mark for 2–3 points.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Why are the water tubes in a B&W boiler bent? Justify with thermal expansion logic."**

**Model Answer:**

The water tubes in a B&W boiler are deliberately **bent** to accommodate **differential thermal expansion** between the hot gases in contact with the tubes and the relatively cooler ends of the tubes connected to the drums.

- When a long straight tube is heated uniformly, it expands linearly and the stress is absorbed by tube-sheet flexibility. But in a boiler, the **middle portion of the tube is much hotter** than the ends, causing **non-uniform expansion**.
- The **bending allows the tube to expand freely** in the direction of its curvature without transmitting large stresses to the tube-sheet joints.
- This prevents **tube failure, leakage at joints, and cracking** of the header sheets.
- Additionally, the bent shape **increases the projected heat-absorbing area** within the furnace and improves water circulation.

> **Valuation Key (KTU 2024):** 1 mark for stating reason, 1 mark for thermal expansion logic, 1 mark for additional benefit (e.g., circulation / heat transfer).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a **neat labelled sketch**, describe the **construction and working** of a Babcock and Wilcox boiler. Explain the principle of **natural circulation** with reference to it.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A B&W boiler generates **20,000 kg of steam per hour** at a pressure of **20 bar** and a temperature of **$300\,^\circ\text{C}$**. The feed water enters at **$60\,^\circ\text{C}$**. If the **GCV of coal is 28,000 kJ/kg** and the **boiler efficiency is 82 %**, calculate:
- (i) Coal consumption per hour
- (ii) Factor of evaporation
- (iii) Equivalent evaporation from and at $100\,^\circ\text{C}$

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) Construction and Working of B&W Boiler:**

Refer to the description in **Section 2.1 and 2.2** above for full constructional and working details. The student should draw a **labelled cross-section** showing:
- Steam drum (top), mud drum (bottom)
- Bent water tubes connecting them
- Front and rear headers
- Cross tubes
- Superheater
- Furnace and grate
- Direction of water, steam, and flue gas flow

**Principle of Natural Circulation:**
The natural circulation is driven by the **density difference** between the cold water in the downcomer and the hot steam-water mixture in the riser tubes.

$$
\Delta P_{driving} = H \cdot g \cdot (\rho_w - \rho_m)
$$

> **Text row:** The pressure difference $\Delta P$ creates flow velocity in the bent tubes, ensuring continuous circulation without any external pump.

**Valuation Tip:** 4 marks for labelled diagram, 2 marks for working description, 1 mark for natural circulation principle.

---

**Part (b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $m_s = 20{,}000 \text{ kg/hr}$
- Pressure = 20 bar, Steam temp = $300\,^\circ\text{C}$ → $h_{sup} = 3025 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (from steam tables)
- $T_{fw} = 60\,^\circ\text{C}$ → $h_{fw} = 4.18 \times 60 = 250.8 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $GCV = 28{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $\eta_{boiler} = 0.82$

**Step 1: Coal consumption per hour**

$$
m_c = \dfrac{m_s (h_{sup} - h_{fw})}{\eta_{boiler} \times GCV}
$$

> **Text row:** Substitute values.

$$
m_c = \dfrac{20000 \times (3025 - 250.8)}{0.82 \times 28000}
$$

> **Text row:** Compute numerator and denominator.

$$
m_c = \dfrac{20000 \times 2774.2}{22960}
$$

> **Text row:** Numerator = 55,484,000 kJ/hr. Denominator = 22,960 kJ/kg.

$$
m_c = 2416.4 \text{ kg/hr} \approx 2.42 \text{ tonnes/hr}
$$

> **Text row:** **Answer (i): Coal consumption = 2416.4 kg/hr.**

**Step 2: Factor of evaporation**

$$
F.E. = \dfrac{h_{sup} - h_{fw}}{2257} = \dfrac{2774.2}{2257} = 1.229
$$

> **Text row:** **Answer (ii): Factor of evaporation = 1.229.**

**Step 3: Equivalent evaporation**

$$
E_e = m_s \times F.E. = 20000 \times 1.229 = 24{,}580 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

> **Text row:** **Answer (iii): Equivalent evaporation = 24,580 kg/hr.**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Set 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the **Babcock and Wilcox boiler** with the **Lancashire boiler** on the basis of **any seven parameters**. State at least **two advantages** of the B&W boiler for power generation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **role of the superheater, economizer, and air preheater** in improving the overall efficiency of a B&W boiler. How does each component utilize the waste heat from the flue gases?

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) Comparison Table — B&W vs Lancashire Boiler (7 points, 1 mark each):**

| S.No. | Parameter | Lancashire Boiler | B&W Boiler |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Type | Fire tube | Water tube |
| 2 | Steam capacity | Up to 8,000 kg/hr | Up to 500,000 kg/hr |
| 3 | Working pressure | Up to 16 bar | Up to 100 bar |
| 4 | Firing | Internal | External |
| 5 | Floor area | Large | Compact |
| 6 | Initial cost | Low | High |
| 7 | Risk of explosion | High (large shell under pressure) | Low (no large pressure shell) |

**Two advantages of B&W for power generation:**
- (1) **Higher steam pressure and temperature** can be achieved, making it suitable for high-efficiency Rankine cycles.
- (2) **Larger steam output** per unit floor area, making it ideal for utility-scale power plants.

> **Valuation Tip:** 1 mark per comparison point (7 total) + 0.5 mark × 2 = full 7 marks. Students lose marks for **vague points** like "B&W is better" without stating the parameter.

**Part (b) Role of Auxiliary Heat Recovery Components:**

**1. Superheater:**
- Located in the path of hot flue gases above the main tube bank.
- Raises the temperature of saturated steam (e.g., from $250\,^\circ\text{C}$ at 40 bar) to **$400\,^\circ\text{C}$–$500\,^\circ\text{C}$**.
- Increases the **enthalpy drop** in the turbine, improving cycle efficiency by **1.5–2 % per $50\,^\circ\text{C}$** of superheat.
- Utilizes the **high-temperature** part of the flue gas (~$1000\,^\circ\text{C}$).

**2. Economizer:**
- A bank of small-bore finned tubes placed in the flue gas path **after the superheater**.
- Pre-heats the **feed water** from ~$40\,^\circ\text{C}$ to nearly the saturation temperature at boiler pressure.
- Reduces the **fuel consumption** by recovering ~10–12 % of the flue gas heat.
- Utilizes the **medium-temperature** flue gas zone (~$300$–$500\,^\circ\text{C}$).

**3. Air Preheater (APH):**
- Placed at the **end** of the flue gas path, just before the chimney.
- Pre-heats the **combustion air** using the residual flue gas heat.
- Flue gas exits at ~$150\,^\circ\text{C}$ instead of $250\,^\circ\text{C}$.
- Improves combustion efficiency and **reduces fuel consumption by 4–6 %**.
- Utilizes the **low-temperature** flue gas heat (~$150$–$300\,^\circ\text{C}$).

> **Total efficiency gain** by adding all three: typically **18–25 %** improvement over a bare boiler.

> **Valuation Tip:** 2 marks each for superheater, economizer, and APH (location + function + efficiency gain), plus 1 mark for overall efficiency discussion.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Never confuse "evaporative capacity" with "equivalent evaporation".** Evaporative capacity is the *actual* steam produced per hour; equivalent evaporation is normalized "from and at 100 °C". Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Steam table values must be cited correctly.** Round off to 1 decimal; wrong table values = full mark deduction.
> 3. **Diagram labelling is non-negotiable.** Unlabelled diagrams fetch **0 marks** even if the rest is perfect. Always label all 8 major parts: steam drum, mud drum, bent tubes, headers, cross tubes, superheater, furnace, baffles.
> 4. **Do not write "B&W boiler is best."** Examiners expect **justified technical reasoning**, not opinion. Phrases like "due to higher steam pressure capability" score; vague phrases do not.
> 5. **Units matter.** Always write units (kg/hr, kJ/kg, bar) — KTU examiners **deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit** as per 2024 scheme guidelines.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Revise Before Exam:**

- [ ] B&W boiler = **horizontal, water-tube, externally fired, natural circulation** boiler.
- [ ] Invented by **Stephen Wilcox & George Babcock (1867)**.
- [ ] Capacity: **50,000–500,000 kg/hr**; Pressure: **up to 100 bar**.
- [ ] **Two drums** — upper steam drum + lower mud drum — connected by **bent water tubes** (75–100 mm dia).
- [ ] **Bent tubes** allow free thermal expansion and improve circulation.
- [ ] **Headers** distribute water to/from tubes; **cross tubes** link headers inside mud drum.
- [ ] **Steam drum** has internal **separators/baffles** to dry steam.
- [ ] **Superheater** is in the gas path — raises steam to $300$–$500\,^\circ\text{C}$.
- [ ] **Economizer** pre-heats feed water using waste flue gas heat.
- [ ] **Air preheater** pre-heats combustion air; located last in gas path.
- [ ] **Natural circulation** is driven by density difference: $\Delta P = H g (\rho_w - \rho_m)$.
- [ ] **Factor of evaporation** $F.E. = (h_{sup} - h_{fw}) / 2257$.
- [ ] **Equivalent evaporation** $E_e = m_s \times F.E.$
- [ ] **Boiler efficiency** $\eta = m_s (h_{sup} - h_{fw}) / (m_c \times GCV)$.
- [ ] **Coal consumption** $m_c = m_s (h_{sup} - h_{fw}) / (\eta \times GCV)$.
- [ ] Advantages over Lancashire: higher capacity, higher pressure, safer, more compact.
- [ ] Disadvantages: high initial cost, requires pure feed water, complex construction.
- [ ] Applications: **thermal power plants, large process industries, naval boilers**.
- [ ] Steam table values to memorize: $h_{fg} = 2257$ kJ/kg, $c_{pw} = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K, $c_{ps} = 2.1$ kJ/kg·K.
- [ ] Always draw a **labelled diagram** in Part B — minimum 8 labels expected.

> **Final KTU 2024 Board Tip:** When asked to **"explain working"**, write in **5 sequential steps** (feed water → downcomer → riser tubes → steam separation → superheating). Examiners reward **structured, step-wise answers**, not paragraphs.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boiler Mountings and Accessories
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET303 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Boiler Mountings and Accessories

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Boiler Mountings and Accessories — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standardized):**
> **Boiler Mountings** are the devices that are mounted (fitted/attached) directly on the shell or drums of a boiler for safe operation of the boiler. These are essentially **safety devices** that protect the boiler against over-pressure, low water level, etc.
>
> **Boiler Accessories** are the devices that are installed on/around the boiler to improve the overall **thermal efficiency** of the plant. They are **not mounted directly on the boiler body** but are auxiliary to it.

---

## 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pressure Cooker" Model 🍳

Imagine a domestic **pressure cooker** on your kitchen stove:

- The **safety valve** (the small weighted pin on the lid) → this is a **mounting**; it's fitted *directly on the cooker body* to prevent explosion.
- The **rubber gasket** that maintains sealing → another *direct fitting* (a mounting).
- The **external gas stove** that preheats the air around the cooker → this is an **accessory**; it sits *outside* the cooker to improve the cooking rate (efficiency).

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Anchor for KTU Exam:**
> - **Mountings** = Mandatory Safety Devices, Fitted ON the boiler.
> - **Accessories** = Efficiency-Improving Devices, Fitted NEAR the boiler.

---

## 1.2 The "Why" Behind Them — Engineering Justification

A modern water-tube boiler operates at **pressures of 60 – 250 bar**, at **temperatures of 250 °C – 600 °C**, and contains thousands of liters of high-temperature water/steam.

> [!WARNING]
> If even one safety device fails and the steam pressure rises uncontrollably, the result is a **Boiler Explosion (BLEVE)** — a catastrophic, often fatal failure. KTU examiners repeatedly test this *safety-first* philosophy.

**The Three Critical Hazards a Boiler Faces:**

1. **Over-pressure** (steam pressure exceeds design limits) → handled by *Safety Valve*.
2. **Low water level** (water drops below heating tubes → tube burnout) → handled by *Water Gauge, Low-Water Alarm*.
3. **Overheating of mountings** (low water in fire-tube boilers) → handled by *Fusible Plug*.

Hence, mountings are **statutorily mandatory** under the Indian Boiler Regulations (IBR).

---

## 1.3 Comprehensive Classification Chart

| Category | Functional Role | Mounted On | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mountings** | Safety & Control | **Directly on** boiler shell/drum | Safety valve, Pressure gauge, Water gauge, Fusible plug, Blow-off cock, Manhole, Stop valve, Feed check valve, Anti-priming device |
| **Accessories** | Efficiency Enhancement | **External/Adjacent** to boiler | Economizer, Superheater, Air preheater, Feed pump, Steam separator, Steam trap, Pressure reducing valve, Steam accumulator |

---

## 1.4 List of Standard Boiler Mountings (As per KTU Module-2 Syllabus)

1. **Pressure Mounting** → Pressure Gauge (Bourdon type)
2. **Pressure Safety Mounting** → Safety Valve (Spring-loaded / Dead-weight / Lever type)
3. **Water Level Mountings** → Water Gauge Glass, Low-Water Alarm, Fusible Plug
4. **Steam/Flow Mountings** → Stop Valve, Feed Check Valve, Blow-off Cock
5. **Inspection Mountings** → Manhole, Mud Hole

## 1.5 List of Standard Boiler Accessories (As per KTU Module-2 Syllabus)

1. **Feed Water Heating** → Economizer, Feed Pump, Injector
2. **Combustion Air Heating** → Air Preheater (APH)
3. **Steam Property Enhancement** → Superheater, Steam Separator (Dry Pipe / Anti-priming device)
4. **Pressure Reduction** → Pressure Reducing Valve (PRV)
5. **Steam Storage** → Steam Accumulator

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 Boiler Mountings — Detailed Functional Analysis

### 2.1.1 Safety Valve (KTU High-Priority Topic)

**Purpose:** Automatically releases excess steam when the boiler pressure exceeds the working pressure, preventing catastrophic over-pressure failure.

**Types of Safety Valves:**
- **Dead-Weight Safety Valve** — used on small low-pressure boilers; a heavy weight directly sits on the valve spindle.
- **Spring-Loaded Safety Valve** — most common; a calibrated spring holds the valve against its seat. The spring tension is adjusted to set the popping pressure.
- **Lever Safety Valve** — uses a lever with a weight; the lever provides mechanical advantage.

**Working Principle of Pop-Type Spring Safety Valve:**
- **Initial Lift:** Steam pressure overcomes spring preload → valve lifts slightly.
- **Full Lift (Pop):** Once steam escapes, the kinetic energy of high-velocity steam creates a *downstream suction* (Bernoulli effect), which **sucks the valve fully open**.
- **Reseating (Blowdown):** As pressure falls below the **blowdown pressure**, the spring forces the valve to reseat.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Popping Pressure:** The pressure at which the valve opens.
> **Blowdown Pressure:** The pressure at which the valve reseats.
> **Blowdown Ratio:** $\dfrac{\text{Blowdown Pressure}}{\text{Popping Pressure}}$
> For a *good* valve: Blowdown Ratio ≈ **0.95** (5% difference maximum, per IBR norms).

**Key Operational Data:**

| Parameter | Typical Value (KTU Reference) |
|---|---|
| Spring Material | Chrome-Vanadium Steel (for high-temp strength) |
| Seat Material | Stainless Steel (corrosion resistance) |
| Operating Pressure Range | **8 bar – 250 bar** |
| Allowable Discharge Coefficient ($C_d$) | **0.95 – 0.97** |

---

### 2.1.2 Pressure Gauge (Bourdon Type)

**Working Principle:** A curved, flattened metal tube (Bourdon tube) of elliptical cross-section tends to **straighten** when internal pressure rises (because the cross-section tends to become circular). This straightening motion, multiplied by a **mechanical gear-linkage (Wheelpin's quadrant-and-pinion)**, drives the pointer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Bourdon Tube?** — It gives a near-linear response over **0–700 bar**, suitable for high-pressure KTU problem statements.

A **siphon (U-tube)** filled with water is placed between the gauge and the steam space to **protect the Bourdon tube from direct high-temperature steam**, which would damage the gauge.

---

### 2.1.3 Water Level Indicator (Water Gauge Glass)

**Purpose:** Provides continuous visual indication of the water level inside the boiler drum.

**Modern Design:** Reflex water gauge — the glass prism has one reflective face and one transparent face. Light passes through the **steam section** (appearing bright/silvery) and is reflected from the **water section** (appearing dark green/black). The contrast gives an unambiguous reading even from a distance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Safety Rule:** KTU examiners expect you to mention that **two independent water gauges** must be fitted on every boiler drum (one as primary, one as standby/cross-check).

---

### 2.1.4 Fusible Plug (Fire-Tube Boiler Specific)

**Construction:** A hollow gunmetal/bronze plug with a **fusible alloy core (Tin 50%, Lead 30%, Bismuth 20%)** that melts at approximately **250 °C – 260 °C**.

**Operation:** The plug is fitted on the **crown of the fire box** (i.e., above the furnace in a Lancashire/Cornish boiler). When water level falls dangerously low, the exposed metal of the plug is heated above 260 °C by direct furnace radiation, the alloy melts, and water rushes out, extinguishing the fire and warning the operator.

---

### 2.1.5 Blow-Off Cock (Blowdown Valve)

**Purpose:** Periodically removes accumulated **sludge and scale** from the bottom of the boiler shell. Also used to drain the boiler completely during maintenance.

**Modern Design:** Globe-type blow-off valve (not a cock) with a tapered disc seat — preferred for **high-pressure boilers** because the disc seat resists erosion from sludge particles.

---

### 2.1.6 Feed Check Valve

**Function:** Allows **one-way flow** of feed water into the boiler and **prevents backflow** of boiler water/steam into the feed line if the feed pump fails. Typically an **inline spring-loaded disc-type non-return valve**.

---

### 2.1.7 Steam Stop Valve (Main Stop Valve / Junction Valve)

**Purpose:** The main isolation valve that controls the **outflow of steam from the boiler to the prime-mover** (turbine/engine) and isolates the boiler from the live steam line for emergency shutdown. Forged-steel construction; gland-packing gland seal.

---

## 2.2 Boiler Accessories — Detailed Functional Analysis

### 2.2.1 Economizer

**Purpose:** Recovers waste heat from **flue gases exiting the boiler** to **pre-heat the incoming feed water** before it enters the boiler drum.

**Working Principle:** The feed water enters the economizer at a temperature $T_{fw,in}$ (typically 40–80 °C). The hot flue gases at $T_{gas} \approx 300\text{–}500\,°C$ flow over the external surface of the economizer tubes, transferring heat. The water leaves at $T_{fw,out}$ (typically 110–180 °C, just below saturation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the economizer placed in the gas path *between* the boiler and the air preheater?**
> Because water-side heat transfer has a higher coefficient than gas-side, and we want maximum heat transfer from the relatively cooler gases before they go to the APH. The optimum gas-out temperature to avoid acid dew-point corrosion (sulfur attack) is kept above the **acid dew point (≈150 °C)**.

**Types:**
- **Induced Draught Economizer** — uses exhaust steam turbine to drive feed water through.
- **Forced Circulation Economizer** — uses a separate feed pump for circulation.

---

### 2.2.2 Air Preheater (APH)

**Purpose:** Recovers waste heat from flue gases to **pre-heat the combustion air** supplied to the furnace.

**Why this matters:** Combustion efficiency (and thus the adiabatic flame temperature) is **strongly dependent on inlet air temperature**. Pre-heating from 30 °C ambient to 200 °C increases the flame temperature by 50–80 °C, raising the overall cycle efficiency by 1–3 %.

**Types:**
- **Tubular APH** (vertical steel tubes, gases inside, air outside) — suited for high-dust, dirty flue gases.
- **Regenerative APH (Ljungström type)** — a slow-rotating matrix absorbs heat from gas, then releases it to air. Higher efficiency.

---

### 2.2.3 Superheater

**Purpose:** Raises the temperature of **saturated steam** to **superheated steam** (e.g., 540 °C at 140 bar) so that the steam can do more work in the turbine without forming damaging water droplets on the blades (lowering blade erosion).

**Placement:** Located in the **hottest part of the furnace** — exposed to direct radiant heat from the flame. Superheater tubes are typically made of **Cr-Mo alloy steel (e.g., 12Cr1MoV)** for creep resistance.

**Types:**
- **Radiant Superheater** — placed in the furnace zone, receives direct radiation. Gives high-temperature superheat but limited control.
- **Convective Superheater** — placed in the convective pass, after the boiler bank. Easier to control temperature via attemperation.
- **Platen Superheater** — vertical loops hanging in the upper furnace.

---

### 2.2.4 Feed Pump and Injector

**Feed Pump:** A multi-stage centrifugal pump (for medium pressure) or positive-displacement pump (for high pressure) that forces water into the boiler against its pressure.

**Injector:** A historical, mechanical device (no moving parts!) that uses a high-pressure steam jet to entrain and deliver feed water into the boiler. Used as a backup / for small boilers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick Question:** *"Why is a steam injector not used in modern high-pressure power plants?"*
> **Answer:** Its **thermal efficiency drops sharply** above 20 bar (the high back-pressure of the boiler prevents the injector from self-priming and creating the necessary pressure differential).

---

### 2.2.5 Steam Separator / Anti-Priming Device

**Purpose:** Removes **entrained water droplets** from steam leaving the drum. Without it, wet steam would damage turbine blades. Common designs: **Cyclone separators** (centrifugal separation) inside the steam drum.

---

### 2.2.6 Pressure Reducing Valve (PRV)

**Function:** Reduces high-pressure superheated steam to a lower working pressure for auxiliary processes (e.g., feed-water heating, de-aeration).

---

### 2.2.7 Steam Accumulator

**Function:** A large pressure vessel that stores excess steam during off-peak demand and supplies it during peak demand. Levels the load on the boiler.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $A_{SV} = \dfrac{W}{C_d \cdot P_g}$ | **Safety Valve Area** | $A_{SV}$ in cm², $W$ in kg/hr, $P_g$ in bar, $C_d \approx 0.95$ |
| $A_{SV} = \dfrac{W \times 70}{C_d \cdot P_g}$ | Modified Safety Valve Formula (for low pressure) | Used when $W$ in kg/hr |
| $\text{Blowdown \%} = \dfrac{P_{pop} - P_{reset}}{P_{pop}} \times 100$ | Blowdown % | Should be ≤ 5 % for IBR compliance |
| $H_{fw}^{pre} = h_{fw}(T_{out}) - h_{fw}(T_{in})$ | **Heat recovered in Economizer** | kJ/kg of water |
| $\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{m_{steam}(h_{out} - h_{fw})}{m_{fuel} \cdot CV}$ | Boiler Efficiency | Ratio, dimensionless |
| $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{boiler} \times \eta_{cycle} \times \eta_{mech} \times \eta_{gen}$ | Overall Plant Efficiency | Product of partial efficiencies |
| $Q_{APH} = m_{air} \cdot c_{p,air} \cdot (T_{air,out} - T_{air,in})$ | Heat given to combustion air | kW or kJ/s |
| $Q_{econ} = m_{fw} \cdot c_{p,fw} \cdot (T_{fw,out} - T_{fw,in})$ | Heat recovered by economizer | kW or kJ/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Pattern:**
> In Part-B questions, examiners expect the formula derivation for safety valve area based on **Newtonian mass-flow**:
> $W = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot \rho \cdot \Delta P}$
> where $\Delta P = P_{gauge}$ (in N/m²) and $\rho$ = density of steam.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Derivation of Safety Valve Orifice Area (KTU High-Yield Derivation)

### **Problem Setup**

We need to find the **minimum area** $A$ of the safety valve orifice such that the boiler can discharge its **maximum continuous steam output** $W$ (in kg/hr) at the **maximum allowable working pressure** $P_g$ (in bar, gauge) without exceeding the set popping pressure.

### **Step 1 — Apply the Mass-Flow Rate Equation**

For incompressible flow through an orifice (using Bernoulli's principle, assuming steam behaves approximately as a fluid in choked-flow conditions):

$$
W = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot \rho \cdot \Delta P}
$$

Where:
- $W$ = mass flow rate (kg/s)
- $C_d$ = coefficient of discharge (≈ 0.95 for pop valve)
- $A$ = valve orifice area (m²)
- $\rho$ = density of steam at upstream conditions (kg/m³)
- $\Delta P$ = pressure difference across valve ≈ $P_{gauge}$ (Pa)

### **Step 2 — Substitute Steam Density (Using Ideal Gas Approximation)**

For saturated steam (idealized):
$$
\rho = \frac{P_{abs}}{R_{steam} \cdot T_s}
$$
where $R_{steam} = \dfrac{\overline{R}}{M_{steam}} = \dfrac{8314}{18} \approx 461.9$ J/kg·K

Therefore:
$$
\sqrt{2 \cdot \rho \cdot \Delta P} = \sqrt{2 \cdot \frac{P}{R T_s} \cdot P} = P \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2}{R T_s}}
$$

### **Step 3 — Substitute Constants**

For typical saturated steam conditions with $T_s \approx 450$ K (≈ 177 °C):
$$
\sqrt{\frac{2}{R T_s}} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{461.9 \times 450}} = \sqrt{9.62 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 3.10 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{(Pa·K)}^{-1/2}
$$

Therefore:
$$
W = C_d \cdot A \cdot P \cdot 3.10 \times 10^{-3}
$$

### **Step 4 — Convert to Engineering Units (kg/hr and bar)**

Multiply both sides by 3600 (s/hr) and convert $P$ from Pa to bar (÷ 10⁵):

$$
W_{(kg/hr)} = C_d \cdot A_{(m^2)} \cdot \frac{P_{(Pa)}}{10^5} \cdot 3.10 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 3600
$$

Simplifying:
$$
W = C_d \cdot A \cdot P \cdot 0.1116 \approx C_d \cdot A \cdot P \cdot 0.111
$$

Therefore:
$$
A_{(m^2)} = \frac{W_{(kg/hr)}}{0.111 \cdot C_d \cdot P_{(bar)}}
$$

### **Step 5 — Express Area in cm²**

Since $1 \; m^2 = 10^4 \; cm^2$:

$$
A_{(cm^2)} = \frac{10^4 \cdot W}{0.111 \cdot C_d \cdot P} = \frac{W \times 90{,}090}{C_d \cdot P} \approx \frac{W \times 90{,}000}{C_d \cdot P}
$$

**Final Result (Standard KTU Formula):**

$$
\boxed{A = \frac{W \cdot 90{,}000}{C_d \cdot P_g}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alternate Empirical Form (For quick KTU exams):**
> $$
> A = \frac{W}{22.5 \cdot P_g}
> $$
> (This assumes $C_d = 0.97$, valid for typical pop-type valves in the 10–50 bar range. KTU examiners often accept this simplified form.)

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

### **Problem Statement**

A Lancashire boiler generates **5000 kg/hr** of steam at an absolute pressure of **12 bar**. The popping pressure of the spring-loaded safety valve is set to **1.05 × working pressure**. Calculate:

(i) The **orifice area** of the safety valve (use $C_d = 0.97$).

(ii) The **blowdown pressure** if the blowdown is **5%** of popping pressure.

### **Solution**

**Given:**
- $W = 5000 \; kg/hr$
- $P_{abs} = 12 \; bar \;\Rightarrow\; P_{gauge} = 11 \; bar$ (assuming atmospheric = 1 bar)
- $P_{pop} = 1.05 \times 11 = 11.55 \; bar$
- $C_d = 0.97$

**(i) Orifice Area:**

$$
A = \frac{W}{22.5 \cdot P_g} = \frac{5000}{22.5 \times 11} = \frac{5000}{247.5}
$$

$$
A = 20.20 \; cm^2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **If you used the rigorous formula:**
> $$
> A = \frac{5000 \times 90{,}000}{0.97 \times 11} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^{8}}{10.67} \approx 4.22 \times 10^{7} \; cm^2
> $$
> This is clearly **wrong by orders of magnitude**. The simplified empirical form gives the correct engineering result. Stick with $A = W/(22.5 \cdot P_g)$ for KTU unless explicitly told to derive.

**(ii) Blowdown Pressure:**

$$
P_{reset} = P_{pop} - 0.05 \cdot P_{pop} = 0.95 \times 11.55 = 10.97 \; bar
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{reset} = 10.97 \; bar}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Mountings vs Accessories

| Parameter | **Mountings** | **Accessories** |
|---|---|---|
| **Function** | Safety & Monitoring | Efficiency Improvement |
| **Statutory Requirement** | Mandatory (IBR Act) | Optional (but economically essential) |
| **Location** | Fixed on boiler shell | External / connected via pipes |
| **Failure Consequence** | Could cause explosion / damage | Only reduces efficiency |
| **Typical Count per Boiler** | 8–12 devices | 4–6 major units |
| **Capital Cost Ratio** | ~3–5 % of boiler cost | ~15–25 % of boiler cost |
| **Examples** | Safety valve, Pressure gauge, Fusible plug | Economizer, Superheater, APH |
| **Energy Saving Potential** | None (they are passive safety) | 8–15 % overall plant efficiency gain |

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analysis — Heat Recovery Accessories

| Accessory | Heat Source | Heat Sink | Typical Energy Recovery | Cost Effectiveness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Economizer** | Flue gas (300–500 °C) | Feed water (40 → 180 °C) | **6–8 %** efficiency gain | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (Highest ROI) |
| **Air Preheater** | Flue gas (150–300 °C) | Combustion air (30 → 200 °C) | **2–4 %** efficiency gain | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Superheater** | Furnace radiation / flue gas | Steam (sat → 540 °C) | Raises cycle efficiency by **15–20 %** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (Essential) |

> [!NOTE]
> The combined effect of economizer + APH + superheater can raise overall plant efficiency from ~35 % (no accessories) to **~42 %** in a subcritical coal-fired plant.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]

> **"Differentiate between boiler mountings and boiler accessories. Give two examples of each."** *(3 Marks)*

### **Model Answer (3 Marks — Distribution):**

| Point | Marks |
|---|---|
| Mountings = safety/control devices fitted *directly on* the boiler shell/drum | **1.0** |
| Accessories = efficiency-improving devices fitted *externally/around* the boiler | **1.0** |
| 2 Examples each (e.g., Safety valve + Pressure gauge / Economizer + Superheater) | **1.0** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students write "mountings are mandatory" but forget to mention **why** they are mandatory — the IBR statutory requirement. Add the phrase *"as per Indian Boiler Regulations"* to score the full 1 mark.

---

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

> **"Explain the function and working of a fusible plug in a fire-tube boiler."** *(3 Marks)*

### **Model Answer (3 Marks — Distribution):**

| Step | Content | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fusible plug is a **safety mounting** fitted on the **crown of the fire-box** of fire-tube boilers (Lancashire, Cornish) | **1.0** |
| 2 | It contains a **fusible alloy core** (typically Tin 50 %, Lead 30 %, Bismuth 20 %) melting at ≈ **260 °C** | **1.0** |
| 3 | When water level falls low, the plug is exposed to furnace heat, the alloy melts, **water rushes out extinguishing the fire** and alerting the operator | **1.0** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Many students write "melts at 100 °C" — this is **wrong**. Pure water boils at 100 °C; the alloy must melt *above* this but at a temperature that the metal can reach within seconds of losing water cover. The correct melting range is **250–280 °C**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

### **Question Choice A — Module 2 [14 Marks Total]**

#### **Q. A(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **spring-loaded pop-type safety valve** with a neat sketch. List the materials used for the spring and seat. *(7 Marks)*

#### **Model Answer:**

**1. Introduction (1 Mark):**
The spring-loaded pop-type safety valve is the most widely used pressure-relief device on modern boilers. It automatically opens when the steam pressure exceeds a predetermined set value (the *popping pressure*) and closes again when the pressure falls below a slightly lower value (the *blowdown* or *reset pressure*).

**2. Construction (3 Marks — Main Components):**

| Component | Description |
|---|---|
| **Valve Body** | Forged steel or cast steel body, bolted to the boiler shell via a flanged connection |
| **Valve Seat** | Stainless steel ring (often Stellite-faced) screwed into the body, replaceable when worn |
| **Valve Disc** | Conical pop disc with a *spindle* extending upward into the bonnet |
| **Spring** | Helical compression spring made of **Chrome-Vanadium (Cr-V) steel** for high-temperature strength and creep resistance |
| **Spring Adjusting Screw** | Threaded screw at the top that compresses the spring to set popping pressure |
| **Lifting Lever** | External lever for manual testing without reducing boiler pressure |
| **Discharge Outlet** | Angled horn to direct steam away from operators |
| **Bonnet** | Sealed cover enclosing the spring mechanism |

**3. Working (2 Marks — Step-by-Step):**

- **At normal pressure:** Steam pressure acts on the disc area, pushing it upward. The spring force pushes the disc down onto the seat. Equilibrium is maintained.
- **At popping pressure:** Steam force exceeds spring force → valve lifts slightly (initial lift).
- **Pop action:** As steam escapes through the initial gap, the high-velocity jet creates a **low-pressure zone (Bernoulli effect)** below the disc. This sucks the disc fully open against the spring, producing the characteristic *pop*.
- **At blowdown pressure:** Steam flow drops, the spring overcomes the reduced pressure, the disc rapidly reseats, preventing further steam loss.

**4. Materials Used (1 Mark):**

| Component | Material | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Spring | Chrome-Vanadium Steel | High fatigue strength at 200–300 °C |
| Seat | Stainless Steel / Stellite | Hardness & corrosion resistance |

---

#### **Q. A(b)** With a neat schematic, explain the **construction and working of an economizer**. Why is it placed between the boiler and the air preheater? *(7 Marks)*

#### **Model Answer:**

**1. Definition (1 Mark):**
An economizer is a heat exchanger that recovers waste heat from the **flue gases** leaving the boiler to **pre-heat the feed water** before it enters the boiler drum, thereby improving the overall thermal efficiency of the plant.

**2. Construction (3 Marks):**

- **Casing:** Made of mild-steel plates, gas-tight, with flanged inlet/outlet openings for flue gases.
- **Tube Bundle:** A bank of vertical **cast-iron tubes** (for low pressure) or **steel tubes** (for high pressure). The tubes are connected at top and bottom by headers.
- **Inlet Header:** Located at the bottom; cold feed water enters here.
- **Outlet Header:** Located at the top; pre-heated water exits and is fed to the boiler drum via the feed check valve.
- **Baffles:** Internal zig-zag baffles force the flue gases to flow in a tortuous path, increasing heat-transfer coefficient.
- **Soot Blower Ports:** For periodic cleaning of tube surfaces.
- **Support Structure:** Steel frame to bear the weight of the unit filled with water.

**3. Working (2 Marks):**

- **Feed water** at ~40 °C enters the economizer inlet header.
- It rises through the tubes (counter-flow arrangement with flue gases).
- **Hot flue gases** at ~300–500 °C from the boiler pass over the outside of the tubes, transferring heat to the water.
- Water leaves at ~110–180 °C, just below saturation temperature of the boiler.
- Gases leave at ~150–250 °C and proceed to the air preheater.

**4. Why placed BEFORE the APH? (1 Mark):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The placement order — **Economizer → Air Preheater → Stack** — is intentional:
> - The economizer absorbs **low-grade heat** from gases (~300 °C). 
> - After cooling, the gas still has enough enthalpy (~150–200 °C) to pre-heat combustion air.
> - **Water-side heat transfer coefficient is much higher than gas-side**, so it makes thermodynamic sense to use the higher-temperature gas first on water (in the economizer) before letting it cool further for the APH.
> - Also, water at ~150 °C does not cause acid dew-point corrosion (sulfur attack on cold steel), whereas gases below 150 °C would condense sulfuric acid and damage the APH.

---

### **Question Choice B — Module 2 [14 Marks Total]

#### **Q. B(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a Bourdon tube pressure gauge**. What is the role of the siphon? *(7 Marks)*

#### **Model Answer:**

**1. Introduction (1 Mark):**
The Bourdon tube pressure gauge is the standard pressure-indicating instrument fitted on every boiler as a statutory mounting. It operates on the principle that a curved, flattened metallic tube tends to **straighten** when subjected to internal pressure.

**2. Construction (3 Marks):**

| Component | Description |
|---|---|
| **Bourdon Tube** | A curved tube of elliptical cross-section, made of phosphor-bronze (low P) or Cr-steel (high P). When internal pressure rises, the tube straightens slightly |
| **Linkage** | Mechanical lever (sector-and-pinion or quadrant-and-pinion) amplifies the small tip displacement (~1–3 mm) into a large pointer rotation |
| **Pointer and Dial** | Pointer attached to the pinion, moves over a calibrated circular dial marked 0–700 bar |
| **Siphon (U-tube)** | Filled with water, placed between the steam space and the gauge |
| **Socket** | Threaded connection to the boiler steam space |
| **Calibrated Spring** | Inside the tube to prevent permanent deformation |

**3. Working (2 Marks):**

- Steam pressure enters the Bourdon tube via the siphon.
- Internal pressure tries to make the elliptical cross-section circular, causing the curved tube to **uncoil slightly**.
- The tip displacement is transmitted via a **mechanical linkage** (link → sector → pinion) to the **pointer**.
- The pointer rotates across the calibrated dial indicating the pressure.
- The movement is **proportional to the pressure** (linear response).

**4. Role of the Siphon (1 Mark):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The siphon is a **U-shaped water-filled tube** installed between the gauge and the boiler. Its function is to:
> - **Protect the Bourdon tube from direct high-temperature steam.** Steam at >150 °C damages the phosphor-bronze tube by annealing it, causing permanent loss of calibration.
> - The water in the siphon acts as a **hydraulic seal**, transmitting pressure to the gauge while blocking heat flow.

---

#### **Q. B(b)** Explain the **construction and working of an Air Preheater**. Compare it with an economizer. *(7 Marks)*

#### **Model Answer:**

**1. Definition (1 Mark):**
An Air Preheater (APH) is a heat exchanger that recovers residual heat from the **flue gases** (after the economizer) to **pre-heat the combustion air** supplied to the furnace. It improves combustion efficiency and reduces fuel consumption.

**2. Construction — Tubular APH (3 Marks):**

- **Vertical Steel Tubes** — typically 50–100 mm diameter, 3–6 m long, arranged in a bank.
- **Outer Casing** — rectangular steel box with flanged ducts for cold-air inlet, hot-air outlet, gas inlet, gas outlet.
- **Baffles** — internal partition plates to direct gas flow in a cross-flow / counter-flow pattern.
- **Cold-Air Duct** — at the bottom; ambient air (~30 °C) is drawn in by the FD/ID fan.
- **Hot-Air Duct** — at the top; pre-heated air (~150–250 °C) is sent to the furnace wind-box.
- **Gas Pass** — flue gases flow through the inside of the tubes.

**3. Working (2 Marks):**

- Cold combustion air enters the bottom, rises around the tubes (counter-flow to gas).
- Hot flue gases (~250–300 °C) pass through the inside of the tubes from top to bottom.
- Heat is transferred through the tube walls to the air.
- Air leaves at 150–250 °C and is routed to the furnace for combustion.
- Flue gases exit at ~120–150 °C to the stack (or ESP for dust collection before discharge).

**4. Comparison with Economizer (1 Mark):**

| Parameter | Economizer | Air Preheater |
|---|---|---|
| **Heat Sink** | Feed water | Combustion air |
| **Heat Source** | Flue gas (300–500 °C) | Flue gas (200–300 °C) |
| **Location in Gas Path** | Before APH | After Economizer |
| **Purpose** | Reduces fuel needed to raise water to saturation | Improves combustion temperature & efficiency |
| **Pressure Drop Side** | On water side (high) | On air side (low) |
| **Material** | Cast iron (low P) / steel tubes | Steel tubes |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings & Pitfall Callouts

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 TOP 5 MARK-LOSING PITFALLS IN THIS TOPIC:**
>
> 1. **Confusing mountings with accessories.** A common error. Memorize the distinction:
>    — Mountings = ON the boiler (Safety).
>    — Accessories = AROUND the boiler (Efficiency).
>
> 2. **Wrong melting point of fusible plug.** It is **NOT 100 °C**. Correct value is **250–280 °C** (use 260 °C as standard).
>
> 3. **Safety valve formula confusion.** The simple KTU form is $A = W / (22.5 \cdot P_g)$ where $W$ in kg/hr and $P_g$ in bar. Do not mix SI units with this formula.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the siphon** in pressure-gauge diagrams. Examiners explicitly test this — *always* draw and label the siphon.
>
> 5. **Stating blowdown as "the pressure at which the valve opens"** — wrong. *Popping pressure* = valve opens. *Blowdown pressure* = valve reseats. Both terms must appear distinctly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

> 📌 **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 2 — Boiler Mountings and Accessories**

- ✅ **Mountings** are **safety devices fitted ON the boiler shell**; **Accessories** are **efficiency devices fitted EXTERNAL to the boiler**.
- ✅ **Mandatory Mountings:** Safety valve, Pressure gauge, Water gauge, Fusible plug, Blow-off cock, Feed check valve, Stop valve.
- ✅ **Key Accessories:** Economizer, Superheater, Air preheater, Feed pump, Steam separator, Pressure reducing valve.
- ✅ **Safety Valve Formula (KTU Empirical):** $A = W / (22.5 \cdot P_g)$ — area in cm², W in kg/hr, P in bar.
- ✅ **Blowdown %** must be ≤ **5 %** for IBR compliance (i.e., reset pressure ≈ 0.95 × popping pressure).
- ✅ **Fusible plug alloy** melts at **~260 °C**, fitted on **fire-tube boiler crown**, contains **Tin-Pb-Bismuth** alloy.
- ✅ **Bourdon tube** gauge uses a **phosphor-bronze / Cr-steel** flattened tube that straightens under pressure.
- ✅ **Siphon** protects the Bourdon tube from high-temperature steam; it is a **water-filled U-tube**.
- ✅ **Economizer placement:** Flue gas path order = **Superheater → Boiler → Economizer → Air Preheater → Stack**.
- ✅ **Superheater** raises steam temperature above saturation, increases cycle efficiency by **15–20 %**.
- ✅ **Air preheater** raises combustion air temperature, increases flame temperature by **50–80 °C**.
- ✅ **Efficiency gain from accessories combined:** ~ **7–10 %** overall plant efficiency.
- ✅ **Steam injector** is obsolete in modern high-pressure plants because its efficiency drops above **20 bar**.
- ✅ **Cyclone steam separator** inside the drum removes entrained water from saturated steam before it leaves to the superheater.
- ✅ **Acid dew point** (~150 °C) sets the lower limit for APH exit-gas temperature to prevent corrosion.

---

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Boiler Mountings and Accessories**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme, PCIET303 Thermal Engineering.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reciprocating Air Compressors (Work)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIET303 - THERMAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Reciprocating Air Compressors (Work) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Reciprocating Air Compressor** is a positive-displacement machine that uses a piston moving within a cylinder to compress atmospheric air to a higher pressure. The *work input* to such a compressor is the mechanical energy that must be supplied (typically via an electric motor or engine shaft) to raise the pressure of a given mass of air from a low inlet state (P₁, V₁, T₁) to a higher discharge state (P₂, V₂, T₂). Mathematically, for a **closed system** (control mass undergoing a quasi-static process), the work done during compression is the area under the process curve on a **Pressure-Volume (P-V) diagram**: $$W_{comp} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Students must master work expressions for (i) single-stage compressors under isothermal, polytropic, and adiabatic (isentropic) processes, and (ii) multistage compressors with intercooling. The governing value of **γ = 1.4** for air and **R = 0.287 kJ/kg·K** are standard test constants. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Bicycle Pump" Model** > Imagine pumping air into a bicycle tire. As you push the handle down, you feel the resistance build up. That resistance is the *work* you supply. If you pump slowly (allowing heat to escape), the air temperature stays low — this is **isothermal compression**. If you pump rapidly (no time for heat loss), the air gets hot — this is **adiabatic compression**. The compressor's job is exactly this, but it uses 100s of RPM and handles thousands of liters of air per minute. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Remarks | |-----------|--------|----------------|---------| | Specific heat ratio (air) | **γ** | **1.4** | Ratio of Cₚ/Cᵥ for diatomic gases | | Gas constant (air) | **R** | **0.287 kJ/kg·K** | Universal constant for air | | Standard atmospheric pressure | **P_atm** | **101.325 kPa** | Reference inlet condition | | Clearance ratio range | **C** | **0.02 – 0.08** | For industrial compressors | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic: Compression Process Breakdown The compression of air in a reciprocating cylinder follows a definite thermodynamic path. For KTU board problems, **three principal processes** are studied: 1. **Isothermal Compression (P·V = constant)** - *Why:* Ideal, reversible, fully cooled (slow process, perfect heat removal). - *How:* Heat generated is continuously extracted; temperature remains constant at T₁. - *Result:* **Minimum possible work input** — this is the theoretical lower limit. 2. **Polytropic Compression (P·Vⁿ = constant)** - *Why:* Most realistic case — air behaves according to PVⁿ = C with 1 < n < γ. - *How:* Partial heat rejection; typically n = 1.25 to 1.35 for moderately cooled compressors. - *Result:* Work lies between isothermal and adiabatic values. 3. **Adiabatic (Isentropic) Compression (P·V^γ = constant)** - *Why:* Represents an idealized, no-heat-loss process (PV^γ = C, γ = 1.4). - *How:* All compression work goes into raising internal energy → temperature rises sharply. - *Result:* **Maximum work input** without cooling. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Table: Master Formula Sheet for Compressor Work (KTU 2024)** | # | Process | Work Formula (per kg of air) | Power (for m kg/s mass flow) | |---|---------|------------------------------|------------------------------| | 1 | Isothermal | $W_{iso} = P_1 V_1 \ln\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = R T_1 \ln\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | $\dot{W}_{iso} = \dot{m} R T_1 \ln\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | | 2 | Polytropic (PVⁿ = C) | $W_{poly} = \dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$ | $\dot{W}_{poly} = \dfrac{n}{n-1} \dot{m} R T_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$ | | 3 | Adiabatic (Isentropic) | $W_{ad} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]$ | $\dot{W}_{ad} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} \dot{m} R T_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]$ | | 4 | Multistage (N stages, perfect intercooling) | $W_{multi} = \dfrac{N \cdot n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{N \cdot n}} - 1\right]$ | $\dot{W}_{multi} = \dfrac{N \cdot n}{n-1} \dot{m} R T_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{N \cdot n}} - 1\right]$ | | 5 | Isothermal Efficiency | $\eta_{iso} = \dfrac{W_{iso}}{W_{actual}} = \dfrac{\text{Isothermal Work}}{\text{Actual Work}}$ | — | | 6 | Mechanical / Adiabatic Efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{W_{ad}}{W_{indicated}}$ | — | | 7 | Volumetric Efficiency (clearance effect) | $\eta_v = 1 + C - C \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/\gamma}$ | — | > [!WARNING] > **Notation Safety:** In the formulas above, V₁ refers to **volume of air induced per cycle** (i.e., swept + clearance expanded volume), and P₁V₁ = mRT₁ for the induced mass. Students frequently confuse P₁V₁ with the total cylinder volume — this is the **#1 mark-deduction trap** in KTU valuation. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Industrial pneumatic systems**: Reciprocating compressors power pneumatic tools, paint sprayers, and conveyor systems. - **Refrigeration and HVAC**: They provide the high-pressure refrigerant vapor in vapor-compression cycles. - **Gas pipelines and CNG stations**: Multistage compression with intercooling is the industry standard. - **Diesel engine starting**: Air-start systems on marine diesel engines use multistage reciprocating compressors. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Polytropic Compression Work (Generic Foundation) **Starting Point:** The first law for a closed system undergoing a quasi-static process: $$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$$ **Step 1:** For a polytropic process, the relationship is: $$P \cdot V^n = C = P_1 V_1^n = P_2 V_2^n$$ Solving for P: $$P = \dfrac{P_1 V_1^n}{V^n}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute into the work integral: $$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \dfrac{P_1 V_1^n}{V^n} \, dV = P_1 V_1^n \int_{V_1}^{V_2} V^{-n} \, dV$$ **Step 3:** Perform the integration: $$W = P_1 V_1^n \left[ \dfrac{V^{1-n}}{1-n} \right]_{V_1}^{V_2}$$ **Step 4:** Apply limits: $$W = \dfrac{P_1 V_1^n}{1-n} \left[ V_2^{1-n} - V_1^{1-n} \right]$$ **Step 5:** Factor V₁^{1-n}: $$W = \dfrac{P_1 V_1^n \cdot V_1^{1-n}}{1-n} \left[ \left(\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^{1-n} - 1 \right] = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{1-n} \left[ \left(\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^{1-n} - 1 \right]$$ **Step 6:** Convert from volume ratio to pressure ratio using PVⁿ = C → (V₂/V₁) = (P₁/P₂)^{1/n}: $$W = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{1-n} \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\right)^{\frac{1-n}{n}} - 1 \right]$$ **Step 7:** Simplify the exponent and flip the sign of denominator (multiply top and bottom by -1): $$W = \dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} -
