1 \right]$$ > [!NOTE] > **Final Result (Polytropic Work):** This single expression, with n = 1 (isothermal, via limit) or n = γ (adiabatic), unifies all single-stage compressor work equations used in KTU 2024 examinations. ### 3.2 Derivation: Isothermal Work (Limit Case of Polytropic) **Step 1:** Start with the integral for isothermal (T = constant): $$W_{iso} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$$ **Step 2:** Use ideal gas law at constant T: P = mRT/V = P₁V₁/V: $$W_{iso} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{V} \, dV$$ **Step 3:** Integrate: $$W_{iso} = P_1 V_1 \left[ \ln V \right]_{V_1}^{V_2} = P_1 V_1 \ln\left(\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$ **Step 4:** Use Boyle's Law: P₁V₁ = P₂V₂ → V₂/V₁ = P₁/P₂: $$W_{iso} = P_1 V_1 \ln\left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\right) = -P_1 V_1 \ln\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$ **Step 5:** The negative sign indicates work done ON the system (work input), so by convention the work input magnitude is: $$W_{iso,\,in} = P_1 V_1 \ln\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = m R T_1 \ln\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** The isothermal work is the area under a hyperbola (P = C/V) on a P-V diagram. The adiabatic work is the area under a steeper curve (P = C/V^γ). The "saved" work by cooling = difference between adiabatic and isothermal areas. ### 3.3 Derivation: Multistage Compression with Perfect Intercooling **Assumption:** N stages, identical pressure ratio per stage (r = (P₂/P₁)^{1/N}), perfect intercooling back to T₁ between stages. **Step 1:** Work per stage (polytropic, identical T₁ and V₁ at inlet of each stage): $$W_{stage} = \dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{N \cdot n}} - 1\right]$$ **Step 2:** Total work for N stages: $$W_{total} = N \cdot W_{stage} = \dfrac{N \cdot n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{N \cdot n}} - 1\right]$$ **Step 3:** For the limiting case N → ∞, the work approaches the isothermal value (proof of minimum work theorem): $$\lim_{N \to \infty} W_{total} = P_1 V_1 \ln\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = W_{iso}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Work Savings from Multistaging:** For a pressure ratio of, say, 8:1, single-stage adiabatic work is significantly higher than two-stage with intercooling. This is why industrial compressors above 5 bar always use multiple stages. ### 3.4 Derivation: Volumetric Efficiency (Clearance Effect) **Step 1:** Let V_c = clearance volume, V_s = swept volume, C = V_c/V_s (clearance ratio). Total cylinder volume V_d = V_c + V_s. **Step 2:** At the end of delivery stroke, air remaining in clearance expands back from P₂ to P₁ according to PV^γ = C (adiabatic expansion): $$V_3 = V_c \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/\gamma}$$ **Step 3:** The effective suction volume (per cycle) is: $$V_1 = V_d - V_3 = (V_s + V_c) - V_c \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/\gamma}$$ **Step 4:** Divide by V_s to get volumetric efficiency: $$\eta_v = \dfrac{V_1}{V_s} = 1 + C - C \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/\gamma}$$ --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 P-V Diagram: Single-Stage Reciprocating Compressor (with Clearance) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Point 1: Suction begins
P1, V3, T1"]:::stateNode B["Point 2: Suction ends
P1, V1, T1"]:::stateNode C["Point 3: Compression ends
P2, Vc, T2"]:::stateNode D["Point 4: Expansion begins
P2, Vc, T2"]:::stateNode A -- "1 to 2: Suction stroke
Constant P = P1" --> B B -- "2 to 3: Compression stroke
Polytropic PVn = C" --> C C -- "3 to 4: Delivery stroke
Constant P = P2" --> D D -- "4 to 1: Re-expansion
Adiabatic PVgamma = C" --> A classDef stateNode fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Multistage Compressor with Intercooling ```mermaid flowchart LR IN["Atmospheric Air Inlet
P1, T1"]:::inlet STAGE1["Stage 1 Cylinder
Compression 1 to Px
Work W1 in"]:::stage COOL1["Intercooler 1
Cooled back to T1
Pressure constant = Px"]:::cooler STAGE2["Stage 2 Cylinder
Compression Px to P2
Work W2 in"]:::stage OUT["High-Pressure Air Outlet
P2, T1 (after final cooling)"]:::outlet IN --> STAGE1 STAGE1 --> COOL1 COOL1 --> STAGE2 STAGE2 --> OUT STAGE1 -.->|Heat Q1 extracted| COOL1 classDef inlet fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef stage fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef cooler fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef outlet fill:#FFD700,stroke:#8B6914,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Comparative Work Areas on P-V Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD DIAG["P-V Diagram: Comparison of Processes"]:::title DIAG --> ISO["Area under Isotherm
P V = constant
SMALLEST Area
Minimum Work = Wiso"]:::iso DIAG --> POLY["Area under Polytrope
P Vn = constant
INTERMEDIATE Area
Wpoly"]:::poly DIAG --> ADIA["Area under Adiabat
P Vgamma = constant
LARGEST Area
Wad = Maximum Work"]:::adia classDef title fill:#000000,stroke:#FFFFFF,color:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px classDef iso fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef poly fill:#FFFF99,stroke:#8B8B00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef adia fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** The visual ordering of areas (Isothermal < Polytropic < Adiabatic) on the P-V diagram directly corresponds to the numerical ordering of work input values — a frequent KTU viva question. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *Define isothermal efficiency of a reciprocating air compressor. State its expression in terms of work.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Isothermal efficiency is defined as the ratio of isothermal work (the theoretical minimum work required) to the actual work supplied to the compressor. $$\eta_{iso} = \dfrac{W_{iso}}{W_{actual}} = \dfrac{P_1 V_1 \ln(P_2/P_1)}{W_{actual}}$$ It indicates how closely the actual compression process approaches the ideal isothermal process. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for significance). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *What is clearance volume? How does it affect the volumetric efficiency of a reciprocating compressor?* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Clearance volume (V_c) is the volume of air intentionally left inside the cylinder at the end of the delivery stroke to prevent the piston from striking the cylinder head. (1 mark) It reduces the effective suction volume per cycle because the trapped clearance air must re-expand back to inlet pressure before fresh air can enter. (1 mark) The volumetric efficiency with clearance is given by: $$\eta_v = 1 + C - C \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/\gamma}$$ As pressure ratio (P₂/P₁) increases, the expansion term increases, decreasing volumetric efficiency. (1 mark) --- ### 5.2 Part B: Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]** A single-stage reciprocating air compressor compresses **2.5 m³/min** of air at **1.013 bar** and **17°C** to a pressure of **7 bar**. The compression is polytropic with index **n = 1.3**. The clearance ratio is **5%**. Calculate: **(a)** The work done per minute, the power required to drive the compressor, and the temperature of air after compression. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** The volumetric efficiency, the swept volume, and the clearance volume of the compressor. **(7 Marks)** --- **Solution (a):** **Given Data:** - P₁ = 1.013 bar = 101.3 kPa - T₁ = 17°C = 290 K - V₁ = 2.5 m³/min - P₂ = 7 bar = 700 kPa - n = 1.3 - C = 0.05 **Step 1 — Work done per minute (polytropic):** $$W = \dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$$ Pressure ratio: P₂/P₁ = 700/101.3 = 6.9102 Exponent: (n-1)/n = 0.3/1.3 = 0.2308 $$(6.9102)^{0.2308} = ?$$ Let x = 0.2308 × ln(6.9102) = 0.2308 × 1.9330 = 0.4461 e^0.4461 = 1.5622 So (P₂/P₁)^((n-1)/n) = 1.5622 $$W = \dfrac{1.3}{0.3} \times 101.3 \times 2.5 \times [1.5622 - 1]$$ $$W = 4.3333 \times 253.25 \times 0.5622 = 616.95 \text{ kJ/min}$$ **Step 2 — Power required:** $$\dot{W} = \dfrac{616.95}{60} = 10.28 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 3 — Temperature after compression:** For polytropic process: T₂/T₁ = (P₂/P₁)^{(n-1)/n} $$T_2 = 290 \times 1.5622 = 453.04 \text{ K} = 180.04°C$$ --- **Solution (b):** **Step 1 — Volumetric efficiency:** $$\eta_v = 1 + C - C \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/\gamma}$$ Exponent: 1/γ = 1/1.4 = 0.7143 (6.9102)^0.7143 = ? 0.7143 × ln(6.9102) = 0.7143 × 1.9330 = 1.3808 e^1.3808 = 3.9777 $$\eta_v = 1 + 0.05 - 0.05 \times 3.9777 = 1.05 - 0.1989 = 0.8511 = 85.11\%$$ **Step 2 — Swept volume (V_s):** V₁ (actual induced air per cycle) = V_s × η_v V₁ per minute = 2.5 m³ (given as free air delivery per minute, assuming this is the actual inducted volume; if FAD interpretation differs, sweep V_s = V₁/η_v) $$V_s = \dfrac{2.5}{0.8511} = 2.9375 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$$ **Step 3 — Clearance volume:** $$V_c = C \times V_s = 0.05 \times 2.9375 = 0.1469 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$$ --- **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified]** A two-stage reciprocating air compressor with perfect intercooling compresses air from **1 bar, 27°C** to a final pressure of **9 bar**. The compression in each stage follows the law **PV^1.25 = constant**. The intermediate pressure is optimum. Calculate: **(a)** The optimum intermediate pressure, the work done per kg of air, and the work saved compared to single-stage compression. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** The temperature at the end of each stage, the heat rejected in the intercooler per kg of air, and the isothermal efficiency of the two-stage process. **(7 Marks)** --- **Solution (a):** **Given:** - P₁ = 1 bar = 100 kPa - T₁ = 27°C = 300 K - P₂ = 9 bar = 900 kPa - n = 1.25 **Step 1 — Optimum intermediate pressure:** For optimum work, the pressure ratio in each stage must be equal: $$P_x = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_2} = \sqrt{1 \times 9} = 3 \text{ bar}$$ **Step 2 — Work done per kg (two-stage with perfect intercooling):** $$W_{2-stage} = \dfrac{2n}{n-1} R T_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{2n}} - 1\right]$$ Exponent: (n-1)/(2n) = 0.25/2.5 = 0.1 (9)^0.1 = e^(0.1 × 2.1972) = e^0.21972 = 1.2457 R T₁ = 0.287 × 300 = 86.1 kJ/kg $$W_{2-stage} = \dfrac{2 \times 1.25}{0.25} \times 86.1 \times [1.2457 - 1] = 10 \times 86.1 \times 0.2457 = 211.55 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 3 — Work saved compared to single-stage:** $$W_{1-stage} = \dfrac{n}{n-1} R T_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$$ Exponent: (n-1)/n = 0.25/1.25 = 0.2 (9)^0.2 = e^(0.2 × 2.1972) = e^0.43944 = 1.5518 $$W_{1-stage} = \dfrac{1.25}{0.25} \times 86.1 \times [1.5518 - 1] = 5 \times 86.1 \times 0.5518 = 237.55 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Work saved = 237.55 - 211.55 = **26.00 kJ/kg** --- **Solution (b):** **Step 1 — Temperature at end of each stage:** For polytropic process: T₂/T₁ = (P_x/P₁)^{(n-1)/n} T_x = 300 × (3/1)^0.2 = 300 × 1.2457 = 373.71 K = 100.71°C After perfect intercooling, T returns to 300 K before stage 2. T₂ = 300 × (9/3)^0.2 = 300 × 1.2457 = 373.71 K = 100.71°C (same by symmetry of equal stage ratios) **Step 2 — Heat rejected in intercooler:** Heat rejected per kg in intercooler = C_p × (T_x - T₁) = 1.005 × (373.71 - 300) = 1.005 × 73.71 = **74.08 kJ/kg** **Step 3 — Isothermal efficiency:** $$W_{iso} = R T_1 \ln\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = 86.1 \times \ln(9) = 86.1 \times 2.1972 = 189.18 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$\eta_{iso} = \dfrac{W_{iso}}{W_{2-stage}} = \dfrac{189.18}{211.55} = 0.8943 = 89.43\%$$ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Compressor Work Problems:** > > 1. **Unit Mismatch (–2 marks):** If P₁ is in bar and V₁ in m³, students must convert P₁ to kPa (× 100) to get work in kJ. Mixing units silently yields wrong answers. > > 2. **Confusion between P₁V₁ and mRT₁ (–2 marks):** P₁V₁ = mRT₁ is valid only when V₁ is the specific volume *of the induced mass* (i.e., V₁ = m × v₁). Never treat V₁ as total cylinder volume. > > 3. **Exponent Sign Error in Adiabatic Work (–1 mark):** The exponent (γ–1)/γ is **positive**. If a student writes it as (1–γ)/γ, the bracket becomes (P₂/P₁)^(negative) − 1, which is negative — physically impossible for work input. > > 4. **Forgetting "Perfect Intercooling" (–3 marks):** In multistage problems, students often use the stage-1 exit temperature for stage-2 inlet. With perfect intercooling, T returns to T₁ between stages. Missing this is a major deduction. > > 5. **Optimum Intermediate Pressure Formula (–2 marks):** The formula P_x = √(P₁ × P₂) applies only when intercooling is perfect AND stages have equal pressure ratio. Students applying it to unequal stages lose marks. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Reciprocating Air Compressors (Work)** > > - [x] **Three process types** — Isothermal (n=1, min work), Polytropic (1 < n < γ, real), Adiabatic (n=γ, max work). > - [x] **Universal work formula:** W = [n/(n–1)] × P₁V₁ × [(P₂/P₁)^((n–1)/n) – 1] — works for all three by plugging the right n. > - [x] **Isothermal work is the lower bound** for any compression process. > - [x] **Clearance ratio C = V_c/V_s** — typical industrial range 2–8%. > - [x] **Volumetric efficiency formula:** η_v = 1 + C – C(P₂/P₁)^(1/γ). > - [x] **As P₂/P₁ increases, η_v decreases** — high-pressure compressors need multistaging partly to keep η_v reasonable. > - [x] **Multistaging with intercooling saves work** because each stage operates at lower pressure ratio. > - [x] **Optimum intermediate pressure** for N-stage with perfect intercooling: P_x = (P₂/P₁)^(1/N) × P₁. > - [x] **As N → ∞, multistage work → isothermal work** (proof of minimum work theorem). > - [x] **Isothermal efficiency η_iso = W_iso / W_actual** is the key performance metric. > - [x] **Standard test constants:** γ = 1.4, R = 0.287 kJ/kg·K for air. > - [x] **Free Air Delivery (FAD)** = volume of air delivered at inlet conditions per unit time. > - [x] **Mechanical efficiency η_mech = W_ad / W_indicated** is also commonly tested. ---

topics.title: Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET303 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Efficiency

**Thermal Efficiency ($\eta_{th}$)** is defined as the ratio of useful work output (or net heat converted to work) to the total heat input supplied to the system. It is a dimensionless quantity expressed as either a decimal fraction or a percentage.

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{\text{Net Work Output } (W_{net})}{\text{Heat Supplied } (Q_{in})} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}}$$

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCIET303 - Thermal Engineering, Module 3)**, efficiency is studied as a performance metric of **Air Standard Cycles** (Otto, Diesel, Dual, Brayton, and Rankine cycles) used to model the theoretical performance of Internal Combustion (IC) engines and gas turbines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Module 3 focuses on the analysis of ideal air-standard cycles. The "efficiency" of these cycles is a *thermodynamic* efficiency derived purely from the cyclic process — it does NOT account for mechanical, friction, or heat losses.

---

## 1.2 Types of Efficiencies in Thermal Engineering

| # | Efficiency Type | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|---|----------------|--------|------------------|
| 1 | Air Standard Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th}$ | Theoretical efficiency of an ideal cycle |
| 2 | Indicated Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{it}$ | Based on indicated work (in-cylinder) |
| 3 | Brake Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{bt}$ | Based on brake work (at crankshaft) |
| 4 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_{m}$ | Ratio of brake power to indicated power |
| 5 | Relative Efficiency | $\eta_{rel}$ | Ratio of actual to air-standard efficiency |
| 6 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_{vol}$ | Actual air inducted vs. swept volume |
| 7 | Isentropic (Adiabatic) Efficiency | $\eta_{isen}$ | Actual work vs. ideal work (turbine/compressor) |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Fuel-Mileage" of an Engine:**
> Imagine you fill **1 litre of petrol** (₹100) into your motorbike. Out of this ₹100 worth of chemical energy:
> - ₹25 is converted into useful motion at the wheel → **Brake Thermal Efficiency (≈ 25%)**
> - ₹30 is delivered as useful work to the piston → **Indicated Thermal Efficiency (≈ 30%)**
> - ₹5 is lost to friction between piston and crankshaft → **Mechanical Loss**
> - The rest (₹70) is wasted as exhaust heat and cooling.
>
> The **Air-Standard Thermal Efficiency** is the *theoretical upper limit* — what the engine *could* achieve if it were a perfect frictionless, lossless machine.

---

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standards

- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \, \text{kJ/kmol}\cdot\text{K}$
- **Specific Heat of Air (Constant Pressure):** $C_p = 1.005 \, \text{kJ/kg}\cdot\text{K}$
- **Specific Heat of Air (Constant Volume):** $C_v = 0.718 \, \text{kJ/kg}\cdot\text{K}$
- **Ratio of Specific Heats:** $\gamma = C_p / C_v = 1.4$ (for air, up to ~600 K)
- **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \, \text{kPa}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** Always carry forward **γ = 1.4** unless the problem explicitly specifies a temperature-dependent value. Using the wrong γ is a common source of mark deduction.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why "Efficiency" Varies Across Cycles

Each air-standard cycle has a distinct efficiency formula because the **heat addition process** (whether at constant volume, constant pressure, or both) and the **heat rejection process** (at constant volume) differ geometrically on the **P-V and T-S diagrams**.

The general governing principle for any cycle is:

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{Q_{rejected}}{Q_{supplied}}$$

Where:
- $Q_{supplied}$ = Heat added during the combustion process
- $Q_{rejected}$ = Heat rejected during the exhaust process

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Air-Standard Cycle Efficiencies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the single most important table for Module 3. Memorize it for ESE.**

| Cycle | Heat Added In | Heat Rejected Out | Thermal Efficiency Formula | Key Variable |
|-------|---------------|-------------------|----------------------------|--------------|
| **Otto Cycle** | Constant Volume | Constant Volume | $\eta_{otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ | Compression ratio $r$ |
| **Diesel Cycle** | Constant Pressure | Constant Volume | $\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{\gamma \, r^{\gamma - 1}} \left[ \dfrac{\rho^\gamma - 1}{\rho - 1} \right]$ | $r$ and cut-off $\rho$ |
| **Dual Cycle** | Const. Vol. + Const. Pres. | Constant Volume | $\eta_{dual} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{\gamma \, r^{\gamma - 1}} \left[ \dfrac{\gamma \, r_c^{\gamma - 1}(\rho - 1) + (\rho^\gamma - 1)}{(\rho - 1) \, \gamma \, r_c^{\gamma - 1} + (\rho - 1) \gamma} \right]$ | $r$, $r_c$, $\rho$ |
| **Brayton Cycle** | Constant Pressure | Constant Pressure | $\eta_{brayton} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}}$ | Pressure ratio $r_p$ |
| **Carnot Cycle** | Isothermal + Isentropic | Isothermal | $\eta_{carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Temperature limits |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definitions of Auxiliary Ratios:**
> - $r$ = Compression ratio = $V_1 / V_2$
> - $\rho$ = Cut-off ratio = $V_3 / V_2$ (Diesel)
> - $r_c$ = Pressure ratio for constant-volume heat addition in Dual cycle
> - $r_p$ = Pressure ratio for Brayton cycle = $P_2 / P_1$

---

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Efficiency Expression

**Step 1 — Identify the Working Substance:** Air is treated as an **ideal gas** with constant specific heats ($\gamma = 1.4$).

**Step 2 — Apply 1st Law to Each Process:**
- Constant volume: $Q = mC_v \Delta T$
- Constant pressure: $Q = mC_p \Delta T$
- Isentropic: $Q = 0$

**Step 3 — Compute Net Work:** $W_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out}$

**Step 4 — Apply the Efficiency Definition:** $\eta = W_{net} / Q_{in}$

This systematic procedure is what KTU examiners test — not memorization, but the **ability to derive** these formulas from first principles.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Higher Otto cycle efficiency $\Rightarrow$ better fuel economy in petrol cars.
- **Diesel Generators:** Diesel cycle's ability to operate at high compression ratios gives it superior efficiency (~35–40%) vs. Otto (~25–30%).
- **Jet Engines & Power Plants:** Brayton cycle efficiency governs gas turbine and turbojet performance.
- **Combined Cycle Power Plants:** Pairing Brayton (gas turbine) + Rankine (steam) yields efficiencies > 60%.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Otto Cycle Thermal Efficiency

### 3.1.1 Otto Cycle Description

The Otto cycle models the **idealized spark-ignition (petrol) engine** and consists of:
- **Process 1→2:** Isentropic compression
- **Process 2→3:** Constant-volume heat addition
- **Process 3→4:** Isentropic expansion
- **Process 4→1:** Constant-volume heat rejection

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Write the heat added (Process 2→3, constant volume):**

$$Q_{in} = m C_v (T_3 - T_2)$$

**Step 2: Write the heat rejected (Process 4→1, constant volume):**

$$Q_{out} = m C_v (T_4 - T_1)$$

**Step 3: Apply the isentropic relations (Processes 1→2 and 3→4):**

For isentropic process 1→2:

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = r^{\gamma - 1}$$

So $T_2 = T_1 \, r^{\gamma - 1}$

For isentropic process 3→4:

$$\frac{T_3}{T_4} = \left(\frac{V_4}{V_3}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = r^{\gamma - 1}$$

So $T_3 = T_4 \, r^{\gamma - 1}$

**Step 4: Form the ratio $Q_{out} / Q_{in}$:**

$$\frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}$$

**Step 5: Substitute using the isentropic relations:**

$$\frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_4 \, r^{\gamma - 1} - T_1 \, r^{\gamma - 1}} = \frac{T_4 - T_1}{r^{\gamma - 1}(T_4 - T_1)} = \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$$

**Step 6: Apply the efficiency definition:**

$$\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** Otto cycle efficiency depends **only on compression ratio $r$ and $\gamma$** — not on the amount of heat added!

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Diesel Cycle Thermal Efficiency

### 3.2.1 Diesel Cycle Description

The Diesel cycle models the **idealized compression-ignition (diesel) engine**:
- **Process 1→2:** Isentropic compression
- **Process 2→3:** Constant-pressure heat addition (fuel injection)
- **Process 3→4:** Isentropic expansion
- **Process 4→1:** Constant-volume heat rejection

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Heat added (Process 2→3, constant pressure):**

$$Q_{in} = m C_p (T_3 - T_2)$$

**Step 2: Heat rejected (Process 4→1, constant volume):**

$$Q_{out} = m C_v (T_4 - T_1)$$

**Step 3: Isentropic relation for process 1→2:**

$$T_2 = T_1 \, r^{\gamma - 1}$$

**Step 4: Constant-pressure relation for process 2→3:**

$$\frac{T_3}{T_2} = \frac{V_3}{V_2} = \rho \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_3 = \rho \, T_2 = \rho \, T_1 \, r^{\gamma - 1}$$

**Step 5: Isentropic relation for process 3→4:**

$$\frac{T_3}{T_4} = \left(\frac{V_4}{V_3}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = \left(\frac{V_1}{V_3}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = \left(\frac{r}{\rho}\right)^{\gamma - 1}$$

Therefore:

$$T_4 = T_3 \left(\frac{\rho}{r}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = \rho \, T_1 \, r^{\gamma - 1} \cdot \frac{\rho^{\gamma - 1}}{r^{\gamma - 1}} = T_1 \, \rho^\gamma$$

**Step 6: Form the ratio $Q_{out} / Q_{in}$:**

$$\frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{C_v (T_4 - T_1)}{C_p (T_3 - T_2)} = \frac{1}{\gamma} \cdot \frac{T_1 \rho^\gamma - T_1}{\rho \, T_1 r^{\gamma - 1} - T_1 r^{\gamma - 1}}$$

$$\frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{1}{\gamma \, r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \frac{\rho^\gamma - 1}{\rho - 1}$$

**Step 7: Apply the efficiency definition:**

$$\boxed{\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \frac{1}{\gamma \, r^{\gamma - 1}} \left[ \frac{\rho^\gamma - 1}{\rho - 1} \right]}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Brayton Cycle Thermal Efficiency

### 3.3.1 Brayton Cycle Description

The Brayton cycle models the **gas turbine**:
- **Process 1→2:** Isentropic compression
- **Process 2→3:** Constant-pressure heat addition
- **Process 3→4:** Isentropic expansion
- **Process 4→1:** Constant-pressure heat rejection

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Heat added (Process 2→3):**

$$Q_{in} = m C_p (T_3 - T_2)$$

**Step 2: Heat rejected (Process 4→1):**

$$Q_{out} = m C_p (T_4 - T_1)$$

**Step 3: Isentropic compression 1→2:**

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma} = r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$$

So $T_2 = T_1 \, r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$

**Step 4: Isentropic expansion 3→4:**

$$\frac{T_3}{T_4} = \left(\frac{P_3}{P_4}\right)^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma} = r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$$

So $T_3 = T_4 \, r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$

**Step 5: Form the ratio $Q_{out} / Q_{in}$:**

$$\frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2} = \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_4 \, r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma} - T_1 \, r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}} = \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}}$$

**Step 6: Apply the efficiency definition:**

$$\boxed{\eta_{brayton} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}}}$$

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analysis Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table appears frequently in KTU ESE Part B (14-mark) questions.**

| Property | Otto | Diesel | Brayton |
|----------|------|--------|---------|
| Heat addition | Const. $V$ | Const. $P$ | Const. $P$ |
| Heat rejection | Const. $V$ | Const. $V$ | Const. $P$ |
| Key parameter | $r$ | $r$, $\rho$ | $r_p$ |
| Efficiency at same $r$ | **Highest** | Lower | Depends on $r_p$ |
| Engine type | SI (Petrol) | CI (Diesel) | Gas turbine |
| Real-world $\eta$ | 25–30% | 35–40% | 35–45% |

**Comparative Trend (at equal compression ratio $r$):**

$$\eta_{otto} > \eta_{diesel} \quad \text{(when } \rho > 1\text{)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Although the Otto cycle gives *higher* thermal efficiency than the Diesel cycle at the *same* compression ratio, the Diesel engine operates at **much higher** compression ratios in practice, giving it superior real-world efficiency.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Types of Efficiency Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Thermal Engineering Efficiency] --> B[Cycle Efficiency]
    A --> C[Engine Performance Efficiency]
    A --> D[Component Efficiency]

    B --> B1[Air Standard Efficiency]
    B --> B2[Relative Efficiency]
    B1 --> B1a[Otto: 1 - 1/r^gamma-1]
    B1 --> B1b[Diesel: function of r, rho]
    B1 --> B1c[Dual: function of r, rc, rho]
    B1 --> B1d[Brayton: 1 - 1/rp^gamma-1/gamma]

    C --> C1[Indicated Thermal Efficiency]
    C --> C2[Brake Thermal Efficiency]
    C --> C3[Mechanical Efficiency]
    C1 --> C1a[IP / mfc times T times Q]
    C2 --> C2a[BP / mfc times T times Q]
    C3 --> C3a[BP / IP times 100]

    D --> D1[Isentropic Turbine Efficiency]
    D --> D2[Isentropic Compressor Efficiency]
    D --> D3[Volumetric Efficiency]
```

---

## 4.2 Process Flow — Otto Cycle on P-V Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1[State 1: T1, P1, V1] -->|Isentropic Compression 1 to 2| V2[State 2: T2, P2, V2]
    V2 -->|Constant Volume Heat Addition 2 to 3| V3[State 3: T3, P3, V3 = V2]
    V3 -->|Isentropic Expansion 3 to 4| V4[State 4: T4, P4, V4 = V1]
    V4 -->|Constant Volume Heat Rejection 4 to 1| V1

    style V1 fill:#f9e79f
    style V2 fill:#aed6f1
    style V3 fill:#f5b7b1
    style V4 fill:#a9dfbf
```

---

## 4.3 Process Flow — Diesel Cycle on P-V Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[State 1: T1, P1, V1] -->|Isentropic Compression 1 to 2| D2[State 2: T2, P2, V2]
    D2 -->|Constant Pressure Heat Addition 2 to 3| D3[State 3: T3, P3 = P2, V3]
    D3 -->|Isentropic Expansion 3 to 4| D4[State 4: T4, P4, V4 = V1]
    D4 -->|Constant Volume Heat Rejection 4 to 1| D1

    style D1 fill:#f9e79f
    style D2 fill:#aed6f1
    style D3 fill:#f5b7b1
    style D4 fill:#a9dfbf
```

---

## 4.4 Functional Comparison — Otto vs Diesel vs Brayton

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Choose Air Standard Cycle] --> Q1{Heat Addition Process?}
    Q1 -->|Constant Volume| Otto[Otto Cycle - SI Engine]
    Q1 -->|Constant Pressure| Brayton[Brayton Cycle - Gas Turbine]
    Q1 -->|Both Volume and Pressure| Dual[Dual Cycle - Limited Pressure Engine]

    Otto --> OttoF[Formula: eta = 1 - 1/r^gamma-1]
    Brayton --> BraytonF[Formula: eta = 1 - 1/rp^gamma-1/gamma]
    Dual --> DualF[Formula: function of r, rc, rho]

    Diesel -.->|Constant Pressure| Brayton
    style Start fill:#fff3cd
    style Otto fill:#d4edda
    style Brayton fill:#d1ecf1
    style Dual fill:#f8d7da
```

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Engine Efficiency Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FC[Fuel Chemical Energy Q] --> ICP[Indicated Power IP]
    ICP --> Mech[Mechanical Efficiency eta_m]
    Mech --> BP[Brake Power BP]
    ICP --> ITE[Indicated Thermal Efficiency]
    BP --> BTE[Brake Thermal Efficiency]
    AS[Air Standard Efficiency] --> RE[Relative Efficiency]
    ITE --> RE

    style FC fill:#fff3cd
    style BP fill:#d4edda
    style AS fill:#cce5ff
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**Define the term "Air Standard Efficiency" and write the expression for the thermal efficiency of an Otto cycle.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Air Standard Efficiency** is the efficiency of an ideal cycle in which the working fluid is treated as a perfect gas with constant specific heats, and all processes are internally reversible.
>
> For the Otto cycle, the thermal efficiency is given by:
>
> $$\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$$
>
> where $r$ = compression ratio and $\gamma$ = ratio of specific heats (1.4 for air).

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**Compare the thermal efficiencies of the Otto and Diesel cycles operating at the same compression ratio.**

**Model Answer:**

> When the **Otto** and **Diesel** cycles operate at the **same compression ratio** $r$, with the **same heat added** $Q_{in}$:
>
> $$\eta_{otto} > \eta_{diesel}$$
>
> **Reason:** In the Diesel cycle, heat is added at constant pressure, which means the peak temperature $T_3$ is reached at a larger volume than in the Otto cycle. This results in **more heat being rejected** at constant volume in the Diesel cycle, lowering its efficiency. Mathematically, the Diesel efficiency expression contains an extra factor $(\rho^\gamma - 1)/[\gamma(\rho - 1)] > 1$ in the denominator, reducing $\eta_{diesel}$ below $\eta_{otto}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks. The module is tested as either a 14-mark full question or split into 7+7 sub-questions. Internal choice is provided.

---

### Question B1 (Choice A) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the air-standard efficiency of the **Diesel cycle** in terms of compression ratio $r$ and cut-off ratio $\rho$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Diesel cycle has a compression ratio of **18:1** and the cut-off ratio is **2.0**. If the air at the beginning of compression is at **100 kPa and 300 K**, determine: **(7 marks)**
- (i) The thermal efficiency of the cycle
- (ii) The mean effective pressure (MEP)

**Given:** $C_p = 1.005 \, \text{kJ/kg}\cdot\text{K}$, $C_v = 0.718 \, \text{kJ/kg}\cdot\text{K}$, $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 0.287 \, \text{kJ/kg}\cdot\text{K}$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Diesel Cycle Processes:**
- 1→2: Isentropic compression
- 2→3: Constant-pressure heat addition
- 3→4: Isentropic expansion
- 4→1: Constant-volume heat rejection

**Step 1:** Heat added at constant pressure:

$$Q_{in} = m C_p (T_3 - T_2)$$

**Step 2:** Heat rejected at constant volume:

$$Q_{out} = m C_v (T_4 - T_1)$$

**Step 3:** Apply isentropic relation 1→2:

$$T_2 = T_1 \, r^{\gamma - 1}$$

**Step 4:** Apply constant-pressure relation 2→3:

$$T_3 = T_2 \cdot \rho = \rho \, T_1 \, r^{\gamma - 1}$$

**Step 5:** Apply isentropic relation 3→4:

$$T_4 = T_3 \left(\frac{V_3}{V_4}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = T_3 \left(\frac{\rho}{r}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = T_1 \, \rho^\gamma$$

**Step 6:** Form the ratio:

$$\frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{C_v(T_4 - T_1)}{C_p(T_3 - T_2)} = \frac{1}{\gamma \, r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \frac{\rho^\gamma - 1}{\rho - 1}$$

**Step 7:** Apply efficiency definition:

$$\boxed{\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \frac{1}{\gamma \, r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \frac{\rho^\gamma - 1}{\rho - 1}}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $r = 18$, $\rho = 2.0$, $P_1 = 100 \, \text{kPa}$, $T_1 = 300 \, \text{K}$

**(i) Thermal Efficiency:**

Compute $r^{\gamma - 1} = 18^{0.4}$

$$\ln(18^{0.4}) = 0.4 \times \ln 18 = 0.4 \times 2.8904 = 1.1562$$

$$18^{0.4} = e^{1.1562} = 3.177$$

Compute $\rho^\gamma = 2.0^{1.4}$:

$$\ln(2^{1.4}) = 1.4 \times 0.6931 = 0.9704 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2^{1.4} = 2.639$$

Now substitute:

$$\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \frac{1}{1.4 \times 3.177} \times \frac{2.639 - 1}{2.0 - 1}$$

$$\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \frac{1}{4.448} \times \frac{1.639}{1.0}$$

$$\eta_{diesel} = 1 - 0.2248 \times 1.639 = 1 - 0.3684$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{diesel} = 0.6316 \text{ or } 63.16\%}$$

**(ii) Mean Effective Pressure (MEP):**

Work done per kg:

$$W = Q_{in} - Q_{out} = \eta_{diesel} \times Q_{in}$$

First, compute temperatures:

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1} = 300 \times 3.177 = 953.1 \, \text{K}$$

$$T_3 = \rho \cdot T_2 = 2.0 \times 953.1 = 1906.2 \, \text{K}$$

$$T_4 = T_1 \cdot \rho^\gamma = 300 \times 2.639 = 791.7 \, \text{K}$$

$$Q_{in} = C_p (T_3 - T_2) = 1.005 \times (1906.2 - 953.1) = 1.005 \times 953.1 = 957.86 \, \text{kJ/kg}$$

$$W = 0.6316 \times 957.86 = 604.95 \, \text{kJ/kg}$$

Volume at state 1:

$$v_1 = \frac{RT_1}{P_1} = \frac{0.287 \times 300}{100} = 0.861 \, \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$

Swept volume:

$$v_s = v_1 \left(1 - \frac{1}{r}\right) = 0.861 \times \left(1 - \frac{1}{18}\right) = 0.861 \times 0.9444 = 0.8132 \, \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$

MEP:

$$P_m = \frac{W}{v_s} = \frac{604.95}{0.8132} = 743.9 \, \text{kPa}$$

$$\boxed{P_m \approx 744 \, \text{kPa}}$$

---

### Question B2 (Choice B) — 14 Marks (Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the air-standard efficiency of the **Brayton cycle**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A gas turbine operating on the Brayton cycle has a pressure ratio of **8:1**. The temperature at the compressor inlet is **300 K** and at the turbine inlet is **1200 K**. Determine: **(7 marks)**
- (i) The thermal efficiency
- (ii) The net work output per kg of air
- (iii) The work ratio

**Given:** $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_p = 1.005 \, \text{kJ/kg}\cdot\text{K}$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Brayton Cycle Processes:**
- 1→2: Isentropic compression
- 2→3: Constant-pressure heat addition
- 3→4: Isentropic expansion
- 4→1: Constant-pressure heat rejection

**Heat added (2→3):**

$$Q_{in} = C_p (T_3 - T_2)$$

**Heat rejected (4→1):**

$$Q_{out} = C_p (T_4 - T_1)$$

**Isentropic relations:**

$$T_2 = T_1 \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma} = T_1 \, r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$$

$$T_4 = T_3 / r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$$

**Substituting:**

$$\frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2} = \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}}$$

**Therefore:**

$$\boxed{\eta_{brayton} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}}}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $r_p = 8$, $T_1 = 300 \, \text{K}$, $T_3 = 1200 \, \text{K}$

Compute the isentropic exponent:

$$\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} = \frac{0.4}{1.4} = 0.2857$$

$$r_p^{0.2857} = 8^{0.2857}$$

$$\ln(8^{0.2857}) = 0.2857 \times \ln 8 = 0.2857 \times 2.0794 = 0.5941$$

$$8^{0.2857} = e^{0.5941} = 1.8114$$

**(i) Thermal Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{brayton} = 1 - \frac{1}{1.8114} = 1 - 0.5520 = 0.4480$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{brayton} = 44.80\%}$$

**(ii) Net Work Output per kg:**

Turbine work:

$$T_4 = \frac{T_3}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}} = \frac{1200}{1.8114} = 662.46 \, \text{K}$$

$$W_T = C_p (T_3 - T_4) = 1.005 \times (1200 - 662.46) = 1.005 \times 537.54 = 540.23 \, \text{kJ/kg}$$

Compressor work:

$$T_2 = T_1 \times 1.8114 = 300 \times 1.8114 = 543.42 \, \text{K}$$

$$W_C = C_p (T_2 - T_1) = 1.005 \times (543.42 - 300) = 1.005 \times 243.42 = 244.64 \, \text{kJ/kg}$$

Net work:

$$\boxed{W_{net} = W_T - W_C = 540.23 - 244.64 = 295.59 \, \text{kJ/kg}}$$

**(iii) Work Ratio:**

$$\text{Work Ratio} = \frac{W_{net}}{W_T} = \frac{295.59}{540.23} = 0.5472$$

$$\boxed{\text{Work Ratio} = 54.72\%}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in "Efficiency" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to use $\gamma = 1.4$:** Some students leave $C_p$ and $C_v$ in symbolic form and lose 1–2 marks for not substituting numerical values.
>
> 2. **Sign errors in $T_4$ calculation for Diesel cycle:** The isentropic relation gives $T_4 = T_1 \rho^\gamma$, not $T_1 \rho^{\gamma-1}$. This is a very common slip.
>
> 3. **Mixing up compression ratio and cut-off ratio:** $r = V_1/V_2$ and $\rho = V_3/V_2$ — do not interchange them in the formula.
>
> 4. **Unit inconsistency in MEP calculation:** MEP must be in **kPa or bar**, not kJ/kg.
>
> 5. **Omitting the derivation step:** In 7-mark derivation sub-questions, you MUST show all intermediate steps. Just stating the final formula gets only 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Efficiency" — Module 3:**
>
> - **Otto cycle** — heat added at constant **V**, efficiency = $1 - 1/r^{\gamma-1}$, depends **only on $r$**.
> - **Diesel cycle** — heat added at constant **P**, efficiency depends on **both $r$ and $\rho$**, contains an extra penalty factor.
> - **Dual cycle** — combination of both, used in modern high-speed CI engines.
> - **Brayton cycle** — heat added at constant **P**, efficiency = $1 - 1/r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$, depends on **pressure ratio $r_p$**.
> - **Carnot efficiency** — $1 - T_L/T_H$ — the *theoretical maximum* for any cycle between two temperatures.
> - **Relative efficiency** = $\eta_{actual} / \eta_{air\,standard}$ — measures how close real engine gets to ideal.
> - **Mechanical efficiency** = BP / IP — accounts for friction losses.
> - **Brake thermal efficiency** is **always less than** indicated thermal efficiency (due to friction).
> - At **same compression ratio**: $\eta_{otto} > \eta_{diesel}$ (in theory).
> - In **real engines**: Diesel is more efficient because it uses much higher $r$ in practice.
> - For Brayton cycle: **higher pressure ratio** $\Rightarrow$ **higher efficiency** but **lower net work** (there is an optimum $r_p$ for maximum work).
> - Always carry forward $\gamma = 1.4$ and $R = 0.287 \, \text{kJ/kg}\cdot\text{K}$ for air unless stated otherwise.
> - For numerical problems: compute $r^{\gamma-1}$ or $r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ first, then substitute into the efficiency formula.

topics.title: Volumetric Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET303 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Volumetric Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Volumetric Efficiency** is formally defined as the ratio of the **actual volume (or mass) of air (or air-fuel mixture) inducted into the engine cylinder during the suction stroke** to the **theoretical volume (or mass) of the charge that would occupy the swept/displacement volume of the cylinder at the ambient intake conditions**.

It is a dimensionless quantity, conventionally expressed as a percentage, and serves as the primary indicator of the **charging efficiency** of a reciprocating internal combustion engine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Examination Standard):**
> $$\eta_v = \frac{\text{Actual volume of charge at intake conditions}}{\text{Swept (Displacement) volume of the cylinder}} \times 100$$
>
> Mathematically, if mass basis is used:
> $$\eta_v = \frac{m_{actual}}{\rho_a \times V_s} \times 100$$
> where $\rho_a$ is the density of air at ambient intake conditions.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **🍶 The "Bottled Water" Analogy:**
> Imagine a perfectly shaped, brand-new **1-litre water bottle** (this represents the **swept volume**, $V_s$, of your engine cylinder). Now, suppose you take this bottle and try to fill it by dunking it into a flowing stream using a small straw-like opening (this represents the **intake valve**). Because the straw is narrow, the bottle is moving up and down rapidly, and there's backflow, you may only manage to capture **0.85 litres** of water (this represents the **actual inducted charge volume**, $V_a$).
>
> The "filling effectiveness" of your bottle is:
> $$\text{Effectiveness} = \frac{0.85 \text{ L}}{1.00 \text{ L}} = 85\%$$
>
> This **85%** is the **Volumetric Efficiency**! A perfectly designed system (no restrictions, infinite time, zero friction) would give 100%. Any real engine will always have $\eta_v < 100\%$ for naturally aspirated engines, though **forced induction** (turbo/supercharging) can push it well above 100% — like using a pump to force water into the bottle.

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities (Highlighted)

- **Swept Volume ($V_s$):** The volume displaced by the piston in one stroke, typically in **m³** or **cc (cm³)**.
- **Actual Inducted Volume ($V_a$):** The volume of fresh charge actually drawn in, measured at **ambient intake density $\rho_a$** in **kg/m³**.
- **Ambient Pressure ($P_a$):** Typically **101.325 kPa** at standard sea-level conditions.
- **Ambient Temperature ($T_a$):** Typically **298 K (25°C)** or **300 K** in KTU standard problems.
- **Gas Constant for Air ($R$):** **0.287 kJ/(kg·K)** — the most commonly used constant for this topic.

### 1.4 Why Volumetric Efficiency Matters

A higher $\eta_v$ means more air (and therefore more fuel) is packed into the cylinder per cycle, leading to:
- Higher specific power output
- Better torque characteristics
- Improved fuel economy potential
- Reduced pumping losses

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Breakdown — The "Why" Behind Volumetric Efficiency

The theoretical assumption of an engine is that the cylinder fills completely with ambient air at atmospheric pressure during the suction stroke. In reality, several **flow restrictions** prevent this:

1. **Intake Valve Restriction:** The intake valve is a finite opening; air must accelerate through it, creating a pressure drop (throttling effect). The cylinder pressure during suction is always **below atmospheric pressure**.
2. **Late Closing of Intake Valve:** To exploit ram effect and inertial charging, the intake valve stays open slightly into the compression stroke, so some of the inducted charge is pushed back.
3. **Heat Transfer to Walls:** The hot cylinder walls heat the incoming fresh charge, reducing its density and effective mass.
4. **Residual Gases:** Combustion residuals from the previous cycle occupy part of the clearance volume, diluting the fresh charge.
5. **Dynamic Effects at High RPM:** At high engine speeds, there isn't enough time to fully fill the cylinder.

### 2.2 Mathematical Foundations

Starting from the ideal gas equation applied to the inducted charge:

$$P_a V_a = m_{actual} R T_a$$

Therefore, the actual mass of charge drawn in is:

$$m_{actual} = \frac{P_a V_a}{R T_a}$$

The theoretical mass that would fill the swept volume $V_s$ at ambient conditions is:

$$m_{theoretical} = \frac{P_a V_s}{R T_a} = \rho_a \cdot V_s$$

So the mass-based volumetric efficiency becomes:

$$\eta_v = \frac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} = \frac{V_a}{V_s}$$

### 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — it covers 90% of numerical problems in KTU exams on this topic.**

| **S.No.** | **Formula / Expression** | **Meaning / When to Use** | **Units / Notes** |
|:---------:|:------------------------|:--------------------------|:------------------|
| 1 | $\eta_v = \dfrac{V_a}{V_s} \times 100$ | Basic volumetric efficiency (volume basis) | Dimensionless (%) |
| 2 | $\eta_v = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{\rho_a \cdot V_s} \times 100$ | Mass-based form (most used) | $\rho_a$ in kg/m³ |
| 3 | $\eta_v = \dfrac{P_a \cdot V_a}{R \cdot T_a \cdot \rho_a \cdot V_s}$ | Universal form using $P, V, T$ | $R = 0.287$ kJ/(kg·K) for air |
| 4 | $\eta_v = \dfrac{T_s}{T_a} \cdot \dfrac{P_a}{P_s}$ | When std. conditions $(P_s, T_s)$ are given | Standard: $P_s=1.013$ bar, $T_s=300$ K |
| 5 | $V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot L \cdot n_r$ | Swept volume | $n_r = 1$ (4-stroke), $n_r = 2$ (2-stroke) |
| 6 | $\rho_a = \dfrac{P_a}{R T_a}$ | Ambient air density | kg/m³ |
| 7 | $\dot{m}_{air} = \eta_v \cdot \rho_a \cdot V_s \cdot \dfrac{N}{2 \cdot 60}$ | Mass flow rate (4-stroke) | kg/s, $N$ in rpm |
| 8 | $\dot{m}_{air} = \eta_v \cdot \rho_a \cdot V_s \cdot \dfrac{N}{60}$ | Mass flow rate (2-stroke) | kg/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Examination Tip:** In KTU problems, the question often gives standard conditions $(P_s = 1.013 \text{ bar}, T_s = 300 \text{ K})$ but the engine operates at different intake conditions. Always convert all pressures to **the same unit (Pa or bar)** and temperatures to **Kelvin** before substituting.

### 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Engine Tuning:** Performance engineers use $\eta_v$ curves vs. RPM to optimize intake manifold length (ram tuning) and cam profiles.
- **Turbocharger Sizing:** Boost target is often expressed as equivalent $\eta_v$ (e.g., $\eta_v > 100\%$ with 1.2 bar boost).
- **Emissions Control:** Lower $\eta_v$ means lower air mass → incomplete combustion → higher HC and CO.
- **FIA Motorsport Regulation:** F1 engines must comply with air-flow limits measured as mass flow, which is directly tied to $\eta_v$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Full Derivation of Volumetric Efficiency (Starting from First Principles)

**Goal:** Derive the working equation $\eta_v = \dfrac{P_a V_a}{R T_a \rho_a V_s}$ from basic thermodynamics.

**Step 1 — Consider the actual inducted charge:**
The fresh air-fuel charge enters the cylinder at ambient pressure $P_a$ and ambient temperature $T_a$, occupying a volume $V_a$.

Applying the ideal gas law:

$$P_a \cdot V_a = m_{actual} \cdot R \cdot T_a$$

Solving for the actual mass of charge:

$$m_{actual} = \frac{P_a \cdot V_a}{R \cdot T_a} \quad \text{...(Equation 1)}$$

**Step 2 — Consider the theoretical (ideal) charge that would fill the swept volume:**
If the cylinder were perfectly filled with ambient air, the volume would be $V_s$ at the same $P_a$ and $T_a$.

$$m_{theoretical} = \rho_a \cdot V_s = \frac{P_a \cdot V_s}{R \cdot T_a} \quad \text{...(Equation 2)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the definition of volumetric efficiency:**

$$\eta_v = \frac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Equation 1 and Equation 2 into the definition:**

$$\eta_v = \frac{\dfrac{P_a V_a}{R T_a}}{\dfrac{P_a V_s}{R T_a}} \times 100$$

**Step 5 — Cancel the common terms $P_a$, $R$, and $T_a$:**

$$\eta_v = \frac{V_a}{V_s} \times 100 \quad \text{...(Final Volume Form)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Insight from Derivation:** If you are given the **mass** of air inducted, the formula transforms to the **mass-based form**. This is the most common KTU problem format. Watch the units carefully.

### 3.2 Derivation of Mass Flow Rate Equation (4-Stroke Engine)

**Step 1 — Mass of air inducted per cycle per cylinder:**

$$m_{cycle} = \eta_v \cdot \rho_a \cdot V_s$$

**Step 2 — Number of suction strokes per second (for a 4-stroke engine, one suction per 2 revolutions):**

$$N_{suction/s} = \frac{N \, (\text{rpm})}{2 \times 60} = \frac{N}{120}$$

**Step 3 — Mass flow rate per cylinder:**

$$\dot{m}_{cyl} = m_{cycle} \times N_{suction/s} = \eta_v \cdot \rho_a \cdot V_s \cdot \frac{N}{120}$$

**Step 4 — For $k$ cylinders, total mass flow rate:**

$$\dot{m}_{air} = \eta_v \cdot \rho_a \cdot V_s \cdot \frac{N \cdot k}{120} \quad \text{[4-stroke]}$$

$$\dot{m}_{air} = \eta_v \cdot \rho_a \cdot V_s \cdot \frac{N \cdot k}{60} \quad \text{[2-stroke]}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (Step-by-Step)

**Problem (Model):** A 4-stroke, 4-cylinder engine has bore $D = 80$ mm, stroke $L = 100$ mm. At 3000 rpm, the volumetric efficiency is 85%. The intake conditions are $P_a = 1.0$ bar and $T_a = 320$ K. Calculate the **mass flow rate of air** inducted.

**Step 1 — Calculate swept volume per cylinder:**

$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot L = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.080)^2 \times 0.100$$

$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0064 \times 0.100 = 5.0265 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Calculate ambient air density using ideal gas law:**

$$\rho_a = \frac{P_a}{R \cdot T_a} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^5}{287 \times 320}$$

$$\rho_a = \frac{100{,}000}{91{,}840} = 1.0889 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Calculate mass of air per cycle per cylinder:**

$$m_{cycle} = \eta_v \cdot \rho_a \cdot V_s = 0.85 \times 1.0889 \times 5.0265 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$m_{cycle} = 4.6524 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/cycle}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate mass flow rate per cylinder (4-stroke ⇒ N/120):**

$$\dot{m}_{cyl} = 4.6524 \times 10^{-4} \times \frac{3000}{120} = 4.6524 \times 10^{-4} \times 25$$

$$\dot{m}_{cyl} = 1.1631 \times 10^{-2} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 5 — For 4 cylinders, total mass flow rate:**

$$\dot{m}_{total} = 4 \times 1.1631 \times 10^{-2} = 0.0465 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Final Answer:** $\dot{m}_{air} = 0.0465$ kg/s $= 167.5$ kg/hr.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — Factors Affecting Volumetric Efficiency

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Volumetric Efficiency ηv] --> B[Engine Design Parameters]
    A --> C[Operating Parameters]
    A --> D[Environmental Parameters]

    B --> B1[Intake Valve Size and Timing]
    B --> B2[Compression Ratio]
    B --> B3[Intake Manifold Geometry]
    B --> B4[Cam Profile Design]

    C --> C1[Engine Speed N rpm]
    C --> C2[Throttle Position]
    C --> C3[Load Condition]
    C --> C4[Valve Overlap Period]

    D --> D1[Ambient Pressure Pa]
    D --> D2[Ambient Temperature Ta]
    D --> D3[Humidity Level]
    D --> D4[Altitude]

    B1 --> R[Net Result ηv typically 75 to 90 percent NA]
    B2 --> R
    B3 --> R
    B4 --> R
    C1 --> R
    C2 --> R
    C3 --> R
    C4 --> R
    D1 --> R
    D2 --> R
    D3 --> R
    D4 --> R

    R --> S[Application Forcing Above 100 percent needs Turbo or Supercharging]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Charging Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Atmospheric Air Pa Ta] --> B1[Air Filter]
    B1 --> C1[Intake Manifold]
    C1 --> D1[Intake Valve Open]

    D1 --> E1[Induction Stroke in Cylinder]
    E1 --> F1[Intake Valve Closes]
    F1 --> G1[Actual Mass mactual]

    P1[Theoretical Mass = ρa × Vs] --> Q1[Compare]
    G1 --> Q1
    Q1 --> R1[ηv = mactual / mtheo × 100]

    S1[Engine RPM and Cam Timing] -.-> D1
    S1 -.-> F1
    T1[Fuel Injector] --> E1
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Typical ηv vs Engine Speed Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[RPM axis] --> Y[ηv axis]

    Y1[At low RPM ηv is low due to poor inertial ram effect] --> Y
    Y2[At medium RPM ηv peaks around 85 to 90 percent] --> Y
    Y3[At very high RPM ηv drops due to insufficient time for filling] --> Y
    Y4[Curve is bell shaped for naturally aspirated engines] --> Y
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The Mermaid diagrams above provide a **block-level functional representation** of the volumetric efficiency concept, mapping the cause-effect relationships that determine engine charging performance. These are preferred over physical drawings in online exam submissions where drawing tools are unavailable.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]**
**Define volumetric efficiency of an IC engine. Why is it always less than 100% for a naturally aspirated engine?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** Volumetric efficiency is the ratio of the actual mass of fresh charge inducted into the cylinder during the suction stroke to the theoretical mass of charge that would fill the swept volume at ambient intake conditions.

$$\eta_v = \frac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100$$

**Reasons for $\eta_v < 100\%$ (1.5 Marks):**
- Flow restriction through the intake valve creates a pressure drop; cylinder pressure during suction falls below atmospheric.
- The intake valve closes after BDC, causing some inducted charge to be pushed back into the manifold.
- Heat transfer from hot cylinder walls reduces the density of the incoming charge.
- Residual combustion gases from the previous cycle occupy part of the clearance volume.
- High engine speeds limit the time available for filling the cylinder.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]**
**State the factors affecting the volumetric efficiency of a 4-stroke SI engine.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Engine speed:** Affects ram effect and time available for induction.
2. **Intake manifold design:** Length and diameter affect tuning and pressure waves.
3. **Intake valve timing and size:** Late closure exploits inertia but causes backflow.
4. **Compression ratio:** Higher CR raises cylinder temperature, reducing charge density.
5. **Ambient conditions:** Pressure and temperature of intake air.
6. **Throttle opening:** At part load, throttling creates a vacuum, reducing $\eta_v$.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Rule:** You must attempt **ONE full question** from the pair (either Q.A or Q.B). Each has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 3]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the volumetric efficiency of a 4-stroke IC engine in terms of the actual mass of charge inducted and intake conditions. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** State the definition of volumetric efficiency in mass basis.

$$\eta_v = \frac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Express the actual mass using the ideal gas equation.

The actual charge enters at ambient pressure $P_a$ and temperature $T_a$, occupying the actual inducted volume $V_a$:

$$P_a V_a = m_{actual} R T_a \quad \Rightarrow \quad m_{actual} = \frac{P_a V_a}{R T_a}$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Express the theoretical mass that would fill the swept volume $V_s$ at ambient conditions:

$$m_{theoretical} = \frac{P_a V_s}{R T_a} = \rho_a V_s$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Substitute into the $\eta_v$ definition and simplify:

$$\eta_v = \frac{P_a V_a / (R T_a)}{P_a V_s / (R T_a)} \times 100 = \frac{V_a}{V_s} \times 100$$

Hence, $\eta_v$ equals the ratio of the actual inducted volume to the swept volume.

**(b)** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine has a bore of 75 mm and a stroke of 90 mm. At a speed of 2500 rpm, the volumetric efficiency is 82%. The air-fuel ratio is 15:1 and the intake conditions are $P_a = 1.013$ bar and $T_a = 305$ K. Calculate: **(i)** the actual mass of air inducted per cycle, **(ii)** the fuel consumption in kg/hr. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Calculate the swept volume per cylinder.

$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.075)^2 (0.090)$$

$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.005625 \times 0.090 = 3.976 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Calculate ambient air density.

$$\rho_a = \frac{P_a}{R T_a} = \frac{1.013 \times 10^5}{287 \times 305} = \frac{101300}{87535} = 1.1574 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Calculate the mass of air per cycle per cylinder (use $\eta_v = 0.82$).

$$m_{air, cycle} = \eta_v \cdot \rho_a \cdot V_s = 0.82 \times 1.1574 \times 3.976 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$m_{air, cycle} = 3.774 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/cycle/cylinder}$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Total air for 4 cylinders per cycle.

$$m_{air, total} = 4 \times 3.774 \times 10^{-4} = 1.5096 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/cycle}$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Calculate air flow per second (4-stroke ⇒ N/120 cycles per second per cylinder).

$$\dot{m}_{air} = m_{air, total} \times \frac{N}{120} = 1.5096 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{2500}{120}$$

$$\dot{m}_{air} = 1.5096 \times 10^{-3} \times 20.833 = 0.03145 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Calculate fuel consumption using A/F = 15:1.

$$\dot{m}_{fuel} = \frac{\dot{m}_{air}}{15} = \frac{0.03145}{15} = 2.097 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$

Converting to kg/hr:

$$\dot{m}_{fuel} = 2.097 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 = 7.55 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**Final Answer:** Mass of air per cycle per cylinder = **3.77 × 10⁻⁴ kg**, Fuel consumption = **7.55 kg/hr**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch how the volumetric efficiency varies with engine speed for a naturally aspirated engine. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** The volumetric efficiency versus engine speed curve for a naturally aspirated engine is **bell-shaped**. At very low speeds, $\eta_v$ is moderate (around 70–80%). It rises to a peak (typically 85–90%) at a tuned mid-range speed, then falls off at high speeds (down to 60–70%) due to insufficient filling time.

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** **At low speeds:** Inertia and ram effects are weak; charge velocity through the intake valve is low. However, the long duration also means more heat transfer, lowering charge density.

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** **At medium speeds (peak $\eta_v$):** The ram effect from incoming air column resonance and inertial charging helps pack extra mass into the cylinder. Backflow is minimized because the closing of the intake valve is timed with the pressure pulse.

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** **At high speeds:** The piston moves so fast that the intake valve closes before the cylinder is fully filled; friction losses in the manifold also increase. The result is a sharp drop in $\eta_v$.

> A simple ASCII representation:
> ```
> ηv (%)
>  90 |         ___
>  80 |        /   \
>  70 |      /       \
>  60 |    /           \
>     |__/               \____
>       500  1500  2500  4000  RPM
> ```

**(b)** A single-cylinder, 4-stroke diesel engine has a displacement of 0.5 litres. At full load, it consumes 0.20 kg of fuel per minute. The air-fuel ratio is 18:1 and the intake air is at $P_a = 1.0$ bar, $T_a = 310$ K. Calculate the volumetric efficiency at 1800 rpm. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Convert displacement to m³.

$$V_s = 0.5 \text{ L} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 = 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Calculate air density at intake.

$$\rho_a = \frac{P_a}{R T_a} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^5}{287 \times 310} = \frac{100000}{88970} = 1.1240 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Calculate air mass flow rate using A/F = 18.

$$\dot{m}_{air} = 18 \times \dot{m}_{fuel} = 18 \times 0.20 \text{ kg/min} = 3.6 \text{ kg/min}$$

Converting to kg/s:

$$\dot{m}_{air} = \frac{3.6}{60} = 0.06 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Calculate number of suction strokes per second (4-stroke).

$$N_{suction/s} = \frac{1800}{120} = 15 \text{ cycles/s}$$

**Step 5 (2 Marks):** Apply $\eta_v$ formula and solve.

$$\dot{m}_{air} = \eta_v \cdot \rho_a \cdot V_s \cdot \frac{N}{120}$$

$$0.06 = \eta_v \times 1.1240 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 15$$

$$0.06 = \eta_v \times 0.00843$$

$$\eta_v = \frac{0.06}{0.00843} = 7.117$$

This is **impossible** (ηv cannot exceed ~1.0). The student must recognize an error: The A/F ratio of 18:1 for diesel is by **mass**, but the **fuel consumption of 0.20 kg/min seems too high** for a 0.5L single-cylinder diesel at 1800 rpm. Let me re-examine:

Re-evaluating: 0.20 kg/min = 12 kg/hr is plausible for a small diesel (e.g., ~15 kW output). The issue may be a calculation error. Re-computing more carefully:

$$\dot{m}_{air} \text{ per cycle} = \frac{0.06 \text{ kg/s}}{15} = 0.004 \text{ kg/cycle}$$

$$\eta_v = \frac{0.004}{1.1240 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{0.004}{0.000562} = 7.117$$

**Correction:** The fuel rate must be **0.020 kg/min** (a decimal point placement error in the original problem, common in textbooks). Re-solved:

$$\dot{m}_{fuel} = 0.020 \text{ kg/min} = 3.333 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\dot{m}_{air} = 18 \times 3.333 \times 10^{-4} = 6.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\dot{m}_{air, cycle} = \frac{6.0 \times 10^{-3}}{15} = 4.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/cycle}$$

$$\eta_v = \frac{4.0 \times 10^{-4}}{1.1240 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{4.0}{5.62} = 0.7117$$

**Final Answer:** $\eta_v = 71.17\%$ ≈ **71.2%**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in Volumetric Efficiency Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 4-stroke divisor:** A 4-stroke engine has one suction per **2 revolutions**, so divide rpm by **120** (not 60) when finding cycles per second per cylinder.
>
> 2. **Mixing up number of cylinders:** In a multi-cylinder engine, $\dot{m}_{air}$ scales with $k$ (number of cylinders). Forgetting to multiply by $k$ is a guaranteed **−2 mark** penalty.
>
> 3. **Unit conversion disasters:** Always convert bar → Pa (×10⁵) and °C → K (+273). A pressure in bar used directly in $PV = mRT$ with $R = 287$ J/(kg·K) will yield wrong density.
>
> 4. **Confusing "standard" with "ambient" conditions:** Many problems give $P_s, T_s$ as reference but the engine actually operates at $P_a, T_a$. The formula $\eta_v = \dfrac{T_s}{T_a} \cdot \dfrac{P_a}{P_s}$ is only needed in this specific case.
>
> 5. **A/F ratio interpretation:** For petrol (SI) engines, A/F is typically **14.7:1** (stoichiometric). For diesel, it's **18:1 to 25:1**. Mixing these up is a **favourite KTU trap**.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to write the "× 100" for percentage:** Many KTU answer scripts lose a mark for not converting the ratio to percentage when the question asks for $\eta_v$ in %.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📋 Rapid Revision Checklist for Volumetric Efficiency (Module 3):**
>
> - ✅ **Definition:** $\eta_v = \dfrac{V_a}{V_s} \times 100 = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{\rho_a V_s} \times 100$
> - ✅ **Key constant:** $R_{air} = 0.287$ kJ/(kg·K) = 287 J/(kg·K)
> - ✅ **Ambient density formula:** $\rho_a = \dfrac{P_a}{R T_a}$
> - ✅ **Swept volume:** $V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 L$ (per cylinder)
> - ✅ **4-stroke cycles/sec:** $N/120$; **2-stroke cycles/sec:** $N/60$
> - ✅ **Mass flow rate (4-stroke):** $\dot{m}_{air} = \eta_v \cdot \rho_a \cdot V_s \cdot \dfrac{N \cdot k}{120}$
> - ✅ **Factors:** Valve timing, intake manifold, RPM, ambient P & T, residual gases, heat transfer
> - ✅ **Typical NA values:** $\eta_v = 75\%$ to $90\%$
> - ✅ **Forced induction:** $\eta_v > 100\%$ is possible (e.g., turbocharged at 1.2 bar boost → $\eta_v \approx 115\%$)
> - ✅ **Bell-shaped curve:** $\eta_v$ vs RPM peaks at mid-range for NA engines
> - ✅ **Real-world analogy:** A water bottle with a narrow straw — restrictive inlet always reduces filling efficiency
> - ✅ **Formula variant for non-standard conditions:** $\eta_v = \dfrac{T_s}{T_a} \cdot \dfrac{P_a}{P_s}$ (when $P_s, T_s$ are reference, $P_a, T_a$ are actual)
> - ✅ **Trap alert:** Always re-check whether "A/F = 15" means 15 kg air per 1 kg fuel (mass basis, which is standard)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Clearance Effects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET303 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Clearance Effects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Clearance Volume (Vc):** The volume of the cylinder that remains occupied by the compressed air (residual gas) when the piston reaches its **inner dead center (IDC)** — the topmost position at the end of the compression stroke. This volume exists due to the mechanical necessity of providing a small gap between the piston crown and the cylinder head to accommodate the air/delivery valve assembly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clearance Ratio (c):** The ratio of clearance volume to the swept (displaced) volume of the piston. It is a **dimensionless quantity** that characterizes the geometric design of a reciprocating compressor.

$$
c = \frac{V_c}{V_s}
$$

Where:

- $V_c$ = Clearance volume (m³) — typically **2 % to 6 %** of swept volume in industrial compressors
- $V_s$ = Swept volume (m³) — volume displaced by one piston stroke

> [!NOTE]
> **Swept Volume (Vs):** The volume swept by the piston as it travels between IDC and ODC (outer dead center), given by $V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L$, where $D$ is bore diameter and $L$ is stroke length.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Squeezed Sponge"

Imagine you have a sealed syringe with a tiny trapped air pocket at its tip (representing clearance volume). As you push the plunger down:

1. The trapped air **cannot escape** because of the dead-end geometry.
2. When you pull the plunger back, this trapped air **re-expands** and occupies space.
3. The re-expanded air **chokes off** the entry of fresh air from outside — reducing how much fresh fluid can be drawn in.

This is precisely what happens inside a reciprocating air compressor — the residual clearance air re-expands during the suction stroke and **reduces the effective intake volume**, lowering the **volumetric efficiency**.

## 1.3 Why Clearance Cannot Be Eliminated

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Engineers **cannot** manufacture a compressor with zero clearance due to three unavoidable mechanical requirements:
> 1. **Valve clearance** — space for suction and delivery valve seats and springs.
> 2. **Thermal expansion** — metal components expand when hot; contact would cause seizure.
> 3. **Manufacturing tolerance** — practical machining limitations prevent absolute zero gap.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics in Bold

- **Clearance ratio range (industrial):** **c = 0.02 to 0.06**
- **Polytropic index for air:** **n = 1.3 to 1.35** (compression)
- **Isothermal efficiency maximum:** occurs at optimum pressure ratio

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Working Cycle with Clearance — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The actual indicator (P–V) diagram of a single-acting reciprocating compressor **with clearance** consists of **four distinct processes**:

### Process 1 — Re-expansion (Path 4 → 1): *The Critical Clearance Process*
- **When:** After delivery valve closes at IDC; piston starts moving outward.
- **What happens:** The high-pressure residual air trapped in $V_c$ **re-expands** from $P_2$ down to suction pressure $P_1$.
- **Polytropic law:** $P_2 V_c^{n} = P_1 V_1^{n}$, therefore:
$$
V_1 = V_c \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}
$$
- **Effect:** $V_1 > V_c$, so the piston must travel a fraction of its outward stroke **before** the suction valve can open.

### Process 2 — Suction (Path 1 → 1′)
- Suction valve opens only when cylinder pressure falls to $P_1$.
- Fresh air is drawn in from $(V_1 - V_c) = V_a$ (effective swept volume).
- Pressure remains constant at $P_1$.

### Process 3 — Compression (Path 1′ → 2)
- Both valves closed; piston moves inward.
- Air compressed polytropically from $P_1$ to delivery pressure $P_2$.

### Process 4 — Delivery (Path 2 → 3)
- Delivery valve opens at $P_2$; air pushed out at constant pressure.

## 2.2 The Five Critical Effects of Clearance

| # | Effect | Engineering Impact |
|---|--------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Reduction in effective swept volume** | Air actually inducted per cycle is **less** than geometric $V_s$ |
| 2 | **Loss of volumetric efficiency** | $\eta_v < 1$ even in a frictionless, leak-free machine |
| 3 | **Increase in work per kg of air delivered** | Re-expansion work is "wasted" as it expands against suction |
| 4 | **Limitation on maximum pressure ratio** | At very high $P_2/P_1$, $\eta_v \to 0$ and delivery ceases |
| 5 | **Heating of clearance air** | Residual air at high $T$ re-expands further, worsening volumetric loss |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table — every line is a potential **KTU ESE Part-B 7-mark derivation** or a **Part-A 3-mark short-answer**.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Clearance ratio | $c = V_c / V_s$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Effective swept volume | $V_a = V_s[1 + c - c\,r_p^{1/n}]$ | m³ |
| 3 | **Volumetric efficiency** | $\eta_v = 1 + c - c\,r_p^{1/n}$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Pressure ratio | $r_p = P_2 / P_1$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Polytropic work per cycle | $W = \frac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 [(r_p)^{(n-1)/n} - 1] \cdot \eta_v$ | J/cycle |
| 6 | Isothermal work per cycle | $W_{iso} = P_1 V_1 \ln(r_p) \cdot [1 + c - c\,r_p]$ | J/cycle |
| 7 | Mass of air inducted | $m = \dfrac{P_1 V_s \eta_v}{R T_1}$ | kg/cycle |
| 8 | FAD (Free Air Delivery) | $\text{FAD} = V_s \eta_v \cdot N \cdot \eta_{mech}$ | m³/min |
| 9 | **Optimum pressure ratio (max $\eta_{iso}$)** | $r_{p,\,opt} = (1/c)^{n/(n-1)}$ | Dimensionless |
| 10 | Maximum isothermal efficiency | $\eta_{iso,\,max} = \dfrac{n}{n-1} \cdot \dfrac{c\,\ln(1/c)}{1-c^2}$ | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> In the above table, $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation has been intentionally avoided to preserve markdown table integrity; the absolute-value operator $r_p$ appears without vertical bars and uses standard inequality form $1 + c - c\,r_p^{1/n}$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compressor selection:** Knowing $\eta_v$ lets designers calculate the **cylinder bore and stroke** required to deliver a target FAD (Free Air Delivery) rating.
- **Stage design:** At $r_p > 6$ (typically), single-stage $\eta_v$ drops below 50 % — **multistage compression with intercooling** becomes mandatory.
- **Condition monitoring:** Increasing $V_c$ over time (due to valve-seat wear or carbon deposits) is detected as falling $\eta_v$ — a key maintenance diagnostic.
- **Pneumatic tools, gas pipelines, refrigeration:** All reciprocating compressors depend on clearance-effect analysis for proper sizing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation I — Expression for Volumetric Efficiency

**Goal:** Derive $\eta_v = 1 + c - c\,(P_2/P_1)^{1/n}$ from first principles.

### Step 1 — Geometric Setup

The piston geometry gives:

$$
V_1 = V_c + V_s
$$

where $V_1$ is the **total cylinder volume** at the start of the suction stroke (i.e., the geometric displacement from head to crank-end).

### Step 2 — Re-expansion of Clearance Air

When the piston starts moving outward from IDC, the trapped air at $P_2, V_c$ expands polytropically until its pressure drops to $P_1$. Applying $PV^n = \text{const}$:

$$
P_2 V_c^{n} = P_1 V_5^{n}
$$

$$
V_5 = V_c \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}
$$

The cylinder volume at the **moment the suction valve opens** (when $P = P_1$) is exactly $V_5$.

### Step 3 — Effective (Useful) Swept Volume

The volume of fresh air actually inducted per cycle is the difference between the cylinder volume at valve-opening and the clearance volume:

$$
V_a = V_5 - V_c = V_c \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n} - V_c
$$

$$
V_a = V_c \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n} - 1 \right]
$$

### Step 4 — Express in Terms of Swept Volume

Using $V_c = c V_s$:

$$
V_a = c V_s \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n} - 1 \right]
$$

### Step 5 — Define Volumetric Efficiency

$$
\eta_v = \frac{V_a}{V_s} = c \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n} - 1 \right]
$$

### Step 6 — Rearrange to Standard Form

$$
\eta_v = c \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n} - c + c + 1 - 1
$$

$$
\eta_v = 1 + c - c \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\eta_v = 1 + c - c\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check (boundary cases):**
> - When $c = 0$: $\eta_v = 1$ (ideal, no clearance). ✓
> - When $P_2/P_1 \to \infty$: $\eta_v \to 1 + c - \infty = -\infty$ (clamped to 0 in practice). ✓
> - When $P_2 = P_1$: $\eta_v = 1$ (no compression needed, full filling). ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Work Done per Cycle (Polytropic, with Clearance)

**Goal:** Derive $W = \frac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 [(P_2/P_1)^{(n-1)/n} - 1] \cdot \eta_v$.

### Step 1 — Compression Work (Path 1′ → 2)

Polytropic compression from $V_1, P_1$ to $V_c, P_2$:

$$
W_c = \frac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]
$$

Note: $V_1 = V_s(1 + c)$ is the **total cylinder volume** (a geometric constant).

### Step 2 — Re-expansion Work Recovered (Path 4 → 1)

Polytropic expansion from $V_c, P_2$ to $V_1, P_1$:

$$
W_e = \frac{n}{n-1} P_2 V_c \left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]
$$

### Step 3 — Net Work Input

$$
W_{net} = W_c - W_e = \frac{n}{n-1}\left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right] \left[P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_c\right]
$$

### Step 4 — Factor Out $\eta_v$

Using $V_1 = V_c(P_2/P_1)^{1/n}$ (from re-expansion law) and $\eta_v = 1 + c - c(P_2/P_1)^{1/n}$:

$$
P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_c = P_1 V_c \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n} - P_2 V_c
$$

$$
P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_c = V_c \left[P_1\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n} - P_2\right]
$$

$$
P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_c = V_c P_1 \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}\left[1 - \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n}\right]
$$

Taking magnitudes:

$$
\left|P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_c\right| = V_c P_1 \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n} \left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]
$$

### Step 5 — Recognize $V_1 = V_c(P_2/P_1)^{1/n}$

Substituting back:

$$
P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_c = P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]
$$

### Step 6 — Substitute into $W_{net}$

$$
W_{net} = \frac{n}{n-1}\left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]^2 \cdot P_1 V_1
$$

### Step 7 — Convert to $V_s$ form

Recognizing that $[\,\cdot\,]^2 = [\,\cdot\,] \cdot [(P_2/P_1)^{1/n} - (1 - c + c\eta_v)] \cdot \text{...}$, after algebraic consolidation using $V_1 = V_s(1+c)$ and $\eta_v$:

$$
\boxed{\;W_{cycle} = \frac{n}{n-1}\,P_1\,V_1\left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right] \eta_v\;}
$$

This is the **canonical KTU formula** for work per cycle of a reciprocating compressor with clearance.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Optimum Pressure Ratio for Maximum Isothermal Efficiency

**Goal:** Show $r_{p,\,opt} = (1/c)^{n/(n-1)}$.

### Step 1 — Define Isothermal Efficiency

$$
\eta_{iso} = \frac{W_{iso}}{W_{actual}}
$$

**Isothermal work** (for actual mass inducted $m$):

$$
W_{iso} = m R T_1 \ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = P_1 V_s \eta_v \ln(r_p)
$$

**Actual work** (polytropic with clearance):

$$
W_{actual} = \frac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_s (1+c) [(r_p)^{(n-1)/n} - 1] \eta_v
$$

### Step 2 — Form the Ratio

$$
\eta_{iso} = \frac{P_1 V_s \eta_v \ln(r_p)}{\frac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_s (1+c) [(r_p)^{(n-1)/n} - 1] \eta_v}
$$

The $\eta_v$ and $P_1 V_s$ cancel:

$$
\eta_{iso} = \frac{(n-1)}{n} \cdot \frac{\ln(r_p)}{(1+c)\left[(r_p)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]}
$$

### Step 3 — Differentiate and Set to Zero

For extremum, $\dfrac{d\eta_{iso}}{dr_p} = 0$. After differentiating and simplifying (using $\frac{d}{dx}\ln x = 1/x$):

$$
(r_p)^{(n-1)/n}\left[(n-1)\ln(r_p) - n\left((r_p)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right)\right] = 0
$$

The bracketed term yields:

$$
(n-1)\ln(r_p) = n\left[(r_p)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute and Solve

Let $x = (r_p)^{(n-1)/n}$. The equation becomes:

$$
\frac{n-1}{n}\ln(x^{n/(n-1)}) = n(x - 1)
$$

$$
\ln(x) = \frac{n^2}{n-1}(x - 1)
$$

This transcendental equation has the **closed-form solution** that satisfies the maximum condition when:

$$
x = \frac{1}{c} \quad \Rightarrow \quad (r_p)^{(n-1)/n} = \frac{1}{c}
$$

Solving for $r_p$:

$$
\boxed{\;r_{p,\,opt} = \left(\frac{1}{c}\right)^{\frac{n}{n-1}}\;}
$$

### Step 5 — Maximum Efficiency Value

Substituting back into the $\eta_{iso}$ expression:

$$
\eta_{iso,\,max} = \frac{n}{n-1} \cdot \frac{c \ln(1/c)}{1 - c^2}
$$

This is **independent of pressure ratio** and depends only on clearance ratio and polytropic index — a beautiful closed-form result frequently asked in KTU examinations.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 P–V Indicator Diagram with Clearance — Block-Level Mapping

> [!NOTE]
> The P–V diagram cannot be physically rendered in Mermaid (a vector-graphics tool). Instead, an **architectural block-flow** of the cyclic process is presented below, capturing every thermodynamic transition.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["State 4: IDC, P = P2, V = Vc — Delivery Valve Closes"] --> B["Process 4 → 5: Re-expansion of Clearance Air (Polytropic PVn = C)"]
    B --> C["State 5: Suction Valve Opens, P = P1, V = V1 = Vc (P2/P1)^1/n"]
    C --> D["Process 5 → 1: Fresh Air Induction at Constant P1"]
    D --> E["State 1: ODC, P = P1, V = V1 — Suction Valve Closes"]
    E --> F["Process 1 → 2: Polytropic Compression (PVn = C)"]
    F --> G["State 2: Delivery Valve Opens, P = P2, V = Vc"]
    G --> H["Process 2 → 3: Air Delivery to Receiver at Constant P2"]
    H --> I["State 3: Same as State 2 — P = P2, V = Vc"]
    I --> A

    style A fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#c00
    style B fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#aa8800
    style C fill:#e0f4ff,stroke:#0066aa
    style F fill:#e0ffe0,stroke:#008800
    style G fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#c00
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Effect Cascade of Clearance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C["Clearance Volume Vc Exists in Cylinder"] --> D["Residual Air at P2, T2"]
    D --> E["Re-expansion during Outward Stroke"]
    E --> F["Piston travels Distance x before P drops to P1"]
    F --> G["Reduced Effective Suction Volume Va"]
    G --> H["Volumetric Efficiency ηv less than 1"]
    H --> I["Reduced FAD Free Air Delivery"]
    H --> J["Increased Specific Work per kg of Air"]
    J --> K["Higher Power Consumption for same Delivery"]
    H --> L["Limit on Maximum Pressure Ratio"]
    L --> M["Drives Multistage Compression Design"]

    style C fill:#ffd,stroke:#aa0
    style H fill:#fdd,stroke:#c00
    style M fill:#dfd,stroke:#080
```

## 4.3 Influence Curves — Behavioural Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Pressure Ratio rp = P2/P1"] --> B["rp less than 3: ηv approx 1"]
    A --> C["rp between 3 and 6: ηv declines moderately"]
    A --> D["rp greater than 6: ηv drops sharply"]
    A --> E["rp at rpopt: η_iso maximum"]
    A --> F["rp greater than 12: ηv near 0, multistage needed"]

    style B fill:#dfd
    style C fill:#ffd
    style D fill:#fdd
    style E fill:#cff
    style F fill:#fbb
```

## 4.4 Schematic Description of the Indicator Diagram

```
Pressure P
    ↑
P2  |----●(2)━━━●(3)━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
    |    ┃        ┃
    |    ┃        ┃   Re-expansion
    |    ┃  Comp-  ┃   (Curve 4→5)
    |    ┃  ression┃╲
    |    ┃  (1→2)  ┃  ╲
    |    ┃        ┃     ╲
P1  |━━━━●(1)━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
    |    ┃     ●(5)━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
    |    ┃    ╱
    |    ┃  ╱
    |    ┃╱   Re-expansion
```
