```
    |    ┃    curve
    0----●----●----●----●----●----●--→  Volume V
        Vc   V5   V1   V2 ... V3

  Point 1: BDC of compression (P1, V1)
  Point 2: TDC start of delivery (P2, Vc)
  Point 3: TDC end of delivery  (P2, Vc)
  Point 4: TDC start of expansion (P2, Vc)
  Point 5: Suction valve opens  (P1, V5)
```

**Key features visible in the diagram:**
- **Area under curve 1→2** = Compression work input
- **Area under curve 4→5** = Re-expansion work (recovered as back-pressure)
- **Enclosed area (1-2-3-4-5)** = Net useful work input per cycle

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Q: Define clearance volume and clearance ratio. Why is clearance provided in a reciprocating compressor?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Clearance Volume (Vc):** The volume of the air/gas that remains trapped inside the cylinder of a reciprocating compressor when the piston reaches the end of its compression stroke (inner dead centre). **(1 mark)**

**Clearance Ratio (c):** It is the ratio of clearance volume to the swept volume of the piston.

$$
c = \frac{V_c}{V_s}
$$

**(1 mark)**

Clearance is provided to: (i) accommodate the suction and delivery valve assemblies, (ii) prevent the piston from striking the cylinder head due to thermal expansion, and (iii) allow for manufacturing tolerances. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sample]`

**Q: With the help of a P–V diagram, explain the effect of clearance on the volumetric efficiency of a reciprocating compressor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The P–V diagram shows four processes: re-expansion (4–5), suction (5–1), compression (1–2), and delivery (2–3). **(1 mark)**

After delivery, the clearance air at $P_2, V_c$ re-expands polytropically during the outward stroke. The suction valve opens only when cylinder pressure drops to $P_1$ at volume $V_5$, where:

$$
V_5 = V_c (P_2/P_1)^{1/n}
$$

The effective swept volume is $V_a = V_5 - V_c$, which is **less** than $V_s$. **(1 mark)**

Therefore volumetric efficiency is:

$$
\eta_v = 1 + c - c(P_2/P_1)^{1/n} < 1
$$

**(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Writing $V_1 = V_c + V_a$ as if $V_a = V_s$ — always remember $V_a = V_s \eta_v$.
> - **Common mistake 2:** Forgetting to use $V_c = c V_s$ when substituting into final expressions — leads to dimensional inconsistency.
> - **Common mistake 3:** Confusing "total cylinder volume" ($V_1 = V_s + V_c$) with "volume at start of compression" — they differ by the re-expansion term.
> - **Common mistake 4:** In optimum-pressure-ratio problems, students often differentiate incorrectly — use the chain rule on $r_p^{(n-1)/n}$ with care.
> - **Mark-loss zones:** Sign of work, units of $\eta_v$ (dimensionless!), and forgetting the natural-logarithm $\ln r_p$ for isothermal cases.

### Choice 1 — Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q(a) Derive an expression for the volumetric efficiency of a reciprocating compressor with clearance. Show that it is a function of clearance ratio and pressure ratio. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Consider a single-acting reciprocating compressor with clearance volume $V_c$ and swept volume $V_s$. The total cylinder volume when the piston is at the outer dead centre is:

$$
V_1 = V_c + V_s = V_s(1+c)
$$

**Step 1 — Re-expansion:** When the piston moves outward from inner dead centre, the residual air at $P_2, V_c$ expands polytropically ($PV^n = \text{const}$) until its pressure equals the suction pressure $P_1$. If $V_5$ is the volume at this instant:

$$
P_2 V_c^n = P_1 V_5^n \quad\Rightarrow\quad V_5 = V_c\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 2 — Effective induction volume:** Fresh air is drawn in only after the suction valve opens. The volume of fresh air actually inducted per cycle is:

$$
V_a = V_5 - V_c = V_c\left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n} - 1\right]
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Volumetric efficiency:** Substituting $V_c = c V_s$:

$$
\eta_v = \frac{V_a}{V_s} = c\left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n} - 1\right]
$$

Rearranging by adding and subtracting $c$:

$$
\eta_v = 1 + c - c\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Discussion (1 mark):** This expression shows $\eta_v$ depends only on the clearance ratio $c$ and the pressure ratio $P_2/P_1$. As $P_2/P_1$ increases, $\eta_v$ decreases. When $c = 0$, $\eta_v = 1$ (ideal case).

---

**Q(b) A two-stage reciprocating compressor with complete intercooling compresses air from 1 bar to 100 bar. The polytropic index is 1.3. The clearance ratio is 4 % for both stages. Calculate: (i) the optimum pressure ratio per stage, (ii) the overall isothermal efficiency, and (iii) the work of compression per kg of air. (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $P_1 = 1$ bar, $P_3 = 100$ bar
- $n = 1.3$, $c = 0.04$ (both stages)
- Intercooling to initial temperature $T_1 = 300$ K (assumed standard)
- $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Optimum pressure ratio per stage:**

$$
r_{p,\,opt} = \left(\frac{1}{c}\right)^{n/(n-1)} = \left(\frac{1}{0.04}\right)^{1.3/0.3}
$$

$$
r_{p,\,opt} = 25^{4.333} = ?
$$

Computing: $\ln(25) = 3.2189$; $4.333 \times 3.2189 = 13.948$; $e^{13.948} \approx 1.145 \times 10^6$ — this is the single-stage optimum, not two-stage.

**For two-stage**, optimum ratio per stage is:

$$
r = (P_3/P_1)^{1/N} = (100)^{1/2} = 10
$$

**(1 mark)** — but verify against single-stage optimum. Since $r = 10 < 1.145 \times 10^6$, the two-stage optimum $r = 10$ is achievable.

**(ii) Overall isothermal efficiency:**

Work of isothermal compression (no clearance, for actual mass):

$$
W_{iso} = R T_1 \ln(P_3/P_1) = 0.287 \times 300 \times \ln(100)
$$

$$
W_{iso} = 86.1 \times 4.6052 = 396.5 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**(1 mark)**

Per-stage polytropic work (with clearance):

$$
W_{stage} = \frac{n}{n-1} R T_1 \left[(r_p)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right] \cdot \eta_v
$$

For each stage with $r = 10$:

$$
\eta_v = 1 + 0.04 - 0.04 \times 10^{1/1.3} = 1.04 - 0.04 \times 10^{0.7692}
$$

$$
10^{0.7692} = e^{0.7692 \times 2.3026} = e^{1.7714} = 5.876
$$

$$
\eta_v = 1.04 - 0.04 \times 5.876 = 1.04 - 0.235 = 0.805
$$

**(1 mark)**

$$
W_{stage} = \frac{1.3}{0.3} \times 0.287 \times 300 \times [10^{0.3/1.3} - 1] \times 0.805
$$

$$
10^{0.2308} = e^{0.2308 \times 2.3026} = e^{0.5314} = 1.7014
$$

$$
W_{stage} = 4.333 \times 86.1 \times [1.7014 - 1] \times 0.805 = 4.333 \times 86.1 \times 0.7014 \times 0.805
$$

$$
W_{stage} = 4.333 \times 86.1 \times 0.5646 = 210.6 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**(1 mark)** — **note** per-stage work calculation here represents the polytropic work input per kg per stage.

Total work for two stages (with intercooling back to $T_1$):

$$
W_{total} = 2 \times 210.6 = 421.2 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**(1 mark)**

Overall isothermal efficiency:

$$
\eta_{iso} = \frac{W_{iso}}{W_{total}} = \frac{396.5}{421.2} = 0.9413 = 94.13\%
$$

**(1 mark)**

**(iii) Work of compression per kg of air:**

$$
\boxed{W_{compression} = 421.2 \text{ kJ/kg}}
$$

**(1 mark)**

---

### Choice 2 — Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q(a) With a neat P–V diagram, explain the actual working of a single-stage reciprocating air compressor. Discuss the effect of clearance on volumetric efficiency. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**P–V Diagram Description (2 marks):**

The actual P–V diagram consists of four processes:

- **Process 4 → 5 (Re-expansion):** Residual clearance air at $P_2, V_c$ expands polytropically to $P_1, V_5$.
- **Process 5 → 1 (Suction):** Fresh air drawn in at constant $P_1$ as piston moves outward from volume $V_5$ to $V_1$.
- **Process 1 → 2 (Compression):** Polytropic compression from $P_1, V_1$ to $P_2, V_c$.
- **Process 2 → 3 (Delivery):** Air expelled at constant $P_2$ from $V_c$ to ... (actually, piston is at IDC throughout delivery).

**Effect of Clearance on Volumetric Efficiency (5 marks):**

Volumetric efficiency is the ratio of actual inducted volume to swept volume:

$$
\eta_v = \frac{V_a}{V_s} = 1 + c - c(P_2/P_1)^{1/n}
$$

**Key observations:**

(i) When $c = 0$: $\eta_v = 1$ — no clearance means ideal filling. **(1 mark)**

(ii) For a given clearance ratio, $\eta_v$ **decreases** with increasing $P_2/P_1$. At very high pressure ratios, $\eta_v$ can drop to zero — meaning **no air is delivered** even though the machine runs. **(1 mark)**

(iii) Higher clearance ratio $c$ causes lower $\eta_v$ for any given $P_2/P_1$. Industrial compressors keep $c \le 6\%$. **(1 mark)**

(iv) Hot clearance air expands more (higher effective $T_2$ means lower $\eta_v$); thus cooling the cylinder improves $\eta_v$. **(1 mark)**

(v) For multistage compressors, intermediate intercooling reduces the temperature of the inducted air, restoring $\eta_v$ for subsequent stages. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q(b) A single-stage reciprocating compressor delivers 8 m³/min of free air. The suction conditions are 1 bar and 27 °C. The delivery pressure is 7 bar. The clearance ratio is 5 % and the polytropic index is 1.35. The compressor runs at 300 rpm. Determine: (i) the volumetric efficiency, (ii) the swept volume of the cylinder, and (iii) the power required to drive the compressor. (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $\text{FAD} = 8$ m³/min
- $P_1 = 1$ bar $= 10^5$ Pa, $T_1 = 300$ K
- $P_2 = 7$ bar, $c = 0.05$, $n = 1.35$
- $N = 300$ rpm, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Volumetric efficiency:**

$$
\eta_v = 1 + c - c(P_2/P_1)^{1/n} = 1 + 0.05 - 0.05 \times 7^{1/1.35}
$$

$$
7^{1/1.35} = 7^{0.7407} = e^{0.7407 \times \ln 7} = e^{0.7407 \times 1.9459} = e^{1.4414} = 4.227
$$

$$
\eta_v = 1.05 - 0.05 \times 4.227 = 1.05 - 0.2114 = 0.8386 \approx 83.86\%
$$

**(2 marks)**

**(ii) Swept volume of cylinder:**

Free air delivered per minute = Swept volume per cycle $\times N \times \eta_v$:

$$
\text{FAD} = V_s \times N \times \eta_v
$$

$$
V_s = \frac{\text{FAD}}{N \times \eta_v} = \frac{8}{300 \times 0.8386} = \frac{8}{251.58} = 0.0318 \text{ m}^3/\text{cycle}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**(iii) Power required:**

Work per cycle:

$$
W = \frac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[(P_2/P_1)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right] \eta_v
$$

Where $V_1 = V_s(1+c) = 0.0318 \times 1.05 = 0.0334$ m³

$(P_2/P_1)^{(n-1)/n} = 7^{0.35/1.35} = 7^{0.2593} = e^{0.2593 \times 1.9459} = e^{0.5045} = 1.6563$

$$
W = \frac{1.35}{0.35} \times 10^5 \times 0.0334 \times [1.6563 - 1] \times 0.8386
$$

$$
W = 3.857 \times 10^5 \times 0.0334 \times 0.6563 \times 0.8386
$$

$$
W = 3.857 \times 10^5 \times 0.01839 = 7093 \text{ J/cycle} = 7.093 \text{ kJ/cycle}
$$

**(2 marks)**

Power:

$$
P = W \times N = 7.093 \times \frac{300}{60} = 7.093 \times 5 = 35.46 \text{ kW}
$$

**(1 mark)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Clearance Effects**

- **Clearance volume $V_c$** = residual gas volume at IDC; **clearance ratio $c = V_c/V_s$**; typical values **0.02 to 0.06**.
- Clearance is **unavoidable** — needed for valves, thermal expansion, manufacturing tolerances.
- **Four processes** in actual cycle: re-expansion (4→5), suction (5→1), compression (1→2), delivery (2→3).
- **Volumetric efficiency formula (must memorize):**
  $$
  \eta_v = 1 + c - c\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}
  $$
- **Effective swept volume:** $V_a = V_s \eta_v = V_s[1 + c - c(P_2/P_1)^{1/n}]$.
- **Work per cycle (polytropic with clearance):**
  $$
  W = \frac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[(P_2/P_1)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right] \eta_v
  $$
- **Work per cycle (isothermal with clearance):**
  $$
  W_{iso} = P_1 V_1 \ln(P_2/P_1) \cdot [1 + c - c \cdot P_2/P_1]
  $$
- **Mass of air inducted per cycle:** $m = \dfrac{P_1 V_s \eta_v}{R T_1}$.
- **FAD** = $V_s \eta_v N \eta_{mech}$ — used in compressor selection problems.
- **Optimum pressure ratio (max $\eta_{iso}$, single stage):**
  $$
  r_{p,\,opt} = \left(\frac{1}{c}\right)^{n/(n-1)}
  $$
- **Maximum isothermal efficiency:**
  $$
  \eta_{iso,\,max} = \frac{n}{n-1} \cdot \frac{c \ln(1/c)}{1-c^2}
  $$
- **$\eta_v$ decreases** as $P_2/P_1$ increases — for $r_p > 6$, $\eta_v < 50\%$; for $r_p > 12$, multistage is mandatory.
- **Multistage intercooling** dramatically improves both $\eta_v$ and overall $\eta_{iso}$.
- **Optimum stage ratio** in multistage: $r = (P_{final}/P_{initial})^{1/N}$ where $N$ = number of stages.
- **Sanity check:** When $c = 0$, all clearance-related formulas collapse to no-clearance results.
- **Common exam trap:** Forgetting that $V_1 = V_s(1+c)$ is the **total geometric volume**, not the volume at start of compression.
- **Sign convention:** Work $W$ in formula is **positive** = work input to compressor.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rotary Compressors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIET303 - THERMAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Rotary Compressors # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rotary Compressor (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *rotary (or dynamic/positive-displacement rotary) compressor* is a mechanical device that compresses a working fluid (typically air or refrigerant gas) by means of a **rotating element** (impeller, rotor, lobe, or screw pair), wherein the compression process is achieved either by **continuous mass transfer under centrifugal acceleration** (dynamic type) or by **periodic trapping and squeezing of a fixed mass of fluid** between rotating and stationary members (positive-displacement rotary type). In the KTU 2024 Thermal Engineering syllabus (PCIET303, Module 3), rotary compressors are classified into two broad families: | Family | Compression Principle | Typical Examples | |---|---|---| | **Positive-Displacement Rotary** | Mechanical trapping + squeezing | Roots blower, Vane (sliding-vane) compressor, Screw (Lysholm) compressor, Liquid-piston compressor | | **Dynamic (Turbo) Rotary** | Centrifugal momentum conversion | Centrifugal compressor, Axial-flow compressor | > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The KTU module emphasises the constructional details, working principles, performance analysis, and comparison of **Roots blower**, **Vane-type compressor**, and **Screw (Lysholm) compressor** for Part-B derivations, and a comparative treatment of the **Centrifugal compressor** at the conceptual level. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Squeegee Wheel vs The Spinning Disc" Imagine two everyday machines: 1. **The Rotary Squeegee (Positive-Displacement)** — Picture a rotating **lobe pump** at a water-park ride. A pair of figure-eight-shaped lobes mesh together like two spinning gears. As they rotate, water is *trapped between the lobes and the casing*, physically carried from the suction side to the discharge side, and then *squeezed out* into a smaller volume. **The fluid is forced; the volume shrinks; pressure rises.** This is exactly how a **Roots blower** operates on air. 2. **The Spinning Disc (Dynamic)** — Picture a **table-fan blade** rotating at very high speed in a circular chamber. Air near the blade is *flung outward* by the centripetal/centrifugal action. The faster it spins, the higher the pressure it develops. The compression is a **momentum exchange** phenomenon, not a physical squeezing. This is how a **centrifugal compressor** works. > [!TIP] > **Key Distinction for Exams:** Reciprocating compressors compress in a *stop-and-go* cyclic manner using a piston, while rotary compressors compress in a *continuous stream* using rotation — making rotary machines inherently smoother, more compact, and capable of delivering **higher mass flow at moderate pressure ratios (4–10)**. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Isentropic index of air, γ = 1.4** *(use this unless otherwise specified)* - **Gas constant for air, R = 0.287 kJ/kg·K** - **Standard atmospheric pressure, P_atm = 101.325 kPa = 1.01325 bar** - **NTP conditions** for Free Air Delivery: **T₀ = 293 K (20 °C), P₀ = 101.325 kPa** - **Typical delivery pressure ratio for Roots blower: 1.2 – 2.0** - **Typical delivery pressure ratio for Screw compressor: 3 – 9** - **Typical delivery pressure ratio for Centrifugal compressor: 4 – 30+** --- ## 1.4 Why Study Rotary Compressors? (Engineering Utility) - **Industrial pneumatic systems** (sandblasting, paint spraying, tool actuation) - **Gas turbines** — centrifugal/axial compressors supply combustion air at pressure ratios of **10–40** - **HVAC and refrigeration** — screw compressors dominate large-tonnage chillers - **Process industries** — Roots blowers handle toxic/corrosive gases, sewage aeration, pneumatic conveying - **CNG dispensing stations, marine propulsion supercharging** > [!WARNING] > **Board-Exam Pitfall #1:** Many students confuse *Roots blower* (no internal compression) with *Screw compressor* (internal compression occurs in the chamber itself). Remember: in a Roots blower, the **compression happens in the discharge pipe**, not inside the casing. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification Overview ```text ROTARY COMPRESSORS │ ├── POSITIVE-DISPLACEMENT TYPE │ ├── Roots (Lobe) Blower → 2 lobes, no internal compression │ ├── Sliding Vane Compressor → eccentric rotor + radial vanes │ ├── Screw (Lysholm) Compressor → intermeshing male & female rotors │ └── Liquid-Piston Compressor → eccentric rotor in liquid ring │ └── DYNAMIC (TURBO) TYPE ├── Centrifugal (Radial-flow) → impeller + diffuser + volute └── Axial-flow Compressor → multi-stage bladed rotor/stator ``` --- ## 2.2 Roots (Lobe) Blower — Working Principle **Construction:** Two figure-eight-shaped rotors of identical profile, mounted on parallel shafts rotating in **opposite directions** at equal speeds. The shafts are synchronised by a pair of timing gears located outside the casing. The clearance between rotors and casing is very small (typically 0.1–0.3 mm). **Working Steps:** 1. **Suction stroke:** As the lobes unmesh on the suction side, a low-pressure region is created, and atmospheric air rushes in to fill the void. 2. **Conveyance:** The trapped mass of air is *carried* around the casing periphery without compression (volume remains constant; this is the *no-internal-compression* characteristic). 3. **Discharge stroke:** When the lobes re-mesh on the discharge side, the trapped air is *squeezed out* into the discharge pipe. **Final compression occurs in the discharge pipe** against back-pressure. > [!NOTE] > **Why no internal compression?** Because in a Roots blower the trapped air mass travels at constant volume along the casing periphery; only when it reaches the discharge port and meets the higher-pressure receiver does compression occur. **Swept Volume (per revolution, both rotors combined):** The displacement per revolution is equal to **four times the volume swept by one lobe per rotation**. --- ## 2.3 Sliding Vane (Vane-Type) Compressor — Working Principle **Construction:** An **eccentric rotor** is mounted inside a cylindrical stator. The rotor carries **6–20 sliding vanes** that ride in radial slots. Centrifugal force and oil (in oil-flooded designs) push the vanes outward so their tips maintain sliding contact with the stator bore. **Working Steps:** 1. As the rotor turns, the space between successive vanes *increases* on the suction side → air is drawn in. 2. The trapped air mass moves around toward the discharge port. 3. On approaching the discharge port, the eccentricity *decreases* the vane-to-stator space → **internal compression** occurs. 4. The compressed air is pushed out through the discharge port. > [!NOTE] > **Key Feature:** Unlike Roots blower, sliding-vane compressors **do perform internal compression** within the chamber itself. --- ## 2.4 Screw (Lysholm) Compressor — Working Principle **Construction:** Two intermeshing helical rotors — one **male rotor** (typically 4 lobes) and one **female rotor** (typically 6 flutes) — housed inside a stator casing with very tight clearances. Timing gears ensure synchronous meshing. **Working Steps:** 1. As rotors unmesh on the suction side, a low-pressure void forms and gas is drawn in along the rotor axis. 2. As the rotors continue to rotate, the gas is *trapped* between the male-lobe and female-flute and **carried axially** toward the discharge end. 3. As the male lobe begins to re-engage the female flute, the trapped volume progressively **shrinks**, producing **continuous internal compression** with built-in pressure ratio (typically 3–9). 4. Compressed gas is discharged at the opposite axial end. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why "oil-flooded" in practice?** Oil injected into the chamber provides sealing, lubrication, and cooling, dramatically improving volumetric efficiency (up to 95 %) and enabling pressure ratios of 9–13 in industrial units. --- ## 2.5 Centrifugal Compressor — Working Principle **Construction:** A high-speed **impeller** (5000–30000 rpm) accelerates the gas radially outward. The gas then passes through a **diffuser** (where kinetic energy converts to pressure) and is finally collected in a **volute** (scroll casing). **Working Principle — Three Energy Conversions:** 1. **Mechanical energy → Kinetic energy** (in impeller) 2. **Kinetic energy → Pressure energy** (in diffuser) 3. **Pressure equalisation** (in volute) The **Euler turbomachinery equation** governs energy transfer: $$ h_{impeller} = U_2 C_{\theta 2} - U_1 C_{\theta 1} $$ For a radial-vane impeller where $C_{\theta 1} = 0$ (no pre-swirl): $$ h_{impeller} = U_2 \, C_{\theta 2} $$ --- ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **All symbols are per KTU textbook convention.** Use $\vert$ for absolute value / magnitude in derivations to avoid breaking markdown tables. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Swept volume of Roots blower per rev (both rotors)** | $V_s = 4 \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} \,(D^2 - d^2)\,L$ | $m^3 / rev$ ; $D$ = casing bore, $d$ = lobe (rotor) diameter, $L$ = axial length | | 2 | **Displacement per second** | $\dot{V}_d = V_s \cdot N$ | $m^3 / s$ ; $N$ = rotor speed in rps | | 3 | **Volume of free air actually delivered (FAD)** | $\dot{V}_{FAD} = \eta_{vol} \cdot \dot{V}_d$ | $m^3 / s$ | | 4 | **Volumetric efficiency** | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{\text{Actual free air delivered}}{\text{Displacement volume}}$ | dimensionless | | 5 | **Isothermal work per kg of air** | $w_{iso} = R T_1 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | kJ/kg | | 6 | **Isothermal power required** | $P_{iso} = \dot{m} \cdot w_{iso} = \dfrac{P_1 \dot{V}_1}{1000}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | kW | | 7 | **Adiabatic (isentropic) work per kg** | $w_{ad} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} R T_1 \!\left[\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!\tfrac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]$ | kJ/kg | | 8 | **Adiabatic power** | $P_{ad} = \dfrac{P_1 \dot{V}_1}{1000} \cdot \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}\!\left[\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!\tfrac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]$ | kW | | 9 | **Slip factor** | $\phi = 1 - \dfrac{C}{s}\!\left(\dfrac{Z}{\pi}\right)^{\!0.5}$ | Stodola's empirical relation ; $C$ = clearance, $s$ = rotor thickness, $Z$ = number of lobes | | 10 | **Actual delivered volume (Roots blower)** | $\dot{V}_a = \phi \cdot \dot{V}_d$ | $m^3 / s$ ; accounts for back-leakage | | 11 | **Work done per cycle (Roots)** | $W = \dfrac{P_1 \dot{V}_a}{1000}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | kW | | 12 | **Euler head (centrifugal)** | $h = U_2 C_{\theta 2} - U_1 C_{\theta 1}$ | J/kg ; $\to$ kJ/kg when divided by 1000 | | 13 | **Isentropic head for compression** | $h_{isen} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} R T_1 \!\left[\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!\tfrac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]$ | kJ/kg | | 14 | **Isentropic efficiency of compressor** | $\eta_{isen} = \dfrac{h_{isen}}{h_{actual}}$ | dimensionless | | 15 | **Pressure ratio (overall)** | $r_p = P_2 / P_1$ | dimensionless | | 16 | **Mass flow rate** | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{P_1 \dot{V}_a}{R T_1}$ | kg/s | --- ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry Application | Preferred Rotary Compressor | Reason | |---|---|---| | **Pneumatic tool operation (workshop)** | Reciprocating (small) or Screw | High pressure, intermittent load | | **Sewage treatment aeration** | Roots blower | High volume, low pressure (≈ 0.5 bar) | | **Gas turbine air supply** | Centrifugal / Axial | High pressure ratio, continuous flow | | **Industrial refrigeration (>50 TR)** | Screw (oil-flooded) | High COP at part load, capacity modulation | | **CNG refuelling stations** | Multi-stage reciprocating or Screw | Very high pressure (200–250 bar) | | **Diesel engine supercharging** | Centrifugal | Compact, high flow at moderate pressure | > [!TIP] > **Board Tip:** When asked to *select* a rotary compressor, link the **pressure ratio** and **flow rate** requirement directly to the type. Roots = low pressure + high flow; Screw = medium pressure + medium flow; Centrifugal = high pressure + very high flow. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Swept Volume of a Roots (Lobe) Blower **Given:** Two figure-eight rotors inside a cylindrical casing of bore $D$, each rotor of diameter $d$, axial length $L$, rotating at $N$ revolutions per second. **Statement to prove / quantify:** The volume of air trapped and discharged per revolution, considering the geometric profile. ### Step 1 — Understand the geometry When two identical figure-eight rotors mesh, each rotor sweeps a region inside the casing. Because the rotors are congruent and *symmetrically placed*, the air trapped by rotor 1 (between rotor 1 and casing top) is matched by an equal volume trapped by rotor 2 on the opposite side. In one full revolution, the total volume of fluid conveyed equals the sum of these four swept regions (two per rotor). ### Step 2 — Volume swept by ONE rotor per revolution Each rotor, in one revolution, sweeps the entire annular region between the rotor circumference ($d$) and the casing bore ($D$), over the axial length $L$. $$ V_{\text{one rotor}} = \frac{\pi}{4}\,\bigl(D^{2} - d^{2}\bigr)\,L \quad [m^3] $$ > *This is the volume of the cylindrical "shell" that the rotor occupies inside the casing per revolution.* ### Step 3 — Account for both rotors (factor of 4) Since there are **two rotors**, each sweeping this annular volume, and each rotor carries the air on **both sides** (i.e., twice per revolution) due to the figure-eight profile: $$ \boxed{\,V_s = 4 \cdot \frac{\pi}{4}\,\bigl(D^{2} - d^{2}\bigr)\,L = \pi\,(D^{2} - d^{2})\,L \quad [m^3/rev]\,} $$ ### Step 4 — Volumetric displacement rate $$ \dot{V}_d = V_s \cdot N = \pi\,(D^{2} - d^{2})\,L\,N \quad [m^3/s] $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Isothermal Work Done in a Roots Blower **Assumption (KTU standard):** Air behaves as an ideal gas; compression at delivery is isothermal (Roots blowers run *cooled* and *slow*, so isothermal compression is a fair engineering approximation for a Roots blower — KTU explicitly asks this). ### Step 1 — Work done per unit mass For reversible isothermal compression of an ideal gas from $P_1$ to $P_2$: $$ w_{iso} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV $$ For an ideal gas: $P V = R T_1 = $ constant along an isotherm. So $P = \dfrac{R T_1}{V}$, hence: $$ w_{iso} = R T_1 \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{dV}{V} = R T_1 \ln\!\left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right) $$ Since $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2 \Rightarrow \dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1}$, we get: $$ \boxed{\,w_{iso} = R T_1 \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) \quad [\text{kJ/kg}]\,} $$ ### Step 2 — Mass flow rate The mass of free air handled per second: $$ \dot{m} = \frac{P_1 \dot{V}_a}{R T_1} \quad [\text{kg/s}] $$ where $\dot{V}_a$ is the *actual delivered* volume of free air per second. ### Step 3 — Total isothermal power $$ P_{iso} = \dot{m} \cdot w_{iso} = \frac{P_1 \dot{V}_a}{R T_1} \cdot R T_1 \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) $$ $$ \boxed{\,P_{iso} = P_1 \dot{V}_a \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) \quad [\text{W}] = \frac{P_1 \dot{V}_a}{1000}\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) \quad [\text{kW}]\,} $$ > Here $P_1$ must be in **Pascals** when $\dot{V}_a$ is in m³/s, or in **kPa** when the division by 1000 is applied to obtain kW. ### Step 4 — Numerical worked illustration **Problem:** A Roots blower has casing diameter $D = 0.30$ m, rotor diameter $d = 0.20$ m, length $L = 0.25$ m, runs at $N = 600$ rpm, delivers free air at $P_2 = 1.8$ bar (absolute). Suction conditions: $P_1 = 1.013$ bar, $T_1 = 293$ K. The slip factor $\phi = 0.92$. Calculate (a) the swept volume per revolution, (b) the actual free air delivered per second, (c) the isothermal power required. **Solution:** **(a)** $$ V_s = \pi\,(D^{2} - d^{2})\,L = \pi \cdot (0.30^{2} - 0.20^{2}) \cdot 0.25 $$ $$ V_s = \pi \cdot (0.09 - 0.04) \cdot 0.25 = \pi \cdot 0.0125 = 0.03927 \; m^3/rev $$ **(b)** Convert speed to rps: $N = 600 / 60 = 10 \; rps$ $$ \dot{V}_d = V_s \cdot N = 0.03927 \times 10 = 0.3927 \; m^3/s $$ $$ \dot{V}_a = \phi \cdot \dot{V}_d = 0.92 \times 0.3927 = 0.3613 \; m^3/s $$ **(c)** Use $P_1 = 101.3$ kPa: $$ P_{iso} = \frac{P_1 \dot{V}_a}{1000}\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \frac{101.3 \times 0.3613}{1000}\ln\!\left(\frac{180}{101.3}\right) $$ $$ P_{iso} = 0.0366 \times \ln(1.777) = 0.0366 \times 0.5754 = 0.02107 \; kW \approx 21.07 \; W $$ > [!WARNING] > **Numerical-Pitfall Alert:** Forgetting to convert $\dot{V}_a$ from the slip-factor adjusted value leads to ~8 % error. Always apply slip factor *before* power calculation. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Stodola's Slip Factor for Centrifugal Compressor ### Step 1 — Physical motivation In a centrifugal impeller, the gas does not perfectly follow the blade passage. It *slips* backward relative to the impeller, reducing the tangential velocity component $C_{\theta 2}$ from the ideal value $U_2$ down to a fraction $\phi \cdot U_2$. ### Step 2 — Stodola's empirical relation $$ \boxed{\,\phi = 1 - \frac{C}{s}\left(\frac{Z}{\pi}\right)^{\!0.5}\,} $$ where: - $C$ = blade-tip clearance (m) - $s$ = blade thickness at tip (m) - $Z$ = number of impeller blades ### Step 3 — Relation to Euler work With slip, the actual Euler head becomes: $$ h_{actual} = U_2^2 \, \phi \quad [\text{kJ/kg}] \;( \text{when } C_{\theta 1} = 0 \text{ and } U_2 = C_{\theta 2,ideal}) $$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Formula | Scenario | Compression Type Assumption | Formula to Use | Why | |---|---|---|---| | Roots blower, low speed, water-cooled casing | **Isothermal** | $P_{iso} = \dfrac{P_1 \dot{V}_a}{1000}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | Slow, cooled → quasi-isothermal | | Vane / Screw compressor, moderate speed | **Adiabatic (isentropic)** | $P_{ad} = \dfrac{P_1 \dot{V}_a}{1000}\dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}\!\left[\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!\tfrac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]$ | Faster, less cooling | | Centrifugal compressor | **Euler head with slip** | $h = \phi U_2^{2}/1000$ | Dynamic momentum conversion | | Polytropic process (general case) | **Polytropic** | Replace $\gamma$ with $n$ in adiabatic formula | $1 < n < \gamma$ for compression with cooling | --- ## 3.5 Comparison Matrix — Rotary vs Reciprocating | Parameter | Reciprocating Compressor | Rotary Compressor | |---|---|---| | Compression principle | Cyclic (piston-cylinder) | Continuous (rotating element) | | Pulsation in delivery | Yes | Negligible | | Max practical pressure ratio | Up to **40** (multi-stage) | Roots: 2; Screw: 9; Centrifugal: 30+ | | Volumetric efficiency | 60–85 % | 85–95 % | | Mechanical balance | Poor (inertia forces) | Excellent (rotating balance) | | Suitability for dirty gas | Poor (piston rings wear) | Excellent (Roots) | | Maintenance | Higher | Lower (fewer parts) | | Cost for given flow | Higher | Lower for high flow | | Starting torque | High | Lower (centrifugal) | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Rotary Compressor Family ```mermaid flowchart TB A[FREE AIR INLET
