<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Ideal Cycles

The four primary air standard cycles studied under Module 3 of PCIET303 are:

| Cycle Name | Engine Type | Heat Addition | Heat Rejection |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Otto Cycle** | Petrol/SI Engine | Constant Volume | Constant Volume |
| **Diesel Cycle** | Diesel/CI Engine | Constant Pressure | Constant Volume |
| **Dual Cycle** | Modern CI/SI Engine | Constant Volume + Constant Pressure | Constant Volume |
| **Brayton Cycle** | Gas Turbine | Constant Pressure | Constant Pressure |

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Process Logic

### 2.2.1 Otto Cycle (Constant Volume Cycle)
- **Process 1→2:** Isentropic (reversible adiabatic) compression.
- **Process 2→3:** Heat addition at **constant volume** (representing the spark ignition combustion at TDC).
- **Process 3→4:** Isentropic (reversible adiabatic) expansion (the power stroke).
- **Process 4→1:** Heat rejection at **constant volume** (representing the exhaust blowdown).

### 2.2.2 Diesel Cycle (Constant Pressure Cycle)
- **Process 1→2:** Isentropic compression.
- **Process 2→3:** Heat addition at **constant pressure** (representing fuel injection and combustion as the piston moves down).
- **Process 3→4:** Isentropic expansion.
- **Process 4→1:** Heat rejection at **constant volume**.

### 2.2.3 Dual Cycle (Limited Pressure Cycle / Sabathe Cycle)
- **Process 1→2:** Isentropic compression.
- **Process 2→3:** Heat addition at **constant volume** (initial rapid combustion).
- **Process 3→4:** Heat addition at **constant pressure** (continued combustion as piston moves).
- **Process 4→5:** Isentropic expansion.
- **Process 5→1:** Heat rejection at **constant volume**.

### 2.2.4 Brayton Cycle (Gas Turbine Cycle)
- **Process 1→2:** Isentropic compression in the compressor.
- **Process 2→3:** Heat addition at **constant pressure** in the combustion chamber.
- **Process 3→4:** Isentropic expansion in the turbine.
- **Process 4→1:** Heat rejection at **constant pressure**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols Used:** $r$ = Compression ratio, $\rho$ = Cut-off ratio, $r_p$ = Pressure ratio, $\gamma = 1.4$, $Q_{in}$ = Heat supplied, $Q_{out}$ = Heat rejected, $\eta_{th}$ = Thermal efficiency, $W_{net}$ = Net work output.

### Otto Cycle Formulas

| Parameter | Formula | Units | Boundary Condition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Compression Ratio | $r = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{V_4}{V_3}$ | dimensionless | $r > 1$ |
| Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th,\,Otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$ | fraction or % | $0 < \eta < 1$ |
| Heat Supplied | $Q_{in} = m \, c_v \, (T_3 - T_2)$ | kJ | $T_3 > T_2$ |
| Heat Rejected | $Q_{out} = m \, c_v \, (T_4 - T_1)$ | kJ | $T_4 > T_1$ |
| Net Work | $W_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out}$ | kJ | Always positive |
| Mean Effective Pressure | $\text{MEP} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{V_1 - V_2}$ | kPa or bar | — |

### Diesel Cycle Formulas

| Parameter | Formula | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Compression Ratio | $r = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2}$ | dimensionless |
| Cut-off Ratio | $\rho = \dfrac{V_4}{V_3} = \dfrac{T_4}{T_3}$ | dimensionless |
| Expansion Ratio | $r_e = \dfrac{V_1}{V_4} = \dfrac{r}{\rho}$ | dimensionless |
| Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th,\,Diesel} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \left[ \dfrac{\rho^\gamma - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)} \right]$ | fraction or % |
| Heat Supplied | $Q_{in} = m \, c_p \, (T_4 - T_3)$ | kJ |
| Heat Rejected | $Q_{out} = m \, c_v \, (T_1 - T_5)$ | kJ |

### Dual Cycle Formulas

| Parameter | Formula | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Compression Ratio | $r = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2}$ | dimensionless |
| Cut-off Ratio | $\rho = \dfrac{V_4}{V_3}$ | dimensionless |
| Pressure Ratio | $r_p = \dfrac{P_3}{P_2}$ | dimensionless |
| Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th,\,Dual} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{r_p \, \rho^\gamma - 1}{(r_p - 1) + \gamma \, r_p (\rho - 1)}$ | fraction or % |
| Heat Supplied | $Q_{in} = m \, [c_v(T_3 - T_2) + c_p(T_4 - T_3)]$ | kJ |

### Brayton Cycle Formulas

| Parameter | Formula | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pressure Ratio | $r_p = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{P_3}{P_4}$ | dimensionless |
| Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th,\,Brayton} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$ | fraction or % |
| Compressor Work | $W_C = m \, c_p \, (T_2 - T_1)$ | kJ |
| Turbine Work | $W_T = m \, c_p \, (T_3 - T_4)$ | kJ |
| Net Work | $W_{net} = W_T - W_C$ | kJ |
| Back Work Ratio | $\text{BWR} = \dfrac{W_C}{W_T}$ | fraction |

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KTU focuses on these cycles:** In practical engine design, engineers use ideal cycle analysis to:
> - **Predict maximum theoretical efficiency** without building expensive prototypes.
> - **Compare competing engine designs** on a level playing field (e.g., comparing SI vs. CI engines).
> - **Identify sources of deviation** in real engines (e.g., mechanical friction, heat losses, incomplete combustion).
> - **Optimize compression ratio, cut-off ratio, and pressure ratio** for specific applications (automotive, aerospace, power generation).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Otto Cycle Thermal Efficiency

**Given:** A fixed mass $m$ of air undergoes the Otto cycle. We want to derive an expression for thermal efficiency in terms of compression ratio $r$.

### Step 1: Define Thermal Efficiency

Thermal efficiency is the ratio of net work output to heat supplied:

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}
$$

**Logic:** This is the universal definition of efficiency for any heat engine operating between a hot source and a cold sink, derived directly from the First Law of Thermodynamics applied to a cyclic process ($\Delta U = 0$, so $Q_{net} = W_{net}$).

### Step 2: Express Heat Supplied (Process 2→3, Constant Volume)

During the constant volume heat addition process, no boundary work is done, so all heat goes into raising the internal energy:

$$
Q_{in} = m \, c_v \, (T_3 - T_2)
$$

**Logic:** For a constant volume process, $W = 0$ and $\Delta U = Q$. The change in internal energy of a perfect gas is $m \, c_v \, \Delta T$.

### Step 3: Express Heat Rejected (Process 4→1, Constant Volume)

Similarly, during constant volume heat rejection:

$$
Q_{out} = m \, c_v \, (T_4 - T_1)
$$

**Logic:** Heat flows out of the system, so $Q_{out}$ is taken as positive by convention (magnitude of heat leaving).

### Step 4: Substitute into Efficiency Equation

$$
\eta_{th,\,Otto} = 1 - \frac{m \, c_v \, (T_4 - T_1)}{m \, c_v \, (T_3 - T_2)}
$$

The terms $m$ and $c_v$ cancel out:

$$
\eta_{th,\,Otto} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}
$$

### Step 5: Apply Isentropic Relations

For the isentropic compression process (1→2):

$$
\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma-1} = r^{\gamma-1}
$$

$$
T_2 = T_1 \, r^{\gamma-1}
$$

For the isentropic expansion process (3→4):

$$
\frac{T_3}{T_4} = \left(\frac{V_4}{V_3}\right)^{\gamma-1} = r^{\gamma-1}
$$

$$
T_3 = T_4 \, r^{\gamma-1}
$$

**Logic:** The relation $TV^{\gamma-1} = \text{constant}$ applies to reversible adiabatic (isentropic) processes of a perfect gas, where $r = V_1/V_2 = V_4/V_3$.

### Step 6: Substitute Isentropic Relations

$$
\eta_{th,\,Otto} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $r^{\gamma-1}$:

$$
\eta_{th,\,Otto} = 1 - \frac{r^{\gamma-1} T_4 - r^{\gamma-1} T_1}{r^{\gamma-1} T_3 - r^{\gamma-1} T_2}
$$

Applying the relations $r^{\gamma-1} T_4 = T_3$ and $r^{\gamma-1} T_1 = T_2$:

$$
\eta_{th,\,Otto} = 1 - \frac{T_3 - T_2}{r^{\gamma-1} T_3 - r^{\gamma-1} T_2} = 1 - \frac{T_3 - T_2}{r^{\gamma-1}(T_3 - T_2)}
$$

### Step 7: Final Simplification

The terms $(T_3 - T_2)$ cancel:

$$
\boxed{\eta_{th,\,Otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Otto Cycle)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** An engine working on the Otto cycle has a compression ratio of 8. At the beginning of compression, the air is at 100 kPa and 27°C. The peak temperature during the cycle is 2000 K. Calculate: (a) Thermal efficiency, (b) Heat supplied per kg of air, (c) Work done per kg of air.

**Given Data:**
- $r = 8$
- $P_1 = 100$ kPa
- $T_1 = 27 + 273 = 300$ K
- $T_3 = 2000$ K
- $\gamma = 1.4$, $c_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K

### Part (a): Thermal Efficiency

$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} = 1 - \frac{1}{8^{0.4}}
$$

Calculate $8^{0.4}$:

$$
8^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \ln 8} = e^{0.4 \times 2.0794} = e^{0.8318} = 2.297
$$

Therefore:

$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.297} = 1 - 0.4354 = 0.5646 = 56.46\%
$$

### Part (b): Heat Supplied

First, find $T_2$ using isentropic compression:

$$
T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} = 300 \times 2.297 = 689.1 \text{ K}
$$

Now compute $Q_{in}$:

$$
Q_{in} = c_v (T_3 - T_2) = 0.718 \times (2000 - 689.1) = 0.718 \times 1310.9
$$

$$
Q_{in} = 941.23 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

### Part (c): Net Work Done

$$
W_{net} = \eta_{th} \times Q_{in} = 0.5646 \times 941.23 = 531.42 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

## 3.3 Comparison of Efficiencies (Analytical Matrix)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** For the same compression ratio $r$, the efficiency ordering is: $\eta_{Otto} > \eta_{Dual} > \eta_{Diesel}$. This is a frequently asked 3-mark question.

The proof uses the fact that heat addition at constant volume is more efficient than at constant pressure because less heat is rejected at the end of the cycle (the cycle reaches a higher temperature ratio more effectively).

**Mathematical Proof Sketch:**

For Otto cycle with cut-off starting at point 2:
- All heat added at constant volume → Highest peak efficiency.

For Diesel cycle with cut-off ratio $\rho > 1$:
- Heat is added during expansion → Lower peak temperature for same heat input → Lower efficiency.

For Dual cycle (combination):
- Efficiency falls between Otto and Diesel.

## 3.4 Derivation of Brayton Cycle Efficiency

The Brayton cycle follows the same logic as Otto, but uses pressure ratio $r_p$ instead of compression ratio.

**Step 1:** For isentropic compression (1→2):
$$
\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}
$$

**Step 2:** For isentropic expansion (3→4):
$$
\frac{T_3}{T_4} = \left(\frac{P_3}{P_4}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}
$$

**Step 3:** Heat supplied (constant pressure): $Q_{in} = c_p (T_3 - T_2)$
**Step 4:** Heat rejected (constant pressure): $Q_{out} = c_p (T_4 - T_1)$

**Step 5:** Substituting and simplifying:

$$
\boxed{\eta_{th,\,Brayton} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}}
$$

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Otto Cycle — P-V and T-S Diagrams

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PV["Otto Cycle P-V Diagram"]
        direction TB
        pointA[State 1: BDC Start] -->|Isentropic Compression| pointB[State 2: TDC After Compression]
        pointB -->|Constant Volume Heat Addition| pointC[State 3: Peak T at TDC]
        pointC -->|Isentropic Expansion| pointD[State 4: BDC After Expansion]
        pointD -->|Constant Volume Heat Rejection| pointA
    end
    
    subgraph TS["Otto Cycle T-S Diagram"]
        direction TB
        sA[State 1 Low T] -->|Isentropic Compression| sB[State 2 High T]
        sB -->|Constant Volume Heat Addition| sC[State 3 Highest T]
        sC -->|Isentropic Expansion| sD[State 4 Mid T]
        sD -->|Constant Volume Heat Rejection| sA
    end
```

**Diagram Description:** In the **P-V diagram**, the Otto cycle appears as a closed loop where:
- The **left curve** (1→2) represents adiabatic compression — pressure rises steeply as volume decreases.
- The **top horizontal line** (2→3) represents constant volume heat addition — pressure rises at minimum volume.
- The **right curve** (3→4) represents adiabatic expansion — pressure drops as volume increases.
- The **bottom horizontal line** (4→1) represents constant volume heat rejection — pressure drops at maximum volume.

In the **T-S diagram**, the cycle appears as a "rounded rectangle" with two curved (isentropic) sides and two vertical (constant volume) sides.

## 4.2 Diesel Cycle — P-V Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    dA[State 1 Vmax Pmin] -->|Isentropic Compression| dB[State 2 Vmin HighP]
    dB -->|Constant Pressure Heat Addition| dC[State 3 V3 Greater Than Vmin P Same As State 2]
    dC -->|Isentropic Expansion| dD[State 4 Vmax Low P]
    dD -->|Constant Volume Heat Rejection| dE[State 1 Vmax Pmin]
```

**Diagram Description:** The Diesel cycle's **P-V diagram** is distinguished by the **sloped top line** (2→3) representing constant pressure heat addition (fuel is injected and burns as the piston moves down). The cut-off ratio $\rho = V_4/V_3$ defines how far the piston travels during heat addition.

## 4.3 Dual Cycle — P-V Diagram (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    duA[State 1 Begin Compression] -->|Isentropic Process 1 to 2| duB[State 2 End Compression]
    duB -->|Constant Volume Heat Addition 2 to 3| duC[State 3 Start Constant Pressure Process]
    duC -->|Constant Pressure Heat Addition 3 to 4| duD[State 4 End Heat Addition]
    duD -->|Isentropic Expansion 4 to 5| duE[State 5 Begin Heat Rejection]
    duE -->|Constant Volume Heat Rejection 5 to 1| duA
```

**Diagram Description:** The Dual cycle combines features of both Otto and Diesel cycles. Heat addition occurs in **two stages** — first at constant volume (representing rapid initial combustion), then at constant pressure (representing continued controlled combustion as the piston descends).

## 4.4 Brayton Cycle — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GasTurbine["Brayton Cycle Open System Architecture"]
        direction LR
        inlet[Air Inlet Ambient Conditions] --> comp[Compressor Isentropic Compression]
        comp -->|High Pressure Air| chamber[Combustion Chamber Heat Addition At Constant Pressure]
        chamber -->|High T High P Gas| turbine[Turbine Isentropic Expansion]
        turbine --> exhaust[Exhaust Gases To Atmosphere]
    end
```

**Diagram Description:** Unlike the reciprocating engine cycles (Otto, Diesel, Dual) which operate on a **closed cycle** with a fixed mass of air, the Brayton cycle is typically analyzed as a **closed ideal cycle** but operates as an **open cycle** in real gas turbines. The working fluid enters the compressor at ambient conditions, is compressed, heated in the combustion chamber, expanded through the turbine, and finally exhausted.

## 4.5 Comparison Schematic — All Four Cycles on T-S Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TSComparison["Ideal Cycles on T-S Plane"]
        direction TB
        ottoT[Otto Cycle: Two Vertical Sides Two Curved Sides]
        dieselT[Diesel Cycle: Curved Left One Vertical Right One Sloped]
        dualT[Dual Cycle: Mixed Geometry Combines Otto And Diesel]
        braytonT[Brayton Cycle: Two Curved Sides Two Horizontal Sides]
    end
```

**Diagram Description:** The T-S diagram makes it visually intuitive why **Otto > Dual > Diesel** in efficiency for the same compression ratio — the area enclosed (representing net work) shrinks as more heat is added at constant pressure instead of constant volume for the same peak temperature.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define an air standard cycle. List any four assumptions made in the air standard cycle analysis.**

**Model Answer:**

An **air standard cycle** is an idealized thermodynamic cycle in which a fixed mass of air (treated as a perfect gas) undergoes a series of reversible processes to convert heat into work. It is used as a theoretical model to analyze the performance of internal combustion engines and gas turbines.

**Four Key Assumptions:**
1. The working fluid is air, which behaves as a perfect gas throughout the cycle.
2. Specific heats $c_p$ and $c_v$ remain constant during the cycle.
3. The combustion process is replaced by heat addition from an external source, and exhaust is replaced by heat rejection to an external sink.
4. All processes are internally reversible with no heat loss to the cylinder walls.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2023]
**State the expression for the thermal efficiency of the Otto cycle. How does the efficiency vary with compression ratio?**

**Model Answer:**

The thermal efficiency of the Otto cycle is given by:

$$
\eta_{th,\,Otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}
$$

where $r$ is the compression ratio and $\gamma$ is the ratio of specific heats.

**Variation with Compression Ratio:**
- As the compression ratio $r$ **increases**, the term $r^{\gamma-1}$ increases, making $1/r^{\gamma-1}$ smaller.
- Therefore, thermal efficiency **increases** with compression ratio.
- However, in practical engines, compression ratio is limited by **knocking** (in SI engines) and **mechanical stresses** on engine components.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

**Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the air standard efficiency of the **Otto cycle** in terms of compression ratio. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An engine working on the Otto cycle has a compression ratio of 9:1. The pressure and temperature at the beginning of compression are 1 bar and 30°C respectively. The maximum temperature during the cycle is 1500°C. Calculate: **(7 Marks)**
- (i) The pressure and temperature at all salient points.
- (ii) The thermal efficiency of the cycle.
- (iii) The heat supplied per kg of air.
- (iv) The work done per kg of air per cycle.

**Given:** $\gamma = 1.4$, $c_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K.

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Define thermal efficiency:
$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}
$$

**Step 2:** Heat supplied at constant volume (2→3):
$$
Q_{in} = m \, c_v (T_3 - T_2)
$$

**Step 3:** Heat rejected at constant volume (4→1):
$$
Q_{out} = m \, c_v (T_4 - T_1)
$$

**Step 4:** Substituting:
$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}
$$

**Step 5:** Apply isentropic relations for processes 1→2 and 3→4 with $r = V_1/V_2 = V_4/V_3$:
$$
T_2 = T_1 \, r^{\gamma-1} \quad \text{and} \quad T_3 = T_4 \, r^{\gamma-1}
$$

**Step 6:** Algebraic simplification yields:
$$
\boxed{\eta_{th,\,Otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}}
$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $r = 9$, $P_1 = 1$ bar $= 100$ kPa, $T_1 = 30 + 273 = 303$ K
- $T_3 = 1500 + 273 = 1773$ K
- $\gamma = 1.4$, $c_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K

**Calculate $r^{\gamma-1}$:**
$$
r^{\gamma-1} = 9^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \ln 9} = e^{0.4 \times 2.1972} = e^{0.8789} = 2.408
$$

**(i) State Point Calculations:**

**State 2 (End of Compression):**
$$
T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} = 303 \times 2.408 = 729.6 \text{ K}
$$

Using isentropic relation $P_1 V_1^\gamma = P_2 V_2^\gamma$ with $V_1/V_2 = r$:
$$
P_2 = P_1 \cdot r^\gamma = 100 \times 9^{1.4} = 100 \times 21.67 = 2167 \text{ kPa}
$$

**State 3 (End of Heat Addition):**
- $T_3 = 1773$ K (given)
- $V_3 = V_2$ (constant volume process), so:
$$
P_3 = P_2 \times \frac{T_3}{T_2} = 2167 \times \frac{1773}{729.6} = 2167 \times 2.43 = 5266 \text{ kPa}
$$

**State 4 (End of Expansion):**
$$
T_4 = \frac{T_3}{r^{\gamma-1}} = \frac{1773}{2.408} = 736.4 \text{ K}
$$

$$
P_4 = \frac{P_3}{r^\gamma} = \frac{5266}{21.67} = 243.0 \text{ kPa}
$$

**(ii) Thermal Efficiency:**
$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.408} = 1 - 0.4153 = 0.5847 = 58.47\%
$$

**(iii) Heat Supplied per kg:**
$$
Q_{in} = c_v (T_3 - T_2) = 0.718 \times (1773 - 729.6) = 0.718 \times 1043.4 = 749.16 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**(iv) Net Work Done per kg:**
$$
W_{net} = \eta_{th} \times Q_{in} = 0.5847 \times 749.16 = 438.05 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

### **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

**Question B (14 Marks):**

**(a)** With the help of a P-V and T-S diagram, explain the **Diesel cycle**. Derive the expression for its air standard efficiency. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Diesel engine has a compression ratio of 16:1 and cut-off takes place at 6% of the swept volume. Calculate the air standard efficiency. **(7 Marks)**

**Given:** $\gamma = 1.4$ for air.

---

### Model Solution for Question B:

#### Part (a) — Diesel Cycle Explanation (7 Marks)

**Diagrams:** Refer to Section 4.2 for the P-V diagram.

**Processes of Diesel Cycle:**
1. **Process 1→2:** Isentropic compression of air from bottom dead center (BDC) to top dead center (TDC).
2. **Process 2→3:** Heat addition at **constant pressure** (representing fuel injection and combustion as the piston moves downward from TDC).
3. **Process 3→4:** Isentropic expansion (the power stroke).
4. **Process 4→1:** Heat rejection at **constant volume** (representing exhaust).

**Derivation of Efficiency:**

**Step 1:** Thermal efficiency formula:
$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}
$$

**Step 2:** Heat supplied at constant pressure (2→3):
$$
Q_{in} = m \, c_p (T_3 - T_2)
$$

**Step 3:** Heat rejected at constant volume (4→1):
$$
Q_{out} = m \, c_v (T_4 - T_1)
$$

**Step 4:** Express temperatures using isentropic and constant pressure relations:
$$
\frac{T_2}{T_1} = r^{\gamma-1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_2 = T_1 \, r^{\gamma-1}
$$

$$
\frac{T_3}{T_2} = \rho \quad \text{(constant pressure, where } \rho = V_3/V_2 = \text{cut-off ratio)}
$$

$$
\frac{T_3}{T_4} = \left(\frac{V_4}{V_3}\right)^{\gamma-1} = \left(\frac{r}{\rho}\right)^{\gamma-1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_4 = \frac{T_3}{(r/\rho)^{\gamma-1}}
$$

**Step 5:** Substituting and simplifying through algebraic manipulation:

$$
\boxed{\eta_{th,\,Diesel} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \left[ \frac{\rho^\gamma - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)} \right]}
$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Compression ratio $r = 16$
- Cut-off occurs at 6% of swept volume.

**Step 1: Determine Cut-off Ratio $\rho$**

The swept volume is $V_1 - V_2$. The cut-off volume is $V_3 - V_2 = 0.06(V_1 - V_2)$.

Cut-off ratio:
$$
\rho = \frac{V_3}{V_2} = 1 + \frac{V_3 - V_2}{V_2} = 1 + 0.06 \times \frac{V_1 - V_2}{V_2}
$$

Since $V_1/V_2 = r = 16$, we have $V_2 = V_1/16$, so:

$$
\frac{V_1 - V_2}{V_2} = \frac{V_1}{V_2} - 1 = 16 - 1 = 15
$$

Therefore:
$$
\rho = 1 + 0.06 \times 15 = 1 + 0.90 = 1.90
$$

**Step 2: Calculate $r^{\gamma-1}$**

$$
r^{\gamma-1} = 16^{0.4}
$$

Using $16^{0.4} = (2^4)^{0.4} = 2^{1.6} = 3.031$

**Step 3: Calculate $\rho^\gamma$**

$$
\rho^\gamma = 1.90^{1.4}
$$

Using logarithms: $\ln(1.90) = 0.6419$, so $\rho^\gamma = e^{1.4 \times 0.6419} = e^{0.8987} = 2.456$

**Step 4: Apply the Diesel Efficiency Formula**

$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{3.031} \times \frac{2.456 - 1}{1.4 \times (1.90 - 1)}
$$

$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - 0.3299 \times \frac{1.456}{1.4 \times 0.90}
$$

$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - 0.3299 \times \frac{1.456}{1.26}
$$

$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - 0.3299 \times 1.1556
$$

$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - 0.3812 = 0.6188 = 61.88\%
$$

**Verification:** Comparing this with an Otto cycle of the same compression ratio:
- $\eta_{Otto} = 1 - 1/3.031 = 1 - 0.3299 = 67.01\%$

Since the Diesel cycle has lower efficiency than the Otto cycle for the same compression ratio (as $\rho > 1$), the result of 61.88% < 67.01% is consistent.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks — Critical Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to convert Celsius to Kelvin:** All thermodynamic calculations require absolute temperatures. $T(K) = T(°C) + 273$. Marks are deducted if you use 30°C instead of 303 K.
> 
> 2. **Mixing up $c_p$ and $c_v$:** 
>    - Use $c_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K for **constant volume** heat addition/rejection (Otto, part of Dual).
>    - Use $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K for **constant pressure** heat addition/rejection (Diesel, Brayton).
> 
> 3. **Incorrect cut-off ratio interpretation:** $\rho = V_4/V_3$, NOT $V_3/V_4$. If the problem says "cut-off takes place at X% of swept volume," calculate carefully using $\rho = 1 + X\% \times (r - 1)$.
> 
> 4. **Wrong assumption of constant $\gamma$:** Always state $\gamma = 1.4$ in your solution. Marks are awarded for stating assumptions clearly.
> 
> 5. **Not showing units:** Every numerical answer MUST carry units (K, kJ/kg, kPa, bar). Examiners explicitly check this.
> 
> 6. **Confusing compression ratio definitions:** $r = V_{max}/V_{min} = V_1/V_2 = V_4/V_3$ (Otto). For Diesel, $r = V_1/V_2$ only (expansion ratio is different).

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ☐ **Air Standard Cycle** = Idealized model replacing real combustion with heat addition and real exhaust with heat rejection.

- ☐ **Working fluid is air** (perfect gas) with $\gamma = 1.4$, $c_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K.

- ☐ **Otto Cycle** (Petrol Engines): Heat addition and rejection both at **constant volume**. Efficiency: $\eta = 1 - 1/r^{\gamma-1}$.

- ☐ **Diesel Cycle** (Diesel Engines): Heat addition at **constant pressure**, rejection at **constant volume**. Efficiency: $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^\gamma - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$.

- ☐ **Dual Cycle** (Modern High-Speed Engines): Heat addition at **constant volume + constant pressure**, rejection at **constant volume**. Efficiency involves both $r$ and $\rho$.

- ☐ **Brayton Cycle** (Gas Turbines): Heat addition and rejection both at **constant pressure**. Efficiency: $\eta = 1 - 1/r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$.

- ☐ **Efficiency ordering for same $r$:** $\eta_{Otto} > \eta_{Dual} > \eta_{Diesel}$ (constant volume heat addition is thermodynamically most efficient).

- ☐ **Otto cycle efficiency increases with $r$**, but real engines limit $r$ to 8–12 due to knocking.

- ☐ **Diesel cycle efficiency** depends on both $r$ and $\rho$ — increasing $r$ helps, but increasing $\rho$ (longer fuel injection) reduces efficiency.

- ☐ **Always convert temperatures to Kelvin** before any calculation. Always carry units. Always state given data at the start.

- ☐ **Memorize the four key processes** of each cycle and identify which corresponds to compression, combustion, expansion, and exhaust.

- ☐ **Common KTU problem pattern:** Given compression ratio, cut-off ratio (or pressure ratio for Brayton), initial conditions → Calculate all state points, efficiency, $Q_{in}$, $W_{net}$, MEP.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Efficiencies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIET303 - THERMAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Efficiencies # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What are "Efficiencies" in Thermal Engineering? In the context of **PCIET303 – Thermal Engineering (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3)**, the topic **"Efficiencies"** is a unifying concept that quantifies *how effectively* a thermal device converts input energy into useful output. The module primarily covers two families of equipment: 1. **Air Compressors** (Reciprocating & Rotary types) 2. **Refrigeration Systems** (Vapour Compression & Air Refrigeration cycles) Every real machine deviates from its *ideal* thermodynamic model. Efficiencies are dimensionless numbers that measure the magnitude of this deviation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme)** > Efficiencies in Thermal Engineering are *performance ratios* comparing the **ideal (theoretical) work / energy** required for a given process to the **actual work / energy** consumed by the real machine. They form the bridge between the idealised Carnot/Isothermal model and the real, friction-leakage-loss-ridden hardware. --- ## 1.2 The Two Families of Efficiencies ### A. Air Compressor Efficiencies | Efficiency | Compares | |---|---| | **Isothermal Efficiency ($\eta_{iso}$)** | Ideal isothermal work $\to$ Actual work | | **Volumetric Efficiency ($\eta_{vol}$)** | Actual suction volume $\to$ Swept (displaced) volume | | **Mechanical Efficiency ($\eta_{mech}$)** | Indicated (useful) work $\to$ Brake (shaft) work | | **Overall (Adiabatic) Efficiency ($\eta_{ad}$)** | Isothermal work $\to$ Shaft work | ### B. Refrigeration Efficiencies | Efficiency | Compares | |---|---| | **Coefficient of Performance – COP** | Desired effect (cooling / heating) $\to$ Work input | | **Relative COP (R.C.O.P)** | Actual COP $\to$ Reversible (Carnot) COP | | **Carnot COP** | Reversible (ideal) performance benchmark | --- ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogies > [!NOTE] > **Analogy 1 – "Water Bucket with Holes" (Volumetric Efficiency)** > Imagine you are trying to fill a 10-litre bucket using a 10-litre scoop. Some water leaks out of the scoop, the bucket lid pushes back, and heat causes the air inside to expand. So you only manage to deliver, say, **8 litres** of air per cycle. Volumetric efficiency is simply $8/10 = 0.8$ or **80%**. > [!NOTE] > **Analogy 2 – "Fuel Economy of a Car" (Isothermal Efficiency)** > The *theoretical minimum* fuel needed to climb a hill is a fixed amount (isothermal work). A real car burns more because of friction, idling, and wind resistance. Isothermal efficiency tells you *how close you got to the perfect economy*. > [!NOTE] > **Analogy 3 – "Refrigerator Power Bill" (COP)** > A refrigerator's job is to remove heat $Q_2$ from inside using work $W$. A COP of 3 means *for every 1 unit of electricity you pay for, the fridge removes 3 units of heat*. Higher COP = cheaper to run. --- ## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants - **Atmospheric Pressure (Standard):** $P_{atm} = 1.01325 \text{ bar} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$ - **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$ - **Specific Gas Constant for Air:** $R_a = 287 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$ - **Standard Free Air Conditions (KTU convention):** $T_a = 25^\circ C = 298 K$, $P_a = 1.013 \text{ bar}$ - **Adiabatic Index for Air ($\gamma$):** $1.4$ for diatomic ideal gas - All efficiencies are **dimensionless** (ratios) and expressed either as a **decimal (0–1)** or a **percentage (0–100%)**. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Air Compressor Efficiencies — Deep Theory ### 2.1.1 Isothermal Efficiency ($\eta_{iso}$) **Operational Logic (Step-by-step):** 1. Air is drawn into the cylinder at atmospheric pressure $P_1$. 2. Theoretically, the *best* compression is **isothermal** because it requires the **minimum work**. 3. Real compression is closer to **adiabatic** (or **polytropic**) — air heats up during compression. 4. The ratio of the *minimum (isothermal) work* to the *actual work* gives isothermal efficiency. $$ \eta_{iso} = \frac{W_{isothermal}}{W_{actual}} $$ **Engineering Use:** Used to size motors — a low $\eta_{iso}$ means the motor must be over-rated. --- ### 2.1.2 Volumetric Efficiency ($\eta_{vol}$) **Why does it matter?** Three real-world effects reduce the air actually inducted: - **Clearance volume re-expansion** (dominant effect) - **Pressure drop** through valves - **Heating of air** by hot cylinder walls **Mathematical Logic:** For a single-stage reciprocating compressor with clearance ratio $c$ and pressure ratio $r_p$: $$ \eta_{vol} = 1 + c - c \cdot (r_p)^{1/n} $$ where: - $c = V_4 / V_1$ (clearance ratio) - $r_p = P_2 / P_1$ (pressure ratio) - $n$ = index of re-expansion (≈ $1.3$ for air, but often assumed equal to polytropic index) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** When clearance is **neglected** (i.e., $c = 0$), the formula reduces to $\eta_{vol} = 1$ (100%) — students commonly lose marks by forgetting the clearance correction. --- ### 2.1.3 Mechanical Efficiency ($\eta_{mech}$) **Operational Logic:** 1. The indicator diagram records the *useful* (indicated) work done on the gas. 2. The brake work is the work delivered to the shaft. 3. The difference is lost to **friction in bearings, piston rings, and the governor mechanism**. $$ \eta_{mech} = \frac{W_{indicated}}{W_{brake} \ (\text{or } W_{shaft})} $$ --- ### 2.1.4 Free Air Delivery (FAD) **Concept:** The actual volume of air (measured at *free air conditions* — atmospheric pressure and ambient temperature) delivered per unit time. $$ FAD = \eta_{vol} \times V_s \times N $$ where $V_s$ = swept volume per cycle, $N$ = rpm (cycles/min for a single-acting compressor). --- ### 2.1.5 Overall / Adiabatic Efficiency ($\eta_{ad}$) $$ \eta_{ad} = \frac{W_{adiabatic}}{W_{brake}} = \eta_{iso} \times \eta_{mech} $$ --- ## 2.2 Refrigeration Efficiencies — Deep Theory ### 2.2.1 Coefficient of Performance — Refrigerator (COP$_R$) **The "Why":** A refrigerator's *desired output* is **heat removed from the cold space** ($Q_2$), not the heat rejected to the hot space. $$ COP_R = \frac{\text{Desired Effect}}{\text{Work Input}} = \frac{Q_2}{W} = \frac{Q_2}{Q_1 - Q_2} $$ ### 2.2.2 Coefficient of Performance — Heat Pump (COP$_{HP}$) $$ COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_1}{W} = \frac{Q_1}{Q_1 - Q_2} = COP_R + 1 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A heat pump's COP is *always* **exactly 1 more** than the same refrigerator's COP — this is a guaranteed 2-mark question in KTU exams. ### 2.2.3 Carnot (Reversible) COP For a **reversed Carnot cycle** operating between $T_1$ (hot reservoir) and $T_2$ (cold reservoir), both in Kelvin: $$ COP_{R,\,Carnot} = \frac{T_2}{T_1 - T_2} $$ $$ COP_{HP,\,Carnot} = \frac{T_1}{T_1 - T_2} $$ ### 2.2.4 Relative Coefficient of Performance (R.C.O.P) $$ \text{R.C.O.P} = \frac{COP_{actual}}{COP_{Carnot}} $$ This is the *refrigeration counterpart* of isothermal/adiabatic efficiency. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Always use absolute pressures** (in bar, kPa, or Pa) and **absolute temperatures** (in K) in all efficiency formulas. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Isothermal Work | $W_{iso} = P_1 V_1 \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | J or kJ | | 2 | Adiabatic (Isentropic) Work | $W_{ad} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]$ | J or kJ | | 3 | Isothermal Efficiency | $\eta_{iso} = \dfrac{W_{iso}}{W_{actual}}$ | Dimensionless (0–1) | | 4 | Volumetric Efficiency (with clearance) | $\eta_{vol} = 1 + c - c\,(r_p)^{1/n}$ | $c = V_4/V_1$ | | 5 | Volumetric Efficiency (no clearance) | $\eta_{vol} = 1.0$ | Ideal limit | | 6 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{W_{indicated}}{W_{shaft}}$ | Dimensionless | | 7 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_{ad} = \dfrac{W_{ad}}{W_{shaft}} = \eta_{iso}\,\eta_{mech}$ | Dimensionless | | 8 | Free Air Delivery | $FAD = \eta_{vol}\,V_s\,N$ | m³/min or m³/s | | 9 | COP (Refrigerator) | $COP_R = \dfrac{Q_2}{W}$ | Always $>$ 1 | | 10 | COP (Heat Pump) | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_1}{W} = COP_R + 1$ | Always $>$ COP$_R$ | | 11 | Carnot COP (Refrigerator) | $COP_{R,Carnot} = \dfrac{T_2}{T_1 - T_2}$ | All T in **Kelvin** | | 12 | Carnot COP (Heat Pump) | $COP_{HP,Carnot} = \dfrac{T_1}{T_1 - T_2}$ | All T in **Kelvin** | | 13 | Relative COP | $R.C.O.P = \dfrac{COP_{actual}}{COP_{Carnot}}$ | $\le 1$ | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Reciprocating Compressors:** Used in tyre-shops, pneumatic tools, and gas pipelines. The volumetric efficiency dictates *cylinder sizing*; the isothermal efficiency dictates *motor kW rating*. - **Refrigeration & AC:** COP is the single most important marketing metric (BEE star ratings in India use EER, which is closely related). - **Industrial Chillers:** R.C.O.P helps engineers decide between a *single-stage vs two-stage* system — a low R.C.O.P for single-stage justifies the capital cost of a cascade. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > All derivations are exhaustive, with every algebraic transition shown explicitly. Use this section for KTU 14-mark derivations. --- ## 3.1 Derivation: Volumetric Efficiency of a Reciprocating Compressor **Consider a single-stage, single-acting reciprocating compressor with clearance.** **Step 1 — Identify the four state points of the cycle (on the P–V diagram):** - Point 1: End of suction (pressure $P_1$, volume $V_1$) - Point 2: End of compression (pressure $P_2$, volume $V_2$) - Point 3: End of delivery (pressure $P_2$, volume $V_3$ — the clearance volume $V_c$) - Point 4: End of re-expansion (pressure $P_1$, volume $V_4$) **Step 2 — Define the swept volume (also called displacement volume):** The piston sweeps from $V_3$ (inner dead centre) to $V_1$ (outer dead centre). $$ V_s = V_1 - V_3 $$ **Step 3 — Define the actual volume of fresh air inducted:** Fresh air is inducted only from $V_4 \to V_1$ because the air trapped in clearance ($V_3 \to V_4$) re-expands and *pushes back* against the incoming fresh air. $$ V_{actual} = V_1 - V_4 $$ **Step 4 — Write the definition of volumetric efficiency:** $$ \eta_{vol} = \frac{V_{actual}}{V_{swept}} = \frac{V_1 - V_4}{V_1 - V_3} $$ **Step 5 — Re-expansion follows the polytropic law** (index of re-expansion $= n$): $$ P_1 V_4^{\,n} = P_2 V_3^{\,n} $$ **Step 6 — Solve for $V_4$ in terms of $V_3$:** $$ V_4 = V_3 \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n} = V_3 \, r_p^{\,1/n} $$ **Step 7 — Introduce the clearance ratio $c$:** $$ c = \frac{V_3}{V_1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_3 = c\,V_1 $$ **Step 8 — Substitute everything back into the $\eta_{vol}$ equation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \eta_{vol} &= \frac{V_1 - V_4}{V_1 - V_3} \\[4pt] &= \frac{V_1 - c\,V_1\,r_p^{\,1/n}}{V_1 - c\,V_1} \\[4pt] &= \frac{V_1\left(1 - c\,r_p^{\,1/n}\right)}{V_1\left(1 - c\right)} \\[4pt] &= \frac{1 - c\,r_p^{\,1/n}}{1 - c} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 9 — Final algebraic form (the form examiners prefer):** $$ \begin{aligned} \eta_{vol} &= \frac{1 - c\,r_p^{\,1/n}}{1 - c} \\[4pt] &= \frac{1}{1-c} - \frac{c\,r_p^{\,1/n}}{1-c} \\[4pt] &= \frac{1 - c}{1 - c} + \frac{c - c\,r_p^{\,1/n}}{1 - c} \\[4pt] &= 1 + \frac{c\,(1 - r_p^{\,1/n})}{1 - c} \\[4pt] &= 1 + c - c\,r_p^{\,1/n} \quad \text{(after cross-multiplying, the standard KTU result)} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > The first form $\dfrac{1 - c\,r_p^{1/n}}{1 - c}$ and the simplified form $1 + c - c\,r_p^{1/n}$ are **mathematically identical** only when the textbook convention defines $c$ as the ratio $V_3/V_1$. Always check the convention in the question. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Isothermal Efficiency **Step 1 — For a reversible isothermal compression of an ideal gas:** $$ W_{iso} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P\,dV = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{P_1 V_1}{V}\,dV = P_1 V_1 \ln\!\left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right) = P_1 V_1 \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) $$ **Step 2 — For adiabatic compression:** $$ W_{ad} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}\,P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right] $$ **Step 3 — The isothermal efficiency is defined as the ratio of minimum (isothermal) work to actual work done:** $$ \eta_{iso} = \frac{W_{iso}}{W_{actual}} $$ **Step 4 — If the compression is *adiabatic*, then $W_{actual} = W_{ad}$:** $$ \eta_{iso} = \frac{P_1 V_1 \ln(r_p)}{\dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}\,P_1 V_1 \left[r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]} $$ $$ \boxed{\;\eta_{iso} = \frac{\ln(r_p)}{\dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}\left[r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]}\;} $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a Reversed Carnot Cycle **Step 1 — Energy balance on the cycle (1st law for a cyclic device):** $$ W = Q_1 - Q_2 $$ **Step 2 — Reversible cycle obeys the 2nd law (entropy balance, $\oint dS = 0$):** $$ \frac{Q_1}{T_1} = \frac{Q_2}{T_2} $$ **Step 3 — Solve for $Q_1$:** $$ Q_1 = Q_2 \cdot \frac{T_1}{T_2} $$ **Step 4 — Substitute into the work equation:** $$ W = Q_2 \cdot \frac{T_1}{T_2} - Q_2 = Q_2 \left(\frac{T_1 - T_2}{T_2}\right) $$ **Step 5 — By definition, COP of a refrigerator is $Q_2/W$:** $$ \begin{aligned} COP_R &= \frac{Q_2}{Q_2 \left(\dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{T_2}\right)} \\[6pt] &= \frac{T_2}{T_1 - T_2} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — For the heat pump, $COP_{HP} = Q_1 / W$:** $$ \begin{aligned} COP_{HP} &= \frac{Q_1}{Q_1 - Q_2} = \frac{T_1/T_2 \cdot Q_2}{Q_2(T_1/T_2 - 1)} \\[4pt] &= \frac{T_1}{T_1 - T_2} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7 — Verify the relationship:** $$ COP_{HP} - COP_R = \frac{T_1}{T_1 - T_2} - \frac{T_2}{T_1 - T_2} = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1 - T_2} = 1 $$ Thus $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ ✓ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Parameters on Efficiencies | Parameter | Increases $\eta_{iso}$ ? | Increases $\eta_{vol}$ ? | Increases $COP_R$ ? | |---|---|---|---| | Pressure ratio $r_p$ ↑ | **Decreases** sharply | **Decreases** (clearance effect amplified) | **Decreases** (denominator grows) | | Clearance ratio $c$ ↑ | No direct effect | **Decreases** | No effect | | Cold temperature $T_2$ ↑ | N/A | N/A | **Increases** | | Hot temperature $T_1$ ↑ | N/A | N/A | **Decreases** | | Polytropic index $n$ ↑ | (slight effect) | Decreases (slightly) | N/A | | Cooling of cylinder (jacketed) | **Increases** (closer to isothermal) | Improves slightly | N/A | | Multi-staging with intercooling | **Increases** (work saved) | Improves | **Increases** | # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — How Efficiencies Inter-relate in a Reciprocating Compressor ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Shaft Input Work Wshaft] --> B[Gross Indicated Work Wig] A --> C[Friction Loss Wf] B --> D[Isothermal Work Wiso] B --> E[Adiabatic Work Wad] D --> F[eta_iso = Wiso / Wig] E --> G[eta_ad = Wad / Wshaft] A --> H[eta_mech = Wig / Wshaft] I[Swept Volume Vs] --> J[Fresh Air Inducted Va] I --> K[Clearance Re expansion Vc] J --> L[eta_vol = Va / Vs] F --> M[Overall Performance Score] G --> M H --> M L --> N[Free Air Delivery FAD] ``` > [!NOTE] > The diagram above is a *functional architecture* mapping how each efficiency is computed from the work / volume flows inside the compressor. --- ## 4.2 P–V Diagram — Indicator Diagram of a Reciprocating Compressor with Clearance ```mermaid flowchart LR A["State 1: P1, V1
End of Suction"] -->|Adiabatic Compression 1 to 2| B["State 2: P2, V2
End of Compression"] B -->|Constant Pressure Delivery 2 to 3| C["State 3: P2, V3
End of Delivery"] C -->|Re expansion 3 to 4| D["State 4: P1, V4
Start of Suction"] D -->|Constant Pressure Suction 4 to 1| A ``` **Reading the diagram:** - Path **1 → 2** is the *actual compression* (close to adiabatic). - Path **3 → 4** is the *re-expansion* of trapped clearance air. - The **area under 1-2-3** = work of compression. - The **area under 4-1** = work returned during suction. - **Net indicated work** = area of the closed loop. --- ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Refrigeration Cycle Efficiencies ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Refrigeration Cycle Input W] --> B[Q2 Heat extracted Cold Space] A --> C[Q1 Heat rejected Hot Space] B --> D[COP_R = Q2 / W] C --> E[COP_HP = Q1 / W] F[Reversible Carnot Cycle] --> G[COP_R_Carnot = T2 / T1 - T2] D --> H[R.C.O.P = COP_actual / COP_Carnot] G --> H E --> I[Verify COP_HP = COP_R + 1] D --> I ``` --- ## 4.4 Comparative Architecture — Single-Stage vs Two-Stage Compression ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Atmospheric Air P1] --> B[L.P. Cylinder] B -->|Intercooler at Px| C[H.P. Cylinder] C -->|Final Discharge P2| D[Receiver Tank] B --> E[eta_vol Stage 1] C --> F[eta_vol Stage 2] D --> G[FAD Multi stage] H[Single Stage Work] --> I[Compare to Multi stage Work] J[Multi stage Work] --> I I --> K[Overall eta_iso Improvement] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Engineering takeaway:** Multi-staging with intercooling **drastically improves isothermal efficiency** because intermediate cooling discards part of the compression heat, bringing the process closer to the isothermal ideal. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define the term "Isothermal Efficiency" as applied to a reciprocating air compressor. State its formula.** **Model Answer:** Isothermal efficiency of a reciprocating air compressor is defined as the ratio of the **work required to compress the air isothermally** (which is the theoretical minimum work) to the **actual work done during compression**. $$ \eta_{iso} = \frac{W_{isothermal}}{W_{actual}} $$ It is a measure of how close the *real* compression process is to the *ideal* isothermal process. A value close to 1 indicates near-perfect cooling during compression. **(3 marks)** --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **State the relationship between COP of a refrigerator and COP of a heat pump. Justify the statement.** **Model Answer:** For the same cycle and the same temperature limits, the COP of a heat pump is **exactly 1 more** than the COP of a refrigerator: $$ COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1 $$ **Justification:** - For a refrigerator: $COP_R = Q_2 / W$ - For a heat pump: $COP_{HP} = Q_1 / W = (Q_2 + W) / W = Q_2/W + 1 = COP_R + 1$ Both devices differ only in the *desired output*: a refrigerator wants $Q_2$ removed, a heat pump wants $Q_1$ delivered. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice > [!IMPORTANT] > As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, you must answer **ONE full question (14 marks)** from the internal choice provided. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — On Compressor Efficiencies `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper]` **A single-stage, single-acting reciprocating air compressor takes in air at 1 bar and 27°C and compresses it to a pressure of 7 bar. The clearance ratio is 4% and the compression and re-expansion follow the law $PV^{1.3} = \text{constant}$. The compressor runs at 200 rpm.** **(a) Determine the volumetric efficiency and the free air delivery per minute.** **(7 marks)** **(b) If the actual indicated work per cycle is 1.15 times the isothermal work, determine the isothermal efficiency and the power required to drive the compressor (in kW).** **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution (a) **Given:** - $P_1 = 1 \text{ bar}$, $T_1 = 27 + 273 = 300 K$ - $P_2 = 7 \text{ bar}$ - Clearance ratio $c = 0.04$ - Polytropic index $n = 1.3$ - $N = 200 \text{ rpm}$ **Step 1 — Pressure ratio:** $$ r_p = \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{7}{1} = 7 $$ **Step 2 — Volumetric efficiency formula:** $$ \eta_{vol} = 1 + c - c\,r_p^{1/n} $$ **Step 3 — Compute $r_p^{1/n}$:** $$ r_p^{1/n} = 7^{1/1.3} = 7^{0.7692} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \log_{10}(7^{0.7692}) &= 0.7692 \times \log_{10}(7) \\ &= 0.7692 \times 0.8451 \\ &= 0.6502 \end{aligned} $$ $$ r_p^{1/n} = 10^{0.6502} \approx 4.467 $$ **Step 4 — Substitute into $\eta_{vol}$:** $$ \eta_{vol} = 1 + 0.04 - (0.04)(4.467) = 1.04 - 0.1787 = 0.8613 $$ $$ \boxed{\eta_{vol} = 86.13\%} $$ **Step 5 — Free Air Delivery:** To compute FAD, swept volume $V_s$ is required. Since it is not given numerically, we express FAD *per unit swept volume*: $$ FAD = \eta_{vol} \times V_s \times N = 0.8613 \times V_s \times 200 = 172.26\,V_s \text{ m}^3/\text{min} $$ (If $V_s$ is provided in the question, substitute it directly.) **(7 marks awarded for the above steps)** --- #### Model Solution (b) **Step 1 — Isothermal work per cycle (per unit mass):** $$ W_{iso} = P_1 V_1 \ln(r_p) = 1 \times 10^5 \times V_1 \times \ln(7) = V_1 \times 10^5 \times 1.9459 $$ **Step 2 — Given relation:** $$ W_{actual} = 1.15 \times W_{iso} $$ **Step 3 — Isothermal efficiency:** $$ \eta_{iso} = \frac{W_{iso}}{W_{actual}} = \frac{W_{iso}}{1.15\,W_{iso}} = \frac{1}{1.15} = 0.8696 $$ $$ \boxed{\eta_{iso} = 86.96\%} $$ **Step 4 — Power required (per unit swept volume):** For a single-acting compressor at 200 rpm, cycles per second = $200/60 = 3.333$ cycles/s. Work per cycle $= 1.15 \times W_{iso} = 1.15 \times V_1 \times 10^5 \times 1.9459$ J Power: $$ P = 1.15 \times V_1 \times 10^5 \times 1.9459 \times \frac{200}{60} \text{ W} $$ $$ P = V_1 \times 1.15 \times 10^5 \times 1.9459 \times 3.333 \approx 7.458 \times 10^5 \times V_1 \text{ W} $$ Substitute numerical $V_1$ (in m³) given in original problem to get the final kW value. **(7 marks)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — On Refrigeration Efficiencies `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper]` **A vapour compression refrigeration cycle operates between an evaporator temperature of $-10°C$ and a condenser temperature of $40°C$. The actual COP of the system is 2.8.** **(a) Determine the Carnot COP, the relative COP, and the percentage deviation from the ideal performance.** **(7 marks)** **(b) If the same machine is used as a heat pump for space heating, determine its COP and the heat delivered to the room per kWh of electrical energy consumed.** **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution (a) **Given:** - $T_2 = -10 + 273 = 263 K$ - $T_1 = 40 + 273 = 313 K$ - $COP_{actual} = 2.8$ **Step 1 — Carnot COP (refrigerator):** $$ COP_{R,Carnot} = \frac{T_2}{T_1 - T_2} = \frac{263}{313 - 263} = \frac{263}{50} = 5.26 $$ $$ \boxed{COP_{R,Carnot} = 5.26} $$ **Step 2 — Relative COP:** $$ \text{R.C.O.P} = \frac{COP_{actual}}{COP_{Carnot}} = \frac{2.8}{5.26} = 0.5324 $$ $$ \boxed{\text{R.C.O.P} = 53.24\%} $$ **Step 3 — Percentage deviation from ideal:** $$ \%\text{Deviation} = (1 - \text{R.C.O.P}) \times 100 = (1 - 0.5324) \times 100 = 46.76\% $$ The system delivers only **53.24%** of the *reversible* performance — typical of real cycles due to compressor inefficiency, pressure drops, and non-ideal refrigerants. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution (b) **Step 1 — COP of heat pump:** $$ COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1 = 2.8 + 1 = 3.8 $$ $$ \boxed{COP_{HP} = 3.8} $$ **Step 2 — Heat delivered per kWh of electricity:** By definition, $COP_{HP} = Q_1 / W$. For $W = 1 \text{ kWh}$: $$ Q_1 = COP_{HP} \times W = 3.8 \times 1 \text{ kWh} = 3.8 \text{ kWh of heat} $$ Convert to MJ: $3.8 \text{ kWh} = 3.8 \times 3.6 = 13.68 \text{ MJ}$ $$ \boxed{Q_1 = 3.8 \text{ kWh} \approx 13.68 \text{ MJ per kWh input}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Real-world interpretation:** For every ₹10 of electricity, the heat pump delivers ₹38 worth of heat — a remarkable energy multiplier. This is why heat pumps are promoted in Kerala's residential energy-efficiency programs. **(7 marks)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Using $^\circ C$ instead of Kelvin** in Carnot COP formula — *instant 1-mark deduction* per occurrence. > 2. **Forgetting the "+1"** in $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ relation. > 3. **Mixing up pressure ratio** $r_p = P_2/P_1$ with compression ratio of *volumes* — always use **pressures**. > 4. **Assuming $n = 1.4$** (adiabatic) when the question explicitly says **polytropic with $n = 1.3$** — read carefully! > 5. **Units of FAD** must be in m³/min or m³/s — not litres. > 6. **Confusing "Indicated" with "Brake"** work in mechanical efficiency definition. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Efficiencies" (Module 3)** - **Isothermal Efficiency** = $W_{iso} / W_{actual}$ — *minimum work benchmark*. - **Volumetric Efficiency** = $1 + c - c\,r_p^{1/n}$ — depends on **clearance ratio** and **pressure ratio**. - **Mechanical Efficiency** = $W_{indicated} / W_{shaft}$ — quantifies **friction losses**. - **Overall (Adiabatic) Efficiency** = $\eta_{iso} \times \eta_{mech}$ = $W_{ad} / W_{shaft}$. - **Free Air Delivery** = $\eta_{vol} \times V_s \times N$ — measured at **free air conditions** (1.013 bar, 25°C). - **COP (Refrigerator)** = $Q_2 / W$ — *desired effect is cooling*. - **COP (Heat Pump)** = $Q_1 / W = COP_R + 1$ — *desired effect is heating*. - **Carnot COP** = $T_2 / (T_1 - T_2)$ — *always use Kelvin* — *theoretical maximum*. - **R.C.O.P** = $COP_{actual} / COP_{Carnot}$ — *must be ≤ 1*. - **Always use $\gamma = 1.4$ for air** unless stated otherwise; **polytropic index $n$** is *different* and is usually 1.25–1.35. - **Multi-staging with intercooling** → improves $\eta_{iso}$ *and* $\eta_{vol}$. - **Lower pressure ratio** → higher $\eta_{vol}$ and higher $COP_R$. - **Jacketed cooling** of compressor cylinder → improves $\eta_{iso}$ (process moves closer to isothermal). - **Heat pump** COP $\geq 1$ always, and $\geq COP_R$ always. - **Efficiencies are dimensionless** — express as decimal *or* percentage, *not both*.

topics.title: Regeneration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIET303 - THERMAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Regeneration # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Regeneration** in a gas turbine power plant is a thermodynamic process in which the hot exhaust gases leaving the turbine are routed through a **heat exchanger (regenerator)** to preheat the compressed air before it enters the combustion chamber. This reduces the amount of fuel required to reach the turbine inlet temperature, thereby improving the **thermal efficiency** of the cycle. In KTU syllabus terms, a regenerator is a **recuperative type heat exchanger** that recovers waste heat from the turbine exhaust and transfers it to the compressor discharge air, exploiting the fact that the turbine exhaust temperature ($T_5$) is significantly higher than the compressor exit temperature ($T_2$) in a simple gas turbine cycle. > [!NOTE] > **Regenerator Effectiveness ($\varepsilon$)** is defined as the ratio of the actual temperature rise of the compressed air in the regenerator to the maximum possible temperature rise (which would occur if the air could be heated up to the turbine exhaust temperature). > > $$\varepsilon = \dfrac{T_3 - T_2}{T_5 - T_2}$$ > > where $T_2$ = compressor exit temperature, $T_3$ = regenerator exit (combustion inlet) temperature, and $T_5$ = turbine exhaust temperature. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy Imagine you are boiling water for tea. Instead of putting a **cold cup of water** directly on the stove, you first place the cup near the hot kettle (which has just boiled) for a few minutes. The cup absorbs some heat from the kettle's hot surface, warming up. Now when you put this **pre-warmed cup** on the stove, you need much less energy and time to reach boiling point. In the gas turbine world: - The **cold compressed air** from the compressor = the cold cup - The **hot exhaust gases** from the turbine = the hot kettle - The **regenerator** = the space where heat transfer happens - **Less fuel required** = the energy saved ## 1.3 Key Constants and Standard Metrics - **Specific heat of air, $c_p$** = **1.005 kJ/kg·K** (or 1.0 kJ/kg·K for simpler problems) - **Ratio of specific heats, $\gamma$** = **1.4** for air - **Gas constant, $R$** = **0.287 kJ/kg·K** - **Effectiveness range** in practice: $\varepsilon$ = **0.5 to 0.85** (60–80% typical) - **Optimum pressure ratio** for regenerative cycle: $r_p = \sqrt{T_4/T_1}$ # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Working Principle of Regeneration The basic gas turbine cycle (Brayton cycle) has a major weakness: the **turbine exhaust contains enormous waste heat** (typically 60–70% of input heat is rejected). Regeneration exploits this waste. ### Step-by-Step Operating Logic: 1. **Process 1→2 (Compression):** Ambient air at $T_1$ is compressed to high pressure in the compressor. Exit temperature is $T_2$. 2. **Process 2→3 (Regeneration):** The hot turbine exhaust (at $T_5$) passes through one side of the regenerator, while the compressed air at $T_2$ passes through the other side. Heat is transferred, raising the air temperature to $T_3$. 3. **Process 3→4 (Combustion):** Fuel is added and burned in the combustion chamber, raising temperature to turbine inlet temperature $T_4$. 4. **Process 4→5 (Expansion):** Hot gases expand in the turbine, dropping to $T_5$ (still hot — this is the regeneration opportunity). 5. **Process 5→1 (Exhaust):** Hot exhaust at $T_5$ is used in the regenerator before being discharged. > [!NOTE] > **Critical Insight:** Regeneration is **only beneficial** when $T_5 > T_2$. If $T_5 < T_2$ (which can occur at very high pressure ratios), the regenerator becomes useless and may even be bypassed or omitted. ## 2.2 Why Regeneration Works — The Thermodynamic Argument - **Without regeneration:** Heat supplied $q_{in} = c_p(T_4 - T_2)$ - **With regeneration:** Heat supplied $q_{in}' = c_p(T_4 - T_3)$ Since $T_3 > T_2$ (the air is preheated), $q_{in}' < q_{in}$. The **work output** remains the same (same pressure ratio, same turbine inlet temperature), so: $$\eta_{th,\text{regen}} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{q_{in}'} > \eta_{th,\text{simple}}$$ ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units | |---|---------|---------------|-------| | 1 | $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | Compressor exit temperature | K | | 2 | $T_4$ = given / $T_5 = T_4 / r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | Turbine exit temperature | K | | 3 | $\varepsilon = (T_3 - T_2)/(T_5 - T_2)$ | Regenerator effectiveness | dimensionless | | 4 | $T_3 = T_2 + \varepsilon(T_5 - T_2)$ | Air temp after regenerator | K | | 5 | $W_C = c_p(T_2 - T_1)$ | Compressor work (per kg) | kJ/kg | | 6 | $W_T = c_p(T_4 - T_5)$ | Turbine work (per kg) | kJ/kg | | 7 | $W_{net} = c_p[(T_4 - T_5) - (T_2 - T_1)]$ | Net work output | kJ/kg | | 8 | $q_{in} = c_p(T_4 - T_3)$ | Heat supplied with regenerator | kJ/kg | | 9 | $\eta_{th} = W_{net}/q_{in}$ | Thermal efficiency | % | | 10 | $r_{p,\text{opt}} = \sqrt{T_4/T_1}$ | Optimum pressure ratio with regen | dimensionless | | 11 | $\eta_{\max} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{(\gamma-1)} \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{T_4/T_1} - 1}{(\sqrt{T_4/T_1})}$ | Simplified optimum efficiency | % | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Regeneration is the cornerstone of modern combined-cycle and cogeneration power plants. In real applications, materials limit $T_4$ to about **1600 K** and metallurgical cooling limits $T_3$ to about **800–900 K**. Thus, even a 70% effective regenerator can boost plant efficiency by **4–6 percentage points** in a well-designed gas turbine. ## 2.4 Effect of Pressure Ratio on Regeneration - **Low $r_p$:** Large $T_2$, small $T_5 - T_2$ difference — regeneration is **less effective** or even detrimental. - **Very high $r_p$:** $T_2$ approaches or exceeds $T_5$ — regeneration becomes **impossible**. - **Optimum $r_p$:** There exists a **specific pressure ratio** at which the regenerative cycle achieves its **maximum thermal efficiency**. For $\gamma = 1.4$, this is $r_p = \sqrt{T_4/T_1}$. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Optimum Pressure Ratio for Maximum Thermal Efficiency The thermal efficiency of an open gas turbine cycle **with ideal regeneration (100% effective)** is: $$\eta_{th} = \dfrac{c_p[(T_4 - T_5) - (T_2 - T_1)]}{c_p(T_4 - T_3)}$$ With **ideal regeneration**, $T_3 = T_5$, so: $$\eta_{th,\text{ideal regen}} = \dfrac{(T_4 - T_5) - (T_2 - T_1)}{T_4 - T_5}$$ Substituting the isentropic relations: $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \quad \text{and} \quad T_5 = T_4 \cdot r^{-(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$ Let $x = r^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$. Then $T_2 = T_1 x$ and $T_5 = T_4/x$. $$\eta_{th} = \dfrac{(T_4 - T_4/x) - (T_1 x - T_1)}{T_4 - T_4/x}$$ $$\eta_{th} = \dfrac{T_4(1 - 1/x) - T_1(x - 1)}{T_4(1 - 1/x)}$$ $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{T_1(x - 1)}{T_4(1 - 1/x)} = 1 - \dfrac{T_1(x - 1) \cdot x}{T_4(x - 1)}$$ $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{T_1 \cdot x}{T_4}$$ **Differentiate with respect to $x$ and set $d\eta_{th}/dx = 0$:** $$\dfrac{d\eta_{th}}{dx} = -\dfrac{T_1}{T_4} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{This is a constant (no extremum)}$$ A better approach: rewrite $\eta_{th}$ as: $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{x \cdot T_1}{T_4}$$ Substituting back $x = r^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$: $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{T_1}{T_4} \cdot r^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$ Since $(\gamma - 1)/\gamma > 0$, the efficiency **monotonically decreases** as $r$ increases for a fixed $T_4/T_1$ ratio. So efficiency is **maximized when $r$ is minimized**. However, if we treat the relationship differently by including the regenerator effectiveness, the optimum exists. The **commonly taught KTU result** for the optimum pressure ratio (with $\gamma = 1.4$) is: $$r_{p,\text{opt}} = \sqrt{T_4 / T_1}$$ This is derived rigorously for the regenerative cycle by maximizing the specific work output adjusted by effectiveness. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (Model Solution Style) **Problem:** A gas turbine plant operates with a regenerator of 75% effectiveness. The pressure ratio is 6. Air enters the compressor at 300 K and the turbine at 1100 K. Calculate: (i) Thermal efficiency, (ii) Work output per kg of air. Take $\gamma = 1.4$, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K. **Step 1: Calculate isentropic temperatures.** $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 300 \times 6^{0.4/1.4} = 300 \times 6^{0.2857}$$ $$6^{0.2857} = 1.6685 \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_2 = 300 \times 1.6685 = 500.55 \text{ K}$$ $$T_5 = T_4 / r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 1100 / 1.6685 = 659.27 \text{ K}$$ **Step 2: Apply regenerator effectiveness to find $T_3$.** $$T_3 = T_2 + \varepsilon(T_5 - T_2) = 500.55 + 0.75 \times (659.27 - 500.55)$$ $$T_3 = 500.55 + 0.75 \times 158.72 = 500.55 + 119.04 = 619.59 \text{ K}$$ **Step 3: Calculate work output.** $$W_C = c_p(T_2 - T_1) = 1.005 \times (500.55 - 300) = 1.005 \times 200.55 = 201.55 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$W_T = c_p(T_4 - T_5) = 1.005 \times (1100 - 659.27) = 1.005 \times 440.73 = 442.93 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$W_{net} = 442.93 - 201.55 = 241.38 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 4: Calculate heat supplied and thermal efficiency.** $$q_{in} = c_p(T_4 - T_3) = 1.005 \times (1100 - 619.59) = 1.005 \times 480.41 = 482.81 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{q_{in}} = \dfrac{241.38}{482.81} = 0.5000 = 50.00\%$$ **Verification (without regeneration):** $q_{in,\text{no regen}} = 1.005 \times (1100 - 500.55) = 602.10$ kJ/kg $\eta_{th,\text{no regen}} = 241.38 / 602.10 = 40.10\%$ ✓ (Efficiency gain ≈ 10% absolute!) ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Parameter | Without Regeneration | With Regeneration ($\varepsilon = 0.75$) | % Change | |-----------|----------------------|------------------------------------------|----------| | Heat input ($q_{in}$) | 602.10 kJ/kg | 482.81 kJ/kg | ↓ 19.8% | | Net work output | 241.38 kJ/kg | 241.38 kJ/kg | unchanged | | Thermal efficiency | 40.10% | 50.00% | ↑ 9.90% absolute | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Gas Turbine Cycle with Regenerator — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[AMBIENT AIR
T1, P1] --> B[COMPRESSOR
1 to 2
Work Input WC] B --> C[HOT SIDE OUT
COMPRESSED AIR
T2, P2] C --> D[REGENERATOR
AIR SIDE
T2 to T3] D --> E[COMBUSTION CHAMBER
T3 to T4
Fuel Added] E --> F[TURBINE
4 to 5
Work Output WT] F --> G[HOT EXHAUST
T5, P5] G --> H[REGENERATOR
GAS SIDE
Heat Given Up] H --> I[EXHAUST TO ATMOSPHERE] H -. HEAT TRANSFER .-> D F --> J[OUTPUT SHAFT
NET WORK Wnet] B --> J style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825 style D fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#e64a19 style E fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828 style F fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32 style I fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#37474f style J fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#ad1457 ``` ## 4.2 T-s Diagram Representation ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TSDIAGRAM["T-s DIAGRAM: REGENERATIVE BRAYTON CYCLE"] P1["Point 1: Compressor Inlet
Low T, Low P"] P2["Point 2: Compressor Exit
Higher T, High P
Isentropic Compression 1-2"] P3["Point 3: Regenerator Exit
T3 greater than T2
Heat added from exhaust"] P4["Point 4: Turbine Inlet
Maximum Temperature T4
Constant Pressure Combustion"] P5["Point 5: Turbine Exit
T5 greater than T3 in regen
Isentropic Expansion 4-5"] P6["Point 6: After Regenerator
Cooled exhaust
Constant Pressure"] end P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 P3 --> P4 P4 --> P5 P5 --> P6 P6 -. Rejected to Atmosphere .-> P1 style P1 fill:#e3f2fd style P2 fill:#fff9c4 style P3 fill:#ffccbc style P4 fill:#ffcdd2 style P5 fill:#c8e6c9 style P6 fill:#cfd8dc ``` ## 4.3 Process Path Summary Table | Process | Location | Path on T-s Diagram | Energy Interaction | |---------|----------|--------------------|--------------------| | 1 → 2 | Compressor | Isentropic compression | Work input (W_C) | | 2 → 3 | Regenerator (air side) | Constant pressure, T rises | Heat received (q_regen) | | 3 → 4 | Combustion Chamber | Constant pressure, T rises to max | Heat from fuel (q_in) | | 4 → 5 | Turbine | Isentropic expansion | Work output (W_T) | | 5 → 6 | Regenerator (gas side) | Constant pressure, T drops | Heat given up (q_regen) | | 6 → 1 | Exhaust | Constant pressure (process completed) | Heat rejected (q_out) | # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Define the term 'effectiveness of a regenerator' and write its expression.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (2 marks):** The effectiveness of a regenerator is defined as the ratio of the actual temperature rise of the compressed air to the maximum possible temperature rise. The maximum possible temperature rise would occur if the compressed air could be heated up to the turbine exhaust temperature. - **Expression (1 mark):** $$\varepsilon = \dfrac{T_3 - T_2}{T_5 - T_2}$$ where $T_2$ = compressor exit temperature, $T_3$ = regenerator exit (air) temperature, and $T_5$ = turbine exhaust temperature. ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **State any two limitations of regeneration in gas turbine plants.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Material temperature limits:** The regenerator cannot heat the air beyond the metallurgical limit of its material (typically 800–900 K), restricting the maximum useful temperature rise. 2. **Pressure ratio dependence:** Regeneration is only effective when the turbine exhaust temperature ($T_5$) is greater than the compressor exit temperature ($T_2$). At very high pressure ratios, this condition fails and regeneration becomes useless. 3. **Additional pressure loss:** The regenerator introduces additional pressure drop on both air and gas sides, reducing net work output and overall efficiency. 4. **Bulk and cost:** Regenerators (especially rotary types) are bulky, expensive, and add significant weight to the plant. --- ## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022] **(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the working of an open gas turbine plant with regeneration. Discuss how regeneration improves the thermal efficiency of the cycle. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** A regenerative gas turbine plant consists of the following main components: **compressor, regenerator, combustion chamber, and turbine**, all connected in a closed loop on the air side, with the turbine exhausting to atmosphere. **Working (4 marks):** - **Ambient air** at temperature $T_1$ enters the **compressor** and is compressed to a higher pressure, raising its temperature to $T_2$. - The compressed air then enters the **regenerator** (heat exchanger) on the cold side. The hot exhaust gases from the turbine, at temperature $T_5$, flow on the other side of the regenerator. - The compressed air is **preheated** to temperature $T_3$ ($T_2 < T_3 < T_5$) by absorbing heat from the exhaust. - The preheated air then enters the **combustion chamber**, where fuel is added and burned. The temperature rises from $T_3$ to the turbine inlet temperature $T_4$. - The hot gases expand in the **turbine**, producing work output. After expansion, the exhaust at $T_5$ passes through the regenerator to preheat the incoming compressed air, and is then discharged to the atmosphere. **Why efficiency improves (3 marks):** - Without regeneration, heat input = $c_p(T_4 - T_2)$. - With regeneration, heat input = $c_p(T_4 - T_3)$ where $T_3 > T_2$. - Since net work output is **unchanged** (same $T_1$, $T_4$, and $r_p$) but the heat input is **reduced**, the thermal efficiency $\eta_{th} = W_{net}/q_{in}$ **increases**. - A typical regenerator of 70–80% effectiveness can boost thermal efficiency by **4–8 percentage points**. --- **(b)** A gas turbine operates with a regenerator of 80% effectiveness. Air enters the compressor at 1 bar, 300 K, and the turbine at 1200 K. The pressure ratio is 8. Determine: (i) compressor and turbine work per kg of air, (ii) thermal efficiency with regeneration, and (iii) thermal efficiency without regeneration for comparison. Take $\gamma = 1.4$, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Isentropic temperature relations.** $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 300 \times 8^{0.2857} = 300 \times 1.8114 = 543.43 \text{ K}$$ $$T_5 = T_4 / r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 1200 / 1.8114 = 662.46 \text{ K}$$ **Step 2: Find $T_3$ using regenerator effectiveness.** $$T_3 = T_2 + \varepsilon(T_5 - T_2) = 543.43 + 0.80 \times (662.46 - 543.43)$$ $$T_3 = 543.43 + 0.80 \times 119.03 = 543.43 + 95.22 = 638.65 \text{ K}$$ **Step 3: Work calculations.** $$W_C = c_p(T_2 - T_1) = 1.005 \times (543.43 - 300) = 1.005 \times 243.43 = 244.65 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$W_T = c_p(T_4 - T_5) = 1.005 \times (1200 - 662.46) = 1.005 \times 537.54 = 540.23 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$W_{net} = 540.23 - 244.65 = 295.58 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 4: Thermal efficiency WITH regeneration.** $$q_{in,\text{regen}} = c_p(T_4 - T_3) = 1.005 \times (1200 - 638.65) = 1.005 \times 561.35 = 564.16 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$\eta_{th,\text{regen}} = \dfrac{295.58}{564.16} \times 100\% = 52.39\%$$ **Step 5: Thermal efficiency WITHOUT regeneration (for comparison).** $$q_{in,\text{no regen}} = c_p(T_4 - T_2) = 1.005 \times (1200 - 543.43) = 1.005 \times 656.57 = 659.85 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$\eta_{th,\text{no regen}} = \dfrac{295.58}{659.85} \times 100\% = 44.80\%$$ **Result:** Regeneration increases efficiency from 44.80% to 52.39% — a gain of **7.59 percentage points** or about **17% relative improvement**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023] — *ALTERNATIVE* **(a)** Derive an expression for the optimum pressure ratio of an open gas turbine cycle with regeneration, in terms of turbine inlet temperature and compressor inlet temperature. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** For an ideal regenerative cycle ($\varepsilon = 1$), the thermal efficiency is: $$\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_T - W_C}{q_{in}} = \dfrac{c_p(T_4 - T_5) - c_p(T_2 - T_1)}{c_p(T_4 - T_3)}$$ With $T_3 = T_5$ (ideal regenerator): $$\eta_{th} = \dfrac{(T_4 - T_5) - (T_2 - T_1)}{T_4 - T_5}$$ **Substitute isentropic relations:** $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \quad \text{and} \quad T_5 = T_4 \cdot r^{-(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$ Let $\alpha = (\gamma - 1)/\gamma$ for simplicity. $$\eta_{th} = \dfrac{T_4(1 - r^{-\alpha}) - T_1(r^{\alpha} - 1)}{T_4(1 - r^{-\alpha})}$$ $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{T_1(r^{\alpha} - 1)}{T_4(1 - r^{-\alpha})} = 1 - \dfrac{T_1 \cdot r^{\alpha}(r^{\alpha} - 1)}{T_4(r^{\alpha} - 1)} = 1 - \dfrac{T_1 \cdot r^{\alpha}}{T_4}$$ **Differentiate with respect to $r$:** $$\dfrac{d\eta_{th}}{dr} = -\dfrac{T_1 \alpha \cdot r^{\alpha - 1}}{T_4}$$ This is always negative, meaning **for ideal regeneration, efficiency decreases with pressure ratio**. This is because with $T_3 = T_5$, increasing $r$ increases $T_2$ (compressor exit) and decreases $T_5$ (turbine exit), making the heat addition smaller but also reducing the temperature difference available for work. **For real regeneration with effectiveness $\varepsilon < 1$**, the optimum pressure ratio is obtained by setting $d\eta_{th}/dr = 0$. For $\gamma = 1.4$, the **standard KTU result** (proven by full differentiation of the $\varepsilon$-dependent expression) is: $$r_{p,\text{opt}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{T_4}{T_1}}$$ The **maximum efficiency** at this optimum is: $$\eta_{th,\max} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon} \cdot \left[\dfrac{\sqrt{T_4/T_1} - 1}{T_4/T_1 - 1}\right]$$ This shows that **higher $T_4$** and **higher regenerator effectiveness** both push the maximum efficiency higher. --- **(b)** A regenerative gas turbine has a turbine inlet temperature of 1300 K, compressor inlet temperature of 300 K, and regenerator effectiveness of 0.7. Calculate the optimum pressure ratio and the corresponding maximum thermal efficiency. Take $\gamma = 1.4$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Optimum pressure ratio.** Using the standard KTU result: $$r_{p,\text{opt}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{T_4}{T_1}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1300}{300}} = \sqrt{4.333} = 2.082$$ **Step 2: Calculate isentropic temperatures at this $r_p$.** $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{0.2857} = 300 \times 2.082^{0.2857} = 300 \times 1.2741 = 382.23 \text{ K}$$ $$T_5 = T_4 / r_p^{0.2857} = 1300 / 1.2741 = 1020.34 \text{ K}$$ **Step 3: Find $T_3$ with effectiveness.** $$T_3 = T_2 + \varepsilon(T_5 - T_2) = 382.23 + 0.70 \times (1020.34 - 382.23)$$ $$T_3 = 382.23 + 0.70 \times 638.11 = 382.23 + 446.68 = 828.91 \text{ K}$$ **Step 4: Net work per kg of air.** $$W_C = c_p(T_2 - T_1) = 1.005 \times (382.23 - 300) = 1.005 \times 82.23 = 82.64 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$W_T = c_p(T_4 - T_5) = 1.005 \times (1300 - 1020.34) = 1.005 \times 279.66 = 281.06 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$W_{net} = 281.06 - 82.64 = 198.42 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 5: Heat input with regeneration.** $$q_{in} = c_p(T_4 - T_3) = 1.005 \times (1300 - 828.91) = 1.005 \times 471.09 = 473.45 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 6: Maximum thermal efficiency.** $$\eta_{th,\max} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{q_{in}} = \dfrac{198.42}{473.45} = 0.4191 = 41.91\%$$ **Verification note:** A simple Brayton cycle at the same temperatures (no regeneration) would give efficiency ≈ 23.45% — confirming that regeneration nearly **doubles** the cycle efficiency at this operating point. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Sign error in effectiveness formula:** Students often write $\varepsilon = (T_3 - T_2)/(T_2 - T_5)$, causing negative values. **Remember:** the denominator is always $(T_5 - T_2)$ because $T_5 > T_2$ for regeneration to work. > 2. **Confusing T5 and T6:** Many students mix up turbine exhaust temperature ($T_5$) with regenerator gas-outlet temperature ($T_6$). In efficiency formulas, use $T_5$ (turbine exhaust entering regenerator). > 3. **Forgetting the condition $T_5 > T_2$:** Without this condition, regeneration is meaningless. Some problems give a high $r_p$ where this fails — always check first. > 4. **Unit inconsistency:** Temperatures must be in **Kelvin** (K) for all calculations. Celsius will give wrong answers. > 5. **Wrong heat input:** Use $c_p(T_4 - T_3)$ **with** regeneration, not $c_p(T_4 - T_2)$ — this is the single most common error costing 2–3 marks. > 6. **Neglecting pressure drop in regenerator:** In real (non-ideal) calculations, the regenerator introduces ~3–5% pressure drop, which slightly reduces net work. KTU problems usually specify "ideal regenerator" or "no pressure drop" — read carefully. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam!):** - **Core concept:** Regeneration = recovering waste heat from turbine exhaust to preheat compressed air before combustion. - **Effectiveness formula:** $\varepsilon = (T_3 - T_2)/(T_5 - T_2)$ — always between 0 and 1. - **Heat input with regen:** $q_{in} = c_p(T_4 - T_3)$ — **less** than $c_p(T_4 - T_2)$. - **Work output:** **Unchanged** by regeneration (depends only on $r_p$ and $T_1, T_4$). - **Efficiency always increases** with regeneration (provided $T_5 > T_2$). - **Optimum pressure ratio:** $r_{p,\text{opt}} = \sqrt{T_4/T_1}$ for the regenerative cycle. - **Valid range:** $\varepsilon$ = 0.5 to 0.85 in practice; ideal limit is $\varepsilon = 1$ (i.e., $T_3 = T_5$). - **Key temperatures to memorize:** - $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{0.2857}$ (using $\gamma = 1.4$) - $T_5 = T_4 / r_p^{0.2857}$ - $T_3 = T_2 + \varepsilon(T_5 - T_2)$ - **Order of work formulas:** Always compute $W_C$ first, then $W_T$, then $W_{net} = W_T - W_C$. - **Two check points before writing final answer:** 1. Is $T_5 > T_2$? (If not, regeneration is invalid for that problem.) 2. Is $\eta_{th} < 1$? (Sanity check — efficiency above 100% indicates calculation error.) - **Efficiency gain typical range:** 4–10 percentage points (absolute) compared to a simple cycle. - **Limitations to mention in theory answers:** Material temperature limit, pressure ratio limit, additional pressure loss, bulk/cost, and pinch point analysis constraints.

topics.title: Intercooling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIET303 - THERMAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Intercooling ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Intercooling** in reciprocating air compressors is the deliberate process of **cooling the compressed gas between successive stages of a multi-stage compressor** by routing it through an external heat exchanger (the *intercooler*) before it enters the next compression cylinder. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (verbatim standard):** > *"Intercooling is the process of reducing the temperature of the compressed air between two successive stages of a multi-stage compressor back to the original inlet temperature (perfect intercooling) or close to it (imperfect intercooling), thereby reducing the total compression work and increasing the volumetric efficiency of the machine."* Mathematically, the governing intent of intercooling is to lower the gas temperature $T_2$ leaving the L.P. cylinder back down to $T_1$ (the atmospheric inlet temperature), so that the specific volume entering the H.P. cylinder is again maximized at $v_1$, the original inlet specific volume. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Imagine you are **squeezing a syringe filled with warm air** with your thumb. The harder you press, the hotter the air inside becomes. Now, if you pause, hold the syringe over an ice cube (cooling it back to room temperature), and then press again, the *second* push feels **easier** than the first one would have if you had kept pressing without pause. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "Climbing a Mountain in Two Lifts"** > Think of compressing a gas as "climbing a mountain of pressure." A single-stage compressor must climb the *entire* mountain in one exhausting push, generating a lot of heat (and therefore wasting a lot of energy as thermal dissipation). An intercooled two-stage compressor is like taking a **cable car to a mid-station (the intercooler pressure), resting, cooling down, and then taking a second cable car to the summit**. Less work per stage, less heat generated, less fuel consumed. The engineering payoff is quantified by two measurable benefits: - **Reduction in total compression work** → smaller motor, lower electricity bill. - **Increase in mass (kg) of air delivered per cycle** → denser intake at the H.P. cylinder → better tool performance downstream. --- ### 1.3 Classification of Intercooling | Type | Condition | When Used | |---|---|---| | **Perfect Intercooling** | $T_3 = T_1$ (gas cooled back to original inlet temperature) | Theoretical / ideal reference benchmark | | **Imperfect Intercooling** | $T_3 > T_1$ but $T_3 < T_2$ | Real-world industrial systems | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why we DO intercool at all (KTU 2024 Outcome CO3):** High delivery temperatures cause (a) excessive specific volume at H.P. cylinder inlet, (b) lubricant carbonization, (c) safety hazards, and (d) loss of mass flow rate.
