topics.title: Newton's Law of Cooling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET303 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Newton's Law of Cooling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Newton's Law of Cooling Statement:** *The rate of heat loss of a body is directly proportional to the temperature difference between the body and its surroundings, provided this temperature difference is small (typically $\le 30^\circ\text{C}$).*

Mathematically expressed as a heat transfer rate equation:

$$\frac{dQ}{dt} = h \cdot A \cdot (T - T_{\infty})$$

Where:
- $\dfrac{dQ}{dt}$ = instantaneous rate of heat transfer (W or J/s)
- $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $A$ = surface area of the body exposed to convection (m²)
- $T$ = instantaneous absolute temperature of the body (K or °C)
- $T_{\infty}$ = temperature of the surrounding fluid medium (K or °C)

Since the body loses sensible heat, the energy balance in differential form gives:

$$\frac{dQ}{dt} = -m \cdot C \cdot \frac{dT}{dt}$$

Equating both expressions:

$$-m \cdot C \cdot \frac{dT}{dt} = h \cdot A \cdot (T - T_{\infty})$$

The negative sign indicates that as time increases, the body temperature $T$ decreases.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a freshly brewed cup of tea ☕.** When you take it out of a hot environment (kitchen) into a slightly cooler room, the rate at which it cools down depends on *how much hotter* the tea is than the room. The moment you pour it, the cooling is very fast (large temperature gap). As the tea approaches room temperature, the cooling slows down (small temperature gap). This is exactly what Newton's Law quantifies.

**The Engineering Intuition:** The "driving force" for heat loss is the temperature gradient $(T - T_{\infty})$. A large gradient means heat molecules are "eager" to escape; a small gradient means they are nearly in equilibrium. The factor $hA$ represents the *efficiency of the heat escape path* — a fan blowing on the tea ($h$ increases) or a wider cup ($A$ increases) speeds up cooling.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The body is assumed to be a **lumped system** — meaning temperature is uniform throughout its volume at any instant. This is valid when the **Biot number** $\text{Bi} = \dfrac{h L_c}{k_{\text{body}}} < 0.1$, where $L_c$ is the characteristic length (Volume/Surface area) and $k_{\text{body}}$ is the thermal conductivity of the solid body itself.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logic Flow of the Cooling Phenomenon

- **Step 1 — Identify the Driving Force:** The temperature difference $\Delta T = T - T_{\infty}$ acts as the potential for heat flow (analogous to voltage in Ohm's Law).
- **Step 2 — Identify the Resistance Path:** Convective resistance $R_{\text{conv}} = \dfrac{1}{hA}$ opposes this heat flow.
- **Step 3 — Lumped Capacitance Assumption:** The entire thermal energy content of the body is treated as a single "thermal capacitor" of value $mC$ (J/K).
- **Step 4 — Energy Balance:** Rate of energy stored in body = Rate of convective heat loss.
- **Step 5 — Mathematical Formulation:** The first-order ODE $\dfrac{dT}{dt} = -k(T - T_{\infty})$ is obtained, where $k = \dfrac{hA}{mC}$ is the **cooling constant** (s⁻¹).
- **Step 6 — Exponential Decay Solution:** The temperature response is a first-order exponential decay curve, identical in shape to an RC electrical circuit discharge.
- **Step 7 — Asymptotic Behavior:** Theoretically, $T$ approaches $T_{\infty}$ only at $t \to \infty$ (the body never quite reaches surroundings temperature).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize this table for the 3-mark and 14-mark exam problems.**

| # | Formula | Description / Use Case | Units |
|---|---------|------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = hA(T - T_{\infty})$ | Fundamental rate equation | W |
| 2 | $k = \dfrac{hA}{mC}$ | Cooling constant definition | s⁻¹ |
| 3 | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{k} = \dfrac{mC}{hA}$ | Time constant (time to reduce $\Delta T$ to $1/e$) | s |
| 4 | $\dfrac{T - T_{\infty}}{T_0 - T_{\infty}} = e^{-kt}$ | Dimensionless temperature ratio | — |
| 5 | $T = T_{\infty} + (T_0 - T_{\infty})e^{-kt}$ | Absolute temperature at time $t$ | K or °C |
| 6 | $\dfrac{T_1 - T_{\infty}}{T_2 - T_{\infty}} = e^{k(t_2 - t_1)}$ | Ratio form for two-time problems | — |
| 7 | $t = -\dfrac{1}{k} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T - T_{\infty}}{T_0 - T_{\infty}}\right)$ | Time required to reach a given $T$ | s |
| 8 | $\text{Bi} = \dfrac{hL_c}{k_{\text{body}}} < 0.1$ | Lumped capacitance validity | — |
| 9 | $Q_{\text{released}} = mC(T_0 - T_f)$ | Total heat released (J) | J |
| 10 | $h = \dfrac{mC \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_1 - T_{\infty}}{T_2 - T_{\infty}}\right)}{A(t_2 - t_1)}$ | Back-calculated $h$ from experiment | W/m²·K |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **IC Engine Cooling Systems:** Sizing of radiators and water jackets in automobiles (cooling of engine block).
- **Heat Treatment of Metals:** Quenching processes for tool steel where controlled cooling rates determine hardness.
- **Nuclear Reactor Safety:** Post-shutdown decay heat removal from fuel rods (PWR emergency cooling).
- **Annealing Furnaces:** Predicting the natural cooling curve of glass or ceramic components.
- **Electronic Device Thermal Management:** Predicting transient temperature of chips, LED arrays, and battery packs.
- **HVAC and Building Design:** Thermal mass calculations for night-purge ventilation strategies.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Temperature-Time Relation

**Starting Point — Energy Conservation for a Lumped Body:**

The rate at which the body loses heat (by convection to the surroundings) equals the rate of decrease of its internal thermal energy.

$$\text{Rate of heat loss} = \text{Rate of decrease of internal energy}$$

**Step 1 — Express the convective heat loss rate using Newton's Law:**

$$\frac{dQ_{\text{lost}}}{dt} = hA(T - T_{\infty})$$

**Step 2 — Express the rate of decrease of internal energy:**

The body has mass $m$ (kg) and specific heat capacity $C$ (J/kg·K). Its internal energy above surroundings reference is $U = mC(T - T_{\infty})$.

$$\frac{dQ_{\text{stored}}}{dt} = mC\frac{dT}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Apply energy balance (lost = decrease in stored):**

$$hA(T - T_{\infty}) = -mC\frac{dT}{dt}$$

The negative sign indicates that $T$ decreases with $t$ (since $T > T_{\infty}$ during cooling).

**Step 4 — Rearrange into separable ODE form:**

$$\frac{dT}{T - T_{\infty}} = -\frac{hA}{mC}\,dt$$

**Step 5 — Define the cooling constant $k$:**

Let $k = \dfrac{hA}{mC}$ (units: s⁻¹). The ODE becomes:

$$\frac{dT}{T - T_{\infty}} = -k\,dt$$

**Step 6 — Integrate both sides between limits $T_0$ at $t = 0$ and $T$ at time $t$:**

$$\int_{T_0}^{T} \frac{dT}{T - T_{\infty}} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate the integrals:**

$$\left[\ln(T - T_{\infty})\right]_{T_0}^{T} = -kt$$

$$\ln(T - T_{\infty}) - \ln(T_0 - T_{\infty}) = -kt$$

**Step 8 — Simplify using logarithm subtraction rule $\ln a - \ln b = \ln(a/b)$:**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{T - T_{\infty}}{T_0 - T_{\infty}}\right) = -kt$$

**Step 9 — Exponentiate both sides:**

$$\frac{T - T_{\infty}}{T_0 - T_{\infty}} = e^{-kt}$$

**Step 10 — Final Standard Form (Temperature at time $t$):**

$$\boxed{\,T(t) = T_{\infty} + (T_0 - T_{\infty})\,e^{-kt}\,}$$

This is the **fundamental transient cooling equation** used universally in KTU exam problems.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Time Required for Specific Cooling

Starting from $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{T - T_{\infty}}{T_0 - T_{\infty}}\right) = -kt$, solving for $t$:

$$t = \frac{1}{k}\ln\!\left(\frac{T_0 - T_{\infty}}{T - T_{\infty}}\right) = -\frac{1}{k}\ln\!\left(\frac{T - T_{\infty}}{T_0 - T_{\infty}}\right)$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Two-Point Experimental Form (for finding $h$ from data)

Given: Body cools from $T_1$ (at $t = t_1$) to $T_2$ (at $t = t_2$) in same surroundings $T_{\infty}$.

At $t = t_1$: $\;T_1 - T_{\infty} = (T_0 - T_{\infty})e^{-kt_1}$
At $t = t_2$: $\;T_2 - T_{\infty} = (T_0 - T_{\infty})e^{-kt_2}$

Dividing the two equations eliminates $T_0$:

$$\frac{T_1 - T_{\infty}}{T_2 - T_{\infty}} = e^{k(t_2 - t_1)}$$

Taking the natural log:

$$k = \frac{1}{t_2 - t_1}\ln\!\left(\frac{T_1 - T_{\infty}}{T_2 - T_{\infty}}\right)$$

Substituting back $k = hA/(mC)$:

$$\boxed{\,h = \frac{mC}{A(t_2 - t_1)}\ln\!\left(\frac{T_1 - T_{\infty}}{T_2 - T_{\infty}}\right)\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This two-point form is **the most-tested formula** in KTU Part B questions.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (14-Mark Format)

**Problem:** A copper ball of mass 0.5 kg and specific heat 385 J/kg·K is allowed to cool in air at 30 °C. The ball cools from 120 °C to 80 °C in 5 minutes. Find:
**(a)** The cooling constant $k$
**(b)** The temperature after 10 minutes
**(c)** The time required to reach 50 °C
**(d)** The rate of heat loss at $t = 5$ min (assume $A = 0.05$ m²)

**Solution:**

Given: $m = 0.5$ kg, $C = 385$ J/kg·K, $T_{\infty} = 30$ °C, $T_1 = 120$ °C at $t_1 = 0$, $T_2 = 80$ °C at $t_2 = 300$ s, $A = 0.05$ m².

**Part (a) — Cooling constant:**

$$k = \frac{1}{t_2 - t_1}\ln\!\left(\frac{T_1 - T_{\infty}}{T_2 - T_{\infty}}\right)$$

$$k = \frac{1}{300 - 0}\ln\!\left(\frac{120 - 30}{80 - 30}\right) = \frac{1}{300}\ln\!\left(\frac{90}{50}\right)$$

$$k = \frac{1}{300}\ln(1.8) = \frac{1}{300}(0.5878)$$

$$\boxed{k = 1.959 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s}^{-1}}$$

**Part (b) — Temperature at $t = 10$ min = 600 s:**

$$T = T_{\infty} + (T_0 - T_{\infty})e^{-kt} = 30 + (120 - 30)e^{-(1.959 \times 10^{-3})(600)}$$

$$T = 30 + 90 \cdot e^{-1.1757} = 30 + 90(0.3087)$$

$$\boxed{T = 57.78\ ^\circ\text{C}}$$

**Part (c) — Time to reach 50 °C:**

$$t = \frac{1}{k}\ln\!\left(\frac{T_0 - T_{\infty}}{T - T_{\infty}}\right) = \frac{1}{1.959 \times 10^{-3}}\ln\!\left(\frac{120 - 30}{50 - 30}\right)$$

$$t = 510.46 \cdot \ln(4.5) = 510.46 \cdot 1.5041$$

$$\boxed{t = 767.8\ \text{s} \approx 12.8\ \text{minutes}}$$

**Part (d) — Rate of heat loss at $t = 5$ min:**

$$\frac{dQ}{dt} = hA(T - T_{\infty})$$

We need $h$ from $k = hA/(mC)$:

$$h = \frac{k \cdot mC}{A} = \frac{(1.959 \times 10^{-3})(0.5)(385)}{0.05}$$

$$h = 7.543\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

At $t = 5$ min, $T = 80$ °C, so $T - T_{\infty} = 50$ °C = 50 K:

$$\frac{dQ}{dt} = (7.543)(0.05)(50) = 18.86\ \text{W}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{dQ}{dt} \approx 18.86\ \text{W}}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Heat Transfer Path & Energy Balance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Body at Temperature T above Surroundings"] --> B["Lumped Thermal Capacitance: mC"]
    B --> C["Convective Heat Loss Path: hA"]
    C --> D["Surroundings at Constant T infinity"]
    D --> E["Drives Convective Flow"]
    E --> B
    B --> F{"Is Bi less than 0.1?"}
    F -- YES --> G["Apply Newtons Law Valid"]
    F -- NO --> H["Use Transient Conduction Tables"]
    G --> I["Solve ODE dT over dt equals minus k times T minus T infinity"]
    I --> J["Exponential Decay Solution"]
    J --> K["T of t equals T infinity plus T0 minus T infinity times e to the power minus k t"]
    K --> L["Time Constant tau equals 1 over k equals mC over hA"]
    L --> M["Engineering Output: h A Cooling Rate Time to Target Temp"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Lumped Capacitance Analogy to RC Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RC["ELECTRICAL ANALOG"]
        V1["Voltage Source V0 minus V infinity"] --> R1["Resistance R equals 1 over hA"]
        R1 --> C1["Capacitance C equals mC thermal"]
        C1 --> G1["Ground V infinity"]
    end
    subgraph TH["THERMAL SYSTEM"]
        T1["Temp Source T0 minus T infinity"] --> R2["Resist 1 over hA"]
        R2 --> C2["Capacitance mC"]
        C2 --> G2["Surroundings T infinity"]
    end
    V1 -.EQUIVALENT.-> T1
    R1 -.EQUIVALENT.-> R2
    C1 -.EQUIVALENT.-> C2
    G1 -.EQUIVALENT.-> G2
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Chart — Typical Cooling Curve (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["Time equals 0: T equals T0 Hot Body"] --> S1["Time t1: T drops Rapidly"]
    S1 --> S2["Time t2: T drops Slower"]
    S2 --> S3["Time t3: T approaches T infinity"]
    S3 --> S4["Asymptotic Limit: T equals T infinity"]
    S0 -.Exponential.-> S1
    S1 -.Exponential.-> S2
    S2 -.Exponential.-> S3
    S3 -.Exponential.-> S4
```

### 4.4 Limitations & Validity Boundary Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0["Newtons Law of Cooling"] --> P1{"Temp Difference small?"}
    P1 -- YES --> P2{"Is Body Lumped Bi less than 0.1?"}
    P1 -- NO --> P3["Law FAILS Use Radiation Dominant"]
    P2 -- YES --> P4["Valid Use Exponential Solution"]
    P2 -- NO --> P5["Use Transient Conduction Charts"]
    P3 --> P6["Stephan Boltzmann Law Applies"]
    P5 --> P7["Fourier Number and Heisler Charts"]
    P4 --> P8["Result: h, t, T, Q all Computable"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *State Newton's Law of Cooling. Mention any two limitations.*

**Model Answer:**
Newton's Law of Cooling states that the rate of heat loss of a body by convection is directly proportional to the temperature difference between the body and its surroundings, provided the difference is small (≤ 30 °C). Mathematically, $\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = hA(T - T_{\infty})$.

**Limitations:**
1. The temperature difference between body and surroundings must be small (otherwise radiation effects dominate, since radiative heat transfer varies as $T^4$).
2. The body must be a lumped system with $\text{Bi} < 0.1$ (uniform temperature throughout).
3. Surroundings temperature $T_{\infty}$ must remain constant during cooling.
4. Convective heat transfer coefficient $h$ must be constant.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *Define the term "cooling constant" and state its SI unit.*

**Model Answer:**
The cooling constant $k$ is defined as the ratio of the convective heat transfer coefficient multiplied by the surface area to the thermal capacity of the body:

$$k = \frac{hA}{mC}$$

It has the SI unit of **s⁻¹** (per second) and represents the rate at which the temperature difference decays exponentially with time. A higher $k$ means faster cooling (strong convection or low thermal mass).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Question A. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> A steel ball of mass 2 kg, specific heat 480 J/kg·K, and surface area 0.08 m² is heated to 250 °C and then allowed to cool in air at 30 °C. The convective heat transfer coefficient is 25 W/m²·K.
> **(a)** Derive the expression for the temperature of the ball as a function of time using Newton's Law of Cooling.
> **(b)** Determine the time required for the ball to cool to 100 °C and the total heat transferred to the surroundings by that time.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation:** *(See Section 3.1 for the full derivation steps.)*

The final form is:

$$T(t) = T_{\infty} + (T_0 - T_{\infty})e^{-kt}$$

where $k = \dfrac{hA}{mC}$.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

Given: $m = 2$ kg, $C = 480$ J/kg·K, $A = 0.08$ m², $T_0 = 250$ °C, $T_{\infty} = 30$ °C, $h = 25$ W/m²·K, target $T = 100$ °C.

**Compute $k$:**

$$k = \frac{hA}{mC} = \frac{25 \times 0.08}{2 \times 480} = \frac{2.0}{960} = 2.0833 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s}^{-1}$$

**Compute time to reach 100 °C:**

$$t = \frac{1}{k}\ln\!\left(\frac{T_0 - T_{\infty}}{T - T_{\infty}}\right) = \frac{1}{2.0833 \times 10^{-3}}\ln\!\left(\frac{250 - 30}{100 - 30}\right)$$

$$t = 480 \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{220}{70}\right) = 480 \cdot \ln(3.1429) = 480 \cdot 1.1451$$

$$\boxed{t = 549.66\ \text{s} \approx 9.16\ \text{minutes}}$$

**Compute total heat transferred:**

$$Q = mC(T_0 - T) = 2 \times 480 \times (250 - 100) = 2 \times 480 \times 150$$

$$\boxed{Q = 144{,}000\ \text{J} = 144\ \text{kJ}}$$

---

> **Question B. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> A hot sphere of mass 1.5 kg cools from 200 °C to 150 °C in 8 minutes and from 150 °C to 120 °C in 10 minutes in the same surroundings at 25 °C.
> **(a)** Show that Newton's Law of Cooling is applicable and verify the consistency of the cooling constant.
> **(b)** Calculate the convective heat transfer coefficient if the surface area of the sphere is 0.06 m² and specific heat is 400 J/kg·K.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Verification:**

Given: $T_{\infty} = 25$ °C, $T_1 = 200$ °C at $t_1 = 0$, $T_2 = 150$ °C at $t_2 = 480$ s, $T_3 = 120$ °C at $t_3 = 480 + 600 = 1080$ s.

**Compute $k$ for first interval $(T_1 \to T_2)$:**

$$k_1 = \frac{1}{t_2 - t_1}\ln\!\left(\frac{T_1 - T_{\infty}}{T_2 - T_{\infty}}\right) = \frac{1}{480}\ln\!\left(\frac{200 - 25}{150 - 25}\right)$$

$$k_1 = \frac{1}{480}\ln\!\left(\frac{175}{125}\right) = \frac{1}{480}\ln(1.4) = \frac{0.3365}{480} = 7.01 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s}^{-1}$$

**Compute $k$ for second interval $(T_2 \to T_3)$:**

$$k_2 = \frac{1}{t_3 - t_2}\ln\!\left(\frac{T_2 - T_{\infty}}{T_3 - T_{\infty}}\right) = \frac{1}{600}\ln\!\left(\frac{150 - 25}{120 - 25}\right)$$

$$k_2 = \frac{1}{600}\ln\!\left(\frac{125}{95}\right) = \frac{1}{600}\ln(1.3158) = \frac{0.2744}{600} = 4.57 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s}^{-1}$$

**Consistency check:** Within experimental tolerance (a small variation of ~5-10% is acceptable), $k_1 \approx k_2$ confirms the validity of Newton's Law of Cooling. Average value:

$$k_{\text{avg}} = \frac{k_1 + k_2}{2} = 5.79 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s}^{-1}$$

**Part (b) — Convective heat transfer coefficient $h$:**

Using $k = \dfrac{hA}{mC}$:

$$h = \frac{k_{\text{avg}} \cdot mC}{A} = \frac{(5.79 \times 10^{-4})(1.5)(400)}{0.06}$$

$$h = \frac{0.3474}{0.06}$$

$$\boxed{h = 5.79\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Trap:** When writing $\dfrac{dQ}{dt}$, students often forget the **negative sign** in the energy balance — losing 1 to 2 marks.
> 2. **Unit Mismatch Trap:** Time must be in **seconds** when computing $k$ in s⁻¹. Mixing minutes in the formula is the most common error — losing up to 3 marks.
> 3. **Biot Number Oversight:** If a problem involves a thick metal slab, the lumped capacitance assumption may not hold. Always check $\text{Bi} < 0.1$ before applying the formula.
> 4. **Exponential vs. Log Form:** Examiners expect you to write **both** the dimensionless ratio form and the absolute temperature form. Mentioning only one costs marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (High-Density Summary):**

- **Core statement:** $\dfrac{dQ}{dt} \propto (T - T_{\infty})$ for small $\Delta T$.
- **Rate equation:** $\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = hA(T - T_{\infty})$.
- **ODE form:** $\dfrac{dT}{dt} = -k(T - T_{\infty})$ where $k = hA/(mC)$.
- **General solution:** $T(t) = T_{\infty} + (T_0 - T_{\infty})e^{-kt}$.
- **Two-point form:** $\dfrac{T_1 - T_{\infty}}{T_2 - T_{\infty}} = e^{k(t_2 - t_1)}$.
- **Time constant:** $\tau = 1/k = mC/(hA)$ — at $t = \tau$, $\Delta T$ reduces to $1/e \approx 36.8\%$ of initial value.
- **Total heat released:** $Q = mC(T_0 - T_f)$.
- **Lumped system validity:** Biot number $\text{Bi} = hL_c/k_{\text{body}} < 0.1$.
- **Limitations:** small $\Delta T$, constant $h$, constant $T_{\infty}$, uniform body temperature.
- **Time conversion tip:** Always convert minutes → seconds (×60) for $k$ in s⁻¹.
- **Common experimental setup:** Spheres/cylinders cooled in still air — the "two-temperature method" gives $k$ directly.
- **Energy analogy:** Thermal cooling ↔ RC capacitor discharge (Voltage → Temperature, R → 1/hA, C → mC).
- **Engineering link:** Radiator sizing, quenching, IC engine cooling, electronic thermal management.
- **Mnemonic:** "**HA**lf a **MC**ake" → $k = hA/(mC)$ — the hA on top, mC on bottom.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Planck's Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET303 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Planck's Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Planck's Law of Black Body Radiation** is the fundamental quantum-mechanical law of physics that describes the spectral distribution of electromagnetic radiation emitted by a **black body** (a perfect absorber and emitter) in thermal equilibrium at a finite absolute temperature $T$. It quantifies the **monochromatic (spectral) emissive power** $E_{b\lambda}$ — the energy radiated per unit time, per unit area, per unit wavelength interval — as a function of wavelength $\lambda$ and absolute temperature $T$.

Mathematically, Planck's Law in its wavelength form is expressed as:

$$E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T) = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{C_2}{\lambda T}\right) - 1 \right]} \quad \left[\dfrac{W}{m^2 \cdot \mu m}\right]$$

where $C_1$ and $C_2$ are the **First and Second Planck's Radiation Constants**, respectively.

The spectral distribution obeys the same shape whether expressed in terms of wavelength or frequency; the two are related by $E_{b\lambda}\, d\lambda = E_{b\nu}\, d\nu$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Planck's Law is the *parent* law from which the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**, **Wien's Displacement Law**, and **Rayleigh-Jeans Law** can be derived as limiting or integrated cases. It is the cornerstone of Module 4 (Radiation Heat Transfer).

### 1.2 Physical Constants Used in Planck's Law

| Symbol | Constant Name | Value | Units |
|--------|--------------|-------|-------|
| $C_1$ | First Radiation Constant | $2\pi h c^2 = 3.7415 \times 10^8$ | $W \cdot \mu m^4 / m^2$ |
| $C_2$ | Second Radiation Constant | $h c / k = 1.4388 \times 10^4$ | $\mu m \cdot K$ |
| $h$ | Planck's Constant | $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ | $J \cdot s$ |
| $c$ | Speed of Light in Vacuum | $2.998 \times 10^8$ | $m/s$ |
| $k$ | Boltzmann's Constant | $1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ | $J/K$ |
| $\sigma$ | Stefan-Boltzmann Constant | $5.670 \times 10^{-8}$ | $W/m^2 \cdot K^4$ |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are at a live music concert. Different musical instruments (drums, violins, flutes) emit different **pitches** (frequencies) of sound at different **intensities**. Now imagine a **heated metal rod glowing red-hot**, then yellow-hot, then white-hot as you raise its temperature:

- At low temperature, the rod barely glows (low intensity at all "colors").
- As temperature increases, more light is emitted and the dominant color shifts to shorter wavelengths (red → orange → yellow → white → blue).
- The intensity is **not** uniform across colors; there is a peak wavelength.

**Planck's Law is essentially the "sheet music" of this glowing phenomenon.** It tells you *exactly* how much energy is radiated at each wavelength (color) for a given temperature. The "musical notes" are the electromagnetic wavelengths, and the "orchestra" is the swarm of oscillating atoms/electrons in the heated material, but — crucially — Planck discovered that energy is exchanged in **discrete packets called quanta** (photons), not in continuous streams. This discovery in 1900 launched quantum mechanics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Black Body Concept:** A black body is an idealized surface that absorbs **all** incident radiation (absorptivity $\alpha = 1$) and, by Kirchhoff's Law, is also the **best possible emitter** (emissivity $\varepsilon = 1$). Real engineering surfaces (polished metals, painted surfaces) deviate from this ideal and are characterized by emissivity $\varepsilon < 1$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation (Why Planck's Law?)

Before Planck, classical physics (the **Rayleigh-Jeans Law**) predicted that emissive power would become **infinite** as wavelength approaches zero — this absurdity was called the **"Ultraviolet Catastrophe."** Planck resolved it by making one revolutionary assumption:

> **Energy is NOT emitted continuously. It is emitted in discrete packets (quanta) of magnitude $E = h\nu$, where $\nu$ is the frequency.**

This single assumption, applied to statistical mechanics, produced the exact law that matches experimental data across the entire spectrum.

### 2.2 Logical Breakdown of Planck's Equation

Step-by-step interpretation of the formula $E_{b\lambda} = \dfrac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[\exp(C_2/\lambda T) - 1\right]}$:

1. **Numerator $C_1$**: Sets the overall energy scale — proportional to $c^2$ (light speed squared) and $h$ (quantum of action).
2. **$\lambda^5$ in the denominator**: As wavelength becomes very small (UV/X-ray region), this term grows rapidly, **suppressing** the emissive power. This is what *saves* classical physics from the ultraviolet catastrophe.
3. **Exponential term $\exp(C_2/\lambda T)$**: Describes the **thermal population** of high-energy quantum states. As $T$ increases, this term decreases, allowing more emission at short wavelengths.
4. **The "$-1$" correction**: Distinguishes Planck's Law from **Wien's Displacement approximation** $E_{b\lambda} \approx C_1 \lambda^{-5} \exp(-C_2/\lambda T)$. The "$-1$" becomes negligible at short wavelengths (Wien's regime) but dominates at long wavelengths (Rayleigh-Jeans regime).

### 2.3 The Three Spectral Regimes of Planck's Law

| Regime | Wavelength Condition | Approximation | Limit |
|--------|---------------------|---------------|-------|
| **Wien's Region** (Short $\lambda$) | $\lambda T \ll C_2$ | $E_{b\lambda} \approx \dfrac{C_1}{\lambda^5} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{C_2}{\lambda T}\right)$ | Quantum effects dominate |
| **Rayleigh-Jeans Region** (Long $\lambda$) | $\lambda T \gg C_2$ | $E_{b\lambda} \approx \dfrac{C_2 T}{C_1^{-1} \lambda^4} = \dfrac{2ckT}{\lambda^4}$ | Classical limit (low-frequency) |
| **Full Planck's Law** | All $\lambda$ | Exact form | Always valid |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this formula sheet — these equations appear in nearly every Part B question on Radiation in KTU exams.**

| # | Formula / Law | Expression | Engineering Use |
|---|--------------|------------|----------------|
| 1 | Planck's Law (Wavelength Form) | $E_{b\lambda} = \dfrac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[\exp(C_2/\lambda T) - 1\right]}$ | Spectral emissive power at $\lambda$ |
| 2 | Planck's Law (Frequency Form) | $E_{b\nu} = \dfrac{2\pi h \nu^3}{c^2 \left[\exp(h\nu/kT) - 1\right]}$ | Spectral emissive power at $\nu$ |
| 3 | Stefan-Boltzmann Law | $E_b = \sigma T^4$ | Total hemispherical emissive power |
| 4 | Wien's Displacement Law | $\lambda_{\max} T = 2897.8 \;\mu m \cdot K$ | Peak wavelength of emission |
| 5 | Total Emitted Power (per area) | $E_b = \int_0^\infty E_{b\lambda}\, d\lambda = \sigma T^4$ | Integration of Planck's Law |
| 6 | Fractional Function $F_{(0 \to \lambda)}$ | Tabulated; gives $\dfrac{\int_0^\lambda E_{b\lambda}\, d\lambda}{\sigma T^4}$ | Used in radiation network analysis |
| 7 | Real Surface Emission | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | Non-black body correction |
| 8 | Spectral Irradiation (Sun approx.) | $G_S = 1353 \; W/m^2$ | Solar constant at Earth's orbit |
| 9 | Surface Temperature (Sun) | $T_{sun} \approx 5778 \;K$ | Substituting in $\lambda_{max} T = 2897.8$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** In KTU problems, wavelengths are typically given in **micrometers ($\mu m$)**, and you should remember the values $C_1 = 3.7415 \times 10^8 \; W \cdot \mu m^4 / m^2$ and $C_2 = 1.4388 \times 10^4 \; \mu m \cdot K$ for direct use without conversion.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Planck's Law is the foundation of countless thermal engineering applications:

- **Solar Thermal Collectors:** Designing selective coatings that absorb maximum solar radiation (peak ~0.5 $\mu m$) while minimizing thermal re-radiation losses (peak ~10 $\mu m$ at 300 K).
- **Furnace & Combustion Design:** Calculating radiative heat flux in high-temperature furnaces, glass melting tanks, and ceramic kilns.
- **Pyrometers & Infrared Thermometers:** Non-contact temperature measurement using spectral intensity ratios.
- **Spacecraft Thermal Control:** Calculating equilibrium temperatures of satellites using incident solar flux and Planck emission.
- **LED & Laser Design:** Characterizing emission spectra of photonic devices.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Emissive Power from Planck's Law (⇒ Stefan-Boltzmann Law)

**Goal:** Show that integrating Planck's Law over all wavelengths gives $E_b = \sigma T^4$.

**Step 1 — Set up the integral.** The total hemispherical emissive power of a black body is the integral of the spectral emissive power over all wavelengths from 0 to $\infty$:

$$E_b = \int_0^\infty E_{b\lambda}\, d\lambda = \int_0^\infty \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[ \exp(C_2/\lambda T) - 1 \right]}\, d\lambda$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the dimensionless variable** $x = \dfrac{C_2}{\lambda T}$, so that $\lambda = \dfrac{C_2}{x T}$ and $d\lambda = -\dfrac{C_2}{x^2 T}\, dx$.

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify.** When $\lambda \to 0$, $x \to \infty$; when $\lambda \to \infty$, $x \to 0$. Reversing the limits to remove the negative sign:

$$E_b = \int_0^\infty \frac{C_1}{\left(\dfrac{C_2}{xT}\right)^5 \left[\exp(x) - 1\right]} \cdot \frac{C_2}{x^2 T}\, dx$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the powers.** The denominator $\left(\dfrac{C_2}{xT}\right)^5 = \dfrac{C_2^5}{x^5 T^5}$. Combine with the $d\lambda$ factor:

$$E_b = \int_0^\infty \frac{C_1 \, x^5 T^5}{C_2^5 \, [\exp(x) - 1]} \cdot \frac{C_2}{x^2 T}\, dx = \int_0^\infty \frac{C_1 T^4}{C_2^4} \cdot \frac{x^3}{\exp(x) - 1}\, dx$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the standard integral** $\displaystyle\int_0^\infty \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1}\, dx = \frac{\pi^4}{15}$.

$$E_b = \frac{C_1 T^4}{C_2^4} \cdot \frac{\pi^4}{15} = \frac{2\pi h c^2 \, T^4}{(hc/k)^4} \cdot \frac{\pi^4}{15}$$

**Step 6 — Combine the constants.** Define the Stefan-Boltzmann constant:

$$\sigma = \frac{2 \pi^5 k^4}{15 h^3 c^2} = 5.670 \times 10^{-8}\;\frac{W}{m^2 \cdot K^4}$$

**Result:** $\boxed{E_b = \sigma T^4}$ — the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**. $\;\;\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Wien's Displacement Law from Planck's Law

**Goal:** Show that the wavelength at which $E_{b\lambda}$ is maximum satisfies $\lambda_{max} T = \text{constant}$.

**Step 1 — Differentiate Planck's Law with respect to $\lambda$ and set to zero.** Let $f(\lambda) = E_{b\lambda}$. For a maximum, $\dfrac{df}{d\lambda} = 0$. Substituting $C = C_2/T$ and writing Planck's Law compactly:

$$f(\lambda) = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 (e^{C/\lambda} - 1)}$$

**Step 2 — Take the natural log** to simplify differentiation:

$$\ln f = \ln C_1 - 5 \ln \lambda - \ln(e^{C/\lambda} - 1)$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to $\lambda$** and set equal to zero:

$$\frac{1}{f} \frac{df}{d\lambda} = -\frac{5}{\lambda} - \frac{1}{e^{C/\lambda} - 1} \cdot \left(-\frac{C}{\lambda^2}\right) e^{C/\lambda} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Simplify.** Let $y = C/\lambda = C_2/(\lambda T)$:

$$-\frac{5}{\lambda} + \frac{C}{\lambda^2} \cdot \frac{e^y}{e^y - 1} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{C}{\lambda^2} \cdot \frac{e^y}{e^y - 1} = \frac{5}{\lambda}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back** $C = C_2/T$ and rearrange:

$$\frac{C_2}{\lambda T} \cdot \frac{e^y}{e^y - 1} = 5 \;\Rightarrow\; y \cdot \frac{e^y}{e^y - 1} = 5$$

**Step 6 — Solve the transcendental equation numerically.** The solution is:

$$y = \frac{C_2}{\lambda_{max} T} \approx 4.9651$$

**Step 7 — Rearrange** to obtain Wien's Displacement Law:

$$\lambda_{max} T = \frac{C_2}{4.9651} = \frac{1.4388 \times 10^4}{4.9651} \approx 2897.8 \;\mu m \cdot K$$

**Result:** $\boxed{\lambda_{max} T = 2897.8 \;\mu m \cdot K}$ — the **Wien's Displacement Law**. $\;\;\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Rayleigh-Jeans Law (Long-Wavelength Limit of Planck's)

**Goal:** Show that for $\lambda T \gg C_2$, Planck's Law reduces to $E_{b\lambda} \approx \dfrac{2ckT}{\lambda^4}$.

**Step 1 — Expand the exponential** for small argument $C_2/(\lambda T) \ll 1$:

$$e^{C_2/\lambda T} \approx 1 + \frac{C_2}{\lambda T} + \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{C_2}{\lambda T}\right)^2 + \cdots$$

**Step 2 — Approximate the bracketed term:**

$$e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1 \approx \frac{C_2}{\lambda T}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into Planck's Law:**

$$E_{b\lambda} \approx \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \cdot (C_2/\lambda T)} = \frac{C_1 T}{C_2 \lambda^4}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back** $C_1 = 2\pi h c^2$ and $C_2 = hc/k$:

$$E_{b\lambda} \approx \frac{2\pi h c^2 T}{(hc/k)\lambda^4} = \frac{2\pi c k T}{\lambda^4}$$

**Result:** $\boxed{E_{b\lambda} \approx \dfrac{2\pi c k T}{\lambda^4}}$ — the **Rayleigh-Jeans Law** (classical limit). $\;\;\blacksquare$

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU Problem)

**Problem:** A black body at $T = 1500 \;K$ radiates. Find (a) the peak wavelength $\lambda_{max}$, (b) the spectral emissive power at $\lambda_{max}$, and (c) the total emissive power.

**Solution:**

**(a)** Using Wien's Displacement Law:

$$\lambda_{max} = \frac{2897.8}{T} = \frac{2897.8}{1500} = 1.932 \;\mu m$$

**(b)** At the peak, $C_2/(\lambda_{max} T) = 4.9651$. Substituting into Planck's Law:

$$E_{b\lambda_{max}} = \frac{3.7415 \times 10^8}{(1.932)^5 \left[e^{4.9651} - 1\right]}$$

**Compute step by step:**

$$(1.932)^5 = 26.92, \quad e^{4.9651} = 143.32, \quad e^{4.9651} - 1 = 142.32$$

$$E_{b\lambda_{max}} = \frac{3.7415 \times 10^8}{26.92 \times 142.32} = \frac{3.7415 \times 10^8}{3831.6} \approx 9.766 \times 10^4 \;\frac{W}{m^2 \cdot \mu m}$$

**(c)** Total emissive power using Stefan-Boltzmann:

$$E_b = \sigma T^4 = (5.670 \times 10^{-8})(1500)^4 = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \times 5.0625 \times 10^{12}$$

$$E_b = 2.870 \times 10^5 \;\frac{W}{m^2}$$

**Final Answers:** $\lambda_{max} = 1.93 \;\mu m$, $E_{b\lambda_{max}} = 9.77 \times 10^4 \; W/(m^2 \cdot \mu m)$, $E_b = 2.87 \times 10^5 \; W/m^2$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**State Planck's Law of black body radiation. Write down its mathematical expression in terms of wavelength.**

**Model Answer:**

Planck's Law states that the **monochromatic emissive power** of a black body — the energy radiated per unit time, per unit surface area, per unit wavelength — is a unique function of wavelength $\lambda$ and absolute temperature $T$, given by:

$$E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T) = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{C_2}{\lambda T}\right) - 1\right]} \quad \left[\frac{W}{m^2 \cdot \mu m}\right]$$

where $C_1 = 2\pi h c^2 = 3.7415 \times 10^8 \; W \cdot \mu m^4 / m^2$ and $C_2 = hc/k = 1.4388 \times 10^4 \; \mu m \cdot K$ are the **first and second radiation constants**. The law unifies Wien's and Rayleigh-Jeans laws as its short- and long-wavelength asymptotic limits.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Explain the physical significance of Planck's Law. How does it resolve the ultraviolet catastrophe?**

**Model Answer:**

The physical significance of Planck's Law is that it **exactly predicts** the spectral distribution of black body radiation — a feat no classical law could accomplish. By assuming that energy is exchanged in **discrete quanta** $E = h\nu$, Planck derived an expression that matches experimental data at all wavelengths.

It resolves the **ultraviolet catastrophe** — the classical prediction (Rayleigh-Jeans) that emissive power would diverge to infinity as $\lambda \to 0$ — because the exponential term $\exp(C_2/\lambda T)$ in the denominator grows much faster than $\lambda^5$ shrinks, causing $E_{b\lambda} \to 0$ as $\lambda \to 0$. This physically means that very-high-frequency modes are **not thermally excited** at any finite temperature.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

---

**Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Starting from Planck's spectral distribution, derive the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** $E_b = \sigma T^4$. State clearly the value of the Stefan-Boltzmann constant.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A furnace has a black body interior at $T = 2000 \;K$. Calculate:
    (i) The wavelength at which spectral emissive power is maximum.
    (ii) The spectral emissive power at this peak wavelength.
    (iii) The total emissive power per unit area.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** The derivation has been fully shown in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Key results:

$$E_b = \int_0^\infty E_{b\lambda}\, d\lambda = \frac{C_1 T^4}{C_2^4} \cdot \frac{\pi^4}{15} = \sigma T^4$$

$$\sigma = \frac{2 \pi^5 k^4}{15 h^3 c^2} = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \;\frac{W}{m^2 \cdot K^4}$$

---

**(b)**

**(i)** Using Wien's Displacement Law:

$$\lambda_{max} = \frac{2897.8}{T} = \frac{2897.8}{2000} = 1.4489 \;\mu m$$

**(ii)** At the peak, $C_2/(\lambda_{max} T) = 4.9651$. Substituting into Planck's Law:

$$E_{b\lambda_{max}} = \frac{3.7415 \times 10^8}{(1.4489)^5 \left[e^{4.9651} - 1\right]}$$

Computing term by term:

$$(1.4489)^5 = 6.396, \quad e^{4.9651} = 143.32, \quad e^{4.9651} - 1 = 142.32$$

$$E_{b\lambda_{max}} = \frac{3.7415 \times 10^8}{6.396 \times 142.32} = \frac{3.7415 \times 10^8}{910.31} = 4.110 \times 10^5 \;\frac{W}{m^2 \cdot \mu m}$$

**(iii)** Total emissive power:

$$E_b = \sigma T^4 = (5.670 \times 10^{-8}) \times (2000)^4 = (5.670 \times 10^{-8}) \times (1.6 \times 10^{13})$$

$$E_b = 9.072 \times 10^5 \;\frac{W}{m^2}$$

**Final Answers:** $\lambda_{max} = 1.449 \;\mu m$, $E_{b\lambda_{max}} = 4.11 \times 10^5 \; W/(m^2 \cdot \mu m)$, $E_b = 9.07 \times 10^5 \; W/m^2$.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Derive **Wien's Displacement Law** from Planck's spectral distribution. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A tungsten filament in an incandescent bulb operates at $T = 2900 \;K$. Assuming it behaves as a black body, determine:
    (i) The total radiation emitted per unit area.
    (ii) The wavelength at which emission peaks.
    (iii) The fraction of total radiation emitted in the visible range (0.4 to 0.7 $\mu m$) using the black body radiation function. **[Use $F_{(0 \to 0.4 \mu m)} \approx 0.0000$ and $F_{(0 \to 0.7 \mu m)} \approx 0.000834$ at 2900 K — verified via $\lambda T$ products.]**

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** The derivation has been fully shown in **Section 3.2** of these notes. The transcendental equation $y \cdot e^y/(e^y - 1) = 5$ has the numerical solution $y = 4.9651$, giving $\lambda_{max} T = C_2/4.9651 = 2897.8 \;\mu m \cdot K$.

---

**(b)**

**(i)** Total emissive power:

$$E_b = \sigma T^4 = (5.670 \times 10^{-8}) \times (2900)^4$$

$(2900)^4 = 7.0728 \times 10^{13}$

$$E_b = (5.670 \times 10^{-8}) \times (7.0728 \times 10^{13}) = 4.010 \times 10^6 \;\frac{W}{m^2}$$

**(ii)** Peak wavelength:

$$\lambda_{max} = \frac{2897.8}{2900} = 0.9992 \;\mu m \approx 1.0 \;\mu m$$

**(iii)** Fraction in visible range:

$$f_{\text{visible}} = F_{(0 \to 0.7)} - F_{(0 \to 0.4)} = 0.000834 - 0.0000 = 0.000834$$

$$\text{Visible Power} = 0.000834 \times 4.010 \times 10^6 = 3344 \;\frac{W}{m^2}$$

**Final Answers:** $E_b = 4.01 \times 10^6 \; W/m^2$, $\lambda_{max} = 0.999 \;\mu m$, $f_{visible} = 0.0834\%$ (very small — explaining why incandescent bulbs are inefficient!).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency Trap:** KTU uses $C_1$ in units of $W \cdot \mu m^4/m^2$. If you mistakenly use $C_1 = 2\pi h c^2$ in SI, your answer will be off by a factor of $10^{12}$. **Always state $C_1$ and $C_2$ with units explicitly.**
> 2. **Exponential Sign Error:** A common mistake is writing $\exp(-C_2/\lambda T)$ instead of $\exp(+C_2/\lambda T)$ in the denominator. Double-check the sign.
> 3. **Skipping Wien Derivation Steps:** Examiners award full marks only if you set $dE_{b\lambda}/d\lambda = 0$, take the logarithm, and derive the transcendental equation. Just stating the result costs 3–4 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the "$-1$" in Planck's Denominator:** Students sometimes confuse Planck's Law with Wien's approximation. The "$-1$" is essential and must appear in the final expression.
> 5. **Final Answer Without Units:** Every numerical answer MUST carry units. Missing units = 0.5–1 mark penalty per answer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Planck's Law**

- ✅ **Planck's Law** is the *fundamental* law of black body radiation; all other radiation laws are derived from it.
- ✅ The first radiation constant: $C_1 = 2\pi h c^2 = 3.7415 \times 10^8 \; W \cdot \mu m^4 / m^2$.
- ✅ The second radiation constant: $C_2 = hc/k = 1.4388 \times 10^4 \; \mu m \cdot K$.
- ✅ **Stefan-Boltzmann Law:** $E_b = \sigma T^4$, where $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \; W/m^2 \cdot K^4$.
- ✅ **Wien's Displacement Law:** $\lambda_{max} \cdot T = 2897.8 \;\mu m \cdot K$.
- ✅ **Rayleigh-Jeans Law** (long-$\lambda$ limit): $E_{b\lambda} \approx 2\pi c k T / \lambda^4$.
- ✅ **Wien's Approximation** (short-$\lambda$ limit): $E_{b\lambda} \approx (C_1/\lambda^5) \exp(-C_2/\lambda T)$.
- ✅ Planck's hypothesis: Energy is quantized as $E = h\nu$.
- ✅ The ultraviolet catastrophe is resolved because $\exp(C_2/\lambda T)$ grows faster than $\lambda^5$ shrinks as $\lambda \to 0$.
- ✅ **Sun's surface temperature** ($T \approx 5778 \;K$) can be derived: $\lambda_{max} = 2897.8/5778 \approx 0.50 \;\mu m$ (green light peak), which matches observations.
- ✅ For a real (gray) surface: $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$, where $\varepsilon < 1$.
- ✅ For KTU numerical problems: use **micrometers** for wavelength; keep $C_1, C_2$ in the units given above; show all intermediate steps for full marks.
- ✅ Memorize the **transcendental equation** $y \cdot e^y / (e^y - 1) = 5$ with solution $y \approx 4.9651$ — this is often asked in Part A derivations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kirchhoff's Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET303 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Kirchhoff's Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# KTU PREMIUM STUDY NOTES: KIRCHHOFF'S LAW OF THERMAL RADIATION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KIRCHHOFF'S LAW OF THERMAL RADIATION** states that **at thermal equilibrium, the monochromatic emissivity of a body equals its monochromatic absorptivity at the same wavelength and temperature.** Mathematically:

$$\varepsilon_\lambda(T) = \alpha_\lambda(T)$$

**Where:**
- $\varepsilon_\lambda(T)$ = Spectral (monochromatic) emissivity at wavelength $\lambda$ and absolute temperature $T$
- $\alpha_\lambda(T)$ = Spectral (monochromatic) absorptivity at wavelength $\lambda$ and absolute temperature $T$

For **total (over all wavelengths)** and **hemispherical (over all directions)** properties, for a **diffuse surface**:

$$\varepsilon(T) = \alpha(T)$$

The total emissive power and absorptivity are related by:

$$E(T) = \alpha(T) \cdot \sigma T^4$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Context:** This topic falls under **Module 4: Radiation Heat Transfer** and is foundational for solving view-factor problems, network radiation analysis, and radiation shield design.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

**Imagine a classroom on a hot summer afternoon:**

Think of a body as a person in two simultaneous roles:
1. **As an "Emitter"** — A person wearing a *dark black shirt* feels hotter because dark surfaces emit radiation more efficiently (think: radiator fins painted matte black).
2. **As an "Absorber"** — The same dark shirt *absorbs* sunlight far more than a white shirt.

**Kirchhoff's insight was profound:** A surface that is a **good emitter** is also a **good absorber** — at the same wavelength and temperature. Conversely, **poor emitters are poor absorbers** (and thus good reflectors).

**Everyday Examples to Remember:**
- **White house paint** in Kerala summers: high reflectivity → low absorptivity → low emissivity → keeps house cooler.
- **Polished aluminum kettle**: low emissivity → low absorptivity → reflects heat back, keeps tea hot.
- **Solar absorber plates**: coated black to maximize $\alpha$ → also maximum $\varepsilon$ (used for absorbing, but loses heat when hot — trade-off!).
- **Tungsten filament bulb**: emissivity close to black body when hot → emits efficiently.

> [!TIP]  
> **Mnemonic for Exams:** **"Good Absorber = Good Emitter"** — remember this and half the concept is mastered!

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants appear repeatedly in radiation problems:

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Units |
|----------|--------|-------|-------|
| Stefan-Boltzmann Constant | $\sigma$ | $5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ | $\text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$ |
| Speed of Light | $c$ | $2.998 \times 10^8$ | m/s |
| Planck's Constant | $h$ | $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ | J·s |
| Boltzmann Constant | $k_B$ | $1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ | J/K |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Black Body Definition (Recap):** A black body has $\alpha = 1$ and $\varepsilon = 1$ at all wavelengths. It is a **perfect absorber and perfect emitter**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of the Law

Kirchhoff's law is derived from the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** and the principle of **thermal equilibrium**. Let us break it down systematically:

### Logical Step 1: Statement of Thermal Equilibrium Assumption
- Consider two large parallel surfaces facing each other.
- Surface **1** is an **arbitrary real surface** at temperature $T$.
- Surface **2** is a **black body** at temperature $T$.
- The enclosed space is evacuated (no convection/conduction) and reaches steady state at temperature $T$.

### Logical Step 2: Energy Balance
- The real surface emits radiation AND absorbs radiation from the black body.
- At thermal equilibrium, the **net heat exchange must be zero** (otherwise temperatures would drift).
- Therefore: **Energy emitted by Surface 1 = Energy absorbed by Surface 1 from Surface 2**.

### Logical Step 3: Apply Definitions
- Spectral emissive power of Surface 1: $E_{\lambda,1}(\lambda, T)$
- Spectral absorptivity of Surface 1 at $\lambda$: $\alpha_{\lambda,1}(\lambda, T)$
- Spectral emissive power of black body: $E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T)$

### Logical Step 4: Equality Condition
$$E_{\lambda,1}(\lambda, T) = \alpha_{\lambda,1}(\lambda, T) \cdot E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T)$$

### Logical Step 5: Definition of Emissivity
By definition, emissivity is the ratio of actual emissive power to black body emissive power:

$$\varepsilon_\lambda(\lambda, T) = \frac{E_{\lambda}(\lambda, T)}{E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T)}$$

### Logical Step 6: Derive the Law
Substituting Step 4 into Step 5:

$$\varepsilon_\lambda(\lambda, T) = \alpha_{\lambda}(\lambda, T)$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **This is the monochromatic form of Kirchhoff's Law** — it holds for every wavelength $\lambda$ independently.

## 2.2 Important Conceptual Distinctions (KTU High-Yield)

| Property Type | Definition | Symbol |
|---------------|------------|--------|
| **Monochromatic / Spectral** | Property at a specific wavelength $\lambda$ | $\varepsilon_\lambda$, $\alpha_\lambda$ |
| **Total** | Integrated over all wavelengths (0 to $\infty$) | $\varepsilon$, $\alpha$ |
| **Directional** | Property in a specific direction $\theta, \phi$ | $\varepsilon_{\lambda,\theta}$, $\alpha_{\lambda,\theta}$ |
| **Hemispherical** | Integrated over all directions of a hemisphere | $\varepsilon_\lambda$, $\alpha_\lambda$ |
| **Gray Body** | $\varepsilon_\lambda$ = constant (independent of $\lambda$) | $\varepsilon = \alpha = \text{const}$ |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Master this table — these are the bread-and-butter formulas for KTU Thermal Engineering.**

| # | Formula | Description | SI Units |
|---|---------|-------------|----------|
| 1 | $\varepsilon_\lambda(\lambda, T) = \alpha_\lambda(\lambda, T)$ | Spectral Kirchhoff's Law | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $\varepsilon(T) = \alpha(T)$ | Total Kirchhoff's (for diffuse surfaces) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $E_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda \cdot E_{b\lambda}$ | Spectral emitted power = absorptivity × black body power | $\text{W/m}^2\text{·μm}$ |
| 4 | $E = \alpha \cdot \sigma T^4$ | Total emitted power for diffuse surface | $\text{W/m}^2$ |
| 5 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{E}{E_b} = \dfrac{E}{\sigma T^4}$ | Definition of emissivity | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$ | Energy balance (absorptivity + reflectivity + transmissivity) | Dimensionless |
| 7 | For opaque: $\tau = 0 \Rightarrow \alpha + \rho = 1$ | Opaque surface simplification | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $\rho_\lambda = 1 - \alpha_\lambda$ | Spectral reflectivity (opaque) | Dimensionless |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Kirchhoff's Law is **not just a textbook theorem** — it powers real engineering decisions:

1. **Solar Thermal Collectors**: Selective coatings are designed so $\alpha$ is high in the solar spectrum (~0.5 μm) but $\varepsilon$ is low in the infrared (~10 μm). This is a **wavelength-dependent trick** that exploits the *spectral* nature of Kirchhoff's Law.

2. **Furnace & Boiler Design**: Internal furnace walls lined with high-$\varepsilon$ refractory bricks maximize heat transfer to the working fluid.

3. **Spacecraft Thermal Control**: Multi-Layer Insulation (MLI) blankets have very low $\varepsilon$ outer layers to minimize radiative heat loss in vacuum.

4. **Radiation Shields**: Polished low-emissivity shields are placed between hot and cold surfaces to reduce net radiation transfer. Each shield roughly halves the heat transfer.

5. **Infrared Thermometry & Thermal Imaging**: Knowing $\varepsilon$ of the target surface is critical; misjudging it gives wrong temperature readings.

6. **Heat Pipes & Electronic Cooling**: Radiator fins in satellites and high-power electronics are designed with controlled surface emissivity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation: Spectral Form of Kirchhoff's Law

### Setup Description

Consider two **large, parallel, isothermal surfaces** facing each other across an evacuated, perfectly insulated enclosure:
- Surface **B**: Black body at temperature $T$ (the reference).
- Surface **A**: A real, opaque, diffuse-gray surface at the same temperature $T$.
- The gap is evacuated → no conduction, no convection.
- Steady state is reached → temperature $T$ is uniform and constant.

### Step 1: Energy Balance on Surface A

At steady state thermal equilibrium, the **net energy exchange between A and B must be zero**. Otherwise, one surface would heat up while the other cools down — violating thermal equilibrium.

$$\text{Net Heat Transfer} = 0$$

The radiation leaving A toward B per unit area at wavelength $\lambda$ is the spectral emissive power:

$$q_{\lambda,A \rightarrow B} = E_{\lambda,A}(\lambda, T)$$

The radiation from B incident on A, per unit area, at wavelength $\lambda$ is the spectral emissive power of B (since B is black, all incident energy is absorbed):

$$q_{\lambda,B \rightarrow A} = E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T)$$

### Step 2: Apply Absorptivity to Incident Radiation

A is a real surface, so it absorbs only a fraction $\alpha_{\lambda,A}$ of the radiation incident on it at wavelength $\lambda$:

$$q_{\lambda,\text{absorbed by A}} = \alpha_{\lambda,A}(\lambda, T) \cdot E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T)$$

### Step 3: Set Energy In = Energy Out (Steady State)

Since Surface A is at constant temperature $T$, the energy it emits must equal the energy it absorbs (this is the **Second Law requirement**):

$$E_{\lambda,A}(\lambda, T) = \alpha_{\lambda,A}(\lambda, T) \cdot E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T)$$

### Step 4: Rearrange to Isolate Emissivity

Divide both sides by $E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T)$:

$$\frac{E_{\lambda,A}(\lambda, T)}{E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T)} = \alpha_{\lambda,A}(\lambda, T)$$

### Step 5: Apply Definition of Emissivity

By definition, **spectral emissivity** is the ratio of the spectral emissive power of a real surface to that of a black body at the same temperature and wavelength:

$$\varepsilon_{\lambda,A}(\lambda, T) \equiv \frac{E_{\lambda,A}(\lambda, T)}{E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T)}$$

### Step 6: Final Result

Combining Steps 4 and 5:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_\lambda(\lambda, T) = \alpha_\lambda(\lambda, T)}$$

**Q.E.D.** — Kirchhoff's Law is proven.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Total Hemispherical Form

### Step 1: Total Emissive Power of Surface A

The total (integrated over all wavelengths) hemispherical emissive power is:

$$E_A(T) = \int_{0}^{\infty} E_{\lambda,A}(\lambda, T) \, d\lambda$$

### Step 2: Substitute Kirchhoff's Spectral Form

Using $E_{\lambda,A}(\lambda, T) = \alpha_{\lambda,A}(\lambda, T) \cdot E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T)$:

$$E_A(T) = \int_{0}^{\infty} \alpha_{\lambda,A}(\lambda, T) \cdot E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T) \, d\lambda$$

### Step 3: For a Diffuse Surface — Pull Out $\alpha$

For a **diffuse, gray** surface, $\alpha_{\lambda,A}(\lambda, T)$ is independent of $\lambda$ and equals a constant $\alpha_A$. Hence:

$$E_A(T) = \alpha_A(T) \int_{0}^{\infty} E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T) \, d\lambda$$

### Step 4: Recognize the Stefan-Boltzmann Integral

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T) \, d\lambda = \sigma T^4$$

Therefore:

$$E_A(T) = \alpha_A(T) \cdot \sigma T^4$$

### Step 5: Apply Black Body Comparison

For a black body at temperature $T$:

$$E_b(T) = \sigma T^4$$

### Step 6: Define Total Emissivity

$$\varepsilon_A(T) \equiv \frac{E_A(T)}{E_b(T)} = \frac{\alpha_A(T) \cdot \sigma T^4}{\sigma T^4}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_A(T) = \alpha_A(T)}$$

This is the **total hemispherical form** of Kirchhoff's Law — applicable to **diffuse, gray** surfaces.

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Student Error:** The total form $\varepsilon = \alpha$ is **only valid for diffuse gray surfaces**. For selective (non-gray) surfaces, $\varepsilon$ and $\alpha$ may differ significantly at any given wavelength, even though at each individual wavelength Kirchhoff's spectral law still holds.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A surface has a spectral emissivity variation given by $\varepsilon_\lambda = 0.3$ for $0 < \lambda \leq 2 \, \mu\text{m}$ and $\varepsilon_\lambda = 0.8$ for $\lambda > 2 \, \mu\text{m}$. Determine the **total emissivity** and **total absorptivity** when the surface receives radiation from a source at $T_s = 1000 \, \text{K}$. Assume the surface itself is at $T = 500 \, \text{K}$.

### Solution Step 1: Recall Kirchhoff's Spectral Law

$$\varepsilon_\lambda(\lambda, T) = \alpha_\lambda(\lambda, T)$$

At any wavelength, the emissivity at 500 K equals the absorptivity at 500 K.

### Solution Step 2: Compute Total Emissivity at T = 500 K

$$\varepsilon(T) = \frac{\int_0^\infty \varepsilon_\lambda(\lambda, T) \cdot E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T) \, d\lambda}{\sigma T^4}$$

**Step 2a — Use black body fraction function:**
$$\varepsilon(T) = F_{(0 \rightarrow 2\mu\text{m})}(T) \cdot \varepsilon_{\lambda,1} + \left[1 - F_{(0 \rightarrow 2\mu\text{m})}(T)\right] \cdot \varepsilon_{\lambda,2}$$

**Step 2b — For T = 500 K, $\lambda T = 2 \times 500 = 1000 \, \mu\text{m·K}$:**

From standard black body radiation function tables:
$$F_{(0 \rightarrow 2\mu\text{m})}(500\,\text{K}) \approx 0.000716$$

This is very small because at 500 K, most emission is in the **infrared beyond 2 μm**.

**Step 2c — Compute total emissivity:**
$$\varepsilon(500) = 0.000716 \times 0.3 + (1 - 0.000716) \times 0.8$$
$$\varepsilon(500) = 0.000215 + 0.799427 \approx 0.7996 \approx 0.80$$

### Solution Step 3: Compute Total Absorptivity from Source at T_s = 1000 K

For a **diffuse** surface, absorptivity is integrated against the **incoming** spectrum:

$$\alpha(T_s) = \frac{\int_0^\infty \alpha_\lambda(\lambda, T) \cdot E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T_s) \, d\lambda}{\sigma T_s^4}$$

**Step 3a — At T_s = 1000 K, $\lambda T_s = 2 \times 1000 = 2000 \, \mu\text{m·K}$:**

From black body fraction tables:
$$F_{(0 \rightarrow 2\mu\text{m})}(1000\,\text{K}) \approx 0.0667$$

**Step 3b — Compute total absorptivity:**
$$\alpha(1000) = 0.0667 \times 0.3 + (1 - 0.0667) \times 0.8$$
$$\alpha(1000) = 0.02001 + 0.74664 \approx 0.7667 \approx 0.77$$

### Solution Step 4: Verify Kirchhoff's Law

Notice that:
- $\varepsilon(500\,\text{K}) \approx 0.80$
- $\alpha(1000\,\text{K}) \approx 0.77$

These are **NOT equal**, confirming that for a **selective (non-gray) surface**, the **total** values $\varepsilon$ and $\alpha$ depend on the temperature at which they are evaluated, even though $\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$ at each wavelength.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** Kirchhoff's law in total form $\varepsilon = \alpha$ holds **only** for gray (wavelength-independent) surfaces. For selective surfaces, you must use the spectral form and integrate.

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Conceptual Framework of Kirchhoff's Law

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    A --> C["Absorbs Incident Radiation"]
    D["Black Body at Temperature T"] -->|"|radiation E_b_lambda|"| C
    B -->|"|equated at equilibrium|"| E["Second Law of Thermodynamics"]
    C --> E
    E --> F["Kirchhoff Law Result"]
    F --> G["Eps Lambda equals Alpha Lambda"]
    G --> H["Spectral Form"]
    G --> I["Total Form for Gray Surface"]
    H --> J["Selective Surface Application"]
    H --> K["Solar Collectors Design"]

    I --> M["Radiation Shield Design"]
```

```mermaid
    S3 --> S4["Step 4: Energy absorbed by A equals alpha_lambda times E_b_lambda"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5: At equilibrium emission equals absorption"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 6: Rearrange to isolate emissivity"]

```
