# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — Short Answer `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: State Kirchhoff's Law of Thermal Radiation. Why is it important in engineering applications?**

**Model Answer:**

Kirchhoff's Law states that at thermal equilibrium, the monochromatic emissivity of a surface equals its monochromatic absorptivity at the same wavelength and temperature.

$$\varepsilon_\lambda(\lambda, T) = \alpha_\lambda(\lambda, T)$$

For a diffuse gray surface, this simplifies to the total form: $\varepsilon = \alpha$.

**Engineering Importance:**
1. Allows prediction of emissivity from easily measured absorptivity (and vice versa).
2. Guides selection of materials for solar collectors, radiation shields, and furnace linings.
3. Critical for accurate IR temperature measurement.
4. Forms the basis of radiation network analysis.

---

### Question A2 — Short Answer `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Distinguish between a black body, a gray body, and a selective surface. How does Kirchhoff's Law apply to each?**

**Model Answer:**

| Surface Type | Definition | Kirchhoff's Form |
|--------------|------------|------------------|
| **Black Body** | $\alpha = \varepsilon = 1$ for all $\lambda$ | Trivially satisfied |
| **Gray Body** | $\varepsilon_\lambda, \alpha_\lambda$ = constant (independent of $\lambda$) | $\varepsilon = \alpha$ (total form valid) |
| **Selective Surface** | $\varepsilon_\lambda, \alpha_\lambda$ vary with $\lambda$ | Only spectral form $\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$ holds |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

> **KTU Pattern Reminder:** KTU ESE Part B questions for Module 4 in the 2024 scheme typically carry 14 marks with sub-parts (a) carrying 7 marks and (b) carrying 7 marks. The student attempts **one of two choices**.

---

### Question B1 — Option A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** Derive the spectral form of Kirchhoff's Law of thermal radiation, clearly stating the assumptions used. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A diffuse gray surface at 800 K has an emissivity of 0.75. The surface receives radiation from a high-temperature source at 1500 K. Calculate:
- (i) The total emissive power of the surface **[2 Marks]**
- (ii) The total radiation incident per unit area from the source **[2 Marks]**
- (iii) The absorbed radiation per unit area **[3 Marks]**

Given: $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$.

---

### Model Solution for Question B1

#### Part (a) — Derivation [7 Marks]

**Assumptions:**
1. Two parallel surfaces facing each other across an evacuated enclosure.
2. One surface is a black body at temperature $T$.
3. The other surface is a real, opaque, diffuse surface at the same temperature $T$.
4. System is at thermal equilibrium (steady state).

**Derivation:**

Let Surface 1 (real) emit $E_{\lambda,1}(\lambda, T)$ and absorb $\alpha_{\lambda,1}(\lambda, T) \cdot E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T)$ from the black body Surface 2.

At thermal equilibrium (Second Law):

$$E_{\lambda,1}(\lambda, T) = \alpha_{\lambda,1}(\lambda, T) \cdot E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T)$$

By definition of emissivity:

$$\varepsilon_{\lambda,1}(\lambda, T) = \frac{E_{\lambda,1}(\lambda, T)}{E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T)}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_\lambda(\lambda, T) = \alpha_\lambda(\lambda, T)}$$

**Marks Distribution:** Assumptions (2), Energy balance (2), Definition application (2), Final result (1).

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem [7 Marks]

**Given:** $\varepsilon = 0.75$ (gray surface), $T = 800\,\text{K}$, $T_s = 1500\,\text{K}$, $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$.

**(i) Total emissive power of the surface:** [2 Marks]

By Stefan-Boltzmann for a gray surface:

$$E = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma T^4$$

$$E = 0.75 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (800)^4$$

$$E = 0.75 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 4.096 \times 10^{11}$$

$$E = 0.75 \times 5.67 \times 40960 = 17,419 \, \text{W/m}^2$$

**E ≈ 17.42 kW/m²**

**(ii) Total radiation incident per unit area from source:** [2 Marks]

Assuming the source is a black body or the incident flux equals black body emission at 1500 K:

$$G = \sigma T_s^4 = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (1500)^4$$

$$G = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 5.0625 \times 10^{12}$$

$$G = 286,994 \, \text{W/m}^2$$

**G ≈ 287 kW/m²**

**(iii) Absorbed radiation per unit area:** [3 Marks]

Since the surface is diffuse gray and Kirchhoff's Law applies:

$$\alpha = \varepsilon = 0.75$$

$$G_{\text{abs}} = \alpha \cdot G = 0.75 \times 286,994$$

$$G_{\text{abs}} = 215,246 \, \text{W/m}^2$$

**G_abs ≈ 215.25 kW/m²**

---

### Question B1 — Option B (14 Marks) `[Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Explain the physical significance of Kirchhoff's Law. Discuss how it applies to selective surfaces used in solar collectors. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** The spectral emissivity of a surface varies as: $\varepsilon_\lambda = 0.2$ for $0 < \lambda \leq 3 \, \mu\text{m}$, and $\varepsilon_\lambda = 0.9$ for $\lambda > 3 \, \mu\text{m}$. The surface is at 600 K and is exposed to solar radiation approximated as black body at 5800 K.
- (i) Compute the total emissivity of the surface at 600 K **[3 Marks]**
- (ii) Compute the total absorptivity for solar radiation **[4 Marks]**

Use black body radiation function: $F_{(0 \rightarrow \lambda T)}$ at $\lambda T = 1800 \, \mu\text{m·K}$ is approximately **0.0856** and at $\lambda T = 17,400 \, \mu\text{m·K}$ is approximately **0.9686**.

---

### Model Solution for Question B1 — Option B

#### Part (a) — Physical Significance and Solar Collectors [7 Marks]

**Physical Significance:**
Kirchhoff's Law establishes a fundamental reciprocity between emission and absorption of radiation at thermal equilibrium. It tells us that a surface cannot selectively absorb without also emitting at the same wavelength.

**Key Points (2 marks each, totaling 6 + 1 for conclusion):**
1. **Good absorbers are good emitters** — surfaces painted black absorb sunlight well but also radiate heat well.
2. **Poor emitters are poor absorbers** — polished metals reflect and are weak emitters.
3. **Wavelength-specific behavior** — the law holds at each $\lambda$ independently, allowing engineering of "selective" surfaces.

**Solar Collector Application:**
A solar thermal collector must absorb maximum solar radiation (wavelengths 0.3 – 2.5 μm) while losing minimum heat by re-radiation (wavelengths 2.5 – 50 μm at operating temperature ~100°C).

**Selective Coating Design:**
- For solar spectrum ($\lambda < 2.5 \, \mu\text{m}$): $\alpha_\lambda$ is **high** (e.g., 0.95)
- For thermal IR ($\lambda > 2.5 \, \mu\text{m}$): $\varepsilon_\lambda$ is **low** (e.g., 0.05)

By Kirchhoff's law $\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$ at each wavelength, so we engineer **materials whose $\alpha_\lambda$ is wavelength-dependent** (e.g., black chrome, nickel-pigmented aluminum oxide coatings).

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem [7 Marks]

**Given:** Two-band emissivity profile, $T = 600\,\text{K}$, $T_s = 5800\,\text{K}$, band boundary $\lambda = 3 \, \mu\text{m}$.

**(i) Total emissivity at 600 K:** [3 Marks]

Product $\lambda T = 3 \times 600 = 1800 \, \mu\text{m·K}$

From the black body radiation function:
$$F_{(0 \rightarrow 3\mu\text{m})}(600\,\text{K}) = F_{(0 \rightarrow 1800 \, \mu\text{m·K})} = 0.0856$$

$$\varepsilon(T) = F_{(0 \rightarrow 1800)} \cdot 0.2 + [1 - F_{(0 \rightarrow 1800)}] \cdot 0.9$$

$$\varepsilon(600) = 0.0856 \times 0.2 + (1 - 0.0856) \times 0.9$$

$$\varepsilon(600) = 0.01712 + 0.82296 = 0.84008$$

**ε(600 K) ≈ 0.840**

**(ii) Total absorptivity for solar radiation (T_s = 5800 K):** [4 Marks]

Product $\lambda T_s = 3 \times 5800 = 17,400 \, \mu\text{m·K}$

From the black body radiation function:
$$F_{(0 \rightarrow 3\mu\text{m})}(5800\,\text{K}) = F_{(0 \rightarrow 17,400 \, \mu\text{m·K})} = 0.9686$$

$$\alpha(T_s) = F_{(0 \rightarrow 17,400)} \cdot 0.2 + [1 - F_{(0 \rightarrow 17,400)}] \cdot 0.9$$

$$\alpha(5800) = 0.9686 \times 0.2 + (1 - 0.9686) \times 0.9$$

$$\alpha(5800) = 0.19372 + 0.02826 = 0.22198$$

**α(5800 K) ≈ 0.222**

**Interpretation:** This surface is an excellent solar absorber (low $\alpha$ = 0.222 in solar band means... wait — actually, high absorption in solar spectrum means low $\varepsilon_\lambda$ in that band). Let me re-check:
- $\varepsilon_\lambda = 0.2$ for $\lambda < 3\mu\text{m}$ → by Kirchhoff, $\alpha_\lambda = 0.2$ in solar band → low solar absorption.
- $\varepsilon_\lambda = 0.9$ for $\lambda > 3\mu\text{m}$ → by Kirchhoff, $\alpha_\lambda = 0.9$ in IR → high IR absorption/emission.

So this surface is actually a **poor solar absorber** (good solar reflector) — useful as a **solar reflector / thermal mirror** (e.g., backside of solar collector insulation).

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often confuse which band is "good absorber" vs "good emitter." Remember — **Kirchhoff ties emissivity and absorptivity AT THE SAME WAVELENGTH.** High $\alpha_\lambda$ in solar band requires high $\varepsilon_\lambda$ in solar band too — which would cause thermal re-radiation loss at operating temperature. This is why **selective coatings deliberately violate the gray-body assumption** — they exploit the wavelength dependence.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exams**

### Core Concept Mastery
- ✅ Kirchhoff's Law: $\varepsilon_\lambda(\lambda, T) = \alpha_\lambda(\lambda, T)$ — **memorize this in both spectral and total forms**.
- ✅ Total form $\varepsilon = \alpha$ holds **only** for **diffuse gray surfaces**.
- ✅ The law originates from the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** (thermal equilibrium).
- ✅ Derivation requires a black body reference surface at the same temperature.

### Engineering Applications
- ✅ Solar collectors: high $\alpha$ in solar band (0.3–2.5 μm), low $\varepsilon$ in IR (2.5–50 μm).
- ✅ Radiation shields: low $\varepsilon$ aluminum surfaces between hot and cold sources.
- ✅ Furnace linings: high $\varepsilon$ refractory bricks for maximum heat transfer.
- ✅ Spacecraft MLI: low $\varepsilon$ outer layers to minimize radiation loss.
- ✅ IR thermography: must know surface $\varepsilon$ to read temperature correctly.

### Numerical Problem Solving
- ✅ Use black body radiation function tables: $F_{(0 \rightarrow \lambda T)}$ for $\lambda T$ products.
- ✅ Stefan-Boltzmann: $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ with $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$.
- ✅ For multi-band emissivity profiles, integrate $\varepsilon_\lambda \cdot E_{b\lambda}$ over each band.
- ✅ Remember $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$ and for opaque: $\tau = 0 \Rightarrow \alpha = 1 - \rho$.

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- ❌ Don't assume $\varepsilon = \alpha$ for selective (non-gray) surfaces in total form.
- ❌ Don't forget to use **absolute temperature (Kelvin)** in Stefan-Boltzmann.
- ❌ Don't confuse $\varepsilon$ (emissivity) with $E$ (emissive power) — one is dimensionless, one has units.
- ❌ Don't apply Kirchhoff's law to non-equilibrium situations — it requires thermal equilibrium.
- ❌ Always verify whether the problem gives $\varepsilon_\lambda$ (spectral) or $\varepsilon$ (total).

### KTU 2024 Exam Quick Tips
- ✅ For 3-mark questions: State the law + give one engineering example.
- ✅ For 14-mark questions: Always write **assumptions → derivation → final boxed result**.
- ✅ Always include units in numerical answers.
- ✅ Show intermediate steps in $T^4$ calculations — partial marks are awarded.
- ✅ Practice black body fraction function table reading; questions commonly test this.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wien's Displacement Law and Stefan Boltzmann's Law (Simple Problems)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIET303 - THERMAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Wien's Displacement Law and Stefan Boltzmann's Law (Simple Problems) # Module 4: Wien's Displacement Law and Stefan-Boltzmann Law ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Wien's Displacement Law > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):** > Wien's Displacement Law states that the wavelength at which the spectral emissive power of a blackbody is maximum is **inversely proportional** to the absolute temperature of the blackbody. Mathematically, the product of the peak wavelength ($\lambda_{max}$) and the absolute temperature ($T$) is a constant. The product constant is universally known as **Wien's displacement constant**, denoted by the symbol $b$, and has a value: $$b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m} \cdot \text{K}$$ Hence the governing equation is: $$\lambda_{max} \cdot T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m} \cdot \text{K}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why is it called "Displacement" Law?** > As the temperature of a body increases, the peak of its emission curve "displaces" (shifts) toward shorter wavelengths (toward the violet/blue/UV end of the spectrum). This is the physical reason behind the term *displacement*. #### Conceptual Analogy — The "Glowing Iron" Intuition Imagine heating a piece of iron in a furnace. When the iron is mildly hot (say $800 \ \text{K}$), you do not see any glow. As the temperature rises ($1200 \ \text{K}$), it glows a **dull red**. Push the temperature higher ($1500 \ \text{K}$), the colour becomes **orange**. At $2000 \ \text{K}$ it glows **yellowish-white**, and at $5800 \ \text{K}$ (the Sun's surface) it appears nearly **white**. The dull red corresponds to a longer peak wavelength ($\sim 2.4 \ \mu\text{m}$ near IR), while the white glow corresponds to a much shorter peak wavelength ($\sim 0.5 \ \mu\text{m}$ in the visible range). The peak has "moved" — this movement is exactly what Wien's law quantifies. ### 1.2 Stefan-Boltzmann Law > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):** > The Stefan-Boltzmann Law states that the **total emissive power** (or total hemispherical radiation leaving a surface per unit area per unit time) of a blackbody is **directly proportional to the fourth power of its absolute temperature**. The mathematical form is: $$E_b = \sigma \cdot T^4$$ where $E_b$ is the total emissive power in $\text{W/m}^2$, $T$ is absolute temperature in Kelvin, and $\sigma$ is the **Stefan-Boltzmann constant**. $$\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W/m}^2 \cdot \text{K}^4$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** The fourth-power dependence is *extremely* sensitive. A small change in temperature causes a *massive* change in radiated heat. Doubling $T$ increases $E_b$ by **16 times** (since $2^4 = 16$). #### Conceptual Analogy — The "Hot Stove and Warm Cup" Intuition Consider two cups of tea. One is at $333 \ \text{K}$ ($60^\circ\text{C}$, luke-warm) and another at $373 \ \text{K}$ ($100^\circ\text{C}$, boiling hot). The temperature difference is only $40 \ \text{K}$ (a $12\%$ increase), but the radiation loss from the boiling cup is: $$\left(\frac{373}{333}\right)^4 \approx 1.504 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{about } 50\% \text{ more heat loss}$$ This is precisely why a hot object cools down very rapidly at first, and then much more slowly as it approaches room temperature — the $T^4$ dependence is *strongly* nonlinear. > [!TIP] > **Real-world connection:** This is the *exact* physical law that explains the operation of **infrared thermometers**, **radiation pyrometers**, **solar collectors**, and **furnace design**. KTU examiners frequently test whether students understand that radiation heat transfer dominates at high temperatures while convection/conduction may be negligible. --- ### Physical Constants Required for KTU Board Exam | Symbol | Quantity | Value | Unit | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $b$ | Wien's displacement constant | $\mathbf{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}$ | $\text{m} \cdot \text{K}$ | | $\sigma$ | Stefan-Boltzmann constant | $\mathbf{5.67 \times 10^{-8}}$ | $\text{W} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{K}^{-4}$ | | $h$ | Planck's constant | $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ | $\text{J} \cdot \text{s}$ | | $c$ | Speed of light in vacuum | $2.998 \times 10^{8}$ | $\text{m/s}$ | | $k_B$ | Boltzmann constant | $1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ | $\text{J/K}$ | | $C_1$ | First radiation constant | $2\pi h c^2 = 0.374 \times 10^{-16}$ | $\text{W} \cdot \text{m}^2$ | | $C_2$ | Second radiation constant | $h c / k_B = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2}$ | $\text{m} \cdot \text{K}$ | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Connection to Planck's Distribution Law Both Wien's Displacement Law and the Stefan-Boltzmann Law are **direct consequences** of Planck's spectral distribution law, which describes the spectral emissive power of a blackbody at wavelength $\lambda$ and temperature $T$: $$E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T) = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{C_2}{\lambda T}\right) - 1\right]}$$ The shape of the curve described by $E_{b\lambda}$ versus $\lambda$ for various temperatures is what enables the two laws. ### Step-by-step reasoning: * **The curve has a single, well-defined maximum** at some $\lambda = \lambda_{max}$. This maximum shifts to lower $\lambda$ as $T$ rises. Setting $\dfrac{\partial E_{b\lambda}}{\partial \lambda} = 0$ yields **Wien's Displacement Law**. * **The total area under the curve** (i.e. $\int_0^\infty E_{b\lambda} \, d\lambda$) is the *total* emissive power. Performing this integration yields the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** $E_b = \sigma T^4$. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Mental Model:** Think of the Planck distribution curve as a "lumpy hill" of energy. Wien's law tells you *where the peak of the hill lies*; Stefan-Boltzmann tells you the *total area under the hill*. Both come from the same hill, just different mathematical operations on it. ## 2.2 Physical Significance of Wien's Displacement Law * It allows us to determine the **surface temperature of celestial bodies** (stars, sun) from the colour of light they emit. * It forms the basis of **optical pyrometry** (temperature measurement from a distance). * It justifies why **incandescent bulb filaments** operate near $2500 \ \text{K}$ to produce visible light ($\lambda_{max} \approx 1.16 \ \mu\text{m}$, near IR-visible boundary). * It explains why a **blacksmith can gauge temperature by colour** without any instrument. ## 2.3 Physical Significance of Stefan-Boltzmann Law * **Heat transfer at high temperature:** Radiation is the dominant mode of heat transfer in furnaces, boilers, and combustion chambers — the $T^4$ law governs it. * **Astronomy:** Total energy radiated by a star can be calculated if its temperature and size are known. * **Climate science:** Earth's energy balance and greenhouse effect depend on this law. * **Engineering design:** Radiation shields (used in spacecraft) are designed using the $T^4$ dependence. > [!TIP] > **KTU Real-world Engineering Application:** In a **thermal power plant boiler**, the furnace temperature is around $1500 \ \text{K}$. The water-tube walls receive radiation that follows $E_b \propto T^4$. A 5% increase in flame temperature gives a $(1.05)^4 \approx 1.215$ — that is, **21.5% more heat flux**! Small temperature changes have huge impact on heat transfer. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) — High-Yield > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the consolidated, board-exam-ready formula sheet. Memorize every row. | # | Law / Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Wien's Displacement Law | $\lambda_{max} \cdot T = b$ | $\text{m} \cdot \text{K}$ | $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ | | 2 | Peak Wavelength | $\lambda_{max} = \dfrac{b}{T}$ | $\text{m}$ | Converts to $\mu\text{m}$ by $\times 10^6$ | | 3 | Stefan-Boltzmann Law | $E_b = \sigma T^4$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ | $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ | | 4 | Total Power Radiated | $Q = \sigma T^4 \cdot A_s$ | $\text{W}$ | $A_s$ = surface area | | 5 | Max Spectral Emissive Power | $E_{b\lambda, \max} \propto T^5$ | $\text{W/m}^3$ | $E_{b\lambda,max} = 1.285 \times 10^{-5} T^5$ | | 6 | Stefan-Boltzmann from Planck | $E_b = \int_0^\infty E_{b\lambda} \, d\lambda$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ | Area under the Planck curve | | 7 | First Radiation Constant | $C_1 = 2\pi h c^2$ | $\text{W} \cdot \text{m}^2$ | $0.374 \times 10^{-16}$ | | 8 | Second Radiation Constant | $C_2 = h c / k_B$ | $\text{m} \cdot \text{K}$ | $1.4388 \times 10^{-2}$ | | 9 | Energy emitted in $\Delta \lambda$ | $E_{b, \Delta\lambda} = \int_{\lambda_1}^{\lambda_2} E_{b\lambda} \, d\lambda$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ | Band emission | | 10 | Frac. of total energy below $\lambda T$ | $F_{0 \to \lambda T} = \dfrac{15}{\pi^4} \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{e^{-n \xi}}{n} \left(\xi^3 + \dfrac{3\xi^2}{n} + \dfrac{6\xi}{n^2} + \dfrac{6}{n^3}\right)$ | dimensionless | where $\xi = \dfrac{C_2}{\lambda T}$ | > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** Rows 5 and 10 are higher-level and may not be asked in *simple problems* but are useful for the rigorous derivation. For typical KTU 2024 scheme problems, **Rows 1, 2, 3, 4, 6** are most frequently tested. ## 2.5 Radiation Constants — Why They Exist The constants $C_1$ and $C_2$ arise from the assumption that radiation is emitted in discrete quanta (Planck's quantum hypothesis). Before Planck, classical physics (Rayleigh-Jeans law) failed at short wavelengths — this is known as the **ultraviolet catastrophe**. $$C_1 = 2 \pi h c^2 = 2 \pi \times (6.626 \times 10^{-34}) \times (2.998 \times 10^8)^2 = 3.7418 \times 10^{-16} \ \text{W} \cdot \text{m}^2$$ $$C_2 = \frac{h c}{k_B} = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 2.998 \times 10^8}{1.381 \times 10^{-23}} = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2} \ \text{m} \cdot \text{K}$$ These two constants appear in the formula sheet because they often appear in derivations, even if numerical problem-solving at the KTU 2024 level usually only requires $b$ and $\sigma$. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Wien's Displacement Law from Planck's Law > [!NOTE] > **Starting Premise:** The spectral emissive power of a blackbody at wavelength $\lambda$ and temperature $T$ is given by Planck's law: > $$E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T) = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{C_2}{\lambda T}\right) - 1\right]}$$ ### Step 1 — Condition for the Peak The maximum of $E_{b\lambda}$ with respect to $\lambda$ occurs where the derivative with respect to $\lambda$ vanishes. So we set: $$\frac{\partial E_{b\lambda}}{\partial \lambda} = 0$$ ### Step 2 — Apply the Product/Chain Rule Since $E_{b\lambda}$ is a quotient of the form $N(\lambda) / D(\lambda)$, we use the quotient rule. Alternatively, the standard textbook approach is to maximise the equivalent form. Let us define: $$f(\lambda) = \lambda^5 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{C_2}{\lambda T}\right) - 1 \right]$$ Maximising $E_{b\lambda} = C_1 / f(\lambda)$ is equivalent to **minimising** $f(\lambda)$. So: $$\frac{df}{d\lambda} = 0$$ ### Step 3 — Differentiate $f(\lambda)$ We use the product rule on $f = u \cdot v$, where $u = \lambda^5$ and $v = \exp(C_2/\lambda T) - 1$. $$\frac{du}{d\lambda} = 5\lambda^4$$ $$\frac{dv}{d\lambda} = \exp\!\left(\frac{C_2}{\lambda T}\right) \cdot \left( -\frac{C_2}{\lambda^2 T} \right) = -\frac{C_2}{\lambda^2 T} \exp\!\left(\frac{C_2}{\lambda T}\right)$$ ### Step 4 — Combine the Two Derivatives $$5\lambda^4 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{C_2}{\lambda T}\right) - 1 \right] + \lambda^5 \left[ -\frac{C_2}{\lambda^2 T} \exp\!\left(\frac{C_2}{\lambda T}\right) \right] = 0$$ ### Step 5 — Simplify Divide throughout by $\lambda^4$ (which is never zero): $$5 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{C_2}{\lambda T}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{C_2}{\lambda T} \exp\!\left(\frac{C_2}{\lambda T}\right) = 0$$ Let $x = \dfrac{C_2}{\lambda_{max} T}$. Then $\exp(x) - 1$ and $\exp(x)$ are related. Substituting: $$5[\exp(x) - 1] - x \cdot \exp(x) = 0$$ $$5 \exp(x) - 5 - x \exp(x) = 0$$ $$(5 - x) \exp(x) = 5$$ ### Step 6 — Solve the Transcendental Equation The solution to $(5 - x) e^x = 5$ is a transcendental (non-algebraic) number: $$x = 4.965\, 114\, 231 \ldots$$ ### Step 7 — Back-substitute to get Wien's Law Recall $x = \dfrac{C_2}{\lambda_{max} T}$. Hence: $$\frac{C_2}{\lambda_{max} T} = 4.9651$$ $$\lambda_{max} T = \frac{C_2}{4.9651} = \frac{1.4388 \times 10^{-2}}{4.9651}$$ $$\lambda_{max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m} \cdot \text{K}$$ ### Step 8 — Final Result > [!IMPORTANT] > $$\boxed{\lambda_{max} \cdot T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m} \cdot \text{K}}$$ > This is **Wien's Displacement Law**, with the constant $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m} \cdot \text{K}$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Stefan-Boltzmann Law from Planck's Law > [!NOTE] > **Starting Premise:** The total emissive power of a blackbody is the area under the entire spectral distribution curve. $$E_b(T) = \int_0^\infty E_{b\lambda} \, d\lambda = \int_0^\infty \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{C_2}{\lambda T}\right) - 1 \right]} \, d\lambda$$ ### Step 1 — Change of Variable Let $u = \dfrac{C_2}{\lambda T}$, so that $\lambda = \dfrac{C_2}{u T}$, and $d\lambda = -\dfrac{C_2}{u^2 T} \, du$. As $\lambda \to 0^+$, $u \to \infty$. As $\lambda \to \infty$, $u \to 0^+$. So the limits flip: $$E_b = \int_{\infty}^{0} \frac{C_1}{\left(\dfrac{C_2}{uT}\right)^5 [\exp(u) - 1]} \left( -\frac{C_2}{u^2 T} \right) du$$ The two negatives (one from $d\lambda$, one from the flipped limits) cancel: $$E_b = \int_0^\infty \frac{C_1 \cdot u^5 T^5}{C_2^5 \, [\exp(u) - 1]} \cdot \frac{C_2}{u^2 T} \, du$$ ### Step 2 — Collect Terms $$E_b = \frac{C_1 T^5}{C_2^5} \cdot \frac{C_2}{T} \int_0^\infty \frac{u^3}{e^u - 1} \, du$$ $$E_b = \frac{C_1 T^4}{C_2^4} \int_0^\infty \frac{u^3}{e^u - 1} \, du$$ ### Step 3 — Evaluate the Integral (Series Expansion) Use the geometric series expansion: $$\frac{1}{e^u - 1} = \frac{e^{-u}}{1 - e^{-u}} = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} e^{-nu}$$ So: $$\int_0^\infty \frac{u^3}{e^u - 1} \, du = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \int_0^\infty u^3 e^{-nu} \, du$$ ### Step 4 — Apply the Gamma Function Identity For any positive integer $n$: $$\int_0^\infty u^3 e^{-nu} \, du = \frac{3!}{n^4} = \frac{6}{n^4}$$ (This comes from the general formula $\int_0^\infty u^k e^{-nu} du = \dfrac{\Gamma(k+1)}{n^{k+1}}$.) ### Step 5 — Sum the Series $$\int_0^\infty \frac{u^3}{e^u - 1} \, du = 6 \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^4} = 6 \cdot \frac{\pi^4}{90} = \frac{\pi^4}{15}$$ The series $\sum_{n=1}^\infty 1/n^4$ is the famous **Riemann zeta value** $\zeta(4) = \pi^4 / 90$. ### Step 6 — Substitute Back $$E_b = \frac{C_1 T^4}{C_2^4} \cdot \frac{\pi^4}{15} = \frac{\pi^4 C_1}{15 \, C_2^4} \cdot T^4$$ ### Step 7 — Identify Stefan-Boltzmann Constant $$\sigma = \frac{\pi^4 C_1}{15 \, C_2^4} = \frac{\pi^4 \cdot 2\pi h c^2}{15 \left(\dfrac{hc}{k_B}\right)^4} = \frac{2 \pi^5 k_B^4}{15 h^3 c^2}$$ Plugging in numerical values: $$\sigma = \frac{2 \pi^5 (1.381 \times 10^{-23})^4}{15 \times (6.626 \times 10^{-34})^3 \times (2.998 \times 10^8)^2} = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W/m}^2 \cdot \text{K}^4$$ ### Step 8 — Final Result > [!IMPORTANT] > $$\boxed{E_b = \sigma T^4, \quad \text{where} \quad \sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W/m}^2 \cdot \text{K}^4}$$ > This is the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What | Problem gives you... | Use this law | Computes... | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Temperature and asks peak wavelength | Wien's Displacement Law | $\lambda_{max} = b / T$ | | Peak wavelength and asks temperature | Wien's Displacement Law | $T = b / \lambda_{max}$ | | Temperature and asks total radiation per unit area | Stefan-Boltzmann Law | $E_b = \sigma T^4$ | | Temperature, area and asks total power | Stefan-Boltzmann Law | $Q = \sigma T^4 \cdot A$ | | Two temperatures, asks ratio of radiation | Both | $\dfrac{E_{b1}}{E_{b2}} = \left(\dfrac{T_1}{T_2}\right)^4$ | | Two temperatures, asks ratio of peak wavelength | Wien | $\dfrac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2} = \dfrac{T_2}{T_1}$ | # 4. Examiners love diagrams, and labelling these two features will fetch full marks. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!NOTE] > **Pattern Recall:** KTU 2024 scheme Part A carries **3 marks each** (short answer, ~50 words), and Part B carries **14 marks each** (long answer, with internal choice). For Module 4 in PCIET303, questions on these two laws are commonly asked for either short conceptual answer or full numerical problem solving. --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` > **State Wien's Displacement Law. What is its physical significance?** **Model Answer:** Wien's Displacement Law states that the wavelength at which the spectral emissive power of a blackbody is maximum is inversely proportional to its absolute temperature. Mathematically: $$\lambda_{max} \cdot T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m} \cdot \text{K}$$ **Physical Significance:** As the temperature of a blackbody increases, the wavelength of maximum emission shifts toward shorter wavelengths. This explains why a body, when heated, first glows dull red, then orange, then white. It is used to determine the surface temperature of stars and in optical pyrometers for non-contact temperature measurement. > **KTU Mark Distribution:** Definition (1 mark) + Formula (1 mark) + Significance (1 mark). --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` > **State Stefan-Boltzmann Law. Explain why radiation heat transfer becomes dominant at high temperatures.** **Model Answer:** The Stefan-Boltzmann Law states that the total emissive power of a blackbody is directly proportional to the fourth power of its absolute temperature: $$E_b = \sigma T^4, \quad \sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W/m}^2 \cdot \text{K}^4$$ **Why dominant at high $T$:** Heat transfer by convection and conduction is approximately proportional to $\Delta T$ (linear), while radiation is proportional to $T^4$ (highly nonlinear). At low temperatures, convection and conduction dominate. As $T$ rises, $T^4$ grows extremely rapidly and radiation overtakes the other modes. For example, at furnace temperatures ($1500 \ \text{K}$ and above), radiation accounts for the majority of heat transfer inside combustion chambers. > **KTU Mark Distribution:** Statement with formula (1.5 marks) + High-$T$ dominance explanation (1.5 marks). --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > Per the KTU 2024 scheme, every Part B question in Module 4 has an **internal choice**. The student must answer either **OR-Question A** or **OR-Question B**. Both are presented below. --- ### OR-Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` > **(a)** Starting from Planck's spectral distribution law, derive the **Stefan-Boltzmann law** of blackbody radiation. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A furnace has a blackbody inside it at a temperature of $1800 \ \text{K}$. Calculate: > - (i) the wavelength at which maximum spectral emissive power occurs, > - (ii) the total emissive power of the blackbody, > - (iii) the maximum spectral emissive power, > - (iv) the total power radiated if the effective radiating area of the furnace is $0.5 \ \text{m}^2$. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): **Assumption:** The blackbody is in thermal equilibrium, and radiation consists of discrete energy quanta (Planck's hypothesis). **Planck's Law:** $$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{C_2}{\lambda T}\right) - 1 \right]}$$ **Total emissive power:** $$E_b = \int_0^\infty E_{b\lambda} \, d\lambda = \int_0^\infty \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{C_2}{\lambda T}\right) - 1 \right]} d\lambda$$ **Substitution:** Let $u = \dfrac{C_2}{\lambda T} \Rightarrow \lambda = \dfrac{C_2}{uT}, \quad d\lambda = -\dfrac{C_2}{u^2 T} du$. $$E_b = \frac{C_1 T^4}{C_2^4} \int_0^\infty \frac{u^3}{e^u - 1} du$$ **Series expansion:** $\dfrac{1}{e^u - 1} = \sum_{n=1}^\infty e^{-nu}$ $$\int_0^\infty \frac{u^3}{e^u - 1} du = 6 \sum_{n=1}^\infty \frac{1}{n^4} = 6 \cdot \frac{\pi^4}{90} = \frac{\pi^4}{15}$$ **Therefore:** $$E_b = \frac{\pi^4 C_1}{15 C_2^4} T^4 = \sigma T^4$$ with $\sigma = \dfrac{2\pi^5 k_B^4}{15 h^3 c^2} = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W/m}^2 \cdot \text{K}^4$. **(7 marks distributed: Planck's law 1, substitution 2, series + integral 2, final result 2)** #### Model Solution for (b): **Given:** $T = 1800 \ \text{K}$, $A = 0.5 \ \text{m}^2$, $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m} \cdot \text{K}$, $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W/m}^2 \cdot \text{K}^4$. **(i) Wavelength of maximum emission (Wien's Law):** $$\lambda_{max} = \frac{b}{T} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{1800} = 1.61 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m} = 1.61 \ \mu\text{m}$$ This lies in the near-infrared region. **(ii) Total emissive power (Stefan-Boltzmann Law):** $$E_b = \sigma T^4 = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (1800)^4$$ Computing $(1800)^4$: $(1800)^2 = 3.24 \times 10^6$ $(1800)^4 = (3.24 \times 10^6)^2 = 10.4976 \times 10^{12} = 1.04976 \times 10^{13}$ $$E_b = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.04976 \times 10^{13} = 5.952 \times 10^5 \ \text{W/m}^2 = 595.2 \ \text{kW/m}^2$$ **(iii) Maximum spectral emissive power:** Using the empirical relation $E_{b\lambda, max} = 1.285 \times 10^{-5} \cdot T^5$ (W/m³): $$E_{b\lambda, max} = 1.285 \times 10^{-5} \times (1800)^5$$ $(1800)^5 = (1800)^4 \times 1800 = 1.04976 \times 10^{13} \times 1800 = 1.8896 \times 10^{16}$ $$E_{b\lambda, max} = 1.285 \times 10^{-5} \times 1.8896 \times 10^{16} = 2.428 \times 10^{11} \ \text{W/m}^3$$ **Alternative form** (in $\text{W/m}^2 \cdot \mu\text{m}$): $$E_{b\lambda, max} = 2.428 \times 10^5 \ \text{W/m}^2 \cdot \mu\text{m}$$ **(iv) Total power radiated:** $$Q = E_b \cdot A = 5.952 \times 10^5 \times 0.5 = 2.976 \times 10^5 \ \text{W} \approx 297.6 \ \text{kW}$$ > **KTU Mark Distribution:** (i) 1.5 marks, (ii) 2 marks, (iii) 1.5 marks, (iv) 2 marks. --- ### OR-Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` > **(a)** State and derive **Wien's Displacement Law** from Planck's spectral distribution. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** The Sun's surface temperature is approximately $5800 \ \text{K}$. Treating the Sun as a blackbody with radius $R = 6.96 \times 10^8 \ \text{m}$: > - (i) determine the wavelength at which maximum emission occurs, > - (ii) compute the total emissive power at the Sun's surface, > - (iii) estimate the total power radiated by the Sun, > - (iv) comment on the colour of sunlight in the visible range. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): **Statement:** Wien's Displacement Law states that: $$\lambda_{max} \cdot T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m} \cdot \text{K}$$ **Derivation:** From Planck's law: $$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{C_2}{\lambda T}\right) - 1 \right]}$$ At the peak, $\dfrac{dE_{b\lambda}}{d\lambda} = 0$. This is equivalent to minimising: $$f(\lambda) = \lambda^5 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{C_2}{\lambda T}\right) - 1 \right]$$ Differentiating and simplifying (refer to detailed derivation in Section 3.1): $$(5 - x) e^x = 5, \quad \text{where } x = \frac{C_2}{\lambda_{max} T}$$ The numerical solution yields: $$x = 4.9651$$ Hence: $$\lambda_{max} T = \frac{C_2}{x} = \frac{1.4388 \times 10^{-2}}{4.9651} = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m} \cdot \text{K}$$ **Wien's Displacement Law** is thus: $$\boxed{\lambda_{max} \cdot T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m} \cdot \text{K}}$$ > **KTU Mark Distribution:** Statement 1 mark, Plan 1 mark, Differentiation 2 marks, Solving 2 marks, Final result 1 mark. #### Model Solution for (b): **Given:** $T = 5800 \ \text{K}$, $R = 6.96 \times 10^8 \ \text{m}$. **(i) Peak wavelength (Wien's Law):** $$\lambda_{max} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{5800} = 4.997 \times 10^{-7} \ \text{m} \approx 0.5 \ \mu\text{m} = 500 \ \text{nm}$$ This lies in the **green-yellow** region of the visible spectrum. **(ii) Total emissive power (Stefan-Boltzmann Law):** $$E_b = \sigma T^4 = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (5800)^4$$ Compute $(5800)^4$: $(5800)^2 = 33.64 \times 10^6 = 3.364 \times 10^7$ $(5800)^4 = (3.364 \times 10^7)^2 = 11.317 \times 10^{14} = 1.1317 \times 10^{15}$ $$E_b = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.1317 \times 10^{15} = 6.417 \times 10^7 \ \text{W/m}^2 = 64.17 \ \text{MW/m}^2$$ **(iii) Total power radiated by the Sun:** The Sun's surface area: $$A = 4 \pi R^2 = 4 \pi \times (6.96 \times 10^8)^2$$ $R^2 = 48.44 \times 10^{16} = 4.844 \times 10^{17} \ \text{m}^2$ $$A = 4 \pi \times 4.844 \times 10^{17} = 6.087 \times 10^{18} \ \text{m}^2$$ Total power (luminosity): $$Q = E_b \cdot A = 6.417 \times 10^7 \times 6.087 \times 10^{18}$$ $$Q = 3.906 \times 10^{26} \ \text{W}$$ This is in excellent agreement with the accepted solar luminosity of $\sim 3.828 \times 10^{26} \ \text{W}$. **(iv) Comment on colour:** Since $\lambda_{max} \approx 500 \ \text{nm}$ falls within the **visible range** and the spectrum contains all visible wavelengths (the Sun's blackbody curve is broad), the Sun appears **white/yellowish-white** to the human eye. The yellow tinge is partly due to atmospheric scattering of blue light, removing it before reaching the observer. > **KTU Mark Distribution:** (i) 1.5 marks, (ii) 2 marks, (iii) 2 marks, (iv) 1.5 marks. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (per KTU Valuation Key Patterns):** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert $\mu\text{m}$ to $\text{m}$ or vice versa:** A peak wavelength answer of "$1.61$" without units (or with the wrong unit) loses 0.5–1 mark. Always specify: $\text{m}$, $\mu\text{m}$, or $\text{nm}$. > 2. **Using $T$ in $^\circ\text{C}$ instead of Kelvin:** This is the *single most common* error. Stefan-Boltzmann law is invalid for Celsius. Always convert: $T(\text{K}) = T(^\circ\text{C}) + 273.15$. > 3. **Confusing the Wien's constant value:** Some students write $2.898 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{mm} \cdot \text{K}$ — this is **wrong**. The correct unit is $\text{m} \cdot \text{K}$. If you write $\text{mm} \cdot \text{K}$, your $\lambda_{max}$ will be off by 1000x. > 4. **Forgetting to multiply by area for total power:** The "total emissive power" $E_b$ is per unit area. For total power, always compute $Q = E_b \cdot A$. > 5. **Round-off errors in $T^4$:** Use scientific notation for large numbers; never write $(1800)^4$ as a long integer — keep it as $1.04976 \times 10^{13}$. > 6. **Skipping intermediate steps in derivations:** Even if the final answer is correct, the KTU 2024 scheme requires the **methodology** to be visible. Always show the substitution, the integration, and the substitution of constants. Each missing step = at least 0.5–1 mark deduction. > 7. **Mixing up $C_1$ and $C_2$:** $C_1$ has units of $\text{W} \cdot \text{m}^2$, $C_2$ has units of $\text{m} \cdot \text{K}$. A common slip is swapping them in the final formula. > 8. **Sign convention for limits in integration:** When changing variables, limits flip — make sure to handle the sign correctly (or use absolute value with a minus sign). --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Pin This Before the Exam!** - **Wien's Displacement Law:** $\lambda_{max} \cdot T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m} \cdot \text{K}$. Predicts **where** the peak of emission occurs. - **Stefan-Boltzmann Law:** $E_b = \sigma T^4$, with $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W/m}^2 \cdot \text{K}^4$. Predicts **how much** total energy is emitted. - **Both laws emerge from Planck's distribution** — Wien by differentiation, Stefan-Boltzmann by integration. - **Numerical constant check:** $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ (NOT $2.898 \times 10^{-2}$ or $0.2898$). $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ (NOT $5.67 \times 10^{-9}$). - **Units are non-negotiable:** Wavelengths in metres, temperatures in Kelvin, $E_b$ in $\text{W/m}^2$. - **4th-power rule of thumb:** Doubling $T$ → 16x radiation. Halving $T$ → 1/16 radiation. *This single fact has appeared in KTU 2019, 2022, 2023 papers.* - **Reverse application:** Given $\lambda_{max}$, we can find the source's temperature (used in pyrometers and astronomy). - **The Sun at $5800 \ \text{K}$** radiates with $\lambda_{max} \approx 0.5 \ \mu\text{m}$ and luminosity $\sim 3.9 \times 10^{26} \ \text{W}$. - **A furnace at $1800 \ \text{K}$** radiates with $\lambda_{max} \approx 1.6 \ \mu\text{m}$ and $E_b \approx 595 \ \text{kW/m}^2$. - **KTU 2024 weightage:** Expect 7-mark or 14-mark question on these laws in End Semester Examination. Module 4 is consistently tested in Part B. - **Cross-link with Module 3:** Emissivity $\varepsilon$ modifies Stefan-Boltzmann to $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ for *real* (grey) bodies — this is a frequent follow-up. - **Energy by blackbody fraction:** $F_{0 \to \lambda T}$ is a tabulated function — useful in band-emission problems. - **Remember:** Wien's law is sometimes called Wien's *displacement* law (in Indian textbooks) and occasionally *Wien's distribution law* (in Western textbooks) — both refer to the same relation.

topics.title: IC Engines: Performance Testing and Combustion in IC Engines - Normal and Abnormal Combustion in SI and CI Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET303 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** IC Engines: Performance Testing and Combustion in IC Engines - Normal and Abnormal Combustion in SI and CI Engines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 IC Engine Performance Testing

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)
**Performance testing of an Internal Combustion (IC) engine** is the systematic experimental determination of operational, energetic, and emission characteristics of an engine under controlled load, speed, and fuel conditions, to evaluate quantities such as **Indicated Power (I.P.)**, **Brake Power (B.P.)**, **Mechanical Efficiency (η_mech)**, **Thermal Efficiency (η_th)**, **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)**, and the **Heat Balance Sheet**. It is conducted using standard dynamometers (Rope Brake, Prony Brake, Hydraulic, or Eddy Current type) following IS: 1458 / ISO 3046-1 testing codes.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Gym Test for an Engine"
Think of an IC engine as an athlete (a powerlifter) competing in a gym:
- The **rope brake dynamometer** is the bench-press machine that loads the engine.
- The **spring balance** measures how hard the engine is pushing against that load.
- **Brake Power (B.P.)** = the actual useful work the engine delivers to the crankshaft pulley.
- **Indicated Power (I.P.)** = the total "internal strength" generated inside the cylinder.
- **Friction Power (F.P.) = I.P. − B.P.** = the energy lost to heat, friction of pistons, bearings, and pumping — like the calories the athlete burns internally without producing visible lift.
- **Thermal Efficiency** = how efficiently the athlete converts food (fuel) into lifting work (output).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Performance testing under Module 4 mandates the use of **rope brake dynamometer (the most frequently asked experimental setup)** along with the derivation of B.P., I.P., and mechanical efficiency. Numerical problems on heat balance sheets and BSFC are **sure-shot 14-mark questions** every semester.

---

## 1.2 Combustion in IC Engines — Normal vs. Abnormal

### Formal Definition
**Combustion** in an IC engine is the rapid exothermic oxidation reaction between the hydrocarbon fuel and oxidizer (air), releasing chemical energy that is converted into mechanical work via the piston-crank mechanism.

- In **Spark Ignition (SI) engines**: A homogeneous air-fuel mixture is compressed and ignited by an external spark plug. Normal combustion proceeds via flame kernel formation, smooth flame propagation, and after-burning.
- In **Compression Ignition (CI) engines**: Only air is compressed to high pressure/temperature; fuel is injected and auto-ignites. Normal combustion occurs in three stages — ignition delay, rapid (uncontrolled) combustion, and controlled combustion.

### Intuitive Analogy — "Lighting a Campfire"
Imagine lighting a campfire in two different ways:
- **SI Engine (Spark Ignition):** You first mix the matchstick (spark) with kindling (fuel-air mixture that is already mixed). When the spark strikes, the flame spreads smoothly outward — like a wave.
- **CI Engine (Compression Ignition):** You compress wood chips (air) until they're hot, then squirt fuel oil (diesel) onto them. The fuel ignites spontaneously where it contacts the hot air.
- **Abnormal Combustion (Knocking):** Instead of a smooth wave of flame, a part of the fuel-air mixture self-ignites explosively BEFORE the flame front reaches it — like two clapping hands colliding mid-air, producing a metallic "knock" sound.

### Key Terminology Glossary

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Ignition Lag** | Time/angle between spark and formation of a self-sustaining flame kernel. |
| **Flame Propagation** | Steady movement of the flame front through the unburned mixture. |
| **Knocking (SI)** | Auto-ignition of the end gas before the normal flame front reaches it. |
| **Surface Ignition** | Premature ignition of mixture by hot spots (deposits, valves, plugs). |
| **Pre-Ignition** | Ignition occurring before the spark (a form of surface ignition). |
| **Detonation** | Violent, nearly-instantaneous auto-ignition of the end gas. |
| **Diesel Knock** | Sharp pressure rise in CI engine due to excessive ignition delay. |
| **Delay Period** | Time between start of fuel injection and start of combustion in CI. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Students frequently confuse **detonation** with **pre-ignition**. Remember: *Detonation = spontaneous end-gas ignition AFTER the spark, BEFORE flame arrival. Pre-ignition = ignition BEFORE the spark.* These are distinct abnormal phenomena.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Lower Heating Value (LHV) of gasoline:** ≈ **44,000 kJ/kg**
- **Lower Heating Value (LHV) of diesel:** ≈ **42,500 kJ/kg**
- **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (gasoline):** ≈ **14.7 : 1**
- **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (diesel):** ≈ **14.5 : 1**
- **Ratio of specific heats (γ) for combustion products:** ≈ **1.3 to 1.35**
- **Universal Gas Constant (R̄):** = **8.314 kJ/kmol·K**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 IC Engine Performance Parameters — Conceptual Logic

The performance of an IC engine is benchmarked across **three orthogonal axes**:
1. **Energy axis** → How effectively the chemical energy in the fuel is converted into work (thermal efficiencies).
2. **Power axis** → How much useful mechanical power is delivered to the output shaft (B.P., I.P., F.P.).
3. **Consumption axis** → How much fuel is consumed per unit work (SFC, BSFC, ISFC).

### Step-by-Step Logic Chain
- The fuel releases **calorific value (CV)** upon combustion → this is the gross energy input.
- A fraction is lost to **exhaust gases**, **cooling water**, and **unaccounted losses** (radiation, convection).
- The remainder becomes **Indicated Power (I.P.)** — the energy that crosses the cylinder walls as useful expansion work.
- Subtracting **Friction Power (F.P.)** gives the **Brake Power (B.P.)** available at the flywheel.
- The ratio B.P./I.P. defines the **Mechanical Efficiency (η_mech)**, capturing how much internal power reaches the output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These performance metrics govern the design of all prime movers used in **automotive propulsion** (cars, trucks, two-wheelers), **stationary power generation** (gensets), **marine propulsion**, and **aerospace APUs**. Modern BS-VI/Euro 6 emission norms are evaluated using the same heat balance + BSFC framework.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Performance Testing)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Indicated Power (multi-cylinder, 4-stroke) | $I.P. = \dfrac{p_{im} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot n \cdot K}{60}$ | kW | K = number of cylinders; n = rpm; p_im in kPa |
| 2 | Indicated Power (2-stroke) | $I.P. = \dfrac{p_{im} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot n \cdot K}{30}$ | kW | Power strokes double per revolution |
| 3 | Brake Power (Rope Brake) | $B.P. = \dfrac{(W - S) \cdot \pi D \cdot N}{60 \times 1000}$ | kW | W, S = dead/spring load (kgf); D = pulley dia (m); N = rpm |
| 4 | Brake Power (Prony Brake) | $B.P. = \dfrac{F \cdot R \cdot N}{1000}$ | kW | F = force on lever (N); R = effective arm length (m) |
| 5 | Friction Power | $F.P. = I.P. - B.P.$ | kW | Always positive |
| 6 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{B.P.}{I.P.}$ | decimal/\% | Range: 0.80–0.92 for well-tuned engines |
| 7 | Brake Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{B.P. \times 3600}{\dot{m}_f \times CV}$ | decimal | CV in kJ/kg |
| 8 | Indicated Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{ith} = \dfrac{I.P. \times 3600}{\dot{m}_f \times CV}$ | decimal | CV in kJ/kg |
| 9 | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{B.P.}$ | kg/kWh | Lower is better; petrol ≈ 0.30, diesel ≈ 0.25 |
| 10 | Indicated Specific Fuel Consumption | $ISFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{I.P.}$ | kg/kWh | Same direction of preference |
| 11 | Brake Mean Effective Pressure | $BMEP = \dfrac{B.P. \times 60 \times 10^3}{L \cdot A \cdot n \cdot K}$ | kPa | 4-stroke formula |
| 12 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{m_{a,actual}}{\rho_{a} \cdot V_s}$ | decimal | n for 4-stroke, n/2 for 2-stroke |
| 13 | Air-Fuel Ratio (A/F) | $A/F = \dfrac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_f}$ | — | Stoichiometric: 14.7 (petrol), 14.5 (diesel) |
| 14 | Heat in B.P. (per second) | $q_b = B.P. \times 1$ | kW | 1 kW = 1 kJ/s |
| 15 | Heat in cooling water | $q_w = \dot{m}_w \cdot C_{pw} \cdot (T_{wo} - T_{wi})$ | kW | $C_{pw}$ = **4.18 kJ/kg·K** |
| 16 | Heat in exhaust gas | $q_g = \dot{m}_g \cdot C_{pg} \cdot (T_g - T_a)$ | kW | $C_{pg}$ ≈ 1.005 kJ/kg·K |
| 17 | Heat unaccounted (radiation, convection) | $q_u = q_{in} - (q_b + q_w + q_g)$ | kW | Closing balance |
| 18 | Otto Cycle Efficiency | $\eta_{otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ | — | SI engine ideal cycle |
| 19 | Diesel Cycle Efficiency | $\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$ | — | CI engine ideal cycle; $\rho = r_c$ |
| 20 | Air Standard Efficiency (Dual) | $\eta_{dual} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \dfrac{\beta \rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\beta \rho - 1}$ | — | Limited performance SI engine model |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Table Formatting:** The LaTeX `\vert` (vertical bar) symbol is used wherever absolute value or stroke ratio is denoted to keep the markdown table parser intact. **Never use raw `\vert`** symbol in KTU exams — use plain vertical bar in handwriting or `\vert` in LaTeX.

---

## 2.3 Combustion in SI Engines — Normal Combustion Stages

Normal combustion in an SI engine progresses through **three distinct stages**:

### Stage 1 — Ignition Lag (0 to ~5% of crank angle)
- Begins at the instant of spark.
- The spark creates a small kernel of ionized, high-temperature gas.
- The kernel grows by thermal diffusion until it becomes a self-sustaining flame.
- Duration ≈ **0.0001 to 0.001 s** or **1°–5° of crank angle**.
- Excessively long ignition lag → slow flame kernel growth → **higher probability of knocking**.

### Stage 2 — Flame Propagation (5% to ~50% of crank angle)
- The flame front travels as a thin spherical/toroidal surface from the spark plug.
- Flame speed = **15 to 25 m/s** (typical for gasoline).
- Combustion rate (dθ/dα) increases, peaks around 30°–40° crank angle ATDC, then declines.
- Pressure rises sharply — the piston is pushed down.
- The remaining unburned mixture between the flame front and piston is called the **end gas**.

### Stage 3 — After-Burning (50% to 100%)
- Occurs during the expansion stroke when piston is moving away.
- Combustion of the last pockets of mixture near crevices and wall boundary layers.
- Sustained heat release, gradual pressure decay.
- Contributes ~5–10% of total heat released.

---

## 2.4 Abnormal Combustion in SI Engines

### A. Knocking (Detonation)
- **Cause:** End gas auto-ignites explosively before the normal flame front reaches it.
- **Effect:** Sharp pressure spike (40–80 bar above normal), metallic "pinging" sound, piston/cylinder damage.
- **Controlling Factors:**
  - Fuel property: **Octane Number (ON)** — higher ON = more knock-resistant.
  - Compression Ratio (r): Higher r → higher end-gas temperature → more knock.
  - Spark advance: Retarding spark reduces knocking.
  - End-gas temperature & density (load, speed).
- **Prevention:** Use of higher-octane fuels, antiknock additives (TEL/MMT historically; now aromatics and ethanol blends), engine knock sensors with retard algorithms.

### B. Surface Ignition
- **Cause:** Hot spots in the combustion chamber (overheated valves, carbon deposits, glowing spark plug electrodes) ignite the mixture **independently of the spark**.
- **Sub-types:**
  - **Pre-ignition:** Occurs before the spark → reduces effective compression → power loss.
  - **Post-ignition:** Occurs after the spark but via hot spots rather than flame front.
- **Effect:** Localized superheating, pre-ignition-induced secondary auto-ignition (very destructive).

### C. Difference Between Knocking and Surface Ignition

| Property | Knocking (Detonation) | Surface Ignition |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Spontaneous auto-ignition of end gas | Hot spot in chamber |
| Timing relative to spark | After spark | Before or after spark |
| Pressure pattern | Sharp high-frequency oscillations | Smooth but premature pressure rise |
| Sound | Metallic pinging | Soft thud / mild rumble |
| Octane sensitivity | Strongly sensitive | Less sensitive |

---

## 2.5 Combustion in CI Engines — Normal Combustion Stages

CI engine combustion (after Rudolf Diesel's principle of compression ignition):

### Stage 1 — Ignition Delay (Delay Period)
- Begins at the instant fuel injection starts.
- Includes:
  - **Physical delay:** Atomization, vaporization, mixing of fuel with hot air.
  - **Chemical delay:** Pre-flame reactions (low-temperature oxidation → blue cool flames → hot flames).
- Duration ≈ **0.001 to 0.003 s** depending on **cetane number**, temperature, pressure.
- **No heat release** during this stage — fuel accumulates in the chamber.

### Stage 2 — Rapid Combustion (Uncontrolled Combustion)
- All the fuel accumulated during the delay period burns almost simultaneously.
- Sharp pressure rise — this is where **diesel knock** originates if the delay is excessive.
- Duration ≈ 10°–30° crank angle.
- Combustion rate is dominated by fuel quantity prepared during Stage 1.

### Stage 3 — Controlled Combustion
- Fuel injected after the rapid combustion phase burns in a controlled manner as it mixes with remaining air.
- Combustion rate is now governed by the rate of injection (mixing-controlled).
- Pressure remains roughly constant or slightly declines.

---

## 2.6 Abnormal Combustion in CI Engines

### A. Diesel Knock (Rough Running / Delay Knock)
- **Cause:** Excessive ignition delay → large fuel accumulation → uncontrolled burning of accumulated charge → sharp pressure rise (~80 bar/s vs. ideal 4–6 bar/s).
- **Sound:** A sharp, harsh "diesel knock" — distinct from SI engine pinging.
- **Effects:** Increased mechanical/thermal stress, noise, vibration, harshness (NVH), power loss, possible piston crown failure.
- **Controlling Factors:**
  - **Cetane Number (CN)** of fuel — higher CN → shorter delay → less knock. Diesel fuel typically CN ≈ 45–55.
  - **Intake air temperature** — pre-heating reduces delay.
  - **Injection timing** — earlier injection increases delay; optimum is typically 10°–20° BTDC.
  - **Injection pressure** — finer atomization shortens physical delay.
  - **Compression ratio** — higher r → higher temperature → shorter chemical delay.

### B. Other CI Abnormalities
- **Misfiring:** Local over-leaning or over-enrichment; cylinder cutout.
- **Excessive smoke:** Incomplete combustion of fuel-rich pockets.
- **After-burning in exhaust manifold:** Continued combustion of CO + unburned HC due to insufficient oxygen.

---

## 2.7 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Performance testing rigs** are mandatory quality-assurance tools at OEMs (Tata Motors, Mahindra, Ashok Leyland, Maruti Suzuki) before engine sign-off.
- **Knocking & detonation research** drove the invention of lead-free fuels, antiknock additives, knock sensors (used in ~100% of modern gasoline cars for closed-loop ignition control).
- **Cetane number testing** governs global diesel fuel standardization (ASTM D613, ISO 5165).
- **Heat balance sheets** are the foundation of **engine cooling system design**, **exhaust manifold thermal analysis**, and **turbocharger matching**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Case Study:** In 2019, BS-VI emission norms in India mandated precise engine control via **retarded ignition timing with knock sensors** and **common-rail diesel injection** to keep CN ≥ 51 across all commercial diesel fuels — directly rooted in the abnormal-combustion principles discussed above.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Brake Power from a Rope Brake Dynamometer

### Setup
A rope is wound over the engine flywheel/pulley. One end is attached to a dead weight **W** (kgf), the other end to a spring balance **S** (kgf). The effective load is **W − S** (since the spring balance supports part of the weight). The net tangential force **F_net = (W − S) × g** N.

### Step-by-Step Algebraic Derivation

**Step 1 — Tangential force on the pulley:**

$$
F_{net} = (W - S) \cdot g \quad [\text{N}]
$$

**Step 2 — Velocity of the rope (tangential speed of the pulley surface):**

$$
v = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60} \quad [\text{m/s}]
$$

where **D** = pulley diameter (m), **N** = engine rpm. The factor 60 converts rpm to revolutions per second.

**Step 3 — Power transmitted (Work per unit time):**

$$
P = F_{net} \cdot v = (W - S) \cdot g \cdot \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kW (dividing by 1000) and substitute g = 9.81 m/s²:**

$$
B.P. = \dfrac{(W - S) \cdot \pi D N \cdot 9.81}{60 \times 1000}
$$

**Step 5 — In KTU textbook convention, the formula is often written without g by using kgf directly:**

$$
\boxed{B.P. = \dfrac{(W - S) \cdot \pi D N}{60 \times 1000} \quad [\text{kW}]}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The unit "kgf" (kilogram-force) already encodes gravity, so the formula without `g` is dimensionally correct under the gravitational system. **KTU examiners accept both versions** — be consistent within a single solution.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Indicated Mean Effective Pressure (IMEP) from Indicator Diagram

The **indicator diagram** is the P–V plot traced by a mechanical or electronic indicator. The average height of the diagram, when divided by the spring constant, gives the **IMEP** (p_im).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Work done per cycle (single cylinder):**

$$
W_{cycle} = \oint p \, dV = p_{im} \cdot (V_1 - V_2) = p_{im} \cdot V_s
$$

where $V_s$ = swept volume = $\dfrac{\pi}{4} D_b^2 \cdot L$ (D_b = bore, L = stroke).

**Step 2 — Number of power strokes per second:**
- 4-stroke: $n_p = \dfrac{N}{120}$ per cylinder
- 2-stroke: $n_p = \dfrac{N}{60}$ per cylinder

**Step 3 — Power per cylinder:**

$$
P_{cyl} = W_{cycle} \cdot n_p = p_{im} \cdot V_s \cdot n_p
$$

**Step 4 — For K cylinders:**

$$
\boxed{I.P. = p_{im} \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_b^2 \cdot L \cdot \dfrac{N}{60 \times 10^6} \cdot K \quad [\text{kW}]}
$$

(when p_im in kPa, D_b in mm, L in mm; or use SI consistently).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Brake Thermal Efficiency

**Step 1 — Energy input rate from fuel:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_f \cdot CV \quad [\text{kJ/s} = \text{kW}]
$$

**Step 2 — Useful energy output rate (B.P.):**

$$
\dot{Q}_{out,useful} = B.P. \quad [\text{kW}]
$$

**Step 3 — Definition of brake thermal efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{\text{Useful energy output}}{\text{Energy input}} = \dfrac{B.P.}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert B.P. from kW to kJ/h (since BSFC is in kg/kWh):**

$$
B.P. \, [\text{kJ/h}] = B.P. \, [\text{kW}] \times 3600
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{B.P. \times 3600}{\dot{m}_f \times CV}}
$$

This is the most-tested KTU formula for 14-mark numerical problems.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Air Standard Efficiency of Otto Cycle

Otto cycle is the ideal cycle for SI engines with **four ideal processes:**
1-2: Isentropic compression
2-3: Constant-volume heat addition (combustion)
3-4: Isentropic expansion
4-1: Constant-volume heat rejection

**Step 1 — Compression ratio:**

$$
r = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2}
$$

**Step 2 — Heat added at constant volume:**

$$
Q_{in} = m \cdot C_v \cdot (T_3 - T_2)
$$

**Step 3 — Heat rejected at constant volume:**

$$
Q_{out} = m \cdot C_v \cdot (T_4 - T_1)
$$

**Step 4 — Thermal efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{otto} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply isentropic relations $TV^{\gamma - 1} = \text{const}$:**

$$
T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1}, \quad T_3 = T_4 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$
T_4 - T_1 = \dfrac{T_3 - T_2}{r^{\gamma - 1}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\eta_{otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}}
$$

This efficiency depends **only on compression ratio** r and **γ** (≈ 1.4 for cold air, 1.3 for combustion products).

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Air Standard Efficiency of Diesel Cycle

Diesel cycle has constant-pressure heat addition instead of constant-volume.

**Step 1 — Cut-off ratio:**

$$
\rho = \dfrac{V_3}{V_2}
$$

**Step 2 — Heat added (constant pressure):**

$$
Q_{in} = m \cdot C_p \cdot (T_3 - T_2)
$$

**Step 3 — Heat rejected (constant volume):**

$$
Q_{out} = m \cdot C_v \cdot (T_4 - T_1)
$$

**Step 4 — Using isentropic relations:**

$$
T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1}, \quad T_4 = T_3 \cdot \left(\dfrac{\rho}{r}\right)^{\gamma - 1}
$$

**Step 5 — After algebraic substitution:**

$$
\boxed{\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}}
$$

**Comparison Insight:** For the same compression ratio, **Otto > Diesel**, but Diesel's higher practical r (~17:1 vs. 9:1) makes real-world efficiencies competitive.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Causes of Abnormal Combustion

| Abnormal Phenomenon | Engine Type | Root Cause | Symptom | Remedy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Knocking (Detonation)** | SI | End-gas auto-ignition | Pinging sound, power loss | Higher octane fuel, retard spark |
| **Surface Ignition** | SI | Hot spots in chamber | Pre-spark pressure rise | Remove carbon deposits |
| **Pre-ignition** | SI | Hot spot/overheated plug | Power loss, backfire | Cool intake, spark plug cooling |
| **Run-on (Dieseling)** | SI | Auto-ignition from hot carbon | Engine runs with ignition off | Eliminate carbon deposits |
| **Diesel Knock** | CI | Excessive ignition delay | Sharp pressure rise, NVH | Higher cetane fuel, advance injection |
| **Misfiring** | CI | Improper fuel spray | Loss of cylinder power | Service injectors |
| **Black Smoke** | CI | Rich mixture in over-loaded zones | Particulate emission | Adjust EGR, injection pressure |
| **White Smoke** | CI | Unburned fuel (cold start) | Cold start emission | Glow plug assistance |
