# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define (a) Knocking in SI engine, and (b) Diesel knock in CI engine. State one remedy for each.**

**Model Answer:**

> **(a) Knocking in SI Engine:** Knocking is the abnormal combustion phenomenon in which the unburned **end gas** (the charge ahead of the propagating flame front) auto-ignites spontaneously and instantaneously before the normal flame front reaches it. This produces a sharp, nearly-explosive rise in cylinder pressure, accompanied by a characteristic metallic **"pinging"** sound and possible damage to the piston, cylinder head, and gaskets.
>
> **Remedy:** Use of higher-octane fuel (or fuels with knock-resistant additives such as aromatics, MTBE, ethanol blends); alternatively, retard the spark timing so that end-gas temperature remains below its auto-ignition temperature.

> **(b) Diesel Knock:** Diesel knock is the abnormal combustion in CI engines caused by an **excessive ignition delay period**, during which too much fuel accumulates in the combustion chamber. When this accumulated charge finally ignites, the resulting rapid combustion produces a sharp pressure rise (≈ 80 bar/° crank angle vs. ideal 4–6 bar/°) and a harsh **"knock"** sound.
>
> **Remedy:** Use of higher-cetane-number diesel (CN ≥ 51); increasing injection pressure to shorten physical delay; advancing or optimizing injection timing; pre-heating intake air.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Differentiate between the Otto cycle and Diesel cycle air-standard efficiencies for the same compression ratio.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Otto Cycle | Diesel Cycle |
|---|---|---|
| Heat addition | **Constant volume** | **Constant pressure** |
| Heat addition temperature | Higher peak | Lower peak |
| For same r and γ | **Higher efficiency** | Lower efficiency |
| Efficiency formula | $\eta_{otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ | $\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$ |
| Cut-off ratio $\rho$ | Not applicable (constant V) | Greater than 1 |

**Key Insight:** Since for any $\rho > 1$, the factor $\dfrac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)} > 1$, we get $\eta_{otto} > \eta_{diesel}$ for the same compression ratio. However, the **Diesel engine is operated at higher practical compression ratios** (≈ 17:1) compared to SI engines (≈ 9:1), so in real-world conditions, both engines achieve comparable brake thermal efficiencies of 30–40%.

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / May 2024]`

**A single-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine has the following test data at full load:**
- Brake load: W = 60 kgf, Spring balance reading: S = 6 kgf
- Effective diameter of rope brake pulley: D = 1.2 m
- Engine speed: N = 300 rpm
- Fuel consumption: V_f = 0.012 L/min, density ρ_f = 0.74 kg/L
- Calorific value of fuel: CV = 44,000 kJ/kg
- Indicated mean effective pressure: p_im = 7 bar
- Cylinder bore: D_b = 100 mm, Stroke: L = 120 mm

**Compute:**
**(a)** Brake Power (B.P.) and Brake Thermal Efficiency (η_bth). **[7 marks]**
**(b)** Indicated Power (I.P.), Mechanical Efficiency, and BSFC. **[7 marks]**

---

### Complete Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Brake Power and Brake Thermal Efficiency

**Step 1 — Compute the net load on the brake:**
$$
W - S = 60 - 6 = 54 \text{ kgf}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the rope brake formula (using kgf units directly, k = 1000):**
$$
B.P. = \dfrac{(W - S) \cdot \pi D \cdot N}{60 \times 1000}
$$
$$
B.P. = \dfrac{54 \times \pi \times 1.2 \times 300}{60 \times 1000}
$$
$$
B.P. = \dfrac{54 \times 3.1416 \times 1.2 \times 300}{60{,}000}
$$
$$
B.P. = \dfrac{61{,}075.2}{60{,}000}
$$
$$
\boxed{B.P. \approx 1.018 \text{ kW}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute mass flow rate of fuel:**
$$
\dot{V}_f = 0.012 \text{ L/min} = \dfrac{0.012}{60} \text{ L/s} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ L/s}
$$
$$
\dot{m}_f = \dot{V}_f \cdot \rho_f = 2 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.74 = 1.48 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute heat input rate:**
$$
\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_f \cdot CV = 1.48 \times 10^{-4} \times 44{,}000 = 6.512 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute brake thermal efficiency:**
$$
\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{B.P.}{\dot{Q}_{in}} = \dfrac{1.018}{6.512} \approx 0.1563
$$
$$
\boxed{\eta_{bth} \approx 15.63\%}
$$

---

#### Part (b) — Indicated Power, Mechanical Efficiency, and BSFC

**Step 1 — Compute swept volume of cylinder:**
$$
A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_b^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.1)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$
$$
V_s = A \cdot L = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.12 = 9.4248 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Indicated Power (4-stroke, K = 1):**
$$
I.P. = \dfrac{p_{im} \cdot V_s \cdot N}{60 \times 2} = \dfrac{p_{im} \cdot A \cdot L \cdot N}{120}
$$

Note: $p_{im} = 7 \text{ bar} = 700 \text{ kPa} = 7 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2$.

$$
I.P. = \dfrac{7 \times 10^5 \times 9.4248 \times 10^{-4} \times 300}{60}
$$
$$
I.P. = \dfrac{7 \times 10^5 \times 9.4248 \times 10^{-4} \times 300}{60}
$$

Working numerator:
$$
7 \times 10^5 \times 9.4248 \times 10^{-4} = 659.736
$$
$$
659.736 \times 300 = 197{,}920.8
$$
$$
I.P. = \dfrac{197{,}920.8}{60} \approx 3298.68 \text{ W} = 3.299 \text{ kW}
$$

(Using the textbook 4-stroke formula: divide by 60 × 2 = 120 if N is rpm counted over 2 revolutions, but with 60 already representing power strokes per second for 4-stroke × 1 cylinder per 2 revs the result is same here. Using the KTU standard 4-stroke formula gives **I.P. ≈ 3.30 kW**.)

$$
\boxed{I.P. \approx 3.30 \text{ kW}}
$$

**Step 3 — Mechanical Efficiency:**
$$
\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{B.P.}{I.P.} = \dfrac{1.018}{3.30} \approx 0.3085
$$
$$
\boxed{\eta_{mech} \approx 30.85\%}
$$

**Step 4 — BSFC:**
$$
\dot{m}_f \text{ per hour} = 1.48 \times 10^{-4} \times 3600 = 0.5328 \text{ kg/h}
$$
$$
BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{B.P.} = \dfrac{0.5328}{1.018} \approx 0.5234 \text{ kg/kWh}
$$
$$
\boxed{BSFC \approx 0.523 \text{ kg/kWh}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mistake:** Mixing rpm `N` with revolutions/second incorrectly. Always remember: 4-stroke has one power stroke per **2 revolutions**, so power strokes/sec = N/60 × (1/2) for 4-stroke.
> 2. **Forgot Spring Balance Subtraction:** Many students compute B.P. with only **W** instead of **(W − S)** — losing **2 full marks**.
> 3. **Pressure Unit Error:** IMEP in bar must be converted to **kPa or N/m²** before substituting in SI units. Bar × 100 = kPa.
> 4. **CV Unit Confusion:** Petrol CV = 44,000 kJ/kg, not 44,000 J/kg. Use consistently.
> 5. **η_mech > 1 Trap:** If B.P. > I.P., check sign of friction power — typically $\eta_{mech} \le 1$. If you get > 1, you've swapped a formula.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice): `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke SI engine has bore = 90 mm, stroke = 100 mm, runs at 3000 rpm. Indicator diagram gives p_im = 8.5 bar. Rope brake gives W = 50 kgf, S = 4 kgf, pulley diameter = 1.0 m. Fuel consumption = 8.5 kg/h, CV = 43,000 kJ/kg.**

**Compute:**
**(a)** Indicated Power, Brake Power, and Mechanical Efficiency. **[7 marks]**
**(b)** Brake Thermal Efficiency and ISFC. Comment on the values obtained. **[7 marks]**

---

### Complete Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — I.P., B.P., and η_mech

**Step 1 — Swept volume of one cylinder:**
$$
A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.09)^2 = 6.362 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$
$$
V_s = 6.362 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.10 = 6.362 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Indicated Power (4-stroke, K = 4):**
$$
I.P. = \dfrac{p_{im} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot n \cdot K}{60}
$$
with $p_{im} = 8.5 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2$, $n = 3000$ rpm, $K = 4$.
$$
I.P. = \dfrac{8.5 \times 10^5 \times 0.10 \times 6.362 \times 10^{-3} \times 3000 \times 4}{60}
$$
$$
I.P. = \dfrac{8.5 \times 10^5 \times 0.10 \times 6.362 \times 10^{-3} \times 12{,}000}{60}
$$
$$
I.P. = \dfrac{8.5 \times 10^5 \times 0.10 \times 6.362 \times 10^{-3}}{60} \times 12{,}000
$$

Inner block:
$$
8.5 \times 10^5 \times 0.10 = 85{,}000
$$
$$
85{,}000 \times 6.362 \times 10^{-3} = 540.77
$$
$$
\dfrac{540.77}{60} = 9.013 \text{ per cylinder per rev}
$$
$$
I.P. = 9.013 \times 12{,}000 / 60 \cdot (\text{recheck})
$$

Let me recompute cleanly:

$$
I.P. = \dfrac{p_{im} \cdot V_s \cdot N \cdot K}{60 \times 2}
$$

For 4-stroke: power strokes per second per cylinder = $N / (60 \times 2) = 3000 / 120 = 25$.

$$
I.P. = p_{im} \cdot V_s \cdot n_p \cdot K
$$
$$
I.P. = 8.5 \times 10^5 \times 6.362 \times 10^{-4} \times 25 \times 4
$$
$$
I.P. = 8.5 \times 10^5 \times 6.362 \times 10^{-4} \times 100
$$
$$
I.P. = 8.5 \times 10^5 \times 6.362 \times 10^{-2}
$$
$$
I.P. = 8.5 \times 6.362 \times 10^{5-2} = 54.077 \times 10^3 = 54{,}077 \text{ W}
$$
$$
\boxed{I.P. \approx 54.08 \text{ kW}}
$$

**Step 3 — Brake Power (rope brake):**
$$
B.P. = \dfrac{(W - S) \cdot \pi D \cdot N}{60 \times 1000}
$$
$$
B.P. = \dfrac{(50 - 4) \times \pi \times 1.0 \times 3000}{60 \times 1000}
$$
$$
B.P. = \dfrac{46 \times 3.1416 \times 3000}{60{,}000}
$$
$$
B.P. = \dfrac{433{,}545.6}{60{,}000}
$$
$$
\boxed{B.P. \approx 7.226 \text{ kW}}
$$

> ⚠️ Note: This low B.P. relative to I.P. implies very high friction — typical of small 4-cyl engines. In realistic tests, B.P. ≈ 80% of I.P.

**Step 4 — Mechanical Efficiency:**
$$
\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{7.226}{54.08} \approx 0.1336
$$
$$
\boxed{\eta_{mech} \approx 13.36\%}
$$

(Low value indicates significant friction loss or computation context — in exam context, present the calculation honestly and add commentary.)

#### Part (b) — η_bth and ISFC + Comment

**Step 1 — Brake Thermal Efficiency:**
$$
\dot{m}_f = 8.5 \text{ kg/h} = \dfrac{8.5}{3600} = 2.361 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}
$$
$$
\dot{Q}_{in} = 2.361 \times 10^{-3} \times 43{,}000 = 101.52 \text{ kW}
$$
$$
\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{7.226}{101.52} \approx 0.0712
$$
$$
\boxed{\eta_{bth} \approx 7.12\%}
$$

**Step 2 — ISFC:**
$$
ISFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \text{ [kg/h]}}{I.P. \text{ [kW]}} = \dfrac{8.5}{54.08} \approx 0.157 \text{ kg/kWh}
$$
$$
\boxed{ISFC \approx 0.157 \text{ kg/kWh}}
$$

**Comment:** A BSFC of ~1.17 kg/kWh (= 8.5/7.226) would be unrealistic (typical BSFC for SI ≈ 0.30 kg/kWh). This indicates either (i) the engine is grossly oversized and lightly loaded, (ii) the test data is synthetic for problem-solving, or (iii) the indicated power reading is anomalously high. **In real-life KTU practicals**, BSFC values cluster around 0.25–0.40 kg/kWh for petrol and 0.20–0.30 kg/kWh for diesel.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Forgetting "K" in the IP formula** for multi-cylinder problems — loses **3 marks**.
> 2. **Mistaking N = 3000 rpm** for power strokes/sec; for 4-stroke, **power strokes/sec = N/120**, not N/60.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert** fuel consumption from kg/h to kg/s for thermal efficiency.
> 4. **Losing marks for "Comment"** in part (b) — KTU mandates the concluding remark for full marks.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### 🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Performance Testing
- [ ] Rope brake B.P. formula: $B.P. = \dfrac{(W - S) \cdot \pi D \cdot N}{60 \times 1000}$ — always subtract **S**.
- [ ] I.P. (4-stroke): $I.P. = \dfrac{p_{im} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot K}{60}$ when p_im in Pa and L, A in meters, output in W.
- [ ] For 2-stroke: divide by **30** instead of **60**.
- [ ] η_mech = B.P./I.P. — typically 0.80–0.92 in real engines.
- [ ] BSFC units are kg/kWh; remember × 3600 in the numerator of thermal efficiency formulas.
- [ ] Heat balance: $q_{in} = q_b + q_w + q_g + q_u$ — must close to within 2–5% in a good test.

### 🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Normal Combustion
- [ ] **SI Engine** has 3 stages: Ignition Lag → Flame Propagation → After Burning.
- [ ] **CI Engine** has 3 stages: Ignition Delay → Rapid Combustion → Controlled Combustion.
- [ ] **CI combustion is mixing-controlled** (after the initial rapid phase), SI is **kinetically controlled** (flame speed).

### 🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Abnormal Combustion
- [ ] **Knocking in SI** = end-gas auto-ignition. Controlled by **Octane Number** of fuel.
- [ ] **Diesel Knock** = excessive ignition delay. Controlled by **Cetane Number** of fuel.
- [ ] **Surface Ignition** vs **Pre-ignition** — hot spots, not end-gas chemistry.
- [ ] **Detonation** is post-spark violent auto-ignition; **pre-ignition** is pre-spark ignition via hot spots.

### 🚀 High-Octane Mnemonic — "ODOR-K"
- **O**ctane → **D**etonation resistance in SI
- **R**atings: **O**ctane high = knock-resistant
- **K**nock tendency inverse with octane

### 🚀 Diesel-Knock Mnemonic — "CAMP-D"
- **C**etane high = delay short = less knock
- **A**dvance injection cautiously
- **M**ix fuel well (high injection pressure)
- **P**re-heat intake air

### 🚀 Key Numbers to Memorize
- Octane Number of iso-octane: **100**
- Octane Number of n-heptane: **0**
- Cetane Number of n-cetyl: **100**
- Cetane Number of α-methyl naphthalene: **0**
- Typical petrol RON: **91–98**
- Typical diesel CN: **45–55 (India BS-VI: ≥ 51)**
- γ for air: **1.4**, γ for combustion products: **1.3–1.35**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Mantra:** *Always draw the indicator diagram, label all stages of combustion on the P–θ diagram, write units in **every** numerical step, and present results with **two significant figures beyond the data precision** to maximize partial credit retention.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Auto Ignition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIET303 - THERMAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Auto Ignition # AUTO IGNITION — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Auto Ignition** is the spontaneous combustion of a fuel–air mixture **without any external ignition source** (such as a spark plug or flame), occurring purely as a result of the mixture being raised to a critical temperature and pressure condition called the **Auto Ignition Temperature (AIT)**. The **Auto Ignition Temperature (AIT)** — also known as the **Spontaneous Ignition Temperature (SIT)** — is the *lowest* temperature at which a combustible mixture of fuel and air will ignite *self-sustainedly* in the absence of any external spark, flame, or pilot source. Mathematically, the condition for auto ignition is expressed as: $$T_{\text{mixture}} \geq T_{\text{AIT}}$$ Combined with the requirement that the mixture must lie within its **flammability limits** (Lower Flammability Limit $\rightarrow$ LFL and Upper Flammability Limit $\rightarrow$ UFL), and the **quenching distance** must not exceed the geometry's smallest dimension. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Haystack Fire:** > Imagine a tightly packed haystack on a hot summer day. There is no matchstick, no lighter — yet after days of slow internal heating (microbial action + ambient heat), the haystack suddenly bursts into flame from the inside out. **That spontaneous combustion IS auto ignition.** The haystack acted as its own "ignition source" once its internal temperature crossed a critical threshold. In an engine cylinder, during the compression stroke, the fuel–air mixture is compressed so intensely that its temperature rises sharply. If this temperature crosses the **Auto Ignition Temperature**, the mixture ignites on its own — *no spark needed*. This is the principle behind **Compression Ignition (CI / Diesel) engines**. ## 1.3 Standard AIT Values for Common Fuels (KTU High-Yield Data) | Fuel | Auto Ignition Temperature (°C) | Engine Category | | :--- | :---: | :--- | | **Methane (CH₄)** | **537 °C** | CNG / LNG | | **Propane (C₃H₈)** | **450 °C** | LPG | | **Iso-Octane (C₈H₁₈)** | **415 °C** | SI Engine reference fuel | | **n-Heptane (C₇H₁₆)** | **215 °C** | CI Engine reference fuel | | **Diesel** | **210 – 230 °C** | CI Engines | | **Gasoline (Petrol)** | **250 – 280 °C** | SI Engines | | **Hydrogen (H₂)** | **500 – 571 °C** | Alternative fuel | | **Ethanol** | **365 °C** | Biofuel additive | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Key Takeaway:** Notice that **diesel has a much LOWER AIT** (~220 °C) compared to petrol (~280 °C). This is precisely *why* diesel engines can rely on **compression alone** to ignite the fuel, while petrol engines need a **spark plug**. ## 1.4 Auto Ignition vs. Forced Ignition — The Critical Distinction > [!IMPORTANT] > **Forced Ignition** (SI Engines): Requires an external energy source — typically an electric spark (~30 mJ) produced by the spark plug. The local kernel of flame then propagates through the mixture. > **Auto Ignition** (CI Engines): The mixture self-ignites homogeneously at *multiple nucleation sites* once the critical temperature–pressure envelope is crossed. No external spark is involved. This distinction governs the entire design philosophy of **SI vs. CI engines**. # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars Governing Auto Ignition For a fuel–air mixture to auto-ignite, **THREE simultaneous conditions** must be satisfied: 1. **Temperature Condition:** $T_{\text{mixture}} \geq T_{\text{AIT}}$ 2. **Concentration Condition:** Fuel–air ratio must lie between the **Lower Flammability Limit (LFL)** and **Upper Flammability Limit (UFL)**. 3. **Time Condition:** The mixture must remain at the elevated temperature for a sufficient **reaction time** — known as the **Ignition Delay Period (IDP)**. ## 2.2 The Arrhenius Foundation — *Why* Auto Ignition Happens Auto ignition is fundamentally a **chemical kinetics** phenomenon. The reaction rate depends exponentially on temperature through the **Arrhenius Equation**: $$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$ Where: - $k$ = reaction rate constant ($\text{s}^{-1}$) - $A$ = pre-exponential (frequency) factor ($\text{s}^{-1}$) - $E_a$ = **activation energy** of the fuel ($\text{J} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1}$) - $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** - $T$ = absolute temperature (K) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Activation Energy ($E_a$)** is the minimum energy "hump" reactant molecules must overcome to react. **Lower $E_a$ → Easier auto ignition → Lower AIT.** This is why paraffins (diesel-range) ignite more readily than aromatics (gasoline-range). ## 2.3 Factors Affecting Auto Ignition Temperature | Factor | Effect on AIT | Reasoning | | :--- | :---: | :--- | | **Higher Pressure** | **Decreases AIT** | Higher molecular concentration → more frequent collisions | | **Smaller Cylinder / Volume** | **Decreases AIT** | Less heat loss, faster temperature rise | | **Turbulence** | **Decreases AIT** | Better mixing, faster heat distribution | | **Higher Octane Number** | **Increases AIT** | Higher resistance to auto ignition (branched hydrocarbons) | | **Higher Cetane Number** | **Decreases AIT** | Easier ignition (long straight-chain paraffins) | | **Presence of Anti-Knock Additives (TEL, MTBE)** | **Increases AIT** | Suppresses pre-ignition reactions | | **Larger Quenching Distance** | **Increases AIT** | Heat loss to walls quenches radicals | ## 2.4 The Ignition Delay Period (IDP) — *KTU Most-Tested Topic* > [!IMPORTANT] > **Ignition Delay Period** is the time interval between the **start of fuel injection** (in a CI engine) and the **start of rapid combustion** (visible pressure rise). It is the *temporal heart* of diesel combustion. The IDP is empirically modeled by the **Wolfer Correlation**: $$\tau_{\text{id}} = \frac{C \cdot e^{E_a / (R \cdot T)}}{P^{n}}$$ Where: - $\tau_{\text{id}}$ = ignition delay period (seconds) - $C$ = empirical constant - $E_a$ = activation energy (J/mol) - $T$ = in-cylinder temperature (K) at injection time - $P$ = in-cylinder pressure (Pa) at injection time - $n$ = pressure exponent (typically **0.7 to 1.5** for diesel) The IDP itself is conventionally divided into **THREE sub-phases**: | Sub-Phase | Duration | What Happens | | :--- | :---: | :--- | | **Physical Delay (I)** | 10–20% | Atomization, vaporization, mixing of fuel with air | | **Chemical Delay (II)** | 75–85% | Pre-flame reactions, radical buildup, **cool flame** formation | | **Propagation Delay (III)** | < 5% | Ignition kernel formation, flame spread | ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Arrhenius Rate Equation | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (RT)}$ | $k$ in $\text{s}^{-1}$, $E_a$ in $\text{J/mol}$ | | 2 | Ignition Condition | $T_{\text{mixture}} \geq T_{\text{AIT}}$ | Plus LFL ≤ $\phi$ ≤ UFL | | 3 | Wolfer Ignition Delay | $\tau_{\text{id}} = C \cdot e^{E_a/(RT)} / P^{n}$ | $\tau$ in seconds | | 4 | Compression Temperature | $T_2 = T_1 \cdot \left(r_c\right)^{\gamma - 1}$ | Adiabatic compression; $r_c$ = compression ratio | | 5 | Compression Pressure | $P_2 = P_1 \cdot r_c^{\gamma}$ | $P_1$ ≈ 1 atm at intake | | 6 | Cetane Number Definition | $CN = \left(V_{n\text{-C16H34}} / V_{\text{total}}\right) \cdot 100$ | Reference: $\alpha$-methylnaphthalene has CN = 0 | | 7 | Octane Number Definition | $ON = \left(V_{\text{i-C8H18}} / V_{\text{total}}\right) \cdot 100$ | Reference: n-heptane has ON = 0 | | 8 | Mean Effective Pressure (Auto Ignition related) | $P_{\text{me}} = W_{\text{net}} / (V_s \cdot N)$ | Used in knocking analysis | | 9 | Flame Quenching Distance | $\delta_q \propto T_{\text{AIT}} / (\rho \cdot c_p \cdot v)$ | Affects AIT in narrow gaps | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Note:** Always write $\vert$ or `\mid` for absolute value notations in derivations — never use the vertical pipe `\|` symbol as it breaks markdown table parsing. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **SI Engine Knock Suppression:** Auto ignition of end-gas ahead of the flame front causes **knocking** — mitigated by higher octane fuels, retarded timing, or knock sensors. - **CI Engine Cold Start:** Higher IDP at low temperatures causes starting difficulty — solved via **glow plugs** and **cetane improvers** (e.g., 2-ethylhexyl nitrate). - **Safety Engineering:** AIT determines the **maximum allowable surface temperature** of electrical equipment in hazardous (Ex-proof) zones — basis for **ATEX/IECEx certification**. - **Rocket & Jet Engines:** Auto ignition in afterburners and ramjets must be precisely controlled to prevent pre-ignition. # Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Arrhenius to the Wolfer Ignition Delay **Starting Point:** We know the global combustion reaction rate constant follows Arrhenius kinetics. We want to derive the **time to reach critical radical concentration** (i.e., ignition delay). ### Step 1 — Initial Rate Expression The reaction rate of fuel oxidation at time $t$ is given by: $$-\frac{d[F]}{dt} = k \cdot [F]^m \cdot [O_2]^n$$ Where $[F]$ and $[O_2]$ are molar concentrations, and $m + n$ defines the overall reaction order (typically **2 to 3** for hydrocarbons). ### Step 2 — Apply Arrhenius for the Rate Constant Substituting $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (RT)}$: $$-\frac{d[F]}{dt} = A \cdot e^{-E_a/(RT)} \cdot [F]^m \cdot [O_2]^n$$ ### Step 3 — Integrate Over the Delay Period Integrating from $t = 0$ (injection) to $t = \tau_{\text{id}}$ (ignition): $$\int_{[F]_0}^{[F]_{\text{crit}}} \frac{d[F]}{[F]^m} = A \cdot e^{-E_a/(RT)} \cdot [O_2]^n \cdot \tau_{\text{id}}$$ Solving the left-hand side for a **first-order** assumption ($m = 1$): $$\ln\!\left(\frac{[F]_0}{[F]_{\text{crit}}}\right) = A \cdot e^{-E_a/(RT)} \cdot [O_2]^n \cdot \tau_{\text{id}}$$ ### Step 4 — Solve for $\tau_{\text{id}}$ Rearranging for the ignition delay period: $$\tau_{\text{id}} = \frac{\ln\!\left([F]_0 \,/\, [F]_{\text{crit}}\right)}{A \cdot [O_2]^n} \cdot e^{E_a/(RT)}$$ ### Step 5 — Introduce Pressure Dependence By ideal gas law, $[O_2] \propto P / T$. Combining constants into $C$ and recognizing the weak temperature dependence in the pre-factor: $$\boxed{\;\tau_{\text{id}} \approx \frac{C \cdot e^{E_a / (R \cdot T)}}{P^{n}}\;}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **Wolfer Correlation** — the most heavily tested ignition delay formula in KTU exams. Notice: **higher $T$ → exponentially shorter delay**; **higher $P$ → shorter delay** (raised to power $n$). ## 3.2 Derivation: Critical Compression Ratio for Auto Ignition For auto ignition to occur during the compression stroke, the **adiabatic compression temperature** must exceed AIT. ### Step 1 — Adiabatic Compression Relations For an ideal Otto cycle (reversible adiabatic): $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot \left(r_c\right)^{\gamma - 1}$$ $$P_2 = P_1 \cdot \left(r_c\right)^{\gamma}$$ ### Step 2 — Set Auto Ignition Threshold Setting $T_2 = T_{\text{AIT}}$ gives the **minimum compression ratio** for auto ignition: $$r_{c,\text{min}} = \left(\frac{T_{\text{AIT}}}{T_1}\right)^{1/(\gamma - 1)}$$ ### Step 3 — Numerical Evaluation (KTU Typical Values) Given: $T_1 = 350 \, \text{K}$ (intake temp, hot day), $T_{\text{AIT}} = 600 \, \text{K}$, $\gamma = 1.4$ (air). $$r_{c,\text{min}} = \left(\frac{600}{350}\right)^{1/0.4}$$ $$r_{c,\text{min}} = (1.714)^{2.5}$$ $$r_{c,\text{min}} = 1.714^{2.5} \approx 3.87$$ **Conclusion:** A compression ratio of only **~4:1** would theoretically suffice for auto ignition of this fuel. CI engines use **14:1 to 22:1** — a healthy margin ensuring *fast and reliable* ignition despite heat losses and finite reaction rates. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Octane Number vs. Cetane Number Trade-off | Property | Octane Number (ON) | Cetane Number (CN) | | :--- | :---: | :---: | | **Engine Type** | SI (Petrol) | CI (Diesel) | | **Resistance To** | **Auto Ignition** (Knock) | **Delayed Combustion** | | **Higher Number Means** | Slower auto ignition | Faster auto ignition | | **Reference Fuel — High** | Iso-Octane (ON = 100) | n-Cetane (CN = 100) | | **Reference Fuel — Low** | n-Heptane (ON = 0) | $\alpha$-methylnaphthalene (CN = 0) | | **Desirable Effect** | Knocking suppressed | Smooth, quick ignition | | **Branched Paraffins** | High ON | Low CN | | **Straight-Chain Paraffins** | Low ON | High CN | | **Aromatics** | High ON | Low CN | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Anti-correlation:** A fuel with a high octane number inherently has a low cetane number — the molecular structure that resists auto ignition in SI engines simultaneously delays ignition in CI engines. KTU frequently tests this inversion. # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Auto Ignition Process Flow — Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Fuel-Air Mixture Enters Cylinder] --> B{Pressure > Critical?} B -- No --> Z[No Ignition; Mixture Compresses Further] Z --> B B -- Yes --> C{Temperature Reaches AIT Threshold?} C -- No --> Z C -- Yes --> D[Chemical Delay Phase: Radical Buildup] D --> E[Cool Flame Formation] E --> F[Hot Flame Nucleation] F --> G[Premixed Combustion Phase] G --> H[Diffusion Combustion Phase] H --> I[Controlled Pressure Rise] I --> J[Power Stroke Initiation] J --> K[Auto Ignition Complete] style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b style D fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 style F fill:#ffebee,stroke:#b71c1c style K fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 ``` ## 4.2 SI Engine Knock — Pressure-Time Trace Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Normal["Normal SI Combustion"] N1[Spark at TDC] --> N2[Flame Front Propagates Smoothly] N2 --> N3[Gradual Pressure Rise] end subgraph Knocking["Knocking Combustion (Auto Ignition of End-Gas)"] K1[Spark at TDC] --> K2[Flame Front Propagates] K2 --> K3{End-Gas Temperature > AIT?} K3 -- Yes --> K4[AUTO IGNITION of End-Gas] K4 --> K5[Abrupt Pressure Spike] K5 --> K6[Engine Knock Sound + Damage Risk] K3 -- No --> K7[Normal Completion] end style K4 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828 style K5 fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c ``` ## 4.3 Ignition Delay Sub-Phases — Time-Decomposition Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD S[Start of Injection: t = 0] --> P1[Phase I: Physical Delay
Fuel Spray Atomization
Vaporization
Air Mixing] P1 --> P2[Phase II: Chemical Delay
Low-Temp Oxidation
Peroxide & Aldehyde Formation
Cool Flame] P2 --> P3[Phase III: Propagation Delay
Hot Flame Nucleation
Rapid Combustion Onset] P3 --> E[End: Start of Rapid Combustion at t = tau_id] style P1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1 style P2 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100 style P3 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c ``` ## 4.4 Pressure-Temperature Trajectory in a CI Engine Cylinder ```mermaid flowchart LR A[1: Intake T1 ~ 350K, P1 ~ 1 atm] -->|Compression Stroke| B[2: TDC Pre-Injection
T2 ~ 900K, P2 ~ 40 atm] B -->|Fuel Injection Starts| C[3: Ignition Delay Period
tau_id ~ 1 to 3 ms] C --> D[4: Auto Ignition Occurs
T = T_AIT ~ 1000K+] D --> E[5: Premixed Burn Phase
Rapid Pressure Spike] E --> F[6: Diffusion Burn Phase
Controlled Heat Release] F --> G[7: Expansion Stroke Begins] style C fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style D fill:#ff8a65,stroke:#bf360c style E fill:#ef5350,stroke:#b71c1c ``` # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Auto Ignition Temperature. How does it differ from Flash Point? `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Auto Ignition Temperature (AIT):** It is the lowest temperature at which a fuel–air mixture ignites *spontaneously without any external ignition source*, and the combustion continues without further heat addition. **Difference from Flash Point:** | Aspect | Auto Ignition Temperature | Flash Point | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Definition | Spontaneous combustion temperature | Temperature at which vapors ignite momentarily on flame application | | External Source | **Not required** | **Required** (flame / spark) | | Temperature Magnitude | **Much higher** (200–600 °C) | **Much lower** (50–200 °C) | | Application | Engine design, safety standards | Fire safety, fuel storage classification | **Memory Hook:** AIT = *self-ignition* (no spark); Flash Point = *vapor flash* (needs a pilot flame). --- ### Question 2: What are the THREE essential conditions for auto ignition of a fuel–air mixture? `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** 1. **Temperature Condition:** The mixture temperature must equal or exceed the **Auto Ignition Temperature (AIT)** of the fuel. 2. **Concentration Condition:** The fuel–air ratio (equivalence ratio $\phi$) must lie within the **flammability limits** — between the Lower Flammability Limit (LFL) and the Upper Flammability Limit (UFL). 3. **Time / Residence Condition:** The mixture must remain at the elevated temperature for a duration greater than the **ignition delay period** to allow the chemical reactions to reach a self-sustaining state. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions — Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) ### Question A: Ignition Delay Period in CI Engines `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]` **(a)** Define **Ignition Delay Period** in a Compression Ignition engine. Explain its **three sub-phases** with neat sketches of the cylinder pressure variation. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Definition (2 Marks):** The Ignition Delay Period ($\tau_{\text{id}}$) is the time interval between the **start of fuel injection** into the combustion chamber and the **point of rapid pressure rise** indicating the onset of auto ignition. **Sub-phases (5 Marks):** **Phase I — Physical Delay (~10–20% of $\tau_{\text{id}}$):** - Fuel leaves the injector nozzle as a high-pressure spray. - Atomization into fine droplets. - Heating of droplets to vaporization temperature. - Mixing of fuel vapor with hot compressed air. **Phase II — Chemical Delay (~75–85% of $\tau_{\text{id}}$):** - Low-temperature oxidation reactions begin. - Formation of peroxides, aldehydes, and other intermediate radicals. - A faint **cool flame** may appear. - This is the **rate-limiting** sub-phase. **Phase III — Propagation Delay (~5% of $\tau_{\text{id}}$):** - A localized ignition kernel forms. - Hot flame propagates outward through the prepared mixture. **Pressure Trace Sketch Description:** The cylinder pressure remains nearly constant during Phases I and II (slight rise due to continued compression), then exhibits a **sharp, almost step-like rise** at the end of Phase III. --- **(b)** Derive the **Wolfer correlation** for ignition delay and discuss how it is affected by **compression ratio** and **inlet air temperature**. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Derivation (4 Marks):** Starting from the Arrhenius rate equation: $$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (RT)}$$ For a fuel–air mixture in a closed cylinder, the time to reach a critical radical concentration (i.e., ignition) is inversely proportional to the rate constant. Combined with the ideal gas law ($[O_2] \propto P / T$), we obtain: $$\tau_{\text{id}} = \frac{C \cdot e^{E_a / (R \cdot T)}}{P^{n}}$$ Where: - $C$ = empirical constant (depends on fuel and atomization quality) - $n$ = pressure exponent (typically 0.7 to 1.5) **Effect of Compression Ratio (1.5 Marks):** Higher compression ratio $\rightarrow$ Higher end-of-compression temperature $T$ AND pressure $P$ $\rightarrow$ Exponentially shorter ignition delay. Effect via $T$ is much stronger than via $P$ due to the exponential. **Effect of Inlet Air Temperature (1.5 Marks):** Higher $T_1$ at inlet $\rightarrow$ Higher $T$ at TDC $\rightarrow$ Shorter ignition delay. This is why **turbocharging with intercooling** improves cold-start in CI engines. --- ### Question B: Auto Ignition and Engine Knock `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **(a)** What is **knocking in an SI engine**? Explain its mechanism in terms of auto ignition of the end-gas. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Definition (2 Marks):** Knocking is the **abnormal, sharp metallic sound** produced in an SI engine when the **end-gas (unburned mixture ahead of the flame front)** auto-ignites spontaneously before the normal flame front reaches it. **Mechanism (5 Marks):** 1. After spark ignition, a normal flame front propagates from the spark plug across the combustion chamber. 2. The **end-gas** — the mixture ahead of this flame front — gets compressed and heated by both piston motion and the advancing flame. 3. If this end-gas temperature crosses the **Auto Ignition Temperature**, the mixture auto-ignites almost instantaneously at **multiple nucleation sites**. 4. This results in two colliding pressure waves producing a **sharp, audible "knock"**. 5. Consequences: power loss, overheating, piston/valve damage. **Preventive Measures:** Use high-octane fuel, retard ignition timing, lower compression ratio, use knock sensors with electronic ignition advance control. --- **(b)** Compare **Octane Number** and **Cetane Number**. Why are they inversely related? **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** | Property | Octane Number | Cetane Number | | :--- | :---: | :---: | | Engine | SI (Petrol) | CI (Diesel) | | Desired Property | Resistance to auto ignition (knock) | Easy and fast auto ignition | | Higher Value Means | Slower auto ignition | Faster auto ignition | | Reference High | Iso-Octane (ON = 100) | n-Cetane (CN = 100) | | Reference Low | n-Heptane (ON = 0) | $\alpha$-methylnaphthalene (CN = 0) | **Inverse Relationship (3 Marks):** The molecular structure of hydrocarbons dictates both numbers simultaneously: - **Straight-chain paraffins** (e.g., n-heptane, n-cetane): Long, linear chains are **easy to oxidize** $\rightarrow$ low activation energy $E_a$ $\rightarrow$ easy auto ignition $\rightarrow$ **low ON, high CN**. - **Branched paraffins & aromatics** (e.g., iso-octane, toluene): Branching and ring structures **sterically hinder** radical attack $\rightarrow$ high $E_a$ $\rightarrow$ difficult auto ignition $\rightarrow$ **high ON, low CN**. Hence, a fuel optimized for petrol engines (high ON) is fundamentally unsuitable for diesel engines (needs high CN), and vice versa. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** > 1. **Confusing Flash Point with AIT** — they are entirely different phenomena (memory hook: Flash Point needs a flame; AIT does not). Students lose 1–2 marks repeatedly on this. > 2. **Writing Wolfer formula incorrectly** — the exponent must be **positive** ($+E_a / RT$), not negative. Getting the sign wrong changes the entire physical trend. > 3. **Saying "high octane = high cetane"** — this is the **opposite** of the truth. The trade-off is **anti-correlated** due to molecular structure. > 4. **Forgetting units** in $k$ and $\tau_{\text{id}}$ calculations — always write $\text{s}^{-1}$ and $\text{s}$ respectively. > 5. **Ignoring the chemical delay** — examiners specifically ask for it because it dominates ~80% of the total IDP. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Auto Ignition** - **Definition:** Auto ignition = spontaneous combustion *without* external spark when $T \geq T_{\text{AIT}}$. - **Three Conditions:** Temperature ✓ Concentration (within flammability limits) ✓ Time (≥ IDP) ✓. - **Arrhenius Foundation:** $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (RT)}$; lower $E_a$ $\rightarrow$ lower AIT $\rightarrow$ easier auto ignition. - **Diesel vs. Petrol AIT:** Diesel ~220 °C << Petrol ~280 °C $\rightarrow$ enables CI engine. - **Wolfer Correlation:** $\tau_{\text{id}} = C \cdot e^{E_a/(RT)} / P^{n}$ — exponentially sensitive to T. - **Three IDP Sub-phases:** Physical (atomization) → Chemical (radical buildup, dominant) → Propagation (kernel formation). - **Knocking in SI engines** = unwanted auto ignition of end-gas. - **Octane Number ↑ ⟺ Cetane Number ↓** — molecular structure dictates both. - **Iso-Octane:** ON = 100, n-Heptane: ON = 0, n-Cetane: CN = 100, $\alpha$-methylnaphthalene: CN = 0. - **Higher compression ratio** $\rightarrow$ higher TDC temperature $\rightarrow$ shorter IDP $\rightarrow$ smoother CI combustion. - **Anti-knock additives:** TEL (now phased out), MTBE, ethanol — raise AIT. - **Cetane improvers:** 2-EHN, DIIP — shorten IDP, aid cold starting. - **Quenching distance** inversely affects AIT — narrower gaps suppress auto ignition. - **ATEX/IECEx safety zones** are based on AIT for hazardous area classification. - **Numerical conversions:** Always convert °C to K by adding **273.15** before using Arrhenius equation.

topics.title: Pre Ignition and Detonation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET303 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pre Ignition and Detonation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Normal Combustion in S.I. Engines (The Reference Baseline)

Before understanding abnormal combustion, every KTU student must firmly grasp the **"Normal Combustion Cycle"** in a Spark-Ignition (SI) engine. Under ideal conditions, the sequence of events is:

1. The piston approaches **Top Dead Center (TDC)** during the compression stroke.
2. The air-fuel mixture is compressed to a ratio typically between **8:1 to 12:1**.
3. The spark plug fires at a precisely calibrated instant (a few degrees **BTDC**).
4. A smooth, hemispherical **flame kernel** originates at the spark plug.
5. This flame front propagates outward at a uniform velocity of about **15–25 m/s** through the homogeneous mixture.
6. Combustion is completed in a controlled, laminar manner just after TDC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Normal Combustion Equation (KTU Definition):**
> Normal combustion in an SI engine is the *smooth, progressive propagation of a flame front* from the spark plug across the combustion chamber, reaching the farthest cylinder walls before the end of the power stroke, without any abnormal pressure rise.

## 1.2 Pre-Ignition — The "Early Intruder"

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology):**
Pre-ignition is the **ignition of the air-fuel mixture in the combustion chamber by a source other than the spark** (e.g., a hot spot, overheated exhaust valve, carbon deposits, or glowing spark plug electrodes) **before the actual spark occurs** at the scheduled ignition timing.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Premature Firecracker" 💥:**
Imagine a classroom where the teacher is supposed to light a firecracker at exactly noon to signal the end of the exam. But a student with a matchstick accidentally lights a different firecracker at 11:55 AM. The whole room fills with smoke *before* the official event. The students (piston) are caught off-guard — the pressure rises too early, fighting the upward motion of the piston. This is **pre-ignition**.

**Physical Manifestations:**
- Pressure rises **well before** the scheduled spark timing (often 10°–30° BTDC more than intended).
- The cause is **physical contact** with a hot surface (typically above **700 K – 800 K** for auto-ignition of gasoline-air mixture).
- Pre-ignition can persist for several cycles, leading to **localized superheating**.
- Severe pre-ignition can lead to a phenomenon called **"Run-on" or "Dieseling"** — where the engine continues to run even after the ignition is switched off.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants/Metrics:**
> - Critical surface temperature for pre-ignition: **≈ 700 K (427 °C)**
> - Pre-ignition generally causes a **loss of power** and **overheating** of components.
> - It is **NOT the same** as knocking (detonation), though it can *trigger* detonation in extreme cases.

## 1.3 Detonation (Knocking) — The "Shock Wave Saboteur"

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology):**
Detonation (commonly known as **engine knock** or **"pinging"**) is the **abrupt, spontaneous, and explosive auto-ignition of the unburned end-gas** in the combustion chamber, occurring **after** the initiation of normal flame propagation, leading to a sudden, sharp rise in cylinder pressure and the formation of high-frequency pressure waves (knock).

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Cannon Inside the Engine" 🎯:**
Visualize lighting a fuse on a small bundle of dynamite. The flame travels smoothly along the fuse (normal flame propagation). However, the remaining unburned dynamite gets squeezed and heated so intensely that it explodes violently on its own, creating a shockwave that slams against the walls. This violent, simultaneous explosion of the **"end-gas"** — that last portion of charge trapped between the advancing flame front and the cylinder walls — is **detonation**.

**Physical Manifestations:**
- A sharp **"tink-tink" or "ping"** metallic sound during acceleration or high load.
- The end-gas auto-ignites due to a combination of high **pressure**, high **temperature**, and sufficient **time** (the **ignition lag**).
- The pressure rise becomes **abrupt** rather than smooth — sometimes the peak pressure reaches **60 bar to 80 bar** in just 2°–5° of crank rotation.
- A **detonation wave** travels at approximately **300–600 m/s** (compared to 15–25 m/s for a normal flame), creating severe vibrations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize This!):**
> "Detonation is the sudden auto-ignition of the end-gas in the combustion chamber of an SI engine resulting in a sharp, almost instantaneous rise in cylinder pressure accompanied by a characteristic knocking sound."

## 1.4 Tabular Comparison at a Glance

| Parameter | Pre-Ignition | Detonation (Knock) |
|---|---|---|
| **Initiator** | Hot surface (carbon, valve, plug) | Spontaneous auto-ignition of end-gas |
| **Timing** | Before spark occurs | After spark occurs (during flame travel) |
| **Nature** | Localized surface ignition | Bulk volumetric auto-ignition |
| **Pressure Rise** | Gradual but premature | Extremely sharp and violent |
| **Sound** | Usually silent or rough running | Distinct "knocking/pinging" sound |
| **Effect on Power** | Power loss | Power loss + mechanical damage |
| **Remedy** | Eliminate hot spots, better cooling | Higher octane fuel, retarding spark, lower compression ratio |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mechanism of Detonation — A Step-by-Step Physics Breakdown

For KTU 2024 examination purposes, you must understand *why* detonation happens. The mechanism proceeds through these distinct stages:

**Step 1: Initiation of Normal Flame Front**
The spark plug creates a flame kernel. The flame front moves outward toward the cylinder walls.

**Step 2: Compression and Heating of End-Gas**
The **end-gas** (the unburned mixture trapped between the advancing flame front and the piston/cylinder walls) is subjected to:
- Compression by the rising piston
- Heat transfer from the already-burned gases ahead of the flame front
- Compression from the expanding burnt gases behind the flame front

**Step 3: Development of Pressure Waves**
As the flame front advances, it pushes the end-gas and creates a pressure differential. The end-gas behaves somewhat like a **spring**, with the flame acting as a piston pushing it.

**Step 4: Auto-Ignition of End-Gas**
If the temperature of the end-gas exceeds its **auto-ignition temperature** AND the time available (ignition delay) is sufficient, the end-gas spontaneously ignites at multiple points almost simultaneously.

**Step 5: Detonation Wave Formation**
The simultaneous auto-ignition of the end-gas creates two colliding flame fronts that compress each other, forming a **detonation wave** that travels at supersonic speeds (**≈ 2000 m/s in some cases**).

**Step 6: Pressure Spike and Knock**
This wave reflects off the cylinder walls, causing localized pressure peaks that vibrate the walls, producing the audible **"knock"**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind the Knock:**
> Detonation is fundamentally a **race against time**:
> - The flame front takes time $t_f$ to consume the charge.
> - The end-gas has a chemical induction delay $t_i$ before auto-ignition.
> - **Knock occurs if $t_f > t_i$** (the end-gas auto-ignites BEFORE the flame front reaches it).

## 2.2 Quantitative Formulation of Knock Tendency

The end-gas auto-ignition is governed by the **Arrhenius-type reaction rate equation**:

$$k = A \cdot e^{\left(\frac{-E_a}{R \cdot T_{eg}}\right)}$$

Where:
- $k$ = Reaction rate constant
- $A$ = Frequency (pre-exponential) factor
- $E_a$ = Activation energy ($\approx 160$ kJ/mol for iso-octane)
- $R$ = Universal gas constant ($= 8.314$ J/mol·K)
- $T_{eg}$ = Absolute temperature of the end-gas (**Kelvin**)

The **ignition delay** (induction period) is inversely related to $k$:

$$\tau_i = \frac{1}{k} = \frac{1}{A} \cdot e^{\left(\frac{E_a}{R \cdot T_{eg}}\right)}$$

The condition for knock is:

$$\tau_i < t_{\text{flame travel}}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1}$ | End-gas temperature after compression ratio $r$ | K |
| 2 | $P_2 = P_1 \cdot r^{\gamma}$ | End-gas pressure after compression | bar or kPa |
| 3 | $\tau_i = \frac{1}{A} \cdot e^{(E_a / R \cdot T_{eg})}$ | Ignition delay (induction period) | seconds |
| 4 | **Knock condition:** $\tau_i < t_{\text{flame}}$ | Detonation occurs if this is satisfied | — |
| 5 | $\text{Octane Number} = \text{ISOC} + \text{ISOH}$ | Reference fuel blend (e.g., 95 octane = 95% iso-octane + 5% n-heptane) | % |
| 6 | $\text{SI Engine: } \text{ON} \uparrow \Rightarrow \text{ knock resistance} \uparrow$ | Higher octane → better knock resistance | — |
| 7 | $\text{CI Engine: } \text{CN} \uparrow \Rightarrow \text{ quality of CI} \uparrow$ | Cetane Number for diesel fuels | — |
| 8 | $\text{SFC} = \frac{m_f}{P \cdot t}$ | Specific Fuel Consumption (indicator) | kg/kWh |
| 9 | $P_{\text{peak, knock}} \approx 60 - 80$ bar | Peak cylinder pressure during knocking | bar |
| 10 | $V_{\text{flame}} \approx 15 - 25$ m/s | Normal flame speed | m/s |
| 11 | $V_{\text{detonation}} \approx 300 - 600$ m/s | Detonation wave velocity (sometimes up to 2000 m/s) | m/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Table Symbols:** All "absolute" or "given" notations use the word form (e.g., "absolute temperature" or $\vert T \vert$) to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Never use raw pipe characters inside table cells.

## 2.4 Factors Affecting Detonation (High-Yield for KTU)

The KTU board loves asking this as a 7-mark or 14-mark question. Memorize these factors grouped by their underlying cause:

### A. Engine Design Factors
1. **Compression Ratio** ↑ → Higher end-gas temperature → More knock. *(This is why S.I. engines have lower CR than C.I. engines.)*
2. **Combustion Chamber Design** — Compact, fast-burning chambers (e.g., hemispherical) reduce knock. Long, narrow chambers (e.g., pre-chamber, F-head) promote knock.
3. **Spark Advance** — More advance → more time for end-gas to ignite → more knock.
4. **Cylinder Size (Bore)** ↑ — Longer flame path → more time for end-gas auto-ignition → more knock.
5. **Distance between spark plug and farthest point** (flame travel distance) ↑ → more knock.
6. **Number of Spark Plugs** ↑ → reduced flame travel → less knock.
7. **Intake Air Temperature** ↑ → higher end-gas temperature → more knock.
8. **Cooling Water Temperature** ↑ → less heat extraction → more knock.

### B. Fuel-Related Factors
1. **Octane Rating of Fuel** ↑ → better knock resistance.
2. **Self-Ignition Temperature** of fuel — fuels with higher auto-ignition temperature are more knock-resistant.
3. **Latent Heat of Vaporization** — fuels with higher LHV cool the charge, reducing knock.
4. **Molecular Structure** — Branched-chain hydrocarbons (e.g., iso-octane) are more knock-resistant than straight-chain (e.g., n-heptane).
5. **Additives** — Tetra-Ethyl Lead (TEL), MTBE, ethanol improve octane rating.

### C. Operating Conditions
1. **Load** ↑ → higher manifold vacuum, higher temperatures → more knock.
2. **Speed** ↑ — Dual effect: increases flame speed (reduces knock tendency per cycle) but reduces heat transfer (increases knock).
3. **Mixture Strength** — Rich mixtures absorb more heat (fuel vaporization) → less knock. Lean mixtures knock more easily.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The concept of pre-ignition and detonation is critical in:
- **Automotive Engine Calibration:** ECU mapping for ignition timing
- **Fuel Industry:** Designing anti-knock additives (e.g., ethanol blending, TEL phasing out)
- **Aviation Piston Engines:** Where high octane fuels (100LL — 100 octane Low Lead) are mandatory
- **High-Performance Engines:** Forced-induction (turbo/supercharged) engines require high-octane fuel due to higher effective compression ratios
- **Alternative Fuels:** Hydrogen (very knock-resistant) and CNG (high octane) are evaluated using these principles

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: End-Gas Temperature After Compression

Consider the end-gas in an SI engine cylinder. It is compressed adiabatically (approximately) from state 1 (just before compression begins) to state 2 (just before the flame front arrives).

**Step 1: Start with the isentropic (adiabatic) relation for an ideal gas:**

$$P_1 \cdot V_1^{\gamma} = P_2 \cdot V_2^{\gamma}$$

**Step 2: Define compression ratio $r$:**

$$r = \frac{V_1}{V_2}$$

Therefore: $V_1 = r \cdot V_2$.

**Step 3: Substitute into the isentropic equation:**

$$P_1 \cdot (r \cdot V_2)^{\gamma} = P_2 \cdot V_2^{\gamma}$$

**Step 4: Simplify by dividing both sides by $V_2^{\gamma}$:**

$$P_1 \cdot r^{\gamma} = P_2$$

**Step 5: Solve for the pressure ratio:**

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = r^{\gamma}$$

**Step 6: Use the ideal gas law to derive the temperature ratio:**

For an ideal gas, $P \cdot V = m \cdot R \cdot T$, so:

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{P_2 \cdot V_2}{P_1 \cdot V_1} = \frac{P_2}{P_1} \cdot \frac{V_2}{V_1} = r^{\gamma} \cdot \frac{1}{r} = r^{\gamma - 1}$$

**Step 7: Final end-gas temperature equation:**

$$T_{2} = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1}$$

Where:
- $T_1$ = Temperature of the charge at the start of compression (after intake) ≈ **350 K** typical
- $r$ = Compression ratio
- $\gamma$ = Specific heat ratio ≈ **1.35** (for burnt gases; for mixture ≈ **1.4**)

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example (KTU Style):**
> If $T_1 = 350$ K, $r = 9$, $\gamma = 1.35$:
> $T_2 = 350 \cdot (9)^{1.35 - 1} = 350 \cdot (9)^{0.35} = 350 \cdot 2.176 = 761.6$ K
> This is dangerously close to the auto-ignition temperature of gasoline (~640 K under pressure).

## 3.2 Derivation: Ignition Delay from Arrhenius Kinetics

The **ignition delay** (or induction period) $\tau_i$ is the time lag between the moment the end-gas reaches auto-ignition conditions and the moment it actually auto-ignites.

**Step 1: Reaction rate $k$ from Arrhenius equation:**

$$k = A \cdot e^{\left(\frac{-E_a}{R \cdot T}\right)}$$

**Step 2: The induction period is inversely proportional to the reaction rate:**

$$\tau_i \propto \frac{1}{k}$$

**Step 3: Therefore:**

$$\tau_i = C \cdot e^{\left(\frac{E_a}{R \cdot T_{eg}}\right)}$$

Where $C = 1/A$ is a constant.

**Step 4: Take natural logarithm for clarity:**

$$\ln(\tau_i) = \ln(C) + \frac{E_a}{R \cdot T_{eg}}$$

**Step 5: Knock occurs when induction delay equals or is less than the flame travel time:**

$$\tau_i \leq t_{\text{flame travel}}$$

$$\therefore \quad C \cdot e^{\left(\frac{E_a}{R \cdot T_{eg}}\right)} \leq t_{\text{flame}}$$

**Step 6: Solving for the critical end-gas temperature $T_{eg, \text{critical}}$:**

$$e^{\left(\frac{E_a}{R \cdot T_{eg, cr}}\right)} = \frac{t_{\text{flame}}}{C}$$

$$\frac{E_a}{R \cdot T_{eg, cr}} = \ln\left(\frac{t_{\text{flame}}}{C}\right)$$

$$T_{eg, cr} = \frac{E_a}{R \cdot \ln(t_{\text{flame}} / C)}$$

**Step 7: Critical end-gas pressure (using ideal gas):**

$$P_{eg, cr} = \frac{\rho_{eg} \cdot R \cdot T_{eg, cr}}{M}$$

Where $M$ is the molar mass of the end-gas mixture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A fuel with **higher octane number** has a **higher effective activation energy $E_a$** OR a higher required $T_{eg, cr}$. This is the molecular reason why branched-chain hydrocarbons resist knock — they require more energy to break their C–C bonds.

## 3.3 Derivation: Octane Number Definition

**Step 1: Reference Fuels Definition:**
- **Iso-Octane (2,2,4-trimethylpentane):** Assigned an octane number of **100** (very knock-resistant).
- **n-Heptane:** Assigned an octane number of **0** (very prone to knock).

**Step 2: Octane Number of a Blend:**

If a fuel produces the same knock intensity as a blend of $x$% iso-octane and $(100-x)$% n-heptane:

$$\boxed{\text{Octane Number (ON)} = x}$$

Where $x$ is the volume percentage of iso-octane in the reference blend.

**Step 3: For fuels with ON > 100 (e.g., toluene, alcohol, pure iso-octane + TEL):**
- The test is run with iso-octane + TEL as reference.
- ON is reported relative to the **ml of TEL per gallon** required to match the knock intensity of iso-octane.

**Step 4: Two Test Standards:**

| Standard | Engine Speed | Used For | Octane Reported As |
|---|---|---|---|
| **RON** (Research Octane Number) | 600 rpm, no intake air heating | Mild conditions | RON |
| **MON** (Motor Octane Number) | 900 rpm, intake air preheated to 150°C | Severe/high load | MON |

**Step 5: Anti-Knock Index (AKI) — Pump Octane Number:**

$$AKI = \frac{RON + MON}{2}$$

This is the value displayed at fuel pumps (e.g., 91, 95, 98 in India).

## 3.4 Derivation: Cetane Number for C.I. Engines

**Step 1: Reference Fuels:**
- **n-Cetane (n-Hexadecane, $\text{C}_{16}\text{H}_{34}$):** Cetane Number = **100** (excellent ignition quality).
- **$\alpha$-Methylnaphthalene:** Cetane Number = **0** (poor ignition quality).

**Step 2: Definition:**

$$\boxed{\text{Cetane Number (CN)} = \text{Volume \% of n-cetane in the reference blend}}$$

**Step 3: Relation between Octane and Cetane:**

There is an **inverse** relationship — fuels that are good for SI engines (high octane) are usually poor for CI engines (low cetane), and vice versa.

$$\text{ON} \uparrow \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{CN} \downarrow$$

$$\text{ON} \downarrow \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{CN} \uparrow$$

**Step 4: Approximate empirical relationship (used in industry):**

$$CN \approx 60 - 0.5 \cdot \text{RON}$$

Or more accurately, with experimental correlation:

$$CN \approx -0.65 \cdot \text{RON} + 79.5$$

## 3.5 Numerical Problem Set — Analytical Solutions

### Problem 1: End-Gas Temperature Calculation

**Given:** Compression ratio $r = 10:1$, $T_1 = 360$ K, $\gamma = 1.4$ (unburned mixture).
**Find:** End-gas temperature $T_2$.

**Solution:**

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1}$$

$$T_2 = 360 \cdot (10)^{1.4 - 1} = 360 \cdot (10)^{0.4}$$

Using logarithmic evaluation:

$$(10)^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \cdot \ln(10)} = e^{0.4 \cdot 2.3026} = e^{0.9210} = 2.512$$

Therefore:

$$T_2 = 360 \cdot 2.512 = 904.3 \text{ K}$$

**Conclusion:** The end-gas reaches 904 K, which is well above the auto-ignition temperature of most fuels (~600–700 K). This explains why a high-compression SI engine is highly susceptible to knock.

### Problem 2: Cetane Number Estimation

**Given:** A diesel fuel has RON = 25.
**Find:** Approximate Cetane Number.

**Solution:**

$$CN \approx 60 - 0.5 \cdot \text{RON} = 60 - 0.5 \cdot 25 = 60 - 12.5 = 47.5$$

Therefore, $CN \approx 48$, which is acceptable for a modern high-speed diesel engine (typically requires $CN \geq 45$).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition Type)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Q: Define pre-ignition in an SI engine. How does it differ from detonation?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
Pre-ignition is the **abnormal combustion of the air-fuel mixture in the cylinder that occurs before the timed spark from the spark plug**, caused by a hot surface inside the combustion chamber such as overheated valves, carbon deposits, or incandescent spark plug electrodes.

**Key Difference (1.5 Marks):**
| Aspect | Pre-Ignition | Detonation |
|---|---|---|
| Timing of cause | Before spark | After spark (during flame travel) |
| Mechanism | Contact with hot surface | Auto-ignition of unburned end-gas |
| Pressure Rise | Gradual but premature | Extremely sharp spike |

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Q: What is meant by the "octane number" of a fuel? Why is it important for SI engines?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
The **Octane Number (ON)** of a fuel is the percentage of iso-octane (2,2,4-trimethylpentane) in a blend of iso-octane and n-heptane that matches the **knocking tendency** of the fuel under standard test conditions in a CFR engine. By convention, iso-octane has an ON of 100 and n-heptane has an ON of 0.

**Importance for SI Engines (1 Mark):**
A higher octane number indicates **greater resistance to knocking (detonation)**. SI engines operating at high compression ratios or with high boost pressures require high-octane fuel (e.g., RON 95 or 98) to prevent engine damage, power loss, and overheating caused by abnormal combustion.
