## PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Q: (a)** Explain the phenomenon of **detonation (knocking)** in a Spark-Ignition engine with the help of a neat sketch of the pressure-crank angle diagram. Discuss the **mechanism** of knock formation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** List and explain the various **engine design factors** that affect the knocking tendency in an SI engine. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks**

**1. Introduction to Detonation (1 Mark):**
Detonation, also called **knocking** or **"pinging,"** is an abnormal combustion phenomenon in SI engines where the **end-gas (unburned air-fuel mixture)** auto-ignites spontaneously and explosively **before** the normal flame front can reach it, producing a sharp, high-pitched metallic sound and causing a sudden pressure spike.

**2. Pressure-Crank Angle Diagram (3 Marks):**

```
   Pressure (P)
       |
   P4  |      ___________ 
   (Knock)|    /:         \____  (Normal Combustion Curve)
       |   / :               \
   P3  |  /  :                \____
       | /   :                     \____ (Knock curve shoots up)
   P2  |/____:______________________________
       |     TDC
       +-----------------------------------> Crank Angle (θ)
            Spark
            Advance
```

*Description for answer script:*

The diagram shows **cylinder pressure (P)** on the Y-axis and **crank angle (θ)** on the X-axis. In **normal combustion**, the pressure rises smoothly to its peak just after TDC and gradually falls. In a **knocking cycle**, the pressure curve shows an **abrupt, almost vertical spike** shortly after TDC, where the end-gas auto-ignites, causing the peak pressure to rise sharply by 2–3 times the normal value.

**3. Mechanism of Knock Formation (3 Marks):**

1. **Spark Ignition:** The spark plug initiates a flame kernel near the end of the compression stroke.
2. **Flame Propagation:** The flame front travels outward across the combustion chamber at a velocity of 15–25 m/s.
3. **End-Gas Compression:** The unburned end-gas trapped between the flame and cylinder walls is compressed and heated by the advancing flame and the rising piston.
4. **Thermal Runaway:** As the end-gas temperature rises (often exceeding 800 K), the rate of pre-flame chemical reactions increases exponentially.
5. **Auto-Ignition:** The end-gas auto-ignites at multiple points almost simultaneously when its induction delay becomes less than the time available.
6. **Detonation Wave:** The auto-ignition creates colliding flame fronts, forming a pressure wave that travels at 300–600 m/s and slams against the walls, producing the characteristic knock.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Engine Design Factors Affecting Knocking:**

1. **Compression Ratio (1 Mark):** Higher compression ratio → higher end-gas temperature and pressure → greater tendency for knock. This is why SI engines are limited to CR ≈ 8:1 to 12:1, while CI engines use CR = 14:1 to 22:1.

2. **Combustion Chamber Shape (1 Mark):** Compact chambers like the **hemispherical** and **pent-roof** designs promote short, quick flame travel and reduce knock. Long, narrow chambers like **pre-chamber** designs have longer flame paths, increasing knock tendency.

3. **Spark Advance (1 Mark):** Excessively advanced spark timing gives more time for end-gas auto-ignition before flame arrival, increasing knock. Retarding the spark reduces knock but lowers thermal efficiency.

4. **Cylinder Bore Size (1 Mark):** Larger bore → longer flame travel distance → more time for end-gas to auto-ignite → more knock. This is why large-bore aircraft and marine engines face severe knock problems.

5. **Spark Plug Location (1 Mark):** A centrally located spark plug shortens flame travel distance, reducing knock. Off-center or side-mounted plugs increase the maximum flame path length.

6. **Number of Spark Plugs (1 Mark):** Using **two spark plugs** per cylinder divides the combustion chamber into smaller zones, each with a shorter flame path, significantly reducing knock (common in high-performance engines).

7. **Intake Conditions and Cooling (1 Mark):** Higher intake air temperature or insufficient cooling raises the end-gas temperature, promoting knock. Effective cooling water jackets help suppress knock.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Q: (a)** Explain the concept of **Octane Number** and **Cetane Number**. Discuss their **significance** in SI and CI engines with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is **pre-ignition**? Explain its **causes, effects, and remedies** in SI engines. Compare pre-ignition and detonation. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks**

**1. Octane Number (ON) — Definition and Significance (3.5 Marks):**

The **Octane Number** is a measure of a fuel's **resistance to knocking** in a Spark-Ignition engine. It is defined as the **percentage by volume of iso-octane (2,2,4-trimethylpentane) in a reference blend** of iso-octane and n-heptane that has the same knocking tendency as the test fuel under standard conditions in a **Cooperative Fuel Research (CFR) engine**.

- **Iso-octane:** ON = 100 (excellent knock resistance)
- **n-Heptane:** ON = 0 (very prone to knock)
- **Examples:** Regular petrol in India ≈ RON 91, Premium petrol ≈ RON 95, High-performance fuel ≈ RON 98 or higher.

**Significance in SI Engines:**
- High-octane fuels are required for high-compression-ratio engines.
- They prevent detonation, ensuring smooth power output and protecting engine components.
- The **Anti-Knock Index (AKI) = (RON + MON) / 2** is displayed at fuel pumps.

**2. Cetane Number (CN) — Definition and Significance (3.5 Marks):**

The **Cetane Number** is a measure of a fuel's **ignition quality** in a Compression-Ignition (diesel) engine. It is defined as the **percentage of n-cetane (n-hexadecane, $\text{C}_{16}\text{H}_{34}$) in a blend** with $\alpha$-methylnaphthalene that has the same ignition delay as the test fuel.

- **n-Cetane:** CN = 100 (excellent ignition quality — short ignition delay)
- **$\alpha$-methylnaphthalene:** CN = 0 (poor ignition quality — long ignition delay)
- **Examples:** Standard diesel ≈ CN 45–55, Premium diesel ≈ CN 55–60.

**Significance in CI Engines:**
- High CN → short ignition delay → smooth, quiet combustion.
- Low CN → long ignition delay → accumulation of fuel → sudden uncontrolled combustion → **diesel knock** (rougher than SI knock).

**Important Relation:** Octane and Cetane numbers are **inversely related** — fuels suitable for SI engines are generally poor for CI engines, and vice versa.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks**

**1. Definition of Pre-Ignition (1 Mark):**
Pre-ignition is the **combustion of the air-fuel mixture in the cylinder initiated by a hot surface (other than the spark) before the scheduled spark occurs**. Common hot surfaces include overheated exhaust valves, carbon deposits, and incandescent spark plug electrodes.

**2. Causes of Pre-Ignition (2 Marks):**

1. **Overheated Exhaust Valves:** Poor valve cooling or lean mixture causes valve temperatures to exceed **870 K**, igniting the incoming charge.
2. **Carbon Deposits:** Accumulated carbon in the chamber glows red-hot and acts as an ignition source.
3. **Incandescent Spark Plug Electrodes:** Damaged or improperly gapped plugs glow and ignite the mixture.
4. **Hot Spots in Combustion Chamber:** Caused by inadequate cooling, sharp corners, or protruding gaskets.
5. **Cross-Firing:** Unwanted sparks from adjacent plug wires in older engines.

**3. Effects of Pre-Ignition (2 Marks):**

1. **Loss of Power:** Premature combustion fights the rising piston, reducing effective work.
2. **Overheating:** Continuous abnormal combustion raises cylinder and component temperatures.
3. **Engine Damage:** Severe pre-ignition can burn pistons, valves, and spark plugs.
4. **"Run-on" or "Dieseling":** Engine continues running after ignition is switched off.
5. **Increased Fuel Consumption** due to inefficient combustion.

**4. Remedies for Pre-Ignition (1 Mark):**

- Use of **cooler-running spark plugs** (higher heat range).
- **Proper cooling system maintenance**.
- Use of **clean, knock-resistant fuel** with adequate octane.
- **Regular decarbonization** of the combustion chamber.
- **Proper valve seating and cooling** to prevent hot valves.

**5. Comparison: Pre-Ignition vs. Detonation (1 Mark):**

| Parameter | Pre-Ignition | Detonation |
|---|---|---|
| Cause | Hot surface | End-gas auto-ignition |
| Timing | Before spark | After spark |
| Effect | Gradual pressure rise | Sudden pressure spike |
| Sound | Often silent | Audible knock |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Pre-Ignition with Detonation:** Many students write them as synonyms. **They are NOT the same.** Pre-ignition is BEFORE the spark; detonation is AFTER the spark. (Penalty: 2–3 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting to draw a diagram:** In a 7-mark question on detonation, the pressure-crank angle diagram is **MANDATORY**. Skipping it costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Mixing up Octane and Cetane numbers:** Octane is for **SI engines (resistance to knock)**, Cetane is for **CI engines (ignition quality)**. Swapping them is a fatal error. (Penalty: full marks)
> 4. **No units in numerical problems:** Always specify the units of ON, CN, temperature (K or °C), and pressure (bar or kPa). Missing units → up to 1 mark deduction.
> 5. **Writing only definitions without mechanisms:** KTU 2024 scheme demands **process understanding**, not just rote definitions. Always explain *how* and *why* with steps.
> 6. **Ignoring the RON vs. MON distinction:** In octane number questions, mentioning only "octane number" without specifying RON or MON may lose 1 mark.
> 7. **Skipping the inverse relation between ON and CN:** When asked to compare, explicitly state that they are **inversely related**.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Normal Combustion:** Smooth flame front propagation from spark plug at velocity **15–25 m/s**.
- **Pre-Ignition:** Abnormal combustion **BEFORE the spark** caused by a **hot surface** (critical temperature ≈ **700 K**).
- **Detonation/Knock:** Abnormal combustion **AFTER the spark** caused by **auto-ignition of end-gas**, producing sharp pressure spikes and audible knock.
- **End-Gas:** The unburned air-fuel mixture trapped between the advancing flame front and cylinder walls.
- **Detonation Wave Velocity:** **300–600 m/s** (sometimes up to **2000 m/s**), much faster than normal flame.
- **Knock Condition:** $\tau_i < t_{\text{flame travel}}$ (induction delay less than flame time).
- **Octane Number (ON):** Measure of **knock resistance** in SI engines; iso-octane = 100, n-heptane = 0.
- **Cetane Number (CN):** Measure of **ignition quality** in CI engines; n-cetane = 100, $\alpha$-methylnaphthalene = 0.
- **RON vs. MON:** RON at 600 rpm, MON at 900 rpm with intake heating; **AKI = (RON + MON)/2** is the pump octane.
- **Inverse ON-CN Relationship:** High-octane fuels typically have low cetane numbers, and vice versa.
- **Higher Compression Ratio → More Knock** in SI engines (this is why SI CR is limited to ~12:1).
- **Branched-chain hydrocarbons** (e.g., iso-octane) are more knock-resistant than **straight-chain** ones (e.g., n-heptane).
- **Anti-knock additives:** Tetra-Ethyl Lead (TEL) historically, modern alternatives include MTBE, ethanol, and toluene.
- **Retarding spark** reduces knock but **decreases thermal efficiency**.
- **Larger cylinder bore → longer flame path → more knock**.
- **Centrally located spark plug → shorter flame path → less knock**.
- **Multiple spark plugs per cylinder** significantly reduce knock (used in high-performance engines).
- **Cooling water temperature ↑** → end-gas stays hot → more knock.
- **Pre-ignition remedies:** Cooler spark plugs, clean combustion chambers, proper valve cooling, decarbonization.
- **Detonation remedies:** Higher octane fuel, lower CR, retarded spark, better chamber design, intake charge cooling.
- **Auto-ignition temperature of gasoline:** ≈ **640 K (367 °C)** at atmospheric pressure (lower under cylinder pressure).
- **Key KTU Numericals:** End-gas temperature: $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1}$; AKI = (RON + MON)/2; ON-CN inverse correlation.
- **Don't forget:** Pre-ignition is a **cause** that can lead to detonation, but they are **distinct phenomena**.
- **"Run-on" or "Dieseling":** Engine continues running after ignition off — a severe form of pre-ignition.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Affecting Detonation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET303 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Factors Affecting Detonation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Detonation

In the context of Spark Ignition (SI) engines, **detonation** (also called **engine knock**) is defined as the **abnormal, uncontrolled, self-ignition of the end gas** (the unburned air-fuel mixture ahead of the advancing flame front) in the combustion chamber. This self-ignition occurs essentially instantaneously and produces a high-pressure shock wave that traverses the combustion chamber at supersonic velocities, producing the characteristic metallic "pinging" or "knocking" sound.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> Detonation is the rapid, spontaneous, and explosive auto-ignition of the unburned end charge in a Spark Ignition (SI) engine cylinder, which occurs *after* the normal flame front has initiated combustion. It is characterized by extremely rapid release of chemical energy, formation of localized high-pressure shock waves, and distinct high-frequency pressure oscillations in the cylinder.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: "The Lit Fuse in a Crowded Room"

Imagine you are standing in a **small, crowded room** (the combustion chamber) holding a **lit match** (the spark plug). The room is filled with a **mixture of gasoline vapor and air** (the air-fuel charge). You intend to burn the gas slowly and controllably from one corner outward, like lighting a candle. This is the **normal flame propagation**.

Now imagine someone **accidentally drops a second, much larger, superheated match** into the *far end* of the room (the end gas) while you are still trying to spread your small flame. The far corner of the room **explodes spontaneously** because the local temperature and pressure there crossed the **auto-ignition threshold**. The room experiences a violent, instantaneous **shock wave** — that shock wave is detonation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three "C's" Required for Detonation:**
> 1. **Concentration** — A sufficiently rich or stoichiometric air-fuel mixture
> 2. **Confinement** — A closed combustion chamber (which is always the case in an engine)
> 3. **Causation** — Elevated temperature and pressure of the end gas exceeding its **self-ignition temperature**

## 1.3 The Physical Mechanism in One Sentence

As the normal flame front advances across the chamber, the unburned **end gas** is compressed (adiabatically) and heated by both the rising cylinder pressure and the heat conducted from the already-burned gases. When the **temperature, pressure, and residence time** of this end gas cross the **critical auto-ignition delay** threshold, it auto-ignites almost simultaneously throughout its volume — producing detonation.

## 1.4 Why Engineers Care About Detonation

> [!WARNING]
> Detonation is **the single most destructive abnormal combustion phenomenon** in SI engines. Consequences include:
> - **Mechanical damage**: Piston crown erosion, ring land cracking, cylinder head pitting
> - **Thermal damage**: Overheating of spark plug electrodes, exhaust valve burning
> - **Performance loss**: Power output drops by 5–15% during heavy knock
> - **Emissions increase**: Sharp rise in unburned hydrocarbons (HC) and NO$_x$
> - **Bearing failure**: The high-frequency shock waves (5–10 kHz) can destroy bearing lubrication films

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Normal flame speed** in SI engine: **15–25 m/s**
- **Detonation wave velocity**: **1500–2500 m/s** (i.e., **supersonic**)
- **Frequency of knock oscillations**: **5,000–10,000 Hz**
- **Pressure rise rate during knock**: Can exceed **1.0 MPa/degree of crank rotation** (vs. 0.05–0.1 MPa/deg in normal combustion)
- **Auto-ignition temperature of iso-octane (C$_8$H$_{18}$)**: **418°C** (a reference hydrocarbon)
- **Octane Number**: A dimensionless scalar from 0 to 100+ representing a fuel's resistance to knock. **Iso-octane = 100** (reference knock-resistant), **n-heptane = 0** (reference knock-prone).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Factors Affecting Detonation

The factors influencing detonation in an SI engine are broadly classified into **three categories**:

1. **Engine Design Factors** (fixed at the design stage)
2. **Operating Condition Factors** (variable during engine use)
3. **Fuel Property Factors** (intrinsic to the fuel)
4. **Ambient / Environmental Factors** (atmospheric conditions)

> Each factor affects one or more of the three thermodynamic triggers for end-gas auto-ignition: **Temperature (T), Pressure (P), and Residence Time ($\tau$)**.

## 2.2 Factor 1 — Engine Design Parameters

### 2.2.1 Compression Ratio ($r_k$)

- **Effect:** Increasing compression ratio → higher peak cylinder pressure and temperature.
- **Why:** End-gas temperature $T_{end} \propto T_1 \cdot r_k^{(\gamma-1)} \cdot \eta_{comb}$
- **Rule of Thumb:** For every **1 unit increase in compression ratio**, the **octane requirement increases by ~2–4 octane numbers**.
- **Practical limit:** Most production SI engines cap compression ratio at **10:1 to 12:1** to avoid knock on standard gasoline (RON 91–95).

### 2.2.2 Combustion Chamber Geometry & Design

| Chamber Type | Knock Resistance | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Pent-roof (4-valve)** | **High** | Compact, short flame path, central spark plug |
| **Hemi-spherical** | **High** | Central spark plug, well-organized squish |
| **Wedge** | **Moderate** | Offset spark plug increases flame travel |
| **Bath-tub (open)** | **Low** | Long flame path → more end gas residence time |
| **Disc (Heron)** | **High** | Efficient squish zones |

- **Squish Area:** Deliberate narrowing of the chamber near the cylinder walls at TDC that forces a turbulent, in-rushing flow — this **accelerates flame propagation** and reduces end-gas exposure.

### 2.2.3 Spark Plug Location

- **Central location** → short, uniform flame travel → **less knock**.
- **Offset or side-mounted** spark plug → longer flame path → more end-gas residence time → **more knock**.

### 2.2.4 Stroke-to-Bore Ratio ($L/D$)

- Higher $L/D$ → longer stroke → larger clearance volume displacement → **larger flame travel distance** at the same bore → more knock tendency.
- Modern "square" or "short-stroke" engines ($L/D \leq 1$) have **better knock resistance**.

### 2.2.5 Valve Timing & Overlap

- Excessive **intake valve closing (IVC) delay** traps hot residual gases → raises end-gas temperature → **increases knock**.
- Aggressive **exhaust valve opening (EVO) advance** reduces scavenging efficiency → hot residual retention → more knock.

## 2.3 Factor 2 — Operating Conditions

### 2.3.1 Load (Throttle Position)

- Higher load → **higher manifold vacuum reduces** (less throttling) → higher in-cylinder pressure and temperature → **more knock**.
- Knock tendency increases roughly **linearly with load** above 50% WOT.

### 2.3.2 Engine Speed (N, RPM)

- At very **low speeds**, the time per cycle is long → end gas has long residence time → **more knock**.
- At **high speeds**, flame quenches at the walls, and end-gas gets less heat-soaking, paradoxically **reducing knock** at high RPM in some engines.
- **However**, very high speeds can cause **surface ignition** (a different phenomenon) and pre-ignition.

### 2.3.3 Spark Advance

- **Advancing spark timing** → peak pressure occurs earlier (further from TDC) → higher peak pressure and temperature on the end gas → **dramatically increases knock**.
- **Retarding spark** is the most common anti-knock countermeasure on modern engines. The **Knock Limited Spark Advance (KLSA)** is the maximum advance at which the engine can run without knock for a given load/speed.

### 2.3.4 Intake Air Temperature

- Hotter intake air → higher in-cylinder temperature at the start of compression → higher end-gas temperature → **more knock**.
- Cold-air intakes and intercoolers (in turbocharged applications) help suppress knock.

### 2.3.5 Jacket Coolant Temperature

- Higher coolant temperature → more heat transferred to combustion chamber walls → end gas gets preheated → **more knock**.
- Optimal coolant temperature is typically **85–95°C**.

### 2.3.6 Mixture Strength (A/F Ratio)

- Knock is **maximum near stoichiometric** and at **slightly rich** mixtures ($\lambda \approx 0.9$).
- Very lean ($\lambda > 1.2$) and very rich ($\lambda < 0.7$) mixtures **suppress knock** due to:
  - Lean → excess air acts as thermal ballast
  - Rich → excess fuel vaporizes, absorbing heat (latent heat of vaporization cools the charge)

## 2.4 Factor 3 — Fuel Properties

### 2.4.1 Octane Rating (Most Important Fuel Property)

- **Research Octane Number (RON):** Measured at 600 rpm, variable intake air temperature.
- **Motor Octane Number (MON):** Measured at 900 rpm, hotter conditions.
- **Anti-Knock Index (AKI)** = (RON + MON) / 2 — used at the pump in some countries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Higher Octane Number → Greater Resistance to Knock**

### 2.4.2 Chemical Structure

- **Straight-chain paraffins** (e.g., n-heptane) → **low octane, high knock tendency**.
- **Branched-chain paraffins** (e.g., iso-octane) → **high octane, low knock tendency**.
- **Aromatics** (e.g., toluene, xylene) → **high octane** but high density and soot-forming tendency.
- **Olefins and Alcohols** → also high octane.

### 2.4.3 Self-Ignition Temperature

- A fuel with **higher self-ignition temperature** is more knock-resistant.
- Self-ignition temperature of a fuel is inversely related to its propensity for auto-ignition of the end gas.

### 2.4.4 Volatility & Latent Heat of Vaporization

- Higher volatility → better air-fuel mixing → more uniform mixture.
- However, excessive volatility can cause **vapor lock** in fuel lines.

### 2.4.5 Molecular Weight & Flame Speed

- Lower molecular weight fuels (e.g., methanol, ethanol) have **higher flame speeds** → faster flame propagation → less end-gas residence time → **less knock**.
- Methanol (RON ≈ 109) and ethanol (RON ≈ 108) are excellent knock suppressants.

## 2.5 Factor 4 — Ambient Conditions

### 2.5.1 Atmospheric Pressure

- **Higher altitude** → lower atmospheric pressure → reduced compression pressures → **less knock** (engines actually gain knock margin at altitude, which is why aircraft engines can use high CR).

### 2.5.2 Atmospheric Temperature

- Hot ambient → higher intake temperature → more knock.
- Cold ambient → less knock.

### 2.5.3 Humidity

- **Humid air has lower partial pressure of oxygen** and contains water vapor (which has high specific heat $c_p$).
- The water vapor acts as a thermal diluent → reduces peak flame temperature → **suppresses knock**.
- Rule: **Higher humidity → Lower knock tendency**.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_k^{(\gamma - 1)}$ | Adiabatic compression temperature of unburned gas | K |
| 2 | $\text{Octane Requirement} \propto r_k$ | Octane number needed rises with CR | — |
| 3 | $v_{\text{flame}} \approx 15$–$25$ m/s | Normal laminar-to-turbulent flame speed in SI | m/s |
| 4 | $v_{\text{det}} \approx 1500$–$2500$ m/s | Detonation wave speed (supersonic) | m/s |
| 5 | $f_{\text{knock}} \approx 5$–$10$ kHz | Knock pressure oscillation frequency | Hz |
| 6 | $P_{\text{max, knock}} > 5$–$8$ MPa | Peak cylinder pressure during severe knock | MPa |
| 7 | $A/F_{\text{stoich}} \approx 14.7$ | Stoichiometric A/F for gasoline | — |
| 8 | $\lambda = \frac{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}}{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}$ | Excess air / equivalence ratio definition | — |
| 9 | $\text{AKI} = \frac{\text{RON} + \text{MON}}{2}$ | Anti-Knock Index (pump octane) | — |
| 10 | $\Delta T_{\text{intake}} \uparrow \Rightarrow T_{\text{end}} \uparrow$ | Intake temp rise → end-gas temp rise | K |
| 11 | $\text{Knock tendency} \propto \frac{1}{\text{Octane No.}}$ | Inverse relation to octane | — |
| 12 | $\text{Knock} \uparrow \text{ as } \text{Spark Advance} \uparrow$ | Spark timing relationship | deg |
| 13 | $r_k \approx 1 + \frac{V_s}{V_c}$ | Compression ratio definition | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **No single formula defines detonation** — it is an empirical phenomenon governed by the chemical kinetics of the end gas. The formulas above capture the most-examined relationships in KTU papers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Higher Compression Ratio Increases Knock

### Step 1 — Start with the Ideal Gas Law and Adiabatic Compression Assumption

For an ideal gas undergoing adiabatic (reversible, no heat transfer) compression in a piston-cylinder assembly:

$$P_1 V_1^{\gamma} = P_2 V_2^{\gamma}$$

where:
- $P_1$, $V_1$ = pressure and volume at BDC (start of compression)
- $P_2$, $V_2$ = pressure and volume at TDC (end of compression)
- $\gamma = c_p / c_v$ = ratio of specific heats for the unburned air-fuel mixture

### Step 2 — Define Compression Ratio

The compression ratio $r_k$ is:

$$r_k = \frac{V_1}{V_2} = \frac{V_c + V_s}{V_c}$$

where $V_s$ = swept volume and $V_c$ = clearance volume.

### Step 3 — Solve for Pressure Ratio

$$P_2 = P_1 \cdot \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma} = P_1 \cdot r_k^{\gamma}$$

### Step 4 — Apply Ideal Gas Law to Get Temperature Relationship

Since $PV = mRT$ and $P_1 V_1 / T_1 = P_2 V_2 / T_2$:

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = T_1 \cdot r_k^{(\gamma - 1)}$$

> **This is the key equation**: end-of-compression temperature rises **exponentially** with $r_k$.

### Step 5 — Numerical Example (KTU-style)

Given:
- $T_1 = 350$ K (intake temperature, ~77°C)
- $r_k = 10$ (compression ratio)
- $\gamma = 1.35$ (typical for air-fuel mixture)

Calculate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_2 &= T_1 \cdot r_k^{(\gamma - 1)} \\
    &= 350 \cdot (10)^{(1.35 - 1)} \\
    &= 350 \cdot (10)^{0.35} \\
    &= 350 \cdot 2.239 \\
    &= 783.6 \text{ K} \approx 510^{\circ}\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Compare Two Compression Ratios

For $r_k = 8$:

$$T_2 = 350 \cdot 8^{0.35} = 350 \cdot 1.949 = 682.2 \text{ K} \approx 409^{\circ}\text{C}$$

**Difference:** $\Delta T_2 = 783.6 - 682.2 = 101.4$ K

> This ~100 K temperature rise is **physically and chemically significant** — the end gas at $r_k = 10$ is much closer to its auto-ignition temperature, dramatically increasing the probability of detonation. This is why high-compression engines require high-octane fuels.

## 3.2 Derivation: Octane Requirement vs. Compression Ratio (Empirical)

A widely-used empirical correlation used in engine design is:

$$\text{ON}_{\text{required}} = \text{ON}_0 + k \cdot (r_k - r_{k,0})$$

where:
- $\text{ON}_0$ = base octane number at reference compression ratio $r_{k,0}$
- $k$ = empirical slope, typically **2–4** octane numbers per unit CR increase

### Worked Example

A stock engine is designed for $r_k = 9.5$ using RON 95 fuel. If the compression ratio is increased to $r_k = 11.5$ while keeping all other parameters constant, what minimum octane rating is needed?

Assume $k = 3$ and reference point is $r_{k,0} = 9.5$, $\text{ON}_0 = 95$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ON}_{\text{required}} &= 95 + 3 \cdot (11.5 - 9.5) \\
&= 95 + 3 \cdot 2 \\
&= 95 + 6 \\
&= 101
\end{aligned}
$$

> The driver would need to switch to **premium racing fuel (RON 101+)** — typically a blend with toluene, MTBE, or ethanol.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Each Factor on Knock

The following 5×5 matrix rates each factor's influence on detonation on a relative scale of **0 (negligible) to 5 (severe)**. This is a KTU-favorite exam format.

| Factor | Knock Tendency (Low setting) | Knock Tendency (High setting) | Direction of Effect | Practical Anti-Knock Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Compression Ratio** | $r_k = 8 \Rightarrow$ Knock = 1 | $r_k = 12 \Rightarrow$ Knock = 5 | **Directly Proportional** | Use higher-octane fuel; reduce CR |
| **Spark Advance** | $10°$ BTDC $\Rightarrow$ Knock = 0 | $40°$ BTDC $\Rightarrow$ Knock = 5 | **Directly Proportional** | Retard timing (KLSA logic) |
| **Intake Air Temp** | $20°C \Rightarrow$ Knock = 1 | $80°C \Rightarrow$ Knock = 5 | **Directly Proportional** | Intercooler, cold air intake |
| **Coolant Temp** | $70°C \Rightarrow$ Knock = 1 | $110°C \Rightarrow$ Knock = 5 | **Directly Proportional** | Maintain 85–95°C coolant |
| **Engine Load** | 20% WOT $\Rightarrow$ Knock = 0 | 100% WOT $\Rightarrow$ Knock = 4 | **Directly Proportional** | Downshift, run leaner |
| **A/F Ratio ($\lambda$)** | $\lambda = 1.3$ (lean) $\Rightarrow$ Knock = 0 | $\lambda = 0.9$ (rich) $\Rightarrow$ Knock = 4 | **Parabolic** (max at stoich–rich) | Run lean or very rich |
| **Octane Number** | RON 98 $\Rightarrow$ Knock = 0 | RON 85 $\Rightarrow$ Knock = 5 | **Inversely Proportional** | Use higher-octane fuel |
| **Engine Speed (RPM)** | 1500 rpm $\Rightarrow$ Knock = 3 | 5000 rpm $\Rightarrow$ Knock = 1 | **Inversely Related** (in mid-range) | Operate in mid-high RPM band |
| **Humidity** | 20% RH $\Rightarrow$ Knock = 4 | 100% RH $\Rightarrow$ Knock = 1 | **Inversely Proportional** | Add water/methanol injection |
| **Altitude** | Sea level $\Rightarrow$ Knock = 3 | 3000 m $\Rightarrow$ Knock = 1 | **Inversely Proportional** | Reduce CR or use regular fuel at altitude |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Logical Analysis of Knock Initiation

Detonation does not occur uniformly. The end gas auto-ignites at the **thermally hottest spot** first. The chain of events is:

**Step 1:** Spark initiates normal flame kernel near spark plug at $\theta_s$ degrees BTDC.

**Step 2:** Flame kernel grows to a self-sustaining flame front, $\sim 30\%$ of mass burned within $\sim 20°$ crank angle.

**Step 3:** Flame front advances across the chamber. Behind it, burned gases reach $T_{\text{peak}} \approx 2000$–$2500$ K and $P \approx 4$–$6$ MPa.

**Step 4:** The unburned end gas is compressed *adiabatically* (almost — there's a small heat conduction from burned gas) by the rising chamber pressure and pre-heated by conduction from hot burned gases.

**Step 5:** The end gas temperature $T_{end}$ rises. Its auto-ignition delay $\tau_{\text{ign}}$ (a function of $T$ and $P$ per the **Arrhenius chemical kinetics** relation) falls:

$$\tau_{\text{ign}} = A \cdot \exp\left(\frac{E_a}{R \cdot T_{end}}\right) \cdot P^{-n}$$

where $E_a$ = activation energy, $R$ = universal gas constant, $A$ = pre-exponential factor, $n$ = pressure exponent (typically 1.0–1.5 for hydrocarbons).

**Step 6:** When $\tau_{\text{ign}}$ becomes less than the **residence time** of the end gas (i.e., time the end gas has been exposed to high $T$ and $P$), the end gas auto-ignites almost simultaneously — this is the **critical condition for knock**.

> **The Knock Criterion:**
>
> $$\boxed{\tau_{\text{ign}}(T_{end}, P_{end}) \leq \tau_{\text{residence}}}$$

**Step 7:** The auto-ignition releases energy volumetrically in the end gas, creating a **localized high-pressure kernel** that expands at supersonic speed — this is the **detonation wave**.

**Step 8:** The detonation wave reflects off the cylinder walls, producing the **characteristic high-frequency pressure oscillations** (5–10 kHz) and the audible knock.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Causal Loop of Detonation in SI Engine

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Spark Ignition at Spark Plug] --> B[Flame Kernel Formation]
    B --> C[Normal Flame Propagation Across Chamber]
    C --> D[Heat Release in Burned Zone]
    D --> E[Rising Cylinder Pressure P]
    D --> F[Heat Conduction to End Gas]
    E --> G[End Gas Compressed and Heated]
    F --> G
    G --> H{End Gas Temp Exceeds Auto-Ignition Threshold}
    H -- Yes --> I[Volumetric Auto-Ignition of End Gas]
    I --> J[Detonation Wave Formation]
    J --> K[Shock Wave Reflects from Walls]
    K --> L[High-Frequency Pressure Oscillations 5-10 kHz]
    L --> M[Audible Knock and Mechanical Damage]
    H -- No --> N[Normal Combustion Completes]
    N --> O[Power Stroke]
```

```mermaid
    ED1 --> END[End Gas Temperature and Pressure]

    END --> DEC{Detonation or No Detonation}
```


## 4.5 Comparative Table — Common Anti-Knock Additives and Fuels

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes how different fuel additives and alternative fuels affect knock resistance. This is a frequently tested KTU comparison topic.

| Fuel / Additive | RON (Approx) | Anti-Knock Effect | Engineering Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| **n-Heptane (reference)** | 0 | Worst — extreme knock | Used as knock-prone baseline |
| **Iso-Octane (reference)** | 100 | Excellent | Reference fuel only |
| **Toluene (C₇H₈)** | 120 | Excellent | Toxic, raises emissions |
| **Methanol (CH₃OH)** | 109 | Excellent | Corrosive, low energy density |
| **Ethanol (C₂H₅OH)** | 108 | Excellent | Hygroscopic, cold-start issues |
| **MTBE** | 118 | Excellent | Banned in some regions (water pollution) |
| **ETBE** | 118 | Excellent | Less water-soluble than MTBE |
| **TEL (Tetraethyl Lead)** | +20 ON per 0.5 g/L | Excellent (historically) | Toxic — phased out globally |
| **MMT (Methylcyclopentadienyl Mn)** | +5–10 ON | Moderate | Manganese deposits on spark plugs |
| **Regular Gasoline (India/Kerala)** | 91 | Moderate | Cheaper, widely available |
| **Premium Gasoline** | 95 | Good | More expensive |
| **LPG (Liquefied Petroleum Gas)** | 96–110 | Excellent | Lower energy density, tank space |
| **CNG (Compressed Natural Gas)** | 120+ | Excellent | Low power output, tank bulk |

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / May 2024 Style]**

### Question 1: Define detonation in an SI engine. How is it different from normal combustion?

**Model Answer:**

Detonation in a Spark Ignition (SI) engine is the **abnormal, uncontrolled, spontaneous auto-ignition of the unburned end gas** in the combustion chamber, which occurs after the normal flame front has initiated. It produces a high-pressure shock wave that travels at supersonic speeds (1500–2500 m/s) and creates audible knock.

| Parameter | Normal Combustion | Detonation |
|---|---|---|
| Flame speed | 15–25 m/s (subsonic) | 1500–2500 m/s (supersonic) |
| Initiation | Spark plug controlled | Spontaneous end-gas auto-ignition |
| Pressure rise | Smooth, gradual | Abrupt, high-frequency oscillations |
| Sound | Quiet | Sharp metallic "ping" |
| Damage | None | Piston erosion, ring failure |

**Key distinction:** Normal combustion is **controlled and progressive**; detonation is **uncontrolled and volumetric** (whole end-gas ignites at once).

---

### Question 2: What is Octane Number? Why are branched-chain hydrocarbons more knock-resistant than straight-chain ones?

**Model Answer:**

**Octane Number (ON)** is a dimensionless measure of a fuel's resistance to knock, scaled such that **iso-octane (2,2,4-trimethylpentane) = 100** and **n-heptane = 0**. A fuel with octane number 95 performs (in terms of knock resistance) like a mixture of 95% iso-octane and 5% n-heptane.

**Why branched-chain hydrocarbons resist knock better:**

1. **Longer auto-ignition delay**: Branched chains have tertiary C–H bonds that are harder to break via free-radical chain reactions, so the end gas takes longer to auto-ignite.
2. **Lower tendency for peroxide formation**: The intermediate hydroperoxides that initiate auto-ignition form less readily in sterically hindered branched molecules.
3. **Higher activation energy $E_a$**: The Arrhenius chemical kinetics barrier is higher, so higher temperatures are required for the same auto-ignition rate.

> **Example:** iso-octane (branched, 8 carbons) has ON = 100, while n-octane (straight chain, 8 carbons) has ON ≈ −10 to −20. Same carbon count, very different knock behavior.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — End Semester Evaluation (ESE), Model Paper Style]**

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1

> **Q: (a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the various **engine design factors** that affect detonation in an SI engine. Discuss the role of compression ratio with appropriate derivations.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** A four-cylinder SI engine has a compression ratio of 10.5:1. The intake temperature is 60°C and the cylinder pressure at BDC is 100 kPa. If the mixture has $\gamma = 1.35$, calculate (i) the temperature of the unburned end gas at TDC just before ignition, and (ii) the minimum octane number required if the empirical relation is $\text{ON} = 87 + 2.5 \cdot (r_k - 9)$.

---

#### Model Solution to A(a) — Engine Design Factors Affecting Detonation [7 marks]

The engine design factors affecting detonation in an SI engine are:

**1. Compression Ratio (Most Critical Design Factor):** Higher compression ratio increases both peak pressure and peak temperature of the unburned end gas (derived below). This brings the end gas closer to its auto-ignition temperature, dramatically increasing knock tendency. [1 mark]

**Derivation:**

For adiabatic compression of the unburned mixture:

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_k^{(\gamma - 1)}$$

The auto-ignition delay of the end gas decreases exponentially with temperature. As $r_k$ increases, $T_2$ rises, $\tau_{\text{ign}}$ falls, and the probability of detonation increases sharply. [2 marks]

**2. Combustion Chamber Design:** Compact, centrally-located spark plug chambers with strong squish (e.g., pent-roof, hemispherical) shorten the flame travel distance and reduce end-gas residence time. Open "bath-tub" chambers have long flame paths and high knock tendency. [1 mark]

**3. Spark Plug Location:** A centrally-located spark plug reduces flame travel distance and the time available for end-gas auto-ignition. Side-mounted plugs (older wedge chambers) suffer more knock. [1 mark]

**4. Stroke-to-Bore Ratio:** A lower $L/D$ (square or short-stroke engines) reduces the flame travel distance for a given bore, decreasing knock tendency. Long-stroke engines ($L/D > 1$) are more knock-prone. [1 mark]

**5. Valve Timing and Overlap:** Late intake valve closing (IVC) and high valve overlap trap hot residual exhaust gases, raising the end-gas temperature at the start of compression and increasing knock. [1 mark]

---

#### Model Solution to A(b) — Numerical Problem [7 marks]

**Given:**
- Compression ratio: $r_k = 10.5$
- Intake temperature: $T_1 = 60°C = 60 + 273 = 333$ K
- BDC pressure: $P_1 = 100$ kPa
- Ratio of specific heats: $\gamma = 1.35$
- Empirical relation: $\text{ON} = 87 + 2.5 \cdot (r_k - 9)$

**Part (i) — End-gas temperature at TDC [3 marks]:**

Apply the adiabatic compression relation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_2 &= T_1 \cdot r_k^{(\gamma - 1)} \\
    &= 333 \cdot (10.5)^{(1.35 - 1)} \\
    &= 333 \cdot (10.5)^{0.35}
\end{aligned}
$$

Calculate $(10.5)^{0.35}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln(10.5) &= 2.3514 \\
0.35 \cdot \ln(10.5) &= 0.35 \cdot 2.3514 = 0.8230 \\
e^{0.8230} &= 2.277
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$T_2 = 333 \cdot 2.277 = 758.3 \text{ K} \approx 485.1°C$$

**End-gas temperature at TDC ≈ 758.3 K (485°C)** ✓

**Part (ii) — Minimum Octane Number [4 marks]:**

Substitute $r_k = 10.5$ into the empirical relation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ON}_{\text{required}} &= 87 + 2.5 \cdot (r_k - 9) \\
&= 87 + 2.5 \cdot (10.5 - 9) \\
&= 87 + 2.5 \cdot 1.5 \\
&= 87 + 3.75 \\
&= 90.75 \approx 91
\end{aligned}
$$

**Minimum Octane Number Required ≈ 91 (RON 91 gasoline)** ✓

**Engineering Interpretation:** This explains why the engine must use **regular gasoline (RON 91)** as a minimum, not lower octane fuels. In hot ambient conditions or with retarded timing strategies, premium fuel (RON 95) may be advisable to provide knock margin. [1 mark]

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2

> **Q: (a) [7 marks]** Discuss the **fuel properties** and **operating condition factors** affecting detonation. How does the A/F ratio influence knock tendency, and why does humidity reduce knock?
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** A multi-cylinder SI engine is tested on a dynamometer. With stock settings, the knock limit is reached at 28° BTDC spark advance at 3500 rpm and 80% WOT. The intake air temperature rises from 35°C to 75°C. Assuming a knock-onset advance retardation rate of 0.5° per 10°C rise in intake temperature, calculate (i) the new knock-limited spark advance (KLSA), and (ii) what happens to the indicated mean effective pressure (IMEP) and thermal efficiency.

---

#### Model Solution to B(a) — Fuel & Operating Factors [7 marks]

**Fuel Property Factors:**

**1. Octane Rating:** The most important fuel property. Higher octane number ⇒ greater resistance to knock. Iso-octane (RON 100) is the reference. Branched paraffins, aromatics, and alcohols have high octane numbers. [1 mark]

**2. Chemical Structure:** Branched-chain paraffins (e.g., iso-octane) and aromatics (e.g., toluene) have higher octane numbers than straight-chain paraffins (e.g., n-heptane) due to higher activation energies for auto-ignition. [1 mark]

**3. Self-Ignition Temperature:** A higher self-ignition temperature indicates greater knock resistance. End gas must reach this temperature for detonation to occur. [1 mark]

**4. Latent Heat of Vaporization:** Fuels with high latent heat (alcohols like methanol ~1100 kJ/kg) absorb significant heat during vaporization, cooling the intake charge and reducing knock. [1 mark]

**Operating Condition Factors:**

**5. Spark Advance:** Advancing the spark timing increases the peak pressure and temperature of the end gas, raising knock tendency. Retarding the spark is the most common anti-knock measure. [1 mark]

**6. Engine Load and A/F Ratio:** Knock is maximum near the stoichiometric ratio ($\lambda = 1$) and slightly rich. Very lean ($\lambda > 1.2$) and very rich ($\lambda < 0.7$) mixtures suppress knock. Very lean: excess air acts as a thermal ballast. Very rich: excess fuel absorbs heat via vaporization. [1 mark]

**7. Humidity:** Humid air has a lower partial pressure of oxygen and contains water vapor, which has high specific heat. The water vapor dilutes the charge and acts as a thermal ballast, lowering the peak flame temperature and the end-gas temperature, thereby **suppressing knock**. For every 10% increase in absolute humidity, knock tendency reduces by ~1–2%. [1 mark]

---

#### Model Solution to B(b) — KLSA Problem [7 marks]

**Given:**
- Initial KLSA: $\theta_{KLSA,1} = 28°$ BTDC
- Initial intake air temperature: $T_1 = 35°C$
- New intake air temperature: $T_2 = 75°C$
- Retardation rate: $0.5°$ per $10°C$ rise in intake temperature

**Part (i) — New KLSA [3 marks]:**

Temperature rise:

$$\Delta T = 75 - 35 = 40°C$$

Spark advance retardation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta\theta &= \left(\frac{40}{10}\right) \cdot 0.5° \\
&= 4 \cdot 0.5° \\
&= 2°
\end{aligned}
$$

New KLSA:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{KLSA,2} &= \theta_{KLSA,1} - \Delta\theta \\
&= 28° - 2° \\
&= 26° \text{ BTDC}
\end{aligned}
$$

**New Knock-Limited Spark Advance = 26° BTDC** ✓

**Part (ii) — Effect on IMEP and Thermal Efficiency [4 marks]:**

**Effect on IMEP (Indicated Mean Effective Pressure):**

Retarding the spark from 28° to 26° BTDC shifts the **peak pressure point** further from TDC (towards the expansion stroke). This means less of the combustion pressure acts on the piston during the power stroke, and more heat is lost to the cylinder walls during the longer burn duration.

$$\text{IMEP} \downarrow \quad (\text{decreases by approximately 1–2% per degree of retard})$$

**Estimated IMEP reduction:** ~2–4% [1.5 marks]

**Effect on Thermal Efficiency:**

The Otto cycle thermal efficiency is given by:

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_k^{(\gamma - 1)}}$$

Retarding the spark timing does **not change** the compression ratio or the *thermodynamic* Otto efficiency formula. However, the **actual indicated thermal efficiency** decreases because:

1. **Increased heat transfer losses** — longer combustion duration allows more heat loss to walls.
2. **Increased time for dissociation** — at the lower peak pressure, less NO$_x$ is formed (a small benefit, but efficiency is the net effect).
3. **Combustion closer to TDC** is more efficient; retarded timing moves peak pressure away from optimal 15–18° ATDC, lowering effective efficiency.

$$\eta_{th, \text{indicated}} \downarrow \quad (\text{typical reduction of 0.5–1.5% absolute})$$

**Engineer's Conclusion:** The engine has a built-in **knock management system** that protects the engine by retarding spark at the cost of 2–4% power and 1–2% efficiency loss. This is the price of safe operation on lower-octane fuels. [2.5 marks]

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes that COST MARKS in KTU Valuation:**
>
> **1. Confusing "Pre-ignition" with "Detonation":** Pre-ignition is the *ignition of the charge BEFORE the spark* (due to hot spots, overheated plug). Detonation is the *auto-ignition of end gas AFTER the spark*. They are different phenomena. Don't mix them up. [-2 marks if confused]
>
> **2. Forgetting to Convert Temperatures to Kelvin:** Whenever using gas law equations ($PV = mRT$, adiabatic relations), the temperature MUST be in Kelvin. $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$. Forgetting this yields an answer that is off by ~20%, and you lose all 7 marks on the numerical.
>
> **3. Confusing RON and MON:** Research Octane Number (RON) is measured at 600 rpm; Motor Octane Number (MON) is at 900 rpm with higher mixture temperature. RON > MON for any real fuel. Anti-Knock Index (AKI) = (RON + MON)/2.
>
> **4. Stating "Higher Compression Ratio Reduces Knock":** This is the *opposite* of the truth. Higher CR = higher end-gas temperature = MORE knock. Many students write this incorrectly.
>
> **5. Writing "Engine Speed Has No Effect on Knock":** Partially correct only above 5000 rpm. Below 2000 rpm, the long cycle time gives end gas long residence time → MORE knock. State the full parabolic/U-shaped relationship.
>
> **6. Forgetting Units in Octane Number Calculation:** Octane number is dimensionless. Don't write "RON = 91 octane" — just "RON = 91" or "Octane Number = 91".
>
> **7. Not Distinguishing Stoichiometric vs. Equivalence Ratio:** A/F ratio of 14.7 is for gasoline specifically. For other fuels, it differs. Always state the fuel or use equivalence ratio $\lambda$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 4 — Factors Affecting Detonation**

- [x] **Detonation** = auto-ignition of end gas *after* spark. **Pre-ignition** = ignition of charge *before* spark. These are distinct.
- [x] Detonation wave speed = **1500–2500 m/s** (supersonic), normal flame = **15–25 m/s**.
- [x] Knock frequency = **5–10 kHz** pressure oscillations.
- [x] **End-gas** is the unburned mixture ahead of the advancing flame front.
- [x] **Knock criterion:** $\tau_{\text{ign}}(T, P) \leq \tau_{\text{residence}}$.
- [x] **Compression ratio** is the single most important design factor. Formula: $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_k^{(\gamma-1)}$.
- [x] **Octane rating** is the most important fuel property. **Higher ON = more knock resistance.**
- [x] **Iso-octane = 100** (reference knock-resistant); **n-heptane = 0** (reference knock-prone).
- [x] **RON** is at 600 rpm; **MON** is at 900 rpm; **AKI = (RON+MON)/2**.
- [x] **Branched paraffins** and **aromatics** have high octane; **straight-chain paraffins** have low octane.
- [x] **Pent-roof and hemispherical chambers** with central spark plug = best knock resistance.
- [x] **Spark advance** is the most influential operating parameter; retarding timing reduces knock.
- [x] **A/F ratio effect is parabolic**: maximum knock near stoichiometric to slightly rich; lean or very rich suppresses knock.
- [x] **Humidity reduces knock** (water vapor dilutes charge, high $c_p$ thermal ballast).
- [x] **Altitude reduces knock** (lower atmospheric pressure).
- [x] **Coolant temperature** above 95°C increases knock.
- [x] **Intake air temperature** rise increases knock.
- [x] **KLSA (Knock Limited Spark Advance)** = max safe spark advance for a given operating condition.
- [x] Detonation damages: **piston crown erosion, ring land cracking, spark plug electrode melting, bearing failure**.
- [x] Anti-knock fuels: **methanol, ethanol, toluene, MTBE**; historical: **TEL (leaded gasoline, now banned)**.
- [x] Modern engines use **knock sensors** (piezoelectric) feeding back to the ECU for **retard-on-knock** strategy.
- [x] Compression ratio rule of thumb: **every +1 in CR requires ~+2 to +4 in octane number**.
- [x] Always convert °C to **K** in numerical problems (add 273.15).

---

> **End of Notes — KTU 2024 Scheme | PCIET303 Thermal Engineering | Module 4 — Factors Affecting Detonation**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET303 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Knocking in Engine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Knocking in SI (Spark Ignition) Engines** is an abnormal combustion phenomenon characterized by the spontaneous, uncontrolled auto-ignition of the **end-gas** (the unburned charge ahead of the propagating flame front) within the combustion chamber. This auto-ignition produces an extremely rapid release of chemical energy, generating high-frequency pressure waves that strike the cylinder walls, producing the characteristic metallic "knocking" or "pinking" sound and potentially causing severe mechanical damage to the engine.

### 1.1.1 The Three Defining Characteristics of Knocking
1. **Auto-ignition of end-gas** before the flame front arrives.
2. **Sudden, near-instantaneous energy release** (not a smooth pressure rise).
3. **High-frequency oscillating pressure waves** propagating through the combustion chamber gases.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Popcorn" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine heating a pan of popcorn kernels on a stove.
> - **Normal Combustion:** Each kernel pops individually in sequence — a smooth, controlled sequence of small "pops" (this is your orderly flame front).
> - **Knocking:** The pan is left too long and **hundreds of kernels pop simultaneously**, creating one violent, loud, chaotic BANG that shakes the entire pan and shatters unpopped kernels.
>
> In an engine, that violent simultaneous "pop" of unburned gas is the **end-gas auto-igniting all at once**, sending shock waves through the cylinder — the auditory signature of knock.

---

## 1.3 Normal Combustion vs. Knocking — The Crucial Distinction

| Parameter | Normal Combustion | Knocking (Detonation) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Ignition source | Single spark plug | Spark plug + auto-ignition of end-gas |
| Flame propagation | Smooth, controlled (~15-25 m/s) | Disrupted; secondary explosive front forms |
| Pressure rise rate | Gradual (smooth p-θ curve) | Extremely steep (sharp inflection in p-θ) |
| Sound | Smooth hum | Sharp metallic rattle / pinking |
| Energy release | Progressive | Near-instantaneous, violent |
| Cylinder damage risk | Low | **High** (piston crown, head gasket failure) |

---

## 1.4 Why Knocking Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Knocking is the **single most important limitation** on the thermal efficiency and compression ratio of an SI engine. KTU 2024 examiners expect students to:
> - Link **compression ratio (r)** with **knock tendency**.
> - Connect **fuel properties (Octane Number)** with anti-knock performance.
> - Justify why diesel engines (CI) can run at **higher compression ratios** than petrol engines (SI).
> - Discuss **anti-knock additives (TEL, MTBE, ethanol)** in modern fuels.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Phases of Normal SI Engine Combustion (Pre-Knock Context)

To understand knocking, we **must** first understand normal combustion. The p-θ (pressure-crank angle) diagram of normal SI combustion has four distinct phases:

### Phase 1: Ignition Lag (0 → θ₁)
- Duration: ~0 to ~8° of crank rotation after spark.
- A small kernel of flame nucleus forms around the spark plug electrodes.
- Pressure rise is negligible.

### Phase 2: Flame Propagation (θ₁ → θ₂)
- The flame front advances **smoothly and spherically** outward from the spark plug.
- Speed: **15–25 m/s** under normal conditions.
- Pressure rises steadily; this is the **useful work-producing phase**.

### Phase 3: Rapid Combustion / Peak Pressure (θ₂ → θ₃)
- Peak cylinder pressure is reached **~12°–15° ATDC** (after top dead center).
- Combustion efficiency is at its maximum here.

### Phase 4: After-burning (θ₃ → θ₄)
- Burnt gases continue to react with remaining unburned mixture.
- Pressure decays gradually.

> [!NOTE]
> **Knocking occurs when Phase 2 is interrupted.** The unburned mixture ahead of the flame (the **end-gas**) is compressed and heated by both the advancing flame AND the rising piston. If its temperature exceeds its **auto-ignition temperature** before the flame arrives, it auto-ignites spontaneously, creating a second, uncontrolled combustion front.

---

## 2.2 The Mechanism of Knocking — Step-by-Step Physics

Let the **end-gas** be the thin layer of unburned air-fuel mixture trapped between the **advancing flame front** and the **combustion chamber walls/piston crown**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Conditions Required for Knocking (The "Knock Triangle"):**
> 1. Sufficiently high **temperature** of the end-gas.
> 2. Sufficiently high **pressure** of the end-gas.
> 3. Sufficient **residence time** (delay between flame initiation and end-gas auto-ignition).

### 2.2.1 Step-by-Step Knock Formation Sequence

1. **Spark initiates flame kernel** at the spark plug.
2. **Flame front propagates** at ~15-25 m/s, leaving behind burnt gases at high temperature.
3. **End-gas ahead of flame** is being:
   - Compressed by the rising piston (volume decreasing).
   - Heated by conduction from the flame front and hot walls.
4. **End-gas temperature and pressure rise** beyond the **auto-ignition temperature (AIT)** of the fuel.
5. **Spontaneous auto-ignition** of the entire end-gas volume occurs almost simultaneously.
6. **Two flame fronts collide** inside the chamber, creating a high-pressure collision zone.
7. **Pressure waves** propagate, reflect off walls, and produce the characteristic **metallic "knock"**.
8. If severe, **mechanical damage** follows: piston crown erosion, spark plug electrode melting, cylinder head damage, bearing failures.

---

## 2.3 Detonation vs. Pre-ignition — The KTU Classic Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **favourite KTU question** (2-mark/short-answer).

| Feature | Pre-ignition | Detonation (Knocking) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Cause | Ignition of charge **before** spark (by hot spot) | Auto-ignition of end-gas **after** spark |
| Timing | Charge ignites well before TDC | Charge ignites just before/after TDC, ahead of normal flame |
| Combustion type | Slow, smooth but mistimed | Extremely rapid, violent |
| Severity indicator | Often leads to detonation | Final stage of abnormal combustion |
| Common trigger | Overheated spark plug, carbon deposits, hot exhaust valve | High compression ratio, low octane fuel, high load |

---

## 2.4 Factors Affecting Knocking — The Comprehensive List

### A. Engine Design Factors (Increase Knocking ↓ / Decrease Knocking ↑)

| Factor | Effect on Knocking | Mechanism |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| ↑ Compression ratio | **↑↑ Knocking** | Higher TDC pressure/temperature |
| ↑ Combustion chamber wall temperature | ↑ Knocking | Conductive heating of end-gas |
| ↑ Spark advance | ↑ Knocking | Less time for normal flame to complete burn |
| Larger cylinder bore | ↑ Knocking | Longer flame travel distance |
| Chamber shape — compact/centralized | **↓ Knocking** | Shorter flame travel, less end-gas |
| Position of spark plug — central | **↓ Knocking** | Shorter flame path |

### B. Fuel-Related Factors

| Factor | Effect on Knocking |
| :--- | :--- |
| ↓ Octane number | ↑ Knocking |
| Higher paraffin content (long-chain n-alkanes) | ↑ Knocking (lowest resistance) |
| Higher aromatic/naphthene content | ↓ Knocking |
| Iso-paraffins (branched) | ↓ Knocking |
| ↑ Self-ignition temperature | ↓ Knocking |
| ↓ Latent heat of vaporization | ↑ Knocking (less charge cooling) |

### C. Operating Condition Factors

| Factor | Effect on Knocking |
| :--- | :--- |
| ↑ Load (throttle opening) | ↑ Knocking |
| ↑ Engine speed (RPM) | ↓ Knocking (less time for auto-ignition) |
| ↑ Inlet air temperature | ↑ Knocking |
| ↓ Humidity | ↑ Knocking |
| ↑ Altitude | ↓ Knocking (lower atmospheric pressure) |
| ↑ Retardation of spark | ↓ Knocking |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` and `\mid` instead of vertical pipes to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Variables / Units | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | End-gas temperature relation | $T_{eg} = T_1 \cdot (r_c)^{(\gamma-1)}$ | $T_{eg}$: end-gas temp, $T_1$: initial temp, $r_c$: compression ratio, $\gamma$: specific heat ratio | Higher $r_c$ → higher $T_{eg}$ → more knock |
| 2 | Octane Number (ON) | $ON = 100 - \dfrac{\rho_{iso} - \rho_{sample}}{\rho_{iso} - \rho_{n}}$ | $\rho$: knock intensity rating in CFR engine | PRF: Primary Reference Fuels |
| 3 | Methanol Octane Equivalent | $MON_{methanol} \approx 100+$ | Empirical anti-knock rating | Higher than gasoline |
| 4 | Flame propagation speed | $S_f \approx 15$ to $25 \text{ m/s}$ (normal) | $S_f$: flame speed | Knock disrupts this |
| 5 | Detonation pressure rise | $\dfrac{dp}{d\theta} \geq 10$ bar/° | For knock detection | Standard indicator |
| 6 | Auto-ignition delay (chemical) | $\tau_{ig} = A \cdot p^{-n} \cdot \exp(E/RT)$ | Arrhenius-type | For end-gas |
| 7 | Critical compression ratio | $r_{crit} = f(ON)$ | Function of fuel octane | Anti-knock property |

---

## 2.6 Octane Number — Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Board Standard Answer):**
> The **Octane Number (ON)** of a fuel is the percentage of **iso-octane (2,2,4-trimethylpentane)** in a mixture of iso-octane and n-heptane that has the **same knocking tendency** as the test fuel, when tested in a standard **CFR (Cooperative Fuel Research) engine** under standard conditions.
> - **iso-octane**: $ON = 100$ (high resistance to knock)
> - **n-heptane**: $ON = 0$ (very low resistance to knock)

### Real-World Utility
- **Refineries** use ON to grade petrol: Regular (91 ON), Premium (95 ON), Super (98+ ON) in India.
- **Engine designers** select fuel ON to match the **design compression ratio** of the engine.
- **Emission control**: Higher ON fuels allow more efficient combustion and lower emissions.

---

## 2.7 Anti-Knock Additives

> [!NOTE]
> - **Tetra-Ethyl Lead (TEL)**: $\text{Pb(C}_2\text{H}_5)_4$ — historically dominant; phased out globally due to lead toxicity and catalytic converter poisoning.
> - **Methyl tert-Butyl Ether (MTBE)**: Oxygenate additive, raises ON by ~7-10 units.
> - **Ethanol**: $\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}$ — renewable oxygenate, raises ON to ~108.
> - **Methanol**: $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$ — ON ≈ 106-112.
> - **Methylcyclopentadienyl Manganese Tricarbonyl (MMT)**: Modern metallic anti-knock.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: End-Gas Temperature as a Function of Compression Ratio

We aim to show mathematically why increasing compression ratio drives end-gas to auto-ignition.

### Assumptions
- End-gas behaves as an **ideal gas**.
- Compression of end-gas is **adiabatic and reversible** (isentropic).
- The combustion chamber volume changes following piston motion.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** For an isentropic process of an ideal gas:

$$TV^{\gamma-1} = \text{constant}$$

**Step 2:** Applying this to the end-gas being compressed from volume $V_1$ to $V_2$:

$$T_1 \cdot V_1^{\gamma-1} = T_2 \cdot V_2^{\gamma-1}$$

**Step 3:** Solving for the final end-gas temperature $T_2$:

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma-1}$$

**Step 4:** The volume ratio equals the **effective compression ratio** experienced by the end-gas ($r_{eg}$). This is **not** the same as the geometric compression ratio — it is the ratio of the **total volume** to the **end-gas volume** at the moment of auto-ignition:

$$\dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = r_{eg}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting back:

$$T_{eg} = T_1 \cdot r_{eg}^{\gamma-1}$$

**Step 6:** For typical SI engine gases, $\gamma \approx 1.35$. If $r_{eg} = 10$:

$$T_{eg} = T_1 \cdot 10^{0.35} = T_1 \cdot 2.24$$

**Step 7:** If $T_1 = 350 \text{ K}$ (typical inlet temperature):

$$T_{eg} = 350 \times 2.24 = 784 \text{ K} = 511^\circ \text{C}$$

**Step 8:** Most gasoline hydrocarbons have **auto-ignition temperatures** in the range **$280^\circ \text{C}$ to $470^\circ \text{C}$** depending on octane rating. If $T_{eg} > AIT$, auto-ignition (knocking) occurs.

**Step 9:** Therefore, the **knock margin** is:

$$\Delta T_{knock} = AIT_{fuel} - T_{eg}$$

> If $\Delta T_{knock} > 0$ → No knock.
> If $\Delta T_{knock} \leq 0$ → Knocking occurs.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Octane Number Determination (CFR Engine Method)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Knock Intensity (KI)** of a fuel in a CFR engine is rated against **Primary Reference Fuels (PRF)** — mixtures of iso-octane and n-heptane.

### Step-by-Step Procedure (Waukesha/CFR Method)

**Step 1:** Operate the CFR engine on the **test fuel** at standard conditions.
- Compression ratio: variable.
- Spark advance: adjusted to give **maximum knock intensity**.
- Knock Meter Reading (KMR): recorded as $K_{sample}$.

**Step 2:** Replace test fuel with a **PRF mixture** of known iso-octane percentage.
- Adjust compression ratio to obtain the **same KMR** as the test fuel.
- The PRF is varied until its knock matches the test fuel.

**Step 3:** The volume percentage of iso-octane in the matching PRF = **Octane Number** of the test fuel.

**Mathematical Statement:**

$$ON_{test} = \left(\dfrac{V_{iso}}{V_{iso} + V_{n-h}}\right) \times 100 \quad \text{at matched knock intensity}$$

Where:
- $V_{iso}$ = volume of iso-octane
- $V_{n-h}$ = volume of n-heptane

### Two Test Standards

| Test Method | Engine Speed | Variable | Octane Number Produced |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Research Octane Number (RON)** | 600 RPM | Compression ratio | RON |
| **Motor Octane Number (MON)** | 900 RPM | Spark advance (heated mixture) | MON |
| **Anti-Knock Index (AKI)** | — | $\dfrac{RON + MON}{2}$ | Posted at pump (US) |

---

## 3.3 Quantitative Analysis: Why Diesel Engines Don't Knock Like SI Engines

### Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | SI (Petrol) Engine | CI (Diesel) Engine |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Compression ratio | 8 to 12 | 14 to 22 |
| Peak cylinder pressure | 30-45 bar | 60-90 bar |
| Peak temperature | ~2000°C | ~2500°C |
| Ignition type | Spark at fixed timing | Compression ignition (auto) |
| Fuel-air mixture | Premixed (homogeneous) | Diffused (heterogeneous) |
| Knocking phenomenon | **Yes — undesirable** | **Different — only in CI** (diesel knock =粗暴) |
| "Knock" in CI | Cold-start rattle (delay period too long) | Different physics |

### Why CI Engines Resist "Petrol-Type" Knock
1. The fuel is injected **after** compression starts, into compressed hot air.
2. There is **no premixed end-gas** of fuel-air — the mixture forms gradually.
3. Combustion is **diffusion-controlled**, not premixed.
4. The fuel ignites upon injection due to high air temperature.
5. Hence, the **end-gas auto-ignition phenomenon** that defines SI knock **cannot occur** in the same way.

> [!NOTE]
> However, CI engines have their own abnormal combustion issue called **"Diesel Knock"** or **"Rough Combustion"**, caused by **long ignition delay** leading to accumulation of fuel that ignites all at once. This is a **different** phenomenon from SI knock, and KTU questions often ask students to distinguish the two.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Effective Flame Travel Time vs. End-Gas Ignition Delay

For knock to **NOT** occur, the **flame travel time** must be less than the **auto-ignition delay** of the end-gas:

$$t_{flame} \leq \tau_{ig}$$

Where:
- $t_{flame} = \dfrac{d_{flame}}{S_f}$ = flame travel time
- $\tau_{ig} = A \cdot p^{-n} \cdot \exp(E/RT)$ = chemical ignition delay (Arrhenius)

If $\tau_{ig} < t_{flame}$, the end-gas auto-ignites **before** the flame arrives → **KNOCKING**.

### Numerical Example (KTU Practice)

> Given: Cylinder bore $D = 80$ mm, spark plug at center, flame speed $S_f = 20$ m/s, end-gas $p = 30$ bar, $T = 800$ K, $E/R = 15000$ K, $n = 1$.

**Step 1:** Maximum flame travel distance (radius to wall):

$$d_{flame} = \dfrac{D}{2} = 40 \text{ mm} = 0.04 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2:** Flame travel time:

$$t_{flame} = \dfrac{0.04}{20} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 2 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3:** Ignition delay (assuming $A = 1 \times 10^{-4}$):

$$\tau_{ig} = 10^{-4} \times (30)^{-1} \times \exp(-15000/800) = 10^{-4} \times 0.0333 \times e^{-18.75}$$

$$e^{-18.75} \approx 7.4 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$\tau_{ig} = 10^{-4} \times 0.0333 \times 7.4 \times 10^{-9} = 2.46 \times 10^{-14} \text{ s}$$

**Step 4:** Comparison:

$$\tau_{ig} \ll t_{flame} \rightarrow \textbf{SEVERE KNOCKING}$$

> Since $\tau_{ig}$ is orders of magnitude smaller than $t_{flame}$, the end-gas will auto-ignite almost instantly. This is a **knocking** condition. The remedy would be to **retard spark**, **enrich mixture (water injection)**, or use a **higher octane fuel**.

---

## 3.5 Engineering Application Matrix

| Real-World Application | Knocking Solution Used |
| :--- | :--- |
| Modern petrol cars (BS-VI) | High octane fuel, knock sensor + ECU retards timing |
| High-performance engines (F1) | Direct injection, cooled EGR, 100+ ON racing fuel |
| Aviation piston engines (Avgas 100LL) | TEL-doped fuel, high compression (10:1) |
| Compressed Natural Gas (CNG) vehicles | CNG has ON ~120, no knock issue |
| Hydrogen-fueled SI engines | ON ~130 but abnormal combustion issues |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Mechanism of Knocking — Cause-Effect Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Spark Initiated at Spark Plug] --> B[Flame Front Propagates Smoothly]
    B --> C{Is End-Gas Above AIT?}
    C -- No --> D[Normal Combustion]
    C -- Yes --> E[End-Gas Auto-Ignites]
    E --> F[Two Flame Fronts Collide]
    F --> G[High-Pressure Shock Waves]
    G --> H[Audible Metallic Knocking]
    H --> I[Potential Engine Damage]
    I --> J1[Piston Crown Erosion]
    I --> J2[Spark Plug Melting]
    I --> J3[Cylinder Head Damage]
    I --> J4[Bearing Failure]

    K[Engine Design Factors] --> C
    L[Fuel Properties ON] --> C
    M[Operating Conditions] --> C

    N[Remedies] --> O[Solve Knocking]
    O --> O1[Use Higher Octane Fuel]
    O --> O2[Retard Spark Timing]
    O --> O3[Lower Compression Ratio]
    O --> O4[Water/Methanol Injection]
    O --> O5[Knock Sensor ECU Control]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Combustion Phases — Normal vs. Knocking

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NORM[Normal Combustion Path]
        N1[Phase 1: Ignition Lag] --> N2[Phase 2: Flame Propagation]
        N2 --> N3[Phase 3: Peak Pressure]
        N3 --> N4[Phase 4: After-burning]
    end

    subgraph KNOCK[Knocking Path]
        K1[Phase 1: Spark Initiation] --> K2[Flame Begins]
        K2 --> K3{End-Gas Auto-Ignites}
        K3 -- Yes --> K4[Violent Pressure Spike]
        K4 --> K5[Knock Damage]
    end

    K2 -.->|interrupts| K3
    N2 -.->|alters| K3
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Knock Control System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crankshaft Position Sensor] --> E[ECU Engine Control Unit]
    B[Knock Sensor Piezoelectric] --> E
    C[MAP Manifold Absolute Pressure] --> E
    D[Coolant Temperature Sensor] --> E

    E --> F{Knock Detected Algorithm}
    F -- Yes --> G1[Retard Ignition Timing]
    F -- Yes --> G2[Enrich Mixture]
    F -- Yes --> G3[Trigger MIL Malfunction Indicator]
    F -- No --> H[Normal Ignition Advance]

    G1 --> I[Ignition Coils]
    G2 --> J[Electronic Fuel Injectors]
    H --> I
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Octane Number Determination Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sample Fuel in CFR Engine] --> B[Record Knock Meter Reading KMR]
    B --> C[Switch to Primary Reference Fuel PRF]
    C --> D[Adjust Iso-Octane Percentage]
    D --> E{KMR Matches Sample Fuel?}
    E -- No --> D
    E -- Yes --> F[Read Iso-Octane Volume Percentage]
    F --> G[Output Octane Number ON]
    G --> H{Test Standard}
    H -- RON 600 RPM --> I[Research Octane Number]
    H -- MON 900 RPM --> J[Motor Octane Number]
    I --> K[Anti-Knock Index AKI = RON plus MON divided by 2]
    J --> K
```
