# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Define knocking in SI engines. How is it different from pre-ignition?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Knocking** is the abnormal combustion phenomenon in SI engines caused by the spontaneous **auto-ignition of the end-gas** (unburned air-fuel mixture ahead of the propagating flame front). This results in a near-instantaneous pressure rise, producing high-frequency pressure waves and the characteristic metallic "knocking" sound, with potential for engine damage.
>
> **Difference from Pre-ignition:**
>
> | Pre-ignition | Knocking (Detonation) |
> | :--- | :--- |
> | Charge ignites **before** spark by hot spots | Charge auto-ignites **after** spark, ahead of flame |
> | Caused by overheated spark plug, carbon, hot valves | Caused by high compression, low octane, high load |
> | Slow, smooth but mistimed | Sudden, violent, with pressure waves |
>
> Pre-ignition often **leads to** knocking, but they are distinct phenomena.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for differentiation table.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Define Octane Number. What is its significance in SI engine fuel selection?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Octane Number (ON)** is the percentage of iso-octane (2,2,4-trimethylpentane) in a mixture of iso-octane and n-heptane that matches the knocking tendency of the test fuel, measured in a **CFR (Cooperative Fuel Research) engine** under standard conditions.
>
> **Significance:**
> 1. **Anti-knock rating** — Higher ON means greater resistance to knock.
> 2. **Engine design freedom** — Higher ON fuels allow **higher compression ratios**, improving thermal efficiency.
> 3. **Fuel grading** — Used to grade commercial fuels (Regular, Premium, Super).
> 4. **Compatibility** — Engine designers match design $r_c$ to the available fuel's ON.
> 5. **Emissions** — Higher ON enables more complete combustion, reducing unburned hydrocarbons.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for significance.)*

---

## 📚 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Note:** KTU 2024 Part B for Module 4 provides an internal choice. **Solve ANY ONE** of the following:

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat p-θ diagram the **phenomenon of knocking** in SI engines. Discuss the role of **end-gas** in its formation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** List and explain the **engine design factors** and **operating condition factors** affecting knocking. How does **retarding spark advance** suppress knock? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Knocking Phenomenon and p-θ Diagram:**

**Step 1 — Normal Combustion p-θ curve:** A normal SI engine p-θ diagram shows:
- A smooth pressure rise starting at spark timing.
- Peak pressure ~12°-15° ATDC.
- Smooth decline during expansion.

**Step 2 — Knocking p-θ curve (describe verbally since diagram is in Section 4):**
- The curve initially follows normal combustion.
- Just before/at TDC, a **sharp inflection or "spike"** appears.
- A second high-frequency oscillation overlays the main curve.
- Peak pressure is **higher** and occurs **earlier** than in normal combustion.

**Step 3 — End-gas and its role:**
- The **end-gas** is the layer of unburned mixture between the **flame front** and the **chamber walls/piston**.
- As the flame advances, the end-gas is:
  - Compressed by the rising piston.
  - Heated by the flame front and hot walls.
- If its temperature exceeds the **auto-ignition temperature (AIT)** before the flame reaches it, it auto-ignites spontaneously.
- This auto-ignition releases energy almost instantaneously, sending a **high-pressure wave** through the chamber.

**Step 4 — Result:**
- Two flame fronts (original + auto-ignited) collide.
- Severe pressure oscillations.
- Audible metallic knock.
- Potential mechanical damage.

**Part (b) — Factors Affecting Knocking:**

**Engine Design Factors:**
1. **Compression ratio** — Higher $r_c$ → higher TDC temperature → more knock. *Most critical factor.*
2. **Combustion chamber shape** — Compact, centralized chambers reduce flame travel distance, hence less knock.
3. **Spark plug location** — Centrally placed reduces maximum flame path length.
4. **Cylinder bore size** — Larger bore → longer flame path → more knock.
5. **Wall temperature** — Hot walls transfer heat to end-gas → more knock.

**Operating Condition Factors:**
1. **Load** — Higher load → higher manifold pressure → more knock.
2. **Engine speed** — Higher RPM → less time for auto-ignition → less knock.
3. **Spark advance** — More advanced → more knock (less burn time).
4. **Inlet air temperature/humidity** — Higher T → more knock; higher humidity → less knock.
5. **Altitude** — Lower atmospheric pressure → less knock.

**Spark Retardation to Suppress Knock:**
- Retarding the spark shifts the entire combustion event to a **larger volume** (later crank angle).
- This lowers the **peak pressure and peak temperature** of combustion.
- End-gas experiences **lower peak temperature** before auto-ignition time window.
- $\Delta T_{knock}$ increases → knock is suppressed.
- **Trade-off:** Slight loss in thermal efficiency and power output.

*(Examiner's note: Diagrams, neat table, and end-gas explanation carry weight. Sketches get 1-2 extra marks.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** What is **Octane Number**? Explain the **CFR engine method** of determining Octane Number. Distinguish between **RON** and **MON**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the various **anti-knock additives** used in SI engine fuels. Why has **TEL (Tetra-Ethyl Lead)** been phased out globally? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Octane Number and CFR Engine Method:**

**Step 1 — Definition:** *(See Part A Q2 above — must be reproduced with key terms.)*

> Octane Number = volume % of iso-octane in PRF (Primary Reference Fuel: iso-octane + n-heptane) that matches the knock intensity of the test fuel in a CFR engine.

**Step 2 — CFR Engine Method:**
- Single-cylinder, variable compression ratio engine.
- Standard operating conditions: 600 RPM (RON) or 900 RPM (MON).
- Test fuel is run, **knock meter reading (KMR)** is recorded at maximum knock.
- Test fuel is replaced with PRF mixtures of varying iso-octane %.
- The PRF % is varied until the **same KMR** is obtained.
- The **iso-octane %** in the matching PRF = ON of the fuel.

**Step 3 — RON vs. MON:**

| Parameter | RON (Research Octane Number) | MON (Motor Octane Number) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Engine speed | 600 RPM | 900 RPM |
| Variable parameter | Compression ratio | Spark advance |
| Inlet air temperature | Ambient (52°C) | Heated (149°C) |
| Severity | Milder conditions | More severe conditions |
| Typical values (premium) | 95-98 | 85-88 |
| Posted at pump (India) | Yes (RON-based) | No |

**Step 4 — Anti-Knock Index (AKI):**
$$AKI = \dfrac{RON + MON}{2}$$
Used as posted octane in the US (e.g., 87, 89, 91).

**Part (b) — Anti-Knock Additives:**

**Common Anti-Knock Additives:**

| Additive | Chemical Formula | Typical ON Boost | Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Tetra-Ethyl Lead (TEL) | $\text{Pb(C}_2\text{H}_5)_4$ | +10 to +15 | Phased out due to toxicity |
| Methyl tert-Butyl Ether (MTBE) | $\text{C}_5\text{H}_{12}\text{O}$ | +7 to +10 | Phasing out (water pollution) |
| Ethanol | $\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}$ | +8 to +10 | Renewable, current favourite |
| Methanol | $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$ | +10 to +12 | Used in racing |
| Tert-Butyl Alcohol (TBA) | $\text{C}_4\text{H}_{10}\text{O}$ | +5 to +8 | Less common |
| MMT | $\text{C}_9\text{H}_7\text{MnO}_3$ | +2 to +3 | Modern metallic additive |
| Ferrocene | $\text{Fe(C}_5\text{H}_5)_2$ | +1 to +3 | Iron-based |

**Why TEL Has Been Phased Out:**

1. **Lead toxicity** — TEL combustion releases lead compounds ($\text{PbO}$, $\text{PbBr}_2$, $\text{PbCl}_2$) that are **highly toxic** to humans, causing neurological damage, especially in children.

2. **Catalytic converter poisoning** — Lead deposits on the surface of catalytic converters (in BS-VI / Euro-VI vehicles) **permanently deactivate** precious metal catalysts (Pt, Pd, Rh). This makes emission control impossible.

3. **Environmental pollution** — Lead accumulates in soil and water, entering the food chain.

4. **Regulatory mandates** — UN, WHO, and most national governments (India: phased out in 2000) have banned leaded petrol.

5. **Health legacy** — Even low-level lead exposure causes IQ reduction in children, kidney disease, hypertension.

**Modern Replacements:**
- Aromatic hydrocarbons (BTEX — Benzene, Toluene, Ethylbenzene, Xylene).
- Oxygenated compounds — **Ethanol, MTBE, ETBE** (Ethyl tert-Butyl Ether).
- **Refining upgrades** — catalytic reforming, isomerization, alkylation to produce high-ON components directly.

*(Total: 14 marks — split evenly between (a) and (b).)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing pre-ignition with detonation** — they are related but distinct phenomena. Always clarify the **timing** (before spark vs. after spark).
> 2. **Forgetting to mention end-gas** — every knocking answer **must** explain the role of end-gas and its auto-ignition. Maximum 1-2 marks lost if omitted.
> 3. **Not distinguishing RON from MON** — a common 2-mark question. Memorize: **R = Research, 600 RPM; M = Motor, 900 RPM**.
> 4. **No p-θ diagram** — examiners award 1-2 marks for a **labeled sketch** showing the difference between normal and knocking pressure curves. Always draw one.
> 5. **Saying TEL is "still in use"** — wrong in 2024 context. Phased out globally except aviation gasoline (Avgas 100LL).
> 6. **Missing units** in numerical problems — flame speed in m/s, pressure in bar, temperature in K or °C.
> 7. **Not explaining the "why"** behind retard-spark — just saying "retard spark" is incomplete. Must explain the thermodynamic reasoning.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Quick Recap — Save for Last-Minute Revision**

### ⚡ Core Concepts
- [x] Knocking = **end-gas auto-ignition** in SI engines, producing a metallic sound and pressure waves.
- [x] Three required conditions: **High T, High P, Sufficient time** ("Knock Triangle").
- [x] Pre-ignition ≠ Detonation (knocking). Pre-ignition is **before spark**; detonation is **after spark, ahead of flame**.

### ⚡ Combustion Phases
- [x] Ignition Lag → Flame Propagation → Rapid Combustion → After-burning.
- [x] Knocking **interrupts Phase 2**.

### ⚡ End-Gas Equation
- [x] $T_{eg} = T_1 \cdot r_{eg}^{\gamma-1}$
- [x] Higher $r_{eg}$ or higher $T_1$ → more knock.

### ⚡ Octane Number
- [x] ON = % iso-octane in PRF matching knock of test fuel.
- [x] iso-octane (ON=100) + n-heptane (ON=0).
- [x] RON at 600 RPM; MON at 900 RPM.
- [x] AKI = (RON + MON)/2.

### ⚡ Anti-Knock Methods
- [x] Use higher octane fuel.
- [x] Retard spark timing.
- [x] Lower compression ratio.
- [x] Water/methanol injection.
- [x] Knock sensor + ECU feedback (modern cars).
- [x] EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation).

### ⚡ Anti-Knock Additives
- [x] TEL (phased out) → Ethanol / MTBE / MMT (modern).
- [x] **Ethanol** is the current leading renewable oxygenate additive.

### ⚡ CI vs. SI Knocking
- [x] SI knock = end-gas auto-ignition (unwanted).
- [x] CI "knock" = diesel knock from **long ignition delay** accumulating fuel (different physics).
- [x] CI engines **resist** SI-type knock due to **non-premixed** combustion.

### ⚡ Quick Numerical Reminders
- [x] $S_f$ (flame speed) = 15-25 m/s under normal conditions.
- [x] $dp/d\theta \geq 10$ bar/° indicates knock.
- [x] Cylinder pressure in SI: 30-45 bar; in CI: 60-90 bar.

### ⚡ Examiner's Magic Words (use these in answers!)
- [x] "End-gas" + "auto-ignition temperature (AIT)" + "flame front" + "shock waves" = full marks.

---

*End of Topic: Knocking in Engine — Module 4, PCIET303 — Thermal Engineering, KTU 2024 Scheme.*
