> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET303 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Alternate Fuels in IC Engines: Biodiesel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Biodiesel** is defined as a mono-alkyl ester of long-chain fatty acids derived from renewable biological feedstocks such as vegetable oils (edible and non-edible), animal fats, used cooking oil, and microalgae lipids. It is produced through a catalyzed chemical reaction known as **transesterification**, in which triglycerides (the primary constituents of oils/fats) react with a short-chain alcohol (typically **methanol** or **ethanol**) in the presence of a catalyst (acid, alkali, or enzymatic) to yield **Fatty Acid Methyl Esters (FAME)** and **glycerol** as a by-product.

According to the **American Society for Testing and Materials (ASTM D6751)** and the **European Standard EN 14214**, biodiesel must meet stringent fuel-quality specifications to be used as a pure fuel (B100) or as a blend with petroleum diesel (e.g., B20 = 20% biodiesel + 80% petro-diesel).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Under the topic "Alternate Fuels in IC Engines," biodiesel is studied as a drop-in replacement for fossil diesel in compression ignition (CI) engines. Students must understand production chemistry, physico-chemical properties, performance, and emission characteristics.

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine you have a thick, syrupy cooking oil (like coconut or sunflower oil) and you want your car engine to run on it. If you pour it directly, the oil is too viscous (sticky) — like trying to drink a milkshake through a coffee stirrer. The engine cannot atomize it properly, leading to poor combustion and carbon build-up.

The **transesterification** process is essentially a "molecular tailoring" exercise: the long, bulky triglyceride molecules are chopped into three smaller, lighter **methyl ester** molecules (the biodiesel), while releasing **glycerin** (a useful by-product used in soaps, cosmetics, and pharmaceuticals). The new biodiesel behaves almost exactly like petroleum diesel, fitting the engine's combustion chamber like a glove.

Think of it as converting a **3-seater sofa** (triglyceride) into **3 individual chairs** (methyl esters) — same material, but now each piece is light, portable, and ergonomic for the engine.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Cetane Number of Biodiesel:** Typically **45 – 65** (higher than petro-diesel: 40–55)
> - **Density:** **860 – 900 kg/m³** at 15 °C
> - **Kinematic Viscosity (40 °C):** **3.5 – 5.0 mm²/s** (closer to diesel than raw oil)
> - **Lower Heating Value (LHV):** **37 – 40 MJ/kg** (vs. ~42–45 MJ/kg for petro-diesel)
> - **Flash Point:** **> 130 °C** (safer to handle than diesel)
> - **Oxygen Content:** **10 – 12 % by mass** (built-in oxidizer for cleaner combustion)
> - **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR):** **≈ 12.5 : 1** by mass (lower than diesel's 14.7:1)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Chemistry of Biodiesel — Transesterification

The transesterification (also called alcoholysis) reaction proceeds in three reversible steps:

**Step 1:** Triglyceride + Methanol → Diglyceride + FAME  
**Step 2:** Diglyceride + Methanol → Monoglyceride + FAME  
**Step 3:** Monoglyceride + Methanol → Glycerol + FAME

Overall Reaction:

$$\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightleftharpoons{\text{Catalyst}} 3\,\text{RCOOCH}_3 + \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3$$

- **Reactants:** 1 mole triglyceride + 3 moles methanol
- **Products:** 3 moles Fatty Acid Methyl Ester (FAME) + 1 mole glycerol

**Why Methanol?**
- Lowest molecular weight alcohol → highest molar excess achievable
- Cheap and easily available
- Produces FAME (not FAEE), which has slightly better cold-flow properties and lower viscosity

**Why a Catalyst?**
The reaction is **slow at room temperature**. Catalysts accelerate it without being consumed:
- **Homogeneous Alkali (NaOH, KOH):** Fastest, used when free-fatty-acid (FFA) content is **< 2%**.
- **Homogeneous Acid (H₂SO₄, H₃PO₄):** Slower, used for high-FFA feedstocks via **esterification** (pretreatment).
- **Heterogeneous / Enzymatic (lipase):** Greener, reusable, but expensive.

### 2.2 Process Flow (Batch / Continuous)

1. **Feedstock Pretreatment:** Degumming, dehydration, FFA reduction (<2%).
2. **Reaction Stage:** Oil + methanol + catalyst in stirred reactor at **60–65 °C** for **1–2 hours** (molar ratio 6:1 alcohol:oil).
3. **Phase Separation:** Gravity settling separates **biodiesel (top layer)** from **glycerol (bottom layer)**.
4. **Purification:** Water washing → drying → vacuum distillation to remove residual methanol and soap.

### 2.3 Why Biodiesel Works in a CI Engine (The "How")

- **Cetane Number** is high → shorter ignition delay → smoother cold-start.
- **Oxygen content** enables **leaner, more complete combustion**, reducing CO and unburnt HC.
- **No sulfur** → eliminates SO₂ emissions and prolongs lubricating oil life.
- **High flash point** → safer storage and transport.

### 2.4 Drawbacks (Critical for KTU Answers)

- **Lower calorific value** → ~8–10% power drop in B100 operation.
- **Higher viscosity at low temperature** → cold-flow issues (crystallization in cold climates).
- **Oxidation stability** lower than petro-diesel → degrades over time.
- **NOₓ emissions slightly increase** due to higher in-cylinder temperatures from oxygen content.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula / Definition | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Molar ratio | $R = n_{\text{alcohol}} / n_{\text{oil}}$ | dimensionless | Typically 6 : 1 |
| 2 | Theoretical yield (mass) | $Y = \dfrac{3\,M_{\text{FAME}}}{M_{\text{TG}} + 3\,M_{\text{MeOH}} - M_{\text{glycerol}}}$ | kg/kg | Practical yield ≈ 95–98% |
| 3 | Energy density ratio | $\eta_E = \dfrac{\text{LHV}_{\text{bio}}}{\text{LHV}_{\text{diesel}}}$ | dimensionless | ≈ 0.88–0.92 |
| 4 | Specific fuel consumption | $\text{SFC} = \dfrac{m_f}{W_{\text{brake}}}$ | kg/kWh | Increases with biodiesel % |
| 5 | Brake thermal efficiency | $\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{W_b}{m_f \cdot \text{LHV}}$ | dimensionless | Slightly lower for B100 |
| 6 | AFR (stoichiometric) | $\text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{air}}}{m_{\text{fuel}}}$ | kg/kg | Biodiesel ≈ 12.5 |
| 7 | Blend notation | $B_x$ → $x$ % biodiesel + $(100-x)$ % diesel | — | E.g., B20, B100 |
| 8 | Cetane number (CN) | Empirical measure of ignition delay | dimensionless | Higher = shorter delay |
| 9 | Kinematic viscosity | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ | m²/s (or cSt) | 1 cSt = 1 mm²/s |
| 10 | Flash point | Lowest T at which vapor ignites | °C | > 130 °C for biodiesel |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always mention **ASTM D6751** (USA) and **EN 14214** (Europe) when discussing biodiesel standards. Examiners reward these tags.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Mass Yield of Biodiesel from a Triglyceride

Let us derive the theoretical mass yield of FAME from **triolein (C₅₇H₁₀₄O₆)**, a representative triglyceride of oleic acid, using methanol.

**Step 1 — Identify Molecular Masses:**

- Triolein: $M_{\text{TG}} = 57(12) + 104(1) + 6(16) = 684 + 104 + 96 = \mathbf{884 \text{ g/mol}}$
- Methanol: $M_{\text{MeOH}} = 12 + 4(1) + 16 = \mathbf{32 \text{ g/mol}}$
- Methyl Oleate (FAME): $M_{\text{FAME}} = 19(12) + 36(1) + 2(16) = 228 + 36 + 32 = \mathbf{296 \text{ g/mol}}$
- Glycerol: $M_{\text{glycerol}} = 3(12) + 8(1) + 3(16) = 36 + 8 + 48 = \mathbf{92 \text{ g/mol}}$

**Step 2 — Apply the Stoichiometric Mass Balance:**

From the balanced equation:

$$\text{TG} + 3\,\text{MeOH} \rightarrow 3\,\text{FAME} + \text{Glycerol}$$

The **limiting reactant** is the triglyceride. Per mole of TG:

$$\text{Mass of FAME produced} = 3 \times 296 = 888 \text{ g}$$

$$\text{Mass of reactants} = 884 + 3(32) = 884 + 96 = 980 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Theoretical Mass Yield:**

$$Y_{\text{theoretical}} = \frac{\text{Mass of FAME}}{\text{Mass of TG (reacted)}} \times 100\%$$

$$Y_{\text{theoretical}} = \frac{888}{884} \times 100\% = \mathbf{100.45\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The theoretical yield is **slightly above 100%** because the methoxy (–OCH₃) groups added to the glycerol backbone weigh more than the glycerol itself. This is the **"mass gain" effect of transesterification**.

**Step 4 — Actual (Practical) Yield:**

$$Y_{\text{actual}} = \eta_{\text{reaction}} \times Y_{\text{theoretical}} \approx 0.97 \times 100.45\% \approx \mathbf{97.4\%}$$

where $\eta_{\text{reaction}}$ accounts for reversible reaction losses, soap formation, and unreacted TG.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Energy Content Comparison and Equivalent Power

A CI engine develops rated brake power $W_b$ on petro-diesel. If switched to B20 (20% biodiesel, 80% diesel by volume), find the **percentage change in SFC** assuming thermal efficiency is constant.

**Given:**

- LHV of petro-diesel = **43 MJ/kg**
- LHV of biodiesel (methyl oleate) = **39.5 MJ/kg**
- Blend B20 (volume basis, density approx. equal to 850 kg/m³ for both)
- $\eta_{bth} = 0.35$ (assumed constant)

**Step 1 — Effective LHV of B20 blend (volume basis, mass-weighted):**

For nearly equal densities, mass fraction ≈ volume fraction:

$$\text{LHV}_{\text{B20}} = 0.20 \times 39.5 + 0.80 \times 43$$

$$\text{LHV}_{\text{B20}} = 7.9 + 34.4 = \mathbf{42.3 \text{ MJ/kg}}$$

**Step 2 — Brake Power for Same Operating Conditions:**

$$W_b = \eta_{bth} \cdot m_f \cdot \text{LHV}$$

Since $W_b$ is fixed and $\eta_{bth}$ is constant:

$$m_{f,\text{B20}} \cdot \text{LHV}_{\text{B20}} = m_{f,\text{diesel}} \cdot \text{LHV}_{\text{diesel}}$$

$$\frac{m_{f,\text{B20}}}{m_{f,\text{diesel}}} = \frac{\text{LHV}_{\text{diesel}}}{\text{LHV}_{\text{B20}}} = \frac{43}{42.3} = 1.0165$$

**Step 3 — Percentage Increase in SFC:**

$$\Delta \text{SFC} \% = (1.0165 - 1) \times 100\% = \mathbf{+1.65\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Switching from petro-diesel to B20 increases specific fuel consumption by ~1.65% (per kg basis). For B100, this rise approaches **~8–9%** because the LHV gap widens.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Biodiesel vs. Petro-Diesel

| Property | Petro-Diesel | Biodiesel (B100) | Effect on Engine |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cetane Number | 40 – 55 | 45 – 65 | Better cold-start |
| LHV (MJ/kg) | 42 – 45 | 37 – 40 | Higher SFC |
| Viscosity (mm²/s @ 40 °C) | 2.0 – 4.5 | 3.5 – 5.0 | Acceptable injection |
| Density (kg/m³) | 820 – 860 | 860 – 900 | Slightly heavier charge |
| Flash Point (°C) | 55 – 65 | > 130 | Safer handling |
| Sulfur (mass %) | < 0.001 | ≈ 0 | No SOₓ |
| Oxygen (mass %) | 0 | 10 – 12 | Cleaner combustion |
| CO Emissions | Baseline | Reduced 30–50% | ✓ Benefit |
| HC Emissions | Baseline | Reduced 30–50% | ✓ Benefit |
| NOₓ Emissions | Baseline | Increased 5–15% | ✗ Penalty |
| CO₂ (well-to-wheel) | Baseline | ~60% lower | ✓ Benefit |
| Lubricity | Good | Excellent | ✓ Benefit |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Biodiesel Production (Transesterification)

Below is a functional block-level architecture for the industrial batch production of biodiesel, covering feedstock handling through final purification.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[feedstockOil] --> B[pretreatmentUnit]
    B --> B1[degummingFFAcheck]
    B1 --> C[reactorVessel]
    M[methanolStorage] --> C
    CAT[catalystNaOHKOH] --> C
    C --> D[stirredReactor60to65C]
    D --> E[settlingTank1hour]
    E --> F[biodieselLayerTop]
    E --> G[glycerolLayerBottom]
    F --> H[waterWashStage]
    H --> I[dryingVacuum]
    I --> J[finalBiodieselB100]
    G --> K[glycerolPurification]
    K --> L[pharmaGradeGlycerol]
    J --> P[blendingUnit]
    D1[dieselStorage] --> P
    P --> Q[B20BlendOutlet]
    P --> R[B100Outlet]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Feedstock oil (edible / non-edible / used cooking oil) enters the **pretreatment unit** where degumming (phosphatide removal) and FFA neutralisation occur.
- Pre-treated oil, methanol, and alkali catalyst enter the **reactor vessel** held at **60–65 °C**.
- After ~1 hour of reaction, the mixture flows to a **settling tank** where gravity separates the lighter **FAME (biodiesel)** layer from the heavier **glycerol** layer.
- Biodiesel undergoes **water washing** to remove residual soap/catalyst, then **vacuum drying** to remove water and trace methanol.
- Glycerol is purified as a saleable by-product.
- Pure biodiesel is then routed to a **blending unit** to produce **B20, B50, or B100** as per market demand.

---

### 4.2 Functional Engine-Performance Impact Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[biodieselProperties] --> B1[lowerLHV]
    A --> B2[higherCetane]
    A --> B3[builtInOxygen]
    A --> B4[higherViscosity]
    A --> B5[noSulfur]
    B1 --> C1[higherSFC]
    B1 --> C2[slightPowerDrop]
    B2 --> D1[shorterIgnitionDelay]
    B2 --> D2[smootherCombustion]
    B3 --> E1[reducedCO]
    B3 --> E2[reducedHC]
    B3 --> E3[increasedNOx]
    B4 --> F1[injectionTimingAdjustment]
    B5 --> G1[zeroSOx]
    B5 --> G2[betterLubricity]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Each **biodiesel property** (left column) cascades into a distinct engine-performance or emission consequence (right column).
- For example, the **lower LHV** → higher SFC + slight power drop; **built-in oxygen** → reduced CO/HC but increased NOₓ.

---

### 4.3 Schematic of In-Cylinder Combustion Differences (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CI_diesel
    D1[injectDiesel] --> D2[noOxygenInFuel]
    D2 --> D3[longIgnitionDelay]
    D3 --> D4[premixedBurnPhase]
    D4 --> D5[diffusionBurnPhase]
    end
    subgraph CI_biodiesel
    B1[injectB100] --> B2[oxygenInFuel10to12pct]
    B2 --> B3[shortIgnitionDelay]
    B3 --> B4[dominantDiffusionBurn]
    B4 --> B5[completeCombustion]
    end
```

This block architecture highlights why biodiesel combusts more completely: the chemically-bound oxygen allows fuel molecules to find oxygen partners faster, reducing soot and unburnt hydrocarbons.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
Define **biodiesel**. What are the major feedstocks used for its production?

**Model Answer:**

Biodiesel is a mono-alkyl ester of long-chain fatty acids (FAME) produced by the **transesterification** of vegetable oils, animal fats, used cooking oils, or microalgae lipids with a short-chain alcohol (usually methanol) in the presence of a catalyst.

Major feedstocks:
- **Edible oils:** Soybean, sunflower, rapeseed, palm, coconut.
- **Non-edible oils:** Jatropha, karanja, mahua, neem, pongamia.
- **Waste / by-products:** Used cooking oil (UCO), animal tallow, yellow grease.
- **Algal oils:** Microalgae lipids (next-generation feedstock).

**(3 marks — Definition 1 mark, Feedstocks 2 marks.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
List **four** advantages and **two** disadvantages of using biodiesel in CI engines.

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**
1. Renewable and biodegradable.
2. Higher cetane number → smoother combustion and cold-start.
3. Lower CO, HC, and particulate emissions; no SO₂.
4. Excellent lubricity → reduces engine wear.

**Disadvantages:**
1. Lower calorific value → higher SFC and slight power loss.
2. Tendency to oxidise and form gums during long storage.

**(3 marks — 4 advantages (2 marks) + 2 disadvantages (1 mark).)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length ESE Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the catalyst** in the transesterification reaction costs 1 mark — always state "in the presence of an alkali/acid catalyst."
> 2. **Mixing up CN values** (biodiesel vs. diesel) is a common error — memorise the ranges: diesel 40–55, biodiesel 45–65.
> 3. **Wrong stoichiometric ratio** — by default, the reaction uses **3 moles methanol per mole TG**, but **practically 6:1** is used to drive the equilibrium.
> 4. Do **not** claim biodiesel is "emission-free" — NOₓ increases; examiners deduct marks for over-generalised statements.
> 5. Always mention **standards** (ASTM D6751 / EN 14214) where applicable.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Comprehensive

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **transesterification process** for biodiesel production in detail. Include the chemical equation, role of the catalyst, and the operating conditions.

**(b) [7 marks)** Compare the **physico-chemical properties and emission behaviour** of biodiesel with petroleum diesel. Discuss why biodiesel causes slightly higher NOₓ emissions.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Transesterification Process:**

Transesterification is the chemical reaction in which the **alkyl group** of an ester (triglyceride) is exchanged with the alkyl group of an alcohol (methanol), producing a new ester (FAME) and a new alcohol (glycerol).

$$\text{TG} + 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightleftharpoons{\text{NaOH/KOH}} 3\,\text{RCOOCH}_3 + \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3$$

- **Catalyst role:** Alkali catalyst (NaOH or KOH, 0.5–1% by mass) accelerates the reaction by deprotonating methanol to form methoxide ion, which is the active nucleophile.
- **Molar ratio:** 6:1 (methanol to oil) — excess methanol drives equilibrium forward.
- **Temperature:** 60–65 °C (just below methanol boiling point of 64.7 °C).
- **Reaction time:** 1–2 hours with continuous stirring at 600–900 rpm.
- **By-products:** Glycerol (~10% by mass), residual soap (if FFA is high), unreacted methanol.

**Process flow (4 steps):**
1. Pretreatment (FFA < 2%).
2. Reaction in stirred reactor.
3. Phase separation (1 hour settling).
4. Purification (water wash + drying).

**(b) Property and Emission Comparison:**

| Property | Diesel | Biodiesel | Engine Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| CN | 40–55 | 45–65 | Shorter delay, smoother |
| LHV (MJ/kg) | 43 | 39 | +8% SFC |
| Viscosity (cSt) | 2.5 | 4.5 | Acceptable |
| Oxygen (%) | 0 | 11 | Cleaner combustion |
| Sulfur | Yes | No | No SOₓ |

**Emission Trends:**
- **CO ↓** 30–50% (more complete oxidation).
- **HC ↓** 30–50% (oxygen-aided burn).
- **PM ↓** 40–60% (no aromatic hydrocarbons).
- **NOₓ ↑** 5–15% due to:
  - Higher in-cylinder peak temperatures from oxygen content.
  - Advanced combustion phasing (shorter ignition delay → combustion starts earlier in the power stroke).
  - Higher adiabatic flame temperature of FAME (~1900 K vs. ~1800 K for diesel).

**(7 + 7 = 14 marks. Properly written equations, comparison table, and NOₓ reasoning fetch full marks.)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 marks]** A CI engine rated at **10 kW** brake power at 1500 rpm operates on **B20 (20% biodiesel + 80% diesel by volume)**. Given LHV of diesel = 43 MJ/kg, LHV of biodiesel = 39.5 MJ/kg, brake thermal efficiency = **32%**, and density = 850 kg/m³ for both fuels, calculate the **hourly fuel consumption (kg/h)** for B20 operation and the **percentage increase in SFC** compared to neat diesel.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the **environmental and economic significance** of biodiesel as an alternate fuel, highlighting its role in **carbon neutrality** and the **rural economy**.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Effective LHV of B20 (mass-weighted, since densities are equal):**

$$\text{LHV}_{\text{B20}} = 0.20 \times 39.5 + 0.80 \times 43 = 7.9 + 34.4 = 42.3 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Brake Power = 10 kW → Energy Output per Hour:**

$$W_b = 10 \text{ kW} = 10 \text{ kJ/s} = 10 \times 3600 = 36{,}000 \text{ kJ/h} = 36 \text{ MJ/h}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Brake Thermal Efficiency Formula:**

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{W_b}{m_f \cdot \text{LHV}}$$

$$0.32 = \frac{36}{m_f \times 42.3}$$

$$m_f = \frac{36}{0.32 \times 42.3} = \frac{36}{13.536} = \mathbf{2.66 \text{ kg/h}}$$

**Step 4 — SFC for Neat Diesel (Reference):**

$$m_{f,\text{diesel}} = \frac{36}{0.32 \times 43} = \frac{36}{13.76} = 2.616 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 5 — Percentage Increase in SFC:**

$$\Delta \text{SFC} = \frac{2.66 - 2.616}{2.616} \times 100\% = \frac{0.044}{2.616} \times 100\% = \mathbf{1.68\%}$$

**Answer:** B20 fuel consumption = **2.66 kg/h**; SFC increase = **~1.68%**.

---

**(b) Environmental and Economic Significance:**

**Environmental Significance:**

- **Carbon Neutrality:** The CO₂ released during biodiesel combustion is **re-absorbed** by the next oilseed crop through photosynthesis, closing the carbon cycle. Net well-to-wheel CO₂ reduction is ~60% compared to fossil diesel.
- **Lower Toxic Emissions:** Reduced CO, HC, PM, and zero SO₂ → better urban air quality, reduced respiratory diseases.
- **Biodegradability:** Spilled biodiesel degrades ~4× faster than petro-diesel → safer for soil and water bodies.
- **No Sulfur / No Aromatics:** Eliminates acid rain precursors and carcinogenic PAH emissions.

**Economic Significance:**

- **Rural Employment:** Cultivation of oilseeds (jatropha, karanja) on **wastelands** creates income for marginal farmers.
- **Energy Security:** Reduces dependence on imported crude oil, conserving foreign exchange.
- **By-product Revenue:** Glycerol sales (pharma, cosmetics) improve the economics of the process.
- **Decentralised Production:** Small-scale biodiesel plants can serve rural cooperatives, reducing logistics costs.

**Challenges:** Feedstock cost volatility, food-vs-fuel debate, and cold-flow issues at low ambient temperatures.

**(7 + 7 = 14 marks — Numerical (7 marks) + Discussion (7 marks).)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick-Recall Bullet Box (Save for Last-Minute Revision):**

- **Biodiesel** = **FAME** (Fatty Acid Methyl Ester) made from **triglycerides + methanol** via **transesterification**.
- Reaction needs a **catalyst** (NaOH/KOH for low-FFA oils; H₂SO₄ for high-FFA).
- **Stoichiometric ratio:** 1 mole TG : 3 moles MeOH. **Practical ratio:** 6:1 (excess alcohol).
- **Reactor conditions:** 60–65 °C, 1 atm, 1–2 hours, stirred.
- **Biodiesel CN (45–65)** is **higher** than diesel (40–55) → better cold-start.
- **LHV ≈ 39 MJ/kg**, ~**8–10% lower** than diesel → higher SFC.
- **Density** 860–900 kg/m³, **Viscosity** 3.5–5.0 cSt at 40 °C.
- **Flash point > 130 °C** → much safer than petro-diesel.
- **Oxygen content 10–12%** → reduces **CO, HC, PM**, but increases **NOₓ**.
- **Zero sulfur** → no SO₂, no sulfuric acid corrosion, longer lube-oil life.
- **Standards:** **ASTM D6751** (USA), **EN 14214** (Europe) — always cite in KTU answers.
- **Notation:** B20 = 20% biodiesel + 80% diesel; B100 = pure biodiesel.
- **Major Indian feedstocks:** Jatropha, karanja, mahua, neem, used cooking oil.
- **By-product glycerol** is sold to pharma/cosmetic industries → improves plant economics.
- **Power drop with B100:** ~5–8% due to lower LHV (countered by turbocharging or **EGR**).
- **Cold-flow problem:** Biodiesel crystallises at low T → use **winterization** or **additives**.
- **Environmental gain:** ~60% lifecycle CO₂ reduction → supports **carbon-credit markets**.
- **Soap formation:** FFA + NaOH → soap (saponification) → reduces yield; hence FFA must be **< 2%**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Strategy Tip:** Always begin any 14-mark answer on biodiesel with a **definition + reaction equation**, then proceed to **properties → engine performance → emissions → standards**. This structure mirrors KTU's marking scheme and guarantees high partial credit even if you miss the last sub-part.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrogen
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIET303 - THERMAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Hydrogen ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition **Hydrogen (H₂)** is the **lightest, simplest, and most abundant chemical element** in the universe. In the context of Thermal Engineering, hydrogen is studied as a **secondary energy carrier** (not a primary energy source) that can be produced from diverse feedstocks and used as a clean, carbon-free fuel for power generation, heating, and propulsion applications. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Hydrogen is a clean-burning fuel that produces only water (H₂O) as a combustion byproduct. Its energy content per unit mass is approximately **142 MJ/kg (Higher Heating Value)**, which is nearly **2.75 times** that of gasoline. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Think of Hydrogen as "Electricity's Twin Brother"** > > Imagine electricity flowing through wires — it is *generated* at a power plant, *transmitted* over distances, and *used* in your home to power appliances. You never "mine" electricity from the ground. **Hydrogen works exactly the same way:** > - It is **manufactured** (not mined) using processes like electrolysis or steam reforming. > - It is **stored and transported** like a battery charge. > - It is **consumed** in fuel cells, engines, or boilers to release energy on demand. > - The only exhaust is **pure water vapor**. This is why engineers call hydrogen an **"energy vector"** or **"energy currency"** — it carries energy from where it is produced to where it is needed, just like electricity or gasoline. ### 1.3 Position of Hydrogen in the Energy Spectrum Hydrogen is unique because: - It is **carbon-free** (no CO₂ emissions when burned or used in fuel cells). - It has the **highest energy-to-mass ratio** of any fuel. - It can be produced from **renewable sources** (solar, wind, biomass). - It can power **fuel cells** with efficiency of 50–60% (vs. 25–35% for IC engines). ### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Atomic Number | **1** | | Molecular Weight | **2.016 g/mol** | | Density (at STP) | **0.0899 kg/m³** | | Boiling Point | **−252.87 °C (20.28 K)** | | Higher Heating Value (HHV) | **142 MJ/kg** | | Lower Heating Value (LHV) | **120 MJ/kg** | | Flame Temperature in Air | **~2045 °C** | | Flammability Range in Air | **4% to 75% by volume** | | Auto-ignition Temperature | **500 °C** | > [!NOTE] > The **extremely wide flammability range (4–75%)** is both an advantage (easy to ignite) and a serious safety hazard (leaks ignite easily), which is why hydrogen systems require meticulous sealing and detection. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why Hydrogen? — The Engineering Motivation The global push for **decarbonization** has made hydrogen central to Thermal Engineering for three main reasons: 1. **Zero Carbon Emissions:** Combustion of H₂ produces only H₂O. 2. **Versatility:** Can be used in IC engines, gas turbines, fuel cells, boilers, and rockets. 3. **Storage of Renewables:** Excess solar/wind energy can be stored as hydrogen via electrolysis (Power-to-Gas concept). ### 2.2 Thermodynamic Classification of Hydrogen by Production Source Hydrogen is color-coded based on how it is produced (this is a **very high-yield KTU topic**): | Color | Source | CO₂ Emissions | Cost | |---|---|---|---| | **Grey Hydrogen** | Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) | High (~10 kg CO₂/kg H₂) | Low | | **Brown Hydrogen** | Coal Gasification | Very High (~20 kg CO₂/kg H₂) | Low | | **Blue Hydrogen** | SMR + Carbon Capture & Storage (CCS) | Low (captured) | Medium | | **Green Hydrogen** | Electrolysis powered by Renewables | **Zero** | High (currently) | | **Pink Hydrogen** | Electrolysis powered by Nuclear | Zero | Medium | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Frequently Asked:** *"Differentiate between Green, Blue, and Grey hydrogen."* — Memorize the color-coding table above. ### 2.3 Hydrogen Production Methods #### 2.3.1 Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) — *Most common method (~95% of global production)* Two-step catalytic reaction: **Step 1 — Reforming (Endothermic):** $$CH_4 + H_2O \xrightarrow{700-900°C, Ni \text{ catalyst}} CO + 3H_2$$ **Step 2 — Water-Gas Shift Reaction (Exothermic):** $$CO + H_2O \xrightarrow{330°C, Fe/Cu \text{ catalyst}} CO_2 + H_2$$ Overall: $$CH_4 + 2H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + 4H_2$$ #### 2.3.2 Electrolysis of Water $$2H_2O_{(l)} \xrightarrow{Electrical \text{ Energy}} 2H_{2(g)} + O_{2(g)}$$ - **Standard electrode potential:** 1.229 V - **Theoretical minimum energy:** 39.4 kWh/kg H₂ - **Practical energy consumption:** 50–55 kWh/kg H₂ (due to overpotentials) - Types: **Alkaline, PEM (Proton Exchange Membrane), Solid Oxide** #### 2.3.3 Other Methods - **Partial Oxidation of Hydrocarbons** - **Biomass Gasification** - **Photoelectrochemical Water Splitting** (research stage) - **Thermochemical Cycles** (e.g., Sulfur-Iodine cycle for nuclear-driven H₂) ### 2.4 Hydrogen Storage Methods This is one of the **toughest engineering challenges** because H₂ has very low density. | Method | Description | Energy Density | |---|---|---| | **Compressed Gas** | 350–700 bar cylinders | Low volumetric | | **Liquefied H₂ (LH₂)** | Cooled to −253 °C | Better (70.8 kg/m³) | | **Metal Hydrides** | Absorbed in alloys (e.g., LaNi₅H₆) | Safe, slow release | | **Carbon Nanotubes / MOFs** | Adsorbed in nano-porous materials | Research stage | | **Liquid Organic Carriers (LOHC)** | Chemically bonded to toluene, etc. | Easy transport | ### 2.5 Combustion of Hydrogen **Reaction with Oxygen (Stoichiometric):** $$2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O + \text{Heat}$$ - **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (by mass):** 34.3 : 1 - **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (by volume):** 2.38 : 1 > [!WARNING] > Unlike hydrocarbons, hydrogen requires **NO carbon for combustion**, hence no CO or CO₂ is produced. However, **high NOx** can form due to the high adiabatic flame temperature (~2045 °C in air). ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL FORMULAS — Memorize for KTU University Exam** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $HHV_{H_2} = 142 \text{ MJ/kg}$ | Higher Heating Value of H₂ | MJ/kg | | 2 | $LHV_{H_2} = 120 \text{ MJ/kg}$ | Lower Heating Value of H₂ | MJ/kg | | 3 | $\eta_{HHV/LHV} = \frac{LHV}{HHV} = 0.845$ | Ratio of calorific values | — | | 4 | $E_{elec} = \frac{V \cdot I \cdot t}{m_{H_2}}$ | Electrical energy per kg H₂ | kWh/kg | | 5 | $\eta_{electrolyzer} = \frac{39.4}{E_{actual}} \times 100\%$ | Electrolyzer efficiency | % | | 6 | $AFR_{stoich,mass} = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{H_2}} = 34.3$ | Mass-based stoichiometric AFR | — | | 7 | $V_{H_2}(STP) = \frac{m}{\rho} = \frac{m}{0.0899}$ | Volume of H₂ at STP | m³ | | 8 | $Q = m \cdot CV$ | Total heat released | kJ | | 9 | $\dot{W}_{H_2} = \dot{m} \cdot LHV \cdot \eta_{th}$ | Power output from H₂ combustion | kW | | 10 | $P \cdot V = n \cdot R_u \cdot T$ | Ideal gas law for H₂ | — | ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **ISRO & NASA** use liquid hydrogen as rocket fuel (with liquid oxygen as oxidizer). - **Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo** — hydrogen fuel cell vehicles (FCEVs). - **Steel Industry** — "Green steel" using H₂ instead of coke to reduce iron ore. - **Ammonia Production** — Haber-Bosch process consumes ~70% of global H₂. - **Refineries** — hydrocracking and desulfurization use massive quantities of H₂. - **Grid-scale energy storage** — surplus renewable electricity → H₂ → re-electrified via fuel cells. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air Requirement for Hydrogen Combustion **Combustion Equation:** $$2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O$$ **Step 1 — Mass of reactants per kmol of fuel:** For **1 kmol of H₂** burned: $$2 \text{ kmol } H_2 + 1 \text{ kmol } O_2 \rightarrow 2 \text{ kmol } H_2O$$ Therefore, per **1 kmol of H₂**: 0.5 kmol O₂ is required. **Step 2 — Molecular weights:** $$M_{H_2} = 2.016 \text{ kg/kmol}, \quad M_{O_2} = 32 \text{ kg/kmol}$$ **Step 3 — Mass of oxygen required per kg of hydrogen:** $$m_{O_2} = \frac{0.5 \times 32}{1 \times 2.016} = \frac{16}{2.016} = 7.937 \text{ kg } O_2 / \text{kg } H_2$$ **Step 4 — Convert to air using the mass ratio of O₂ in air (23.2% by mass):** $$m_{air} = \frac{m_{O_2}}{0.232} = \frac{7.937}{0.232}$$ $$\boxed{m_{air} = 34.21 \text{ kg air / kg } H_2}$$ This is the famous **stoichiometric air-fuel ratio for hydrogen = 34.3 : 1** (by mass). > [!NOTE] > For comparison: gasoline has AFR ≈ 14.7, methane ≈ 17.2. Hydrogen needs **more than double the air per kg** because it contains no carbon. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Theoretical Minimum Energy for Water Electrolysis **Electrolysis Reaction:** $$2H_2O_{(l)} \rightarrow 2H_2 + O_2$$ **Step 1 — Enthalpy of formation of liquid water:** $$\Delta H_f^\circ (H_2O, l) = -285.83 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ **Step 2 — Gibbs Free Energy of formation (this is the *minimum electrical work* required):** $$\Delta G_f^\circ (H_2O, l) = -237.13 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ **Step 3 — Per kg of H₂ produced, convert using M = 2.016 kg/kmol:** For 1 kmol H₂ produced, 1 kmol H₂O is consumed. $$W_{min,electrical} = \frac{\Delta G_f^\circ \times 1000}{M_{H_2}} = \frac{237.13 \times 1000}{2.016}$$ $$\boxed{W_{min,electrical} = 117,625 \text{ kJ/kg} = 32.67 \text{ kWh/kg } H_2}$$ **Step 4 — Total (Thermodynamic) Minimum Energy from Enthalpy:** $$W_{min,total} = \frac{\Delta H_f^\circ \times 1000}{M_{H_2}} = \frac{285.83 \times 1000}{2.016}$$ $$\boxed{W_{min,total} = 141,781 \text{ kJ/kg} = 39.38 \text{ kWh/kg } H_2}$$ **Step 5 — Electrolyzer Efficiency:** $$\eta_{electrolyzer} = \frac{W_{min,electrical}}{W_{actual}} = \frac{32.67}{W_{actual}} \times 100\%$$ A typical PEM electrolyzer operates at 70–80% efficiency (HHV basis), consuming ~50 kWh/kg. --- ### 3.3 Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style) > **Problem:** A fuel cell vehicle consumes hydrogen at a rate of **1.2 kg/hr**. The fuel cell efficiency is **55%**, and the LHV of hydrogen is **120 MJ/kg**. Calculate the electrical power output. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Mass flow rate:** $$\dot{m}_{H_2} = 1.2 \text{ kg/hr} = \frac{1.2}{3600} \text{ kg/s} = 3.33 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$$ **Step 2 — Energy input rate (chemical):** $$\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_{H_2} \times LHV = 3.33 \times 10^{-4} \times 120 \times 10^6$$ $$\dot{Q}_{in} = 40{,}000 \text{ W} = 40 \text{ kW (thermal input)}$$ **Step 3 — Electrical power output (applying efficiency):** $$\dot{W}_{elec} = \eta_{FC} \times \dot{Q}_{in} = 0.55 \times 40$$ $$\boxed{\dot{W}_{elec} = 22 \text{ kW}}$$ --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Hydrogen Production Routes | Parameter | SMR (Grey) | Electrolysis (Green) | Coal Gasification (Brown) | |---|---|---|---| | **Feedstock** | Natural Gas + Steam | Water + Electricity | Coal + Steam/O₂ | | **Temperature** | 700–900 °C | 60–800 °C (PEM/SOEC) | 800–1300 °C | | **CO₂ Produced** | ~10 kg/kg H₂ | 0 kg/kg H₂ | ~20 kg/kg H₂ | | **Energy Input** | Natural gas (chemical) | Renewable electricity | Coal (chemical) | | **Maturity** | Fully commercial | Commercial (growing) | Mature but polluting | | **Cost (USD/kg)** | $1–2 | $3–6 | $1–2 | | **KTU Tag** | Most-used method | Future of H₂ | Polluting legacy | --- ### 3.5 Energy Content Comparison (Useful for KTU Numerical Problems) | Fuel | LHV (MJ/kg) | AFR (mass) | CO₂ (kg/kg fuel) | |---|---|---|---| | **Hydrogen** | **120** | 34.3 | **0** | | Methane (CH₄) | 50 | 17.2 | 2.75 | | Gasoline | 44 | 14.7 | 3.30 | | Diesel | 43 | 14.5 | 3.20 | | Methanol | 20 | 6.4 | 1.37 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip:** When asked to compare, always emphasize: *Hydrogen has the highest LHV per unit mass but the **lowest energy per unit volume** (~8.5 MJ/L liquid vs. gasoline ~32 MJ/L). This is why storage is the biggest challenge.* ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Hydrogen Production–Storage–Utilization Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Renewable Energy
Solar Wind Hydro] --> B1[Power to Gas
Electrolyzer] A2[Natural Gas
Coal Biomass] --> B2[Steam Reforming
Gasification] B1 --> C[Green H2] B2 --> D[Grey Brown H2] C --> E[Purification
and Compression] D --> E E --> F1[Compressed Gas
350 to 700 bar] E --> F2[Liquid H2
minus 253 C] E --> F3[Metal Hydrides
LOHC] F1 --> G1[Fuel Cells
PEM SOFC] F2 --> G2[IC Engines
Gas Turbines] F3 --> G3[Industrial Heating
Steel Ammonia] G1 --> H1[Electricity
Mobility] G2 --> H2[Mechanical Power] G3 --> H3[Process Heat] H1 --> I[Only Water
as Emission] H2 --> I H3 --> I ``` ### 4.2 PEM Electrolyzer Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph InputLayer A[DC Power Supply
from Solar or Wind] B[Deionized Water
H2O Feed] end subgraph ElectrolysisCore C[Anode
Oxygen Evolution
Reaction OER] D[Proton Exchange
Membrane PEM] E[Cathode
Hydrogen Evolution
Reaction HER] end subgraph OutputStreams F[O2 Vent
Purity 99 percent] G[H2 Gas Stream
Purity 99.99 percent] end subgraph Auxiliary H[Heat Exchanger
Thermal Management] I[Compressor
Storage Tank] end A --> C A --> E B --> C C --> D D --> E C --> F E --> G G --> I C --> H E --> H ``` ### 4.3 Hydrogen Color-Coding Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Hydrogen Production
Method Chosen]) --> Q1{Feedstock Type} Q1 -->|Natural Gas
No CCS| Grey[Grey Hydrogen
CO2 Released] Q1 -->|Natural Gas
With CCS| Blue[Blue Hydrogen
CO2 Captured] Q1 -->|Coal| Brown[Brown Hydrogen
High Pollution] Q1 -->|Water plus
Renewable Power| Green[Green Hydrogen
Zero Emissions] Q1 -->|Water plus
Nuclear Power| Pink[Pink Hydrogen
Zero Emissions] Grey --> End([Application
Industry Mobility]) Blue --> End Brown --> End Green --> End Pink --> End ``` ### 4.4 Hydrogen Fuel Cell Working Principle ```mermaid flowchart LR A[H2 Fuel
Inlet] --> B[Anode
Oxidation] C[O2 Air
Inlet] --> D[Cathode
Reduction] B --> E[Electron Flow
through External
Circuit DC Load] E --> F[Electrical Work
Output] D --> G[Proton Path
through Electrolyte] B --> G G --> D B --> H[H2O Product
Water plus Heat] D --> H ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define Green Hydrogen. Why is it considered the fuel of the future?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Green hydrogen is hydrogen produced by the **electrolysis of water using electricity generated from renewable sources** such as solar, wind, or hydropower. The overall reaction is: $$2H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{Renewable Electricity}} 2H_2 + O_2$$ It is considered the **fuel of the future** because: - (1) **Zero carbon emissions** — no CO₂ is released during production or combustion. - (2) **Highest energy-to-mass ratio** (LHV = 120 MJ/kg). - (3) **Enables storage of intermittent renewable energy** as a chemical fuel. - (4) **Versatile applications** — fuel cells, IC engines, industrial heating, ammonia, and steel. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *State the difference between HHV and LHV of hydrogen. Why is LHV used for hydrogen ICE engines?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **HHV (Higher Heating Value) = 142 MJ/kg** — includes the latent heat recovered from condensation of water vapor formed during combustion. - **LHV (Lower Heating Value) = 120 MJ/kg** — assumes water leaves as vapor, and this latent heat is *not* recovered. LHV is used for **hydrogen ICE engines** (1 mark) because the exhaust gases leave the engine at **very high temperatures (~800–1000 °C)**, well above 100 °C, so the water vapor does **not condense** and the latent heat cannot be recovered. Hence the *effective* energy available is LHV, not HHV (2 marks). --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A [14 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Explain the **Steam Methane Reforming (SMR)** process for hydrogen production with neat reaction equations. State its main drawback. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **stoichiometric air-fuel ratio (by mass)** for complete combustion of pure hydrogen. Show every step. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution (a) — SMR Process [7 marks]** **Steam Methane Reforming (SMR)** is the most widely used industrial method, accounting for ~95% of global hydrogen production. **Step 1 — Primary Reforming (Endothermic): [2 marks]** $$CH_4 + H_2O_{(steam)} \xrightarrow{700-900°C, Ni \text{ catalyst}} CO + 3H_2 \quad \Delta H = +206 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ Natural gas (methane) is reacted with high-pressure steam in reformer tubes packed with a nickel catalyst. Heat must be supplied externally. **Step 2 — Water–Gas Shift Reaction (Exothermic): [2 marks]** $$CO + H_2O \xrightarrow{330°C, Fe_2O_3/CuO \text{ catalyst}} CO_2 + H_2 \quad \Delta H = -41 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ The CO produced is further reacted with steam to produce additional H₂ and CO₂. **Step 3 — Overall Reaction: [1 mark]** $$CH_4 + 2H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + 4H_2$$ **Step 4 — CO₂ Separation (Pressure Swing Adsorption): [1 mark]** The CO₂ and residual impurities are removed using PSA units, leaving high-purity H₂ (>99.99%). **Main Drawback: [1 mark]** - **Large CO₂ emissions** — for every kg of H₂ produced, ~10 kg of CO₂ is released, making SMR a **carbon-intensive** ("Grey hydrogen") process. This conflicts with global decarbonization goals. Mitigation: add Carbon Capture & Storage (CCS) to obtain *Blue Hydrogen*. --- #### **Model Solution (b) — Stoichiometric AFR Derivation [7 marks]** **Combustion Reaction:** $$2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O$$ **Step 1: Per kmol of H₂, O₂ required = 0.5 kmol [1 mark]** **Step 2: Masses involved** [1 mark] $$m_{H_2} = 0.5 \times 2.016 = 1.008 \text{ kg} \quad ; \quad m_{O_2} = 0.5 \times 32 = 16 \text{ kg}$$ Wait — let us re-state. For **1 kg of H₂**, O₂ required is: $$m_{O_2} = \frac{16}{2.016} = 7.937 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 3: Convert O₂ to air** [2 marks] Oxygen constitutes **23.2% by mass** in dry atmospheric air. Therefore: $$m_{air} = \frac{m_{O_2}}{0.232} = \frac{7.937}{0.232}$$ $$m_{air} = 34.21 \text{ kg air per kg H}_2$$ **Step 4: Conclusion** [1 mark] $$\boxed{(A/F)_{stoich,mass} = 34.21 : 1}$$ **Step 5: Verification & Remarks** [2 marks] This is approximately **2.33 times** the AFR of gasoline (14.7:1). The high value arises because hydrogen contains no carbon, so all combustion oxygen must be used to combine with H₂ alone. The result has been experimentally validated and is a standard reference in combustion engineering. --- #### **Question B [14 Marks — Alternative Choice]** **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **PEM (Proton Exchange Membrane) Electrolyzer** for green hydrogen production. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A fuel cell power plant consumes **5 kg/hr of hydrogen** (LHV = 120 MJ/kg). The overall efficiency is **50%**. Calculate: **(i)** Thermal power input, **(ii)** Electrical power output, **(iii)** Volume of H₂ consumed per hour at STP (ρ = 0.0899 kg/m³). **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution (a) — PEM Electrolyzer [7 marks]** **Definition [1 mark]:** A PEM (Proton Exchange Membrane) electrolyzer uses a solid polymer electrolyte to split deionized water into hydrogen and oxygen using renewable electricity. **Components [2 marks]:** 1. **Anode** (positive electrode) — where water is oxidized: $$2H_2O \rightarrow O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^-$$ 2. **Cathode** (negative electrode) — where protons are reduced: $$4H^+ + 4e^- \rightarrow 2H_2$$ 3. **PEM membrane** (e.g., Nafion™) — conducts only H⁺ ions, separating gas products. **Working [2 marks]:** - Deionized water is fed to the anode side. - DC power (from solar PV / wind) is applied across the electrodes. - Water splits at the anode; O₂ is vented out; H⁺ ions migrate through the membrane to the cathode. - At the cathode, H⁺ ions combine with electrons to form pure H₂ gas. **Advantages [1 mark]:** High purity H₂ (99.99%), compact design, fast dynamic response suitable for intermittent renewables. **Limitation [1 mark]:** Requires expensive platinum-group catalysts and high-purity water. --- #### **Model Solution (b) — Numerical [7 marks]** **Given:** - $\dot{m}_{H_2} = 5 \text{ kg/hr}$ - $LHV = 120 \text{ MJ/kg}$ - $\eta_{overall} = 50\% = 0.5$ - $\rho_{H_2,STP} = 0.0899 \text{ kg/m}^3$ **(i) Thermal power input: [2 marks]** $$\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_{H_2} \times LHV = 5 \times 120 = 600 \text{ MJ/hr}$$ Converting to kW: $$\dot{Q}_{in} = \frac{600 \times 10^3}{3600} = 166.67 \text{ kW (thermal)}$$ **(ii) Electrical power output: [2 marks]** $$\dot{W}_{elec} = \eta \times \dot{Q}_{in} = 0.5 \times 166.67$$ $$\boxed{\dot{W}_{elec} = 83.33 \text{ kW}}$$ **(iii) Volume of H₂ consumed per hour at STP: [3 marks]** $$\dot{V}_{H_2} = \frac{\dot{m}_{H_2}}{\rho_{H_2}} = \frac{5 \text{ kg/hr}}{0.0899 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$ $$\boxed{\dot{V}_{H_2} = 55.62 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}}$$ This very high volume illustrates **why hydrogen storage is a major engineering challenge** — even 5 kg of H₂ occupies a large space, motivating the use of compression (700 bar) or liquefaction (−253 °C). --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **AFR confusion:** Students write 34.3 for *both* mass and volume ratios. Mass ratio is **34.3**, but volume ratio is **2.38**. Examiners deduct 1 mark if not specified "by mass" or "by volume". > > 2. **HHV vs LHV units:** Always write units (MJ/kg). Writing just "142" without units = −0.5 mark. > > 3. **Reversed reactions:** Writing SMR as $CH_4 + 2H_2O \rightarrow 4H_2 + CO_2$ is **acceptable**, but forgetting the catalyst and temperature conditions loses 1 mark. > > 4. **Color-coding confusion:** Pink and Green hydrogen are **NOT** the same — Pink uses nuclear, Green uses renewables. Examiners test this. > > 5. **Missing stoichiometric balance:** Forgetting to balance the half-reactions in electrolysis. Always verify: mass and charge must balance. > > 6. **Conflating fuel cell and battery:** A fuel cell is *not* a battery — it generates electricity as long as fuel is supplied; a battery stores finite energy. > > 7. **Units in numericals:** $\dot{Q}_{in}$ in MJ/hr is NOT power. Convert to kW by dividing by 3.6. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for Hydrogen (Module 4)** > > ✅ **Definition:** Hydrogen is a **secondary energy carrier / energy vector**, not a primary energy source. > > ✅ **HHV = 142 MJ/kg**, **LHV = 120 MJ/kg** (memorize both). > > ✅ **Stoichiometric AFR (mass) = 34.3:1**; (volume) = 2.38:1. > > ✅ **Combustion reaction:** $2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O$ (only water as product). > > ✅ **Color Coding (4 main types):** > - **Grey** — SMR, no CCS (most common, polluting) > - **Blue** — SMR + CCS (transitional) > - **Brown** — Coal gasification (most polluting) > - **Green** — Electrolysis + Renewables (zero carbon, future) > - **Pink** — Electrolysis + Nuclear > > ✅ **SMR Reactions:** > - Reforming: $CH_4 + H_2O \rightarrow CO + 3H_2$ > - Shift: $CO + H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2$ > > ✅ **Electrolysis minimum energy:** 39.4 kWh/kg (HHV basis), 32.7 kWh/kg (electrical basis). > > ✅ **Storage challenge:** Low volumetric density → use 700 bar compression or LH₂ at −253 °C. > > ✅ **Flammability range:** 4–75% in air (very wide → safety hazard). > > ✅ **Adiabatic flame temperature in air:** ~2045 °C (highest among common fuels → causes high NOx). > > ✅ **Key applications:** Rocket propellant, fuel cell vehicles (FCEVs), ammonia synthesis, green steel, grid-scale energy storage. > > ✅ **Fuel cell efficiency (50–60%)** is **double** the IC engine efficiency (25–30%). > > ✅ **Power-to-Gas concept:** Surplus renewable electricity → H₂ → stored → re-electrified when needed. > > ✅ **For numericals:** Always show unit conversions explicitly (MJ ↔ kWh ↔ kJ).

topics.title: Natural Gas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET303 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Natural Gas

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Natural Gas (NG)** is a naturally occurring hydrocarbon gas mixture consisting primarily of **methane (CH₄)** along with smaller fractions of higher hydrocarbons (ethane, propane, butane) and non-combustible gases (CO₂, N₂, H₂S, water vapour). It is found in porous geological formations, often alongside crude oil (associated gas) or in dry reservoirs (non-associated gas), and is extracted through drilled wells for use as a clean-burning fossil fuel.

In KTU Thermal Engineering terminology, natural gas is classified as a **gaseous fossil fuel** of the **paraffinic (alkane) family**, distinguished from manufactured gases (producer gas, water gas, blast furnace gas) by its higher calorific value and lower pollutant emissions.

### 1.2 Typical Composition of Natural Gas

| Component | Chemical Formula | Mole Fraction (% vol) |
|---|---|---|
| Methane | CH₄ | 85 – 95 |
| Ethane | C₂H₆ | 2 – 5 |
| Propane | C₃H₈ | 1 – 3 |
| Butane | C₄H₁₀ | 0.5 – 1 |
| Pentane + | C₅⁺ | 0.1 – 0.5 |
| Carbon Dioxide | CO₂ | 0.1 – 1 |
| Nitrogen | N₂ | 0.1 – 5 |
| Hydrogen Sulphide | H₂S | trace |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of natural gas as the "energy milk" of the Earth. Just as dairy milk is stored in udders deep inside a cow, natural gas is trapped inside the pores of rock layers deep underground. When we drill a well, we are essentially "milking the Earth." It is the cleanest fossil fuel, much like white rice is the cleanest grain — it produces the least soot and ash when "cooked" (combusted) compared to coal (brown rice with husks) or oil (sticky molasses).

- **Molecular weight of typical NG ≈ 19 kg/kmol**
- **Specific gravity (with respect to air) ≈ 0.55 to 0.65**
- **Density at STP ≈ 0.8 kg/m³**

### 1.4 Forms of Natural Gas Supply

> [!NOTE]
> Natural gas reaches the consumer in **three physical states**, each with its own engineering infrastructure:
> 1. **PNG (Piped Natural Gas):** Transported through high-pressure steel pipelines in gaseous state.
> 2. **CNG (Compressed Natural Gas):** Compressed to **200 – 250 bar** for vehicle fuel and small distribution.
> 3. **LNG (Liquefied Natural Gas):** Cooled to **−162 °C** and liquefied for oceanic transportation (volume reduced ~600×).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification by Source

- **Associated / Dissolved Gas:** Coexists with crude oil in reservoirs. Extracted during oil production.
- **Non-Associated Gas:** Found in dry gas reservoirs without significant crude oil.
- **Coal-Bed Methane (CBM):** Adsorbed on coal seams, extracted by dewatering.
- **Shale Gas:** Trapped in shale rock formations, extracted by hydraulic fracturing.
- **Biogas:** Anaerobic digestion of organic matter (not natural in origin but compositionally similar).

### 2.2 Combustion Chemistry of Natural Gas

The dominant combustion reaction is that of methane:

$$CH_4 + 2O_2 \longrightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O + \text{Heat}$$

In 1 kg of natural gas, the mass fractions of constituent elements typically are:
- Carbon (C): ≈ 0.74
- Hydrogen (H): ≈ 0.24
- Nitrogen + inert gases: ≈ 0.02

### 2.3 Why is Natural Gas Preferred?

- Highest **hydrogen-to-carbon ratio** among fossil fuels → more energy per kg of CO₂ released.
- No particulate matter (PM) emissions.
- Negligible SO₂ emissions (if sweetened to remove H₂S).
- Higher flame speed and cleaner combustion.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table compiles every formula you will need for solving Natural Gas problems in the KTU 2024 ESE.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stoichiometric O₂ (mass basis) | $O_2 = \dfrac{8}{3}C + 8H - O$ | kg O₂ / kg fuel |
| 2 | Stoichiometric Air (mass) | $A_{st} = \dfrac{O_2}{0.232}$ | kg air / kg fuel |
| 3 | Stoichiometric Air (moles) | $A_{st} = \dfrac{4.76 \times O_2^{moles}}{1}$ | kmol air / kg fuel |
| 4 | Air-Fuel Ratio (A/F) | $AFR = \dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}}$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | GCV by Dulong | $GCV = 8080 C + 33900\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240 S$ | kcal/kg |
| 6 | GCV (kJ/kg form) | $GCV = 33800 C + 141800\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 9220 S$ | kJ/kg |
| 7 | LCV = GCV − 9 × H₂O | $LCV = GCV - 587 \times 9 H$ | kcal/kg |
| 8 | Wobbe Index | $W = \dfrac{GCV}{\sqrt{SG}}$ | kcal/Nm³ |
| 9 | % Excess Air | $EA\% = \dfrac{A_{actual} - A_{st}}{A_{st}} \times 100$ | % |
| 10 | Flue gas O₂ ↔ Excess Air | $EA\% = \dfrac{O_2^{flue} \times 100}{0.264\,N_2^{flue} - O_2^{flue}}$ | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Wobbe Index** is the single most important parameter for gas interchangeability. Two gases with the same Wobbe Index deliver the same heat output through the same burner orifice. This is the reason LPG, CNG and PNG are not interchangeable despite all being hydrocarbon gases.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Generation:** Combined Cycle Gas Turbine (CCGT) plants using natural gas achieve efficiencies up to **62 %**, the highest among fossil-fuel plants.
- **Domestic:** PNG for cooking (replacing LPG in metro cities), space heating.
- **Automotive:** CNG vehicles emit ~25 % less CO₂ than petrol vehicles.
- **Industrial:** Direct-fired furnaces, glass melting, ceramic kilns.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Problem Setup (Typical KTU-Level)

**Given:** Natural gas of composition (by volume / mole): CH₄ = 90 %, C₂H₆ = 5 %, C₃H₈ = 3 %, N₂ = 2 %.

**Required:**
1. Theoretical (stoichiometric) air required per kg of fuel.
2. Air-fuel ratio on mass basis.
3. Higher Calorific Value (GCV).
4. Lower Calorific Value (LCV).
5. Wet and dry flue gas analysis when burnt with **20 % excess air**.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Conversion of Composition (Mole → Mass)

> Take **100 kmol** of fuel as basis.

| Component | Moles | Mol. Weight | Mass (kg) |
|---|---|---|---|
| CH₄ | 90 | 16 | 1440 |
| C₂H₆ | 5 | 30 | 150 |
| C₃H₈ | 3 | 44 | 132 |
| N₂ | 2 | 28 | 56 |
| **Total** | **100** | — | **1778** |

Therefore **1 kg of fuel contains:**
- $C$ from CH₄: $90 \times 12 / 1778 = 0.6076$ kg
- $H$ from CH₄: $90 \times 4 / 1778 = 0.2025$ kg
- $C$ from C₂H₆: $5 \times 24 / 1778 = 0.0675$ kg
- $H$ from C₂H₆: $5 \times 6 / 1778 = 0.0169$ kg
- $C$ from C₃H₈: $3 \times 36 / 1778 = 0.0608$ kg
- $H$ from C₃H₈: $3 \times 8 / 1778 = 0.0135$ kg
- $N_2$: $56 / 1778 = 0.0315$ kg

**Totals:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C &= 0.6076 + 0.0675 + 0.0608 = 0.7359 \text{ kg/kg fuel} \\
H &= 0.2025 + 0.0169 + 0.0135 = 0.2329 \text{ kg/kg fuel} \\
O &= 0 \text{ kg/kg fuel} \\
N &= 0.0315 \text{ kg/kg fuel}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Stoichiometric Oxygen Required

By elemental balance, every kg of carbon consumes $\tfrac{8}{3}$ kg of O₂, and every kg of hydrogen consumes **8 kg** of O₂.

$$\begin{aligned}
O_2^{st} &= \frac{8}{3} \cdot C + 8 \cdot H - O \\
&= \frac{8}{3}(0.7359) + 8(0.2329) - 0 \\
&= 1.9624 + 1.8632 \\
&= 3.8256 \text{ kg O}_2 \text{ per kg fuel}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.4 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air Required

Atmospheric air contains 23.2 % O₂ by mass. Therefore:

$$A_{st} = \frac{O_2^{st}}{0.232} = \frac{3.8256}{0.232} = 16.49 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **theoretical (stoichiometric) A/F ratio for typical natural gas ≈ 16.5 : 1** on mass basis. Memorise this number — it is one of the highest A/F ratios among common fuels (gasoline 14.7, kerosene 15.1, coal 6–10).

### 3.5 Derivation: Higher Calorific Value (GCV)

Using Dulong's formula in **kcal/kg**:

$$\begin{aligned}
GCV &= 8080\,C + 33900\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S \\
&= 8080(73.59) + 33900(23.29 - 0) + 0 \\
&= 594\,607 + 789\,531 \\
&= 1\,384\,138 \text{ kcal per 100 kg}
\end{aligned}$$

So per kg of fuel:

$$GCV = 13\,841 \text{ kcal/kg} = 57\,930 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

### 3.6 Derivation: Lower Calorific Value (LCV)

Each kg of hydrogen produces 9 kg of water, which carries away latent heat at **587 kcal/kg of water vapour**.

$$\begin{aligned}
LCV &= GCV - 9 \times H \times 587 \\
&= 13\,841 - 9 \times 0.2329 \times 587 \\
&= 13\,841 - 1230 \\
&= 12\,611 \text{ kcal/kg} \\
&= 52\,787 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.7 Derivation: Flue Gas Analysis with 20 % Excess Air

**Actual air supplied:**

$$A_{act} = 1.20 \times A_{st} = 1.20 \times 16.49 = 19.79 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$

**O₂ supplied:** $19.79 \times 0.232 = 4.591$ kg

**Excess O₂ left unreacted:** $4.591 - 3.826 = 0.765$ kg

**N₂ from air:** $19.79 \times 0.768 = 15.20$ kg + fuel N₂ 0.0315 kg = **15.23 kg**

**Combustion Products (per kg fuel):**

| Product | Source | Mass (kg) |
|---|---|---|
| CO₂ | From C | $\tfrac{11}{3} \times 0.7359 = 2.698$ |
| H₂O | From H | $9 \times 0.2329 = 2.096$ |
| O₂ | Excess | $0.765$ |
| N₂ | Air + fuel | $15.23$ |
| **Total flue gas** | — | **20.79** |

**Dry flue gas composition (ignoring H₂O):**

$$\begin{aligned}
CO_2 &= \frac{2.698}{20.79 - 2.096} \times 100 = 14.43\,\% \\
O_2 &= \frac{0.765}{18.694} \times 100 = 4.09\,\% \\
N_2 &= \frac{15.23}{18.694} \times 100 = 81.47\,\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Wet flue gas composition:**

$$\begin{aligned}
CO_2 &= \frac{2.698}{20.79} \times 100 = 12.98\,\% \\
H_2O &= \frac{2.096}{20.79} \times 100 = 10.08\,\% \\
O_2 &= \frac{0.765}{20.79} \times 100 = 3.68\,\% \\
N_2 &= \frac{15.23}{20.79} \times 100 = 73.26\,\%
\end{aligned}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Natural Gas Supply Chain — Block Diagram

The following flowchart illustrates the complete journey of natural gas from the wellhead to the end consumer.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[NG1 Oil Well] --> B[NG2 Gas Processing Plant]
    B --> C[NG3 Pipeline PNG]
    B --> D[NG4 LNG Liquefaction Plant]
    D --> E[NG5 LNG Tanker Ship]
    E --> F[NG6 Regasification Terminal]
    B --> G[NG7 CNG Compression Station]
    C --> H[NG8 Industrial Burners]
    C --> I[NG9 Power Plant CCGT]
    C --> J[NG10 Domestic PNG]
    F --> I
    G --> K[NG11 CNG Vehicles]
    I --> L[NG12 Electricity Grid]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the diagram, **CCGT** stands for **Combined Cycle Gas Turbine** — the most efficient large-scale power generation topology using natural gas. The plant consists of a Brayton cycle (gas turbine) topped by a Rankine cycle (steam turbine) using the hot exhaust to generate steam.

### 4.2 Combustion Process Flow — Block Architecture

The following diagram maps the functional architecture of natural gas combustion in a furnace/boiler/gas turbine combustor.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[F1 Natural Gas Fuel] --> M1[F2 Mixing with Combustion Air]
    P2[F3 Atmospheric Air] --> F4[F4 Air Filter and Compressor]
    F4 --> M1
    M1 --> I1[F5 Ignition Spark or Pilot Flame]
    I1 --> C1[F6 Combustion Chamber]
    C1 --> C2[F7 Flame Stabilisation Zone]
    C2 --> H1[F8 Heat Release to Working Fluid]
    H1 --> H2[F9 Hot Flue Gas]
    H2 --> E1[F10 Exhaust to Chimney]
    H1 --> U1[F11 Useful Work Steam or Power]
```

### 4.3 Combustion Reaction — Schematic Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[R1 CH4 plus 2 O2] --> R2[R2 Flame Reaction Zone]
    A1[A1 7.52 N2 from Air] --> R2
    R2 --> R3[R3 CO2 plus 2 H2O plus 7.52 N2]
    R3 --> R4[R4 Flue Gas Stack]
    R2 -.heat.-> R5[R5 Energy Released minus 802 kJ per mol]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Heat of combustion of methane at standard conditions:
> $$\Delta H_c = -802 \text{ kJ/mol} = -55{,}500 \text{ kJ/kg}$$
> This negative sign denotes heat **release** (exothermic reaction). Always quote magnitudes (positive value) when stating calorific value.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCIET303]`

Define natural gas. List any four advantages of natural gas over coal as a fuel.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Natural gas is a naturally occurring mixture of hydrocarbon gases, principally methane (CH₄), found in porous underground rock formations either associated with crude oil or in dry reservoirs.
>
> **Advantages (4 × 0.5 = 2 marks):**
> 1. Higher calorific value (~ 55 MJ/kg) compared to coal (~ 25 MJ/kg).
> 2. Almost complete combustion — no smoke, ash, or soot.
> 3. Negligible SO₂ emissions after sweetening.
> 4. Easy transportation through pipelines and lower handling losses.
> 5. Can be directly used in IC engines without complex pre-treatment.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PCIET303]`

What is **Wobbe Index**? Why is it critical for natural gas interchangeability?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** The Wobbe Index (W) of a fuel gas is the ratio of its calorific value to the square root of its specific gravity relative to air:
> $$W = \dfrac{GCV}{\sqrt{SG}}$$
>
> **Significance (1.5 marks):** Two gases with the same Wobbe Index deliver the same heat output to a burner operating at a given pressure, regardless of changes in composition. This makes Wobbe Index the governing parameter for **interchangeability** of fuel gases — a critical safety and performance criterion when switching from one gas supply (e.g., town gas to natural gas) to another.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert volume % to mass %** before applying Dulong's formula. (−2 marks)
> 2. **Using 23 % instead of 23.2 %** for O₂ fraction in air (acceptable, but state clearly). (−0.5 marks)
> 3. **Reporting GCV of natural gas as ~ 8000 kcal/kg** (this is the value for *carbon*, not NG). (−1 mark)
> 4. **Not specifying whether flue gas composition is wet or dry** — both must be computed if excess air is given. (−1 mark)
> 5. **Mixing up kg/kg with kg/kmol** in stoichiometric air calculation. (−1.5 marks)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCIET303]`

A sample of natural gas has the following composition by volume:
CH₄ = 86 %, C₂H₆ = 8 %, C₃H₈ = 4 %, N₂ = 2 %.

**(a)** Calculate the stoichiometric air required for complete combustion of 1 kg of fuel. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the higher calorific value (GCV) of the fuel and the wet flue gas composition when the gas is burnt with 25 % excess air. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Mole-to-mass conversion (Basis: 100 kmol of fuel)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CH}_4 &: 86 \times 16 = 1376 \text{ kg} \\
\text{C}_2\text{H}_6 &: 8 \times 30 = 240 \text{ kg} \\
\text{C}_3\text{H}_8 &: 4 \times 44 = 176 \text{ kg} \\
\text{N}_2 &: 2 \times 28 = 56 \text{ kg} \\
\text{Total mass} &= 1848 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Elemental mass fractions per kg fuel**

$$\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{86(12) + 8(24) + 4(36)}{1848} \\
&= \frac{1032 + 192 + 144}{1848} = \frac{1368}{1848} = 0.7403 \\
H &= \frac{86(4) + 8(6) + 4(8)}{1848} \\
&= \frac{344 + 48 + 32}{1848} = \frac{424}{1848} = 0.2294 \\
O &= 0, \quad N = 56/1848 = 0.0303
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Stoichiometric O₂ (1.5 marks)**

$$O_2 = \frac{8}{3}C + 8H = \frac{8}{3}(0.7403) + 8(0.2294) = 1.974 + 1.835 = 3.809 \text{ kg/kg fuel}$$

**Step 4: Stoichiometric Air (1.5 marks)**

$$A_{st} = \frac{3.809}{0.232} = 16.42 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: GCV by Dulong (2 marks)**

$$\begin{aligned}
GCV &= 8080\,C + 33900\,H \\
&= 8080(0.7403) + 33900(0.2294) \\
&= 5981.6 + 7776.7 \\
&= 13\,758 \text{ kcal/kg} \\
&= 57\,565 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Actual air supplied (1 mark)**

$$A_{act} = 1.25 \times 16.42 = 20.525 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$

$$\text{O}_2 \text{ supplied} = 20.525 \times 0.232 = 4.762 \text{ kg}$$

$$\text{O}_2 \text{ excess} = 4.762 - 3.809 = 0.953 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3: N₂ in flue gas (1 mark)**

$$N_2 = 20.525 \times 0.768 + 0.0303 = 15.763 + 0.030 = 15.793 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4: Combustion products (1 mark)**

$$\begin{aligned}
CO_2 &= \frac{11}{3}(0.7403) = 2.714 \text{ kg} \\
H_2O &= 9(0.2294) = 2.065 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Total wet flue gas (0.5 mark)**

$$M_{total} = 2.714 + 2.065 + 0.953 + 15.793 = 21.525 \text{ kg/kg fuel}$$

**Step 6: Wet composition (1.5 marks)**

$$\begin{aligned}
CO_2 &= \frac{2.714}{21.525} \times 100 = 12.61\,\% \\
H_2O &= \frac{2.065}{21.525} \times 100 = 9.59\,\% \\
O_2 &= \frac{0.953}{21.525} \times 100 = 4.43\,\% \\
N_2 &= \frac{15.793}{21.525} \times 100 = 73.37\,\%
\end{aligned}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PCIET303]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **stoichiometric air requirement** per kg of a hydrocarbon fuel containing carbon (C), hydrogen (H) and oxygen (O) by mass, stating clearly the chemical equations on which your derivation rests. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A natural gas has the following composition by volume: CH₄ = 92 %, C₂H₆ = 4 %, C₃H₈ = 2 %, N₂ = 2 %. It is burnt in a gas turbine combustor with 150 % excess air. Find (i) the actual air-fuel ratio, and (ii) the dry flue gas analysis in mole percent. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

The complete combustion reactions of the constituent elements are:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Carbon:} \quad & C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 \\
\text{Hydrogen:} \quad & 4H + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O
\end{aligned}$$

**From carbon balance:**
1 kg of C consumes $\frac{32}{12} = \frac{8}{3}$ kg of O₂ → for $C$ kg of carbon, $O_2 = \frac{8}{3}C$ kg.

**From hydrogen balance:**
1 kg of H consumes $\frac{32}{4} = 8$ kg of O₂ → for $H$ kg of hydrogen, $O_2 = 8H$ kg.

If the fuel itself contains $O$ kg of oxygen, this oxygen is already partially oxidised; the net O₂ required from external air is:

$$O_2^{net} = \frac{8}{3}C + 8H - O \quad \text{(kg O}_2 \text{ per kg fuel)}$$

By mass, air contains 23.2 % O₂ and 76.8 % N₂. Therefore stoichiometric air:

$$A_{st} = \frac{O_2^{net}}{0.232} = \frac{100}{23.2}\left(\frac{8}{3}C + 8H - O\right) \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$

This is the fundamental expression used in **all** KTU combustion problems.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Mass composition per kg of fuel**

Basis: 100 kmol fuel = 1840 kg (verify: 92×16 + 4×30 + 2×44 + 2×28 = 1472 + 120 + 88 + 56 = 1736 kg)

$$\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{92(12) + 4(24) + 2(36)}{1736} = \frac{1104 + 96 + 72}{1736} = \frac{1272}{1736} = 0.7327 \\
H &= \frac{92(4) + 4(6) + 2(8)}{1736} = \frac{368 + 24 + 16}{1736} = \frac{408}{1736} = 0.2350
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Stoichiometric O₂ (1 mark)**

$$O_2^{st} = \frac{8}{3}(0.7327) + 8(0.2350) = 1.954 + 1.880 = 3.834 \text{ kg/kg fuel}$$

**Step 3: Stoichiometric Air (1 mark)**

$$A_{st} = 3.834 / 0.232 = 16.53 \text{ kg/kg fuel}$$

**Step 4: Actual A/F (1 mark)**

$$A_{act} = 2.5 \times A_{st} = 41.32 \text{ kg air/kg fuel} \quad (\text{since } EA = 150\,\%)$$

**Step 5: Dry flue gas (3 marks)**

$$\text{O}_2 \text{ supplied} = 41.32 \times 0.232 = 9.586 \text{ kg}$$

$$\text{O}_2 \text{ excess} = 9.586 - 3.834 = 5.752 \text{ kg}$$

$$\text{N}_2 \text{ from air} = 41.32 \times 0.768 = 31.73 \text{ kg}$$

$$\text{CO}_2 = \frac{11}{3}(0.7327) = 2.687 \text{ kg}$$

$$\text{H}_2\text{O (discarded for dry analysis)} = 9(0.2350) = 2.115 \text{ kg}$$

$$\text{N}_2 \text{ from fuel} = \frac{56}{1736} = 0.0323 \text{ kg}$$

Total dry flue gas:

$$M_{dry} = 2.687 + 5.752 + 31.73 + 0.032 = 40.20 \text{ kg/kg fuel}$$

**Step 6: Dry composition in mass % then convert to mole % (1 mark)**

$$\begin{aligned}
CO_2 &: \frac{2.687}{40.20} \times 100 = 6.68\,\% \text{ mass} \\
O_2 &: \frac{5.752}{40.20} \times 100 = 14.31\,\% \text{ mass} \\
N_2 &: \frac{31.76}{40.20} \times 100 = 79.01\,\% \text{ mass}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion to mole % using Mᵢ × 100 / Σ(Mᵢ × yᵢ):**

| Component | Mass % | M | Mass% / M |
|---|---|---|---|
| CO₂ | 6.68 | 44 | 0.1518 |
| O₂ | 14.31 | 32 | 0.4472 |
| N₂ | 79.01 | 28 | 2.8218 |
| Σ | — | — | 3.4208 |

$$\begin{aligned}
CO_2^{mole} &= \frac{0.1518}{3.4208} \times 100 = 4.44\,\% \\
O_2^{mole} &= \frac{0.4472}{3.4208} \times 100 = 13.07\,\% \\
N_2^{mole} &= \frac{2.8218}{3.4208} \times 100 = 82.49\,\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Verification: Σ = 100 % ✓**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Natural Gas (PCIET303 Module 4)**
>
> - [x] Natural gas is **predominantly methane (85–95 %)**; remainder is C₂H₆, C₃H₈, N₂, CO₂.
> - [x] Molecular weight ≈ **19 kg/kmol**; Specific gravity relative to air ≈ **0.6**.
> - [x] Three supply forms: **PNG, CNG (200 bar), LNG (−162 °C)**.
> - [x] Stoichiometric A/F of typical NG ≈ **16.5 : 1** — highest among common fuels.
> - [x] Dulong's formula for GCV in kcal/kg: $\,GCV = 8080\,C + 33900(H - O/8)$.
> - [x] Dulong's formula for GCV in kJ/kg: $\,GCV = 33800\,C + 141800(H - O/8)$.
> - [x] LCV = GCV − **587 × 9 × H** (because 1 kg H → 9 kg H₂O, each carrying 587 kcal latent heat).
> - [x] **Wobbe Index** $W = GCV / \sqrt{SG}$ governs gas interchangeability.
> - [x] Excess air formula: $EA\,\% = \dfrac{A_{actual} - A_{st}}{A_{st}} \times 100$.
> - [x] Always state **wet vs. dry** when reporting flue gas composition.
> - [x] Atmospheric air: **23.2 % O₂ + 76.8 % N₂ by mass** (or **21 % O₂ + 79 % N₂ by mole**).
> - [x] Conversion of volume % → mass % requires multiplying by molecular weight.
> - [x] Air-fuel ratio on **mass basis** is the KTU standard unless explicitly asked for molar A/F.
> - [x] Conversion of flue gas mass % → mole % uses the ratio (mass % / Mᵢ) normalised to 100.
> - [x] Always verify Σ (composition) = 100 % as a sanity check.
> - [x] **NG is the cleanest fossil fuel** — major application in **CCGT power plants** (η up to 62 %).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is LPG?

**Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG)** is a clean-burning, fossil-derived gaseous fuel that is stored and transported in liquefied form under moderate pressure. It is a mixture of light hydrocarbons, predominantly **propane (C₃H₈)** and **butane (C₄H₁₀)**, with trace amounts of propylene, butylene, and isobutane.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCIET303 – Module 4):**
> LPG is classified as an **alternative clean fuel** for Spark Ignition (SI) and modified Compression Ignition (CI) engines. In KTU examinations, it is treated as a low-emission gaseous fuel whose combustion chemistry directly influences the magnitude of regulated pollutants (CO, HC, NOₓ) emitted from an IC engine.

### Standard Properties at a Glance

| Property | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Boiling Point (at 1 atm) | **−42 °C (propane)** to **−0.5 °C (butane)** |
| Storage Pressure (typical) | **7–10 bar** (gauge) |
| Octane Number (RON) | **104–110** (very high) |
| Calorific Value (HHV) | **~46 MJ/kg** |
| Auto-ignition Temperature | **~410 °C** |
| Flame Speed | **~3.5 m/s** (faster than gasoline) |

---

## 1.2 What is IC Engine Pollution?

An IC engine expels a mixture of combustion products and unburnt species through its exhaust. Because combustion in a real cylinder is **non-ideal** (finite time, finite mixing, finite temperature), the exhaust contains substances that are harmful to human health, the ecosystem, and the climate.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Six Major Pollutants (Memorize the acronym CHONPS-C):**
> 1. **C** – Carbon Monoxide (CO)
> 2. **H** – Unburnt Hydrocarbons (HC)
> 3. **O** – Oxides of Nitrogen (NOₓ)
> 4. **N** – Particulate Matter (PM)
> 5. **P** – Lead (Pb) compounds
> 6. **S** – Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂) and CO₂ (greenhouse gas)

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Kitchen Stove and the Car Engine"

Think of a domestic LPG stove in a closed kitchen. When the burner is **properly tuned (stoichiometric air–fuel ratio, λ ≈ 1)**, combustion is complete — water vapour and CO₂ escape, but the kitchen stays clean. Now imagine the same stove with a **partially blocked nozzle**: a yellow flame appears, soot forms, and you feel dizzy. The yellow flame means **incomplete combustion** — CO and unburnt hydrocarbons are leaking out.

Your car's engine is a far more violent version of this stove, operating thousands of times per minute under enormous pressure and temperature. The same three things that pollute your kitchen — **too little oxygen, too little time, and too low a temperature locally** — are precisely what generate CO, HC, and particulate emissions. **NOₓ**, in contrast, forms when the *opposite* happens: *too much* local temperature (above 1377 K or **1100 °C**), causing atmospheric N₂ to oxidize.

So **LPG is like a perfectly tuned, premixed, vaporized fuel** that almost guarantees complete combustion — which is why it is celebrated as a clean alternative fuel in the IC engine pollution chapter.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why LPG is Considered a "Clean" Fuel

LPG's environmental advantage comes from its **molecular simplicity** and **physical state**:

* **Already gaseous on injection** → eliminates the wall-wetting and slow vaporization losses that produce HC emissions in petrol engines.
* **No aromatic or olefinic content** (unlike gasoline) → very low soot/tendency to form particulates.
* **No sulphur** → negligible SO₂ emissions.
* **No lead additives required** (its high RON of ~105 makes anti-knock additives unnecessary) → zero lead emissions.
* **Lean-burn capability** → because of high octane rating, LPG engines can run at air–fuel ratios of up to **λ ≈ 1.6** without knock, dramatically lowering CO and HC and moderately lowering NOₓ.

---

## 2.2 Chemistry of the Major Pollutants

### (a) Carbon Monoxide (CO)

* **Why it forms:** Insufficient O₂ inside the flame front (locally rich mixture) prevents complete oxidation of carbon to CO₂.
* **Key reaction:** $C + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \longrightarrow CO$
* **Dominant condition:** Cold start, idling, rich mixtures, low load.
* **LPG behaviour:** CO emissions are **~50–60 % lower than gasoline** because LPG vaporizes perfectly and mixes uniformly with air.

### (b) Unburnt Hydrocarbons (HC)

* **Why it forms:** Flame quenching at the cold cylinder walls (quench layer of ~0.1–0.5 mm) and crevice volumes (spark-plug threads, piston-ring gaps) trap unburnt fuel.
* **LPG behaviour:** Much lower HC because the fuel is already a gas, so wall quenching is reduced.

### (c) Oxides of Nitrogen (NOₓ)

* **Why it forms:** The extended **Zeldovich mechanism** (3-step chain reaction).
* **Threshold temperature:** Above **~1377 K (≈ 1100 °C)**.
* **Dominant condition:** High in-cylinder pressure and temperature (high load, lean burn at high power).
* **LPG behaviour:** Slightly higher NOₓ under stoichiometric conditions (because of higher flame temperature), but **lower NOₓ in lean-burn mode**.

### (d) Particulate Matter (PM)

* **Dominant in:** Diesel engines, due to heterogeneous combustion and soot formation from rich regions.
* **LPG behaviour:** Practically **zero PM** because LPG contains no high-molecular-weight aromatics.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master every row in this table. KTU 2024 ESE questions frequently test the chemical-balance stoichiometry and lambda definitions.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Variables / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Stoichiometric A/F ratio (gasoline) | $(\text{A/F})_{\text{stoich}} = 14.7$ | Mass-based, unitless |
| 2 | Stoichiometric A/F ratio (LPG) | $(\text{A/F})_{\text{stoich}} \approx 15.4$ | Slightly leaner than gasoline |
| 3 | Relative A/F ratio (λ) | $\lambda = \dfrac{(\text{A/F})_{\text{actual}}}{(\text{A/F})_{\text{stoich}}}$ | $\lambda < 1$ rich; $\lambda = 1$ stoich; $\lambda > 1$ lean |
| 4 | Equivalence ratio (φ) | $\phi = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | $\phi > 1$ rich; $\phi < 1$ lean |
| 5 | LPG combustion (propane) | $C_3H_8 + 5O_2 \longrightarrow 3CO_2 + 4H_2O$ | Stoichiometric |
| 6 | LPG combustion (n-butane) | $C_4H_{10} + 6.5 O_2 \longrightarrow 4CO_2 + 5H_2O$ | Stoichiometric |
| 7 | Air for 1 kmol fuel (C₃H₈) | $\text{Air} = 5 \times (100/21) = 23.81$ kmol | Using 21 % O₂ in air by volume |
| 8 | Air–Fuel mass ratio (C₃H₈) | $\text{A/F} = \dfrac{23.81 \times 29}{44} \approx 15.69$ | M(C₃H₈) = 44, M(air) = 29 |
| 9 | Zeldovich (thermal) NOₓ | $\dfrac{d[NO]}{dt} \propto \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | Arrhenius-type dependence on T |
| 10 | Three-way catalyst efficiency | $\eta_{\text{CO}} > 95\,\%$, $\eta_{\text{HC}} > 90\,\%$, $\eta_{\text{NO}_x} > 90\,\%$ | Operates only at λ ≈ 1 ± 0.01 |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Public Transport (Kerala SRTC buses):** Many municipal bus fleets in India run on LPG because of low PM and CO emissions in dense urban corridors.
2. **Forklifts and Warehouse Vehicles:** LPG is preferred indoors because of low toxic exhaust.
3. **Three-Wheeler Auto-Rickshaws:** LPG retrofits are very common in Kochi, Bengaluru, and Delhi.
4. **Power Generation:** LPG standby gensets produce much cleaner emissions, qualifying them for indoor use and low-emission zones.
5. **Refrigerant Substitute:** LPG (specifically R-290 = propane) is replacing HFC refrigerants due to zero Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP).
