# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric Air Required for LPG (Propane)

This derivation trains the student to write a balanced combustion equation and convert it into a mass-based A/F ratio — a frequent KTU Part-B sub-question.

**Step 1: Write the stoichiometric equation for propane (C₃H₈)**

$$C_3H_8 + a\,O_2 \longrightarrow b\,CO_2 + c\,H_2O$$

**Step 2: Balance carbon atoms**

$$3 = b \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; b = 3$$

**Step 3: Balance hydrogen atoms**

$$8 = 2c \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; c = 4$$

**Step 4: Balance oxygen atoms**

$$2a = 2b + c = 2(3) + 4 = 10 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; a = 5$$

**Step 5: Balanced equation**

$$C_3H_8 + 5O_2 \longrightarrow 3CO_2 + 4H_2O$$

**Step 6: Convert O₂ requirement to air requirement**

Air contains **21 % O₂ by volume (mole basis)** and **23 % O₂ by mass**. Since chemical equations are in moles:

$$\text{Air required per mole of fuel} = 5 \times \dfrac{100}{21} = 23.81 \text{ kmol of air}$$

**Step 7: Convert to mass-based A/F ratio**

Using molecular weights: $M_{\text{air}} = 28.97$ kg/kmol, $M_{C_3H_8} = 44$ kg/kmol.

$$\text{A/F} = \dfrac{m_{\text{air}}}{m_{\text{fuel}}} = \dfrac{23.81 \times 28.97}{1 \times 44} = 15.68$$

**Step 8: For commercial LPG (60 % C₃H₈ + 40 % C₄H₁₀ by mass)**

For butane: $C_4H_{10} + 6.5O_2 \to 4CO_2 + 5H_2O$

$$\text{A/F for butane} = \dfrac{(6.5 \times 100/21) \times 28.97}{58} = 15.43$$

Blending:

$$\text{A/F}_{\text{blend}} = 0.6 \times 15.68 + 0.4 \times 15.43 = 15.58$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result (matches literature value ≈ 15.4–15.7):** A stoichiometric A/F ratio of ~15.5 confirms that LPG requires slightly more air per kg of fuel than gasoline (14.7), but its energy content per kg is also higher, so volumetric fuel consumption differs.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effect of Equivalence Ratio on Pollutant Formation

The three regulated pollutants vary systematically with λ. KTU questions often ask you to *explain with a sketch* or *tabulate* the trends.

**Analytical Matrix — Variation of Pollutants with λ**

| λ (Relative A/F) | Mixture State | CO | HC | NOₓ | CO₂ | Thermal Efficiency |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0.7 | Very rich | **Very high** | High | Low | Low | Low |
| 0.9 | Rich | High | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate |
| 1.0 | Stoichiometric | Moderate | Moderate | **Very high** | **Maximum** | Moderate |
| 1.1 | Slightly lean | Low | Low | High | High | **High** |
| 1.3 | Lean | **Very low** | **Very low** | Moderate | High | High |
| 1.6 | Very lean (LPG limit) | Near zero | Near zero | **Low** | Moderate | Moderate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why NOₓ peaks near λ = 1.0:**
> The in-cylinder temperature is maximum near stoichiometric, and NOₓ formation depends exponentially on temperature (Zeldovich equation). A 100 K rise in peak temperature *doubles* the NOₓ rate.

**Trend Identification (for exam writing):**

* CO decreases monotonically as λ increases (more O₂ available for complete combustion).
* HC peaks at very rich (insufficient O₂) and very lean (poor flame propagation), minimum in between.
* NOₓ peaks near λ = 1, where combustion temperature is highest.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Energy Released per kg of LPG

The calorific value (CV) of LPG is calculated from enthalpies of formation using Hess's Law. For propane:

$$C_3H_8 + 5O_2 \longrightarrow 3CO_2 + 4H_2O$$

$$\Delta H_c = \sum \Delta H_f(\text{products}) - \sum \Delta H_f(\text{reactants})$$

Using standard values (in kJ/mol): $\Delta H_f(\text{C}_3\text{H}_8) = -104.7$, $\Delta H_f(\text{O}_2) = 0$, $\Delta H_f(\text{CO}_2) = -393.5$, $\Delta H_f(\text{H}_2\text{O, l}) = -285.8$.

$$\Delta H_c = 3(-393.5) + 4(-285.8) - (-104.7) - 5(0)$$

$$\Delta H_c = -1180.5 - 1143.2 + 104.7 = -2219 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

Per kg of propane:

$$\text{HHV} = \dfrac{2219 \text{ kJ/mol}}{0.044 \text{ kg/mol}} = 50.43 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The accepted value is **~46 MJ/kg** for commercial LPG (which is a propane–butane blend, and butane's HHV is slightly lower at ~45.7 MJ/kg).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Why Lean-Burn Engines Reduce All Three Pollutants

This is a frequent KTU 14-mark question. Write it as a logical proof.

**Premise:** LPG has RON ≈ 105, so it can sustain stable combustion at λ = 1.4–1.6 without knock.

**Step 1 — CO Reduction:**

The CO formation rate is limited by the local O₂ concentration. In lean burn (λ > 1), excess O₂ ensures the equilibrium $2CO + O_2 \rightleftharpoons 2CO_2$ lies far to the right.

**Step 2 — HC Reduction:**

HC originates in the quench layer and crevice volumes. With lean burn, the cylinder bulk temperature is lower, and the quench layer is thinner, so fewer fuel molecules escape unburnt.

**Step 3 — NOₓ Reduction (the key advantage):**

In lean burn, excess air acts as a thermal sink. The peak in-cylinder temperature drops by approximately:

$$\Delta T \approx \dfrac{(\lambda - 1) \cdot m_{\text{air}} \cdot c_p}{m_{\text{mixture}} \cdot c_{p,\text{mixture}}}$$

A 100 K drop in peak temperature approximately **halves** the thermal NOₓ (Arrhenius exponential).

**Conclusion:** Running LPG at λ ≈ 1.5 simultaneously reduces CO, HC, AND NOₓ — a regime impossible with gasoline due to knock. This is the *single most important reason* LPG is recommended for clean-fuel IC engines.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. List the major pollutants emitted from an IC engine. Mention how the use of LPG as a fuel reduces these pollutants.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The major pollutants from an IC engine are:

1. **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** — product of incomplete combustion.
2. **Unburnt Hydrocarbons (HC)** — from quench layer and crevice regions.
3. **Oxides of Nitrogen (NOₓ)** — from high-temperature oxidation of N₂.
4. **Particulate Matter (PM)** — especially in diesel.
5. **CO₂ and SO₂** — combustion products and acid-rain precursors.

**How LPG reduces them:**

* Already gaseous → uniform mixing → **lower CO and HC**.
* No aromatics and no sulphur → **lower PM and SO₂**.
* No need for lead → **no Pb emissions**.
* High RON (≈ 105) → **lean-burn operation (λ ≈ 1.5)** reduces NOₓ and CO simultaneously.

---

### **Q2. Why is LPG preferred for use in indoor vehicles like forklifts?** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

LPG is preferred for indoor vehicles because:

1. It produces **very low levels of toxic CO and HC** (almost complete combustion due to gaseous state).
2. It generates **negligible particulate matter and no black smoke**.
3. No sulphur → no SO₂.
4. Combustion is **clean**, making it suitable for poorly ventilated environments like warehouses.
5. Additionally, LPG does not contaminate stored goods with soot or odour.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Always balance the combustion equation** before substituting into any A/F formula. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for an unbalanced equation.
> 2. **Do not confuse RON with MON.** LPG's RON is 105, but its MON is around 89 — explicitly state the type.
> 3. **Mention λ values** when comparing lean burn vs stoichiometric. Vague statements like "LPG gives less pollution" fetch only partial credit.
> 4. **Units matter.** Always write A/F as a dimensionless ratio and report calorific value in MJ/kg, not kcal/kg.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **formation mechanism of CO, HC, and NOₓ** in an SI engine. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **emission control techniques** used in modern SI engines, with a focus on **three-way catalytic converters and exhaust gas recirculation (EGR)**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

### Part (a) — Formation Mechanisms

**1. Carbon Monoxide (CO):**
* Forms when the local air–fuel mixture is **richer than stoichiometric** (locally fuel-rich pockets).
* The reaction $C + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \to CO$ is incomplete because of insufficient O₂ within the flame-front reaction zone.
* CO formation is **highest during cold start, idling, and acceleration** (transient rich operation).
* The dissociation reaction $CO_2 \rightleftharpoons CO + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2$ is also favoured when the post-flame gases cool slowly (quenching), preventing the reverse reaction.

**2. Unburnt Hydrocarbons (HC):**
* Caused by **flame quenching** at the cylinder walls (a quench layer of 0.1–0.5 mm where temperature drops below the auto-ignition value).
* Trapped in **crevice volumes** — spark-plug threads, piston-ring gaps, head-gasket regions.
* **Wall wetting** by liquid fuel (in port-injection petrol engines) creates a fuel film that survives combustion.
* **Combustion-chamber deposits** absorb and later release HC.

**3. Oxides of Nitrogen (NOₓ):**
* Formed by the **Zeldovich thermal mechanism** above 1377 K (1100 °C).
* Main reactions:

$$N_2 + O \rightleftharpoons NO + N$$

$$N + O_2 \rightleftharpoons NO + O$$

$$N + OH \rightleftharpoons NO + H$$

* NO formation is **exponentially dependent on temperature** (Arrhenius-type).
* Maximum at **stoichiometric** or slightly lean operation (peak in-cylinder temperature).
* NO freezes as gases cool, so post-combustion control requires rapid cooling or chemical reduction in the exhaust.

### Part (b) — Emission Control Techniques

**1. Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC):**
* Contains **platinum (Pt), palladium (Pd), and rhodium (Rh)** as catalysts on a ceramic honeycomb substrate.
* Performs **three simultaneous reactions**:
  * $2CO + O_2 \to 2CO_2$ (oxidation)
  * $C_xH_y + O_2 \to CO_2 + H_2O$ (oxidation)
  * $2NO_x \to N_2 + xO_2$ (reduction)
* Operates efficiently **only at λ ≈ 1 ± 0.01** — hence the closed-loop oxygen (lambda) sensor is essential.
* Achieves **> 90 % conversion** of all three pollutants.

**2. Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR):**
* A portion (5–15 %) of the exhaust gas is recirculated into the intake manifold.
* Inert exhaust gases **lower the peak combustion temperature**, suppressing NOₓ formation.
* Trade-off: slightly higher HC and PM at high EGR rates.
* Modern EGR is **electronically controlled** by the ECU using a solenoid valve.

**3. Other Auxiliary Systems:**
* **Positive Crankcase Ventilation (PCV):** routes blow-by gases back to the intake — eliminates HC from crankcase emissions.
* **Evaporative Emission Control (EVAP):** traps fuel vapours from the tank in an activated-carbon canister — eliminates evaporative HC.
* **Air Injection System:** pumps fresh air into the exhaust manifold to burn residual CO and HC.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain why **LPG is considered a clean alternative fuel** for IC engines. Compare its properties and emissions with gasoline. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the **stoichiometric combustion equation of propane**, and derive the **stoichiometric air–fuel ratio by mass** for pure propane. Comment on how this ratio differs for **commercial LPG (60 % propane + 40 % butane)**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

### Part (a) — LPG as a Clean Alternative Fuel

**1. Fuel Properties Comparison**

| Property | Gasoline | LPG (Propane) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Octane Number (RON) | 90–95 | **104–110** |
| Calorific Value (MJ/kg) | 44 | **46** |
| Latent Heat of Vaporization (kJ/kg) | 305 | 425 |
| Stoichiometric A/F | 14.7 | 15.5 |
| Boiling Point (°C) | 35–200 | −42 |
| Sulphur Content | 30–50 ppm | < 10 ppm |
| Aromatics | 25–35 % | 0 % |

**2. Reasons LPG is Cleaner:**

**(i) Gaseous State on Injection:** LPG is already a vapour when injected, eliminating wall-wetting → **lower HC**.

**(ii) No Aromatics:** Absence of high-molecular-weight aromatic compounds → **no soot or PM**.

**(iii) High Octane Number:** Allows **lean-burn operation** up to λ ≈ 1.6, reducing all three regulated pollutants simultaneously.

**(iv) Low Sulphur:** Negligible SO₂; modern LPG is also lead-free.

**(v) Uniform Mixing:** Excellent homogeneity of LPG–air mixture → **more complete combustion → lower CO**.

**3. Limitations:**

* Lower energy density per litre (must be stored under pressure).
* Requires heavy pressurized tank — **weight penalty**.
* Cold-starting is harder due to high latent heat of vaporization.

### Part (b) — Stoichiometric Combustion of Propane

**Step 1 — Balanced equation:**

$$C_3H_8 + 5O_2 \longrightarrow 3CO_2 + 4H_2O$$

**Step 2 — Air required per mole of propane (using 21 % O₂ by volume):**

$$n_{\text{air}} = \dfrac{5 \times 100}{21} = 23.81 \text{ kmol air/kmol fuel}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of air per mole of propane** ($M_{\text{air}} = 28.97$):

$$m_{\text{air}} = 23.81 \times 28.97 = 689.78 \text{ kg per kmol of fuel}$$

**Step 4 — Mass of propane per mole** ($M_{\text{C}_3\text{H}_8} = 44$):

$$m_{\text{fuel}} = 1 \times 44 = 44 \text{ kg per kmol}$$

**Step 5 — Stoichiometric A/F by mass:**

$$(\text{A/F})_{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{689.78}{44} = 15.68$$

**Step 6 — For commercial LPG (60 % C₃H₈ + 40 % C₄H₁₀ by mass):**

For butane: $C_4H_{10} + 6.5O_2 \to 4CO_2 + 5H_2O$

$$(\text{A/F})_{\text{butane}} = \dfrac{(6.5 \times 100/21) \times 28.97}{58} = 15.43$$

**Step 7 — Blended A/F:**

$$(\text{A/F})_{\text{blend}} = 0.6 \times 15.68 + 0.4 \times 15.43 = 15.58$$

**Comment:** Commercial LPG has a stoichiometric A/F ratio of **≈ 15.5**, which is higher (leaner) than gasoline (14.7). This is why LPG vehicles use **larger-volume venturi mixers or differently sized injectors** compared to petrol systems. The stoichiometric ratio informs the ECU mapping for closed-loop lambda control.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Final-minute scan before entering the exam hall):**

* LPG = **C₃H₈ + C₄H₁₀ blend**, stored as liquid under **7–10 bar**.
* **RON ≈ 105**, **HHV ≈ 46 MJ/kg**, **A/F stoich ≈ 15.5**.
* Stoichiometric combustion of propane: $C_3H_8 + 5O_2 \to 3CO_2 + 4H_2O$.
* **Six major pollutants:** CO, HC, NOₓ, PM, CO₂, SO₂ (+ lead historically).
* **CO** forms due to rich mixture / incomplete combustion → maximum at cold start and idling.
* **HC** forms due to flame quenching, crevice volumes, and wall wetting.
* **NOₓ** forms via the **Zeldovich mechanism** above **1377 K**, peaks near λ ≈ 1.
* **PM** is mainly a **diesel** problem; LPG produces negligible PM.
* **LPG advantages:** gaseous, no aromatics, no sulphur, no lead, lean-burn capable, low emissions.
* **LPG disadvantages:** lower energy density, pressurized storage required, harder cold start.
* **Three-way catalyst** works only at **λ = 1 ± 0.01**; needs oxygen (lambda) sensor feedback.
* **EGR** lowers NOₓ by diluting intake charge and reducing peak flame temperature.
* **TWC conversions:** $\eta_{\text{CO}} > 95\%$, $\eta_{\text{HC}} > 90\%$, $\eta_{\text{NO}_x} > 90\%$.
* **Lean-burn advantage of LPG** (λ = 1.4–1.6) — **all three pollutants drop simultaneously** (impossible with petrol due to knock).
* Always specify units, balance equations, and quote λ values in KTU answers.
* **Indian emission standard = BS VI (since 2020)** — equivalent to Euro 6.
